comments.commentBody: Spread thin layer of mayo on buns and toast in the pan before you add the oil to cook the burgers. You’ll never go back.

You look at it and think: What kind of human jaw was this built for?
To take a bite, you’d have to unhinge your jaw like a python going after a wild boar.
One chomp and the whole thing collapses like a Jenga tower.
Let’s be honest — this isn’t food, it’s a structural challenge with a sesame seed bun.
@Andre I don’t agree with you, but your comment made me laugh out loud. I love this burger.
I gently hand mix a tablespoon or so of my nice barbecue sauce into each burger as I form it.  Keeps them moist (along with the fat) and very yummy.
@sarah I will sometimes put a very light smear of BBQ sauce on the outside before cooking.  The sugar in the sauce helps make a nice crust giving the burger a great mouth feel.

I prefer the ketchup, lettuce, onion, and tomato on the top such that you bite through these in the way to the meat.  I usually put pickles and brown mustard on the bottom so that those flavors hit the tongue first.  Also, I will live with the risk of possible salmonella (or whatever) for the pleasure of eating a nice red, juicy, delicious burger!
I like to crisp up the buns in the burger fat.

I always buy ground beef that is ground in the store, not the bricks that are tightly packed. I then, somewhat laboriously, pull parts of the beef off in shreds onto a good size piece of foil creating a mound of the meat. I add dried herbs and then divide the mound into 2 to 4 equal sizes and then form the burgers.
Biting into a burger that has hard areas of packed meat is unpleasant and changes the mouth feel.
Great on a lettuce "bun" too.

I can verify that messing around with the meat makes it stiffer. A big juicy burger is better when it's less like a flap and more like a tend layer of juicy goodness. When I make meat loaf, which I prefer to be stiffer, I mix beef and pork and go at it with a power handblender to make it finer and resultantly stiffer.
I’m sure that the NYC Health Department would close J G Melon if they served their burgers cooked to 125 degrees.
In his NYT "burger 101" video, Kenji encourages fresh-ground beef, not cryovac-ed. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VOQeRJHkP40
I use beef tallow as my oil and it takes the burgers to a new beefy level.
bookmark "Kenji’s Tips for the Best Burgers You’ve Ever Had | J. Kenji López-Alt | Cooking 101 | NYT Cooking," video embedded in recipe above.  Other "NYT cooking 101" videos are great, too.

It sounds yummy! I just had a delicious burger from a restaurant at Trail Lake Lodge, Moose Pass, AK and I think one difference in their burger was the absolutely perfect and greasy bun! Some sort of brioche, but they must have toasted it on the grill with the grease..
I do beyond meat

I feel like letting the rest on a cutting board or separate plate for at least five minutes is a necessary step, at least if you’re going for medium to medium rare burgers. But otherwise this is a great approach and one I actually prefer over the grill

After watching the (whole burger) video twice and buying the meat that I thought would work, I am finally feeling confident in my burger-making ability. Added some grilled jalapenos to the burger sauce. Cooked in the cast iron skillet and developed a lovely burnished crust. Thank you @Kenji for taking the time to give us the primer. This diner recipe produces a delicious result and now I can go forth and make good burgers any time.

The most important thing with making the “perfect burger“ is how you construct it after it’s done. Very important to give it a little rest while you’re adding the condiments. I prefer putting my lettuce on the bottom underneath the burger to keep the burger from making the bottom bun too soggy. After that… Build away to your heart’s content.
Burgers are the worst thing you can cook on a barbecue. The only good burger is a greasy, juicy smashburger, cooked on the stove in an iron skillet. Save the outdoor barbecue for sausages and steaks.

Disagree. Food often is about context. And here in the north woods of Minnesota a burger on the outdoor grill (I cook only with charcoal) is preferable to anything cooked on a skillet. Don't get me wrong, I understand the benefits of cooking indoors on the skillet (especially burgers) ... But in summer, especially when it's hot (no A/C here) a well-prepared burger (with some brats) on a grill smells and tastes amazing. Mine definitely will be way better after reading/watching this.
FYI: In the video, Kenji says his sauce is 1:4 ratio mayo to ketchup, but what he actually does is 4:1 mayo to ketchup
I've found that the sirloin beef makes really good burgers that retain the shape and dont fall apart.

Sirloin is way too lean (7-10 percent fat) to make a juicy burger.  Ground chuck is much better (15-20 percent fat). Personally, I have my beef custom ground at my local butcher from a blend of short rib, chuck, and brisket point (not the flat) coarsly ground which yields a +/- 25 percent fat patty.
I agree that ground sirloin, grass-fed if at all possible, has great flavor, and I don't need or want the extra fat introduced with chuck or even brisket.  The heart wants what it wants, though, yes?
Still searching for the perfect burger blend to get a truly beefy steakburger. Latest was 75% flap sirloin 25% chuck. Almost there.
Yes!!! A thick burger with a juicy hot pink center is everything. I personal HATE the smashburger fad.

I recently found out about keeping salt on the exterior, and I can wholeheartedly endorse that. I use 2 teaspoons of store-brand kosher salt per pound of beef. It will seem like a lot, but the burgers I made last week didn’t taste salty, just well seasoned.

Use a burger mix from the butcher with scraps which contain 20-25% fat.  This amount of fat tends to be challenging on a BBQ grill so I prefer a cast iron pan. Next, I prefer a a thinner, flatter, style of burger.use a lid from a Mayo jar to form my patties.
I sear both sides of the burger in a medium hot pan for a nice thick crust (Maillard reaction). I know the burger is done when the top of the flipped burger shows juice. Lastly butter and griddle your buns.
mayo and siratcha to gild the lily

Angus beef farm. Smashburgers, nope, squashes out moisture. 85/15 Angus sirloin blend. 80/20 loses too much size, rendering fat, Dad said. The night before, form the burgers, 1/4 lb. each. Douse both sides with Worcestershire sauce, coarse black pepper, Adobo seasoning powder or garlic and onion powder, kosher salt flakes and put in a shallow dish and tightly cover with plastic wrap. Start on hot side of grill, flip once to cooler side, pouring pan juices on burger. Let rest under foil 8 min.
Many (most?) people love smashburgers...
Try spreading softened butter on the bun before toasting. It really contributes flavor to the burger.
Spot on.  Great burgers.  Followed the recipe mostly, Chuck hamburger.  Super.

Over the years I have simplified my burger.  Ingredients: Toasted sesame seed bun, grilled burger, a single, thin slice of sweet onion on the burger and a dollop of ketchup (used sparingly) on the plate.  My goal is to enjoy the taste of the burger with few extra competing flavors.

would like some advice on Turkey Burgers - my wife insists that her burger is turkey. The store bought pre-made turkey burgers always fall apart and ooze a pinkish slime.  There seems no way to pull a turkey burger off f a grill,\.

We can be way too precious about how to make a hamburger on the grill. My personal opinion is fresh hamburger meat with about 20% fat is best and we don’t have to dance around with seasonings and whether to touch it beforehand.   Any hamburger as long as it’s fresh. I went to the business Costco and got 10 pounds of the 80% and froze it fresh. (it all comes to the store shipped in those tubes anyway no matter where you get it. ) The first batch is the best and the rest is acceptable. And I use a Weber charcoal grill because I always have.

In the winter, I cook it on the stove with a pan. Not as good but OK. As my mother-in-law did I cut potatoes up like french fries and sauté them with the burger although the potatoes do take longer.

The main thing is not to get too wound up on the importance of what you’re doing.
If you use a leaner ground beef you can fold in some shredded cheddar cheese to add moisture without the grease.

In my feeble attempt to make burgers a little bit "healthier," I buy 90% hamburger meat and add some olive oil to achieve the proper fat/meat ratio.  It works well for me, so maybe worth a try for others. (It changes the feel of the meat when forming the burgers, but grills up really nicely and with a good char.)

All you need as per a credible chef:

1. Use 75/25 ground beef! It's the perfect blend to keep it juicy and let the fat render out during the cook to help get that beautiful crust! 80/20 works as well, but it's slightly worse than this blend for smashburgers.
2. When you form the 3-4 oz smashburger balls, don't overwork the beef or handle it too much. You want it to really spread out when smashing and this helps get that amazing texture that Smashburger are known for! Just loosely form it with your hands and your done!
3. When cooking, preheat a cast iron pan or griddle over medium high to high heat. You want the cooktop to read 450° F or more. I aim for 475-525 most of the time! You also want to avoid using any oil, it will actually prevent the beef from sticking which is needed for that perfect crust!
4. Season it up after smashing and Let it cook 80-90% (90 seconds) through before flipping, and when you flip you need to make sure you carefully scrape up the edges! This will make sure the crust stays in tact, which is the whole point of a Smashburger! Then cook long enough to melt cheese and done!

@Jeff P. 
You basically restated the technique here, except for the size of burger. Kenji calls for 2 pounds of meat for 4 burgers. That seems too big for me, and I like the idea of a 4 oz smash burger.

@Nana cooks
I believe the recipe calls for 16 oz. Beef.
i agree and don't forget the Gelatin, it really does make a difference! I sometime have gone as far as 60/40.

Followed this recipe to the letter but instead of buns I used doppleganger soups, and instead of lettuce I used a vhs copy of 1994’s Cape Fear, and instead of smash burgers I used continental existentialism.
@Ace Now THAT sounds delicious!

I've done smash burgers. Some, like my wife, wants them paper thin; in the belief they'll be 'crispier'. If the burger is thinner than the cheese, that's a deal breaker for some of us. 

If you've ever gone to a 5 Guys, you can watch them smash all the juice out of the burger. They hammer that thing to death. I'll make one for my wife, but I'll stick with a thick, decent burger that doesn't have all the juice smashed out of it.

My trick is right after smashing the patties into the skillet I add thinly sliced onion (and an optional squirt of yellow mustard) right on top of the patty.  Then when the first side is nice and browned I flip so the onions are on the bottom for the second half cook.  If they're thinly sliced they cook quickly, and they insulate the bottom half of the patty slightly so you have a little longer to melt your cheese.  Plus I like cooked onion on a burger and it streamlines the process vs cooking them separately.

Also sometimes I'll put a couple drops of water and throw a lid or upside-down steel bowl over to help it steam the cheese for some extra melt.  It happens very fast so you can take it off quickly and not steam the patty too much.

A wide, high-quality putty knife also works well for scraping up the burgers. Kenji also makes a "Fake Shack" burger, with the sauce, that I highly recommend. I make extra sauce to serve with a side of deep fried tots. (Tots are far easier to do at home than fries and they always come out perfectly.)
@Andy what is the sauce you’re talking about? tia.
For the buns: Martin's Potato Rolls have never disappointed!

Agree w Jeff P re 75/25 ratio.   The extra fat improves beef flavor as they cook in it.   Instead of American or cheddar,  “classics” though they may be,  try a light coat of finely crumbled  Danish blue cheese.   Less generic cheesiness, more compliment to earthy iron flavor of good beef.    Works even for folks who think they dont like blue cheese, its very mild. I do these on outside flattop at 475.    No lettuce, tomato, or ketchup.  Nothing wet.  Why destroy that beautiful crunch?  Just one slice of crisp applewood smoked bacon, halved, on top of the cheese.  Lightly toasted buttered potato bun,  perfection!!
@Ed Kass, never seen that ratio in stores. Do you get it at your local butcher?

@Frank

Schweid and Sons Signature Blend at ACME or Shoprite, or at other large grocery stores i believe.
@Frank My Walmart, United supermarket (part of Albertsons), and H-E-B carry it. Very common at least here and the cheapest of the hamburger choices.

I discovered that a smash burger is completely devoid of taste by itself. It needs copious amounts of cheese, onions, ketchup, mustard & other condiments & those are what you really taste, not the burger.

I think that smashburgers are one of the worst culinary trends of recent years.  It is almost impossible to cook such a burger less than medium (please, no finger-wagging about the dangers of undercooked meat-I have never had a problem as I am careful about where I order burgers).    Also, I understand wanting some char or a bit of crustiness on a burger's exterior, but the idea of crunchy, dried out edges  is, in the words of Hall & Oates, a no-can do.
I use waygu ground beef from Costco.

I agree with Nate wholeheartedly about the thinly sliced onions. The original Depression era smash burgers were made this way to make a pound of meat go further. When I make mine, they’re about 2 ounces ground chuck, 2 ounces onions. And cheese, of course!

People act like this is some new revelation. The reason everyone is in love with them is they remind them of old school drive-in burgers. That's how they would be done, super thin, mostly to save money using less meat.
90/10 ground buffalo or 95/5 Emu hands down the absolute best.
Is there a reason for using a steel pan vs cast iron? If so, what adjustments are needed for cast iron?

@Geophany

He explains why in the video — it’s about different surfaces in the pan

Refrigerate the lightly formed balls of meat for at least 20 mins before smashing. The cold meat holds together better and allows for a craggier edge and a better crust. Since the fat will render more slowly, it will allow for a more "meaty" flavor.  Been doing that for years!

I found using a meat pounder worked well to smash the beef into a patty.  One swift press did the trick.  I did the stacked 2 ounce patties and used a cheese plane to cut pepper jack.  Instead of putting the cheese on in the pan, I laid it on each burger after I putting it on the bun.  Since the cheese was so thin it melted right in.

These burgers and most recipes for burgers are overly large (high). How do you eat it without disassembly or squashing it and making a mess. Photographs lovely but doesn't work as a handheld food item

my favorite smashburgers are from a local place here (The Emerald Mallard, in Felton, CA), made with Waygu beef thats like 50% fat, and cooked in tallow on a very hot griddle, smashed paper thin, and crispy brown, and full of that grilled meat Unami flavor.  They are indeed cooked with some thin diced white onion, and melted cheese over each patty, usually served as a double on a big soft bun, some 'secret sauce', diced lettuce, thin slices of dill pickle, and some other fixings.
God the Emerald Mallard is amazing

This note is really in response to Kenji’s rules about making better burgers, with which rules I agree entirely.  And I would add one more, along the lines of the "have your buns ready” rule, specifically have your toppings, lettuce, sauce, tomatoes, etc ready at room temperature.   There’s nothing that puts me off a nice hot burger faster than to have it topped with chilled lettuce and tomatoes and pickles.

Unless your only reason is to not heat up the kitchen, if you are going to light your grill, why would you ever cook burgers on a griddle on the grill?   Griddle defeats the whole purpose of grilling.   When I don't have the time to pre-heat and fuss with the grill, I will just throw them on the griddle on the stove.

I do smash burgers on a flattop on my gas grill to avoid smoking up my house and having to clean my cooktop, counter, backsplash floor and range hood just to make a burger. Smash burgers spatter a lot. 

I assume you're using charcoal. That'd be a lot of trouble for a burger.

Too much. Too big. I want to see a video of anyone eating the burgers that started this article.  What percent landed in the eaters lap or fell on the floor?  Or. Your shouldn't need to use a fork to clean up all the droppings on the plate.  Thinner, less of everything.  Then have a second one!
The internet is riddled with fetish videos of any kind of person you can imagine eating messy burgers.

I have never understood the attraction of the smashed burger.  If you are making the effort to get beef with a certain percentage of fat, why do you want to press all the moisture out of it.  Get beef, or mixed meats, with lower fat content, make the patties thinner, and you will get a crispy burger that is also juicy.  I never turn a burger more than once since that is all that is needed to seal in the juices.
How am I supposed to bite into this?  My mouth opens only so far!
Try Traeger's Blackened Saskatchewan Rub on your burgers. It adds wonderful flavor to the meat.

One of the worst ways to destroy good meat is making a smash burger. The cooked smash burger tastes like cardboard by itself. What you are really tasting is the ketchup or mustard, onions & cheese. Make a regular half pound burger, cook it gently on both sides, do not smash it & you’ll have a delicious, juicy, tasty burger.

I’ll remain with my 1” thick bison burger. Slowly grilled to perfection without the garden party and dairy store. The purists here in Minneapolis remain committed to their molten cheese in the middle Juicy Lucy’s.
There are no potato buns to be had in Australia. Very unfortunate.
Use a fork to mix up the raw burger and ingredients you want to avoid over-handling.

I disagree with the Lean to Fat ratioo...80/20 for me is too lean...I do somewhere between 70/30 and 60/40 lean to fat...that's what gets all that juicy flavour and crispy edge texture when you smash it down.
This works with toasted pita bread.   Also added some Chili Crisp to the sauce  which was nice.

The video was fun, but left me with the same question I always seem to have:  how do you scale for serving more people?

It's easy with grilled burgers, but how do you scale for smashburgers or 'diner burgers'?

My guess is, you can't, unless you have a large flat cooking griddle?

Peeps?

About 10 seconds till removal, put buns with no condiments with a slice of cheese on top of the burger. Put on lid and steam until cheese is melted...a few seconds. My mom did this and her smashed burgers were scrumptous...also, only salt when the burger is on your plate..since not everyone likes or can have salt.
How much gelatin—one packet?

I gotta add a pitch for grinding your own beef if you have a grinder.  Makes an enormous difference in flavor. In order to reduce overall time and effort I usually just get a big chunk of chuck, grind a lot more than I need, freeze the extra for next time.

Absolutely amazing. I followed the recipe as written except I used thinly sliced havarti between the patties. The only thing I'll do differently is to use my grill outside. It was quite the cleanup and the kitchen friendly smoke detectors didn't like it either. 
It was a great way to use locally sourced beef from my neighbourhood dairy farm.
Turned out perfectly!  I use hamburger that is 70% sirloin & 30% chuck, for juiciness.
Start with good quality ranch-raised beef, if possible.
100% ground bison with ca. 1T of butter loosely mixed in is terrific.
Served these tonight with Eric Kim’s Yum Yum sauce from NYT Cooking and some caramelized onions and got rave reviews.

Cast iron skillet on a hot gas grill.  Everything else the same.

Delicious burgers.

My burgers are always overcooked because I want to kill the germs in the center. He says for a smash burger, 90 seconds on the first side and just long enough to melt the cheese on the second side.   I’m afraid the center won’t get to 160 degrees.  Should I take the pan off the burner and put a lid on it if it’s not up to 160?

He says to use a small 6” stainless steel pan for one individual thicker type burger.  I don’t have a 6” stainless steel pan.  I would really like to master cooking thick juicy burgers.  I have a 10” carbon steel pan, a 6” cast iron pan, a 6” Caraway non-stick pan, a 6” All-Clad Teflon type pan, and a 12” stainless steel pan.  It’s like the Goldilocks of pans. I don’t have the one that is “just right”!  I really want to make one “safe” but juicy thick burger.  

Two questions:  
1) Since I don’t have a 6” stainless pan, which of my pans should I use for a thicker burger?  
2) Should the meat thermometer say 160 degrees before I take the burger out of the pan, or should I cover the pan and let the burger rest in the pan until it gets to 160, or should I take the burger out of the pan, cover the burger until it gets to 160?

@P R use the smallest size pan you own. It might be 8” or 9” or 10”. Whatever. Doesn’t matter.  The smash burgers described in the article are definitely cooked through.  But please don’t cook thick burgers to 160.  That’s unnecessary.  130 is fine. Live a little.
Sam's siders are perfect for making double smash burgers.
How do you eat these?
I want a veggie smash burger. Who's got the goods?
I don’t think we’ll ever make regular hamburgers again. These are amazing, and so easy. My husband’s first time making smash burgers and they were perfect!

The preamble clearly states this recipe "starts with a love of store bought mac and cheese". I got that immediately. But a whole lot better. A little more involved that chucking chopped bacon into a box of pasta but the pay off was worth it. And very kid friendly. My 4 year old had helping after helping, "went to bed" got out of bed 40 minutes later saying, "I'm hungry." And had 2 more bowls before heading back to bed for the 5th and final time. I think. Love that kid.

For folks who don't eat bacon or other pork products, I find that canned tuna fish works just great to give the type of dish that salt and flavor zip.  I suppose that fresh tuna, minced clams, or anchovies could also work, but haven't gone that route yet.
Yes! It's called tuna-noodle casserole.
Perfect for Lent, thank you.
@Fred Chanania or capers (my sub for anchovies)

I like this wedding of simple pasta and peas (a Sicilian standard) and carbonara.  I always sautee onion (and whatever, diced mushrooms, a few roma tomatoes?), then throw in the peas for a few minutes while the pasta cooks.  No pancetta or bacon - just a simple veggie dish.  Doesn't need the garlc, IMO, but do as you like.  A little egg and cream - what's not to like? Plenty of pecorino, freshly ground pepper, and everything is wonderful.

Wow, stop defining whether this is Italian or Not-Italian, Carbonara or Not-Carbonara - it's simply a good simple meal! No need to label it! We whipped this up on a busy week night in no time. Enjoy it for what it is!
TSP ham bouillon in the sauce instead of using the bacon grease for the scallions and garlic.  A TSP of evo for the scallions and garlic.  Thanks.
I never knew there was such a thing as ham bouillon until I read this comment. You have just made my world a better place.
Better Than Bouillon Ham Base is the one I use.

For  a real taste sensation, substitute salami for bacon/pancetta. Cut salami in 1/4- to 1/2-inch dice and saute in a dry skillet until just slightly crispy. Remove and add to pasta when sauce is finished along with parsley.
This is such a weird hybrid recipe. No doubt it's tasty for some, but it's an alien, overly-complicated concept for an Italian.

This was really good, but 1 pound of pasta is WAY TOO MUCH. I ended up using only half the pasta. Using 1 pound of pasta would have resulted in having to double the eggs, cream, cheese, and pasta water. Also, it will increase the number of people it will feed to 8-10.

I loved this!! Super easy and delicious. 

I added some lemon zest to give it some freshness and it brought it to the next level. (If my family sees this, don't mock me. I've never met a dish that wasn't improved by lemon zest.)

An simpler variation is an invention of my wife. Mary Ann's Peas & Pasta begins by sauteing about half a small onion in some good olive oil. We prefer the onion thinly slivered and cooked until nicely caramelized. Add a pinch of crushed red pepper, then add a can of small/baby peas. Heat and enjoy as a tasty vegetable side, or cook up a few ounces of small shells, elbows, ditalini, or broken angel hair and add to the peas for a hearty vegan entree. Grated cheese is nice, too. Enjoy!

Made as the recipe was written, choosing cavatappi for the pasta, and everyone being served thought this was perfectly delicious.  Noting some disliked the use of mint in this, we all thought it was a great flavor accent along with parsley and scallion greens.  With so many recipes and so little time and calories to spare, not a lot of them get to the keep and repeat file but this one did.  The little bit of leftovers we had worked really well added to a frittata the next day.

Macaroni and peas is something many of us ate growing up (and still love). Usually elbow macaroni, tomatoes and peas (in my house it was canned peas).  It used to be cheap & filling.  This version is much fancier!
With a working mom, in my house it was Kraft macaroni & cheese with peas, tuna and mushrooms… almost like a cheater’s tuna casserole.

I’m convinced people think there’s not enough sauce because they aren’t adding an adequate amount of pasta water. More than saucy enough as written, just make sure to cook it that last 2 minutes. Tripled the amount of pepper, which is to say, I added red pepper flake with the pancetta, and about a tablespoon of freshly ground pepper with the onions and garlic. Next time, may finish with some lemon zest mixed with some toasted breadcrumbs and try it with some tortellini. Love this recipe!
My family loved this. I mixed peas and some fresh grilled sweet corn. I also omitted the mint and added a squeeze of lemon instead. Will make again for an easy weeknight dinner.

This was very good, and easy/quick enough for a family weeknight dinner. I doubled everything, but added even more peas and a little more garlic. I used pancetta and skipped the herbs bc I didn’t have them - didn’t miss them, but next time will try some lemon zest as recommended by another reviewer.

I made this according the to recipe, but I found that there wasn't much cheese taste to it.  We didn't finish it, but we saved the leftovers.  It made a pretty good pasta salad when eaten cold and with extra salt and pepper and oil though.  It just didn't taste like mac and cheese at all.

Overly complicated recipe for what should be a simple pasta. 

Of course, I did not return the pasta with the egg/cream/cheese sauce to the flame: one should never actually cook the cheese and/or eggs: they should be added to hot pasta off-flame, mixing them well with however much of the pasta water you need to create the silkiness as for a carbonara.
Any recommended substitutions for the ham/bacon? Although I love it, my parents' don't eat pork.

This is essentially overcomplicated carbonara. When I make carbonara I use diced sun dried tomatoes. Don't saute them, just add them in the final stage. You can also use the SDT oil when you saute the other ingredients, for extra flavor.
Since we I don’t care for ham I use tuna as a quick and easy protein substitute.
Someone above mentioned canned tuna. I'd also like it with deeply caramelized mushrooms.
You can easily make ham broth using a smoked ham hock cooked slowly with some aromatics
This is just a variation of carbonara with ham and peas. And just as tasty.

This recipe for macaroni and peas is amazing. I followed it exactly, changing nothing except instead of macaroni I used Romain lettuce and instead of the bacon and Parmesan cream sauce I used a ginger vinaigrette on top of the lettuce—came out flawless!  Hubby and kids gobbled it up, will def be making again!
I was so skeptical for the final call for parsley, the green part of the scallions, and especially the mint... but wow!!! What a beautiful surprise!
Delicious!

I used very good bacon, thick, fatty and Smokey. Had good Parmesan freshly grated. Had organic raw heavy cream, thick and silky. Fresh farm eggs recently hatched. Had the fresh herbs. I thought this would be really creamy. We thought it was very bland , uninteresting. If read so well but not my favorite by any measure
I really really wanted to love this recipe but, as written, it’s just meh. The flavors are there but muted. This dish needs more: pancetta, garlic, peas, mint. Go for it.

I used to make proper carbonara up in the hills above Fabriano in the Marche with young locals. They explained it was what the charcoal makers (carbonari) had to hand - a hard dried sausage and bird eggs and herbs. Never cream or onions. I’m still a purist - just get a good Italian sausage!
Relatively simple dish, tasty meal, but one pound of pasta is way too much for a serving of four. I think halving the amount would work better.
Found this recipe to sadly be very bland. It needed loads more salt and herbs. I added ground mustard powder for some extra flavor which helped a little bit.
Startlingly good.  Can’t wait for fresh peas in spring to do it again.

Easy to make and my husband enjoyed it. I did double the garlic, which I think helped add a bit of flavor, and also used about half the amount of pasta called for in the recipe (thank you, previous commenters!). Would make again, although I might not add the amount of mint and parsley called for next time, because I felt their herbiness ( is that even a word?) took away from the lovely peas.
Next time I'll do it without the mint

I haven’t crashed and burned on a recipe this badly in a while. 
As a guideline I stick to only 5 star reviews on NYT Cooking… but as a Kenji disciple and with a package of frozen pancetta in our freezer I was tired of looking at, I decided to ignore said guideline and give it a try. 
Like another poster this went into the garbage. Another comment cited the sauce splitting. From what I can tell the eggs in ours actually curdled. I am not sure what step cooked the eggs but the end product was just not good. I followed the recipe closely - including removing the pancetta pan from the heat and cooling the mixture with pasta water. Whenever it happened, something here went badly awry in our hands :( Worst fail I’ve had on a recipe on this site by a wide margin. Back to those guidelines.

This sounds tasty.  My Sicilian Grandfather's favorite was a can of canned peas (yes, really) added to a pan of fried chopped onion and garlic. Liquid and all.  A few shakes of red pepper flakes is my addition.  Let simmer a few minutes while the macaroni boils.  Add cooked macaroni to the pea mixture, let simmer a few minutes more, grated cheese on top.
Add salt and pepper, too.

A super star fill-up meal when time pinched, or just plain feeling lazy on a cold and snowy day here in Ottawa, Ontario. If stuck for ingredients, sour cream works, extra old cheese, and sliced capocollo given a 30 second blast in microwave. Frozen peas, fresh peas or sliced up green beans are good substitutes (tried lima beans once...not again). No fresh parsley? Don't fret....a sprinkle or two of dry oregano hits the spot.
When do you stir in the eggs and cheese with the pasta? Step 4.5? Seems like Kenji left out that step in the directions.

Edith or anyone else:  What is SDT oil? I got nowhere with a Google search.  Presumably ChatGPT is correct, it said:
"SDT cooking oil could refer to "Sunflower, Dalda, and Taj" cooking oil, which is a blend of sunflower oil, Dalda (a brand of vegetable ghee), and Taj oil (another brand of cooking oil). This blend might be popular in certain regions or marketed under this specific name by a particular company."
Sun dried tomato (mentioned earlier in same comment)
Sun Dried Tomato?

SDT oil = Sun-dried tomato oil. And I think the poster who suggested that because they think this recipe is "overly complicated" has really different tastes from mine. I'm really not a big fan of sun-dried tomatoes, and can't really imagine their tough, chewy, tomato skins in a carbonara, at least not with any pleasure. 

I like the sound of this recipe, particularly the fresh herbs. That's going to take it to the next level.
Am I the only one who thinks 1 lb of pasta for the amount of ingredients is way too much? This would have been perfect for me with 1/2 lb. It wasn’t bad, it was bland.

I don’t know if I did something wrong (I followed the recipe to a T!) but this was not good. A first for me and NYT cooking. Weirdly bland but then also just too saucy. Sadly, we threw most of it out.
For anyone in Australia or NZ, this also works great with light cooking cream rather than full fat cream. (Apologies, US cooks, i have no idea what the equivalent would be in the US).
For those of us allergic to egg, can the eggs be left out and cream increased?

I only changed 2 things. I used GF pasta and for herbs I used baby arugula. Very fresh market bacon made this delicious. My only complaint is the sauce didn't come together the way I would have liked. My instincts told me to add past and peas later but followed instructions. Regardless it was all eaten up.
I made half the recipe using chickpea pasta but it seemed like I should have made the full sauce recipe. It turned out a little bland. Also half the recipe was 4 servings for us.
Sauce separated in pan. More instructions needed if this is so finicky.
Skip parsley and mint; they don’t add anything. Scallions are good, but would add green and white parts BOTH during the garlic sauté phase
This was just OK. I think it would be better with pancetta. I had bacon and it didn’t add much to the dish.
I don’t know if it would work with this recipe, but my usual go-to sub for pancetta or ham is lox.
kinda boring.  The next night I added some pulled rotisserie chicken and a quick béchamel sauce and to the leftovers.  It made it better.
I didn't use egg and added a little arugula at the end and it was delicious
I expected my Mac and cheese loving son to gobble this up, but he turned up.hos nose.  No matter, it was delicious pasta, just not Mac and cheese.
We enjoyed this tonight.    Not enough peas for me.  Next time I will double the peas.
use butter instead of oil

Left out the cream and used a little bit of a modified béchamel with only one egg at the end-where is the egg in this recipe description anyway? – Lots of Parmesan, not as much as they recommend…Was delicious anyway! Cracked pepper!

I’ve been making a version of this for 40 years, this is a very good recipe and it really works. I did it exactly as written. It’s a great way to get your kids to eat peas, I’ll tell you that. 20 years ago, my kids wolfed it down coming home from school. I would recommend doubling the peas, they just work! Have plenty of extra grated Parmesan! Love this recipe!

This is pretty good , but I would add some salt and pepper, garlic and onion powder to the cheese sauce next time. The heavy cream is flavorless so it needs that boost. Also, I added dried pepper flakes for a kick!
Wife hates peas, substitute frozen succotash
My children just adored this recipe. It’s so exciting to have a big hit and get to add something new to the dinner rotation. Thank you!

I wanted a big creamy bowl of comfort food and this was perfect. I made one serving for myself, 56 g of pasta (yikes so tiny) but I halved the sauce and used a full cup of peas. I had a chicken sausage to use up so I subbed that for the bacon. Really, really yummy, I’ll revisit this! I think mushrooms would be a good add
I would put the stuffing on top. Because you're going to cook them crease down, right?
That sounds right to me!

This was a HIT with both the cook (me) and the various audiences headed home on long drives.  Came together in a flash (I used a baking soda recipe for the dough so it didn’t take very long) and the pocket design so much easier to pack up and send with them than bags or containers.  Wrapped in foil they stayed warm until the first rest stop. Beginning of tradition CONFIRMED.
I do this with flour tortillas or pita bread. Much easier and faster with no dough to roll out. Just be careful not to use too much liquid so that the wrap does not get soaked.
Maybe throw in some green bean casserole for color, or so you can pretend it's not just a carb on carb on carb roll.
@agrodolce hey I put cranberry sauce in!
My family has been making this for years. We call it a FowlZone. Can't go wrong
Love “FowlZone!” Will be applied.
Has anyone tried freezing these?

On Thanksgiving...for a midnight snack my 1st husband of 29 yrs. would do this, but with a dinner roll...the best part of Thanksgiving, ALWAYS!  Don't skip baking those dinner rolls, folks!  You will not regret it.

I got excellent results making these with a tube of store-bought crescent roll dough. Separate the dough into four rectangles and roll out to about 5 inches by 7 inches.  I lined my filling down the center of each, then pulled the sides and ends up and pinched together at the corners. This left a slight opening at the top which is covered with the slice of cheese.  They baked up beautifully and were delicious.

I make a giant empanada with Pillsbury refrigerated pie crust.  Unroll one crust on a greased cookie sheet or parchment paper.  On one haf layer some stuffing, mashed potatoes, diced turkey, any leftover veggies, and some gravy.  Fold the other half over and crimp the edges securely.  Brush the top with an egg wash and cut 6 slits to vent.  Bake at 425 degrees for about 25-30 minutes until golden brown,  Makes 2 nice size servings for dinner.

Really delicious. I made 8 ~a week ago, and tried an experiment - I took 2 out halfway through baking, and froze both those and 2 that had been cooked the whole time. We reheated all of them from frozen tonight. I tried a 350F oven so that the outside wouldn't get scorched before the inside was cooked. Both sets tasted just fine (with the half-cooked ones staying in the oven a bit longer to brown on the outside more - 25 vs 35 min), but I think the texture of the fully-cooked ones was better.
WINNER, WINNER, TURKEY DINNER!!!!  These were fun to make, fun to eat and a great way to use leftovers.  Fantastic!!!
I have a batch of No Knead bread dough started for tomorrow.  It makes very good pizza dough--easy to stretch thin.

Kids love making these and it’s a great activity for the family! I buy fresh pizza dough from my local organic market. Also have ricotta, shredded mozzarella, sun dried tomatoes and roasted red peppers for those who wanted more traditional turkey and cheese. My objective is to use up the turkey and stuffing.
Could you prep and then freeze these, unbaked?

I tried this with TJ pie dough (frozen), used a dough-cutting ring to make circles, filled and then crimped edges to make empanadas.  Varied filling:  turkey and cheese, turkey and mashed potatoes, turkey and cranberry sauce.  The empanadas were a bit delicate (flaky) but delicious.  Agree with so many here-- a creative way to use leftovers.

LOVE ME some Jim Gaffigan! Back to the recipe, I have an extra frozen pie crust. Is it too delicate to take on the Hot Pocket responsibilities? Is it sturdy enough to be the pocket? Because there's no way I'm going to the store today, unless I go in my PJ's...

What had brought pizza dough and left for turkey, sweet potatoes with marshmallows, green with French onions, fresh cranberries, gravy and cheddar/American shredded.  Way to rehear the Stromboli tomorrow.  Always a great on the leftovers.
Ooh, I may try this. I usually combine everything, add an egg, and make croquettes. But this looks equally tasty.
Can they be frozen?
This would be great with a batch of the dough for the Samosa Pie - like British meat pasties.

An engineering question: How does the stuffing/dressing absorb juices? It will be on top since it went down first but then you flip them over to bake them seam side down. (I see someone else addressed this issue.)
It works!!
These were fun to make, and Hot Pockets have a place in our family history, but I think I prefer leftovers cold and separate.
Works well with store bought burrito wrap and a lot less work. Use gravy to seal the wrap sides together.
I used a Pillsbury frozen pie crust. They are a delicious and easy post-Thanksgiving lunch.

These are fun. Making them turned out to be a family activity; quite good as completed and as, yes, leftovers of leftovers. They hold together surprisingly well for hand-held eating. Infinite variations are part of the fun. Get the dough as thin as possible or they will be bready on the bottom.

I made these tonight (Monday after Thanksgiving) and was amazed at how good they were, even after eating leftovers for three days. There’s something magical about putting them all together, wrapping them in pizza dough, sprinkling parm on top, and then baking until the dough is crunchy.  I’ve gone from dreading another plate of leftovers to being sad my leftovers are now gone.

After I strained the stock I made from the carcass, I ended up with a great deal of shredded turkey “broth meat” that still had flavor & moisture. Instead of putting it aside for cat food, I mixed in gravy, grated apple, leftover cranberry sauce, leftover braised red cabbage, some fat from the stock, &  poultry seasoning, and cooked it in these pockets. Probably should have made smaller ones, but they were a hit even though we had no stuffing or sweet potato to gussy them up. Win.
Made today with puff pastry wrapped around leftover organic spatchcocked, air dried chicken, stuffing, mashed potatoes and gravy. Scrumptious way to package up and serve leftovers. Delish.

Annoyed with the quantities of food grandchildren leave on their plates, and diplomatically buttoning your lip? If it’s stuff they actually like and are just not into it today, you can put it right back in their sweet like mouths the next day in these pockets. Plus condiments or ketchup dip.
Has anyone tried this with puff pastry or pie crust?

I have not tried with those but have tried with eggroll wrappers and they are AMAZING! Everyone loves left-over eggrolls with gravy dipping sauce! I imagine left-over hand pies(pie crust) or turn overs(puff pastry) would be just as amazing and just as big a hit with left-over consumers. The beauty of T-day left-overs is the myriad of ways people have for repurposing them. MMMMMMM! Enjoy!

TIP
You can also use canned pizza dough or thawed frozen puff pastry for this. Divide and roll the dough into 6 (7-by-6-inch) rectangles. When forming the pockets, fold one side over the other and crimp the edges with a fork to seal. Bake as directed.
I do this with puff pastry and call them Thanksgiving Turnovers!! YUM!

It's a turkey turnover! Great idea. 
Seeing a brand name referenced made me leery.

I did this with homemade rough puff last year and am excited to try with store bought fresh pizza dough, far easier. I made sure to layer the Turkey last, keeping in mind that you will crimp and seal and then turn the pockets seam side down to bake. If you think of the texture in the bite, it’s nice to have the Turkey on the bottom and the softer layers on top. Temper the urge to add gravy inside, there is already plenty of moisture. This is a fun little meat hand pie.

It's American Bubble and Squeak!  I'm married to a  Brit and our after-holiday feasts involve warming what we got in a skillet, cracking and egg or two and cooking over-easy, and then of course cheese is requisite.  Though I love the idea of Kenji's hot pockets, the practical part of me just can't fathom wrapping all those leftover carbs in a big sheet of more carbs!  Way too heavy I think....

In my experience it’s better if you choose which leftovers to include in a dish like this instead of  taking the kitchen sink approach.  Individual pies (or a whole one) using pie dough, turkey and/or ham, onions and whatever leftover veg you have, with gravy to moisten results in a dish that tastes delicious—- and intentional.  We serve the cranberries on the side and don’t add cheese on top, it detracts from a hand pie especially.

I wonder if this would work with Pillsbury crescent roll dough.  I think someone mentioned using pie crust to make a handheld pie/pasty.  I have leftover pie crust dough, so I’m going to try a terrine style pie and serve it with the leftover gravy.
I love this idea. Sometimes a put a hot dog and extra gravy in a pocket. I bake it at 350 for 25 minutes then I dip it in warm mash potatoes and finish it off with a pumpkin spiced beer. Brav
These tasted amazing but were far too fiddley to make. Leftovers are supposed to be easy!
We make “burritos” using flour tortillas with same ingredients.  Everyone’s favorite use of leftovers from Thanksgiving!  Cranberry chutney or relish, made to order for each individual.
I am already thinking of other applications. Great idea!

I did these as described, with pizza dough from TJs. I had a small Thanksgiving meal (1/2 breast roulade from Ina Garten's recipe, gravy from the juices, herby stuffing for two, canned whole cranberries, roasted green beans). I'm left with enough turkey and gravy for a couple sandwiches, enough cranberry sauce for muffins. No stuffing (maybe next time I'll double). This was SO easy; didn't worry about exact dough size/shape or precise amounts. Took about 30 min prep and 20 min bake.  Yum!
These were a hit on the day after Thanksgiving. Even though I followed the recipe, though, they were quite a bit larger than Chinese egg rolls.
This is delicious. But I recommend leveling this up and really stepping into the holiday season: Baste in Egg Nog & Gravy. This is the way.
These work really well. They're themselves actually even better the next day, in true leftover fashion. Great idea!

Made with fast bread flour-based pizza dough from my machine. Here's what was left over: stuffing with sausage and bacon, turkey, green beans, gravy, apricot cranberry sauce. Added rough cut parmesan cheese on top. Result was outstanding! Split the extra one with my wife for lunch today, after some air fry time, and it was still awesome.
This was so good the day after thanksgiving!  Super easy, flexible, and quick to make.  The « hot pocket » was a fun way to use up leftovers.

These turned out really nice.  I second what another reader mentioned - stuffing should go on top since you're flipping it seam down.   Also protip - if you don't want to make your own pizza dough, just buy it from your favorite local pizzeria.
Used a can of leftover pilsbury crescent roll dough for the pastry. Quick and tasty way to switch up the leftovers!
Made this for dinner last night - easy, delicious and the family loved it. What could be better? Great way to use Thanksgiving leftovers.

I am one of the minority who do not find Thanksgiving leftovers interesting so this is a great way to disguise them. They are also the perfect dinner for a rivalry game night! They came together during halftime and were ready before the 3rd quarter was over. My house divided (UF/FSU) agree this recipe must be a tradition.
This was so delicious! Mine turned out the size of a large burrito though.
This was delicious! I ended up turning them more into buns than hot pockets but definitely doing this again next year with leftovers. Slice of cheddar on top was a nice cheesy touch.
Recipe directions are easy to follow. I always have my homemade pizza dough on hand so it was a good use of leftovers and a fun project for the day after Thanksgiving.

These turned out great! I reversed the order of the layers since I turned them seam-side down to bake. The sweet potatoes took over the taste of the other ingredients a bit, so I may leave those out next time.  Yummy with gravy for dipping.
This was delicious! Excellent use of T-Day leftovers - this will go into our annual rotation.
Interestingly this was already done by Franky on his show Struggle Meals.
I tried this recipe with a pie shell instead of pizza dough and topped it with cheese.  It worked really well--came out more as a Thanksgiving pot pie.

This was an excellent way to use delicious thanksgiving leftovers. I basically made this as No Recipe Recipe. I love the versatility of being able to use whatever leftovers you have for the filling. I went with the puff pastry and I didn’t see the need to roll out the dough. I stretched it into shape and it was fine. I skipped the cheese because I didn’t think it was needed. I’m looking forward to making it again with next year’s leftovers!

As other have mentioned, switch the order for the stuffing. Leave stuffing for last (seam side down) so it can absorb the goods. Apart from that, great recipe and the crunchiness from the pizza dough like calzone here really adds something else. I did brush with olive oil and added a tad of salt to the top along with parmesan. Delicious.

Saved my crumbly lentil loaf (chia egg is inferior to flax egg) from food waste. I made two smaller hot pockets and one larger one whose dough was thicker--the thicker one was more aesthetically pleasing. Recommend reheating or even do final heating on a cast iron skillet to get the bottom nice and crunchy. That and lots of mashed potatoes make this super good and something my kids devoured. TJs whole cranberry sauce, sliced butternut, TJs mashed potatoes with cream cheese, lentil loaf--gorgeous
Very easy, look nice and keep well - an excellent destination for TG leftovers!

This was so popular I’ve used it as a go-to for any casserole or stew that is great the first two nights and boring on the third. Best ones have included potato, (like turning potato kugel into knishes)and including a tart condiment like cranberry sauce, chili sauce, or chutney punches it up. Freezer roll doughs are easy if you don’t have a good pizza dough source. Some chives in the dough don’t hurt.
thank you! I came here to look for exactly this--how to do this with leftover pizza dough @Louise
This is a mesmerizing video. The crunch when the hot pocket is cut in half is my favorite part. I’m in!

Yes! Made this tonight using my own sourdough discard pizza dough. Ate it like a French Dip with gravy on the side (and some in the pocket, of course). Might have been better than our original Thanksgiving meal.
An absolute hit! Genius way to serve (and keep) leftovers. My second batch is in the oven right now!

No steamer required.  Put a large piece of foil on a heatproof platter and oil lightly to prevent the fish from sticking. Season with aromatics you like, slash a couple time if it is a plump fish, and top with another piece of foil.  Crimp the edges well.  Cook at the top of a 450 F oven.  When done, top with your soy mixture of choice and a few Tbsp of hot sesame oil to make everything pop.

I did this one time with a 3lb striped bass; self proclaimed fish haters came back for more.
I've used this method too.  This works best with larger fish.
@Judith no need, but though both are delicious steamed tastes different than roasted.

This is a classic Cantonese dish.  The fish will look better if you slash parallel to the spine and place aromatics (ginger, scallions) inside the slash and the belly.  After steaming, drain the liquid (its fishy smelling); pile more ginger sticks and scallions on top of fish and slowly pour hot oil on the aromatics to infuse the aroma.  Place fresh cilantro over fish. Pour the soy mixture around the fish so that when the velvety chunks of fish is served, dip the chunks in the soy mixture.

Classic dish in every Cantonese household.  Draining the fish is key after steaming as the residual cooking liquid is quite fishy.  Always served with alot of white rice - the soy / oil dressing is much prized as a sauce for rice.
Depending who's eating with us, I don't drain the fish fluid.  My Filipino husband and in laws like it this way.

Wife is Chinese. This is her staple. Most often uses a silver pomfret c. 1lb cleaned, I'd guess. Would argue always worth scoring the flesh a couple of times, even with a smaller fish, and slipping a bit of the finely sliced aromatics in there. Also means you get a bit more of the flavour and crisping from the hot oil to finish.
Most important- start with a live healthy fish swimming in a tank from an asian seafood market. They will gut and scale for you.
Yes! So true.

Made exactly as written and it was fantastic (would expect nothing less from a Kenji recipe ;). A few tips for next time: double the amount of sauce and reduce sugar by half. I microwaved the sauce for 20 seconds to help the sugar dissolve more easily. Don’t skip the hot oil step- though I used a spoon to spoon it over so as not to make a mess.

This was one of my grandmother's specialities.  I make this dish at least twice a month.  To test for doneness, I use a chopstick near the meatiest part of the fish and it's done when it goes through.  For a little extra pizzazz, I like to fry some oil and ginger until crispy and put it on top before heating the oil to pour over the fish.  Also I like a little splash of rice vinegar in the sauce for a bit more contrast.  *French chefs kiss* The little pieces of meat near the bone are the best.

Lots of folks (including chef Kenji) instructing disposal of the residual cooking liquid, some saying it's too fishy.  I humbly disagree.  My parents, who were restauranteurs, combined the liquid into the soy/wine/sugar mixure.  Saute finely chopped scallion whites and ginger in vegetable oil until aromatic.  Add the soy sauce/sugar and wine mixture and fish liquid, stir, to a simmer.  Thicken with corn starch slurry. Adjust saltiness.  Pour over fish. Makes a rich, savory sauce.

I agree with Judith.  No steamer required.  An American rendition from my Cantonese mother’s fish.  I use a 500 degree oven for 10-15 minutes with just ginger, soy, sesame oil and scallions in foil that is crimped air right with half of a Costco salmon fillet.   It’ll puff out dramatically and cut open with scissors. Add cilantro if you have it. The easiest dinner and kids love it.

I made this using parchment paper instead of the foil approach described in another comment, using small trout caught by a friend. Two small trout per parchment, laid on the bed of greens and I inserted some into a pocket in each fish. I stapled the parchment closed, and placed them on a cookie sheet. Baked at 400F for 10 to 12 minutes.  Once baked, I topped as directed and used the soy topping which added good flavor, and I used hot chili oil instead of making it as directed. Very good flavor!
Where do I buy a tangled bird's nest to toss the aromatics into? Is this some kind of technical term?
The tangled Birdsnest refers to all of the herbs and scallions you have rinsed in the salad spinner

I am not a cook...just like to eat.  However this is something I grew up with.  You can also put the fish on a microwaveable plate, top with the seasoning, cover with saran wrap and microwave for a few minutes.  test for doneness and pour the soy sauce and oil on top and finish microwaving for another 45 seconds.  It is just as good as steaming.
This is the way my Cantonese in-laws do it.  It's wonderful and quick.
This looks like fun but how do you serve it once it is on the table?   Do you cut off head and tail and then slice through the whole fish?   Or do you leave spine on the serving dish?  Any tips?

Lil traditionally serve whole as shown. Use serving spoons to gently scoop flesh and toppings from one side, then either lift the head, spine & tail off to uncover the other half or flip the whole fish (with 2 utensils). Depending on species the bones will come free more or less easily and intact (when I was a kid often got carp which always had loose bones). If you or guests don't want to do at table then debone before serving--that way you get to keep the scrumptious bits behind the head.

@Lil
Before cooking, make a slit on either side of the spine the length of the fish. After cooking, use spoon or spatula and slide under meat perpendicular to the spine. After top meat removed, use same tool to lift spine and ribs up and away from bottom half.

I don’t have great knife skills so prepping the aromatics took a lot of time. The final taste was excellent but next time I would make twice the amount of sauce. Also, next time, I will try the aluminum foil packet method as described in the previous comments because I could then accommodate any sized fish.

Delicious dish.  Good call, Judith, on cooking the fish at a high temp in foil.  It was almost equivalent to steaming, with plenty of flavor.  I used trout but will try branzino next time. Thanks all for your helpful comments.
We cooked this in a fish poacher with marbles under the tray. Amazing dish.
Can we perhaps get a video with this one? I feel rather daunted by the number of steps in this one, delicious though it seems.
Double the sauce. Or, if you're cooking closer to 2.5lbs of fish, triple it and leave some on the side. You won't regret it!!

I found this easy to make and delicious. I used a whole branzino, about 1.75 lb. It was a real hit.

A few changes I made to suit my kitchen:
- If, like me, you do not have a steamer, cook it en papillote, or in foil, at 425F. Comes out beautifully.
- I would recommend making twice the sauce, as the amount suggested here felt short. 

One note for the editors: I think this recipe could be improved by arranging it differently. It’s quite a simple dish, because you’re just making a salad and saving the waste to stuff the fish with, but the steps make it seem complex.

I went with wrapping the fish in parchment and baking (10 minutes per inch). Made with local steelhead filets - totally divine. Subbed white white vinegar for shaoxing, didn’t have ginger either. Still incredible, and quick.

I never use plastic Wrap in the microwave. It can melt onto the food or leave undesirable deposits on the food. 
I cover the food with waxed paper or a glass/ceramic plate or cover.

Lil -- Responding to your question about how to serve the fish.  If you are just eating with your immediate family, use a spoon to scoop pieces of fish for the kids.  The adults can use chopsticks to pick the meat off the bones.  Once the top of the fish is eaten, use a knife to break the spine of the fish at the neck.  Remove the head.  Carefully lift the entire spine and tail from the flesh.  Save the head, spine and tail for stock!  Now the other half of the fish is easily accessible!
Pickerel/Walleye are great in this dish. The sweetness of their flesh pairs superbly with the soy/ginger mixture.
I prefer poaching the whole fish in simmering water with a little salt and slices of ginger. This reduces any gaminess with dilution in the water bath.
I have made this with chopped scallions and chopped fermented black beans and it is delicious.

Adding to Judith’s note about using a foil packet in the oven, I have made this on a charcoal grill while camping 

Best, then, to serve smaller, individual packets with a small deboned, butterflied trout for each guest

The sizzling oil over the scallion, ginger and chili makes a delicious noseful!  A bit of roasted sesame oil   makes perfect sense!

I have done this too.  I have used a grill and wrapped in foil and lay on hot coals.  Depending on the fish and the size, this should take anywhere from 5 to 10 minutes.  Check when you hear sizzling.

This looks delicious, and there are many good suggestions from commenters.  I particularly appreciate that this dish is relatively low in sodium, compared to many Asian recipes in the NYT food section.
Leave out the Sugar and Shaoxing wine. Put 1 tbsp of Mirin instead.

Enjoyed the dish, but my 'memo to self' for next time is: it's critical to keep everything hot by the time it gets to the table. Well warmed serving platter and hot plates are essential here. I'd even consider warming up that soy sauce blend you pour over. Yes, the hot oil helps, but it quickly dissipates. 
I used Pomfret from Chinatown (2 fish for 4 people) - very affordable and delicious. Big bones that stay on the spine too, so nothing to catch anyone or, spoil the joy!

So I took a look at this recipe and said no way I’m doing all that work for lunch by myself. I filled my wok halfway up with water, added aromatics, ginger, cilantro and scallions, and steamed a big fresh tilapia. Used the sauce in the recipe. Had with rice and fresh green beans. 

Simple, delicate and delicious.

Directions are a bit wonky
Double or triple the sauce 
Cut up the grains otherwise they’re hanging out of your mouth like a goat 
Steam time was perfect for a whole trout
A video would be super helpful here, including how to portion and serve. Thank you, looks delicious.
YouTube has many videos of this dish by celebrated chefs Ken Hom, Adam Liaw, etc

As Pacific Northwesterner, I made this with salmon filets, whole steelhead, and tail ends of halibut.
Kenji's version is fancy for a party.  When I pour on the hot oil I put the plate into the sink to save the mess and danger.  A little less dramatic but cleaner.
I have this for decades in my microwave. I thought I invented it LOL
My partner loves this and said we should definitely make it again. I only had a couple of scallions and no cilantro so I can only imagine how good it would be with those
Best dish ever! I made it very often. The instructions are easy to follow and the outcome is great.
Make sure you serve with plenty of steamed white rice. Been eating this classic Cantonese staple for decades.

Having been brought up in Hong Kong on many a  steamed fish, I suggest trying a microwave to steam this recipe. You’ll need to watch it closely to monitor the heating level, but you’ll get that smooth silkiness and it’s cooked in minutes. Start cooking at power level 3 and slowly adjust upwards to a finishing few seconds at 8 or so. The veggies will stay perky but not raw. You’ll also avoid the bother of Jerry-rigging a suitable whole-fish-sized steamer because the microwave is, in fact, a steamer. Just use your serving dish of choice to lay the fish on its veggie and condiment nest, cover with more artfully arranged nesting, add a dash of sherry, and put all in the microwave—no cover needed. Afterward the whole cooked shebang can go right to the table. Ingredient freshness is Everything in Cantonese cooking, so take a generous bag of ice to the fish store to carry your cleaned fish home, then keep the fish on ice in your fridge until cooking it that same day.

This looks intriguing, and I'm really wanting to increase the variety and styles of fish we eat. A couple of newb questions though (bear with me pls): A: assume a whole rounded fish like a trout, does one cook whole "as is", or are we cleaning the guts out? If the latter, how to? 
B: recipe mentions de-scaling. Is this species dependent, or a rule of thumb for all? 
C: Portioning - 1 fish per person? A big fish for all? Do we all (4 of us) just pick at it family style? 
Thanks!

When you buy the fish, they will usually clean out the guts and descale for you. Yes, by all means clean out the fish well guts and scales.
Asians usually have a couple of main courses at dinner so 1.5 lb whole fish is OK for 4 people plus a vegetable dish and of course rice.
I’m am thinking buying whole fish at our Asian market here in NC would be fine (no tank fish) but if not I’m sure someone will tell me. You can not find whole fish in Durham except at Asian markets
You can steam fish fillet as well. I steam the black cod fillet or Chilean sea bass found at Costco.
I subsumed the fish for cod, the....?breakdown?....tater tots. Enjoy.

I bought a 2 lb striped bass and slashed as indicated.  However, it was nowhere near done after twelve minutes.  Steaming a whole, thick fish is always going to take longer.  It was done after about 20 minutes.  Otherwise, the dish was good.  I will try the foil baking method as the steaming set-up was a pain and not quick.

Two questions:  1) My grocer washes all herbs before selling -- why  put the aromatics in spinner with water? Can't I cut them and add without this step?
2) Where is Judith's note about using wrapping in foil in oven? Would like to see her instructions.
I add a splash of fish sauce to the sauce, and limes to serve. I realize this makes it more Thai than Cantonese.. but fish sauce is just so good.
This is really really good. I used sesame oil instead of canola and it works!
No wok, no problem. I use my Val shaped Dutch oven.
Regarding how to serve a whole fish- this is the way I do it (but usually leave the head and tail on) https://youtu.be/rhoIOrf06-Q?feature=shared

Great recipe inspiring some changes; I used 1" thick ahi tuna steaks which I first marinated in dry sherry, soy sauce, ginger, garlic, chile bean paste and sesame oil, 40 minutes or so. I steamed the fish for 8 minutes and they were tender and moist with rare centers. I used thinly sliced jalapeños in lieu of Thai chilies (all I had on hand). Delicious! Served over lime-cilantro rice.

Very popular Cantonese dish, I have made it often. Instead of steaming I bake the fish in the oven. In an oven proof casserole dish, I place some cleaned leafy coriander stems, some julienned scallions & ginger. Next place the fish on top of the herbs, sprinkle few drops of water, cover with the lid & bake for about twenty minutes. When ready, place more julienned scallions & coriander on top of the fish, sprinkle with caster sugar & pour over soy sauce & the hot oil. Sizzling & aromatic.

I tried out my new convection steam oven and it came out great with the setting on Steam @ 210F, for about 9-10 minutes. I tried an Orata whole fish, about 1 lb, and some spicy chili crisps oil (as another commenter I think suggested) to substitute for the chili flakes and hot oil, and it was out-of-this-world good. Should have done 2 whole fish!
Really nice. Light and delicate. Keeping this recipe forever.
very tasty but perhaps the most confusingly written recipe on here.
for quicker easier recipe use a fish fillet for simpler procedure rather than whole fish and no bones.
If you don’t have a steamer, parchment paper and a microwave is the easiest way to go. Fish in papillote.

This is a recipe we repeat often. My two young girls can't get enough. I always make extra eggs because they just gobble them up. The grownups like to top with Sriracha mayo to spice it up. Quick, easy, nutritious and things I generally keep stocked.

Halved the recipe, for two. Will certainly cook again, and certainly cook more egg. No need to add sugar in the chicken, as mirin is already sweet. We didnt have ginger pickle, so i pickled thinly sliced cucumber, grated carrot and thinly sliced (lengthways) spring onions in 1 tsp white sugar and 1.5 tbs vinegar for 30 mins, then drained, to top the dish. Worked very well.
I would be inclined not to rinse the pan either time it is suggested here.

Everyone really enjoyed this meal. I will definitely be making it again. 

I couldn't find shoyu so I instead diluted low sodium soy sauce. I also made some fresh pickles with cucumbers, green onions, rice vinegar, sugar and salt. Highly recommend adding that.

Terrific, a fun-to-plate crowd pleaser! I did peas for the kids, spinach for the adults. Didn’t have shoyu but tamari worked great. The liquid didn’t evaporate (and I hadn’t even added the water) but that was no big deal - the chicken was still delicious. Going to use the leftovers in a lettuce cup today. The pickled ginger was such a yummy finishing touch!
How do you keep the cooked foods hot while cooking the remaining foods?
I cheated and used store bought kung pao sauce on the chicken which makes the dish even easier and still very tasty.

This was a hit! I used green beans sliced for our veg, and subbed in coconut aminos since we can’t do shoyu. It took me longer than the posted time but I can see plenty of ways to speed up the process and plan to put this in our regular rotation.

Made this with mostly ground beef and some crumbled tofu to finish up a block in my fridge. Next time I'll do all tofu or buy ground chicken bc the beef flavor wasn't my favorite here. Didn't have sake on hand so instead I added one extra T of mirin (didn't add sugar) and threw in just a tiny dash of rice wine vinegar. Quick pickled some cabbage before I started cooking to be my pickled topping. Delicious, simple and filling! Adding sake to the shopping list for when I make it again
Any ideas what can sub for ginger?  Seems like a great recipe that needs some sort of pickle to cut the richness but we don't like ginger.
I subbed with pickled radish and it fit in great!

An excellent video is available here: https://youtu.be/ShD9Hf4tRKc?si=WqBPh6kOJstb3cQ- When I brought those "bunny apple slices" with my bento box to my university in NY when I was a student there, they were an instant hit. Bunny-shaped apples are hugely popular among kids in Japan.
Thank you for giving me a lunch inspiration. I’ve been in a rut. I also just made the white chicken chili for the same reason.
Will be adding this to the rotation for sure. Easy to put together and was a hit with the whole family.
Excellent! Great flavors, nutritious, and flexible. Easy to involve kids, and 10 out of 10 rating from my 8 year old. She has requested this in her lunches as well.
My 14yo proclaimed this “delicious!” It was an easy weeknight dinner, and the adults used Asian condiments to add heat and other flavors -- rice vinegar, chili oil, furikake, etc. — to taste.
We thought this was great. And it actually DOES take only minutes, unlike most NYT recipes which seem to assume you have a sous chef doing all your mise en place!

This is great as written, but feel free to use it as a base for other toppings, additions, and alterations. Try doing some air fried green beans with an asian flavored sauce. Have some leftover pickled onion? Sure! Did you make Hetty Lui McKinnon's Hoisin-Tahini Sauce earlier in the week and have some leftover? Throw it on there! Make it yours and it can even be different every time.
Delicious and easy! Don't skip on the pickled veg - I couldn't find red pickled ginger but found pickled radish and it worked very well.
Simple and nutritious.
I whipped this up tonight with miso soup.  I used kale for my greens.  More eggs next time, I think. The toppings are the best part! I'm having it for lunch tomorrow, too.

This is a fantastic convenient recipe for busy folks. It’s easy to shop for, quick to cook, easy to double and reheats well. I doubled the recipe and my husband and I ate it for 4 days. 

To double, just do the turkey in two batches. No need to batch the eggs, they can all be cooked at once. Ditto for the veggies.

I also omitted the additional sugar and it came out plenty sweet. For reference, I use Ohsawa mirin which has 3.5g of sugar per tablespoon.

I did not have a large nonstick or carbon steel pan, so I used my 12-inch stainless steel skillet for the meat (not the eggs). With a small amount of oil the meat does not stick, even with all the liquid. 

The process is easy, just add a small amount of oil to the pan, add the meat and mix in the other ingredients. The oil will disappear into the meat mix and there will be meat stuck to the pan, this is ok. Just cook over a medium flame without disturbing and give it some time to cook at the bottom. Then use a thin metal spatula to get under the layer of cooked meat to lift it off the pan. This will be around halfway through the cooking time. The meat should come off easily and might even have some browning. Mix and finish cooking. You’ll notice that the oil which was embedded in the meat will eventually find its way to the bottom of the pan and prevent it from sticking there.

Perfect easy bento meal and quite light. I thought the sweetness was well-balanced, but it would be easy to take out the granulated sugar in the meat mixture and I think it would still be great. I used sugar snap peas for the greens and they were delicious. In the future I could see just make edamame from frozen since I usually have some of those on hand. It looks so cute in a bento box!

Wonderfully flexible recipe. We've done it with scrambled eggs as here and with poached. We've added caramelized red onion, used chicken, used plant-based meat. We cooked up snap peas with jamon and added Serrano slices. We've served it on roasted potato. This is just a wonderful starting point for creativity and it's a fine recipe in its own right.

title: Japanese Potato Salad With Mentaiko Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

comments.commentBody: Suitable substitute for mentaiko?
Try caviar.

Russet potatoes are not available here in Japan — so for a more truly authentic version of the dish, 男爵薯 (danshakuimo; also known as Irish Cobbler) would be the potato to use. Some local variations: Supermarket potato salads usually have slices of ham and occasionally iceberg lettuce mixed in — but the truly great potato salads nix the carrots/corn for chunks of smoky bacon and jammy eggs.
What's a "jammy egg?" Same as soft-boiled? Planning to make Japanese potato salad these weekend for a party. Unfortunately, cannot use roe or ham as my daughter doesn't eat fish or meat.

@Lisl a "jammy egg" is a cooked egg that is in between soft boiled and hard boiled. try to imagine the consistency of jam, in between juice and jello. the yolk shouldn't run but it won't be as pale and dry as a fully hard boiled egg. while typing this i realized a picture is worth a thousand words as they say, so here's one! https://minimalistbaker.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/01/Jammy-Eggs-11.jpg
@HND and another name for Russet potatoes in Japan would be -メクイン, which I use to make mashed potatoes. 男爵 are not equivalent.

I made this with baby Yukons cut in half, took the eggs out sooner, and couldn’t find the mentaiko so I simply left it out. I really liked all the veg mixed in!  
I might put a dash of fish sauce in the leftovers to make up for the lack of fishy flavor.
How do I use the Mentaiko? Do I take it out of the sac?  Do I cook it first?  I looked it up and there are many ways to use it.
Yes, you would take it out of the sac, scraping it to get as much as possible. Like removing sausage meat from the casing.
I can't wait to add mentaiko to my next batch of Japanese potato salad! However, I always salt my carrot slices along with the cucumber; it takes some of the raw crunch out of them and adds flavor.
When salting the veggies, leave them set for a couple of hours for the salt to absorb.  Be sure to drain off the very salty liquid produced.
Do you need to chop the mentaiko, or prep it some other way?  Or simply mix it in?

This reminds me of my mother-in-law's usual herring potato salad at Christmas, which here in South Africa occurs in summer.
So it is a basic potato salad with hardboiled eggs AND mixed with small pieces of roll mops, cubes of gherkins, tart granny smith apples, cubes of cooked beetroot, cubes of cheddar, and gently mixed with some home-made mayonnaise. I like to add caraway seed and some white pepper.
Save an unnecessary step: Mix mayo and rice vinegar and sugar all together then pour over potato, egg, cuke etc mixture. And I used a few drops of fish sauce instead of roe because really.

Consider salt-cured anchovies as a substitute for mentaiko.  I blended 4 of them into the mayonnaise along with some gochujang and fresh tarragon.  Adding hard-boiled eggs as recommended will change the flavor by smoothing out the edge (and complexity) of the dressing.  You may find this either positive or negative, but taste the salad before you add the eggs.

I’m a bit disappointed. This tasted nothing like the potato salad that I’ve had in Japanese restaurants. It was way too sweet and needed a lot more vinegar, which made it runny. And to the person who thinks MSG syndrome  is false: I get a blinding migraine, my hands blow up, heart races, and find it hard to breathe. I worked in Asia, and this is actually a real thing for some.
Won't be trying this recipe with metaiko or tarako because it seems you can't buy them online and there are no Japanese stores within 200 miles of my house. What would be a suitable substitute?
Amazon has it, if that helps.
Don't forget to take the mentaiko out of it's sac before mixing it in!
Would Bonito flakes be a good substitute for the mentaiko?
Kenji is such an OG. I love everything this guy puts out.

Mentaiko is a little or a lot spicy depending on the brand. Tarako is the non-spicy version. Kenji says to add it raw, you would scrap it out of the sack. And add it to your dish. Or you could grill it in the sack. Lay it on some foil and add sesame oil or olive oil on top and grill it until cooked. Then just slice it as is and add to the dish. We often eat this as a topping for rice or all mixed up in a pasta sauce. Yum!

I live in SF - there are several Japanese grocery stores - I got 2 sacs for $6.00. Frozen. The other one is in the freezer. It is very easy to use - you just scrape the mentaiko into the bowl. Made as directed with ingredients listed - it was a huge hit, disappeared in about 10 minutes.  Thank you Kenji!
I’ve used 2/3 rice vinegar & 1/3 Kewpie Mayo in potato salad for decades after living in Japan. I makes a fantastic lighter dressing for cole slaw too b
Made it without corn or radishes. Nice! Would use less sugar and more rice wine vinegar
I consider a jammy egg to be similar in texture to the one you get in a good bowl of ramen--on the hard side of soft-boiled, with the white fully cooked and the yolk thick and syrupy in texture.
what can you substitute if you don't want to use sac mentaiko or tarako?
Wonderfully japan...

I wonder if bottarga, dried mullet roe, might be a suitable replacement for the mentaiko (I keep some in my freezer for spaghetti bottarga).  Adrienne’s suggestion of fish sauce also sounds like it would add that fishy hit.
I wouldn't use fish sauce. Bottarga tastes pretty different IMO, it's not as strong a flavor as mentaiko, but it would still be good so worth trying out!

For me, bottarga is far too delicate for a dish like this. It would get lost. Given how expensive it is I save it for dishes where it is the dominant taste ingredient - such as pasta as you mention. 

But this perspective all depends on your budget. If it’s not a big deal for you, give it a try!  Why not?  That’s what is so much fun about cooking.

It would be "very" helpful to know how to use the mentaiko.  I never heard of it until this recipe.  Is it raw when you buy it?  Do you cook it first?  I don't eat raw fish, so I would like some clarification on this.  The recipe sounds very good, and I think the fish would add flavor, but it would have to be cooked for me.

I typically dress potatoes when they are slightly warm but I note this recipe calls for dressing them when they are cold. J.KLA tends to consider all options before offering guidance; does anyone have a good explanation for this strategy?

Delicious.
