I did riff slightly... increased quantities (used 5 potatoes), and increased veggie ratio, using a higher percentage of cucumber, radish, scallion, and especially carrots, for which I used the rainbow variety -- so pretty! I didn't have the mentaiko, so I used some skipjack tuna flakes I had on hand along with Trader Joe's gochujang sauce. I'd say it came out with just the right balance of umami and bite.

I needed to increase the rice vinegar to get the zippy flavor in the potatoes. My vinegar says ‘mild’ so maybe that’s why???  

Interested to see if anyone’s tried this with canned fish (tuna, salmon, mackerel). Hard to get the roe in my area (Sacramento, CA, USA). Gonna try tins of tuna today.
Regarding mentaiko, split the sac with a knife, scoop out the eggs, and then take the back of the knife and scrape the eggs still stuck to the sac.
I had a version of this in at an Izakaya in Japan. It was served in a suribachi and had potato chips which was excellent addition.

My new favorite potato salad 
Used the red potatoes I had on hand. I omitted fish but used some furikake for a little seaweed flavor 

Very good!

This is good and was a hit at the bbqs I took it too. I didn't have Kewpie at the time so used Hellmans. Did not add the fish roe. Sprinkled a little shichimi togarashi on top at the end. Def the best mayo based potato salad you will have!
Creamy, crunchy, comforting with tangy/sweet/umami/fresh flavors. Delicious! I used about a tablespoon of fish sauce in the dressing in place of mentaiko (I made roughly a double batch).
@Magpie You put the vinegar and sugar on the potatoes while they're hot.

While the dish sounds delicious, it is a problem for diabetics in that it combines white potatoes, carrots, and corn--three vegetables with a high glycemic index. I suspect most, like me, would know that, but folks preparing dishes to be served to known or unknown diabetics might not be aware.

Hi Kit, white potatoes that have been cooked and then cooled are actually lower to medium glycemic index (around 49) due to the resistant starch that forms with cooling. Corn and carrots are also not high glycemic index - raw sweet corn has a glycemic index rating of around 52-55, and raw carrots have a glycemic index of around 16. Anything below 55 is considered low glycemic index - high glycemic would be a score of 70 or above. Sincerely, a Registered Dietitian.
Where can you buy mentaiko? Any other substitutes?

Mia talked about smoked salmon, tinned mackerel and salmon roe in her comments. I think those would work well.  If you're looking for the salty kick, the salmon roe might be best but I'm going to try it with smoked salmon to see how it goes!
mentaiko looks to be very expensive. Fish Sauce seems a good substitute.

If anyone lives in Spain, you can find selected roe-type products for a few Euros in the supermarket, like at Mercadona with this Caviartic. Not the same, but gets you there for cheap with this great recipe. https://es.openfoodfacts.org/producto/8435129718062/caviartic-sucedaneo-de-caviar-negro-ubago
They're absolutely nothing like each other.

The Kewpie mayo has a distinct flavor, definitely try to get some for this and other recipes. If, like me, you’re sensitive to MSG they do make a version without it. I’ve found it in international sections of larger supermarkets. The few times I’ve bought Kewpie mayo online it contained MSG, a lot of it.

MSG is found naturally in Umami-heavy foods… this “Chinese restaurant syndrome” has been discounted by mainstream scientists as more of a myth than reality. 

If you go to Chengdu, and get some Hot Pot, you will often find MSG included as a table condiment. Go for it.
I made it without the Mentaiko, but it was delicious. Can't wait to try it with.
I made this with Yukon golds and wild coho salmon roe- delicious!
Excellent even without the mentaiko!
Asking out of ignorance but could fish sauce be used in plce of mentaiko?
Yea Kenji. Love this.  Looking forward to more of your recipies.

Great recipe - granted I’m not someone who follows recipes exactly, but rather uses them to inspire new ways of using what I have on hand. Thus, omitted the fish roe, though I’d love to try that sometime. More or less made the rest as written, just used reg cucumber and yellow potatoes. Genius idea to include cucumber and carrot in a potato/egg salad, and it had never occurred to me to quarter then slice these 2 veggies - so I appreciated learning this (albeit - elementary) move for my mise en place!
Procuring ingredients for the weekend: mentaiko is often sold spicy...? Should I hunt for not-spicy? Thanks!
Kind of similar to the Russian “olivier” salad. For tanginess without the fish, add apples and cilantro to the Japanese version. Gives it a nice mix of salty, sweet, herby and creamy flavors.

Very good! I've been to Japan and the original recipe seems very faithful. I thought "that's not enough potatoes" until I saw it with all the vegetables added. I also thought it would be too sweet (Japanese style) so I only added half a teaspoon sugar (half). My husband, who has been to Japan more than I have, wanted me to add a pickly flavor, so we added some pickle juice. And he hates pickles! Anyway, a great recipe and if you riff on it a little bit I don't think you're going to make it any less Japanese.

title: Whipped Cream Cheese With Mentaiko Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

comments.commentBody: This is the umami spread of my dreams! Thank you Kenji Lopez-Alt for this recipe!
Would tarama (a Greek take on salted fish roe) be a reasonable substitute here?  Thanks.

Probably, but I'm no expert.  According to the blog, Real Greek Recipes, "Tarama is the cured eggs of carp fish, grey mullet, or cod. In Greece, we have two types of Tarama. The one we call the White one, which is, in fact, a yellowish one, and the Pink one. Authentic Tarama is originally white (yellowish), but the pink color is added to it only to make it look more eye-catching. The flavor in both types is almost exactly the same (the white one being a touch stronger)."
Does anyone know why the ratings are relatively low and/or have any field notes? It's possible people just aren't into the flavor, and that's fine. Thanks!

Debbie, Tarama sounds delicious (i really love it). Having tasted both, i think it works just as well. Surely its not the same, of course, but the delicious combo stands and works for the same principles!

Let the mentaiko speak for itself - this recipe contains a lot of strong additional flavours in my opinion  - mentaiko has it's own particular special and lovely flavour - olive oil, lemon zest and especially the lemon juice cloud the flavour palette unnecessarily - as does the crème fraiche - I would use a nice normal heavy cream instead - the shiso leaves are of course a wonderful addition - keeping it simple here makes a huge difference :)

@bb I agree with your general point, but I’ll add that this recipe is the creature of a particular time, a place, and a very particular intent. Like Kenji explains, it traces its origins to wafu spaghetti, a type of pasta dishes that emerged in Tokyo in the 1960s as a “Japanese take” (wa fu) on Western (mostly Italian). The chef that popularized mentaiko spaghetti specifically used olive oil and butter, two pretty unctuous and dominant flavors, in it. It was also seen as a comfort food, not unlike Chinese-American on our side of the orb. 

Simplicity and purity of flavor is as good a culinary lodestar as any, but this wasn’t meant to highlight the subtleties of mentaiko. I don’t mean to downplay your recommendations! They sound like they’d make a better showcase of the ingredient, so we’re better off for having them. But this specific mentaiko recipe has a different and very specific aim —homaging a 1960s-Japan take on Western— and so it has to resort to evocatively bold flavors.

To clarify the tip's incomplete clarification, tarako and mentaiko turn out to be forms of roe from Alaskan pollock, a type of cod. So where these are unavaible, other fish roes could perhaps serve as substitutes.
Despite being in a Japanese American family, no one is familiar with Mentaiko. A google search reveals it to be very expensive. Anyone know affordable quality options?

I wonder whether bottarga would work. It's also kind of expensive (but a little goes a long way). You can get it in Italian specialty shops. Here in New York, Buonitalia always has it in the refrigerator. It keeps forever so you can buy some, use a little and save the rest for another time.
@Helen, the fish roe is what is expensive, probably because of availability. You may be able to substitute other types of fish roe.that is more affordable.
Online purchase of the roe is expensive.  Try a local Asian supermarket instead.   We have moderately priced here at an H-Mart in Mesa, Arizona.   Hence, it's possible.

I’ve never seen miso used for this dish before. Most recipes in Japan call for either soy sauce or mentsuyu for umami. I would also scale way back on the lemon — just a tiny drizzle at the end should suffice.

I regularly make mentaiko pasta and have found cream in any form to be actually pretty unnecessary (akin to how cream is unnecessary in carbonara). Instead I use butter to give it the richer texture that lets the mentaiko's flavor shine more instead of drowning it in cream or creme fraiche or god-forbid sour cream. Agree with the poster that said they haven't seen miso in this dish before. Seems like this is a completely different dish at this point. Probably tasty, but not really mentaiko pasta
This recipe has way too many ingredients for a mentaiko pasta…  and unnecessarily complicated. Let the mentaiko speak for itself.

I wouldn't use bottarga in place of mentaiko. They are not the same.  Mentaiko is fresh and bottarga is dried.  You can find spicy cod roe in your local H-Mart Korean market.  You can also find it in the frozen section of H-Mart if you can't find it in the fresh section. I would also skip the crème fraîche, olive oil, egg yolk, miso and lemon juice/zest.  I recommend using mentsuyu and butter.

There is almost never any need to reserve pasta water and then drain the pasta. Just transfer the spaghetti with tongs or a pasta spoon directly from the water into the sauce. Your pasta water remains in the pot, hot and ready to use. Here in Italy, colanders gather dust.

Despite some reviews claiming this recipe to be more complex than it should be - I think the acidity and the umami adds noticeable but not overwhelming nuance to the dish. As someone who had been cooking the super simple version of mentaiko pasta - I think this is the best version possible. Noted here though I scaled back on the miso (didn’t like the miso I have on hand) and used white vinegar (just a little bit) since I didn’t have lemon.
To Helen—Tarako is cheaper than mentaiko and has a similar umami-ness. You could get about 5 oz (145 gr) of mentaiko for about $16, vs $11 for tarako.
I'm very happy to see a mentai pasta recipe here. Mentai pasta & mentai fried rice have been part of lives for years. Thank you!

It will be interesting to try the readers' suggestions for omitting lemon, cream, etc., but I found this recipe to be absolutely delicious using parsley, except you need to double the sauce.  The flavor of Mentaiko is special, but I plan to experiment with other more available roes like salmon and tobiko in this same basic recipe.  The sauce provides a nice counterbalance to very briny flavors.
recipe should get a medal  and JKL-A should be in every Hall of Fame.

There are shelf-stable varieties of these sauces (both tarako and mentaiko) that are imported from Japan.  I can find them easily at Japanese markets where I live, but you could probably also order them online.  Obviously this isn't going to taste the same as if you were to make the sauce with fresh/frozen tarako or mentaiko, but it is a great cheap, fast substitute.  I personally love them and I'm not ashamed to admit it.  You could dress them up Kenji style, too.
How does mentaiko compare to bottarga?
Can you please describe mentaiko and tarok? I'd like to know before I eat it.
They are fish roe. These fish eggs are tiny, and have a salty snap when the egg bubbles snap. They also have a nice briny flavor. I prefer mentaiko but not by much.

I've made a version at home often using Kewpie mayo as the base for the sauce, instead of crème fraîche, olive oil, and egg yolk. I started making it this way because several of the izakayas we like here in NYC use Kewpie for this delicious dish -- it's fast, and creates an unctuous umami flavor that's unbeatable.
Buccatini id not skinny

I found tarako at H-Mart, about $7 per pound; labeled "pollack roe". Showed the article to the fish mongers, no one had any idea what tarako or mentaiko was, but it was there in the frozen fish section. Wasn't sure about the membrane after getting out the roe...composted! Anyway, loved the spaghetti!

Found Metaiko for $6.00 a package about 2oz at Dainobu in Manhattan. I've seen at other asian specialty stores as well like Hmart in midtown. They sell frozen fyi just that a little beforehand in your fridge. Can't wait to try.
Does anyone know if I can use canned pollock roe? I can only find ikra mintaya, which seems like it might work?
I suggest Kalles Kaviar (also cod roe) from IKEA.
Any ideas for online sources?  I only found one product, pricey, on Amazon, and tried several Asian markets and online sites with no luck.  Lots of packaged Mentaiko pasta sauce mixes, however!

I was really excited to make this dish. So much that I waited weeks to get Roe. But it was indeed a disappointment. I am not sure if it was the taste or texture. But neither my nor my friends could finish our plates. In fact, It wasn’t terrible. But after a bite we were all done.

For all of you struggling and considering spending an arm and a leg on the mentaiko: You can find roe in Asian supermarkets such as H Mart, for an attainable price. H Mart specifically has had several varieties of roe in the frozen Japanese foods section. Best of luck.
This is tasty but not as good as other recipes I've made because the other ingredients completely drown out the tarako. Disappointing
Made the instant version today, with somen, butter, and milk. Tasty!

I've had mentaiko pasta with simply butter, salt, and pepper (which is fantastic), but sometimes I'll add in 1/4 cup of kimchi (juice and all), and top with parmesan, which is my favorite way to have mentaiko.
Would tobiko (flying fish roe) work for this?
For the lactose intolerant among us, anyonehave a suggested substitute for the dairy ingredients (crème fraiche or heavy cream)?
Isn’t Italian battarga very similar to mentaiko? If it is, it is easily found on line, e.g-Amazon.

Two very different animals. Bottarga is dried and cured; it's almost like parmesan cheese and can be grated. Unlike Mentaiko which is salted, but not dried, still sort of in its fresh form (albeit frozen and seasoned).

I Googled those two ingredients and found out that they are different forms of seasoned Pollack roe.Interesting. Could Shad Roe be utilized as a substitute? You would first have to season it yourself.

We have been doing this recipe for a few years now. My son loves it. The quick and easy version of this takes 10 min - including boiling Angel Hair.  Any fish roe - come in little wood boxes - will do. We tried at least two different ones - currently I have karasi in my freezer.  While it’s still frozen - pull off membrane. Melt 2 tbsp of butter and mix in the roe -  2 or 3 sacks.   It is salty and spicy enough, we don’t use extra soy sauce.  Mix in wet pasta. Add cut up Nori. Eat it. :)
It’s not a good recipe. The ingredients synergize in an unpleasant way. It’s not clean-tasting. I’ve never had a mentaiko pasta I didn’t like - until now.
Can I find tarako and mentaiko at WalMart?

A new favorite.  I’ve made this several times and omitted the egg, cream and miso paste based on the comments here.  Added melted butter as seen in online videos for mentaiko pasta.  Latest twist was to grate some bottarga di muggine (cured grey mullet roe) I had on hand into the mentaiko sauce.  I liked the result.  The mentaiko and bottarga were distinct but complementary.

I bought the mentakio and was a excited to taste this dish but honestly the dish tasted like nothing much ; pretty sure adding some butter to pasta would have given a very similar dish for 1/4 the price . The Japanese student staying with us said this is how it’s supposed to taste. I don’t get the appeal! Won’t be making again.
Has anyone tried this with Taramosalata instead of roe and crème freche? It seem like it might be a similar consistency

Totally agree with those who say leave out the miso, lemon and olive oil. A very disappointing recipe. Will definitely look for another and simplify the process as this was not at all what mentaiko spaghetti should be like.

I finally located mentaiko somewhat locally and got to try this and I’m so glad I did! I understand those who want to the mentaiko speak for itself, as it’s an interesting flavor on its own, but I loved the fresh, bright flavor contrast with the crème fraiche and lemon. We had it with a salad with sesame dressing and carrots roasted in a maple-miso glaze. I also liked how quickly it came together. This will make it into the regular rotation.

Hmm. I've never had mentaiko spaghetti, but I think the ladle of pasta water diluted the sauce too much. It took about 30 minutes for the pasta to absorb all the liquid, at which point we'd been eating it for 25 minutes. As the water got absorbed, the dish became more flavorful. It was still very subtle and mild (my mentaiko from Sunrise Mart wasn't spicy at all but very delicious nonetheless). If I try it again, I'll reserve some pasta water but only add it if the sauce needs moistening.
I ruined this with the lemon. Way too much!!

stats.Total Time: 15 minutes, plus at least 8 hours’ marinating
stats.Cook Time: 15 minutes, plus at least 8 hours’ marinating

comments.commentBody:
Good to see Kenji point out the difference between the different brands of Kosher Salt and Table Salt again. It's something that more people should know about because it greatly affects recipes and I find is a source of a lot of problems for people who follow recipes and think salt is salt.
Table salt is shaped as little cubes, Morton's Kosher is big flat chunks, Diamond Crystal Kosher is big pyramids. For a given volume (ie a tablespoon), the shape affects how much fits. More = up to 2x saltier
Do you salt the skin side of the filets? Or do you salt the flesh side, and then turn them skin-side up? Directions aren’t clear.

I saw this procedure on Kenji's Instagram and tried it with frozen wild Sockeye fillets. I defrosted the fillets in the fridge the evening before, the next morning I patted them dry, salted both sides (ie, all over), and placed them on a paper towel-lined plate in the fridge uncovered until dinner. The result was perfectly crisp skin, no spatter, and deliciously moist salmon. I will not make pan-seared salmon any other way now. Thank you, Kenji!

Especially for salmon from the grocery store (including pre-portioned frozen packages), I would suggest first splashing the fish with sake and letting it rest for five or ten minutes before patting dry; then apply the salt. As Nami Chen of “Just One Cookbook” fame notes, this will remove any hint of fishy odors.

When I make it, I’ll wrap the salted fillets in paper towels and seal them in a Ziploc bag with the air removed, and cure them for up to a week in the ’fridge.

b&

I'm curious why sea salt, in all its many splendid forms and variants (from 'fleur de sel' to Maldon flakes), is very rarely seen, if at all used, in every NYT Cooking recipe? What's with this seeming Kosher salt monopoly? Is it a taken for granted New York thing? Especially with seafood...and in this case, here with salmon...why would I prefer kosher to proper sea salt?

Fleur de Sel or Maldon are finishing salts; they're expensive and more about the texture their crystals offer to the dish. An application like this where the salt is going to completely dissolve into the salmon would be a huge waste of nice salt and money.

We have been doing this for years. Salt the skin only. You want to use enough salt such that you can't see the skin peering through from below. Wait 30 minutes. Rinse salt off with cold water then pat the fillet completely dry with paper towels. Pan fry skin side down. Overnight salting is not necessary - who has time?. Table salt is just fine - it's going to be washed away. We use this method on a hot stainless steel frypan with good quality oil and the skin does not stick.

I've tried your technique before, but it seems to me to be a different thing which produces different results. I prefer the texture you get with a longer dry brine. The time involved is necessary for the chemical changes that brining induces. Why don't you try it? You might be surprised, and if not you've wasted very little active time.
It seems that using the stated amount of salt would yield a very salty entrée. What am I missing?
Should you rinse the remaining salt off before cooking or just start frying away?
there won't be any remaining salt as it dissolves. in any case, don't rinse. :)

Prepared a giant Costco filet of salmon as Kenji directed. The results were amazing: much more appealing texture, better flavor and far less mess to clean up. I didn’t separate it into servings until after I cooked it: much easier all around.
Are you salting only the skin side, or both sides of the salmon?

For a middle of the road approach to save brining over night, press and pat the fillets as dry as possible, sprinkle them with kosher salt and black pepper, and let them sit in the fridge uncovered for at least half an hour - or as long as you can. Pat them dry gently again and put them into a very hot cast iron skillet, skin side down, with a screen spatter guard cover for 1 or 2 minutes. Then flip for 10 secs for color. Prolly not as good as the recipe, but similar results.
Can anyone suggest why pan frying is preferable to roasting in the oven skin side up? This is my preferred method and I get great results every time.

The recipe gives options for pan frying skin down and for broiling skin up - both hit the skin with more direct heat more quickly than roasting, resulting in crispier skin at a given level of doneness than roasting. This may be a terminological thing, though. When you say roast, I am imagining an oven set to 450, with the heat coming from the bottom, possibly with a convection fan on. Some ovens now have a "roast" function that additionally heats from the top more like broiling.
Where oh where is the instruction to WIPE OFF THE SALT before cooking! If one omits this step (as this one did), the result is incredibly salty fish, so salty it's hard to eat.
Salty beyond tolerance AND I rinsed them before cooking as directed.

Divided a big piece of sockeye in so I could try both broiling & pan frying methods. Strongly preferred broiled for 3 reasons: 1) skin stayed in tact & was crispy whereas pan skin was torn & not crispy 2) broiled fish cooked evenly & was very moist whereas pan fish cooked unevenly &  was dry 3) broiling was far easier on process & cleanup. For ref., my oven rack was 8 in. from broiler & I cooked 8 min for well done. Perfection! Will try w/ king salmon next time as sockeye was too lean for me.
Horrible! Way too salty. We threw it out and wasted really good salmon. We wouldn’t feed it to our dog.

I just made this and I am finding it hard to understand many of the comments.  This is some of the best salmon I've had.  It wasn't too salty at all and I'm wondering if some others used table salt instead of eg Kosher salt.
Help! It was way too salty!! Should you rinse it off before cooking?

I used frozen fillets every time and made this more than twice. This last time I figured, let’s try and brine them directly from frozen. I expected a watery mess when I took it out of the fridge after a day of dry-brining but to my delighted surprise it was very much in the same state as how I would’ve thawed first and dry-brined - maybe even firmer flesh, which was better! Obviously from now on I will dry-brine directly from frozen!

This was so good!  Confusingly good for the fact it’s simply salting and refrigerating. 
I’m not really a salmon fan. I don’t hate it, but let’s say I eat it because it’s good for me and I get lots of fresh for free.
This was the yummiest salmon, way of making salmon, I’ve ever had!
I did it pretty much as it says. Put paper towel in the bottom of a glass dish. Patted salmon dry. Ground sea salt all over it. Tin foil cover. Fridge for 24hrs. Removed paper towel. Rubbed olive oil all over. Put in oven, skin side up, on broil for 6ish mins. SO. GOOD.
This is absolutely my experience. I find the negative notes so perplexing. That's cooking for you, though - experiences differ.
The pan should be heated for two minutes on medium high, and then turn down to medium low when the salmon is added. That’s the only way to get the skin crispy
C'mon Kenji.  For someone famous for attention to every detail preparing a dish, you left out whether the salmon skins have (or have not) had the scales removed.
I live in Alaska and we never scale salmon. Might be different for farmed.

A few months ago, I was planning to make Eric Kim's NYT crispy skinned salmon recipe and had the same question. I actually emailed Serious Eats. Dan Gritzer replied that the salmon should be scaled. He appeared surprised that is was not sold already scaled!
@Ken, writing from Alaska here. We catch and eat a whole lotta salmon - sockeye and coho mostly, the occasional king. Never ever heard of scaling them.

Used Costco skinless filets. Probably too thin and without skin made this almost unedible -- - way too salty. Not sure I would experiment with another expensive salmon purchase only to get a similar result.
I found this to be very salty. Don’t know that I’ll use this method again. And I do know about the differences in salts, so I don’t think that was the issue.

simply spectacular. I used sockeye salmon (around 1 lb, thin fillet), & dry-brined for a day & a half  (great that you can do the dry-brine over 2 days, and not worry about it going bad, used Maldon sea salt flakes, why not?). The consistency of the salmon was more solid than I had ever had, yet moist (~ 6 minutes under the broiler at high). the crunchy skin was a fantastic complement to the firm yet moist flesh. This will be my go to recipe for a quick, delish & classy dinner plate.

I do this with frozen fish - salmon, halibut, etc. When I take the fish out of the packaging to thaw, I salt it liberally all over, then put it in the fridge to thaw in a single layer overnight, uncovered or loosely covered. I pour off any water that collects during the thawing process as it’s thawing. It seasons the fish all the way through. Even with Melissa Clark’s Salmon With Anchovy Garlic Butter (the sauce is quite salty) I salt the fish and use less or no salt in the sauce.
Would this technique work for other fish? What about thin filets, like sole?
Is there any reason this would not work with salmon on the grill?
It works well. Grill skin side down after lightly oiling for similar time/temp as you would salmon that you haven't performed this technique on.
If I want to add other spices besides salt, would I add them to the dry brine or just before cooking?

Great idea, the concept translates well from beef!  You didn't say, but my assumption was to salt both sides of the salmon, and not just the skin?  The recipe never specifically said that, but it concentrated on the skin, so I wouldn't want folks to just assume to just salt the skin side...
He says to salt all over - in other words, both sides.
I remove remove the skin and crisp it separately. The exposed fat adds flavor to the fish when cooked in a hot cast iron skillet, also this approach allows one to season both sides of the fish.

An easy prep and maximum flavor found in this recipe. As is so often the case, simple preparations take time. I salted two 7-oz sockeye fillets before going to bed. Also, I used a large dinner plate with double paper towels on the bottom and loses foil on top. About 18 hours later, I withdrew the fillets from the refrigerator. Around two hours later, the fillets hit the cast iron pan per JAKL’s instructions. Ten minutes later, I plated. Skin was crispy as advertised; fillets were ~well done. 👍🏽

Salted each fillet using 1/4 tsp regular table salt. In fridge 24 hours in single layer on paper towels on a plate lightly covered by foil. Rubbed oil all over just before placing in cast iron pan heated to smoking hot. Skin side down 4 minutes. Turned, took pan off heat. Left fish skin side up in pan for 2 min more. Served with lemon and dill. Not overcooked. Yummy crisp skin. Mother's day dinner. Great success. For those who might find it salty, lemon cut it just right.

Is there any point to dry brining the salmon if you are going to grill it on a cedar plank?  the skin wouldn't be crispy, right?  But it sounds like it would still prevent the white albumin from oozing out.....

You will experience some change in the texture of the flesh - I think it has a better, juicier bite. But the changes to the skin are more dramatic. I certainly wouldn't say it's wasted on cedar plank salmon, only that it's more worth it for broiled, grilled, or pan-fried salmon.

This is very similar to Melissa Clarke’s recipe for Brown Sugar-Cured Smoked Salmon which I’ve used for years. If you want to grill, you could merge the recipes. Thick fillets like Coho or King work best. If you’re worried about Sodium, rinse and dry as she recommends.
What is the salt content after cooking.  I.e. people on a low or no salt diet..

Which salt you use is important to people who want or need to watch their sodium intake. Diamond Crystal table salt has 430mg. of sodium per 1/4 tsp.; Diamond Crystal kosher salt has 280mg. of sodium per 1/4 tsp. Sometimes, it doesn't matter which type of salt you use, in which case, I'm inclined to go with the lower sodium kosher salt.

I tried this brining method today and cooked the filets on the grill. I bought the filets at noon, patted them dry, salted them, and they were in the fridge by 12:30. Cooked them at 8. It was superb and works just as well on the grill as it does in the broiler or a skillet. The fish did not stick at all to the grill grates. This is the first time I have been able to flip the salmon to get the top grilled without resulting in torn skin and meat. It worked like a charm and the salmon was superb.
Does a brine make sense if you are serving with a soy-based sauce? If so, does one rinse the salt off before cooking?
Can this be done with other types of fish? Say hake or cod; I'm not a big fan of salmon but would like to try the technique.

I've been quick curing salmon for years now but not this simply. Like a good stereo system this will really let the source shine through. I had an ok piece of salmon and it was pretty darn good. Can't wait to try it with something top notch.

I was worried about stinking up my fridge with this recipe, but for what it's worth I have had my salmon fillets sitting in my fridge for over 24 hours now, and my refrigerator does NOT smell of salmon. Also, for all the people asking whether you're only supposed to salt the skin side, Step 1 says to season each fillet "all over," which I'm assuming means on both sides.
For the fridge odor: put a little bowl of vinegar in the fridge for a day to get rid of the smell. My grandma would place little dishes all over her house after cooking stinky fish and it worked!

My favorite part about the comments section after having been a member for years?  Completely changing the recipe and doing it in a definitive manner.  Don't get me wrong, sometimes the comments are genuinely helpful but if you are just here to say "no, do this" then you should get your own website.  At the very least, make the recipe as is and then offer alternatives.
This technique did not work for me. I followed directions but salmon turned out dry and too salty even though I used 1/2 teaspoon diamond kosher salt, 1/4 on each side.

I only had an hour to prep before dinner. I took the salmon out of it's wrapper, dried well with paper towels, and set on a rack and put in the fridge. No salt at this point. When ready to fire, I put the cast iron pan on medium-low heat for 2 min, rubbed both sides of fish well with olive oil, and added to the dry pan. Skin side down for four minutes, flipped, salted, and cooked for about two minutes more. No spatters & skin was incredible! I put additional S&P to taste at the table. Fantastic!
Note that the recipe says "Season each fillet all over ..." - since there are several questions about this.
This method is life-changing. The texture of the salmon is amazing, and the flavor absolutely shines. No odor whatsoever in the fridge, if that's a concern. I will never not dry-brine again!

It cooked beautifully and had none of that white stuff that sometimes oozes from the salmon. BUT it was WAY TOO SALTY FOR ME! I prepared it for lunch for me and a friend and I could not eat it. It was a fresh, wild salmon slice of fish weighing a pound.I used kosher salt and baked it in the oven.
We tried this, overnight and the rest and the result was salt-fish, almost inedible.

I went ahead and grilled it after brining for 5 hours. I rinsed off the salt before grilling over a med high fire. Excellent result, a bit salty, but it’s the first time we’ve eaten the skin along with the fish.

One tip to share.  I tend to buy MSC certified frozen sockeye or coho.  If you thaw it, it will release a lot of water and if you leave the fillets in the water it will make them even more water logged. Better to thaw them in a strainer with a plate underneath so the water doesn’t get trapped.

I made this exactly as written and found the recipe to have two flaws:
1. Too salty. Next time I will salt the skin side only, rinse after the brine and dry thoroughly, then cook it.
2. For stove-top cooking, medium-low is too low. I wanted crispy skin and medium-rare inside and ended up with overdone salmon with barely crisped (and very salty) skin. Next time will cook on medium-high.
Turns out the notes in the "Most Helpful" section weren't. Should have looked at all notes before cooking.

If the skin is dry enough, it will crisp on medium-low but will need longer than if it hits a very hot pan. If I've got salmon with super dry skin (best way to get that is from sitting in the fridge uncovered), after it sears at high heat I quickly turn the heat lower to prevent the albumin issue which increases when cooking the salmon more quickly at a higher heat.

I was always taught that when you were preparing Salmon or Barrumundi (Asian Perch), to get a crispy skin drag the blade of a sharp knife against the grain of the skin, to clean the skin and then pat down dry and and add salt.  This gives you a perfectly dry skin.

Maybe I did something wrong, but the result I had was very dry, very salty salmon. I suppose if I wanted to store it in the shed over the winter, that would be fabulous. But for dinner the other night? Not so much.

Tried the technique with two previously frozen, wild-caught, Sockeye fillet portions. Seared in a cast-iron pan, it worked well--no albumin, nice firm fish, crispy skin! I eyeballed the salt and it turned out a bit salty to our taste, so next time I'll follow exactly to see if that makes a difference. And will definitely try it again this summer when we're grilling a side of salmon--nice to have it so firm and easy to flip! Served over garlicky mustard greens and herby quinoa salad. Terrific!

I’ve made this twice, once with an overnight and half day brine and once about 6 hours. The longer brine time was noticeably better. I also did it with a big piece of king salmon, skinless, cooked on top of sliced lemon rounds on the grill. That came out great!
Way too salty!!! So disappointing!

Too salty. I used 3/4 teaspoon of Diamond Crystal Kosher salt on 7 oz pieces of coho salmon, right in the middle of the recommended range. Any more and it would have been inedible. I am notorious for salting my food heavily, but this was too much. Next time I will add less. It did make the salmon unbelievably non stick on the grill though, which was pretty amazing!

The Diamond Crystal Salt I use is not marked as Kosher, and it does not come in a "tower" as another commenter said - it is flaked and comes in a bag.  I hope this helps - I have always had great success using this salt for years.

The salmon turned out just enough too salty to make it unpleasant even though I used less than 2/3 of a teaspoon of Morton kosher salt per 6 oz filet (sockeye). I cooked two filets with a light rinse and then patted them dry.  After tasting them I cooked the other two filets after a thorough rinse.  They still were too salty.  More like a cured fish.  I had left them in the refrigerator for about 16 hours.  I also did not see an improvement in the texture of the fish.

I’ve made this recipe several times and it always turns out really well. I never overcook my salmon with this method. Rinsing the fish would probably undo the work of making the fish drier so that might not be wise. If it’s too salty, be sure to use the lower amount of salt and consider adjusting based on the size of the filet. It’s definitely a little saltier but it shouldn’t taste so salty it’s inedible.

This strategy works really well, especially for crispy skin! I love that the fish is more seasoned throughout and the way that it becomes “nonstick” is pretty remarkable. I didn’t believe that 105 would be med rare because the sides still looked so pink and waited till 115 to remove from the pan, but kenji was right. Eating it at medium was still great.
Excellent recipe! I used in on pan-fried steelhead trout. I often struggle with cooking fish (I'm not very gifted in the kitchen) but this came out really well and I loved the lack of splatter.

Wow, followed directions, even wiped some salt off, and it was still waaaaay too salty. I only salted the fleshy side. Or was I just supposed to salt the skin side? I’m going to rinse the remaining fillet and use it in salad.

This salt brine is extremely effective, it acts as a quick cure. But even going light on the salt after reviewing comments we found it very salty, really too salty to eat as a fish main. 

We used leftovers as a substitute for lox with bagels and cream cheese and liked that better.
Would this work with MSG or other salts that have less "saltiness" flavor?
Has anyone tried this method for grilled salmon?
Greenink: "all over"... Sometimes it helps to read twice, or more. I've had to do it more often than I care to admit. Cheers.
Can you brine frozen salmon while it defrosts in the refrigerator overnight? Or must you thaw it first?

Why all the bother brining overnight. I pat the filets dry and salt the skin (about an 1/8 tsp) and put it under the broiler 'skin side up' at lease 6 inches from the heat for 5-7 minutes depending on the thickness. The skin crisps nicely and the fish is perfectly cooked. Sometimes, I get the little globules of fat - just scrape off with a fork. A quick meal that doesn't require any forethought.

Funnily enough, the introduction to the recipe explains why the longer brine, giving a few main reasons. Reducing those globules of albumin (not fat) is one of the reasons, though not the most important one. The others are: less oil spatter, better texture, it's harder to overcook, less pan sticking, and even crispier skin. Perhaps you don't care about these things, but the bother is well worth it to some (including this commenter).
Will dry-brining work for any/most skin-on fish filet?

We eat a lot of Lake Michigan king and coho salmon, as well as Alaskan on occasion but never eat the skin. Also we remove the gray layer underneath the skin where most of the contaminates are concentrated. I sear fillets bare side down then flip and cook them until the flesh easily slips off the skin.

What type of skillet? I don’t have a cast iron. Despite my many attempts to season it and being patient for months! Food was still sticking. So lacking a cast iron what to do? I know most nonstick dont seer well. Thoughts?

The dirty little secret is that cast-iron skillets do not always become nonstick even when properly seasoned and sealed. Food still might stick, but at least you won't get teensy weensy bits of metal in it. Just coat the fish with oil as directed, and try again.
But if you really can't use a cast-iron skillet, just use the heaviest skillet you do have, not a flimsy aluminum one that warps on the heat. (I use a heavy All Clad when I don't use cast-iron.) Heat it well and proceed.

I typically cook my salmon in a 500 degree BBQ grill, using a cast iron skillet.  I use EVO to coat each salmon steak before placing on the skillet.  Three minutes per side. Can I use this salting technique with my cooking method?  Should I adjust cooking time or level of heat?

You can absolutely use your technique, though I'd suggest leaving the salmon about another minute or two on the skin and cooking the flesh side equivalently less. That will result in slightly crispier skin and slightly more evenly cooked flesh (because the skin side insulates somewhat). I'd probably adjust the times like so if using your method even if I wasn't dry-brining the fish, so if you've tried my way and like your times better then maybe stick with them. I hope you tried it!
Can this be done on an outdoor grill, first grilling the skin, then flipping it and grilling the other side?
Does anyone have experience with sous vide after dry brining salmon overnight?   That’s how I cook salmon; foolproof.  But I salt just 30 min before the bath.
Step 6 -7 is confusing. Does this mean broiling is required after pan cooking?
No, it is an alternate method of cooking the salmon.
Has anyone tried this using a gas grill?
How much time is too much for dry brining? I have about 1.3 pounds of wild sockeye. Thank you!
I only brined my 1.17 pound filet for about 3 hours. Worked great.
This dried out the salmon far more than the traditional cooking methods. I wouldn’t do this again.
Is there any reason the brined salmon Can't be grilled?
Had a 1.17 pound skin-on salmon filet. Only had regular table salt or coarse sea salt (will be buying some kosher salt soon). Broiled it as instructed. It was excellent!
First time commenting here.  I didn't have 8 hours or overnight to brine - only 2- 1/2 or so.  And still it was amazing.  Best way of cooking salmon.  A real keeper.
Can good results be achieved on a Traeger using this preparation?
Can you use a seasoned salt? Does the flavor impart anything extra?

I hit on a recipe for air-fryer salmon that worked really well, kept things dry, gave a nice color, and crisped the skin. I'll try Kenji's dry-brining but will use the air-fryer again (I have the kind that's more like a toaster oven than a bucket, makes it easier to see what's going on). I loved the salt proportions given--I know the difference between kosher and other salts, but didn't know there was such a difference between the brands.

Curious what people think about doing this with frozen salmon fillets. I always dry brine poultry and meat if I'm organized and have done that from frozen, but curious about others' experiences with doing this with fish. Given that ideally one is removing a frozen fillet from the vacuum wrap and letting it thaw loosely covered in the fridge for hours, any reason not to do both at the same time? Also curious if there is substantial protein loss, as the article said salt precipitates proteins?

This works well on frozen fish that brines as it defrosts. There is not substantial protein loss - the precipitation of proteins is from solution in the muscle fibers to a solid form in those same muscle fibers. If anything, brining would increase protein (by a miniscule amount) because less albumin coagulates and drops into the pan. I love this technique and I hope you tried it out!
Would this work with tuna?
Can I do this with steelhead trout?

All in on this! Fabulous! I am also going to apply it to my seared trout. I've been dry-brining forever - chicken, turkey, red meat - and never thought to use the method on our weekly fresh fish dishes. I'm starting tomorrow.
With this helpful discussion re: the different kinds of salt, what about Sea Salt?

Many great deals on Bristol Bay wild sockeye salmon going on right now... or pre-order before the season and get it direct from the fisherpeople. Wild sockeye is also the lower greenhouse gas emissions choice, when compared to farmed: "For consumers in the U.S. and Europe, wild salmon products have 46–86 % and 12–71 % lower greenhouse gas emissions than farmed Norwegian salmon, respectively, depending on species and product form." https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969722
Game changer even with smoking on a cedar plank! No sticking at all and cooked perfectly.
Can you use a salt substitute for the same results?

I mix equal parts table salt and sugar and liberally coat the salmon (mostly flesh side because I’m lazy) for 15-30 min while I prep the grill or pan. Or prep other dishes. Rinse off, pat dry and cook as normal. I can’t go back to uncrowned salmon.
How does pink Himalayan salt fit into the equation? Would it compromise the final results at all? I prefer it due to the lower sodium content, for health reasons.

The flavor from salt is sodium chloride. Himalayan salt has a little less salt because of the larger crystals and other nutrients. If you need to cut back more find a potassium salt, but who knows id that will work in a recipe!
Would this method be helpful when grilling salmon?
I am grateful for everyone's comments. but I'll never remember them all. I want to print ALL these notes for my own use, but I don't know how to accomplish that. Can someone help?

The print version specifies "one teaspoon salt" per fillet, which seems an enormous amount of sodium/salt per serving.  Happy to see it's more nuanced here. But how low can you go and still get the desired effect?

Over salted.  This recipe is flawed.  I love salt and I use a lot of it, so when a dish is over salted to me it will be decidedly over salted to most people. 

Rinsing the salt off the fish is the only way to fix this.  Then you’ll have to pat it dry with paper towels.  Unless you like to eat the skin I don’t see this recipe worth the trouble.

This method produced well-seasoned and perfectly cooked salmon with crispy skin! The downside was that the fish smell in my fridge and during/after cooking was really strong - not something I typically have any issues with when cooking salmon. Next time I'll try salting it in the morning rather than the night before.
Much too salt for my taste.
Can you put the salmon on a grill? Or does it have to be pan-fried or broiled?
Any input on how this would work, followed by cooking the salmon using the anchovy garlic butter recipe from the NYT from a few years ago?
A huge hit! What a great recipe. My 2 year old grandson wanted to eat one thing and one thing only: the skin. The adults shared - grudgingly.
Is the salt measurement for each piece or total?
Can you do this method with other fish? My husband doesn’t care for salmon or “steaky” fish. Just curious what you’d recommend! Thank you!
Can you do this with other fish? My husband doesn’t care for “steaky” fish. Just curious what other fish this would work with. Thanks in advance for your help!
Would this work with grilling the salmon on a cedar plank?

The book "Japanese Home Cooking" has a faster method.  For one filet, put 1/2 tsp. salt in a plastic bag.  Add 1/2 cup saki (I use sauvignon blanc).  Slosh bag to dissolve salt.  Add filet and slosh for 20 sec.  Remove filet and hang over bag to drain.  Put filet on heat immediately.  Works like a charm.

If I try this on my salmon steak, do I rinse off the salt and pat dry before using my paste type jerk?  I don't need to salt the fish with the well seasoned jerk paste.  We then usually grill (flipping to char both sides).
Can this be done with frozen salmon?
according to other cooking notes. yes. but thaw them correctly, as explained by others.
I’m confused - is the broiling part a continuation of the overall cooking process?  Or is it a separate method ( skillet OR broil)?
I have a new induction cooktop. The directions say not to preheat an empty pan. I'm especially reluctant to try preheating a pan for as long as 2 minutes. Any advice out there?

HI
I have an induction cook top. I have preheated my pans low temp  and slow for more than 2 minutes with no issues.

I regularly preheat my cast iron pans on induction range, normally for 5 to 10 minutes in preparation for searing steaks.  I don't preheat non-stick pans.  You probably want to experiment with times and temperatures. I think the admonition against preheating may be related to the safety of putting things into a preheated pan.
How about grilling this salmon (over a gas or charcoal grill)?
What does this do to the sodium content of the salmon. We are no-salt added dieters.
It increases the sodium content, which is the purpose of the recipe.
It is truly a salt bomb; this probably isn't a suitable recipe for you.

Am I cooking the salmon skin side down in a skillet and then skin side up under the broiler, or are these two different alternative methods? I’m wondering because all the steps are numbered consecutively. Also I never see instructions for rinsing off the salt.
See my reply to Patty below.  Must be two alternatives, but confusing numbering!

The technique works beautifully: no liquid run-off at all, and a firmer, more juicy filet than I usually manage.  Heat level is always tricky, though, and my notion of medium-low didn't produce the browned and quite crisp skin I hoped for.  Next time, I'll try closer to medium and maybe heat my skilled a little longer.  But I'll never fry salmon without the dry brine again.
I also think thay nonbrand Kosher salt will work fine.  I'm trying it tonight.

Can you brine the salmon as indicated then use a marinade (e.g., Salmon with Lemon Herb, https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/5824-salmon-with-lemon-herb-marinade?surface=cooking-carousel&fellback=false&req_id=566996751&algo=cooking_preranked_all_MiniLM_L12_v2_title_desc_step_ing&variant=0_cooking_pretrained_title_desc_step_ing&imp_id=911555280). I would marinate them skin side up with just enough marinade to come up the sides of the salmon, but not into the skin.
It was really tasty, but really salty! Wipe it off prior or what? I want to make it again but less salty. Ideas??
Anyone try this brining, then grilling?
Like Greenink, I'm wondering whether to salt both sides or just flesh? I've been taught that salting the skin makes it less crispy.

Wow!  This simple step made for a delicious, firm and juicy salmon filet.  Just be sure to blot the fish dry before cooking to remove excess salt and any moisture.  I pan seared it skin side down and covered it once the skin wa crisp and lowered the heat to basically steam the rest of the fish. I'm usually not a fan of salmon skin but it crisped up beautifully this time.  Perhaps I'll experiment with adding some aromatics to the brine rub next time.
Worked great! Cooked on my barbecue

I typically like to let my skin-on sockeye defrost overnight in the fridge, uncovered (does not leave a fishy smell), because it dries out the skin - even without the salting. The skin gets super dry and so when it hits the pan it sears easily. But I'm going to try this method because the albumin is troublesome. I also think here the reason for cooking it at a lower heat is that I've read it helps keep the flesh from shrinking quickly and releasing the white albumin.

Dunno if it is related, but yesterday, I very lightly salted a thick salmon filet about one hour ahead of cooking and poached it in court boullion.  Have NEVER made such a tender, luscious piece of salmon before. And none of that white stuff on top, either.
I did this for just a couple hours and it turned out great.
Can this method be used on the grill? I assume so but still wondering
Do you have any experiences with grilling the salmon after it is prepared with salt in this manner?
Is the broiling a finishing step or an alternate method?

I followed this exactly, and did 24-26 hours of brine.  It didn’t help enough with stove spatter. Texture was good but I really need to find out if this will transfer to an outdoor grill or to an oven roast method.
Kenji is my hands-down fave on NYT cooking, and this recipe just shows why. Thanks!!
Very good. I did some for an hour and a half, and cooked the rest the next day, 24 hours later.
It was much more firm and the white stuff was almost non existent. It turn out a bit salty when we added a soy sauces based sauce to serve.
The salmon I buy comes skinless.  Might there be any change in the cooking directions or timing for skinless salmon fillets?

Followed exactly except I eyeballed the salt and rinsed the salmon. I let it set overnight. It worked REALLY well. I cooked the filet really simply-- just some pepper and a pinch of cumin. Every bite was perfectly seasoned and the texture was amazing. And they weren't lying... I put the fish in the pan and there was zero sizzle. I thought I hadn't preheated at first. 
Probably going to do this to every piece of fish I cook in the future.
I'd be interested in any tips on preparing this on the grill.
Embarrassed! I submitted my question before seeing yours. I hope someone has tried this and had good results.
Does this technique work for those of us who like to grill salmon (gas grill)?
Salmon with skin is often not available, either frozen or fresh where I shop. How big a difference will this make?
Cannot believe I now know the secret to perfectly cooked salmon with the crispiest skin ever.  Excellent!
Can you use this method when you grill it?

I made this with yellowtail, which I happened to have in the freezer. I planned to brine it overnight but it ended up at a full 48 hours because I was disorganized. It was wonderful—absolutely crispy skin and perfectly moist meat. (I did it on the stovetop.)
Using less than 1/2 tsp of Diamon Crystal salt, I found the salmon had a lovely texture, but it was far too briny for me.
We loved the crispy skin but the salmon ended up too salty for our taste. Not giving up but next time will only salt the skin.
Could salmon prepared this way be grilled?
I've been making salmon this for a long time, my secret is a cast iron skillet and can be achieved without a 'dry brine." Try it.

Experienced vegetarian cook learning to cook salmon. Salted with DC kosher salt and placed in refrigerator for 18 hours. Coated all surfaces with 1 Tbsp. EVOO, heated pan and placed filets skin side down. Skin stuck pan despite trying to move filets as described. After 5 minutes, flipped filets (minus skin) for a minute and removed from pan, leaving the lovely “crispy skin“ behind. Texture of cooked fish was perfect. Will use more oil next time to keep skin on filets! Poorly written recipe.

I prepared this with some fresh Copper River salmon.  Four stars.  I rested the salted fish about six hours.  All cooktops are different and with mine, I required about twice the time as the recipe indicates.  It was very good.  And, yes, there was a bit of fishy smell in my ‘frig.  I would make this again.

What a great technique! I did this with wild caught Coho salmon for a dinner party. Served the filets cold over a green salad (with lots of “good stuff” in it, since it was one of our first hot days of summer. The salmon was very flavorful — compliments abounded. Thank you!

Inadvertently went with the 2 day uncovered method. Fillets were very dry going in.   Nice crispy skins but overcooked at 4 min.  Pretty red color and texture of hot smoked salmon. Next time, shorter brine and cook time.
We believe we follow directions correctly. We found the salmon almost in their opening for 530 edible because it was so salty.
It's a complete salt bomb. Avoid this approach.

I used a half teaspoon of Diamond kosher salt on the meat side only and found this to be just enough. If you are wary of over-salting your salmon, and making this for the first time, I suggest following suit. You can always add additional salt once it's plated, to taste.

As much as I really wanted to love this recipe, I tried it twice-salting once for 3 hours and once for 24 hours. Yes, there was no spatter in the pan, but honestly, that’s not my cooking priority. What I got was slightly salty, softer meat and nothing else. There’s just no taste to salmon when you do this. And the texture and look of the fish is just not attractive.

I really liked how this improved the texture of sockeye salmon, and the skin was delightful-- never ate the skin before trying this recipe. I used 2 tsp kosher salt to season two sockeye slabs  (about 14 oz each), they sat in the fridge about 10 hours, I broiled skin side up for 6 minutes for well done fish & crispy browned skin. Next time I will try using less salt and omit the coating of oil, the fish had plenty of fat without the added slick of oil.

Tried this method with striped bass, a less fatty fish, thick center cut including the thin flap. Salt does need to be adjusted. Also heat: skin browned but did not get quite crisp enough, so I will go back to medium-high heat. Flesh tightened up nicely. Best was not having to score the skin to keep that side flat against the pan; scoring this allow juices to run out. Definitely worth another try.

I used total of 1 ½ tsp of diamond kosher salt for 18 oz is salmon, will probably use a little more next time. Brined for 24 hrs. Seared to 100 deg F internal temp. Absolutely perfect! Definitely not too salty.

Tried this method last night with four beautiful (and pricey) filets of steelhead trout. I am never again cooking salmon or its relatives any other way. The result was spectacular, as wife and guests kept exclaiming.

I don’t know if I did something wrong, but I did not like the way this came out at all. The texture was way off to me. Not sure how to describe it but thick flakes and somewhat rubbery by comparison to my typical baked salmon.
I tell you what, for me this recipe is too salty for an entree (and I used less salt than directed), but it's LOVELY served cold with a salad.

For thin and small fillets even 1/2 tsp of (diamond crystal) salt seems a tad too salty. Other than that, this is fantastic and even turns mediocre salmon into a fine dinner. I’ve done this twice and used broiler each time. Fridge doesn’t smell fishy at all while brining. This will be my go to method for cooking salmon. Thank you Kenji!

I used arctic char for this recipe and it was fantastic. BUT I rinsed the salt off and patted dry before cooking. It was still at the upper limit of saltiness after an overnight brine.

I also cook the fish much closer to the broiler and flip it skin-side-down to color before I take it out.

This was so yummy! Normally salmon is a wildcard for me because I don’t make it often… broiling sounded easier and my skin came out burnt (probably not far enough from the heating element) but it still tastes awesome! Next time I’ll pan fry.
Loved this! Hubby told me to put this on rotation.
This is a salmon game-changer! Highly recommend.
Brined for 9 hours. Nothing special. We enjoyed the slow cook on low temperature recipes more.
I am never making salmon any other way again. <3
This is the best salmon I’ve cooked, and with a son-in-law in the Alaska fish processing business, we eat a lot of salmon!

For our European members: I dry-brined for 24 hours. Standard supermarket salmon will yield crumbs of crispy skins, but not the lovely crackers described. I'll see if I can get fresh sockeye at the marketvand report back.
Because of my memories of oft-made gravad laks, I included black pepper and chili flakes in the brine. Also added fresh chipped dill along with the lemon juice at the end. It was delicious.
We used 1 tsp of Morton’s per filet and it was too salty

Big surprise! Morton's Kosher is almost as salty as table salt, and 71% saltier than Diamond Crystal. Of course it was way too salty; you didn't follow his recipe. There's a reason so many people have great results with this technique. The kind of salt makes a huge difference. If you used the low end of Kenji's range of Diamond Crystal, 1/2 tsp has 560 mg of sodium. Your 1 full tsp. of Morton's Kosher has 1,920 mg; table salt has even more at 2,360. Huge difference!

The times for this are so dependent on filet size that they’re basically useless. Medium-low skillet taking 4-5 min to brown skins? After turning it to medium mine still took 15 minutes. And I should have ignored his advance to move them around while browning, it just ended to ripping the skins

I am obsessed with this method. We recently were gifted a good amount of Alaskan salmon, and I made it this way every time. So good, unmatched. The only way I'll cook salmon with/without any sauce. I use the broiler method and love it!! I tell everyone I know!

Been dry brining salmon for years with a 5 to 1 ratio of salt to sugar by weight   a little black pepper, for just 15 to 20 min before (briefly and not thoroughly) rinsing, drying, re-seasoning, pan searing on high heat skin side down (initially pressing on the fish to maximize contact with the pan and reduce warping as the flesh tightens) for a few minutes, then flip for 1 min. Broil some veggies and prep some farro or rice while all this is going on. Dinner in <30 minutes from start to finish.

I’ve made both gravlax and hot smoked salmon starting with dry brines but it never occurred to me to use that same method as a way to make delicious, tender, nonstick salmon for dinner.  It’s an easy method for foolproof salmon.
Has anyone tried this with any other type of fish besides salmon?

I found this produced a salt focused piece of fish not unlike cooking smoked salmon which is sold here in the UK and frankly it was disagreable.  I will not have much respect for his advice going forward.
Arrrgh.  Way tooooo salty.  All the subtle salmon flavour is obliterated

What kind of salt did you use and how much? You will certainly obliterate the subtle salmon flavor if you oversalt. He says "Season each fillet all over with ½ to 1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt...aiming higher or lower, depending on your taste." Table salt has 2,360 mg of sodium per tsp, but Diamond Crystal flakes have 1,120, less than half. Kenji's method works, but use the right salt and adjust to taste.
I was not at all impressed with this process.  The change in fresh salmon was disagreeable.  I cannot recommend the process
Probably the worst NYT recipe on here based on the cooking technique. Following to a T, the recipe came out horrendously cooked with the skin not crispy and completely stuck to the pan.
This came out very good!  It was a little salty but in a good way.
I'm putting it in the fridge now but don't understand how some here say that it's way too salty and others that it's delicious. Should I rinse it before grilling it?  Thanks.

Thank you, Kenji. After trying this, I can hardly go back to cooking non-brined salmon and see it get covered with unpleasant gray goo. 

The first time I tried doing this, I dry-brined some sockeye filets overnight. I then pan-seared it the least amount of time necessary to get crispy skin but found the flesh tough, dry, and too salty. Since then, I won’t brine sockeye for much longer than an hour; that allows the fish to retain some moisture but will still eliminate the albumen problem. 

Atlantic, King, and Ora-King do tolerate overnight brining a lot better, though I find that 95% of the payoff from the brining is attained in a couple of hours: the flesh is evenly salted and can be cooked rare while staying firm and juicy.

So much conflicting advice! I live in Spain where I can't buy any of the salt brands mentioned. I  have no idea how much salt to use. And I agree that it's not clear whether you salt both sides of the fish and whether you wipe off the salt before cooking.

Although this answers a rather old question: Diamond Crystal is the de facto standard salt in professional kitchens. and bakeries.  It is free of additives (unlike table salt), has a uniform grain size (unlike Morton's) that is easy to handle and distribute and is available in bulk packages.  It has only recently been marketed to consumers outside of larger cities. Smallest package is 4 pounds.  Fashionable finishing salts are nice to have but are a waste in the cooking or baking phase.

Doesn't anyone STEAM salmon? Boiling 2-3 inches of water under an Asian bamboo steamer with 2 generous salmon portions skin down takes 5 minutes; the salmon tastes exquisitely sweet. A spatula transfers fish unbroken to plate(s). (I) add a couple twists of a mill containing mixed peppercorns to each portion and serve with salad or green vegetable. (Uncrisp skin is fine by gourmet cat.) Steamer size does limit sharing but one could steam 2 more portions while the first two wait 5 minutes in foil.

I have not used Table Salt in years - my favorite salt is Diamond Crystal, but be aware that it is not as "salty" as regular Table Salt, so you need to adjust the recipe if using it.  I will definitely be dry-brining my next salmon based on this recipe, and will report back.

This technique has completely changed how my family feels about eating salmon. Not only is the flesh firmer, it's aesthetically more pleasing - I forget the name of the white, gunky stuff that can ooze out of salmon - but it doesn't happen when I brine the salmon for a few hours. 

Just be sure to remember to wipe the salt off before cooking. I first wipe with a wet paper towel, then a dry one.
I’m new to cooking so please be kind.  Question- is the salt removed and if so, how? I don’t see that step in the recipe.

title: Changua (Colombian Bread and Egg Soup) Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

comments.commentBody:
Two things: 
1. Use unsweetened rice milk if you’re lactose intolerant. It’s just as good!
2. Day old or stale French bread is the best. If it’s not a little hard, you can toast it.

My husband is half Colombian and grew up eating this on dreary mornings: memories we continue to create with our family in the wilds of Wyoming on blustery, grey days (we call them Changua days). Thanks Kenji, for the love that comes through your version!
This was not for me.  Incredibly bland. Tested like hot milk with some random additions of cheese, herbs and egg.  Maybe if I had a hangover….

On a trip to Colombia, I was served changua for breakfast, and found it to be a quintessential comfort food. I told myself I would remember what it was called and look up a recipe when I got home. My memory failed me. Thank you, Kenji López-Alt, for allowing me to prepare it tonight, when it was just what I needed. I made it according to the recipe (with chicken stock), threw in a few chick peas that were in my fridge and needed to be used up, and it was delicious.

I prefer the "caldo de huevo"....from the Santander region, just a bit of potatoes, a bit of milk to make it silky, cilantro, a bit of scallion, salt and couple of poach eggs...some people add bits of "tostao" or stale bread, I prefer not to, simple but delicious. :)

I make something similar to this for breakfast every day: mix 1/4 cup chia seeds with 1/2 cup hot water or chicken stock, stir until thick (a minute or so). Top with cheese such as Monterey Jack, and heat in microwave to melt it. Fry two eggs over easy, put in chia seed bowl, top with spring onion, salt, pepper. Mix and enjoy. It’s like eggs and grits.

I’ll make anything Kenji suggests. I had homemade chicken broth and heavy cream. I used all chicken broth and put in 1/4 cup heavy cream. I had extra mache greens, potatoes and shredded mozzarella as well as aji Amarillo—-yeah yeah I know it’s not the recipe but the point being you can doctor up this soup any which way and you’ll end up with something delicious. Bottom line a soup in the morning with whatever you have leftover and eggs with cheese is a great way to start your day.

This reminds me of a soup my mom made for healing and comfort. She called it Macaroni and Milk. Basically elbow macaroni, milk, water, butter salt and pepper. If she were feeling fancy, she’d melt in a slice of American cheese. Real hard scrabble New England Yankee comfort food.

I use brazilian pan de queso with the changua. It is a good substitute to almojabanas.
And if you would like it spicy, I use some tabasco drops after serving.
I literally gasped when I saw this headline. As a Colombian, thank you for adding this. It’s cool to see other people are liking this recipe

I remember driving to villa de lleya at the crack of dawn,  from Bogota, when we stopped at a little truck stop in the road. It was cold,  we were half asleep still and yet the giant cauldron of changua was most welcoming.  Hot milky soup with a dropped egg and that soaked bread...YUMMM!

I really loved this!  I thought the broth was especially delicious, and it was a grey, drizzly cold spring day, this cheered me up immensely!  I used a container of homemade croutons that I had made from baguettes, from the freezer, so there was some herbs and spices in this soup as a result.  I also used a tablespoon of Knorr's chicken granules instead of salt.....
Thank you for sharing this for a flash of memories from my childhood in Colombia.
I made this when Kenji posted it on his youtube channel. Didn't have cheese though, so had to leave that out. Still, it was pretty good!

This was very flavorful with chicken bouillon and plenty of scallions and coriander. Used large croutons I had saved from old bread. These croutons were rock hard but worked here nicely for chewy consistency.

It was great to use this recipe to get rid of some stuff from the fridge. Fairly tasty but seemed to lack strong flavor. Next time I make this recipe, I'll make sure to brown some churrizo with the scallions before I throw everything else in

I have been under the weather the last couple of days and was fiending changua.   My dad used to make it every Saturday and Sunday morning .   I made it this morning mostly following the recipe and used queso fresco instead of mozzarella because that’s how dad did it.   We never had it with the bread and I wasn’t a fan so I ate around it.   Really tasty.  Now I have these wet cubes of bread.   I’m toasting them up to if I can turn them into changua croutons.

I loved this. It was so quick and really comforting. I didn't use the cheese or herbs but used chicken stock instead of the water and dried cubed sourdough bread. This is my new favourite way to use leftover stale bread!
If you add fried, crispy chopped bacon and remove the excess grease before serving, you’ll elevate the already wonderful changua to the next level.
add 2 tablespoon of salted butter and changes everything

I considered making this dish as I had some free time between classes, but I opted for kraft mac and cheese instead—best of luck to whoever cooks this tho, though it looks promising and very traditional.
First time for me to see the term "lean dinner rolls." I assume this means rolls w/o butter/oil?

Just love it! Comfort food for rainy Sundays or cold mornings. Used as breakfast in Colombia. 

For bread you can use white loaf bread, toasted and steal.
Made it with corncob broth instead of chicken and added some ramps alongside the scallions - ideal comfort food!

I love this. But one thing to note: if You do it with the chicken stock then DO NOT add the milk. This Dish,with either chicken or bouillon are better in sepárate recipes but definitely not together with the milk. The bouillon one is much better for the hangover, and, it is not served with eggs typically.  The changua, and the beef broth are both sold as breakfast. All types are normally served with Pones!
Easy and heartwarming. Good for breakfast during cold winter mornings.
Thank you! I made this Changua for breakfast this morning and it was delicious !!
Yummy
Tasty and so easy to make! Some people said it was bland so I added garlic and ginger and it was perfection.

I like to sauté a bit of minced onion (1/4 cup)  in the pot before adding the milk. Less bland that way. My husband’s family, from Bogota, does not serve this with cheese, but it’s a great suggestion. They’d serve with hot chocolate and put the cheese in that!
Very fast, very nice. Used homemade chicken broth. When salted properly, not blend at all!
Delicious
Easy pass. Can't eat milk products, and do not like eggs. Surely there are betrer tasting hangover "cures" than this white MoM stuff.
don't make it then. why do we need to know you don't like milk or eggs...
So delicious!!!! Added potatoes and pasta for some more substance.

title: Pizza Sauce for Chicago Thin-Crust Pizza Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

comments.commentBody: Pay attention here, folks: the sauce is uncooked. That's crucial for a fresh tomato taste, which is crucial for Chicago pizza no matter what kind of crust. For a lighter touch on the tomatoes--making them more like one of the toppings--I use petite diced tomatoes plus vinegar, sugar, and herbs. I don't add salt or garlic, because there's so much garlic and salt in the sausage and the cheese.  I distribute pinches of tomato atop the cheese & discard the remaining liquid.

I made this yesterday and man is it good. I used SMs (the yellow can) and Penzey's pizza seasoning since I didn't have Italian seasoning. It has fennel seed and a little red pepper, which were great additions, and I have to get in a plug for them - their spices (and company ethos) are fantastic.

@Ally
Right on both counts!   Penzy’s is an excellent moderately-priced source.  Great reliable product!

Lately I've been dumping the canned tomates into a sieve over a sauce pan and pressing them gently with a fork to release much of their watery juice.  This gets reduced over heat by 2/3rds.  Then the two parts go into in a bowl where the tomatoes get gently but thoroughly crushed with a fork only, to avoid breaking open the seeds with a spinning blade. I find a 400ml can, even of good quality san marzano tomatoes still contains about 100ml of very watery juice. Too wet as is for a fast oven.

This sauce also works well for Detroit and NY style pizzas. Adding a tsp of onion powder is good, and add more sugar to taste.  Canned tomatoes from store work great and home canned tomatoes are amazing if you have them.  Tomatoes will already be pre-cooked but it helps to simmer the sauce to thicken it to a coat-the-spoon texture and remove excess water.

I made this sauce and used it on pizza tonight, and the Italian seasoning was overwhelming to my taste. I’m a Chicagoan and have not had a sauce anywhere here with this much Italian seasoning. I’d suggest using two tsps instead of 2 tbs and adjusting to taste from there.

I made this sauce and split it into two portions. The first portion was cooked down before using and the second portion was uncooked. There was a mild yet noticeable difference in flavor and color of the two. 

The cooked one goes better on a pizza that can handle bold flavors or doesn’t require a lot of sauce. The uncooked version is lighter and brighter but mildly so as compared the other.

Solid Midwestern pizza sauce.  I did half the spices, half the garlic and half of the sugar. It is nearly perfect,  except still a little sweet. I am going to skip the sugar altogether next time around.

This is inspiring me to cook pizza next week while my son is out from Chicago.  I have found a variety of flavor from which brand of canned tomatoes I use in my day-to-day cooking.  My preference is Cento.  Any recommendations from the author or denizens of Chicagoland?
I used Cento for our last pizza. I usually use Red Gold tomato products which are grown and processed in Indiana.
As a Chicagoan, this is a really good representation of a Midwest sauce. It’s made even better by making ahead at least a day or so. It makes a big difference in how bold the sauce can be.
I made pizza from scratch, but really wanted to eat just this sauce.

Spent the better part of two years trying different pizza sauce recipes. I wasn’t trying to recreate something specific, just looking for what I could make and never tire of. This sauce is it. 

I’ve tried both uncooked and cooked. I’m partial to the cooked, but the flavor of the uncooked works better on lighter brighter pizzas. I like to cook it down on a low heat to blend the flavors. It’s more of a way to concentrate the flavors rather than change them.

My go to pizza sauce. We make a sourdough crust (Sam’s recipe) and bake 8 min at 600 on a pizza stone on our Weber Genesis.   I use 1/4 C sauce per pizza (1/2 recipe covers 4 pizzas).  The sauce slightly caramelizes to umami goodness.  Slight mods - 13 oz high quality chopped tomatoes and no sugar.  Stirred together and left to sit to meld flavors.

I’ve made two thin-crust pizzas from these recipes, and except for the time involved, we love them. These were recently seen on America’s Test Kitchen, and the simplicity of the crust and sauce and sausage are deceptive - no special ingredients at all. We’ve been married almost 54 years and are getting a pizza stone for an anniversary present.
I used one of those hand immersion blender rather than get out the blender or food processor. Worked perfectly.
Fast and tasty. I subbed 1 teaspoon each dried oregano and basil for the Italian seasoning and halved the sugar. This works great with the quick pizza dough recipe.

Awesome sauce. Used a can of Bianco whole tomatoes, no paste, but everything else except rice for vinegar. lightly cooked the minced garlic and simmered for 20 minutes.  Not as sweet as some our thin crust pizza sauces which is a good thing. Baked it in my Kamoda Joe jr with some hickory chips and it came out awesome. Made in Chicago Pullman National Park!
Perfect recipe! I just added a few extra cloves : )
Really loved the flavor of the sauce. Only added a bit more salt and I found it had a good balance of sweet and savory.

Delicious on our homemade crust, using our home-grown tomatoes. I did cook it, just to reduce it and make it thicker. I will make this again. I did use an immersion blender, as was suggested by another reviewer. This will be my go-to pizza sauce recipe.
Beautiful recipe :) so quick and easy & tastes simple and perfect.

Didn't have tomato paste so I had to simmer it down a little to thicken. Went extra heavy on the garlic cloves because I didn't have garlic powder. Also subbed in a 50-50 mix of white vinegar and apple cider vinegar for the red wine vinegar because (you guessed it) I didn't have the red wine vinegar. 

Still turned out incredibly tasty on a homemade pizza.

I make this as written - no modifications.   Use a scant 1/4 Cup on 12 in thin crust sourdough crust (Sam Sifton’s recipe).  Baked at 500 on gas grill with pizza stone.  The heat caramelizes the sauce to perfection.  Pizza Friday is on!
Made this last night for a pizza party on Saturday.  It’s absolutely delicious!
Perfection. I'm from Chicago.
this makes no sense. The verbiage says"cooking down canned tomatoes" but the recipe has no cooking

"In Chicago, pizza sauce tends to have an intensely savory flavor that comes from cooking down canned tomatoes..."
