Read: Most Chicago pizza places cook down their tomato sauce.

"The sauce cooks pretty thoroughly on the pizza, so cooking it in advance changes it only incrementally."

Read: Kenji found that cooking it down isn't necessary, as it cooks on the pizza itself. It does change the flavor, but only marginally. If you want to cook it down, do so to your liking.
Really good!  Totally reminds me of the pizza sauces I had growing up in the Chicago burbs.
3.5 stars - it's good but nothing special.
Easy and good.  My favorite kind of recipe
Reminds me of home!  Easy recipe, and delicious.
I really like this easy-to-prepare sauce and have used it on pasta as well (after cooking it a bit since it has raw garlic)…
This is easy and delicious. It makes enough for multiple small pizzas, and freezes well in small containers.
Super easy. Maybe halve the sugar.

I agree with Frank - the first time I made this I thought the sauce was too liquidy, and ended up straining it at the end. The next time, I crushed the whole tomatoes and strained those first before combining everything. Much better.

This sauce tastes like childhood nostalgia! If you ever had a pizza lunchable, this is like a grown-up version of it in all the best ways. I could keep eating this off a spoon if I didn't have an actual pizza to make.

I much prefer the fresh sauce over a cooked one. I make a sauce something similar to this one. What makes a big difference is that I use Muir Glen Fire Roasted Crushed Tomatoes - they are the best by far.

I much prefer the fresh sauce over a cooked one. I make something similar to this one. What makes a big difference is that I use Muir GlenFire Roasted Crushed Tomatoes. Add the tomatoes last and barely process them if you want to preserve the tomato chunks. It's never runny like with whole tomatoes.
Good recipe.  USed siracha and pepper flakes, brown sugar, and more red wine vinegar than recipe.
Great recipe! Thanks much!
Too sweet for me.  I used 2 tsp sugar - next time 1.

We love this!
I cook it and use it as pasta sauce for the kids. Only 2 garlic cloves, 2 teaspoons herbs, 1 tsp sugar.
Everybody adores it : )
Used fresh tomatoes, cooked for about 10 minutes. Left slightly bigger chunks of tomatoes. Turned out delicious !
I fully intended to cook down this sauce but after tasting it (so good!) I did not bother. Very flavorful and balanced. And so easy! I will never buy pizza sauce again. 5 stars.

title: Chicago Thin-Crust (Tavern-Style) Pizza With Sausage and Giardiniera Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

stats.Total Time: 35 minutes, plus at least 3½ hours for the dough

comments.commentBody:
Spectacular!
Indeed, I think it is way past time to categorize Pizzas by some town or city name. As he points out, the mistaken idea that Chicago is deep dish is found to be immediately wrong when you actually eat a few from around town.

The hints he has are a nice collection and summary, making a pizza very much like a unique one found in the upper midwest at a few places in Wisconsin and Minnesota and Upper Michigan.

Bravo for the tips.  They all make a difference.  Nicely written article.
I'm in the UP of Michigan and agree that this isn't just a Chicago pizza. It's very common here in the north woods, and nice to see it get the recognition it deserves.

Thrilled to see this - the pizza I had growing up in the Chicago burbs. Always square cut. But giardiniera is for Italian beef!! We ordered our pizza “garbage style” - sausage as here, plus onion, artichoke, green pepper, mushroom, black olive, and anchovy. I know Kenji says 2-3 toppings max but this combo was SO good.
Could you use Muffaletta condiment? It’s finely chopped Giardiniera mixed with garlic, capers and olives and olive oil.
Yum!!!  That would be good, too.  :)

I would not.  Muffuletta spread is is not the same as giardiniera.  It is an olive salad, and is quite a bit saltier.  It also would be a lot more oily as a result of the small chopping size.  Most the the oil or vinegar packing can drip off of the giardiniera before you put it on the pizza.  The olive salad from a muffuletta would be much more substantial and you would really have to drain it off somehow.  

Also, as some noted, giardiniera on a pizza is not particularly common in Chicago.

I agree with Ryan. I have never had or seen giardiniera on pizza and I’ve been eating thin crust pizza in Chicago all my life. We put that stuff on Italian beef sandwiches, though I’m willing to try it on pizza. It’s the sausage I don’t eat anymore though, having gone vegetarian.

When we see the word "discard" in a recipe, this is a moment to be skeptical. In this case, the oil from giardiniera is a deliciously flavored oil. Drain the giardiniera so that the oil flows back into the jar and use it for cooking or making dressings.

This was a fantastic pizza. 5 out of 5, absolutely delicious.

However, cooking raw sausage in a 500 degree oven is a stinky mess. I'm not one to shy away from messes in the kitchen, especially if the resulting dish is delicious, but I will be cooking the sausage before putting it on the pizza from now on. We have an oversize kitchen hood that vents outside, and that couldn't keep up. Both the pizza steel and the oven had to be scrubbed, and the house still smells of burnt grease the next day.

My mother, Giuseppina Capriotti, made the best pizza and always from scratch and very much like this. She always cut it in squares.  We didn’t call it bar style.  And she cut it with “the big scissors.”  Never a knife or roller. Logically I know it couldn’t change the taste, but….
I wish that Kenji had gone one step further, and researched the 'special' cheese blend that would have made this St.Louis/Imo's style pizza, a legend for those who know.
A friend from St. Louis just Goldbelly'd me a ton of Provel, and we are having Imo's tonight! I don't want it all the time, but when you're craving it, nothing hits like Imo's.

I think that America's Test Kitchen did the St. Louis style with a cheat for the Provel which is a St. Louis thing. I live between Chicago and St. Louis and will take part of the ATK sauce and this crust from Kenji.

Great recipe!  We baked the pizzas around 6:30 PM so I rolled the dough at 10:00 AM; cooked the sausage ahead and drained on paper towels.  In my oven on the “Stone” setting I baked them until the crust got golden brown, about 7 minutes.  Our new go-to recipe now for thin-crust pizza.  Thanks!

Wow, I’m a convert.  Ridiculously good pizza.  This recipe makes hosting a pizza party infinitely less stressful.  I made the dough, sauce, & sausage as written.  All winners and easy to amend to your liking.  For the pizza, in a pinch, I subbed pepperoncini for the guiardiniera.  Was a hit but can’t wait to try the original.
Don't skip draining the giardiniera oil - you want it as dry as possible. I made pizza once by just spooning the sopping condiment right on top and the final product was absolutely swimming in oil.

I have started using parchment paper to bake on saving on cleanup. It also comes in handy when baking more than one pie, You can start building the next pie on the parchment while the previous pie is baking and your pizza peel will be available. A certain big box outlet sells a wider parchment which works well.

I grew up in Chicago and made my dough Tuesday to try this recipe this weekend.  For years I've been trying to figure out the thin crispy dough trick (my favorite is Monical's Pizza from when I lived in Champagne, IL back in the '60s). In Chicago my dad would bring home Home Run Inn pizza every Sunday and we couldn't wait until he cut open the bag it was wrapped in. Note that the sausage was cooked UNDER the cheese. Thanks for taking this on. What a project!!

Even with sauce all the way to the edge, I found 14” to be noticeably too thin a crust.  I had much better luck with 12”, cooked for 11 min at 500 degrees on a cookie sheet.

Just based on timing this to be made for dinner, I had good results drying the flattened dough for ~9 hours during the day, after 3 days cold fermenting in the fridge.

I grew up in Chicago and second everyone else’s comment that I’ve never heard of giardiniera on pizza.  But to each their own, I suppose!

Great tips for making pizza like I grew up eating downstate in Springfield and can't find around the SF Bay area. The closest I've found here is Papa Murphy's take'n bake. I prefer a crisp crust with a little give rather than cracker crisp but that's an easy adjustment with this dough

This was exceptional! I’m a Chicagoan and cooked it for friends here, and we all loved it. I followed the recipe exactly— dough, homemade sausage, and sauce— with the small exception of cutting the sugar in half in the sauce just so I could add if needed. Honestly it wouldn’t have been too sweet with the full 1tbsp, I don’t think. Did one pie with sausage and giardiniera and one with just sausage. Will definitely be making again— I really don’t think it needs any tweaks.
Aaaaannnd, finally my lifelong quest for a good tavern pie at home is complete.  Thanks kenji!!
Pizza skin? Is that what it’s called in Chicago?
This recipe nails the tavern style cracker crust. I am blown away by how exact of a replica this recipe created.  Toppings are flexible, it’s pizza.
I'd like to freeze the second ball for later use.   When should I do this and how would I use it after it thaws?

Thank you, Kenji.  This pizza is so very good.  The instructions are easy to follow and the dough is easy to work. We didn't have giardiniera in the pantry, so I added a bit more mozzarella--but not too much.  It will be hard to have only one pizza night each week.

Started the dough on Monday and baked tonight (day 4). Even though I live in Chicago and have easy access to great pizza, I was so excited to try this recipe - the pics looked incredible. Unfortunately mine didn’t turn out, crust was way too thin and didn’t have much flavor. Made the sauce recipe as well and tasted too much like tomato paste for me. I am really looking forward to the reviews to roll in for this recipe to see if it was user error or if others have same experience.

Dough prep - I cold fermented for about 20 hours (then let it rise at room temp for about 3). I rolled out my dough in the AM and cooked the pizza for dinner. All-in-all about 1.5 days. I'm sure 5 days will taste better, but 1.5 makes some great pizza!

Sauce - You need a fraction of the sauce. If you use a 28 oz can of tomatoes, you will have way too much. A 14 oz can is plenty.

Cheese - Same here: 16 oz of mozz for 2 pizzas is too much for this delicate dough. I used just over 1/2 that.
Kenji answered the question about using an Ooni or other pizza oven and not a regular oven in his Instagram post. His answer: don't

Quite a revelation.  I make a New York/New Haven style weekly and was not even aware this "tavern style" was a thing.  As usual Kenji's instructions, followed exactly, worked great.  It took a while, and extra coaxing, for the low hydration dough ball to come together in my food processor, but it did.
Here's my quantities for 2 13" pies:
For 13" pizza
260g bread flour
130g water
6g sugar
6g salt
1g yeast
26g oil

I followed this recipe exactly, right down to the JP Graziano giardiniera, and I used San Marzano tomatoes in the sauce. The pizza turned out amazing and was as good as any tavern pizza I’d ever had. Thank you, Kenji, for an incredible recipe!

Excellent—made every component as directed and despite my worries at the minimal rise (after 3  days in fridge) the dough still behaved as described. 

Things to note: pizza skins take up a lot of space in the kitchen while they cure! I doubled the recipe for a pizza party and finding a place for four 14’’ skins all day was a puzzle. 

It’s tempting to rush the cooking process, but waiting for the stone to reheat between pies is crucial for crisp crust!
Next-level, outstanding recipe/technique. The answer to my thin-crust dreams!!

I love this recipe—both the original and as a jumping off point to explore other toppings. The crusts are thin, but sturdy and dry after the 24 hrs at room temp.—easy to handle and transfer from peel to steel. I fire up my oven, or in warmer weather, the grill and let the pizza steel heat for about 45 min at 500. Pizzas are ready in 6-8 min with the crispiest crust and nicely dappled with char underneath. A crowd pleaser that makes most everyone nostalgic for late nights out at the bar.

I have made this twice. The first time was good, but the crust was a bit chewy and the second crust, that I froze, shattered while thawing. The one we had was good though. The second time went very well. I let the dough dry longer (the first one I just dried the morning of) and the crust turned out great. One pizza I did sausage and pepperoni and the second one I did mushrooms (which I sauteed with fresh oregano) and prosciutto, topped after it came out of the oven with arugula. It was great.

I used a cast iron pizza pan. Be careful putting your cheese too close to the edge. It will run off, burn, and create quite a bit of smoke! Luckily, I saw a previous warning (thanks) and removed my fire alarm ahead of time. The second one I made, I took the sauce to the edge, but left a border for the cheese, and there was much less smoke. I still had a small runoff of cheese or there wouldn't have been much of any. Fantastic pizza and worth all the work.

Use a pizza steel not a stone. The steel does not lose heat when you put the cold dough and toppings on it like the stone does. You will not believe the difference until you pull it out of the oven. The steel can be used on the stove top too. The steel will lift your pancakes to fluffy heaven.
This is a great recipe. I’m a lifelong Chicagoan, and giard on pizza is definitely a thing especially in the Southside joints.

This is a great recipe. Note: this technique does NOT work with gluten free crust. I have made it numerous times with traditional wheat flour with great success, but when I attempted to do a gluten free crust using Caputo fioreglut for a guest, it buckled and crumbled during curing. So I am doing another batch of dough that I will have to roll thin as bake directly for her. No big deal but note for anyone trying to do a gf version of this. Skip the curing.

Chicago pizza, by which I mean this flat, crispy crusted, square cut pie has MORE cheese on it than you see here. It's a fairly thick layer. The sausage will be fennel prominent.  The old school ovens with rotating steel shelves were able to bake the crust to crispy tender- no soft limp center pieces. Man, the joint my father worked at as a teenager, a million years ago just closed! Save giardiniera for beefs. Try mushrooms & peppers for real deal.

This tastes like my childhood, and I grew up in KC, MO (so yes, generally Midwest, not just Chicago!). The only thing that could make the experience more authentic would be a sit-down Ms. Pac-Man in my dining room.
Has anyone made this in an Ooni pizza oven?
That will be my method. I have the new electric Ooni Volt 12 which I think will be the perfect tool to cook this style. I may bump temp up to 550F and do another at 500F to see difference.
Any details wrt baking partially on a pan, then transferring to steel, as I think was mentioned as optimal in KLA's Insta?
What if you do not have a pizza peel, any tips?
Get a pizza peel

Drew, the article at the end says: If you do not have a baking stone or steel at least 14 inches square, 12-inch pizzas can also be baked on smaller stones or on the back of a large overturned aluminum sheet tray set on an oven rack while the oven heats.
But there's no recipe for either the dough or the sauce.

The links for both are in the ingredient list and the article: Dough https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1024060-chicago-thin-crust-tavern-style-pizza-dough Sauce: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1023970-pizza-sauce-for-chicago-thin-crust-pizza Sausage: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1024059-chicago-style-italian-sausage
The technique of drying the pre shaped skins overnight is interesting, one I’ve never heard of, but willing to give it a try. The toppings  are also very interesting.
Has anyone tried this in their Ooni yet? If so, how did it launch? Any tips or tricks?

Left off the sausage and giardiniera and didn’t sprinkle my peel with semolina or cornmeal because I was worried about those burning (it launched fine). After 7 minutes it was ready to come out, and alas the bottom was burned.  I may move my baking steel back up to the top rack to see if that helps.  This was very good, but a similar pie can be had for much less effort by using Kenji’s flour tortilla hack. After curing, I wrapped up my second dough skin and stuck it in the freezer…

Giardineira is a pickle and can be packed in oil or vinegar. I prefer the vinegar-based, which I think would give a nice sour point to the pizza taste profile. Muffuletta salad is packed in oil. However, I've not used giardiniera on pizza - usually use on wet beef sandwiches.

This was ridiculously good. I was a bit confused by some of the topping amounts and whether they should be for one pizza or two. 12 oz cheese on one pizza seems like a lot. But besides that, the sausage, dough and sauce recipes are all fantastic, and combined with the giardiniera made my favorite homemade pizza ever.

Wonderful pizza but one of the most bizarre recipes I’ve ever read! It works out great unless you have children, dogs or cats, or pretty much anybody who needs to use the kitchen for the 20 hours when you’ll have 3 14”inch pizzas laying out on parchment paper on the counter…. If you have a huge kitchen…go for it!!
Similar problem - my solution - stuck the skins, overnight, in my unheated oven.  I skin on each rack...
where can I find "Kenji's flour tortilla hack?"
On serious eats.com, look for his bar-style pizza.

1. The sauce recipe makes enough for dozen of these large pies, be ready to freeze a lot of it.
2. Dough is a revelation.  Takes some planning, but the result was excellent and it seems easily repeatable for the home cook. Will definitely make it again.
3. Applying raw sausage was a little weird and it dominates the flavor of the finished product, in a good way.  
4. Mine was a bit overdone at 10 min, should have checked at 8.
5. Baking steel was a big mess afterward.
The sauce recipe only made enough for 3-5 pies, if you follow the recipe and put 3/4c-1c per pie.

Thank you for this recipe! I have missed Chicago pizza since leaving 40 years ago.  This dough is perfection. And it was all so easy. Just need space.  I would cut some of the salt in the sauce and increase the sugar a bit, just my preference. Sauce is enough for 4 pies.  I look forward to making this again with my favorite toppings, fresh mushrooms and onions.  This sausage was delish and enough for four pies also. Oh, and I would cut the cheese back a bit.
Did I see somewhere that after the crust is rolled out and dried it can be stored in the refrigerator or the freezer?

We really liked this, it scratched the itch! We're in a very dry climate in Colorado so I will shorten the drying time a bit next time I make it, we had a few cracks. We used plant-based sausage but otherwise adhered to the recipe. I used half and half spicy/mild giardiniera, was perfect and worth buying both kinds.

The crust technique looks interesting--will have to try it.

HOWEVER. I lived in New York for fifteen years and currently in Chicago for more than twice that long (my age is none of your business....) and have never ever seen or heard of pizza in Chicago with giardiniera, whether in a tavern, pizzeria, Italian restaurant, frozen food aisle, you name it. And to my mind it sounds somewhere between peculiar and singularly unappetizing. It is true in Chicago does cut pizza in immoral squares. Ugh.

Rubinos on Main Street here in Bexley ( east Columbus, Ohio) 
Less than a mile from home

This pizza turned out so GOOD. The sauce was the secret star of the show, make sure you get it nice and spiced. I cooked this recipe twice. The first time I let the pizza skins sit out for 12 hrs, the other I let them sit out for 3 days, and boy did it get dry. Make sure it dries nice and flat or it won't get that nice crust on the bottom.

No complaints about this recipe, I encourage you to follow Kenji's recipe to the letter.

Living 30+ years in Chicago (near north side), I've never had a pizza like this. But I made it and it was pretty good. I would make a couple changes next time. I used Marciano's mild gardineira and mild Italian sausage. Next time I will go for the spicier versions of each. Also, the amount of Parmesan/Romano made the end result way too salty for me. I would half the suggested amount.
This was very tasty.  But, how crispy should the crust be?  I haven’t had Chicago-style pizza, so I might have had the wrong expectation.
Wow bringing me back to Chicago suburbs, absolutely worth the wait!

This was very easy dough - I only cured one night and it turned out perfectly!  Precooked the sausage for he sausage pizza and made second pizza with pepperoni, pickled jalapeños & a drizzle of hot honey.  Served with the Sam Sifton Lucali salad - absolute pizzeria perfection!

I was suspicious about this recipe given I had the same problems others have mentioned-the dough did not rise much at all. I put in the fridge right away. I assumed it might have been my yeast, but decided to forge ahead. It sat in the fridge for 4 days.  I took it out a couple hours before rolling and left it out for about 24.  I baked on a steel in my Anova precision oven.  It came out exactly as the pizza I am familiar with living in the Midwest!

This recipe is so fantastic. First attempt came out great. Dough is so easy to work with. Question: Anyone skip the rolled out drying step and just go straight from the roll out to pizza? I had the doughs in the fridge for a few days but then decided I needed the pizza TONIGHT!

I generally like a chewy crust, but I like the way this turned out. I cooked it on a baking steel heated at 500 for an hour. The pizza was finished and well browned on the bottom in about 3.5 minutes. So keep an eye on it if you have a baking steel.

I made this pizza for a second time and wanted to share my experience.  This time I took the dough about at 11 am. Rolled it out at 1 and let it sit until 5:30 pm.  I did not notice any difference from then previous time when I left out for about 20 hours.  Still tasted great even the the dough was not nearly as dried out.  Crust was still crispy.

Outstanding recipe!! I was able to let the crust rise in the fridge for about 36 hours, rolled out the crust early in the morning and it was nice and dry by cooking time. Used store bought sauce. One pizza was sausage and Mama Lils peppers because I couldn’t find Chicago style peppers near me. Used store bought sausage and added extra spices to dress it up. Second pizza was mushroom and pepperoni. All 5 of us woke up thinking about these pizzas and wanting more!!

We loved this recipe so much I splurged and bought a FibraMent pizza stone for the Big Green Egg. I'm going to buy one for our cottage gas grill as well because wow, just WOW. The stone made a huge difference in the quality of the finished product. The pizza took mere minutes to bake. The crust was crisp and released easily with lovely spotting on the bottom. The sausage was thoroughly cooked. And the taste... divine. My only change will be to use hot giardiniera instead of mild.
Do you think I could make this in my Ooni?  I am cooking the sauce since it will not get much cook time in the oven

Everything was going absolutely wonderfully for my Father's Day Chicago Tavern Style pizza, recreating my dad's favorite pizza as a surprise. Until I got to the step "transfer to the pizza stone" and the sausage rolled into the oven. I apparently need to spend more time researching that part.
This is a great recipe.  Love it!

This pizza was a hit at my house.  It will be my go to home pizza.  Plan on starting 7 days ahead.  Make the dough on a Sunday.  Let it rest in the fridge for the week.  Roll out the dough Friday and it's ready to go Saturday evening.   I recommend his online video for a full a visual run through with helpful tips.  Thank you JKLA for all the time and effort you put into this.

A last minute change required that the pizzas be baked on a griddle style grill. After the long ferment and curing process I decided to go ahead and try rather than try to hold the crusts any longer. I was unsure that the sausage would cook through so I pre-cooked that first.
The result was fantastic. 
Having a thin crust meant the pizza was cooked to crisp perfection. The cheese melted but did not brown. Had I thought to bring my little kitchen torch, I could have taken care it on the spot.
...I baked these on a stone in a gas grill and they turned out perfectly
Can you store unused pizza skins?

This was a spectacular recipe. I’m from Milwaukee, so this tasted like home to me. Made 4 days ahead, rolled out morning of since I live in Arizona, and I found by dinner time the dough matched the consistency in Kenji’s YouTube video of this recipe. Used a baking steel. Everyone loved this sausage and the sauce. Also works great with cupping pepperoni. Super thin and crisp crust, holds up well. I found 8 oz cheese was plenty for one pie if you don’t want to buy more than a pound.

Just wanted to share if you use a baking steel where the shorter end is exactly 14 inches, I found it helpful to have a layer of foil under the steel and continuing to the back of the oven, and even up the back of the oven a bit. Then as toppings melt/slide off a little as you are taking the pizza out, you can use a scraper to push the debris over to the foil and keep cooking. Makes continuous pizza-making easier and less mess.
No Giardinera so used Eggplant and Pepper slices in olive oil and it was delicious
Terrific recipe and very forgiving.

The emperor has no clothes. It's not spectacular. It's not fantastic. It's fine, no more, no less. I've been eating this style of pizza in Chicago for over 50 years and it is quite good as a bar snack. I think that is all it was ever meant to be. Kenji studied this for how many months?? The dough is just a drier version of NY pizza, rolled thin. Buy yourself a Home Run Inn frozen pizza and save yourself a lot of time and trouble.
So much more satisfying to make it yourself and this version is def. better than a HRI frozen pizza.  The dough is perfect.

@LindaMock sounds like you still live in Chicago and can eat this any time you want to?  We moved to the Detroit suburbs 22 years ago and have been craving Chicago thin crust pizza ever since and are so excited to try this!  Thank you Kenji for the time and effort you put into this recipe!
what if i want to cook this in my pizza oven?  Should the temp still be 500?
@KTE what makes you think the cooking apparatus adjusts the necessary temperature
Does anyone have suggestions about cooking this from frozen?

Excellent pizza. Even after 3 days in the fridge, I did find it difficult to roll out to 14 inches as dough pulled back some. Didn’t seem to affect the end result too much. Would recommend vegetarian sausage as it cut down on the amount of grease and Marconi giardiniera was a great addition.  Chicago girlfriend loved it!
@Barton Fink, giardiniera on pizza is amazing!

Made this tonight and here were a few tips:
- In the video, it looks like the cheese has melted off the side, presumably onto the steel. This also happened to me (which makes for tasty cheese crisps), but getting it off was impossible before my next bake. It burnt into the subsequent pizzas and was frustrating. 
- Accidentally put it in the fridge the second night instead of leaving it at room temp all night. Didnt make a difference
- Definitely make sure sauce goes to the edges, otherwise crust just ends up burnt and not as nice
- Make sure to measure, I did one at 12” and another 
 at 14” and the latter was significantly better tasting 

Overall great recipe, tasty and would cook again!

Made this as written but took the dough out about six hours before cooking based on other comments here and in the dough recipe itself. We made with spicy impossible sausage and Marconi giardiniera and it was unreal.

Tried this recipe alongside others. I traded out the giardiniera for mushrooms and olives. The result was very good. The crust came out fine and believe JKLA and me when it says its really thin. Would not make this dough recipe again though; too much time and babysitting compared to the other recipes that were equally good and much quicker. My guests and I regaled this recipe and the others equally in the comparison.
Question about flour. In the video he says AP flour, but in the recipe link it says bread flour? Which flour is better to use here, or does it not matter? Thanks, pumped to make this!

stats.Total Time: 30 minutes, plus at least 3 hours’ and up to 5 days’ resting

comments.commentBody: Hey Kenji -- we singletons love pizza too! Can the second boule of dough be frozen?

I'm going to give this recipe 5 stars for the accompanying article and recipes.  This is taking me down a path I believe to finally a great home pizza.  I've always known the secret was in the dough but thought my issues were ingredients.  Over fermenting and drying, so simple and now the science explained and usable in a home kitchen.  Thank you.

My experience with using semolina or corn meal for dusting is that it adheres to the bottom of the crust and can burn black and bitter when it hits the pizza stone. I've long used rice flour instead to make my dough slide off the peel.

@William Wescott. I’ve been in the pizza business for many years and in Chicago. We only use corn meal on the cutting board so the cut pizza can slide into the box or onto a serving plater.  Tavern style pizza is always cooked in a pan so you don’t have to throw the pizza in the oven with a peal.  Good luck everyone !
Is it possible to make a pile of crusts and freeze between parchment paper for quick use?
I bake my pizzas on parchment paper. No problem getting it to slide off the peel or back on at the end of baking. Just grab the edge of the paper off the stone and onto the peel.

Take it from a former pizza cook: always let your pizza dough rise at room temp for at least 2 hours before letting it ferment in the fridge. If you want to freeze the extra dough, I would let it sit in the fridge at least overnight so the yeast can develop some more flavor.
Our dough making at home was transformed by switching to 00 flour.   It’s all soft wheat and ground very fine.   All-purpose flour is a mix of hard and soft wheat.
For the ferment (step 3), straight into the fridge? Or proof on counter first?
It’s now going on 8 hours and the dough hasn’t risen. I always worry on these recipes that use such a small amount of yeast

Maybe I missed something.  I made the dough as directed and refrigerated for 3 days. Rolled out easily and left out overnight as instructed.  I wasn't making the pizza until the evening after I rolled it out and as it was beginning to crack and become too dry. I laid damp paper towels over the crusts and covered with a dish towel.  The damp towels worked and the pizza was delicious.  In the future, I will roll out the dough about 6 to 8 hours before cooking.  Anyone else have this issue?

Yes! I just made this recipe tonight and had the same experience. After rolling out last night, I left them out overnight. By midday, they weee drying and cracking. I ended up baking them and they still turned out pretty good. I should’ve done your damp towel trick, but I’ll definitely roll out same day for dinner pizza next time. Thanks for the tip!
I, too, rolled out the night before and the crusts were starting to crack by the following evening. Making again tomorrow night with a same day roll out/dry/bake to assess the difference.

For those having trouble with no rise, if using a kitchen scale, go against your instincts and use the volumetric measurement for the yeast. Most kitchen scales are not accurate to the gram and you could be adding too little. Also doubling the yeast to half a teaspoon if doing the fridge method won’t do any harm.
He means refrigerate straight-off without three hours on the counter.

Folks, you´re not supposed to let the dough rise 3 hours and then refrigerate it. Those are  separate options of three: (1) rise at room temp, then roll; OR (2)refrigerate, overnight, so it ferments slowly or (3) refrigerate for 3 or 5 days. Then roll. This is THIN crust. It´s not supposed to proof like a loaf of bread. I put my dough directly in the fridge, left it overnight, and it turned out great.  It was really easy to work with and we loved the results.

Weighing is great, but please remember that one reason the practice of sifting flour was common for hundreds of years was to fluff compressed flour in order to achieve accurate measuring. If you don't have a scale  - just shake the flour through a fine strainer (use a flour sifter if you have one), then measure it without compressing it in the process. Or whisk the flour in a bowl or fluff with a spoon and then sprinkle the desired amount into your measuring cups to achieve the proper density.
Or just get a scale, they are cheap and work great. No reason in today's world to not have one.

I have been making these regularly and here are my tips: I leave the doughs in ziplocks on the counter for 2 hours, then refrigerate for 3-5 days. Freezing after day 3 also works fine. I take them out of the fridge in the morning the day I want to make them for dinner. I let the dough rest on the counter for an hour or two, then roll out to side. I have found that I need to roll them without flour on silicone mats, they need some grip to be able to roll out to 14 inches. Rolling them on parchment didn’t work for me, it just bunched up and wouldn’t lay flat. I let them cure uncovered for the day, about 8 hours. I bake them on a pizza steel. I think you don’t want to disturb the skin on the dough while fermenting or punch out the dough until you’re ready to use it.  Probably fine if you’re going to use both servings at the same time.
Love this dough recipe.  I wasn’t a fan of the sausage/gardenara pizza, thought it was too salty and greasy.  But his  has become my go-to dough recipe the any pizza I make!
@Scott it's actually a lot of yeast for it to rise in 3 hours. I noticed that I had pizza dough not rising appropriately, and my yeast was dead.
I’d love to use some of my ancient sourdough starter in this. Any advice for quantity of starter or additional tips?
Aren't the densities of kosher vs table salt different? Isn't 7 grams of table salt "saltier" than 7 grams of kosher salt?
Has anyone tried to convert this to a gluten free recipe? I’ve used the ATK flour blend and a ratio of 2tsp/120g xanthum gum to flour. But it seems dry in the kneading stage. Tips?
Why couldn’t the dough be par-baked at a low temp instead leaving it out to dry overnight? Trying this now…

This recipe seems incomplete.  After you go through the complete process, you end up with a pizza round in the early morning.  What are you supposed to do with a pizza crust at breakfast time?  I would appreciate clarification. Thanks. Jim

This is the one!  If you have been searching for a true thin and crispy crust this is perfect.   I followed the recipe and fermented for 4 days.  It was easy to roll out and dried perfectly and ready to use the next day.   You have to be a fan of cracker crust pizza because the crust could not be any thinner.   I am so excited to be able to ale this pizza on a regular basis.  Thank you!

YES. This dough is amazingly easy to work with, and the results are stellar. Based on comments I rolled the dough out at 7:30am, let dry all day, and baked at 6pm. Love that the recipe makes 2 boules at a time, so one can go in the freezer for future pizza. Thank you, Kenji!!
Pork turns quickly, I wouldn’t keep for a week in the fridge.  Portion out and freeze what you won’t use in a day.
Agree 100%.   The prepared sausage should sit in the fridge overnight to let the spices merge with the meat, then either use it or freeze it right away.

My father, a Chicago Italian with a mother born in Italy, said NEVER put garlic in your Italian sausage.  We always used fennel, salt, black pepper and red pepper flakes - nothing else.    We ground pork shoulder (multiples sometimes) and kneaded it for quite a while in a big stainless bowl until it got that sticky texture adding a few dribbles of ice water to assist the mix.  The rest of the process was pretty much the same.    Relatives conveniently stopped by to get a share!
The sausage was fantastic. Will make my own Italian sausage from now on. Why are any of us buying prepared sausage when it is this easy and sooo good?! And we know what is in it!

Since moving away from Chicago, I have really missed sausage tavern style pizza. This really scratches the itch. The texture is perfect. Although it is very tasty as it is written, I would agree that the addition of garlic is not necessary. Next time, I will reduce the cloves to 1/2 or leave out all together.
Made it as written with great results, but next time I’ll cut out the garlic.  It overpowers the fennel.
I love this recipe. I make a vegan version with Mushrooms. I cook them ahead of time so that they are not wet in the pizza.
Which cut(s) of pork are typically used in sausage (if not the shoulder)?

@Rachael  Kenji has mentioned in previous pork sausage recipes that you can use store-bought ground pork. You need a good fat ratio in pork sausage, something like 80% lean at most. I imagine store-bought ground pork varies in fat content from store to store, so some experimentation may be necessary.

When I make his Food Lab sausage, I grind a pork shoulder and add some pork belly for extra fat. There's a big difference in taste and feel with the extra fat.

Pork butt is another option.  I use the same cuts used for pulled pork.   I've even made sausage from a picnic that was on sale - had to trim the skin and it was much leaner than usual, but it came out ok.
A pound of meat for two pizzas?  Seems like it would completely cover the pizza in a very thick layer if not have leftovers?
This is everything I've been searching for ever since I left Chicago...
It's easier to just get sausage and add chopped fennel seed to it. I like Johnsonville sausage.

Of course you are right, but for me the point of making sausage at home is what is does not have in it, which in the case of Johnsonville is: dextrose, corn syrup, BHA, 'natural' flavors instead of actual herbs, and propyl gallate.
@Neal, the recipe for the pizza says 1/2 recipe of the pork mixture.

I'd have to agree with Antonine' s comments. Being in Chicago the standard for pizza sausage is as he stated just fennel, salt, black pepper and red pepper. That said, I did make this recipe and it was quite tasty but I just can't see all those flavors going well with pizza; there's just too much going on. I'm sure it would work well fried up with some peppers or made into a patty and served on a nice Italian roll.

Johnsonville mild pork sausage links (just take it out of the casing) is also just like Chicago Italian sausage. I have no idea if it has garlic, but it has fennel. The stuff that isn’t in links doesn’t taste the same.

This was fantastic sausage for the tavern pizza. One of the people eating this doesn’t normally like sausage on their pizza and they loved this version. I used half this recipe for one of the pizzas and then just cooked off the rest in a pan (fried little pieces in a bit of oil). Great on its own too.

Excellent sausage recipe. I used store bought ground Duroc pork and made small changes. For a half batch, I added 1 TB of red wine, reduced the salt to half tsp, and used a scant 1/4 tsp chili flake. It was definitely salty enough and only a little spicy. I let it rest for 6 hours, but overnight would be even better. Great on our pizza and also in stuffed poblanos with beans, onions and cheddar.

Sumptuous soup, although I could not find the Kobacha today so bought 3 large bags of frozen cubed butternut. Skipped the oven roasting and just put everything in a big pot on the stovetop until carrots were soft and then used the immersion blender.  Absolutely delicious.
Kabocha squashes, unlike butternut, has delicious edible peel.
Add an Ancho chili for a classic Southwestern take on this soup.

I don't understand how this can be made in 1 1/2 hours if the vegetables have to be in the oven for that long. It doesn't take into consideration the amount of time cleaning, slicing and also the blending. Add another hour to include prep time.
I make soup like this every winter. If you have leftovers, try adding some coconut milk and curry paste or powder.

I make soups like this all the time, and I am definitely going to try this method in other recipes.  One note about this one, though:  I loved roasting the squash with its skin.  After 1 1/2 hours it was certainly tender enough to chew, but my immersion blender couldn't handle it.  I ended up using a food processor, but if I had to do it again, I would either cut the squash in much smaller chunks or just peel it.
@KWH I roasted the squash skin on, then in the pot with the broth and after 1-1/2 hrs skin was very soft and I could use my immersion blender no problem.

If you click on the link of the story, this recipe came from, the whole point is using squash that doesn’t need to be peeled. I know Kabocha squash peel is edible when roasted, but I don’t think the soup pictured was made with peel on. I think there’d be a lot of green flecks in it but I’m gonna try making it and will let you know.

I made this yesterday as written with 2 unpeeled kabocha squash (@4 lbs.) and 2 pears. Roasted 90 minutes, then simmered in 5 cups veg broth 30 minutes more. After immersion blending, soup looked like green split pea soup. Added a cup of water to thin it - still thick though. Wonderful subtle flavor.

@frankeee kabocha squash is orange?  Why do you think it turned green?  
Hmmm

I make a very similar soup but with different spices: Madras curry, smokey paprika, cinnamon, salt, cayenne, and black pepper.I do peel the squash as those grown in my neck of the woods have a fibrous texture. I also simply toss the vegetables into a slow cooker with 1T olive oil and cook for 4 hours on high. I also finish with 1/2 cup of dairy, usually 1/2 and 1/2 or whole milk.
Okay, so it's not the same but it is very good!

I put it all as written except no olive oil ( I can’t tolerate fats) in the instant pot for 30 min then natural release.   Mashed then immersion blended k squash skin and all.  Took some tweaking with salt pepper orange juice    I added pear balsamic vinegar and pomegranate molasses too instead of the honey.  I thinned it out a bit with trader joe almond cashew macadamia milk    It made a lot of soup! I’ll do variations over the next few days adding cherry tomato.   Maybe topping with raw apple

I have to say that it gets even better in the fridge for a couple days.  I added shrimp, canned chopped clams and some ham with hearts of palm chopped up lasagna noodles (have you tried them?). Came out delicious.  I have been having for breakfast and lunch.
I don't get the peel. Is it really soft enough to blend and eat?
Absolutely! I've eaten a lot of kabocha skin. It's barely noticeable. I do scrape the pulp out of the skin in order to make prettier pumpkin pie.
@Anne yes but with more cooking time once on the stove top.
The soup in the picture was probably made with a kuri squash, which has a red skin.

This soup was delicious! Made a few tweaks. Used butternut squash and roasted everything on a sheet pan. Since I had no fresh apples, i used a few tablespoons of unsweetened apple sauce instead. I also used more stock since I prefer my soup less thick. Finished the soup off with a dollop of coconut milk. Will definitely make again.

After accidentally steaming a winter squash to mush years ago, I came up with a similar, easier, recipe.  Squash, water, onion, celery, ginger & spices went into the pot, plus some soymilk & a little peanut butter while blending it up. No caramelizing, no roasting, no sheetpan. It was so good that I brought it to a Thanksgiving dinner, where a friend prononounced it better than sex (best compliment ever!)  It's hard to go wrong with these ingredients. So happy to see this recipe here!! Enjoy!

Kabocha peel is definitely edible, but it muddies the golden glow of the finished soup. I made this recently (before seeing the recipe) and added chopped chestnuts — those that come roasted & ready to eat in foil-lined packets — and seasoned it with sage instead of thyme or rosemary. Sprinkled each serving with more chopped chestnuts drizzled with sage-infused butter. Splendiferous!

I’m in the process of making this now (the veggies are in the oven), but I was surprised at how hard the Kabocha squash was to cut. I used a very sharp 10” chef knife, but the hardness of the sqash and the lack of stability of a round vegetable make cutting it more difficult than peeling, cutting and cleaning butternut sqash.

Does anyone have any suggestions?
pop it in the microwave for 4 minutes before cutting.  Much easier

Disappointing that the food stylists won over a true representation of the recipe as written. Using peel on kabocha yielded a soup with with lots of bits (too big to be called flecks) in the soup. This was even using a Waring commercial immersion blender. The peel is tender and the soup was very good but it DOES NOT look like the accompanying image.

Not made it yet. Kabocha is very similar to Buttercup squash. I've grown both and have bought both many times, and cooked them regularly. To be honest, I am not sure about cooking and using the  skin. And I can't say that I've seen a skin difference between Kabocha and Buttercup. There is a delicious orange variety of Kabocha which might work well. Worth trying, but the green skinned Kabocha or old fashioned Buttercup would leave green flecks.

My partner made this following the recipe exactly. 50 minutes in the dutch oven at 375 didn't yield tender veggies. The squash was pretty much impossible to blend. We ended up simmering our veggies in stock on the stove for 15 minutes, then bleeding. Overall fine soup. Needs salt, and unclear where/when to salt. Probably won't make again, but definitely riffable.

This was amazing. I wasn’t planning squash soup in June but I couldn’t find kabocha squash at the grocer all winter…but suddenly they had them this week. I followed the recipe almost exactly as written (no honey/syrup needed and used a meyer lemon), used the last quart of thanksgiving turkey stock in the freezer, and roasted the squash seeds in olive oil and soy sauce to top the soup. It was worth interrupting June…with Nov. So tasty.

The soup is excellent. The method is a revelation! Just chop everything up and roast it in the dutch oven before adding stock and using the immersion blender. So easy!  I'm going to be experimenting with cauliflower, tomato and all kinds of soups. Thank you!

I made a similar soup today but roasted everything including the garlic and used sweet potatoes and carrots, using olive oil and Montreal steak mix which is my go-to and is amazing!!! Three white onions and celery were sauteed, then the roasted veg added plus smoked paprika, sea salt, paprika, and coconut milk. It totally rocks.
The idea to put coconut milk in this is brilliant!  I am going to use this!

This is an outstanding soup. I went with kuri (unpeeled) squash. In the accompanying article, Kenji says you don’t need to peel kuri or kobocha squash after roasting. They can purée right in. I found that using the vitamix was a more efficient way to blend the soup at the end than the immersion blender which had more trouble with the squash skin. Excellent soup.
Thin- skinned squashes can be roasted and eaten with the skin on. I do this with butternut often. Just roast till it’s very soft.

Delicious, easy, and forgiving - I made a smaller batch using two bags of frozen butternut, some frozen carrot coins, powdered ginger, dried rosemary, and one carton of stock. Had to let it roast a bit longer bc of the frozen veg. Creme fraiche and chili oil on top was also a good choice!
Definitely microwave the squash for five minutes to make it soft enough to cut. I poke some holes in it first-- not sure if that's necessary!

OMG, this soup is divine! Carving kabocha squash sent me to Urgent Care last year, however, so this season I got a tip from my CSA: cook the squash first, beginning in a cold oven on a sheet pan after poking holes all over it with a fork or point of a knife. Turn oven to 375 and bake for about 45 minutes. That makes it far easier to carve. Proceed with the recipe, which, as others have noted, takes longer than described. But it’s worth it!
Used butternut and it was fantastic . Big thanks to kenji for honoring our time, sanity , and humanity with this one pot recipe !

Very tasty and easy to make. This is the first squash recipe I've made that doesn't require peeling the squash - the skin gets roasted and blended with the other ingredients. If you use Kuri squash, the soup has beautiful bits of orange peeling.

Made this with precut butternut squash from Costco which saves time. Otherwise followed the recipe exactly.  Roasting adds wonderful depth of flavor.  Don’t skip it.  The mix of sweet and savory flavors is perfection!  My new favorite squash soup.

This was really good. I initially used a half tablespoon of salt and pepper, then added at least another half tablespoon of salt to balance the flavors. I also ended up using the juice of one whole lemon to brighten it up (1/2 was not sufficient).
Do you leave the peel on the squash?
Yup! "skin on and quartered"
Yes. The recipe calls for the squash to be "skin on and quartered..." as the first on the list of ingredients. I was also a bit confused about this.
I don’t see if you should or shouldn’t remove the peel
Seems like we’re all talking about skin. I too will roast and eat squash skins but wonder about how well they’ll blend up. I guess I’ll try it and find out.

I made this soup with butternut squash, but otherwise followed the recipe. I was surprised to find that it was very bland. Not sure why the nice aromas during roasting resulted in a such an unremarkable soup.
try more salt
What if you don’t blend it? Anyone try it without blending?
I think I would like it better unblended. Just cut everything into smaller pieces. Great idea!
Only had one small sugarpie pumpkin and half of a butternut on hand. Used those, no apple, everything else as directed. Soup came out wonderfully!
Parsnips, red lentils and miso all made nice additions. Paired with thickly buttered sourdough for a complete meal.

The kabocha squash has a pleasant, subtle earthy taste. It is a thick soup which we prefer when it is our main course for dinner. Round one was cooked according to the recipe. I could see adding some complementary spices to provide additional flavors. 

The kabocha skin cooked through with additional oven time and pureed with some specs of green remaining without impacting the smoothness of the soup.

So good!! I was skeptical about this soup because of the lack of cream/milk/etc and planned on adding some, but truly no need! 

I used butternut and acorn squash, left on the skins, and used an immersion blender. First time eating the skins on these and there’s no bitterness or texture issues. So much easier than peeling them!

I seasoned it with Cajun seasoning for a touch of spice and added triple ginger. It’s perfect and easy!

Made this as written and really enjoyed it. I used kabocha squash, but the color was not off putting to me - a sort of deep orange with a few dark green flecks. Roasted some chickpeas to use as a topping and make this a full meal. Would love more recipes using this ‘roasted soup’ technique!
90 minutes roasting was way too short.

Looks great and will definitely try it. But just a word of warning - I discovered the hard way that if you use an immersion blender in a Le Creuset Dutch oven your will permanently damage the enamel lining.
Should the ginger get pulled out with the herbs before blending?
Kenji is a genius.  Delicious and very easy.  One thing I would add is garlicky croutons from day old sourdough bread.

This is a truly genius soup strategy. I still roast the squash/pumpkin on a sheet pan, but do everything else in the Dutch oven. I use a lot of lime juice and add some curry powder at the end for more of a kick. If I end up with too much pumpkin for the batch so just freeze the slices for later.
Use less maple syrup

This is the easiest and best soup ever. I come back to it over and over again. I put al the ingredients on a sheet pan and roast at 400F. Usually the onions look a bit burnt—it’s all good. I don’t use herbs, but add curry powder, maple syrup, and lime juice. Serve with some crème fraiche. Everyone loves this soup.

I couldn’t find kombucha squash, so used buttercup. After baking, I cooled the squash on a rack and then used a grapefruit spoon to remove the squash from the peel. I forgot to add olive oil or herbs before baking. The flavor was remarkably complex and flavorful.

Brilliant!  I approached this like making stock: all languishing veggies go in. Peeled butternut squash, some red bell pepper, onion, a jalapeno, carrots, cooked potatoes,  I roasted them on a sheet pan to get optimal caramelization, then all into the stockpot. Left out the apple and sweetener but added a chipotle and a bit of adobo sauce for spice. The easiest delicious soup ever!

This was delicious.  I used roasted butternut squash since I grow it and dried apples because they were there.  The soup was quite hearty and had a lovely sweetness so I didn't bother with the maple syrup.  Definitely a keeper.  I don't see why this couldn't be made with leftover roasted veggies too.
So easy to make a big batch and freeze in individual portions! We add a little cream/milk too
Love this soup, and any recipe that maintains flavor while minimizing steps.

This was possibly one of the best soups I've made. I'd never used kabocha squash before and was dubious about cooking it with its peel. After roasting a little longer than 90 minutes the squash was tender and blended easily with the other vegetables. Try cutting the squash into eighths, and definitely roast the vegetables. Roasting brings out the depth of each vegetable's flavor. This soup is a winner!

This soup was easy and delicious. I used Kuri squash which is beautiful and bright orange. I liked not having to peel the squash and putting everything in one pot. The roasting made the soup so flavorful. It made a large amount so I had enough to give away and some to freeze.
Thanks for the great recipe.

This soup is excellent. I roasted the squash (butternut) separately, covered for 1 hour because I was afraid my immersion would not be able to purée the skin. Otherwise followed the recipe as written. Do not omit the lemon juice. It really takes it to a different level.
My go-to work soup for the fall and winter. Delicious and filling. Add a crusty roll and I don’t need to worry about the afternoon munchies.
Love this soup! Didn’t add maple syrup (apples sweeten it enough).
I actually found this to be a bit bland. I added much more salt and pepper, cinnamon, nutmeg, cardamom, pumpkin spice… I just kept adding until it was flavorful.

We really enjoyed this! I baked the whole kabocha for 10 minutes before cutting it - softened it up just enough that I could get the job done. Our squash was a homegrown variety selected for sweetness so no maple syrup required.

The soup remained a lovely deep orange despite the green squash being cooked and pureed with the exterior skin on. The ginger was the hardest thing to get to puree up - might chop it into smaller pieces next time.

I made this with butternut squash -- more work because the skin has to be removed -- but well worth the effort. The ginger as called for in the recipe is not nearly enough -- I used a much larger amount. I diced several red potatoes, leaving the skins on, which thickened the soup. I used vegetable stock to soften the squash. After the vegetables were all cooked, I threw it all, batch by batch, in the blender. This makes a lot of soup, so use a large pot! Delicious alongside crusty bread.
Made it exactly as in the recipe although my Dutch oven is a bitt too small.  Absolutely delicious soup.  Used green kabocha squash this time but have a red one for the next batch.
I make a similar recipe using butternut squash.
Yes kabocha and Japanese style Kuri squash skins are edible, unlike other in the hard squash family, but feel free to peel if you prefer
Is the weight estimate for the squash including the seeds and pulp?
I suspect the pictured soup was made with the kuri squash, giving it that lovely hue.
We use butternut squash.  All our grocery stores have it in abundance.
Why is there no timing for leaving the soup on the stovetop at medium low heat?

I love puréed soups, and I love squash, so I thought this was going to be a favorite. Unfortunately, it was completely vile. I used kabocha squash and was skeptical of leaving the peel on, as it seemed very tough. After pureeing, the soup was a very unappetizing color, but more unfortunately, it tasted awful.

I made the soup today and just tasting it, I felt it needed something like more sweet. It is a vile color; it is the peel or the blackened bits in the pot? I used Kabocha squash. The pictures in the paper were so lovely!
Would a delicata stand in well enough for the kabocha squash/
We make this squash soup faster and easier in a Vitamix blender we bought 8 years ago at Costco.
Delicious, adaptable, loved it more than the sheet pan version.

Please include the chopping time. 
This was sweeter than I expected but I still enjoyed it. I did use butternut squash because that is what I had. It smelled amazing while cooking. And I used a masher I stead of a blender because I wanted it chunkier. 

Whoever suggested pear balsamic vinegar, wish I had seen that!
So could I roast everything but the squash and then add a can of pumpkin?
I think I'll soften everything in the microwave first then put on one or two sheet pans in a single layer for baking.  Ought to really cut down on the time.
I grow red kuri squash and can highly recommend it's use if you can find it.
This is a nice healthy soup.
Pretty forgivable and riffable, I found the texture improves if you have time to pass it through a sieve, but not necessary!
I used pumpkin, that I peeled - soup was lovely!

I made this soup tonight using a kabocha squash. I cut the squash up in smaller pieces than what the recipe called for. After roasting the vegetables and fruit (2 cored, chopped up Granny Smith apples), I moved the Dutch oven to our stove top, added both boiling water and 1 can of coconut milk with its cream to the pot, then cooked the soup 30  minutes or so until the squash was tender. I used our immersion blender to puree it. The soup was very flavorful and colorful. We love this recipe.
I use my SS cleaver to cut tough squash.
Microwaving the whole kabocha for 4-5 minutes makes it super easy to slice.

I used ordinary butternut squash, nuked for 4 mins (fork-pricked skin in a few places) and then quartered it, cleaned it out then easily peeled off the skin. Also: Carrots take way longer to roast through than squash, so make sure to chop them into about 1 in cubes. Apples also took surprising long, but not as long as carrots.

I made it with delicata squash, because that's what I had on hand. Wonderful! After making it to recipe using the orange juice option (though with less broth), I added peanut butter, ground cardamom, ground chipotle chili, apple cider vinegar & lime juice. Turned out great!

Maybe it’s a little ‘’tedious’ to make roasted squash soup by roasting the squash and separately sauteeing the onions, carrots, etc., but this is just mediocre soup by comparison with a slightly more work-intensive way. It’s lazy soupmaking and the outcome demonstrates that.
Because I used delicata, I needed to leave out the maple syrup. Delicata is so sweet.
Easy to prepare. Easy to cook. Easy to serve. Easy to clean up - and delicious as well! What more could you ask? Followed recipe closely and used vegetable broth as liquid.

A lovely soup for a cold winter’s evening. The work of the soup is the chopping — the squash, onions, the apple, the ginger, the carrots. Then it’s in the oven roasting for the hour plus, followed by a couple-minute blitz with the immersion blender. Easy. And yes, to some of the questions, the squash is skin-on, and blends in nicely. I used delicata squash, another squash with edible skin. Yum. Oh and this made a ton of soup.
I have never seen kabocha or kuri squash in any store.

As a first step, I just throw the squash onto a sheet pan for 20-45 minutes at 350 depending on its size and my plans for it. Then I can proceed to easily slice dice or peel without struggle. Would not go back to cutting raw squash again!

Just took veggies out of the oven and while the butternut squash was tender, the carrots were not. So I am simmering on stove till they are. Like the one pot idea and ingredients. Added yogurt and nutmeg after blending. Delish!

This recipe was not a favorite for me and my wife. First, I discovered that I do not like the taste of kabocha squash as much as butternut or acorn squash. I suppose this would work with either of those or maybe kuri squash. The second problem was that I used the full 4.5 pounds of kabocha squash instead of the lower 3.5 pounds. This gave the soup the consistency of sludge and made the appearance unappetizing. I cut the leftovers with another 2 cups of chicken stock which helped.
This recipe was deelish - as is! I followed the recipe exactly and was very pleased with the result on a cold, windy February day. Thank you! Will make again soon.

Made this with butternut squash, and just roasted the squashes for an hour at 375° on a baking sheet. Put the rest in the pot on the stove, then added the butternut squash, skins and all, into the pot with the broth and broke it up with a spoon, simmered for a bit, then batched it into a blender and returned to the pot for a few minutes. 

Seasoned with dry sage, thyme, and rosemary throughout, and it turned out really good. Only did a tablespoon of honey as the butternut is sweet enough.
Thanks for the recipe Kenyi! So simple and delicious.

Kabocha skins were far too hard to blend in, even after roasting for an extended time with water poured over occasionally. I added the stock and boiled on stovetop then scraped off the squash from the skins. Recipe made a lot and I’m not in love with it!

This was quite wonderful! I think it is a pretty forgiving recipe. I had only one squash, I used dried thyme, I barely chopped the carrots and apples, and it came out great. It was very thick, and I'm wondering if some coconut milk to thin it might be a welcome variation.
This soup got thrown away. The kabocha skin made it bitter. There was no taste despite my doubling (and tripling) the spices. Embarrassing to present.
better than maple syrup for a drizzle? Pumpkin seed oil. Or a trifecta of olive oil, pumpkin seed oil, and good balsamic.

I popped my 3  Lb kabocha squash in the microwave for 6 minutes and made it much easier (and safer) to cut. I cut it into smaller wedges than quarters. I roasted it for about 1 hour. was surprised that I didn’t have to simmer it with broth after it came out of the oven. I added dried thyme. To the reader who questioned the 1.5 hour estimated cooking time, I think it helps to microwave the squash first, cut into smaller wedges and just blend it without simmering.
Excellent! Made the recipe as shown using kabocha. Sprinkled a tiny bit of cumin.Used juice of 1/2 a lemon, didn’t use maple syrup. Used thyme. Next time will try rosemary.

I thought this was easy and delicious! I think the key is patience with the roasting- I used an entire hour and a half to roast the veggies. Amazingly complex for a simple soup. No issues with the peel of the squash. Lots of leftovers that will be fun to play with- different spices, coconut milk, etc
Excellent and easy. One person suggested adding an ancho chili…

After some tinkering, I think I will enjoy this.  I roasted all of the ingredients on a sheet pan, at least 1.5 hrs.  I should have microwaved the squash as others have suggested.  Glad I peeled.   I don't think my little immersion blender would have handled the skin.  Added more chix broth than called for.  Added an ancho chile, chipotle chili powder, a bit of cumin and a tiny pinch of clove.  Added the maple syrup.

Unfortunately, this was not the one pot wonder I had high hopes for.

The microwaving of the squash is an extremely important step to making the prep-work a little less burdensome. I’m grateful to the commenters for that tip. This was a tedious and pain-staking process for me, and with an end result that just was meh. There wasn’t quite a “hero flavor” here as the kabocha squash was chalky and bland. I didn’t blend all the way to leave some pieces for a variety of texture—very much regret that.
No syrup needed. Roasting brings out the sweetness of the squash and fruit. It’s delicious.

A comforting and simple soup. The magic of this soup is multi-faceted: 1)  You must roast the veggies in oven, it deepens and blends taste. 2)  lemon, maple syrup and some Maldron salt at the end are transformative, and, 3) Use high quality, home-made if possible stock.

Made a small version with an Acorn squash and a sweet potato. Stuck to a proper proportion of carrots, onion and apple, but had no fresh ginger. It was great as soon as I blended it, but I "elevated" it with a bit of bacon grease for flavor and body. Ate the first bowl standing up - so delicious.  And even though I down-sized the recipe, I'll be eating well for a few days. Thank you!

We had Tutume squash that we didn’t pick early enough for it to behave like summer squash. It was the size of a small pumpkin and a very tough orange skin.  We cut it into wedges, seeded it, and added a sliced onion and a large handful of baby carrots. Roasted as instructed in a Dutch oven 1.5h. Once cooled in fridge, we peeled it, added a heavy tsp of ground ginger, a 1/2 can of coconut milk, 3 c water, and another tap of salt. Whizzed with immersion blender. It was good.
Drizzle it with pumpkin seed oil instead of olive oil.

Delicous! Didn’t use the sweet (honey/syrup) or the spices (thyme/rosemary) but not on purpose - just got everything else down and it came out perfect so didn’t need to add. Honestly I thought I would need to add some cream or heavy cream like other squash soups. Additionally, for some reason my supermarket didn’t have fresh ginger tonight, so I used the jarred garlic/ginger I get from the Indian market and roasted a teaspoon of each in the “cup”of the quartered squash and it was heavenly.
Does anyone know of a better way to slice into some of these squash other than with a chain saw?
my suggestion would be to skip the ginger as it overpowered and distracted from the great squash taste.
On cutting kabocha and similar dense squash . . . I had a 3.5 lb kabocha . . . I used a cleaver and a rubber mallet on a cutting board. Worked brilliantly!

Riffs: I roasted at a higher temperature to get things done faster, with no ill effects. Use a potato masher after taking the pot out of the oven before using the immersion blender. Add very tiny amounts of broth at a time. Used the juice of a whole lemon. Added just a touch of heavy cream when everything else is done. Serve with toasted, salted squash seeds to put on top. I enjoy garnishing with a few dots of Frank's hot sauce; I can see adding a dab of yogurt or sour cream as well.
Also: add six or so cloves of garlic to the roasting veggies. Oh, my.

11/23 Made as directed except one apple. Sweet is not a favorite flavor. Amazing process. The pot gets the brown roasted sweetness on the bottom and the broth immediately absorbs it.I roasted the squash 10" longer as the squash skin was not quite done. Used a little broth to deglaze and used immersion blender and added broth until got desired consistency.  So delicious. Will add a little lemon juice before serving-no honey. Did add ginger but not herbs
Put the whole kabocha in the oven for 5-10 minutes to soften a bit for chopping (adjusting your roasting time) or use a cleaver.

Add to Dutch ovens baking:
Cut back squash to 1/2 to 2/3
Leave same amount onion 
Double ginger
Add one chopped,deseeded hot pepper, like Serrano
Add heaping spoon yellow curry, like hot Jamaica curry or penzys yellow

Once blended, in addition to stock:
Add rice vinegar
Lemon juice
Cut back on honey added

Garnish
Roasted coconut shredded, fish sauce, sweetened rice wine vinegar, olive oil
Ba
Roast at 425 until lightly toasted, be careful not to burn

Thank you, JKL-A for wonderful technique!! Gorgeous & delicious prior to blending portion; next time I'll leave half as is & add white beans before wilting spinach in broth. New batch turkey stock, small cut rutabaga, golden beet, Gold Rush apple, butternut, sweet potato, onion & thyme. 1/4 c cider not advised; sweet enough without. I highly recommend trying the one-pot oven roasting method which is the point.
Plus good farm carrot
The recipe calls for 6 cups of liquid and orange juice, which made the soup very watery. I would recommend less or reduce it down which would increase the cook time

Used a peeled butternut squash, 4 smallish carrots, 2 yellow onions, 1 pear, a 2”x1/2” piece of fresh ginger, 4 cups of vegetable stock   about 1cup of water: the pear and ginger makes this soup sing!

I made this with 2 unpeeled kabocha squashes (4 1/2 lbs) and only one apple; otherwise, I followed the recipe as written.Roasted for about 80 minutes, at which time the squash was very tender, but the peel felt firmer than I'd expected. I knew an immersion blender couldn't handle the pureeing, so I used my Vitamix. The resulting mixture was incredibly viscous and an unattractive khaki color. I didn't care for the taste either. Had to add lots of water, but the mouth feel was still unpleasant.

Made this exactly as written with kabocha squash. It came out excellent. I blended it in a Vitamix until  there were no more green flecks of skin and the soup was still orange. Didn’t find the maple syrup to be necessary.

I intend to make this in my Instant Pot!  It seems to me that, after roasting the squash, (I’m probably going to buy some precut cubes), I will use the sauté function for the veggies, and then pressure cook for about 7-10 minutes, tops!  If it’s too thin, I’ll use a cornstarch slurry. 
I will post the results here.
Used HALF of a small Hubbard squash. Halved, seeded and peeled.

I made this with butternut squash. Tested the peel after roasting and it seemed soft enough to blend and eat. Chickened out and discarded the skin after scooping the flesh out. Will just peel and quarter the butternut next time. It was a fussy job to get the flesh off the skin after roasting.

Made in my toaster over, halving the recipe and cutting the squash into smaller pieces. All went well, but the color of the soup is unappealing, like green pea soup, and a bit too sweet. I'll play around with the leftovers today and add coconut milk and other spices.
does the skin stay on? If not, when is it removed?

Love this one-pot method! I didn’t have fresh herbs so used dry herbs, but added coconut milk, paprika, cayenne, and a tiny bit of fish sauce…I also happened to have half a cabbage in the fridge, so I cut that up to roast at the same time as the other veggies. Putting that in at the end gives the otherwise smooth soup some texture
Maybe a bit tart as I prepared it - I used orange juice not lime juice.  Next time will add a bit of brown sugar or maple syrup to counter.
I was so skeptical of this recipe - blending in the skins of the squash but I gave it a try and amazing!  I followed the recipe and I thought it was delicious as presented. Super easy.

I followed the recipe.  I love that everything gets thrown into the pot.  So easy to make.  The only thing I would do differently is omit the maple syrup.  I don’t think (for me) that it is needed.  With the pear or apple it doesn’t need anymore sweetness.

An easy to make soup of remarkable depth. I added 4 cloves of garlic and doubled the amount of ginger which built more layers of rich flavor. An instant favorite with a sure place in the winter rotation.

Delicious!  But next time I would roast the veg on a sheet pan — it took closer to 2 hours (and upping the oven temp to 425) for me to get the butternut squash I used to the point of tenderness.  I made mine with duck stock, which made it extra rich and velvety.  My husband appreciated the bits of texture from the apple skin, onions, carrots, etc.  I’ll definitely make this again.
Don’t use a vitamix for this. I did and it ended up super gummy. I should I used the immersion blender

You must peel the kabocha squash for this recipe. Even after long roasting, an immersion blender can't handle the skin, and if you leave it on, the soup is a horrible color.  Otherwise, a wonderful recipe. I've used homemade chicken stock, vegetable broth, and water at different times, and the chicken stock is best
Rather than using a bowl, use a large measuring cup with a a handle and spout -- it is much easier to control adding the eggs to the pan that way.
Delicious made with half and half. Fewer calories than cream, butter or olive oil… I think!
I second @Tim O.’s suggestion. I poured the whisked eggs into the pan through a sieve, which also helped the texture.
The best scrambled eggs are to add cream cheese and a little milk to beaten eggs. Cook in butter in a double boiler over low heat stirring constantly.

I agree! I add 1.5 tbd whipped cream cheese to 3 eggs. Whisk well. You will still have some lumps. Melt 2pats butter in small non stick pan on medium low heat. Add egg mixture. Continuously stir with silicone spatula. Add salt, pepper and grated cheddar if you wish. Eggs will slowly expel moisture and cook throughly while staying very soft. It takes a whopping 10 minutes of continuous stirring to cook but these eggs are creamy, delicious and just about foolproof.

The brown butter version takes this from great to you’ve got to be kidding great.  It’s rich but not cloying.  The salt, herbs and rest prior to cooking are all important to bring out the best   

I love the silky texture of scrambled eggs after growing up on dried and over cooked basic eggs.

I guess the idea here is to replace to usual butter or olive oil with heavy cream. Worth trying. But I did not love the resulting flavor. And - though I may have blown the execution - I would not call the resulting texture "velvety".

Don’t listen to the haters who never actually cooked the recipe. This is the easiest and tastiest way to make velvety eggs, even easier than Kenji’s other method with corn starch. I only used the initial heavy cream to start and didn’t feel like we needed more the second time I tried it. Definitely adjust to your runny-ness preference. Awesome way to make eggs and I’ll never go back!

Made this as written and it was exactly how Kenji describes it here - velvety. Great scrambled egg variation that after making once becomes very easy to eye ball. Added to the rotation! 

Another favorite for anyone interested is Ruhlman’s Soft Scrambled Eggs with Chive and Goat Cheese.

Lactose intolerant here! I had some coconut cream open in the fridge and decided to give it a go, fully expecting the end result would be too coconutty. So glad I experimented! Yes, you can taste a faint coconut flavour, but the texture of the eggs is incredible and this recipe feels very forgiving if you get distracted for a few seconds too long. Will be making this again.
Heavy cream is quite low in lactose.

@Steve P 
Intolerant means "no", not just a little bit. But I occasionally find lactose free half and half.

While I followed the recipe pretty exactly and the results were resplendent, I am not sure whether I really got the hang of Kenji’s technique … or just made incredibly rich, slow-simmered eggs. (Delicious, sure, but not the point of this recipe.) This is the kind of recipe where it would help to have a video, to see what the “egg ribbon” is meant to look like and how vigorously the cream should be simmering. Content gods, take note!
Make this recipe! Just made it for the first time using half & half and fresh chives (I reduced to about 1 tbsp) and the eggs turned out exactly as pictured and absolutely delicious.
Made this recipe nondairy with oatmilk, vegan cream cheese and a little bit of oil. First time eating soft scrambled eggs like this. Delicious!
First time making these fabulous scrambled eggs and it definitely won’t be the last! So good! Thanks for this wonderful recipe!!!
Made as directed,using crème fraiche.  Came out perfectly.  It was delicious, and very rich.
Always take eggs off heat a few seconds before they finish cooking as their own heat will continue to cook them.

I just made these and they are tasty. My skillet may have been a little too hot so the eggs set a bit too much in the skillet in the 15 seconds before I started stirring. 

I would switch the titles of the two Kenji López-Alt scrambled egg recipes. With all the cream in this recipe, this should be the Extra Creamy Scrambled Eggs, and the other recipe (with the butter and corn starch) should be the Velvety Scrambled Eggs.
This was not good. Texture and taste resembled undercooked scrambled eggs; sensation was that was going to make myself ill. However, cats liked them just fine.

Recipe called for table salt, so I used standard Morton’s.  That must have been wrong.  I usually like a lot of salt, but these were way too salty…. Basically inedible.  Which was a shame, because otherwise they seemed good.  Will try again with modification.
Yeah, surprised at the amount of salt in this. In the author's Food Lab scrambled egg recipes, he uses half this amount, which is still pretty salty.

had a bit of extra heavy cream in the fridge, so i decided to give these a try. good — but not as good as kenji’s extra creamy scrambled eggs with the cornstarch and cold butter. for me, those remain the gold standard.
are these on fresh tortillas and it was the highlight of my day. thanks Kenji!!

Read about it yesterday and made it today. Even with 3 eggs and slightly reduced salt/herbs it was stunning. Threw in some leftover grilled salmon and minced green onions and had it with homemade focaccia - what a treat

for those complaining about the number of steps: if you put some kind of oil in the pan and let it heat before cooking, how is that different from putting cream in the pan and heating it? Scramble the eggs in the bowl, with some salt and pepper- how is that an extra step? Wait- that's right- you have to let it sit for 15 minutes! That's the extra step!!

Made them this afternoon (who doesn't love eggs for lunch?) and was quite impressed. Very creamy!  I mixed half-and-half with a bit of cream, added some crumbled feta to the end of the cooking.  Omitted additional cream drizzle..  One thing: the herbs release water when they cook and break down, so next time I might just reserve them for on top.
