I grow microgreens and happened to have dill and chervil on hand  - they added a lot of flavor, and proabably were less watery than herb leaves.

Too much salt!
Taste wise ended up tasting quite salty.

Probably just use 1/4 teaspoon next time and see how that is. 

Made exactly per the recipe (with chives).

My favorite way to make scrambled eggs! The only thing I changed was using a saucepan instead of a skillet. My BIL is Chinese-Canadian & has always used a pan. The results are always pillowy curds. Perhaps it makes the technique easier.

All you need for scrumptious soft scrambled egg is hot melted butter   4-8 eggs (nr depending on both the size of the crowd and of the pan)   salt to taste. No need to beat eggs beforehand: just crack them quickly onto hot butter, add salt, and stear yolks well into whites with a silicone spatula; a charming melange ensues. Do not overcook - take the pan off as soon as the eggs are well mixed trough but not solidified. Continue stearing, then plate out. Top with finely chopped chives if desired.
We call these “frybled eggs” in our house.
Thanks. This was fun and easy. Made it for the companion and me, with fresh chives, thyme (and Trout eggs caviar for moi) and the last few slices of challah toasted. Hit the spot.
Adding some more extra cream at the end is a really clever trick.
This is all about technique. Keep trying, it's worth it.

I scaled it down to 2 eggs, which meant I probably used a little more cream.  I had some chopped parsley and tarragon.  Very delicious and velvety as billed.  Served it with my wife’s holiday stollen.  What at great way to start the new year!
Wonderful recipe, Kenji.  Almost quiche-like in texture.  By accident, we had to reheat in Chef Mike, and they were still perfect.
Very nice, creamy, don't be shy with the herbs! Maybe a touch of hot sauce.

Fun New Year’s Day change to the normal. I think we could do a 1/4 of salt or switch to kosher and the full 1/2 tsp. Really tasty and great thing to do with that little bit of heavy cream left from the holidays.
Agreed. I loved them bu the table salt made my batch too salty.
This were very velvety, maybe more than I liked but next time will try with browned butter. It is just nice to add this style to the menu!

On the browned-butter eggs, when should the butter be added? There is no butter in the original recipe. And why would it be necessary to cool down the pan with cream before adding the eggs? The original recipes calls for heating the cream before adding the egg. And I don’t have an 8-inch cast iron pan or a carbon steel pan, only stainless steel in that size.

I have been known as the queen of scrambled eggs … grin. I love this version and will try it. My version which has many less calories and is much easier. In a well buttered, nonstick fry pan, I add 2 eggs that were beaten with 1 tablespoon of milk. When the butter foams, I lower it immediately and cook slowly so that they stay creamy and they don’t brown.
The best scrambled eggs I have ever had!  Delicious
There are 4 Tbls in a 1/4 cup.

These live up to the hype! Perfection. 

(I only had half & half in the fridge, so that’s what I used, but I can tell cream would be rad.)
Very nice, but 1/2 tsp of table salt was way too salty! !/4 tsp of table salt or Diamond Crystal would have been better- will try that the next time
Sea salt would be much  less salty tasting.  I have been using it for years and haven't had a "too salty" since I started.
Thanks. Very good. I'll add herbs and feta next time.

WOW! First time responder.
Only used whole milk and butter. No heavy cream on hand. Used fresh thyme and snipped chives. Otherwise cooked as per recipe. Easiest thing I ever made and absolutely delicious. No going back to other methods after this. Ten stars.

Just prepared this now—I opted for scallions as the add-in as that's all I had on hand.

This recipe immediately hurtled towards a tied* first place position for favorite breakfast eggs, over the course of, what, fifteen minutes prep and cooking time? And finally a convenient, why-didn't-I-think-of-that use for leftover heavy cream!

*The other recipe occupying this top spot is Cantonese-style scrambled eggs—peep the YouTube channel Chinese Cooking Demystified for a great recipe.

Tried this exactly as the recipe states, delicious. I make a lot of eggs, omelettes, etc. So I was surprised how differently the eggs taste with that bit of creme fraiche. I use that a lot too, in all kinds of things. The transformation is stunning. Really delish.
Dill can be used as well, if one does not have sour cream we found plain full fat greek yogurt was an acceptible substitute

All these variations of how to cook scrambled eggs crack me up (no pun intended). Keep it simple, flavor however you want with herbs, etc. but the important thing is technic. A pad of butter is all you need in your pan to prevent sticking. A wisk is your best friend. Beat the eggs with the wisk in a bowl first then scramble in the pan with the wisk. Key is low heat, and constant wisking. I have an induction range and it makes it easy to regulate temp and avoid overcooking.

Another home run for Kenji! Made as directed with brown butter plus half and half. Texture and taste over the top. As I finished the plate, I added a dollop of Kenji’s immersion blender mayonnaise (a true miracle) just to get an idea of egg salad as he suggests. Can’t wait to do a large batch and keep in fridge until I devour it all.
Can you make this with whole milk instead of cream?

If you were to add bite sized pieces of lox (smoked salmon) to this recipe would this be done at the beginning when adding the herbs to the mix, or at the end when pouring the remaining cream and stirring vigorously?
I would add lox at the end - you don't really want to cook it.
Doubled this recipe as written and the eggs were outstanding - creamy and delicious. Will cut back on the salt next time as 1/2 tsp of table salt (for a doubled recipe) is a bit much.

I tried but found the recipe bland and not really a great way to bring out the true if subtle deliciousness of farm fresh eggs. Sixty years ago my farm wife aunt made the best cream-enhanced scrambled eggs by simply beating a couple of straight-from-the-hen eggs with a few tablespoons of straight-from-the-milk-separator thick cream and stirring the mix briefly into a hot buttered frypan. Both main ingredients enhanced each other while, imho, this recipe brings out the best in neither.
A nice change from standard scrambled eggs. I'll make this recipe a few times a year.

Variation on the cold cream addition, combining others wisdom:  I put well fluffed/beaten eggs (with a dash of milk in them as desired) in to pan on low-ish heat that has barely bubbling butter (not too much).  Poor in eggs, add salt and pepper as desired.  Keep temp as is and be patient.  Gently keep fluffing the sides as they set. Add dollops of creme fraiche (how much depends on preference).  Fold gently.  (I also love to then chopped fresh chives).  Heat off right away.
Made them with non-dairy almond/coconut milk creamer. Delicious! Can’t wait to try the egg salad with the leftovers.

In the accompanying article, he browns 1 Tbs heavy cream cream rather than 1 Tbs butter; odd that there's no mention of the difference. Seems like you'd get more of the browned flavor, since that comes from milk solids.

Made these as written. Delicious and easy (once I saw how it works). I left some in the pan to test the claim that they work as leftovers and even cold from the pan, my wife, who doesn't care much for eggs at all, declared them a winner.
A bit too salty but otherwise DELICIOUS!
Eh … they were OK? But I wouldn’t say velvety, and the flavor was nothing special. I may try once more, because Kenji rarely misses.

I’m going to be the annoying one! Scrambled eggs can be velvety and creamy without adding anything other than egg (and some butter for the bottom of the pan). Just don’t overcook them! Be brave and remove from heat before they look finished. You won’t need any cream or milk at all.
I cooked this Recipe with a little twist. Added butter before I added the cream in the end. Came out very good.
Good but not great. Not the epiphany I was hoping for.

I did not do this perfectly but it still was quite successful. Half and half instead of cream. Timing: the melted butter and cream lingered for a while, as I waited for the toast to catch up. Very good in all the variations, I am sure.

I made these eggs this morning exactly as instructed, and they were spectacular. I halved the recipe, using two high-quality eggs, and used snipped fresh dill and parsley as my herbs of choice. I found them to be quite velvety! This would be a good company breakfast. I wonder if multiplying the recipe would work as well as halving it did?
In the notes Lopez-Alt states that it can be doubled in a 10-inch skillet.
Excellent tip with the browned butter. Thank you J.Kenji Lopez-Alt.

After trying multiple methods over the years from various chefs & America’s Test Kitchen, I find that the most consistent and easiest method for light and fluffy scrambled eggs is to heat up one tablespoon of avocado (or other neutral oil) for every 4 eggs in a good nonstick pan over medium heat for 3 mins or until starting to smoke and then swirling and gently folding eggs until curds form and eggs are slightly wet looking. Add salt prior and total cook time is a minute or less.

I think I am missing something.  It calls for. 1/4 of cream which would be 3 Tablespoons.  It says to cook down 3 tablespoons and add the eggs slowly.   Then when eggs are cooked, slowly add the remaining cream.  I don’t see that there is any remaining cream.
A quarter cup is 4 tablespoons.
1/4 cup is four tablespoons
@Zed I thought a quarter cup was three tbs! Oops…
Melt butter then a little cream cheese in pan. Voila!
This is an incredibly easy recipe and serves up great, creamy eggs. Didn't add herbs but followed the recipe to the letter otherwise. Thank you, Kenji!

Absolutely decadent!!  Just loved the creamy deliciousness comforting me after I took down the Christmas tree.   Ate this with oatmeal toast slathered with butter and fresh fig preserves I am a child about Christmas and comfort food.  Thank you !

The trick is to stir/fold, fold/stir over VERY low heat. You may even remove the pan from the heat for a few seconds at a time. I like to add finely grated parmesan. Make sure your serving plates are warmed in the oven. The miracle of French creamed eggs is that they hold the heat for so long--they're like a warm hug for your mouth!
I find doing it very quickly over medium heat using olive oil produces the best results.
To everyone who has discovered that cream is too rich: After several tries, I now use whole milk and add the grated parmesan--they still turn out wonderfully creamy and rich tasting!
Camilla rocks!
Made this today after first sauteing some onions in sherry and also adding bits of chopped lox. It was the creamiest, most decadent lox-eggs-onions I've ever had. Truly a new years' treat.

I wanted a simple but delicious dinner, so I made these velvety eggs as written, except I used less eggs, less cream and skipped the added cream at the end. Fresh herbs from the garden, a pinch of Diamond Crystal Kosher salt and freshly ground pepper. A piece of sourdough bread toasted, spread with Earth Balance and fig jam. As I hoped, it was simple and delicious! I'll definitely make it again and again.
I stir in a teaspoon of potato starch to create the most wonderfully fluffy scrambled eggs!
had some leftover heavy cream, so i gave these a try. good — but i actually prefer his version of scrambled eggs with the cornstarch and cold butter.

I use olive oil. There is something about the chemical reaction of the two together that produces fluffy, tender scrambled eggs (no other oil seems to do it). Just use an inverted spatula to push the eggs around until they are just barely done. Wait 3 minutes to let them continue to cook on a warmed plate and you have perfect eggs.
Does anyone have a go-to dairy-free sub for heavy cream for this recipe?

NutPods Original creamer (the unsweetened one) is the best heavy cream substitute I've found, without the overwhelming sweetness of coconut milk alone (I think it's a blend). I've used it for cream sauces, quiches, etc, though not sure how it simmers on its own!
Lots of fun to make and very tasty - thank you for the recipe!

One of the comments suggested using ricotta. I tried this: I wisked 5 jumbo eggs while warming 1 tbs of butter and 1 tbs (heaping) soft whole milk ricotta in a seasoned iron crepe pan on very low. Keeping the heat low, pour in the egg and, with patience, slowly scramble until they JUST come together loosely, no more, as the cooking will continue on the plate a bit. Serves two.
 It was an amazing success. Don't forget to heat your plate ( 1 minute +/- in the micro) before making this.
After a busy day. I made these for myself then my husband when he arrived home. I threw a little cheese on top of these beauties. Yummy, yum, yum.
I miss the butter.

I'm not sure what went wrong (perhaps my stove temperature?), but I'd describe the results as "mushy," rather than velvety. I prefer preparing scrambled eggs the more conventional way and adding cream cheese. But worth a try! Others have loved this, so might be just me...
I made the brown-butter version but with whole milk instead of cream, for less richness. Absolutely delicious.
Use half the amount of salt and omit the final tablespoon of cream and these are fine.

i'll never make scrambled eggs any other way! What a treat! AND so easy. I used 3 eggs and 3 T cream but skipped the final T of cream, couldnt wait to taste. Next time I'll try it with 2 eggs just for me. And crispy bacon from the microwave makes a high protein & fat dish no gluten.

I just made-- adding finely minced ham and an extra egg. The eggs were so tender and silky, although my pan and simmering cream must have not been hot enough somehow, because I had to wait a solid 2 minutes or so before I could fold the eggs. Delicious. Will make again soon!

I’m wondering if the call for table salt in this recipe is an error? The texture and creaminess of these eggs were excellent, but they were very salty and I will definitely adjust (either the amount or type) next time.
Use sourdough bread - freshly sliced - for the toast. Make it crunchy and with whipped butter before scooping the velvety eggs on top.

Best special occasion scrambled eggs ever!
Made for new year 2023, topped with a dab of crème fraiche, caviar & chives.
So I had 6 egg yolks left over from baking Dorie Greenspan's Tiger Cake, and thought:  would it be just too decadent to do this recipe only with egg yolks???  Nope!  :-)
I made this- i think- and liked it. However the technique is so different I'm not sure that I made it as intended. A video would be helpful.
I like adding yogurt to eggs cooked in olive oil to reduce the amount of water one gets from butter. A slash of cold cream at the end  plus any special herbs
Quite a pleasant surprise, though not “velvety.” Smooth and goes especially well with Herbes de Provence with goat cheese. I plan to adapt this to omelettes.
I don't do cream, but used a little oil and butter swirled, added nonfat milk and a bit of buttermilk, then the eggs, and result was tender, delicious, and, for me,  more healthy.  Yum.

I love this method. Delicious and wonderful tender texture. Easy to adjust number of servings: 1 T cream per egg. Save out 1/4 of the cream for the final drizzle. I used a rubber spatula to turn and mix.
I used whole milk, as I had it on hand, and added sautéed onions and wilted greens (baby spinach and arugula) just as the eggs came out of the pan. The eggs were amazing(:

Made this with butter and buttermilk. Added spinach, tarragon, and black pepper to the beaten eggs. I wasn't quite sure how to tell when it was done. Yet it seems very versatile and relatively fool proof. I cooled it off with more buttermilk at the end and spooned it onto toast. Yum.
I love scrambled eggs. But I need an idea for doing this, but without cream, butter or milk ie. lactose free.
OMG this is now our house style for scrambled (when we have cream on hand). Only thing is they seemed a little salty, so we’ll probably cut back (or maybe the recipe assumes kosher salt?).
Followed the recipe exactly and it was so, so delicious!

So this was really tasty, but super rich, so be ready to pair with some OJ or a Mimosa.  Single serving should be enough for two and toast is a must.  Don’t worry about a fatty meat like bacon or sausage this will satisfy the savory fatty craving.

I used Califia heavy cream substitute, which I've been experimenting with in baking. Little nerve wracking when it went liquid but the eggs cooked as promised. Velvety without being oozy and held their heat while eating. Went great with challah toast. If you're feeding people with different scrambled-doneness preferences, you can serve as you cook saving the most done for last.
Cut salt in half!
Cut salt in half.

My husband always complains that I'll eat eggs in a restaurant, but never at home. With this recipe, the eggs taste like the restaurant, at home. Did cut the salt in half (doctor's orders) but otherwise followed the recipe. Will make again.

These eggs were fully cooked and fluffy instead of rubbery or tough. I followed the directions as written except I let them sit after whisking with salt only while I gathered the rest of my equipment, 5 min. Normally fluffy is followed by a bit of “watery”, which I despise so to avoid watery, I have always tended to end up with rubbery, whether I make the eggs or I eat out. This recipe has solved the problem at home. I made a second batch where I halved the recipe and it worked just as well.

We’re these good? Yes. Mind-blowingly better than the “basic” Kenji eggs (salt and let hang out for ten minutes, no massive amounts of butter or cream necessary)? Not particularly. I may try once more with the browned butter, but I suspect I will be saving my extra calories for elsewhere and stick with the basic version.
A lot of effort - and extra fat - for scrambled eggs that *may* be a little better than other scrambled eggs .
I’m no wimp when it comes to salt, but based on other egg dishes (including Kenji’s other fabulous one) this quantity looked way high. Indeed, a scant 1/4 teaspoon was the proper amount for taste.
Delicious.  I didn't use all the cream, just the initial 3 tbsps.  Worked fine for me.

Nice recipe. Cooked exactly as directed, though using chopped fresh Basil leaves as my herb. Everyone liked it...a lot.  To get the "velvety" look in the photo, your eggs need to be a bit undercooked. Served atop buttered toast. I did let my beaten egg mixture rest for a quarter hour before finishing. Find this a nice change of pace to usual fried eggs or omelets. Simple. Now I have something special to whip up when I have that wee bit of heavy cream leftover in the refrigerator.
Great dish cooked exactly as recipe called for. Used chopped chives in the egg mixture. When serving garnished with a spoon of black caviar and a few more chives. Outstanding!

Near daily scrambled egg eater here. Made the recipe exactly as described and it was good, but to me, pretty disappointing given it’s nearly double the calories. Love his recipes normally, but was excited for a splurge here and was disappointed. Perhaps I just like the flavor of unadulterated eggs more than most! Texture wasn’t anything mind blowing to me. 

If you want an egg dish that is a mind blowing change in texture try Eric Kim’s microwave steamed eggs!
So delicious!  I used garlic chives and parsley from my garden. Outstanding!  I will make again as a special treat.
Made this as written and loved the texture and creaminess but 1/2 teaspoon is way too much salt. Will make again and cut salt by at least half!

It's a great idea - but I hope that one tells one's guests at brunch that there's dairy in the eggs. I wouldn't expect it if someone said they were serving scrambled eggs - and it would ruin my breakfast.
I love this recipe so much. I’ll never stop loving this recipe
Tried these as written. Not as velvety as the slow cooked French method.

I cut the salt in half to 1/4 tsp and it was perfect. Didn't use any herbs. These are great. Not an everyday method of making eggs, but for a nice breakfast or a weekend breakfast they are great. I did find that they eggs didn't absorb that much liquid after cooking. I'll try again next time with just the initial 3 tablespoons of liquid. I used mostly cream and a little whole milk, but I think they still be great with half cream and half milk.
I made this exactly as written and it was good. Was it game changing? No, but it was still good enough to make again. The eggs came out very soft and creamy, as would be expected.

The takeaway from this technique is to briskly stir the eggs in a bowl and let them rest for the prescribed 10-15 minutes, regardless of the cream technique.  The resulting full recipe was surely decedent and made me feel as though I was dining at a fine restaurant.  As with normal eggs, it;s all about the additives.  I used an Italian seasoning blend, (oregano,marjoram, thyme, basil, rosemary & sage)

I made this with whole milk rather than cream and it turned out great. I think you could cook in whole milk and finish with cream without sacrificing anything on creaminess or taste.  I had some cilantro and green onion as herbs with sliced tomato and avocado on the side. It was quite nice.
Made this again with just the initial 3 tablespoons of cream and not any after cooking and it worked better.

My mother always made scrambled eggs the VERY low heat, VERY long time way. She never added  anything but salt , pepper, and butter, and I’ve never had better. I’ll try the cream method as it sounds supremely delicious, because—cream! But it will take a lot to persuade me that my mother’s eggs can be surpassed.

Very delicious, and delivered exactly what it promised. I thought it was a little salty with the full half teaspoon of salt, and might cut that back next time. Otherwise, a perfect use for some leftover crème fraîche.
I prefer boiled or poached eggs but if I am making scrambled, this is what I’m having!
A little too salty but otherwise good.

For the effort, this was the most perfect scrambled egg recipe I’ve ever made! Extremely easy and quick too. They tasted at least as good as the half-hour, many ingredient scrambled egg recipes I’ve made over the years. My new favorite!

Beating the eggs thoroughly and letting them rest is one of the keys to scrambled eggs. I used a Ninja smoothie cup, pulsing for about 10 seconds to whip the eggs properly. Yes, it's one more thing to clean but totally worth it for these scrambled eggs.
Combine this recipe with J. Kenji's recipe for extra-creamy scrambled eggs (combine the cornstarch-water mixture with the eggs, then add it all to the pan with the cream).  You're welcome.
I loved scrambled eggs that were cooked slowly and stirred constantly, but so much work for breakfast!  These are as good or better.  So happy to have this recipe!
Too much salt for me. Use a bit less. Otherwise, delicious

Fantastic recipe! Echoing what another commenter said, even though the cooking times are all very short, the recipe is very forgiving. I was probably 10-15 seconds long on every step and it still turned out great. Also, I didn’t add the cream at the end and it was still a lovely texture. Next time I’ll remember to buy chives though. 
Fyi for 3 eggs instead of four, use 2 tbsps   1 tsp of cream initially.
Spectacular way to turn leftover egg whites into stand alone breakfast treat

I made this after searching for creme fraiche, as I had most of an absurdly priced package left after ikura caviar and blinis night. Used dill and parsley as my herbs as that's what I had. Figured dill and eggs are bfs, despite dill not being listed. Was luscious and a welcome surprise, nearly justified the $10 creme! Didn't have table salt, was a little shocked a NYT recipe called for it! Subbed Diamond Crystal kosher, same amount, and let sit the full 15min before remixing. All was well.

@Bree La Fin I share your reluctance to lay out a lot of money for crème fraîche.  It’s super easy to make, especially if you have an instant pot with a yogurt setting.   Whenever I have heavy cream leftover from some recipe, I give it a little longer shelf life by making crème fraîche with it.
Delicious with some uncooked, chopped red onion added at the serving!

I've been just mixing in about a tablespoon of sour cream and a tablespoon of heavy cream into four eggs before beating them. Melting about a tablespoon of butter in a 310 degree induction (probably not all that accurate, but it seems to work for me) and cooking the eggs. No salt until on the plate, never had any water come out of the eggs, but I did eat them pretty quickly--I'll try Kenji's salting next time.

Made these eggs with tarragon, basil, and scallions on top of sourdough toast. They needed a little something so I added some halved cherry tomatoes on top and that really brought everything together.

Ina and Gordon can keep these. I finally broke down and wasted eggs on this recipe. It was as disappointing as it has always looked to me. Velvety? No. Just runny eggs that make you immediately feel the need to give your tongue a good brushing.
Best scrambled eggs ever.  Forgot to add the creme at the end, and it didn't make a difference.
The best scrambled eggs I have ever made. I used thyme which I love with eggs. Looking forward to trying the brown butter version. I just need to lay in some better bread for the toast.

Had a yen for creamy, light, nearly runny eggs for dinner and this method delivered. Cooking with cream iso oil seems to gentle the process—a buffer against browning.

Notes-to-Self:
Heat black pepper in butter, add a glug of not fastidiously measured half-and-half. Use a medium skillet (8-inch seems too small). 

The point of the egg-pouring technique seems to be directing the stream into the cream as much as possible. 

Extra cream at the end only needs to be stirred in quickly—going at it vigorously will break up the lovely folds. 

Next time I’ll make the toast to serve with. Or serve with smoked salmon and latkes.

I want with smoked salmon and latkes.
@PADR your wish has been granted by the NYT cooking crew.. check the video out as of 4/8/25.
This is the stuff eggy dreams are made of. I don’t know why I needed a recipe, but I did. And it was great!

After watching the video of Kenji enthusiastically eating bite after bite of these eggs, I now feel compelled to make them.  So cute haha.  And I definitely have a much better feel for the technique after seeing it in action!

I have made this many times as my go to for scrambled eggs. I have used both heavy cream and half and half depending on what is in my refrigerator. Heavy cream definitely is better. The eggs stay moist and velvety. I have added shredded cheese when I take the eggs of a few minutes before they are completely cooked. I have made a Mexican version with oregano tomato pieces or salsa.
Just made this. Did anyone find this wildly salty? Half a teaspoon of regular table salt for four eggs seems wildly high.
This works just as well with non-dairy half and half or non-dairy heavy cream. I used nut pods original with good success

title: Mayo-Roasted Thanksgiving Turkey With Gravy Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

comments.commentBody: Doesn't leaving the dry brine on the turkey while cooking cause it to be too salty? Should we wipe down or rinse it before applying the herb mayonnaise?
Nope, not at all. I’ve dry-brined countless birds and they’re never too salty. Please do not rinse your turkey!

@Logan Bacon I realize I’m replying to an old comment, but chiming in anyway. This was my first time cooking a turkey. I followed the recipe exactly and my family and I actually did find it to be a bit too salty, but still tasty. I used a store-bought frozen turkey which probably had a salt solution added already, so maybe that was the issue. Otherwise the recipe was great. Very flavorful and received good reviews from the family. Looking forward to trying the same mayo method with a chicken.

Another tip: if you plan to insert a probe to monitor the bird's temperature, insert it into the thickest part of the breast, instead of the thigh as you normally would. Spatchcoking makes the breast the thickest part of the bird, and I have found it takes longer for the breast to reach done-ness than the thigh.
Spring-loaded poultry shears make short work of taking out that backbone. I made sure that I purchased poultry shears that come apart for thorough cleaning.
@Leslee not necessary with a well serrated bread knife, as long as the knife has some good teeth
@Leslee. Our butcher handles the spatchcocking.  They’ve got the right tools.

If you prefer Indian flavors in your turkey, then replace the flavorings in the mayo with an Indian masala. We prefer the Aachi brand these days, and the tandoori or the meat masala works well. Kenji's spatchcocked brined technique with the Indian mayo has been our go to technique for poultry, and it gets consistent rave reviews. If you like, you can add minced garlic and ginger into the mayo-masala for complexity.

All good. But I'd use Duke's mayo, or make my own from 3 egg yolks and a C of olive oil. Since I've experienced these aiolliscious wonders, Hellmanns and Best Foods are huge disappointments.

If you're so unlucky to not have Duke's in your area (I guess it's a Southern thing) make homemade.  There are dozens of videos online showing how to do it with an immersion blender. Surprisingly easy and satisfying.

Side note:  spell check did not balk at "aiolliscious!" Who knew!
You can also just whisk it.

If you're like me and likely to use your hands to spread the mayo on the bird, saving extra mayo for sandwiches might not be a good idea. I'd take a spoonful out and reserve it first to avoid a salmonella sandwich.

Roasting chicken with a mayo-based marinade has changed my life - I recommend to all my friends and I am so excited to try this version with turkey. NO you cannot taste or detect any degree of mayo after roasting (or cooking on a pan). It tastes like oil or butter in the best way. Mayo is the best hack for super juicy, perfectly browned, flavorful meat, especially when you have very little time to marinate!
Where are the directions for spatchcocking the turkey?

I’ve been dry brining my turkeys and chickens for several years. I wouldn’t do them any other way.  I do rinse them and dry well before the slathering commences.   Make ahead gravy……it’s not only better as the flavors meld it also makes the day a lot less stressful.

It needs to be said that dry brining should NOT be used on a supermarket turkey that has been injected with a salt-based solution, as most of these turkeys (Butterball, etc.) are. I have used this method -- both splatchcocked and roasted whole on a large baking stone. The results are as phenomenal as Kenji promises!
Loosening the skin is a key step– it creates a very thin gap between the flesh and skin, allowing the skin to crisp better.
I have my butcher do the spatchcocking. Life can be easy!

Made this for a Friendsgiving this weekend. Several guests said it was the best Turkey they tasted in their life! Not sure how this is only rated 4 stars by many… super easy, flavorful and moist. Due to timing I brined it about 30 hrs before and put the mayo on 6 or so hours before cooking. My 15 lb Turkey took about 1hr 45 mins to cook. This will likely be my go to recipe for years to come!

The article that accompanies this recipe is the best explanation of how and why brining works, bar none. He explains the science in very clear, understandable language, without dumbing it down. Thank you for this article and recipe!

Theentire turkey is covered with skin, so when you say,” skin side up” which side is that?   Howsabout saying, “ Breast side up/down” or somesuch?

Also, when requiring that something be brined or marinated before cooking, suggest you state this requirement as FIRST sentence  in the directions, “ e.g., “ 12 to 24 hours before prepping and cooking, dry brine the turkey”
@Tbaleh Once the bird is spatchcocked, the opened “cavity” is not covered in skin and would be the non-skin side. Otherwise, the inside is now outside.
Totally off topic, but mayonnaise is also the best thing to use instead of butter when making grilled cheese sandwiches.

We did thanksgiving a week early with a 21-lb turkey. Spatchcocked, followed this recipe, roasted the bird at 450F for 30 min, then at 350F until a probe in the breast was 150F (2 hrs more). Rested 30 min loosely tented with foil. It was incredibly juicy, beautiful color and with cracklingly crisp skin. I'll never do it any other way again! If you are looking for the perfect turkey recipe, this is it. I used orange juice+zest instead of lemon, and fresh sage, rosemary, and thyme for the herbs.
We're having this for Thanksgiving. The only mayonnaise used in my house is Duke's. All others are banned.

For this year's pre-Thanksgiving meal we chose to try Kenji's recipe, with minor alterations. Our bird was a Butterball; they're already brined, so a dry rub was unnecessary. 

Secondly, we didn't have a pan large enough to hold a spatchcocked 16 lb turkey. So we cut out the backbone (used it to make a fresh stock), separated the leg quarters and kept the breast bone-in. Slathered with the herb mayo, both breast and leg meats were the tastiest, and moistest, we've ever had. Highly recommended.
If you want to have herb mayo for use in sandwiches later, make sure to set it aside before you go digging fingers into the herb mayo and rubbing it all over the bird. Holy salmonella!

I’ve made Kenji’s dry-brined turkey over a baking steel for the last few years and it’s awesome. That recipe included baking powder with the kosher salt during the dry-brining step. I wonder why no baking powder is included in this recipe.

Be careful which salt you use! 
1/2 Cup Diamond Kosher salt = 72g Salt
1/2 Cup David’s Kosher salt = 115g Salt
So if using other salt (Morton’s, David’s, Himalayan etc) adjust from there. (By weight, all salts are equivalent, except low sodium.) 
This is why Europeans use weight instead of volume, especially for baking.  Also useful for ground coffee. 
(One cook found her turkey too salty, I wonder which salt she used?)
I’ve used tin snips to spatchcock a turkey.
I purchased poultry shears that are spring loaded and come apart for easy and thorough cleaning.
I will try this by adding Puerto Rican sofrito to the mayo.

I usually preach the Kenji Gospel, but man, this was disappointing. The mayo coagulated and bunt in places, and the skin didn't brown as well as it would If I had just used oil. This is the first Kenji recipe I haven't raved about. I guess even the best whiff sometimes.

I’ve been spatchcocking my Thanksgiving turkey for years. It cooks fast which is so nice. Indian spices love turkey! I just mix various spices I have such as curry, cumin, garam masala, etc. The blend is never the same but turkey is very forgiving. My only trouble is slicing the spatchcocked bird. Still perfecting my skills! Finally, add lemon juice to whatever gravy you make. It really punches up the flavor. You won’t be sorry!
I made the herb mayo with cilantro instead of parsley, and the turkey tasted super fresh. This recipe changed my coworker's opinion on turkey, it was so the best turkey I have ever had.

Anyone have thoughts on whether or not to use convection w/ this technique?  Will be my first go with spatchcocking and with a mayo marinade, but I usually do use my convection setting with a non-spackcocked turkey or chicken.
I set my oven to convection roast, and placed on the third slot from the bottom so it sits at the bottom of the blower which is in the back of my oven.
Whoa, this came out way too salty.

Do not let 'butterflying' a turkey give you pause. I did a turkey this way for Thanksgiving on my Weber grill last year. Juicy breast, done legs and thighs. You'll never go back to 'whole bird' again.
Good video to show spatchcocking?
Spatchcocking video. Very similar recipe, but not the herby Mayo on the bird and veggies in the pan which definitely needs done. https://youtu.be/cLWv68KZ1yY
Question: The recipe calls for 1/2 a cup of salt. I don't see any directions to rinse the salt off. That seems a bit too much salt to me?

I made this and it worked perfectly. I've roasted probably 50 turkeys in my 64 years and tried a dozen (or more) different recipes. This one combines innovation (the right way to check internal temp and the whole mayo thing) with a grandma's wisdom (resting really matters) and relative simplicity (no silly brining bags or special roasting methodology). One of the best ever.

My experience with splatchcocking is that it is difficult to find shears that are sharp, and leveraged properly, not to make the task exhausting for the hands of anyone but a lumberjack.  During a home improvement project one recent year, my grip exhausted and not 1/4 away through the turkey, my eye caught my DeWalt sawzall, and I said to myself "why not?". I have been sawzalling the backbone out of my annual splatchcocked turkey ever since.  This is now my favorite part of Thanksgiving prep.

Perhaps the best Thanksgiving Turkey I've ever made and I have made many. HOWEVER that is way too much salt. I started with a quarter cup and even then used half of that, just an 1/8th  cup. It was plenty seasoned and I am a salt lover. I could even have used less. One other note is that spatchcocking a Turkey is not for most home cooks; I ended up using a hammer and heavy cleaver to bang my way through the large bones. Better to ask your butcher to do this for you. The gravy was brilliant!
Most important tip, use Dukes Mayo, not that other stuff.

We had a guest that is recovering from Lyme disease and alpha-gal, I assured him that it was NOT a Butterball and had no "butter" in it.  He was moved to tears that I made an effort.
Best ever, fabulous!
(Seriously, he ate 3 servings...)
This recipe is worth it for the homemade mayo notes alone.  I mean--an immersion blender and a wide-mouth mason jar??  No gentle drizzling?  No food processor to clean???   Heck yeah.

My MIL and now my wife loads the pan with a lot of veggies.  When the turkey is done, everything in the bottom of the pan is pureed as a base to make a different sort of gravy for the turkey.  Good stuff in the pan, why just toss it.

This was wonderful. The turkey was amazingly juicy and flavorful, even though I overcooked it a bit (It was done so quickly it surprised me). This will now be my go-to turkey recipe for Thanksgiving dinner. The gravy was fantastic, The most flavorful I’ve ever made. I made the gravy the day before and just enhanced it with drippings from the turkey at the last minute. Avoids that gravy anxiety just before serving.

I don't get it. If cooking breast-side up in a roasting pan slows the cooking of the dark meat, why not reverse it, with the turkey in a roasting pan back-side up? I guess turn it for the last half-hour to brown the skin... No?
I think the skin would get too soggy. My skin always come out perfectly brown when I spatchcock, which has become my default method for roasting poultry. I wouldn’t mess with it - it works great.

60 years of making turkey - once a year, so I always forget how!  This is The. Best. Ever!  Got a fresh turkey, spatchcocked. Used less salt. Made it ahead last year, and I’m doing it again this year. Had a retired wholesale butcher friend slice it. Froze it with some of the gravy and re- heated it. Beautiful presentation (red grapes and rosemary springs).

My turkey is almost 20lbs- (big crowd).  Should I adjust the temperature down even more, to 375? Or start at 400? Also, I'm guessing 4.5 hours, anyone with experience? I don't own poultry shears and I am positive my cheap kitchen shears (which come apart easily) cannot in anyway cut out a backbone.

Spatchcocking is hard because turkey bones are bigger than chicken bones. Don’t let this stop you. I find that instead of poultry shears, with turkey I use a keyhole saw which you can pick up at the hardware store for a few bucks. Yes, wash it thoroughly before and after use. Lastly I am thinking of cooking the bird, spatchcocked, on the BBQ. Maybe with most of the burners off or low. This frees up the kitchen and oven. Mesquite wood chips soaked and then wrapped in foil placed on the fire add f
No point in soaking the wood chips--ever. What little water gets absorbed into the very thin outer wood layer just creates a brief period of steam, not smoke. Think about it: wood floats in water.
@Bill - the water is not for smoke, it's to prevent the wood from burning.

Spatchcocking is difficult -- why not separate the wings and leg/thigh quarters from the breast with a sharp knife and lay them out on the wire rack as shown in the photo?  Cooks Illustrated recommends this method and it works beautifully.
I found it very easy to spatchcock even a very large turkey with a good pair of kitchen shears (I call them my "chicken choppers"). And I'm a senior with arthritic fingers.
I’ve never had a problem spatchcocking, but yes, you could break down the whole bird if you prefer. I personally find that much more difficult than spatchcocking!
This sounds lovely, but my husband hates mayonnaise and can detect even slight amounts in food. I wonder if there is a discernible taste in the drippings or if herbs mask it.
There isn't a discernible taste.  If you can make homemade and you'll lessen the possibility even more.

Last year I made Samin Nosrat’s buttermilk-brined spatchcocked turkey (used Joyce Chen shears as she did in her NYT video to cut out the backbone).  Results were great and folks raved.  Wondering how this method using mayo and dry brining will compare? It will be fun to try!  Last year, For the person asking about a mayo substitute due to an allergy, find out which mayo ingredient the guest is allergic to—eggs, oil, vinegar?  If it is the eggs, there are vegan mayos available.

Hi and thank you! My fully roasted turkey breast is never that dark in color so I am excited to try this mayo recipe! I assume I'm able to use this mayo recipe with just the turkey breasts (or even deboned breasts?) -- I usually cook two at the same time -- do you have any particular guidance on the recipe if we are roasting just turkey breasts? Thank you!!!
Kenji…if you roast the bird on a shallow baking sheet won’t the juices spill everywhere when removing pan from oven?

I’ve cooked a lot of spatchcocked turkeys and I’ve never found that there’s so much juice it’s overflowing. You do need to be careful removing it from the oven, but that’s the case with anything. My baking sheet does have slightly higher sides than some I’ve used, though, which helps.
I won’t be spatchcocking because I’m cooking a 22 lb bird this year. Will the marinade work just as well or is it particularly to the spatchcock method?
I always brine my turkey (not dry-brine) because it keeps the texture of the meat moist.  Can I use the mayo method after I dry the turkey?  Seems that it would make for a nice crisp skin.
Yes that should be fine. BTW, I have not noticed a difference between the wet and dry brining process in the resultant moistness.
Microwave the veggies first. I find that they sometimes are still hard if you don't.
I use aviation snips (usually used for cutting sheet metal) to cut out the backbones of very large birds when spatchcocking.
Does this technique work for larger turkeys?  16-18 lb?  20 lb?  What would an estimated cooking time be?

Yes - I have made a 15lb bird. I remember the overall cooking time being shorter than with the conventional way. The only hard part is cutting through the ribs at the backbone for a large bird. Ideally, you also want to press down on the breast and crack the breast bone to somewhat flatten the breast. 

Maybe have the butcher do it?

So many questions ... is "skin side down" a thing now? Are nervous first-time cooks going to comprehend that? Turkeys have skin on all sides. Wouldn't "breast side up" be wiser? The tips rightly use that term, which furthers the point. And why does it pointedly specify blending the mayo paste/baste in a tall container that will just fit an immersion blender inside, when IG Reels shows Lopez-Alt whizzing it right up in a wide 4-cup Pyrex?
When you spatchcock a bird, you remove the spine and flatten the whole thing out, so one side actually doesn't have skin as it's the interior of the turkey :)
Quick tip on the mayo, which is easy and delicious: the egg needs to be room temperature. Otherwise, it's just a mess.

I have spatchcocked and dry brined my Thanksgiving turkey for the last 10 years and not only do they cook more quickly, they are moist and delicious. Every, single, time.  I have always used an herb butter under and on the skin (which turns out shatteringly crisp!) but will try this mayo version this year. I expect it to be at least equally delicious.

Duke's Mayonnaise is more lemony than Hellman's and doesn't seem as heavy...
I cooked this today and gorgeous browning,wow
Duke's mayonnaise has no added sugar, which is why it tastes better than any other store-bought mayonnaise.
I tried it on a spatchcocked chicken, and now I will (finally) do a turkey because this recipe cannot fail!

Made the dry brine and herb mayo on a small chicken as a test run before the big event.  Bird was evenly golden brown and juicy-luscious!  We've used Julia Child's recipe for deconstructed turkey for many years and will tweak with the herb mayo this year.

Incredible turkey! Made it using a 14lb bird, cooked it at 400F for 2 hours (used a meat thermometer to determine when to pull it. Waited until the breast meat reached 155F). The best turkey I’ve had, the breast meat was so juicy and perfectly seasoned. It is a little more work spatchcocking the bird but the time savings and juicy meat are worth it!!

To Scott and others who plan to smoke your turkey: yes, you can use mayonnaise to coat your turkey before smoking. I can attest that it works well. I allow my brined turkey to dry in the fridge before applying the mayonnaise. I sprinkle on my desired dry spice blend after applying the mayonnaise. But Scott, I suggest that you read up on smoking a turkey, as 6 to 7 hours is a lot longer than is normally suggested for the temperature range that is normally recommended.

Not really a full review, but some notes for myself and anyone curious. Tried using a 7.5lb chicken, as I wanted to give it a test run before the holiday. One person who'll be at Thanksgiving has an egg allergy, so I wanted to see if a vegan mayo would work in place of the real deal.

And it does! I made sure to buy a brand that lists aquafaba in the ingredients (Hellman's vegan mayo is only oil+lemon juice), and the skin was crispy and delicious. Excited to make this with a turkey!
Made this with a chicken to test it out. It is PHENOMENAL. The mayo mix is also wonderful, can’t wait to use it for sandwiches. Will definitely be implementing this for Thanksgiving.
Question - would this work with vegan mayo? I have someone coming who is allergic to eggs.

I made this using vegan mayo and it did! I would just recommend finding a vegan mayo that has "aquafaba" or other vegetable protein in it, as Kenji mentions that the egg protein in the mayo is what helps brown the skin. I used the brand Sir Kensington's.

I made practice version of this on a chicken in anticipation of Thanksgiving. It was delicious--meat incredibly moist and the skin was crispy and tasted almost fried. I'll definitely be using this method for Thanksgiving!
Use Duke’s Mayo
Agree!

@Tbaleh The recipe calls for a spatchcocked turkey, hence the skin side up/down. 

Also, since the first rule of recipes is to read the entire thing thoroughly before proceeding, there is no need to rearrange the steps. Anyone who takes the required time to read the whole recipe (vs taking time out to criticize it) should have no problems or frustration understanding the process.

The whole bird version with the pizza stone results in the best turkey I’ve ever made.  Beware that it cooks faster than you might think.  Check the temperature after an hour so see where you at on the doneness scale and watch it closely after that.
Did you put the roasting pan directly on the baking stone or on one rack above the stone?

I don’t love turkey and begrudgingly eat it every year in November. But having just made the herbed mayo, I’m already looking forward to turkey sandwiches. I might make a batch to keep on hand for sandwiches.
In step 1, 1 teaspoon of salt goes UNDER the skin of each breast?

I'm a fan of spatchcocking and dry-brining, but the mayonnaise "marinade" was an absolute revelation.  Totally delicious, and I look forward to trying this with chicken, and with other flavor combinations in the mayo.  We made a 12.5 lb farm-raised bird and dry brined for 48 hrs.  The only difference was that our total cooking time was closer to 2 hours, but that's still half of what it would take to roast the bird whole, and it makes carving so much easier.  This recipe is a keeper.

If cooking a turkey breast (bone-in) instead of a whole turkey, what would the adjusted cooking temperature (and time) be? The recipe calls for a higher cooking temperature for a smaller turkey. (please forgive the basic question, I'm still new to roasting turkey...)
Has anyone made this recipe with an 19-pound turkey? Can I use a roasting pan rather than a baking sheet for similar results? Cut off the legs to have it fit?
My exact question! What if you have a larger bird than the recipe indicates?

The recipe clearly states it's for a 10-14 lb turkey. Everything would have to be adjusted for a larger bird, including the size of the pan you cook it in. I have a 12-1/2 lb turkey and it fits perfectly into either a sheetpan or ordinary roasting pan. It's brining in my fridge now.
Could you roast this turkey on a rack set over stuffing/dressing instead of veg?

Made this this past Sunday - family issues meant we had to meet a few days early - and it was a big hit. However, there was no evidence of crispy skin, even with overnight dry brining in the frig. I think the mayo coat canceled out any crispiness. No matter, it was a hit, and a lot easier to cook than traditional turkeys, although carving was confusing since the pieces were horizontal, not vertical. Dopey me.

My spatchcocked 15 pounder won't fit on the sheet pan and the only option I've read here is to cut the bird in half and roast in 2 pieces. Any other ideas appreciated! It's also not possible to leave it flat on the pan in my fridge uncovered overnight. Could I brine it and leave it in a loose bag instead? Thank you, I can't wait to eat this!
This is my question, too!  Mine is 17lbs and I'm afraid the 11x17 sheet pan I have isn't large enough! I don't have a "full sheet" pan...

This is a very good approach to a turkey, especially if you don’t have a lot of time to dry-brine. A French press carafe works just great as the “tall skinny container” for blending the herb mayo

Best turkey I have ever made, hands down.  Never not doing this again.  13 lb bird, cooked on convection @400, done in 1 hr 25 min.  Skin is the crispiest and tastiest ever.  Kenji is, as ever, a genius.
I find forgoing the sheet pan and placing the turkey directly on the oven rack makes for even crispier skin and more even cooking. Just make sure your kids clean the oven when you’re done.

I didn't baste - recipe is really a breeze!

I’ve roasted a lot of turkeys in my time: some great, some slightly dry, most simply good. I spent Thanksgiving 2022 on my own. Defrosted a year-old WFM organic free range 11 lb bird and thought I’d give this a whirl. I used Duke’s Mayo; the rest of the recipe as stated.
Reader, this was absolutely the most delicious turkey I’ve ever made. Tender, juicy, flavorful, nicely herbed but still tasted of TURKEY.
And the gravy was a bonus!
Make this bird. You’ll want to GOBBLE, GOBBLE!!

A superb result except for the soggy skin. 
The color was gorgeous tho and tasted great.  Can anyone suggest why the skin was limp?  So many notes submitted by users , some advice needed from the nyt folks?

I made this today for the first time, even thought I had some reservations about using mayonnaise to coat the turkey. But I trusted the chef , and I also trusted the other reviewers who’d had the initial reaction.  

It was fantastic, with a beautifully browned skin and luscious flavor. I’m a concert now. Thanks, Kenji Alt-Lopez!

I found the turkey too salty, others were okay with it. The skin was perfectly browned, breast was moist, but thighs/legs needed to go back in for an extra 10 minutes. I had to cover the breast after 25 min, and then thigh 10 mins later using a convection oven on roast @ 425, done in 75-85 min. Next time I'd lower the rack to bottom third, have oven on 400, decrease salt by half, and may put wine or cider underneath as many of the vegetable were shriveled and black by the end.

Turkey Day may be over, but this way of cooking poultry will be used all year. Made as directed, but my mother bought a 20Lb turkey, so had to adapt. Ended up making it on a gas grill set at 375 degrees fahrenheit for about 2 hours. Middle two burners were set to low, the two outside burners were set to medium high. Hope this helps anyone who wants to cook their bird on the grill.

Regarding comments  about too much salt...having dry brined meats  before and gotten the hang of it  I figured I wouldn't be using all the salt mentioned in the recipe....NYT  please note:  some advice/caution  to those trying this the first time would be a great suggested edit to the recipe.  I also watched a couple of you tube videos to see how much should land on the bird.
I adapted this for a 7-pound bone-in turkey breast, so I basically made 2/3 of the recipe. It was good.

Dry brined with a mixture of 1 Tbsp kosher salt to 1 tsp baking powder per a previous Serious Eats article. Did not rinse. Our spatchcocked 14 lb bird took only 1.5 h to roast and came out amazingly juicy and delicious, with crispy skin. The stock was a unappealing green color from the herb mayo drippings, but made a normal colored gravy.

I applied this method to an 8lb bone-in turkey breast. Dry brined in the fridge for two days before applying mayo mix and roasting (425 for 30 mins, then 325 for the rest of the time.) Gorgeous mahogany crispy skin! The flavor of the turkey was lightly herbal - really, really delicious. DEFINITELY reserve at least half of the herb mayo - it was amazing on sandwiches made of thxgvg leftovers. This will be my go-to recipe for turkey from now on.

Thanksgiving 2022:  Everyone said this was the most juicy turkey ever.  The butcher removed the backbone for me and I prepared the turkey exactly per the recipe and it was easy and delicious.  I did not remove any of the salt/brine before roasting.  Because I use Mark Bittman's excellent make ahead gravy recipe from this site, I didn't use that part of the recipe.  Scattered halved shallots under the wire rack and they were a small fantastic side dish as well.

Have to give this 5 stars.  Just did this for just a turkey breast (with bones) and it was excellent.  Breast was about 8.3 pounds, so cut the herb mayo recipe in half and used dried herbs (reduced amounts) instead of fresh.  Also just set the oven at 350 and didn't adjust it, used a thermometer to ensure it was at 165 internal, it took a little less than 2 hours.  Covered it for the last hour or so after the skin browned up.  Soooo good and may never do a whole turkey again either.

To those whose pan veggies burned, I have an answer! Halved campollini onions. I used about 20. The cooling rack was slightly elevated off of the pan initially (totally stable though), but after 40 minutes of cooking, they compressed. The result was decadent, soft onions drenched in turkey fat that I drizzled with balsamic vinegar and served in a bowl next to the gravy. YUM!
This was too salty to enjoy.  I like the method and will try again using less salt.
I made a half batch for a roast chicken.  The herb mayo is super yummy. The chicken came out beautifully, very moist but not under-cooked.

I also found this way too salty. I’ve read through all the comments and it seems some cooks also shared this feeling, and some didn’t. Yes, Diamond Kosher Salt. Yes, I normally love salt. Ultimately, a vote for cutting the salt down from this cook.
Does the herb mayonnaise not break apart when cooking in oven?

Used this procedure with a 22 pounder. Had to use a sharp, clean hatchet to take out the backbone. Since the procedure allowed for multiple days it was easy to prepare, clean and be ready for the next step.

I'm going to start doing this with chickens now. Excellent!

This roasting method yielded an extremely moist 17 pound bird that we promptly devoured. But the herb mayo didn’t add a lot of flavor. I might try mixing different spices into the Mayo next time because it does adhere the seasoning to the skin quite well.
I’ve done this twice but instead of the mayonnaise I used some olive oil.  It came out great both times. Very flavorful and moist.  This is a keeper.

Did not brine the turkey, but did let it air dry in the fridge for 2 days. Cut out the back bone and removed the wings. Roasted them and then used them for stock for gravy. Further cut the bird to have two thigh/leg combo's and the whole breast. Coated the bird with the mayo mixture. Baked at 400 F. 
Wow! Best turkey skin I have ever eaten. The white and dark meat turned out moist and flavorful, and the gravy was superb. Cooked dressing separately and it was not as good as a stuffed dressing.
Hands down the best turkey I've ever made. The skin was crisp and flavorful, and every part of the bird was juicy and tender. This will be my go-to get recipe from now on.
Absolutely perfect. I couldn't believe how soft and moist the breast meat was and the drumsticks were super

This is amazing! Made as written. I had a small 10# ish bird. Took only 70 min at 425.  Great depth of flavor. Gravy was perfect. I made stock a day ahead while the turkey dry brined. Easy peasy. Give it a shot!

Excellent recipe. Made it for Friendsgiving and everyone loved it.

My mods:

1) use a kosher turkey if you can; no need to dry brine and you're not paying for salt solution
2) deconstruct the bird and roast the two sets of legs/thighs separate from the breast meat on a wire rack. you can pull the legs and thighs if they come to temp quicker.  wings and back use for stock.
3) cook at 300 degrees, let it rest, and then blast at 500 right before carving

My new gold standard turkey recipe.

I will never roast a turkey any other way! This year I bought a pre-brined bird, so skipped the brining step but let it sit already spatchcocked in the fridge for a few hours to dry out the skin prior to putting on the mayo. Highly recommend making your own mayo- Kenji’s two minute mayo never disappoints. Taste the drippings before incorporating them into your gravy to avoid potentially over salting. Beautifully crisp skin and juicy meat.
can you do this recipe on a grill? If so- any modifications folks would suggest?

After making this recipe with turkey last December, I decided to do it with a large chicken today—that works too! The flavor is delicious, but I wish the skin would be crisper.

Spatchcocked a 14 pound prebrined supermarket bird and prepared per the recipe, then roasted on a Weber grill with indirect heat, some soaked hickory chips on the coals, and a beer can of water on the side for evaporative moisture. Stuck a digital thermometer probe in the breast, covered, never peeked until it reached 170f, around 2 hours. Never had a bird more perfect. New go-to method. I decided on this recipe for my first time hosting Thanksgiving for my family. It turned out absolutely perfect following the recipe exactly. Not a single bit of dry meat on this bird. Our oven runs a little hot so mine was done in 75 minutes. This will be my go-to recipe for future Thanksgivings.
Great, cooked fast, spatchcocked is the way to go. 15lb bird done in 1.5 hours. Great flavor and texture

I dont know if I used too much of it but i felt the mayo was too thick/heavy and it just sat on/under the skin. 🙁 i wish the recipe stated how much mayo mix to use but i still think the mayo flavor is too much for my taste.

Wonderful recipe - made as directed. Turned out super juicy and delicious. 16 lbs spatchcocked turkey around 2 hours in the oven until 150 degrees reached in the thickest part of the breast - next to the wish bone. Depends. If you use a low-priced olive oil, or even a lower priced supermarket extra virgin olive oil (which typically doesn't have much flavor), olive oil would be fine. But don't use good EVO. Not only will is impart a strong flavor from the oil while cooking but it's a huge waste of money. If you like the EVO flavor, you can always have some at the table for people to drizzle over their turkey if they wish.

It'll take a little longer, maybe another 15 to 20 minutes.  That's what your meat or instant read thermometer is for.

In Kenji’s previous article for Serious Eats on dry brining a turkey, the turkey rests on a rack on a rimmed sheet pan. This recipe suggests putting the turkey straight onto a sheet pan. Given the discussion in the article about the turkey reabsorbing it’s own liquid, the new instructions make sense, but does anyone know if using a rack to keep the bird over the sheet pan in the fridge (not while roasting, definitely will use a rack while roasting!) makes a difference? I’ve done it both ways, and I couldn’t tell a difference. When I cook a whole bird, I actually started dry brining the day before I cook and spatchcocking it *after* - it takes up a bit less room in the fridge that way!
Not a difference of significance. The fridge time is meant to dry the skin. If spatchcocked and placed on the pan skin up it will achieve this. Rack not required.

In a pinch, if you really couldn’t find turkey necks, you could probably bulk up the turkey spine with some chicken backs or other parts.

Re: question about stuffing: I spatchcock turkey every Thanksgiving. In my home stuffing is king, so even w a stuffed bird, a large casserole dish is required in addition. I slather my turkey with mayo every year (though I've never tried an herb-infused version like this one) and there's no "mayo" taste at all. It just results in a gorgeously browned bird!

My only hesitation is the gravy. When I roast a whole turkey I use the fond and the liquid the turkey releases along with homemade stock, etc. to make delicious, rich gravy. This one, flavored with soy sauce gives me pause.

I haven’t made this specific mayo-rubbed turkey, but I’ve used the gravy recipe countless times. It’s my default gravy. It’s so good! The soy sauce is just a bit of umami/salt. When I made gravy the “old fashioned” way, I added Worchestershire sauce - soy does the same thing (I often use both now). Give it a try, I think you’ll like it.
The soy sauce here adds some salt (which is why he is calling for low-sodium stock) but more importantly adds umami.  The amount called for isn't enough to "flavor" the gravy.
Since there are only two of us to feed in our house, I buy turkey parts: one breast, two thighs, two legs, and two wings. There is still plenty of leftover turkey for sandwiches or just munching.
I might do that next year! We got a half turkey this year, but there weren’t quite as many leftovers as I might have liked. Maybe I’ll just get an extra breast next time.
Do you rinse off the brining salt before cooking?

No as there is no need. Use Diamond Crystal if you can. If not available use table salt but half the amount called for; if using Morton use three-quarters the amount called for. The salt will draw water, mix with it, and some will return into the bird carrying the salt with it, seasoning the bird inside. If you don't overdo the salt there is no need to rinse.
Spatchcocking sounds like a good idea, but doesn’t seem that a 16-20lb turkey would fit on a half sheet pan

As an alternative, and if your oven is large enough, you can actually cut the turkey fully in half and roast it on two separate pans. I have had one truly massive bird that there was just no way I could get to fit when it was spatchcocked - Kenji’s baking steel method is my go-to for that situation, and would probably work just fine with this recipe as an alternative.
We typically spatchcock and grill our turkey. How do you think the herb mayo will stand up to grilling?
If you are roasting the bird on the grill (indirect heat, in a pan or not) the mayo is fine. If you are using direct heat then the mayo is more likely to burn.
I would love to try this but would like advice on any modifications, particularly relating to dry brining, if using a kosher bird.
If using a kosher bird skip the dry-brining as it has already been salted.

Kenji has an article on the differences between “regular” and kosher turkeys on Serious Eats that should help. The gist is: don’t dry brine a kosher Turkey, it’s already plenty salted. However, make sure you loosen the skin and get your herb rub under there. That bit of space between the meat and skin helps your skin get delicious and crispy. I might also omit, or at least cut back, on the salt in the mayo. You can always add more after cooking if you need, but you can’t take it out.
I'm planning to use a Whole Foods brined turkey (wet).  Do I just towel dry the bird and then apply the herbed mayo?
Yes but don't skip the skin separation.

I don't see why not. It's a standard technique in Asian cooking.
What is "skin side up"?  Do you mean Breast Side up?

If the turkey is spatchcocked (the backbone cut out) it becomes one flat-ish piece of poultry, so the skin side would be what the outside was before the cutting, that goes up and the inside (no skin) goes down.
3 yolks 1 cup mayo
Can Kewpie mayo be used?  Maybe it’s too sweet, but love that Mayo in general.
Kenji said on Instagram that Kewpie doesn’t work in cooked recipes, generally - it tends to get a fishy taste.

Butterflying (or spatchcocking) does not remove skin -- just backbone.

Probably, only because a typical V-rack may end up compressing the top of the bird.

Doing the Turkey on a rack may be a little different..just use a thermometer if you have one..if not a sharp knife into any fleshy areas and see the juices run clear..to the person who wants to use Kewpie mayo..why not?it’s your Turkey!
Now to the stuffing issue….we’ll yes,I hear your angst!
If I want to make extra stuffing or like in this instance I would do Stuffing Balls and roast them alongside the vegetables
If I make this in a regular roasting pan, would I need to use the rack or just put it in the pan?

I normally use a cookie rack on a flat sheet pan. You want air circulation on the bottom on the spatchcocked bird for even cooking. If you don't have a cookie rack, then I'd go with the rack in the roasting pan.
Could I follow the same mayo method if smoking the bird vs. roasting?
I assume you could use a turkey breast (bone in) with this recipe, adjusting for time as in your Times recipe for the Roast Turkey Breast?
Yes this should work fine.
Aren't the juices from the roasted turkey too salty to use in the gravy?

No. After the brining process, and before you start cooking, you can wash off the excess salt on the surface and then dry it. The salt that has infused the bird will be at the appropriate salt level. I normally reduce the salt slightly on the mayo sauce to balance out.

I plan to smoke my turkey this year. I'm intrigued by the mayo idea, but does anyone have thoughts (or experience) on using this mayo technique with a 6 to 7-hour smoke? My hunch is that it would still work fine.

My experience (albeit non-mayo) has been that smoking the bird produces excellent flavor and texture in the meat, but you can never get crispy skin at those low temperatures. Pit needs to be at least 325F for skin to crisp up.

I've been smoking a turkey for years,  but I find slow smoking overwhelms the turkey.  I smoke my 14 - 16# turkey at 300F  for a much shorter time  & find it to be just right  - And don't forget to Brine

Two birds are certainly doable with this method. When it comes to cooking two birds in the same oven, I would certainly try to have a baking steel or stone somewhere in the oven to help retain heat and safeguard from any temperature fluctuations. Two birds will also take a bit longer to cook, there's no getting around that. Be diligent in monitoring internal temp. I'd recommend convection. Not only will help maintain temperatures, it will also help give the bird that nice crisp skin.

I'm assuming this wold work just as well if you skipped the spatchcock and just roasted the individual parts on a sheet pan and cranked the oven up at the end to get browning?
Made this with a large chicken, adjusting salt and cooking time—-oh so very very perfect!
Doing 2 birds, 1 per this recipe, 1 deep fried, and was planning on dry-brining both for 72ish hrs - do you all think that will over salt this bird? Or go for 72 hour dry brine?
If roasting whole, do you just leave the pre-heated baking stone on the lowest rack, and the place the turkey in its pan on a rack above that, and not touching the stone?
Our brood really loved this pie, they’ve dub it it “Pancake Pie!” My only recommendation is to add an extra half a tablespoon of cornstarch and a teaspoon of vanilla to the mix.
Anyone try this recipe with a buttermilk brine (instead of dry)?

Don't buy garam masala. It's very simple to make your own. I use a recipe from Charmaine Solomon but there must be an infinite number of recipes on line. Fresh is always so much better than from a jar.
Sounds delicious.  I'm afraid to spatchcocking with family coming over.  SO, how long will it take to cook turkey whole as described above?  Thank you
I want to use this method but plan to grill my turkey, any suggestions or modifications?

If roasting a whole turkey in a deep pan elevates the breast too much, wouldn't doing it upside-down keep the breast cooler?  I do my chickens this way, at 350F, and they're always juicy (with soggy breast skin, but I don't care about that).  Just curious.
Will brining make the bird inedible?
Don't salt brine a kosher bird.  Good luck!  Read all the notes and you will do fine!

I cooked a turkey on my gril yesterday using this recipe for the rub and it turned out great. I wet brined for a day and a half, rinsed and dried the bird, rubbed with the herbed mayo, then grilled at 350 degrees in a roasting pan on my three burner gas grill with the middle burner off. I also included a smoker box with cherry and hickory wood chips for a little smoked flavor. It took about 4 hours and turned out great. Next time I'll try spatchcocking and refresh the wood chips more frequently.
Thanksgiving's just the two of us this year and I can't be bothered with a turkey. Will this same method work for a whole chicken? What alterations do you suggest?

I have a 14 lb turkey and want to try the cook it whole on a rack, over cookie sheet method. 
Wondering if the turkey drippings will spill over the cookie sheet?
Can this mayo smothered turkey be put on a smoker grill?  If so, what is recommended temp of grill in order to crisp the skin?
Use less salt on the breast. I made this and it was great but too salty.

A question on roasting temperature and time on a small spatchcocked turkey. I got a French 6.6-pound turkey, which is too small to be mentioned in recipes or guide I can find on NYT Cooking. This recipe suggests that the smaller the bird, the higher temperature with shorter time. Would I roast at 450 degrees for a shorter amount of time? Or would that be too high?
I blitzed my Hellman’s mayo with the herbs in my Vitamix and the mayo broke, now I have broken mayo herb infusion.  What to do?
I'm planning on keeping the turkey whole (not spatcocked)  can the turkey be stuffed?
Can u stuff this Turkey? I see no mention of that
Can I do Step 6 ahead of time?
I did, and it was a huge help given the limitations of space and timing of the Thanksgiving dinner.

It's about 15 minutes too late for me to ask this question, but...    Am I supposed to spatchcock it BEFORE dry brining?   Seems like it shouldn't really matter except for possibly making it easier to get the salt on the inside of the bird.
For spatchcocking shears are sometimes a chore on turkey; it's tough to get through all the bones. Instead, I get out my trusty jigsaw and use the fast soft wood saw blade. It takes seconds.
Are you serious? That is insane.
thank you so much for sharing this recipe. it was very helpful.
Did I misread the recipe or are there no pan drippings in the gravy?
I had to read on to step seven to see that the liquids from the cooked turkey get added to the broth/stock mixture.
To anyone who has a problem spatchcocking your turkey, you can often get the butcher to do it for you at the store you purchase it from.  Just make sure to ask them to keep the spine for you.
Why does the author say "skin-side" down or up? Is this a reference to the breast-side?
Yes - once it’s spatchcocked, the breast side is the “skin” side
I have dry brined with William Sonoma dry brine mix with great success. Since it contains herbs and spices already can I spread plain mayo on the turkey?
Adapted this recipe for an 8 pound chicken and it worked great. I accidentally over-salted the gravy. User error, just remember you’re finishing it off with soy sauce!
Not sure what is meant by “skin side.” Are we supposed to remove the skin from one side? And what is “spatchcocking”?

Terry, too salty is my worst nightmare. (OK, there are worse things…)
What kind of turkey did you use? Kosher? Supermarket? 
And how much of what kind of salt? 
And did you rinse it before the mayo and the roasting?

Planning on using this green-mayo recipe but without the spatch-cock. (Large Turkey!) 
Anyone have experience or opinions regarding combining the Mayo with the upside-down technique, or the baking-stone technique, to cook the dark meat hotter than the breast? 
Also, since the spatch-cock enables faster cooking, is a longer or hotter roast going to do something terrible to the mayo marinade?
Omitted the soy sauce
Do you rinse the turkey before putting on herb mayo and cooking? Seems like a lot of salt.
Do you rinse the turkey after dry brining?

From serious eats:Generously sprinkle the salt mixture on all surfaces by picking up the mixture between your thumb and fingers, holding it six to 10 inches above the bird, and letting the mixture shower down over the surface of the turkey for even coverage. The turkey should be well coated with salt, though not completely encrusted.

Warning: You will most likely not need all of the salt. In some cases, less than half will be okay; it depends on the size of your bird and your salt preferences.
Better directions from J. Kenji López-Alt than the ones in this recipe: https://www.seriouseats.com/quick-and-dirty-guide-to-brining-turkey-chicken-thanksgiving

How to find a good restaurant near to you ? I’m a chef and food lover also. I always try to find a new place to eat. But in every area there is no a specific person that tells you about a right place to eat. So, i tried and find a great menu site that always guides you to go to a right place. Give you every kind of food recipe and food restaurants. Visit here https://ranarecipes.com/chinese-buffet-near-me/ https://ranarecipes.com/asian-buffets-near-me/
I’d like to use this recipe just on turkey legs. Any suggestions on how to do this?
Trying this out this year instead of my go-to Alton Brown method.   The recipe says to roast it skin side up.  My turkey has skin all over :)  I'm assuming this means breast side up?
Once it is spatchcocked and flattened out, all of the skin is on one side.  And yes, it will be breast side up.

I've made an error that I'm wondering if it's okay, or actually brilliant. Due to too many things going on at once yesterday, when my daughter was making the herbed mayo, we added two whole shallots instead of scallions!! Do you think that's okay or should I remake it this morning??
Do I need to was off the salt after brining?
Should not use extra mayo mix for sandwiches, etc if your hands have contacted it after you have touched the turkey. There is risk of inoculating the mayo with salmonella, etc.
@Scot NC - Ahhh the Duke’s vs Hellman’s debate - dividing southerners for generations.
Anyone know if this would still be okay to make stock with afterwards? I always make turkey soup with the carcass, but I’m wondering if the mayo might have an impact on the stock…
making my first turkey and using this method today. question, why would you cook the smaller birds at a higher temp and the larger birds at a lower temp? help... it's almost showtime!

the bigger the bird the longer it takes for heat to get to the center of the bird. lower for longer means you'll get a more even cook, if you roast it higher the outside will be overcooked before the inside is done. 

Good luck!

Totally a convert for spatchcocking a turkey and mayo. However I may have overdone the salt in the dry brine or in the mayo. My turkey was not store bought or kosher, so no salt prior. Do folks rinse the dry brine?
I found it way too salty too-especially the breast where you apply it directly to the meat.

OMG, OMG, OMG!!!

This is the best recipe! The turkey is divine and so full of flavor even before the gravy went on!

Grateful for the bird that gave itself for our table this year. Blessings all.

This was incredible. My relatives said it was the best turkey they've ever had. It was very tasty, tender & juicy, with a great crispy skin. Our 12 pound turkey took 85 mins. We will always do turkey this way now. (Note: We only had a butterball turkey, which is pre-brined. Wasn't quite sure how to handle that with the dry brine in the recipe. Decided to pat dry the turkey the night before, separate the skin, and just put a very small amount of salt. Then continued the recipe as written.

11 lb turkey cooked in 75 minutes at 400° pure convection. (Used Duke mayo for the “rub” base.) The gravy was a big hit too. Thumbs up from everyone at our family table. Thanks NYT and Kenji for this recipe.

I've wet- and dry-brined poultry before, and suspected this recipe may come out too salty -- but by consensus, it was just right. I'd describe it as about the same saltiness as deli turkey, but oh, so much tastier. The herb mayo made it crisp, golden-brown and flavorful. All steps in process were followed pretty closely. The gravy was smooth and rich. I'll use this technique the next time I roast a chicken. I look forward to making a Thanksgiving sandwich tomorrow using some reserved herb mayo.

This is the best turkey I have ever made, rave reviews all around. The only change I made was reducing the salt for the dry brining to 3 tablespoons and adding extra carrots under the rack (also delicious). This will be our go-to turkey from now on.

Superb.  Spatchcocking wasn't easy but not impossible.   Dry brined for 36 hours uncovered in fridge and used a good amount of the mayo rub; 2 hours in oven for a 20 lb bird using 6 minutes per pound rule found online for spatchcock turkey cooking.
