Honest - no hint of mayo in the final flavor.  Very tender, juicy, not salty.  Per reviews,  added wine and broth to pan and avoided scorching or smoking.   Easy peasy Thanksgiving.

I made this today for Thanksgiving dinner and it got rave reviews! The turkey stayed very moist (especially the breast meat) and it looked phenomenal! I was surprised that it was so easy. I did not spatchcock the bird, but it was still easy to apply the mayo mixture and very easy to cook. I will definitely be making this again. Don't wait till the last minute to get your fresh herbs, though, like I did. I had to skimp on the sage and grabbed the last of the parsley but it still tasted divine!
I will never make turkey any other way.  Perfect juicy meat, perfect crisp skin.  But use Duke’s Mayo!
Great turkey recipe

Well made it tonight for thanksgiving-so so salty, for me inedible.
Never saw the response to the question about rinsing it before cooking so I didn't, I only used half of the salt in the recipe.
The sauce was delicious and would use that again-no dry brining for me thank you.

Just made this today and it was a total success.  Next time I might wipe the salt off before a bit, but my family likes it.   It was far less stressful than I have experienced either as the cook or observing.  Thank you.
This was excellent. I told no one I used mayo, bc many have an aversion to it. The flavor was fantastic. I will never cook a Turkey any other way. Took less time to cook, and the outcome was prefect.
Yes and yes on the dry brining, but the skin came out soggy and mottled. Will go back to dry brining and a butter baste next year.

This was a disappointment. I was leery of the dry brine salting schedule, but did it anyway. Turkey came out too salty, and I am a fan of salt.

Oh well, at least the skin will be good. Nope. Soggy skin because it was saturated with mayonnaise! 

This a terrible recipe. My wife is rinsing the leftover turkey to remove some salt. To be clear, the recipe called for a half cup of salt. I used a quarter cup for a 14.5 lb turkey.

This worked lovely - put the bread dressing under the turkey (on a rack, in case I needed to remove the stuffing), and our 11.5 lb turkey was done in 90 minutes - very moist and flavorful.   The herb mayo is a winner - used some of the leftovers to coat brussels sprouts for roasting.

Really fantastic. Took around 80 min for our 14ish-lb bird. I used about 1/4 cup of kosher salt rather than the prescribed 1/2 c for the dry brine because it seemed like a lot. I'm glad I didn't use the entire amount, I think it would have been too salty (and I like salt). Didn't produce too many juices for the gravy. Best turkey I've made.

In the word of James Comey…Lordy! This made for one fine turkey. Spatchcocking my 16 lb turkey was a gory sport— had to use my felco pruners to get that backbone out, but the the spatchcocking/dry brining/ mayonnaise slathering made for the best turkey I’ve ever cooked. I’m 61 and have done this every year since 37.

Made this for the big day and it was a winner. The gravy was the star - I used home made chicken stock and added a squeeze of lemon juice at the end. Not sure 150 is high enough, I had a 17lb bird, let if go to 165 in the breast, rested for 30min and it was perfectly done everywhere.

Shhh…my son hates Mayo. Double shhh…as a good Midwesterner, I only use Miracle Whip. And at noon today, as I prepped the bird, I took a walk on the wild side. I slathered it with Miracle Whip, salt and peppered it generously, and roasted it. Best turkey ever, they raved (including my son). No telltale scent of any Mayo, gorgeous burnished color, and juicy dark AND white meat. Works!
Spatchcocked a 10 pound turkey. 450 degrees recommended by another recipe. Perfection ( no Mayo.)

Oh wow—disappointed. I’ve spatchcocked and dry brined my turkeys for years and always used an herb butter compound all over under the skin just as prescribed in this recipe using the mayo mixture. As a number have reported the skin came out flabby whereas I’ve always had a crackly crips skin using butter. The turkey meat itself was fine due to the dry brine, but I’ll go back to my tied and true recipe. https://nomnompaleo.com/post/66597843666/butterflied-big-bird-spatchcocked-turkey

I ended up using all the Mayo made and my skin did end up not crispy but delicious. I also found out using a rubber basting brush help get better coverage. Definitely make the gravy way before if you can or possibly use less of the stock if you’re on a time crunch and want goopy gravy. It still came out delicious but I like my gravy to be more thick and thin. I used the amount of salt the recipe asked for to brine and used more than a teaspoon for each breast. Didn’t over salt it, but I like sal

Been doing the mayo thing for years.  The turkey is never dried out and presents beautifully.  
My kids do the "dirty" work.  If you "allow" their help, be sure to have more mayonnaise than called for in this recipe, because a good amount winds up slathered on the helpers' arms,  clothes and the table.  Totally worth it!

Heritage turkey was kind of tough but the brining and Duke’s mayo mix worked out well. Might cut back slightly on the salt like leaving it out entirely in the mayo mix. Was slightly too salty. Also might just try an ordinary small turkey next time. The coating created a beautifully browned bird. For me, this is a keeper. Thanks, Kenji.

Fabulous! I cooked my Turkey a day ahead with this recipe, then, after it rested, carved it. I displayed the slices on a Nambe platter, which goes from fridge to oven, and heated it for 20 minutes before serving.
A thin layer of broth ladled on top after carving kept it very moist.
Thanks for the great recipe.
Excellent. I dry-brined the turkey for 36 hours. It was juicy and tender, except for the parts where I couldn’t get the salt under the skin.
Cooked too high too fast. 11 lb turkey already over cooked in a hour. I would turn down oven after it browns.
I made this turkey following the recipe exactly and it was by far the best turkey I have ever had!

A  no joke. Was skeptical but now I know. Trust in the magic of mayo. Will def use herb Mayo for other roasts and spreads. Can’t wait to do this again. 

NOTE: when spatchcocking, found it hard to just snap the breast bone in half. Def took back of a heavy chefs knife to split it.

I tried this recipe today on a turkey breast. Unfortunately we overcooked the turkey breast, and ended up with dry turkey, but the skin tasted awesome. We cooked it on convection roast and I think we had the rack we cooked it on too high. I would love to see recipes for convection roasting. It's really a pain to have to read a recipe and the oven directions and try to put it all together. I will definitely try again.

I was skeptical of this recipe at first but then thought why not try it. I had my turkey spatchcocked, dried the skin well with paper towels and then salted 24 hours before cooking. Pulled the turkey out (which the skin was shriveled and dark) I proceeded and slathered on the mayo, cooked for 90 minutes and it was the BEST TURKEY EVER! Amazing flavor, juicy and beautiful, crisp skin!
Best turkey I ever had.  Followed recipe  without subs/changes.  It was great.

I've found Mr Lopez-Alt's recipes very reliable (his bread especially) but this wasn't very successful.  The dry brining was very effective, the breast meat remained moist and tender.  So far so good; I'll repeat that.  But the herb mayonnaise tended to burn on the fowl (managesble), and a good bit of it slid to the roasting pan and burned badly, rendering the drippings unusable.  If I did this again, I'd use a lot less of the mayonnaise mix, and keep a little liquid in the roasting pan.
This came out WAY too salty for our taste. Used Morton’s coarse style kosher salt and a free range Mary’s turkey. Very disappointing.

Did you use 6 tbsp or 1/2 cup? Morton's has smaller salt crystals so there's more "salt" by volume. Also, the one thing I wish Kenji was more clear about is that you might not need all the salt depending on your bird and how aggressive you are getting into the nooks and crannies.

The most difficult part was spatchcocking the turkey due to the lack of a proper spring-loaded poultry shears. I made the turkey and gravy by the directions. The only reason I did not give 5 stars is that it was a bit on the salty side. Will reduce the amount of salt next time (I used Diamond Crystal). Brining did a good job of thoroughly seasoning the meat. Cooked a 12.5 pound fresh turkey @400 for 90 min. Skin was crisp and meat was juicy. Guests loved it.
The gravy works perfectly with 2 cups of stock.
This was bar none the best turkey I've ever had. All other recipes are being deleted.
So moist and delicious! Wipe salt off top after brining and before mayo. My turkey was delicious but salty

For thanksgiving 2022, I prepared my 10 pound turkey following this recipe. The spatchcocking certainly allowed for more even cooking and considerably reduced roasting time. But the skin on the underside of the bird didn’t brown at all and was unappealing and oily so after cutting up the parts I returned it to the oven on broil. 
The bird was cooked nicely but the mayonnaise made the bird seem oily and the herbs over powered and competed with the gravy I made from a homemade stock reduction.

Followed the directions exactly as written and it was a big disappointment for us too.  Agree with those who found it way too salty. I guess you DO need to rinse the brine off the bird.  And our 12 lb. Mary’s turkey cooked way too quickly with the breast meat being overcooked in an hour and five. We found the gravy somewhat bland as well. 
The skins on the bird was indeed beautifully brown and crispy — though way too salty.

This recipe takes at least 2 hours of prep time - and the end product is not an improvement over more simple recipes. I am a big fan of spatchcocking, and thought the idea of using the backbone along with the giblets to make the gravy is a good one, however the veggies and herbs overtake the meat flavor with this recipe.
This was the worst turkey we have ever had. It was so salty it was almost inedible and dry as a bone.  It looked delicious but the skin was soggy and the salt was all you could taste.  Yuk.

Made it as a whole bird and it looked and tasted great! Didn’t taste overly herby—and I think more lemon would be a nice addition. 
Will do this one again!!
Grilled whole turkey with this method.  Made half the recipe of the herb mayo.  Poured one large bottle of Scrumpy’s hard apple cider in bottom or roasting pan.

The best and most beautiful turkey ever, as reported by my family and friends. I started air-drying on Tuesday, but an extra day could help if you have a turkey over 11.5 pounds. The herb mayo is terrific and did its job for flavor, juiciness, and tenderness. My convection was set to roast mode. The browning is significant and even. I checked with my thermometer at 60 minutes, 75 minutes and pulled the turkey out at 85 minutes. I made the gravy on Mon. and added pan juices while reheating it.

Kenji once again comes through! I’m not a huge mayo fan, but the herb mayo in its own was delicious, and when roasted on the turkey it helped to create one of the most delicious turkeys ever. Followed the spatchcocking and cooking instructions the the letter and the turkey was cooked to perfection. 

I would definitely keep a close eye on the turkey with an instant read thermometer around an hour in, and also rotate the sheet pan the turkey is on every 20-30 minutes to ensure even browning.

Also one other tip to those in the future doing the dry brine, you don’t have to use the whole half cup of salt. You just want to make sure the bird has a nice even coating of salt in every nook and cranny, but don’t want to start caking it on. If there’s still some visible salt on the turkey when you take it out to cook, I would wipe it off. I would say we used between 1/4-1/2 cup of salt for a 13.5 lb bird.

Fabulous recipe. My bird was 15#, took 2 hours but could have been maybe 15# less. Never had a turkey with juicy breast meat before this! Though the legs were slightly over done. The mayo herb blend was very good, not too salty w the brining. At the end I defaulted to my standard southern cream gravy bc I was too tired from all the TDay prep to read another recipe. Can’t go wrong w cream gravy on everything from fingers to dressing and potatoes ;-)

Overall great recipe,  got lots of compliments on Thanksgiving.  A large set of tin snips will work if you can't find poultry shears for spatchcocking.  The mayo doesn't change the taste of the turkey at all.   Pay attention to the "low sodium" in the broth.  My gravy was a bit salty.

I have been cooking Thanksgiving for 30 years. Note: you can spatchcock with kitchen shears. Where there is a will there is a way. This method was most successful in terms of flavor, ease, execution. Made as directed, with the exception of using mayo with olive oil (Hellman's). For comparison, I spatchcocked one 12 lb bird the night before and roasted a second 12 lb bird using traditional method Thanksgiving Day. Never again. The spatchcocked  bird yielded better results by far.

This bird was perfection. I used a locally raised bird with no pre-salting. If you only use the amount of salt recommended and let it brine for 1-2 days, the salt absorbs and there is no need to rinse. Rinsing would undo the drying of the skin that occurred during the brine, which is what makes it crispy. The mayo rub had spectacular flavor and everyone, including picky children, raved about the flavor- even in the breat meat! I have a winner for years to come, thanks Kenji!

This wasn’t my favorite turkey.  It was my first time dry brining and spatchcocking, and I thought the bird was dryer and saltier than with the wet brines. I may need to try again just to see if maybe it was a one-time thing. 

The mayo rub was great - I think I may have added a little too much herbs to mayo, and not blended it quite enough, but it worked well on the turkey, and made for absolutely delicious sandwiches. Made my own mayo.

The spatchcocked 14 lb bird was a tad too big for the half sheet baking tray that is the only thing I have that can fit in my oven and even close to fit the bird. It was hard to maintain enough stock in the pan to prevent burning while not having it so high it dripped out - a mess in the pan and the gravy didn’t have the benefit of drippings. 

Definitely line the bottom of your oven well with aluminum foil if you don’t have a deep sided pan.

I used 6 TB of Mortons Kosher salt on our 13 lb. turkey and it was very salty.  I did not brush the salt off before roasting as all recipes said that was unnecessary. After looking at several dry brining recipes the next day I read that one recommended ratio is 1 TB Mortons to every 5 lbs. of turkey which means I used twice the amount of salt. I won't be eating the leftovers.

Over-salted and over-done.  Used a remote thermometer in the breast, pulled the bird at 150 f.  Carry-over must have have overcooked it, should've pulled it earlier.  Dry brine was uneven and generally too salty, my fault, used Morton's - be careful.  Mayo marinade was excellent. No fault of Kenji's - he does his homework - just beyond my skill level on the first go.

Delicious! I dry brined on Wednesday morning with a store bought brine mix. Did not rinse off.
My 14lb bird took about 1.5 hours to cook at 400. Made the stock on Wednesday too. Since there is no basting, the drippings were scarce so did not use in gravy.
Winner Winner Turkey Dinner !!! 80 minutes in the oven. 60 minutes to rest. Just as good as my “go to” deep fried turkey without the extra fuss.

Made this last night and almost everyone said it was the best turkey they had ever had. I think this will be our Thanksgiving turkey preparation from now on. Wondering how necessary the diced vegetables under the baking rack are? We did Sam Sifton's more traditional gravy rather than this one. Amazing!

For once a truly moist turkey. We used a plain old organic Butterball, followed the directions, and it was excellent. I have "mayonnaised" a turkey before: the dry brining, alongside the herbs added to the mayo, makes all the difference.
We made this. It was the best turkey I have ever eaten. Didn't change a thing. Logan: Don't rinse or wipe off the salt.

I tried Kenji’s recipe for dry brining the turkey and did wipe the brine off before applying the mayo. I added orange zest, cumin, chili powder, chopped sage and parsley to the mix. Best turkey ever! I starred spatchcocking my turkey years ago and combined with this dry brine technique and the miracle of mayo, I’m never going back!
Way too salty! The turkey was inedible. I followed the recipe exactly. The gravy was great, as was the herb coating. So disappointed.

The Best Turkey!  The mayonnaise makes for a wonderfully tasty  and juicy bird - even the breast meat, and I am an avowed dark meat fan.  The dry brining worked quite well, but I think the salt might be best thought of as a maximum amount for a large bird.  I used roughly half of the amount for an 11 pound turkey.  You’re not curing it, so it should seem somewhat heavily salted, but you will need to use your senses.  You can spatchcock without shears using a decent chef’s knife.

Disappointing. I usually use the Braised Turkey Parts recipe to outstanding success. It’s more work and more time standing at the stove and this year I needed a simpler recipe. I am an experienced cook and have catered on the side - I followed the recipe exactly. I’m used to my braised Turkey falling off the bone and this was a giant struggle to carve this bird. The taste was disappointing and parts of it were still unpleasantly green. I won’t try it again.

Dutifully watched the temperature but one breast was slower hitting temp than the other.  Should have pulled it sooner as the turkey wasn’t moist.  I think this may have been my least successful turkey ever.  Turkey is such a big endeavor and cost, will think some more before I try again.  It does get points for cooking fast.

This is by far the most delicious turkey we have ever had. Moist, flavorful, and even better, it cooked in just under 90 minutes (12 pound turkey). The butcher spatchcocked it for me, so preparation at home was easy. Just make sure the pan is large enough for the legs to spread out and leave the breast area open. This will be our go to recipe for turkey from now on.

Made as written except I ended up with a longer dry brine (48 hours).  The turkey was delicious - all the meat was tender, the skin was crisp and it only took about 1.5 hours for an 11 pound bird.  Will make again next year!  Spatchcock and mayo for the win!

This was great. We're going to do it again next year. I do wish the salt was specified by weight. Per Hugh's comment, we used 72 grams of salt, and thought this was a bit too much. The meat was wonderful. The skin was a little too salty, although still tasty. Next year we'll try maybe 55 grams.

As to why Kenji dropped the baking powder from this recipe: probably the mayo negates the effects of the baking powder, which creates small bubbles on the surface of the skin.
Remind people to spatchcock before they brine.

So easy. I prepared the stock the night before with the wings (not recommended, but I cut them off because nobody ever eats them), neck, and backbone. Will definitely use this method and recipe in years ahead.

With all due respect, I will be returning to Samin Nosrat’s Buttermilk brined turkey next year. That one was spectacular, cooked quickly and evenly, beautifully browned and extremely moist. This Mayo turkey was extremely salty, even though I could only bring myself to use about 1/2 the salt called for. It cooked and browned unevenly, and the dark meat is rubbery. Very disappointing.

Superb turkey. I made my own mayo from high quality olive oil. The herb mayo infused the bird with lovely flavor  and it was super moist. The spatchcocking works - an 11lb bird cooked in 80 min perfectly evenly.  Will be doing this again!!

I’ve been preparing my holiday turkey “a la J. Kenji spatchcock method” for years now to rave reviews,
so thought I’d give the herby mayo a try. It was so delicious and easy.

Great recipe, the meat was very juicy but as other comments already noted too salty. I think if I were to do this recipe again I would wash off the dry brine before putting the mayo herb sauce on the turkey. 

Also I would recommend doubling checking if your cooling rack is oven safe. I originally used a non-stick cooling rack and had to switch it out five minutes in because I realized it wasn't oven safe.

This is a great recipe and I tried for Thanksgiving yesterday. We had a 12 pound all natural turkey and after dry brining the day before and the mayo rub, it only took about 70 minutes for it to be done. The meat was juicy and very tasty!
Came out waaaaaay to salty.  Was I supposed to rinse after try brining.  I wiped excess salt off but did not rinse the turkey.

We made this yesterday including even opted to make our own mayonnaise.  As Tony R notes, we inserted into the breast as we spatchcocked the bird.  We checked at the 1 hour and at 1:20 and the bird barely moved from 129 degrees.  Alarmed we got another thermometer out and had the same result.  We then gave it 15 more minutes and it jumped to 160 degrees.  We will definitely make again but will be sure to keep a much closer eye and not let the bird fool us again.

Brining the spatchcocked turkey went fine, but…when we blended the mayo and herbs in the Vitamix, it broke down into an oily slurry. Nonetheless, we forget ahead spreading the thin goo under the skin and on top. After about 1hr45mins the 15#turkey looked GREAT. Tasted wonderful, too. So don’t be afraid to use the mayo even if it breaks down. Delicious!

The mayo/dry brine combo did result in a juicier and more flavorful bird- but the gravy was very salty. Perhaps the additional soy sauce and the drippings from the brined bird was a bit much? Nothing a little more broth and roux couldn’t fix
Yes, the veggie broth under the turkey was very salty.  I used a bit of it to add to the (unsalted) chicken broth for gravy, and it was great.

I had high hopes for this recipe, and followed it pretty exactly, but it was too salty in the end. The other thing I would note is that in making the herbed mayo, if I were to do it again I would grind up the herbs separately and then add to mayo. By blending the herbs and mayo together with a blender, the mayo separated and it became more like herbs in oil, thus negating the purpose of making something that would stick to the turkey instead of immediately dripping down into the pan.
Delicious recipe!! Overcooked a bit due to cooking in an unfamiliar kitchen without a thermometer. Leftover herb mayo fantastic in leftovers today!

Over-salted disaster! Maybe it didn't brine properly because my fridge was too cold. 12 lb heritage bird wasn't completely defrosted when I brined exactly as directed, and there was still salt visible when I applied the herb mayo. (Strongly recommend putting herbs in food processor then stirring into mayo. Following instructions left mayo separated and thin.) 
Spatchcocked bird cooked quickly, but no more evenly than normal. Herb flavor overwhelmed by saltiness. Nearly inedible. What a waste.

One of the best and easiest turkey recipes we have ever done. Since we were cooking for 20 people, we doubled the mayo and did two smaller 9lb turkeys. By cooking two turkeys on a gas grill to reserve oven space for other dishes, we didn’t spatchcock so we could get them both on the grill together in their own foil roasting pans. The dry brine was easy and made a nice canvas to add the mayo. The birds cooked quickly in ~2.5 hours and rested 20 min. Came out fantastic and the meat juicy.

I've salted and spatchcocked chicken previously which worked very well, so Kenji's scientific explanation was very plausible (as usual).  However, I think it might be a better idea to split the dark meat from the white to ensure proper moisture for each. The mayo worked well to avoid basting and kept the meat moist and gave the skin a beautiful golden color.

The mayonnaise coating is an excellent base for a Cole slaw. Thinly slice some green and purple cabbage, shred some carrot, and add a couple of tablespoons of the mayonnaise coating with a few tablespoons of red wine vinegar. Perfection!

Fabulous!!!

Adapted it to a three pound (double) turkey breast, and I overshot the cooking time, but it was awesome.  Halved the herb mayo which was a bit too much, but really just fine.  Put a layer below and above the skin.  Used one teaspoon of salt on each side of each breast, and it wasn't salty at all. The skin was crispy and delicious like bacon, the gravy was the star of the meal! So delicious.  I didn't have quite enough stock so I made up the difference with white wine.

A word of caution on the instructions.  

For the gravy, if you drop your mirepoix into almost smoking oil on high, your onions will brown long before the carrots are even soft; well under 15 minutes.  Must turn the heat down to get to "lightly browned."

This was the best Turkey I ever made and frankly the best I’ve had in any setting. Mix and matching a pan and cooling rack took some creativity, but o figured it out. The dry brining was easy enough, a little under the skin of the breast, a little under the thigh skin, made it rain on the exposed side of the bird (the breast cavity) and maybe a tablespoon on the skin side. Likely a total of 3-4 Tbsp. total salt. Made the herb Mayo as described, used a cup and a half and had a little left over.
This was the most moist turkey I have ever made.  The leftover herbed mayo is ideal for turkey sandwiches the next day.

I used a well-trusted Greek brine to brine the turkey for 24 hours, and then followed this recipe exactly to the T.
The turkey was a massive success this year, and got rave reviews. Thank you, Kenji!
Perfect -- couldn't believe how tender and juicy the breast meat was. Followed recipe exactly except roasted at 450F for the first 15mins then 400F for an additional 75 minutes for a 15lb bird
Too salty.  Not by a lot, but next year I'll cut the salt, or rinse the turkey.  Which?

I was concerned the amount of salt for the dry brine would make the turkey meat salty but it didn’t. We used Diamond kosher salt and followed the recipe directions exactly. The turkey and gravy was so flavorful—the meat was moist and perfectly seasoned. We loved the way it turned out so much we are already planning to make it again for Christmas. We wouldn’t change a thing on the prep of this recipe.

Not sure what went wrong?! 13lb turkey with thermometer in thigh. Seems that the top cooked reaching 170 degrees F, so I let it rest for an hour and then carved it, but the lower half of the turkey was not fully cooked (pink to raw). Should I have cooked skin side down then halfway skin side up?

I read all the review notes. Many reviewers said to cook the turkey to 165 degrees. But I took the chance and followed Kenzi’s advice, cooking a Butterball to only 150 (actually I hit 155 using convection). The result was the most tender and perfectly moist (almost juicy) turkey I’ve ever had. It was not undercooked at all. 

Just in case, I cooked a Thanksgiving “backup turkey” using Mark Bittman’s spatchcock recipe. That too was perfect, after two days of dry brining an organic turkey.

I cooked a 24 lb spatchcocked turkey.  I started brining it on Monday evening and cooked it on Thursday morning.  It was so big that I had to brine and cook the legs on a separate roasting pan.   I used the convection roast feature on my oven and cooked it at 400 for 30 minutes, then reduced the temp to 350.  Estimated cooking time for a spatchcocked turkey is 6 min/lb. Start checking it after it has been cooking 4 min/lb. and cook until the breast temp measures 150 and thigh measures 165.

First time hosting Thanksgiving for my extended family in 20 years and this was a HIT!!!!  I was concerned with the presentation and everyone was all about it!!! Made sense!!!  Very forgiving recipe. I started the air brine late and it was perfection.  I just might make another turkey for Christmas!!!

Thank you!!!  The little sister has proven herself in the kitchen!
This was the best turkey I have ever made - by far.
A waste of effort. The skin was not crispy and it was difficult to salvage enough drippings from the pan to make gravy. The herbed mayo does make good sandwiches, though.

Not sure what I did wrong, but I made the mayo a couple of days ahead, and it completely separated. It was a pile of green goo with oil floating on top. I used Duke's mayo and fresh herbs and followed the recipe exactly! Maybe too many herbs? I added more mayo right before using it to reconstitute it, but it still looked nothing like the photos. BUT, the turkey was amazing. Moist, flavorful and delicious. One of my guests even asked, "How did you get the turkey to be so moist?" A keeper recipe!

I tried this, minus the spatchcocking. The mayo baste and the gravy were great, and I will do both again.  But the meat was too salty and the skin was inedibly so - couldn't even throw it into the stockpot with the carcass the next day. Most of the vegetables in the roasting pan just got burned along with the drippings, so fewer tasty drippings for the gravy and a waste of vegetables.

After 25 years of turkey cooking, this recipe is THE BEST I've ever make.
The skin was crispy and the meat succulent! I followed the directions (dry brine then 
fresh herb mayo). It did take longer for the bird to reach 155 degrees than the directions.
Everyone was full from appetizers so the wait was a good thing!
Thanks NYT Cooking for a winner of a recipe!!
The recipe should mention spatchcocking but once I worked out that that was the intention, this recipe was amazingly easy and produced the moistest turkey in years, great flavor too!

I'm a novice when it comes to cooking turkey. To prepare, I practiced making the mayonnaise which was easy and delicious. My butter spatchcocked the bird and cut all the odds and ends (neck, etc.) into small pieces, in preparation of making gravy. I think the bird cooked a bit more than I anticipated but it was pretty good. Oddly, I wondered why I didn't have any substantial amount of liquid under the turkey, to add to the gravy.
15 lb turkey cooks for 100 mins

I'm a Kenji fan and have made his spatchcocked turkey before (from his book) with good results. This one I don't get. It was moist but the herbs just get carbonized and add little flavor except maybe a little under the skin. I'll try a plain mayo slather next time.

I opted to try this (with whole, not spatchcocked turkey) instead of my usual Julia Child olive oiled cheese clothed method. Going back to Julia next year.  I will make the Mayo to put on my sandwiches tho. That was delicious.  My turkey was not as browned as I’m used to and the skin was not crispy. It seemed like a great idea, but not a success at my house.

Thanks for the good notes.  I rinsed the salt, patted dry with paper towels before slathering.  All turned out most excellent, and I will definitely spatchcock again.  It was helpful to have an assistant hold the knife while I whacked it with the rolling pin.  The only challenge was the timing.  I had a nearly 16 lb turkey and needed about 2.5 hours at 400 degrees and then 30-45 minutes to rest.  Very tender and moist, great flavor.

One more thing, I put the back, neck and other goodies into the Instant Pot with onion, bay leaves, peppercorns (whatever you like in your stock) for 60 minutes with very long natural release for super, super easy stock for the gravy (and plenty left over for the soup on Saturday).

Roast the neck, backbone, wing tips and excess skin for a more flavorful stock for the gravy. Conserve energy by pre baking some of the other thanksgiving dishes with it. 
20lb turkey took 2 hrs and 15 mins @ 375°F.

My dry brine was all absorbed. I really liked this recipe. I used the backbone from spatchcocking along with the neck and made my gravy the day before Thanksgiving. I didn’t have any pan drippings (roasted a farm fresh turkey, is that why?) but the gravy was delicious without adding drippings.

This was my first time roasting an entire turkey - and it was a success! I got some help flattening the turkey after removing the backbone, but poultry shears really did the job otherwise. Made a beautifully browned and perfectly cooked turkey. Made the gravy and it had a nice rich flavor as well (I did have some drippings from the bird to add to the stock and no problems with burned veggies). Would make again!
Best turkey I ever had. Incredible! Dry brine, spatchcock and mayo for the win! Can’t go back after this. :)

This worked very well. The butcher took out the back bone, and that worked just great. I think it’s a misnomer call this brining. A brine is a liquid, usually with salt and sugar. This is just salting the meat generously before you cook it. I don’t think you need to wash it off. It came out very well. The mayo covering was delicious.

Absolutely the tastiest turkey EVER.  Don’t use Morton’s Kosher salt; it is too salty.  Instead use Diamond Crystal.  Enjoy the crispy skin while it’s hot.  The herby mayo is delicious on leftover turkey sandwiches.  The gravy is also excellent.  Thanks be to Kenji!

Followed the recipe to a tee and it turned out just like the picture.  The skin browned quickly so I tented it under an hour.  Only downside is there were no pan drippings, but gravy recipe with reserved backbone was delicious.  Also cook time was 2 hours for 7.5 pound turkey breast (turkey with wings and legs removed).
This was lauded as the best turkey we'd ever made, from guests who are not big on turkey to begin with. Definitely making it again!

This was by far the crispiest and tasty turkey I have made in a long while. The skin was super crispy, much like the results of fried turkey without the major hassle. Also, had way more flavor from the herbs in the mayo. Thank you Kenji! Next up, making Samin Nosrat's Turkey Tikka Masala with the leftovers. Another 5 star winner that knocks it out of the park.

This was a pretty disappointing recipe.  A far cry from Samin Nosrat’s buttermilk turkey or just using herb butter on a brined bird. Mayo may stay on the bird marginally longer than butter, but trace amounts of butter taste dramatically better than trace amounts of melted mayo on a roasted turkey.  Some tucked away corners never melted and the mayo sauce was gloppy and flavorless.

Used Morton kosher salt and cut amount to 1/3 cup. Followed all other parts of the recipe to the letter.  Turkey was about 12 pounds. Cooked very quickly just slightly under 1 1/2 hours. Meat was tender, juicy and flavorful. salt level was good but hit a salty spot on the thighs. Herb mayo was a banging and will make awesome sandwiches tomorrow.  Gravy was good, no complaints. Overall, pleased with dinner & would make again.

I've used Kenji's approach to spatchcocking a roast chicken for awhile and was eager to experience it with a turkey.  No way and no how.  I'm a 5'1" cook but even my male sous chef, a pretty strong guy, was unable to do this.  Next time, the butcher does it!  Still used Kenji's recipe from the Cooking Lab for the whole bird, and it was the best turkey ever!

I've had plenty of turkey that was dreadful, to the point I felt I just didn't like turkey. This recipes proves that I can love a turkey meal, rather than just suffering through it.

One small note - the half cup of salt is much too much unless you are using very coarse salt. I started with the half cup, but only applied 2/3rds, and even that left the bird a touch too salty. Half a cup of fine sea salt is probably more on the mark.

Everything else in this recipe was brilliant.

This was by far the best turkey we have ever made (even though our oven DECIDED TO STOP WORKING ON WEDNESDAY THE DAY BEFORE THANKSGIVING)!!!!) 
We had already spatchcocked the bird and made the  herb mayo. My husband ended up grilling the turkey on our smallish Weber charcoal grill. The gravy also turned out perfectly even without any pan drippings.  Highly recommend this recipe.

came out great.  Salted for 36 hours and didn't use quite all of the salt called for.  The veggies/drippings were a little dark so I didn't add to my gravy.  I thought my gravy was a little salty but others did not.  I didn't have low-salt broth (used better than bouillon and would just make it weaker next time).  13.25b turkey cooked in 90 minutes and was juicy and well-seasoned.  Definitely repeating next year.

This was so so so successful!  Biggest challenge was getting under the skin without tearing it. But I prevailed. Well worth dirtying the hand blender to make the green mayo.  Thank you NYT and KLA for your smarts!

This was amazing. Dry brined 2 days and cooked whole with the baking steel. 17 lb bird cooked freakishly fast, in 2.5 hours! Everyone raved about it and the drumsticks were particularly luscious. Made a ton of gravy and hardly any leftovers!
Wonderful, go to recipe for future Thanksgivings. Thank you Kenji! Grateful. It really worked! I no longer dread the Turkey.

Didn’t make the gravy part of the recipe, but the turkey with the mayo itself was moist and flavorful, perfectly seasoned. One of the best I’ve made, and I usually try a new twist each year. The make-your-own mayo in the stick blender jar is just GENIUS.
Super moist flavorful turkey. Used this recipe with a heritage bird and it turned out perfectly.

This was fabulous!  I’ve used a dry brine and the spatchcocking technique previously, but have never made a mayo rub.  Everyone loved how moist and tasty the turkey was this year!  I’m going to try the technique on roasted chicken too - a five-star repeater recipe for sure!
Great recipe, would repeat. I’d just go a little easier on the salt on the breast for the dry brine - I think I might’ve gone overboard on that one. Otherwise outstanding all around!
Do you put the salt for bringing UNDER the skin you have now loosened ot just on the outside of the skin?

Best Turkey ever. Spatchcocking a Heritage breed was a challenge, and we have really good poultry shears. Next time I might opt for a younger Turkey next time. Salt was not a problem, as I see many comments mentioning too salty. I used Diamond Crystal, and brined for 24 hours. The Mayo rub performed brilliantly. 
The skin was crispy, breast juicy and thigh meat melted in your mouth. Super easy to carve. Our go-to turkey recipe.
The mayo works well. Brushed it on beneath the breast skin and over it for extra flavor. As suggested the mayo doesn’t melt off and the seasoning stays on well. Skin was beautiful.

The best turkey ever, and I’ve hosted at least 20 Thanksgivings. Definitely need to get better shears, though. Be prepared: spatchcocking a turkey takes way more muscle than a chicken. But so worth it.
I was pretty worried about trying this out for the first time at Thanksgiving, but it was a huge hit. Everyone at the table said it was the best turkey they ever ate.

This worked beautifully with a 10# turkey. The bird was perfectly cooked in under an hour, and since we only have one oven, it made the day much easier because we could cook and reheat all the sides and have everything ready together. Almost a miracle!. The bird was very flavourful and super moist. I'll never cook a turkey any other way! Served with Eric Kim's stuffing recipe—although with gluten free breads, and a bit of Italian sausage—and it was deeelish as well.

Tried this on a locally sourced bird for Thanksgiving this year, but dry-brined covered for a day, then left uncovered for a second day to let the skin dry completely.  Rubbed on the Hellmans and a couple of hours later had a delicious, beautiful bird. 

Do yourselves a favor and find the Kanji video on how to care a spatchcocked bird - makes for a beautiful presentation as well.

I ended up being given a much larger turkey than planned—supply chain, avian flu, my grocery store said—so didn’t spatchcock.  However, I did take the legs off and cook them separately, while otherwise following the recipe.   It was work but resulted in the best turkey and by far the best gravy I have ever made.  So it was worth it, and I will do this again next time.

Rather than using the liquid from the roasting tray for gravy I deglazed the pan and kept the liquid to make turkey soup the next day. I didn’t skim the fat from the stock since it was full of flavor. Best stock ever!
Inspired! Everyone loved it so much for Thanksgiving we are making it again for Christmas Day.  I doubt I will ever use another roast turkey recipe.

I would also like to know how others handle the extreme saltiness of the turkey. What do you do about the clumps of salt all over the brined bird? We grabbed handfuls of it off before lathering on the mayonnaise layer - which we had decided not to “season generously with salt “, as the recipe stated.

Best turkey I've made.  Dry brining was perfect for a heritage bird we ordered, and the mayo coating was an easy way to get that lovely skin.  I used avocado mayo and it worked great.  First time I had cooked a spatchcocked bird, and it made things so much simpler, tho I confess I had the butcher do the work....
Absolutely phenomenal turkey this year. LOVED!
Can you do this with a larger turkey? 20 plus lbs?

Loved this recipe. Will always spatchcock turkey or chicken going forward. Brilliant. I followed the recipe exactly except that I used a tablespoon of Gochujang as the mayo flavoring instead of herbs. It wasn't spicy but added some brightness to the flavors. Also - this recipe makes the best juices for gravy - much less grease to deal with and lots of great flavor. It got rave reviews even from the folks that don't usually like turkey (which includes me).

It's not hyperbole that this was the BEST turkey and gravy ever in 40+ years of cooking a turkey. I followed the recipe to the letter, except I roasted it in an electric roaster for oven space. The skin doesn't get quite as crispy, but that didn't stop us from devouring it. It's the first time we didn't have pounds of left-over turkey that eventually just ended up in dog food bowls. The leftover drippings made an amazing soup! Mayo is king from now on in my house!
Buttermilk, not mayo, for the win. Buttermilk brine for several hours.

This recipe was SO good. Even after I accidentally started removing the breast bone instead of the backbone, it still turned out. Pretty much everyone who had it said it was the best turkey they’ve had. Thank you!

I thought the splaying, brining and cooking method where great.  Next time however I will skip the mayo and go for compound butter.  It would create a much better flavor.  OXO shears made the job easy.

I had a 10 pound turkey, but I cooked at 400 (425 was recommended). After one hour the bird was very dark. It was done in an hour and 15 minutes. In future, I would cook at 350. 
Was afraid it would be overcooked because it was so dark, but it was fine. The salt from the dry brining was not apparent.

The herb mayo mixture was great. I used the recipe as given with lemon zest, scallions, parsley, sage and thyme. I roasted the turkey whole, and it came out tender and delicious. I used 1/2 cup salt for a 20 lb turkey and it was enough. It was a messy process getting the mayo under the skin everywhere; I wonder if there’s an easier way to do it using a pastry bag or something.
"Amazing, amazing, amazing!  Never thought turkey could taste this good.  Really Aunt Rose this is good." shared my 19 year old nephew.
Does the mayonnaise break apart in the oven

My husband, looking on as I followed the recipe as written for the dry brining, "isn't that too much salt?"  I insisted on the 1/2 cup of kosher salt as instructed.  We grilled the turkey on a Traeger.  Our result...it was almost inedibly salty.  I was able to salvage leftovers by making turkey vegetable soup without additional salt.  Next time, I'll try using a lot less salt for dry brining and rinse the bird before coating with the herb mayo.

For those of you who cooked the turkey on a baking stone, did you spatchcock it and did you still use a rack under the bird over the veggies? I feel like the rack takes away the point of using a baking stone?

My 12.13 pound turkey would not fit on a standard household 1/2 sheet pan. The thighs and wings reached just beyond the edge of the pan which would have left my oven a mess. Luckily I had a 3/4 size commercial sheet pan on hand. Turkey is at it's best in my opinion when it gets a flavorful 24 hour wet brine. I enjoyed this recipe but probably will not repeat.
Easily the best turkey recipe I have made in the last 10 years.

The spatchcocking things worked well. Bird cooked evenly and quickly which probably minimized any drying out. The mayo was a bit of a dud, as I didn't really get the crisp skin I was looking for. Still very good and I'd do it again.
Exceptional, 100% worth the prep and effort. Best turkey I've ever eaten.

Can someone explain the logic behind cooking a smaller turkey at a higher temperature as written in Step 3? It would seem that a larger bird would require the higher temperature (if cooking times are equal) and that a smaller bird would be more susceptible to drying out. Experts please way in.

Cooking times are not equal. Cooking is done by final temperature, not by time.  You lower the oven temperature for the bigger bird to ensure it gets fully cooked without burning, considering it will stay in the oven significantly longer than the smaller bird (a 16lbs bird takes at least an hour longer than a 10 lbs bird).
2nd year in a row making this, because it turned out fantastic last year! Relatively straightforward, and I love having leftover herb mayo!
If cooking as a whole bird, what do we do with the veggies? Trying this recipe for the first time and am totally intimidated by the spatchcocking! Thanks for your help!
Oops, don't include the period on the link in my previous comment for it to work correctly.

Poultry shears will make the spatchcocking way easier! I follow the instructions given in another one of Kenji's recipes https://www.seriouseats.com/butterfiled-roast-turkey-with-gravy-recipe. Veggies are just used as a base to keep turkey moist while baking. and for the gravy.
I made this last year with a whole bird and it was fantastic. Can anyone recommend a modification for a breast-only version? We have a smaller group this year.

Assuming your are doing a full bone-in breast (5lbs), I would reduce the salt to 4 TB for the brine, increase the oven temperature to 450F and start checking final temperature (150F) after 40 min. I also don't think you can skip basting (every 10 min or so) if doing a breast only version. Buy some chicken or turkey wings for the gravy. Personally, I would select a different recipe - with a wet brine and less salt - if doing only the breast.

I made this for Christmas dinner last year and everyone said it was the best turkey they ever had....some of the credit, too, I think was using a Mary's organic turkey.  Getting ready now to make this recipe again for Thanksgiving and can't wait!
anyone cooked this using kewpie mayo for the marinade?

What can we use in place of mayo that’s egg free?  We have a family member with a severe egg allergy…I was planning to use copious amounts of European butter instead but if there’s a vegan mayo alternative that would work that’s been tested, please let me know.
This is, hands down, the best Turkey I have ever had.
Best recipe I've used, I actually skip the brine portion of this recipe though.
My 10 pound Turkey was fully cooked by 50 min.

Made today with Hellman’s for the second year in a row. It’s the second best turkey recipe after the Buttermilk Brined turkey (which I can’t eat after contracting Alpha-gal). The leftover mayo is fabulous on sandwiches!

When making this for Thanksgiving, 2023, I made the gravy with suggestions of this recipe, but just put the simmered veggies, along with their water and the schmaltz from our roast chicken together and used the immersion blender to purée it all together. Delicious and pretty healthy. My daughter showed me this trick when she and her husband went Paleo several years ago.
My gravy did not thicken after prolonged simmering. Next time I’ll add more flour.
Made this the first year to much success and left the bird whole. This year I was so proud to spatchcock it—cooked in about 70 minutes for an 11-12 lbs bird. So thankful for the recipe.
Made exactly as written. Most perfect, delicious, moist, packed full of flavor bird we've ever had. Outstanding. Can't imagine making turkey any other way ever again.

I've followed this recipe for two years in a row, and I intend to use it for all of the Thanksgivings I'll ever host in the future.  The result is not just a delicious, moist turkey, but it also is beautiful when it comes out of the oven. The skin turns a gorgeous crusty brown. The drippings yield an excellent gravy that everyone heaps on their mashed potatoes and stuffing.  The only recommendation I have is that you double the amount of mayo -- save some for sandwiches with the leftover turkey.
I strongly recommend adding lemon juice and a hot green chili to the mayo for the next day sandwiches.
This was so delicious! We won our family’s annual turkey contest this year. Followed the recipe as written up to the cooking — smoked it at 350 for a couple of hours and it turned out great.

I love this recipe. This was the second year I made it and it is a huge hit with everyone. The breast meat is juicy and flavorful. 
Spatchcocking the turkey was intimidating the first time, but it makes a difference in the cooking time and makes the day so much easier.

Used this recipe for my first Thanksgiving turkey. Nerve wracking but came out beautifully. Next time I'll make the mayo dressing ahead of time to allow the flavors to meld. The tip to check at the 1hr mark was perfect, too.

Have roasted spatchcocked turkeys many times, this year was the first using the herbed mayonnaise. Game changer!
15 lbs took 90 minutes in a convection oven. The legs were done in 60 minutes and had to be protected with foil while the breast cooked. (Turkey was from d’Artagnan.)

I followed this exactly as written for using a regular 14 lb. supermarket turkey (pre-brined, but I still salted mine anyway) and it was fine, nothing special, but definitely not bad.  I felt like the herb mayo didn't really make a huge difference, although the reserved portion was great on sandwiches later.  I will probably try a new turkey recipe next year, something with a little more oomph.
When it says to rotate half way through cooking g, does that mean to turn over so skin side is up or just rotate the roasting pan ?
just the roasting pan, to get an even cook across all sides within the oven, ovens tend to have uneven, hot areas that will dry out or brown one section more than others

Quick-text: 12lb at 405 for 40, flip, 400 for 40. Still not ‘done’ even at temp. Samples very salty. Flip. Return to oven at 385 for 25. Texture was great, saltiness was reduced, everything felt and tasted done.
I have made the marinade with Duke’s and Best Foods aka West Coast Hellman’s and liked them both.  I also made it with homemade mayonnaise and it was definitely NOT AS GOOD.
How would you do this on a gas grill?

This is the only way I will make turkey from now on. 

Also, regardless of whether I am making a turkey, I make the herb mayo year round so I always have it in my fridge for sandwiches or tuna/chicken salad. It's that good.

I dry brined my turkey Tuesday night instead of Wednesday.  Have I ruined it???  I used to wet brine starting on Tuesday and I jumped the gun and didn’t read the 12-24 hours… And I’ve made this recipe before and loved it.  I’m proceeding with this turkey… don’t want to waste.  :)
@Dorene I dry brine all my meats including Turkey and chicken for at least 2 days if I can. Nothing better than knowing you have a 2 or 3 day salted chicken in your back pocket ready to roast up.
How long to cook a spatchcock cocked 23lb turkey with this recipe ?

Made this for second time this Thanksgiving. 10 pound bird took 50 mins. Spatchcocking was easy with a good pair of shears. Family said it was the "perfect turkey." Breast was tasty and moist. Gravy was outstanding.

This is my favorite turkey recipe.  I’ve tried wet brines, cheesecloth soaked in butter, deboned and rolled, basted every 15 mins… I’ve never fried but im not going to because this was simple, quick, and absolutely perfect.
Used 1/4 cup Diamond on a 10.5 lb bird. 16 hours with the brine. Very good results.

I'm doing this turkey for the second time around; I have a ton of Italian parsley and sage from my garden. The lemon zest is key to the herb mayo; Make a full two cups because it's tasty enough to eat on anything. This time I'm doing the broth for the gravy in the pressure cooker. Aas, I'm out of carrots, But since I'm going to save most of the broth anyway, I can add those later and simmer them for a bit.

I think this is definitely a 5 star recipe.  This is by far the best recipe for obtaining juicy and flavorful white meat that does not taste like sawdust. I agree with other comments: if you don't rinse and dry the dry-brined bird.  If you don't, the turkey is a tad too salty- not inedible- but definitely highly salted.

Ive made this 2 years in a row and it's always a crowd pleaser. There was no need for dry brining as the turkey came pre-brined in a salt solution (I think it was a butterball). I made a little more herbed mayo then suggested as it was a 16 lb turkey. It took maybe 2 hours and let it rest about 20 minutes.

Made this recipe last night for my book club Christmas party. I am known for making an excellent turkey, stuffing, and gravy...and I have spatch-cocked and dry-brined a couple of times before, but this turkey was the best yet. The meat was so moist and flavorful. I dry-brined it for two full days.
Is there a difference when using low-fat versus normal mayo?
Herby butter is awesome. I used it on other foods.
Does this work with a wet brine vs a dry one? And obviously just make sure it’s patted down and dry before adding mayo mixture?
That's what I'm thinking too... I always get a wet brined bird from the butcher.
@-luke If the Turkey is already brined I think you would just skip that step completely. It's why you wouldn't do this with a Butterball.
That's right, I might skip the gravy being that wet brined birds are too salty for gravy...
For a 16-18 lb turkey, what is temp and cooking time?
@Blake We used this recipe last year and it was amazing. Definitely repeating this year. Our 17 lb turkey took about 2 hrs. Started at 400* oven for an hr then lowered to 350*
Cab you use Chosen Avocado Mayo instead of Best Foods?
Rinsing the dry brine off your turkey defeats the entire purpose of the dry brine itself. Just follow the directions, people...
Reduce the salt, and this recipe yields the yummiest turkey ever. My favorite in 30+ years of roasting a meat I really don’t care for.

Breast and thighs still cook at different rates in spatchcocked bird. I ended up with overcooked breast meat. I will never again cook the intact bird but rather separate out light and dark meat parts for cooking at varying durations.

I used the Cook's Illustrated version of salt-brined roast turkey for years. Last year I tried this recipe and I'll never go back! It's much easier and faster than the old recipe., and it is absolutely delicious! You will never regret using this recipe. It is to die for!

One week afree thanksgiving dinner and i’m still getting compliments on my first ever turkey. Absolutely fabulous, so damn delicious, super easy recipe with 10/10 results. Only change I made was adding more herbs and spices to my dry brine per Melissa Clark’s recommendation.

Kenji changes everything. What an amazing bird. I tell ya, no one ever says after t-day or xmas, "Boy that was some great turkey." We make turkey cause we have to. Well not after making this. Absolutely mind blowing good. The dry brine def works wonders. You gotta get a little personal with the turkey's breasts, but you'll get over it and the turkey never complains. The key to this has to be the herb-mayo. Game changer. The fresh herbs actually are a flavor blast-off. The only turkey option IMHO

I cooked this for the fourth time for this Christmas.  I agree with the comment to monitor doneness at the thickest part which was the breast.  For the number of people (and expectations of the leftovers) I did a 16 pounder.  If you have a butcher they can easily spatchcock it for you when you buy it.  I got worried about the saltiness (I misread teaspoons as tablespoons) so about an hour in I wiped some of the salt off. Also, nobody liked the idea of mayonnaise.  I substituted olive oil until it seemed the right consistency.  It was delicious and is now all gone including handing out leftovers and making the leftover turkey posole.
I add fennel wedges to the vegetable mix. Also, after cooking the turkey on the vegetables, I gather the vegetable mix,  puree it in a blender, and then freeze it to add to soups

title: Niku Udon (Japanese Beef Noodle Soup) Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

comments.commentBody: Namiko Chen's "Just One Cookbook" (which I access online) has several similar recipes, one of which I make frequently, "Simmered Beef with Ginger". She includes a YouTube tutorial for how to slice meat thin. Put a nicely marbled rib eye or chuck in the freezer for approximately 90 minutes to 2 hours. Remove and slice across the grain as thin as you want. It's that easy and makes these simmered meat dishes much more accessible.

WOW! Made this tonight pretty much to a T and it was wonderful. I only use low-sodium soy sauce so it could have benefited from a bit of extra salt, but that’s an easy adjustment for next time. For those near a Wegmans, they have very nice thin sliced shabu shabu style ribeye - it’s pricey (!) but worked beautifully in this. Used packages of Fortune pre-cooked udon noodles from the regular grocery store shelf (discarded the accompanying flavor packets).
Fresh udon are scarce here, but fresh Italian pasta is not - subbed in bucatini and used what my butcher calls "Philly meat".  Delicious and simple.  Thank you, Kenji.

Shichimi and nanami togarashi are two versions of, basically, the same thing. They are formulated slightly differently because a few ingredients like hemp seeds Were prohibited in some jurisdictions.
Rather than buying the small bottles I buy the large bags of S&B brand from AMAZON which is much cheaper per ounce.  If one is to only use it infrequently the small bottles are ok and like any spice may be fresher when used.

I made this for dinner tonight. It's absolutely easy and super delicious. This is going in our regular rotation. 

I used some leftover tri-tip (nice and fatty) that I sliced super thin on my meat slicer (thanks, wedding gift that seemed a little silly at the time!) Next time we'll use another piece of it that we have in the freezer. 

I feel like you could also thinly slice up some bok choy or something like that and put it in right at the end if you want a hit of vegetables other than onions.

I read this recipe yesterday and started craving it immediately. Luckily we live near an H Mart, so I was able to get all the ingredients and make it tonight. I made it per the recipe and it came out great. My whole family (including 2 teenage sons) loved it.

Easy and very tasty. I used dashi packets I got on Amazon - 4 packets in 2 quarts water. Costco has thinly sliced rib eye for just this purpose. I'd up the noodle quotient. Our Walmart (of all places) has packages of udon in their small but interesting Asian section. Where I live there aren't many grocery options but Walmart; they don't have that wide of variety but there's buried treasure sometimes (for some reason they carry frozen NYC bagels which are better than anything I can find here).

I have made this 3 times, trying to get it "right". Done trying.
1: Takes 3 pots to make & results in no leftovers.
2: If using fresh udon, knock off the extra flour and use A LOT of water or the cooking water turns into a starchy mess. It does not reheat well; don't cook more udon than will be eaten at that meal. Frozen was more forgiving. 
3: Ribeye varies. Be careful to select beef with little connective tissue or the meat will be stringy and inedible, no matter how thin. 
YMMV.

I was worried about simmering the meat for so long, but it turned out tender and delicious. 
I served with fresh, grated ginger and a little Boon sauce (chili oil).  Thank you Kenji, this was awesome!
Watched Kenjis YouTube Chanel which helped

For broth:
Used 5 cups water with 5 tsp hondashi granules. Threw in a hunk of mataki mushrooms, two green onions and brought to a boil. Added in 3 tbs soy sauce, 2 tbs mirin & 2tsp sugar. Brought to a simmer with mushrooms.

I read this and had to make it! Ordered Dashi "teabags" and Udon noodles. Bought "Philadelphia" shaved  beef and scallions. Forgot about the sake and mirin! But we had dry vermouth for the sake and I used apple cider vinegar for the mirin. This was so delicious! And the heat lovers in my family added chili paste to theirs. Comforting, yummy recipe!

So, so delicious. Used ginger as others had suggested, but other than that, exactly as the recipe said. The first time this year my husband is taking leftovers to the office for lunch… that says it all.
Simply delicious!  Had to add more soy sauce and mirin to the dashi. And I tossed some baby bok choy in with the beef for crunch.  Will definitely make this again!
Subbed Shaoxing wine for the Sake and it turned out great.

Tried this recipe tonight. It was really good. Next time I would cut the Bonito Flakes a bit to tamper the fish flavor. But that’s a personal taste (my wife disagrees). I would also add the sliced beef to the pan at the last minute to keep it tender. Will definitely add this to our regular list. Drink the rest of the Sake with the meal. Great pairing.

So, for a mostly vegetarian who does not like sea weed (among other leafy vegs) use a flavor sub, courtesy of a web search: dried mushrooms, dashi powder, bonito flakes (hate fish, use a sub, dried shiitake mushrooms, I have plenty ) and use any of the new alt meat crumbles instead of beef. I'll use Chinese cooking wine instead of mirin and instead of sake? I don't have it or any colorless hard liquor... hmm, white wine? I have a lot of them...the rest of the ingredients are simple :)

The beef gets tough when simmered for 10-15 minutes and it doesn't take on much flavor.
So ultimately not that good.

@Kater oh wow yeah, he does recommend that, that’s crazy, anyone who cooked thinly sliced beef at KBBQ or hot pot knows that’s wayyy too long. In boiling water it takes what, a minute to cook? I would drop that in right at the end. If you ever go to a pho place they’ll sometimes give you the thinly sliced beef raw and you just drop it in the hot bowl of soup.
I add a ton of thinly sliced mushrooms to the beef. So good!

Delicious!  I used good sake and good hand shaved Wagyu (partially frozen and cut thin with a sharp knife).  Plus I have two hungry teenage kids so I used 1.5 lbs steak, 30 ounces of fresh udon, 1/3 extra of the soy/mirin/sake, but 8 Cups of broth was still plenty.  I let the beef marinate in the soy/mirin/sake for a little while before cooking.  I sautéed the onions to put some color on them before dumping in the beef and sauce.  (One more thing, my wife is Filipina, so instead of adding extra soy and mirin to season the final broth I splashed in some fish sauce and Maggi 😂)

This is the first time I have used kombu in any recipe. The first time I made this dish I didn't know what to do with at after soaking as instructed. Turns out I don't have to waste it. Second time around I saved it and made tsukudani. It's absolutely delicious and makes for a great snack and side dish.
I'm a huge cheater, but believe it or not, this is delicious made with granulated dashi and ground beef!
@Michael McDaniel how did you cook the ground beef?
@Michael McDaniel did you cook the ground beef with the cup of liquid?
Made this for dinner tonight. We loved it! Added fresh shiitake mushrooms and some chopped rainbow chard that needed to be used up to the meat mixture at the end. Delicious!
Really cook. Togarashi was really helpful to bring out the flavors. QQ could you cook the noodles in prepared Dashi?
would use the same quantities of bonito and kombu called for two servings. Maybe even put in a seafood stock pellet. Also, skip the sugar completely. It was much too sweet.

Really nice, simple, comforting soup. I made a few adjustments--I added green onion, shallots, and garlic to the soup stock as it was boiling and strained it out with the katsuobushi. I think this added some more depth to the flavor.

Won’t the beef go tough if you cook it for 10 - 15 minutes? Or does the longish cooking time actually bring it back to tenderness? When I’ve had this kind of dish in hot pot style Asian restaurants normally the beef is just cooked for a moment so it stays v pink and tender. Any advice?
I am sure the recipe is correct, but it was a little bit of a stretch as we are new to dashi. Strong fish flavor. I am still breaking my family in to fish sauce. A little much for a first experience.
How would you adapt the recipe (if at all) if you use the Mentsuyu (Soy-Dashi-Mirin mix) from Kenji’s latest cookbook. Would you still make the dashi even though the Mentsuyu has dashi elements?

This is the first NYTimes recipe that I had to immediately make. If this is what the cook is eating at home then Kenji is doing some mighty fine eating!  Yum!!   We loved it!   I made it with shabu  beef from Costco. I used white wine instead of sake and I doubled all the portions to have extra.  I did end up adding salt because that is my taste and added more soy sauce and miring to the broth.   That is very easy to adjust according to your own taste.  Just taste it and add more if you want.
On shaved beef.  Can I lightly freeze a piece of Tri tip and then slice it thin?
Absolutely. I used some cooked tri tip we had for dinner last night. It worked really nicely because it's nice and fatty so it doesn't get rubbery.
I added a bit of chili flakes to the broth, and before serving, sprinkled the bowls of soup with chopped cilantro and it was great!
Trader Joe's usually has thinly sliced beef that should work for this recipe.  Aldi often does.  For cooks who live near an H-Mart, the udon noodles and dashi ingredients should be easy to find.

Using instant  Dashi packets—available at my Wegmans—and subbing dry sherry for the sake made this easy and avoided opening a bottle of 
sake for a couple of glugs.
The mirin is sweet enough without adding sugar.

We enjoyed it. Added shiitake and bok choy for some crunch and greens. Ended up using half the dashi, half the meat, and almost all of the sauce. Worked well for us with the pound of udon noodles and the extra veggies. Put the other half of the dashi in the freezer. Don't have shichimi togarashi so topped with Korean red chili threads which added a great visual accent. As suggested, light freezing really helped with slicing the beef. I had forgotten to put it in early, but 30 minutes helped.

I love this recipe because it's introduced me into the beautiful world of Dashi and solidified my decision to further explore Asian cooking techniques via 'The Wok'.
I used the overnight kombu recipe and found it lacking in flavor at first, then I reread the directions and it states using tablespoons of soy/mirin per serving (I was using teaspoons). That really brought things together and the subtle dashi flavor in the background was wonderful. I will be perfecting this, thank you Kenji!

The beef over rice with a soft boiled egg is great. The broth, while a vital ingredient, isn’t worth having alone. I topped it with toasted sesame seeds and chili crisp. Made with the simmered kabocha squash as a side vegetable.
Recommend marinating beef in soy, citrus and ginger. Fry beef in wok. Add vegetables and ladle in  dashi and remaining marinade.

I made this dish last night, and though it felt a big hectic as I don’t have much counter space right next to my stove, but I had all the ingredients at the ready. FYI Trader Joe’s has shaved beef in their fresh meat case. I made it just as written, only used one flavor packet from the refrigerated Udon I got at our local Asian food market. I was able to find the Dashi pepper there. I’m kicking myself for not putting more scallions and some Baby Bok Choy in it, but it was delicious as was!

Delicious!  Our Japanese market had all the necessary ingredients I didn't have in my pantry including dashi powder, and I used Boar's Head rare roast beef thinly shaved.  The broth is rich and flavorful and very satisfying.  Definitely a keeper.
Really, really good. Subbed shaoxing for sake; used shabu-cut ribeye. Should use quite a bit more udon noodles if you expect to serve four people, maybe two pounds.
Nice recipe. Probably not authentic, but I added a dash of toasted sesame oil for extra flavor, since sesame goes so well with beef.
Rare thin-sliced roast beef from the deli worked fine in my white-person opinion. So good that I made it two nights in a row at my husband’s request. Easy and straight-up deelish.
Could you use vermouth instead of sake?

Wow! This was my first attempt at a Japanese noodle dish and I have to say I was really happy with the way it turned out. I didn’t have sake so I subbed with Chardonnay but will use sake next time. The udon noodles I had were not very salty so I amped it up a bit with some extra soy. Our local Costco carries thinly sliced beef for shabu shabu which were perfect for this dish.

Made this for dinner since I’ve been craving nikutama for a few weeks now. If you live close to a World Market they sell Dashi powder in their food section. Added a poached egg to mimic how it is served in restaurants and it was great
I've made this twice added bok choy for a bit of veg. The udon soup was delicious. I used the dashi sachets that were recommended as a substitute in the original article.
made with hondashi and hot pot meat. yum
Thank God for H Mart. Half the ingredients came from there.

Very good! Even my picky Chinese husband enjoyed it. I followed directions to a T, but did use HonDashi as a shortcut and threw some baby bok choi in the broth for some veggies. Per my husband’s suggestion, next time I’ll marinate the meat in some soy sauce and mirin before cooking it!
I chopped up a head of broccoli and threw it in while the water was boiling for the udon. Yummy!

Several suggestions:
1) If you have time, leave the kombu in room temperature water for 30 minutes or so beforehand. If you find yourself in a hurry often, get dashi teabags: they’re designed to steep more quickly.
2) The onion takes much longer to cook than beef, so start simmering the onion and add the meat later.
3) Shaved lean pork works just as well, arguably better as it keeps flavor more readily when simmered. Most udon restaurants in Japan use beef, but home chefs often use pork.
@zogrin what cut (or kinds of cuts) of pork is best?
I tossed pre-cooked udon noodles in the Dashi to skip a step and save a pot! This dish was great, I would add some greens and mushrooms to round it out a bit. It’s going in the rotation.
I needed to add salt because it lacked taste once all was said and done. It was pretty great then, but I feel the meat needs some additional umami, and the dashi felt too similar to miso soup.

This hit just right for a vegetarian dinner! Subbed a block of extra firm tofu in for the beef. Sliced the tofu thin, into 24ish 2”x4” pieces. Pan-fried the tofu in the skillet with sunflower oil and some sea salt. Cut into thin strips after pan-frying them. Made the dashi with 7-8 dried shiitake in place of the bonito flakes. Double or triple the soy sauce in the dashi. Didn’t have Togarashi—furikake and chili crisp were tasty subs. Got rave reviews from everyone!

I made this and it was very good, but definitely in the comfort food category, not adventurous and super-flavorful. I added extra togarashi to kick it up a bit. Next time I'm adding a little grated ginger to the dashi, I think.

- 5 tsp of dashi granules in 5 cups of water + 3 T soy sauce + 2 T mirin + 2 green onions, chopped in 1/2 to infuse flavor + a splash of sake
- Sautéed everything in step #2 **except the beef**, PLUS an 8 oz. package of sliced mushrooms + the stems of a head of Chinese broccoli (cut in 1/2 lengthwise & chopped @ an angle like the scallion whites) until soft
- Added the beef and chopped Chinese cabbage leaves at the end, in order to keep the beef tender
- Drizzled with chili crisp
YUM!!!!!
best egg for gyudon is an onsen egg ...
Can you use another protein like chicken thighs?
I'd also like to know, as I'm mostly vegetarian and never eat beef.

Kenji (or fellow posters):  do you have any recommendations for how to make a vegetarian dashi with sufficient umami to replace to replace the missing katsuobushi?  Suggestions I've seen so far include using shiitakes and dulse (with dulse replacing the kombu), but any other ideas would be very helpful.  Thanks very much

Why would you replace the kombu for a vegetarian dashi? Kombu is kelp and is a sea vegetable. 

I use dried shiitake steeped in water to make a strong broth concentrate and combine with the kombu. After adding the mirin and soy sauce in the recipe I don't think there will be a big flavor difference between it and awase dashi (I also use a little sake). My vegetarian friends in Japan make vegetarian dashi this way a lot!
When do you add the togarashi here?  I don't see it in the instructions
at the end - step 5, "Serve immediately with shichimi togarashi to taste." :)
Very tasty cold weather meal. A little confused about how much soy sauce to use. Do you add it once or twice?
Twice. Once to the meat and onions and again to the dashi / broth
just a small fyi, recipe mentions sake in list of ingredients, but no mention in preparation.
It's part of the liquid you boil the beef & onions in. See step 2.

@Jascha 
Goes in when you cook the beef. It’s in a string of ingredients, easy enough to Miss.
I know there are different kinds of sake.  Can someone recommend what would work well in this recipe? Thank you!

If we don't eat beef, would chicken work as well?
Thanks!
I made it and to me the dashi-based broth resembles a beef broth, so chicken in a beef base I don't think would be very good.
What kind of saki should I buy to cook with?

Made with an elk roast. I waited for things to be "not soupy" and the meat was overcooked. I think next time I will do it with the roast whole and then slice thin afterwards so that I have a medium rare temperature.

I’m 50/50 on this recipe. The beef gets 100 and the dashi gets 100. I found the dashi to be very weak in flavor. It seemed the kombu and bonito flakes should have been doubled or the water halved.  I followed the recipe exactly using a scale to weigh ingredients and doubled the soy sauce and mirin. Weak sauce I’d say because we still needed to douse it with extra soy sauce, and I strongly considered putting the flavor packets from the udon in the dashi.

Kenji, I practically idolize your food science know-how but this was a very rare miss for me. Found the sweetness to be overwhelming. When I have various mushrooms and complementary veg needing using I'll clean the fridge and have another go with less sake/mirin and no added sugar.
Just saw this. I'll use Habanero in my Frankenstein recipe above :)
What might be a workable substitute for the beef?  It need not be vegetarian, but I am allergic to beef and chicken.
Mushrooms would be good.

mirin   soy sauce
Use 1/4 # beef per person
Nice and easy. However would add the beef to the sauce nearer to the end so it doesn’t overcook.
A little to fishy tasting for our liking. May try with beef broth next time.

Made this last night for my udon loving family. It was delicious, but HOLY CATS SO MANY POTS AND PANS. We get a decent soup base from 99Ranch and I’m not sure that the dashi broth was worth the effort over the base. We added slightly sautéed baby book Choi and I steamed some shrimp shu mai for the kids. I’d love this recipe rewritten for two pans instead of three.
Cooked as printed and it was totally delicious.  An economical Sukiyaki, without the veggies.
I loved it but E wouldn’t even eat it.

This was amazing!  I only had dried flat udon noodles. It was still very good but if anyone has a recommendation for an online source for fat chewy udon noodles please let me know  Or I may need to make a pilgrimage to H-Mart.
I made this using the Trader Joe's Thai vacuum packed shelf-stable noodles which were a reasonable substitute for dresh udon noodles which were not easy to find in my neighborhood.
I did not care for the broth. I found it too fishy but my husband thought it was fine. I might try the vegetarian version, or half and half, if I make it again.
Shave beef at supermarket works better than anything you try and cut.

This came out great. I was cooking for a family member who is wheat-free, so I used soaked and softened pad thai noodles which worked well. I used #1.5 of beef for the four of us and I also added a little sliced bok choy near the end of the simmer. Put both chili crunch and tamari on the table for people to use as they liked. Delish!

So tasty, after trial and error I now add somewhat less mirin to the final base. Prefer it a little more savory, little less sweet. But great either way!

Also, I always prefer thick fresh Udon if you have access. The dried stuff never 'feels' quite like real Udon to me, although I'm sure technically it is.
Trader Joe’s always has shaved beef.

I would think subbing a furikake of choice for the togarashi would not be remiss if that's what you have on hand and/or don't care for the heat of togarashi.  This is going on the "make immediately" list!  We will have it this week.

This is delicious in every way. Pick up some bone marrow while you’re shopping for the beef. Roast in the oven and then top your udon with the marrow. Oh my god! Sautéed Asian greens are a great addition, too, but that’s just if you want to go overboard.
First time to make Dashi. Easy and tasty. Used Simply Asia dried udon noodles which are now available in our grocery store. Added bok choy as others suggested for veggies.
If one doesn't have udon but instead has dried soba, what would this be like made with soba?

Really sensational. I had a really excellent dashi in the freezer, but not quite enough, so topped off the stock with a cup or two of homemade chicken broth from the freezer. This udon is fantastic -- I woke up this morning thinking about it.
made this, followed recipe mostly exactly. found it boring, bland. Not a keeper for us.
Added ginger to onion/beef mix. Added bok choy to lightly cook at very end
So, why can’t you go to the meat or deli counter in the grocery store and ask them to shave a steak (across grain)?

Made this recipe as written with quality ingredients from a Japanese Grocer including fresh udon. Amazing. Simple, well written recipe and as good an Udon as any I have enjoyed at nice Japanese restaurants.
followed recipe, wasnt delighted. quite bland.
