This was delicious. We ate double the amount of udon noodles -- half a pound (one pack) per person. Time-saving tip: the directions on the package of frozen udon noodles says you can microwave them in the plastic wrap -- no need to boil a pot of water!
has anyone made batches of homemade dashi and frozen it? how are the reheated results?
@premade, frozen dashi? It comes out perfectly fine.
Why not use some deli roast beef sliced to order?!
30 minutes if you have someone who can prepare all the ingredients for you. I don't chop that fast.

I have made this at least 20 times. It is perfect as it is but I also love to add some veggies: thinly sliced carrots or asparagus, a handful of sliced shitakes, scallions. And heat up some gyoza to go with it. I just use the instant dashi grains to make the starting broth.

This is fantastic!  Exotic comfort food.  I was unable to source the pre-sliced beef, so I bought a 20 ounce ribeye and put it in the freezer for about 45 minutes before slicing thinly with a very sharp knife.

Alternate but delicious preparation for the beef… I didn’t have sake but prepared the beef as written subbing in dashi and a tsp of rice wine vinegar for sake. I cooked the beef in a cast iron skillet without adding the additional cup of dashi.  The udon dish as a whole was great but the real winner is the beef! 
It is a quick, flavorful, kid friendly protein that will be part of my family dinner rotation for rice bowls and lettuce wraps in addition to udon.

OK, I'll bite (pun intended): Where can I buy udon noodles that resemble those in the photo accompanying this recipe? My local asian grocery stores stock only noodles labeled "udon" that are the same thickness as soba noodles.  Thanks for any assistance.  

BTW, I'd describe gyudon as "Japanese beef, onion and rice bowls" because onions are such a major ingredient in this dish.

Made tonight and will make again, but it came down to the quality of the meat. At our local Asian market I bought "beef for soup" but it was way too tough. I'll get higher quality next time and, as suggested by others, freeze it briefly and shave myself. However, I do question the long saute for the meat. "Simmer at high heat" seems like an oxymoron. Those who have been successful: did you follow the suggested 10-15 minutes?

Delicious! We decided to "veggify" it by adding sweet potato (cooked gently in the warm dashi and removed when done), mushrooms (sauteed in a skillet), and baby bok choy (skillet). We combined the veggies together but kept them separate from the beef until soup assembly. Definitely making this one again!

toe Questions: Did I see you pour Saki from a small can? Where can I get that?! 
My beef was very tough - any suggestions on getting a tender result?

Used Japanese dashi powder “tea bags” and Philly cheesesteak shaved beef from Wegmans. It’s less than half the price of their shabu-shabu  shaved rib eye and in this application just as good. Also added some thin sliced small mushrooms.  

I used bucatini rather than udon noodles and reduced sodium Japanese style soy sauce in an attempt to prevent my cardiologist from dropping me from his patient list. 

Make the broth first and use same pot to cook the pasta to make it a two pot supper.  

A nice meal whose flavor reminded me of my visits to Japan. 

A nice supper
Delish!! And easy!! Added bok choy and shiitake mushrooms and used ramen in place of udon. Couldn’t get shaved meat so I shaved my own, ie thinly sliced mostly frozen sirloin flap steak…easy peasy
Our experience is that this took closer to an hour.
Perfect, 10/10, Kenji does it again. Cook exactly as he says.

A little disappointed. We made this with medium dry sherry in place of sake but otherwise followed the recipe. Used ribeye for shabu shabu from H Mart. Neither of us liked the broth that much--too sweet and fishy. I've used bonita flakes for a miso soup that was off-the-hook. Maybe it was the combo of kombu and beef flavor we didn't like. I should have skipped the Tbl of sugar given the sherry; could have used shaoshang rice wine. Green part of scallions should not simmered as per recipe.

I love this recipe! It comes together so quickly and tastes like it took hours. To make this even easier, I cook the noodles in with the beef and onion mixture, and just add a little extra dashi stock/water. I would also start cooking the onions first for just a few minutes before adding the beef, since the beef cooks so quickly and can get chewy fast.
This recipe was easy to understand, fun, simple, and delicious. I loved the video and found it very helpful. Can’t wait to make this again.
How long does katsuibushi last? (I've had some in the pantry for a while and am wondering if it's still okay.)
I make this about once a week right now.  It's so good.  I've poured over rice, too.  I get NY strip or similar, freeze it it/thaw just enough to shave it off in very this sheets.

Great recipe! I made one modification to the recipe, which was that I browned the onions and beef before deglazing with the dashi and sauces. Although Kenji says that it's unnecessary due to the flavourful ingredients, I thought that there could be little wrong with enhancing the flavour even more. I read about a Pakistani chef, who came from a tradition of not browning meat, and switched over to browning to improve dishes.

title: San Francisco-Style Vietnamese American Garlic Noodles Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

comments.commentBody: I’ve been using my meat tenderizing mallet for smashing garlic lately. Turns it into a creamy paste. Does a great job and I’ll use it with this recipe.
I do the same. I put the garlic between small pieces of parchment or waxed paper to keep the all the bits and for easy clean up.

As a Vietnamese immigrant whose parents owned a restaurant for decades in Denver, I would like to add another essential condiment/sauce in the canon of Vietnamese cooking called Maggi. My family makes this dish using Maggi and not fish sauce. Just thought I'd offer this up as another way to make these yummy noodles. Maggi, in its iconic curvaceous dark brown bottle, is not made of soy but wheat protein. You will often that tangy saltiness in Vietnamese sandwiches.

@Gigi
I’m wondering if all the other ingredients are the same as Kenji’s recipe. I see that your parents substituted Maggi for the fish sauce. I get my Maggi at a Vietnamese grocery store and I always thought it was a substitute for soy sauce, but I see now that it’s not. I’m very glad to get this information.

I keep a large bulb of garlic, roots intact, in a small vase (one that held a hyacinth bulb purchased at Aldi works well). Use the tall green shoots from the garlic instead of scallions to add flavor and greenery to any number of dishes. The roots that grow swirl in a lovely pattern in the bottom of the vase, and the bulb will continue to produce shoots for weeks.

@Eileen
This is a GREAT tip!
@Eileen is the garlic in soil or the roots just in water?

Have been cooking pasta in a skillet for years. Use approximately 3 cups of water per 12 oz of pasta in 12-inch skillet. Turn heat to high. Cook approximately 10 minutes, stirring frequently, adding more water towards end if needed as noodles absorb the water and soften. Add favorite sauce - or transfer as recommended in this recipe and toss till you are happy with the finish.

DO not buy crushed garlic in a jar. You can get away with whole peeled garlic in a crunch. Garlic is easy to use peel a few bulbs and leave it whole in your fridge and then you will have the pleasure of using garlic in your cereal in the morning.(just to see if anyone is reading)
I love garlic any time of the day or night.
@Jacques B I have arthritis which limits the use of my hands. I use “jarlic” as well as prechopped vegetables. These items allow me to continue cooking  and enjoying meals with my family.
@LLV. You GO! We all do the best we can!

Unusual for me, completely followed the recipe, no changes. I'll never cook pasta in a pot again.Added a fair amount of the pasta water, to the end product and tasted like we had a cream sauce. This is amazing, no leftovers ...and chop the garlic however you want!

I have made this dish dozens of times over the last few years and it's always delicious. I do have a suggestion, however. The amount of sauce needs to be at least doubled from what is suggested above. Keep the butter and garlic on a very low flame so the garlic doesn't burn. Turn the heat off before adding the cheese.

I found a garlic "rocker" on Amazon while browsing kitchen utensils. I's a bent bar shape with small holes in the center. You press down and rock back and forth on a garlic clove and it pushes the garlic through the holes, releasing the most intense garlic flavor. It's much like a press, but without the messy clean up and frustration of getting all the garlic out of the press. It cleans up in about 15 seconds.
I'll never mince garlic again!
I think there is also a little tangerine juice to counter the garlic, as in their sumptuous roast crabs...

1- Trust and follow the pasta cooking method - but stir to unstick from pan  
2- Use all the garlic even if it seems like too much 
3- Add a bit more of each of the sauces, and more like 1/2 c. cheese 

I ended up not even needing to drain the pasta, end result was perfectly creamy/emulsified, not greasy in the slightest. I told my Roman-born husband it was like Vietnamese carbonara. We added lots of black pepper (like the crab dish has). Anyone tried it w/ bottarga instead of cheese?

I like to gently massage the garlic cloves first, with some soothing ambient music in the background. This results in a mellower flavor.
I also make my own oyster sauce, using bivalves that I raise myself in a saltwater aquarium in my basement.
And it goes without saying that Brown Cow Parmigiano Reggiano is a must for this recipe.
Delish!

I add a about a tablespoon (basically a big pinch with my fingers) of flour to my garlic. Makes it a little bit more of a paste, and the sauce is a little drier and thicker, making it more like the garlic noodles I love getting from various places in San Francisco. Cook up some shrimp and add them in along with a bit of sriracha. Yum.

I’ve been messing around trying to replicate this recipe for over thirty years, with pretty decent results. I use some Maggi seasoning, with or without soy sauce. And I almost always throw in a bit of mayonnaise.  Love the suggestion for a touch of tangerine juice.
Care to share your current recipe (he says, hopefully)?
@KR. I’m so sorry, I just saw this request, three years rate. Alas, I have no recipe, it’s in my head.
In Kenji's book (The Wok) he says the garlic should weigh about 60-70 grams.
When you cook pasta in shallow water, do you add salt?  Nobody mentions it.
Normally I would ... but given the soy and oyster already in the sauce, it might be overkill.  Will have to make this and let you know.

Substitute for oyster sauce…hoisin sauce
Sub for fish sauce….soy sauce
This is pretty good.  I added 2 cups frozen peas and 1 1/2 cup of shredded roast chicken which made it a main course.

I have a wonderful fist sized beach rock with a flat side that I use when crushing multiple cloves of garlic. Much faster than my chefs knife and it gives me pleasure using it as well as seeing it on my counter.
I agree with mashing the garlic with the flat side of a knife. Ever since the Galloping Gourmet did it, I have never looked back.

Pasta is absolutely delicious. Made tonight. Added salt to the pasta water, and next time I won’t. Might be nice to add that note to the instructions as most cooks are trained that pasta water needs salt. Had a few problems with the Parmesan clumping on the serving utensils. Might be interesting to add a Parmesan rind to the pasta water and then omit the Parmesan shavings.

Delicious simple tasty recipe. Added extra soy sauce. Cooking noodles in a flat pan makes a huge difference both for the noodles and that leftover starchy water. Do not salt pasta water! Would be great cooking egg into it last minute too. Will make many times. Thanks Kenji!

This was delicious. I would only request a weight for the garlic. With most recipes, if I misjudge a particular size of a clove or 2 of garlic, it won't make much difference. With 20 cloves, it can absolutely make a difference! This may have been the first thing I ever made where I actually thought I had too much garlic in it. I didn't know that was possible.

I wish the NYT and other recipe sites would talk about garlic as the wildly different substance it is depending on variety, freshness, and where it's grown - or if you're using garlic from an actual head of garlic instead of  peeled sold in a bag/jar. A recipe calling for 20 cloves should really have some notes about adjusting based on what garlic you're using. Notes here talk about too much garlic flavour, or not enough. 20 cloves of one garlic or another will make a huge difference

We have prepared this dish for years using soy sauce together with Maggi sauce rather than fish sauce or oyster sauce and leave out the Parmesan.  With the garlic smashed, mashed, chopped or crushed, it always comes out delectable (and totally vegan).
If you have been using Maggi and omitting fish sauce, oyster sauce, and parmesan, then you have not been preparing this dish for years.

The author of this article suggests adding raw shell-on shrimp at the beginning of the cooking process. WHY shell-on? I find nothing quite as annoying as having to plunge my hands into a beautifully cooked dish to remove shrimp shells. It destroys the dish, my hands, and any pleasure that might come from eating the shrimp. Food preparation should result in something that can be eaten and enjoyed with a modicum of fuss. Shell-on shrimp mixed with noodles, garlic, butter and cheese is not that.

So I am the weird person who likes shrimp cooked in the shell ... I think it's moister and I like crunching the shells.  However, my family shares your perspective ... in compromise, I make a broth with the shells and cook the pasta in the shrimp broth.

Shrimp cooked in their shell are tastier, just like meat on the bone is better than boneless.  When I make gumbo I leave the shrimp in their shells and even throw in the bones and skin I trimmed off the bone in/skin on chicken thighs because it adds flavor to the dish.

I wonder if could the frozen garlic puree from Trader Joe's be used?
1 cube = 1 clove
@cary, Maggi closely matches the flavor profile of a ‘light soy sauce,’ despite not being made of soy, and would work great. My Vietnamese mother has always had a soft spot for Maggi.
Use a plastic sandwich bag and rubber mallet; when done smashing them, turn bag inside out and drop garlic into sauce.
Made this exactly following the recipe. Delicious. Garlicky but not overly sharp. We had some leftover, so I put a bit of the pasta water in the container with the noodles to keep them moist.
Thanks for that idea, Sandra!

20 cloves of garlic? For that many, recipe writers need to get with the fact that people with access to a farmer's market or home garden are using hard-neck garlic a lot these days. The cloves of a hard-neck can be four times larger than the soft-neck garlic which is sold in grocery stores. When using one or two cloves, it's easy to estimate an adjustment, but with 20, a person could go very wrong! Instead of 20 cloves, how about an approximate weight?
Whatever happened to the garlic press? I’ve had mine now for 50(?) years. First kitchen gadget I ever bought. Still think it’s the best way to smash garlic.

Pecorino Romano or Parmesan? Really different flavor profiles. Given the very large amount of sodium in the liquid ingredients, stick to the less salty Parmesan. Better yet, use Grana Padano, which is a delicious, less-salty and more economical grating cheese.

Against my better judgment, and thinking what do I know anyway and that K L-A is the expert (enjoy his book btw). . . I followed the recipe and used a "skillet" to cook the noodles. Pro tip -- Don't use an iron skillet! Yuck, noodles taste like iron. I can't get the taste out of my mouth! Iron noodles!

I made this recipe once by mincing the garlic with a knife, and another time by smashing it in a mortar and pestle. Using a mortar and pestle is an absolute must, the garlic becomes creamier and less harsh, making for a much more cohesive sauce. This recipe is in my opinion wonderful with the smashed garlic but underwhelming with knife-minced garlic.

Delicious, talk about umami! This is ambrosia for garlic & pasta lovers. I used a fair amount of the pasta (capellini) cooking water. I salted the pasta water but will reduce or eliminate salt next time given the amount needed & the saltiness of the sauce ingredients. Also added some leftover sautéed spinach. We will make this again (& again!).

It seemed too easy to be true.  Spaghetti?  Is that authentic?  And it's so specific-- San Francisco Style Vietnamese American Garlic Noodles.  I had doubts.

BUT, since I live in SF, and we were having crab for a New Years Dinner, I made this, and even though it took a grand total of 15 minutes to make (isn't good cooking supposed to be time consuming?), it was perfect.  Just perfect.  I have followed the career of Lopez-Alt for some time, and he is amazing.  Whatever he says, I'll make!!
This recipe didn't work for us. It may have been better without the cheese, which seemed discordant here.
Any suggestions for veganizing this vis-a-vis the fish/seafood sauces? Thx.

There are lots of vegan fish sauces and oyster sauces available, and they taste great.  

I live in Northern Virginia, so I get them from H Mart, but you can also find online.  The vegan oyster sauce I prefer is Kikkoman. 

ps I just checked the bottle and it says vegetarian, not vegan - but I don't see anything in the ingredient list that's non-vegan.
I've used Yondu vegan fish sauce (not in this recipe, not yet) and like it very much. You can get it online but I found mine at a health food store.

Adding a powder of dried porcini mushrooms, with a bit of powdered nori, to the soy sauce is a very nice replacement. Use a high quality fermented soy sauce because the richness of flavor matters.

Kalamata olives ground into a paste capture some of the funky flavor of fishes sauce.

Grated manzanilla olives make a nice topping instead of Parmesan cheese.

Delightful. Told my kids it was Vietnamese garlic and oil. No cheese for me but the kids went for it. Would like a bit of heat next time add a teaspoon of chili flake or a fresh chili. Definitely a keeper.

Honestly I don't get all the garlic talk. Either you like it or you don't. Either you have a preferred way of preparing it or not. If garlic isn't your thing, maybe not make this recipe. Just saying. I am always recalculating recipes to serve two, so I used ten cloves of garlic. Perfect for our taste. We visited Thanh Long in SF for the roast crab. Now I'm sorry I didn't pay more attention to the noodles! This recipe is a winner!

Maybe people didn't cook the garlic long enough, it's hard to get it to the exact stage when you aren't used to cook with that much. When the garlic still remains a little raw it can be quite pungent. But apart from this hunch I agree, and you can also guess from the amount in the recipe that it will be really garlicky. There should be no real surprise.

Made this, love it. Regarding the garlic prep, I quickly and easily got all 20 cloves pulverised in a mini food processor. This dish was a big hit with my college kids and the next morning we pan fried the leftovers with eggs for a good, savory breakfast.

I used the trusty food processor to mince the garlic in about 6 seconds. Believe me, the power of the garlic was not diminished because it wasn’t crushed. I find it sautés better cubed than crushed - far less browning and burned bits and it doesn’t clump. Just my option but if you don’t feel like mincing 20 cloves don’t feel that your final product will suffer for it.
I have a similar excellent, easy recipe that uses Maggie seasoning instead of soy sauce. Consider the swap.

Amazing! If you don’t like this, you didn’t go it right. 
Made single serving by dividing by 4. I will stress you need to undercook your pasta. My cheese clumped but I turned the heat up and added more and more pasta water and stirred until it turned into the gorgeously creamy sauce in the picture. 
 had no oyster sauce so I doubled the soy and fish sauce. I am amazed that, even with 5 cloves garlic in 1 portion, the garlic flavor was subtle. Creamy smooth and umami.

Use a garlic press -- releases the powerful flavor and cooks beautifully. 

Already been said, but more water necessary to make the noodles...

I've made this twice now, and I wouldn’t change the ingredients or the technique except for one thing: grabbing the slippery pasta with tongs is ridiculous. Next time I make it (probably tonight) I'll drain the pasta with a pitcher under the sieve to catch the pasta water. The pasta goes all straight into the sauce, and the pasta water is easily poured into the pan as needed.

I’ve made countless NYT Cooking recipes, from quick and easy to crazy and complex. THIS. IS. THE BEST. I double all the sauce ingredients except for the garlic. Good lord. A divine, garlicky food coma. Just make it.

I cooked it with a few adjustments and would change somethings if I make it again.
I smashed the garlic then minced it. Got great garlic flavor. I added about 1 tbs of minced, fresh ginger.
I added 1 tsp brown sugar and 2 tsp sambal too.
It tasted like it was missing something so I added a few splashes of mirin at the end.
I will add double the sauce ingredients (except garlic/ginger) next time and make less pasta as the overwhelming flavor was garlic. It was imbalanced— it was begging for acid.
I didn’t have oyster sauce so I doubled the fish sauce and soy sauce and still tasted like I remember in SF! Purchased pre-peeled garlic cloves to save time.

Made this last night  - was a hit!  I used two tweaks I discovered on YouTube for this dish: 1. Bucatini noodles are thicker and have a great chewy mouth feel 2.  Added a ladle-full of pasta water to the minced garlic in the wok to parboil it a bit, and boiled it away.  Then added the butter, and finally the sauces.  This tamps down the garlic bite  - I love garlic, but too much garlic doesn't love me

I have made this numerous times over the past couple years for my family. I have made it with rice noodles mostly but ANY noodle will work. I have even used Banza for an easy complete meal. Made as written, tastes like the best "pasta roni" ever. With simple adjustments to the recipe you can change the flavor profile to fit any meal. Like sesame oil and extra fish sauce for a more asian feel or extra cheese and roasted garlic for a more italian feel. The possibilities are endless! A Must try!

I've been roasting garlic bulbs (wrapped in foil with a teaspoon or so of olive oil) when using the oven - as long as I'm not baking a cake.   To substitute for the 20 medium cloves in the recipe, just squeeze one roasted bulb and smash with a knife - or even the bottom of a glass.

Ready in 15 minutes, right. Takes that long just to peel and prepare the garlic. I wish these "easy" recipes would include realistic time estimates for how long it would take someone who's not a professional chef to make them.
I used a mix of black garlic and white garlic cloves to deepen the flavor and add some sweetness
The next time I make this I will add just a touch of cayenne of red pepper flakes.  I put the peeled garlic cloves between sheets of plastic wrap and mashed them with a meat tenderizer.
Delicious...but adding parmesan cheese is too fusion for my taste.

This recipe was a successful dinner for: 2 spoiled by NYC eating options adults and 2 ten year old kids (one is adventurous the other is not when it comes to food). No leftovers. Repeat requests. Served with crispy asparagus (olive oil, salt, pepper). The only deviation from the recipe: I sprinkled toasted sesame seeds.
Video: https://youtu.be/wK9OHVxB_Z8
This is one of my favorite NYT recipes. I follow it closely but I double or sometimes triple the sauce ingredients as we like a lot of sauce. We use buccatini and love this recipe.
Made this exactly as written except doubled the sauce ingredients as other readers suggested and it was 11/10 absolutely perfect. Don’t skip the scallions. Totally delicious.
I’m no shrinking violet but this was just way way too much garlic. I made it as written but the 20 cloves- which did give me pause- made it completely overpowering. It went in the trash.
This stuff is addictive! I reduced the butter and used a mix of parmigiano reggiano & pecorino.  Didn’t have fish sauce so I used a dab of anchovy paste mixed with soy sauce & rice vinegar.
I used squid ink pasta, added 1 lb of shrimp and garnished with lao gan ma spicy chili crisp. Perfect!

If you are cooking crab at the same time you make this recipe….then cook the pasta in the same water you boil crab. The mix of crab water and starch of noodles along with the ingredients suggested creates the most similiar flavor to the restaurant dish. Use that water to thicken the sauce.

Now that I'm older and the arthritis in my hands is an issue I've needed to make adjustments.  Garlic and ginger that comes in a tube is easy to use and adds a nice flavor.  It may not be the same as smashing, dicing or grating fresh bulbs or roots but better than nothing.
I have lately take him to using a microplane grater for garlic. Your fingers will smell, but it is very efficient.

I was going to say this too! Why would anyone not use a microplane?Though twenty is a lot for that method, it's a true. A great trick for getting garlic smell off fingers is to rub them on anything made of stainless steel under running water. I use the metal handle of my microplane--magic!
Any thoughts or suggestions about a substitute for the cheese?
Yes!  We used vegan Parmesan (Follow Your Heart) and it was delicious.  Kept all the other ingredients the same.  We have a milk allergy.
It's good without cheese.

It's too much pasta. Sauce smells like the most amazing clam spaghetti (all that garlic & butter) you have ever tasted, and then disappears in a cooked pound of spaghetti. Next time I may add pasta water to sauce before adding cooked pasta. Give it more liquid to emulsify the cheese.  Tomorrow I'm going to sauté mushrooms, add some jamon and jazz this dish up.  Add cracked pepper too.
Added bok choy and mushrooms and maggi sauce, didn’t have oyster sauce
This is really good served with a fried egg on top. The gooey yolk makes it like a carbonara umami bomb.

Yeah hey everybody, glad everyone is enjoying this recipe. I also enjoyed it but it is too much garlic. 70g of garlic is too much. You look at it and you're like "wow 20 cloves, that looks like way too much garlic". Well I'm here to confirm that. It's too much garlic. I just got through eating a bowl and I loved every bite but it's too much garlic. I am all garlic now, my whole body is garlic and my mind and emotions and soul. Going to need to shower and maybe call a faith healer?

After seeing this on my IG feed 20x a day for months, I finally decided to get the ingredients and make it. I HATE when I’m disappointed with recipes I was excited about. Am I the only one who was expecting more of an umami bomb? I just got garlic. I didn’t really taste anything else. I followed the recipe as written and…I’m totally bummed.
everyone loved it! very easy to make and kid approved! served alongside soy glazed chicken thighs (soy sauce, honey and garlic) and everyone thought it was a great pairing

Go light on salt in pasta water bc of saltiness of other ingredients. 

Made Sept 2022 with substitutions based on ingredients at home.

Kikkoman Soy Sauce instead of oyster sauce 
Worcestershire Sauce instead of Light Soy Sauce 
Coriander instead of spring onions for freshness
Felt it needed: 
- a bit of lemon juice (very little), 
- crushed dry chilli for heat, 
- fresh black pepper,
- extra cheese.
Would be interesting to try with the smallest bit of numbing oil.

I made this using a little less garlic and substituted a small amount of anchovy paste for the fish sauce. It was delicious! I added sautéed shrimp and served it over raw spinach. Makes a great quick dinner.
For folks who try to eat dairy free, this was delicious with nooch and a butter substitute.

Easy and delicious.  Because I'm lazy, I use pre-minced garlic and smash it to a paste in a mortar and pestle.  Much faster and still tastes good.  I have cooked the noodles according to the recipe and while it works it makes a mess.  So now I just boil them in a deep pot as I normally would and after I drain them I drop them back in the deep pot and add the garlic sauce.  I reserve some of the water just in case.  Much easier and less messy and comes out exactly the same!
Excellent dish, ready in no time. The umami is strong. I recommend finishing with some Madagascar black pepper. Hi from France.
Garlic cloves vary greatly in size so I'm curious to know approximately what "20 medium cloves" minced or mashed translates to in tablespoons or dry ingredient cup measure. Anyone?
As I recall from a video on the recipe, 50-60 grams, when I made it it was rough chopped and smashed with a chefs knife, I did not use a garlic press

I’ve been eyeing this recipe for quite a while and finally took the plunge. Married to someone of Italian descent, I had to make it clear that this wasn’t his mama’s spaghetti. He lapped it up and wished there was more. Added wild shrimp. Clean plates; happy people.
While this was certainly garlicky enough, we needed more soy sauce, fish sauce and oyster sauce. Also added chile crisp at the table. Very easy and tasty dinner.

Yum!!!! Much tastier than the ingredient list would seem.  I cut the recipe in half, but next time I think I'll keep the full amount of the sauce and just cut the pasta amount in half.  I did the smash-then-mince thing with the garlic, because I don't own a mortar & pestle, and it worked perfectly well.  Additional ideas for toppings/mix-ins next time:  mixed seafood (Trader Joe's sells this frozen, ready to cook), shelled edamame, cut up broccoli, halved grape tomatoes, or sliced bok choy.

Watch the video . . . it really prepares you well for the actual making of the dish.  The amount of pasta water needed is greater than the instructions suggest, and of course you DON'T add the cheese until AFTER you've turned off the heat.  (It's exactly the same way you make cacio e pepe.)
Quite delicious. I will say - I added (unsalted) peanuts to mine, which added a nice amount of textural variety, for those of us that crave that type of thing.
Yondu is a great vegan/vegetarian substitute for fish sauce and has a ton of umami flavor. I get it on Amazon. It’s great on risotto too
I used Maggi instead of light soy sauce. Maggi is the magic ingredient in all Vietnamese kitchens.

I’d seen this recipe pass through a few times, and when I saw it was one of the most popular of 2022 I tried it out. I made it for lunch on a Saturday and it was so amazing that I made it again for dinner that night. I made it exactly as written except for halving it each time. No idea how leftovers taste since there weren’t any. Easy and quick and absolutely delicious.

Made for one - myself. Delicious. Made following modifications- added dash of Worcestershire sauce, splash of white wine (why not),pinch of red pepper flakes, and handful of baby spinach. Used one pat  of butter as I am not a butter lover and half of other sauce ingredients for about quarter of lb of spaghetti. Made generous serving and ate every bit.

I reduced the garlic to about eight cloves and this was delicious! I added some shrimp for a delightfully simple weeknight meal. The sauces and the cheese gave it all the salty goodness it needed. Plan to use this as a starter recipe for many meals to come!

Insanely good... used only 3/4 lb pasta for 2 of us. ( I like more stuff ratio)...I had hoped to have a leftover. We ate it all! I will make this again and again. ..oh and used the meat mallet trick... way to go!

Do this right (and don't be me): use fresh garlic, not preminced! I was being lazy and while the dish was good, it really would have sung with fresh garlic. It needed a little more salt and a little heat so we added a pinch of red pepper. I made a super simple stirfry of shrimp and broccoli with garlic and soy sauce (blanched broccoli for 1 min first) and mixed it in when the noodles were done. Will definitely repeat with fresh garlic.
Really delicious.  Second time I made it I added seeded chopped fresh Campari tomatoes at the end and it was fabulous.
I used less fish sauce and added a bit of toasted sesame oil and it was perfect.

Yum!!!! Much tastier than the ingredient list would seem.  I cut the recipe in half, but next time I think I'll keep the full amount of the sauce and just cut the pasta amount in half.  I did the smash-then-mince thing with the garlic, because I don't own a mortar & pestle, and it worked perfectly well.  Additional ideas for toppings/mix-ins next time:  mixed seafood (Trader Joe's sells this frozen, ready to cook), shelled edamame, cut up broccoli, halved grape tomatoes, or sliced bok choy.

1/2lb spaghetti
12 garlic cloves
3T butter
couple t soy
couple t fish
T oyster

1c water
1/4t/ea cornstarch & salt

Oh, these are ridiculously tasty and easy. I put a bulb of garlic in a kitchen towel and just smashed away. Bucatini—decadent. But next time, absolutely more sauce. Used vegan oyster sauce and homemade vegan fish sauce. Chef’s kiss!

We’re a garlic-lovin’ family, so I thought this would be a slam dunk, but we all found it bitter and overwhelmingly garlicky. It was just…unpleasant. I wouldn’t make it again, and that’s saying something, considering how much I love garlic.

This was amazing! I never usually leave notes, but I had to for this! This is going to go into serious rotation in our house. He also has a YouTube video showing how to make this, which was nice to watch.

This actually has a "Vietalian" vibe, with the spaghetti & cheese(s).  

I do love shallow-cooking pasta rather than boiling it in a tank of water.   Nice, rich broth.

Only item I'd add is maybe a squeeze of lime or some shredded basil or cilantrol.
Our veggie guest needed for protein so we added a LB of tofu cubed. It broke up a bit but that was fine. Also added mustard greens and mushrooms for a complete dinner! LOVE this recipe!
Don't salt the water when you cook the pasta.  The soy sauce, fish sauce and parmesan bring plenty of salt.

For true garlic lovers, this is an amazing dish! Using some of the pasta water, it comes together perfectly. No extra sauce needed! Don't be tempted to add in extra parmesan, as the balance of flavours here is perfect. The little garnish of scallion greens toward the end is the final, crowning touch. This reheats well in the microwave too, but you may want to reserve a little pasta water in the fridge as well, just to prevent it from that left-over pasta stodginess.

I made this per the recipe and felt like it was a bit too salty so I added a splash of rice vinegar and some sugar. Next time I’d try honey and lime, chili flakes sautéed with the garlic or some chili sauce at the end. extra scallions!

In the past year I stopped using a garlic press and have been using Dorot Gardens Crushed Garlic cubes I find at Whole Foods in the frozen veg section. Seems to be as good as fresh and eliminates all the garlic miseries. Worcestershire sauce is a good substitute for the oyster sauce.
Just made this for the first time. I'm not sure I like it, but I can't stop eating it. Is that normal?

For an extremely easy (and fragrant) way to process the garlic, you can roast 1 or 2 whole heads (depending on their size) at 375° for 35-40 minutes. You can then simply squeeze the buttery, roasted garlic cloves out directly into your oyster, soy, and fish sauce mixture. It's a mellower flavor than using fresh garlic, so take that into consideration. As a late spring/early summer treat, try adding a handful of fresh garlic scapes and some Thai basil for a little variation.
Didn’t have oyster sauce so I used miso. Excellent
Very good. Recommend throwing in a few red pepper flakes.
Way too garlicky for me. Don’t recommend.

I recommend adding a quarter cup or so of pasta water after you've transferred the pasta to the sauce. (You need to bring the pasta to nearly al dente.) Turn off the heat before adding the cheese to prevent clumping. Stir vigorously until cheese disappears into the pasta, then turn heat back on and toss until everything is creamy, adding small amounts of pasta water as needed.

I've never been to Thanh Long but House of Xian in San Francisco does this with hand pulled Chinese noodles. And it's only like $12. Is there a reason not to use a food processor to mince the garlic?

YiaYia, a mortar and pestle crushes the garlic, releasing its flavors and oils, but a food processor just cuts it up finely, and the hard sides of the garlic are still intact, albeit smaller.  But, little of the garlic’s flavor is released when it is simply cut into small pieces, so if you don’t have a mortar and pestle, it’s better to crush the garlic with the flat side of a heavy knife, and then mince it.

If you have a small sized processor, go for it. If you only have a full sized unit, then its probably not quite enough garlic, and it will fly out to the sides of the bowl and not get evenly chopped or chopped small enough. I usually grate my garlic on a microplane.

The only reason to not use a food processor is the cleanup hassle.

People will forever repeat (the untested mantra) that a mortar and pestle does miraculous things that a knife or food processor can't do, but given how this is cooked, there's no way a side-by-side blind comparison would ever detect a difference.

I have also been using my meat tenderizer "pounder" (it's not a mallet) for smashing garlic too. First I cut off the stem of the clove. Then I smash lightly to release the skin. Then I cover the clove with a piece of plastic wrap or a plastic bag, which is usually lying around. Then I smash hard with glancing blows, lift the plastic and maybe give a quick crosswise chop. I have a small hand so smashing with a knife doesn't work and this method is much faster to do than describing it!

Patrick is right about the mortar and pestle being so much better than a food processor. And this is true for anything from basil to chickpeas. But the best technique to crush the garlic sans mortar is to finely dice the garlic and then firmly slide the flat of the blade across the garlic until it's pureed.  The process is easier if you sprinkle a little salt on the garlic, but as this dish is already pretty salty, I'd skip that step.

A few years ago, Kenji taught us about sodium citrate and how you can use it to emulsify cheese in liquid. So, after adding the pasta to the sauce in Step 3, I mixed a 1/4 tsp of sodium citrate, the grated cheese and one cup of hot water left over from the pasta. I gave it a stir and then poured it over the pasta. It worked. No clumps of cheese!

To help anyone with arthritis who is looking for an easier way to deal with garlic - the rocker tool mentioned by @Brad is not the solution. The tiny round holes in the tool are not sharp; they are just holes and the metal is thick. As a result, the amount of pressure required can be actually painful for those with any sort of compromised strength or dexterity. It’s a great suggestion for most but, as someone with RA since my 20’s, I’ve found many “helpful” gadgets require “normal” strength.

Absolutely delicious, but I struggle with making the pasta in a skillet. Any tips?

I keeps forever, and I have never had a complaint about the taste of the garlic in the meals I prepare and have many empty plates and pans as proof of success.

I second this.
