I used an immersion blender and neutral oil (grapeseed or canola) to turn the garlic into a paste. The recipe turned out well despite the additional oil. I also added some chopped cilantro as a garnish at the end.

I have used this recipe numerous times to go with Dungeness crab.  It is very good as is, but I like it even better with a tablespoon of chopped ginger, a tablespoon of miso, some red pepper flakes and lots of chopped scallions.
Too salty. Do not salt the pasta water.
Hit it with a dollop of Chinese chili crisp. Heaven!
Garlic press

I used Banza angel hair and it turned out well. 

Reheats well. 

I used a mortar and pestle but next time would just use processor because even though we have a large mortar, 20 cloves took a while to get into a paste.
The flavor of the dish was bland. Have other cooks used anything to step it up a notch? But it was very easy to prepare and the flavors did intensify by the next day.
This recipe makes people so happy! I served it with grilled chicken thighs and a side of cantaloupe. Perfect dinner to make your friends smile!
Why cook the pasta in a skillet?

Just made this and it was so delicious.
I added mushrooms 🍄 to the recipe to make it a bit more substantial. So yummy and thank you for the amazing recipe!

I use 2 pkgs  Chinese egg noodles which are a bit chewy (like original recipe) and for authentic flavor and texture. After boiling, strain then run water thru it to avoid sticking. I also add 3 tsp brown sugar and chili flakes (or cayenne) to taste and 1/4 sesame oil.
Use jar garlic if you want.  No one will notice or care about the various techniques y’all use to get the same result.  They just want to eat, already!

1.  Crushing/mincing the garlic is the easy part - for me the hard part is pelling them!!  Any suggestions?

2.  The addition of dairy is off putting to me, don't see any comments about that.  Something about the asian ingredients/sauces doesnt gel w/ cheese to me - but I haven't cooked the recipe yet...just seeing it now. Ch
Finally got around to making this. Delish! We both loved it as is but it was also great with come chili-crisp on top!

Made this tonight. Really enjoyed it. Added de shelled shrimp to sauce and cooked in sauce for 4 minutes. Then added pasta. Marinated shrimp in garlic salt, garlic, soy sauce, fish sauce, and chili oil. We like scampi a lot but this might have been even better!
I made this the other night.  Followed the recipe to a "T" and it was... fantastic!  Made it again and subbed Maggi sauce for the fish sauce.  It went from fantastic to sublime.  Love this dish!

As a native San Franciscan who grew up down the street from Thanh Long, I can tell you that nothing compares to their garlic noodles, but living so far away now, I feel like this recipe gets you pretty much there without hopping on a plane to visit the restaurant.

My modifications: using fresh egg noodles (Chinese grocery store) and a wok. Do step 1 without removing from heat, then immediately add the egg noodles and parmesan cheese, then scallions. (Optional dash sesame oil at end.)

Pretty tasty but -- I would at least double the amount of sauces. I found that I needed to add extra soy, oyster, and fish sauce to get a deeper flavor.  Using the amounts in the recipe as written, I tasted pasta, garlic and butter but wasn't really getting Asian flavor.
Love the idea of using a beautiful beach rock to crush garlic!
I love this dish but I always add asparagus and shrimp. It’s so easy and delicious
This is the same technique I use for cacio e pepe. Turn the heat off when you add the cheese.
Too salty

In Step 3 the recipe says: "Increase the heat to high, add the cheese to the wok, and stir with a wooden spatula or spoon." In his demonstration video, Kenji López-Alt says to turn off the heat before adding the cheese to the noodles. He says doing so will keep the cheese from melting into a lump in the pot. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=seY_K4P6rOM&ab_channel=NYTCooking
We made this with black garlic.  So tasty!  Added a few grilled shrimp for garnish.
The cookbook version of the recipe calls for 20 cloves (60 to 70 g) of garlic.
These noodles are excellent with fried salmon patties and a Brut champagne. See: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1024728-salmon-patties
Watch Kenji make this dish on YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=seY_K4P6rOM
Dear God! How did Master Kenji know this is what I needed in my life!!! Amazing! Delectable! Garlicky! I added sautéed shrimp, and I'm in heaven!!! Thank you!!

adapted it a bit for more oof.

-boil shrimp, take shrimp out, save shrimp water 
-boil egg noodles in shrimp water. save shrimp noodle water for later
-sautee a lot of garlic with butter 
-add nuoc mam, soy sauce, oyster sauce mix into garlic butter. quick simmer
-add noodles and shrimp pasta water (enough water to coat noods)
-cook until everything thickens a bit
-top with parmesan and spicy chili oil
Really yummy. I started with some pepper flakes in the pan and used 1.5 butter. Didn’t salt water, did salt garlic in Mortar & Pestle. Husband said it’s a winner
Excellent! I did double the sauces, as someone suggested.  Served with micro cilantro.

After 2 attempts to make this, I'd love to try again with y'all's guidance! 

First time I used a MiniPrep on the garlic; the next I flattened/chopped by hand. Both times the garlic came through tasting bitter and metallic. Didn't burn it either time, but the 2nd time I was careful to cook the garlic v slowly. The result was literally inedible. 

I've seen the back + forth here re garlic prep but didn't see if anyone had this problem. I'd be grateful for any advice!
What’s a vegetarian option for fish sauce and oyster sauce? I actually intensely dislike anything fish tasting
sounds like a half a teaspoon of chili flakes would make this even better
Anything would make this better
I really have to apologize, but this was disgusting. I followed the directions carefully and it was awful. I now have to go make something else for dinner after mincing 20 cloves of garlic...

Yeah, we had the same experience, Mary.

For us, it was so salty it was unbelievable.  And inedible.

And I followed the recipe to the letter, including using low-salt soy sauce. (I tried making it a year ago, with the same result)

But there are so many commenters here who clearly loved it, that I think there's just a ton of people out there who love heavy salt in their meals.
This was lick your plate excellent! The only thing I did differently than the recipe was use an entire bunch of scallions and mix them in to the noodles before serving.
Someone mentioned Maggi instead of fish sauce. As a plant-based eater in NZ, I add Marmite and some capers with the garlic into the vegan butter-olive oil mixture. A lot of umami...
This felt almost too easy and made a great dinner with shrimp and roast brussel sprouts. Thanks!

I wanted a side dish for snow crabs and this recipe happened to show up in my inbox. Per reader feedback, I doubled the amount of sauce,  juiced  1/2 of a tangerine, and drizzled toasted sesame oil on top.  I had Trader Joe's Parmesan, Romano, and Asiago cheese so I used that. I also weighed 60 grams of garlic and it was perfect and not overwhelming. I would like to try this again and substitute rice for the pasta.

Just made this. It was absolutely delicious!  I sautéed a handful of shrimp along with the garlic as suggested, and used bucatini instead of spaghetti. I’ll definitely be making this recipe again (and again)!  Don’t skimp on the garlic.  In my opinion, you can’t have too much garlic! I served it with roasted asparagus.  This was definitely a perfect accompaniment.

The only reason I gave this two stars and not one is that the method of cooking the pasta and the resulting texture of the sauce is excellent.  However both my husband and I found the flavors to be completely unsatisfying.  But I will try this method with other ingredients.  it's essentially a quick cooking risotto with pasta instead of rice.

You should watch Kenji's YouTube video of him making this dish: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=seY_K4P6rOM&t=395s&ab_channel=NYTCooking You'll see (at 7'33") that he does not add the cheese until AFTER the heat is off. He explains why (at 8'32"): adding the cheese while the heat is still on increases the chance that "the cheese will clump up and turn into a congealed ball of parmesan instead of staying creamy and emulsified." I'm glad I watched the video first. Mine turned out perfectly.
Am I the only one who ended up with too much pasta and too little sauce?
This was fantastic! I used tamari because I didn't have shoyu or light soy sauce in hand. Will make again with shoyu. I think the sauce would be great on fried rice.
I used a food processor to mince the garlic before I read the comments. Now I'm going to lie awake all night and berate myself for not using a mortar and pestle. WHAT A WORTHLESS FOOL I AM.

Added lightly seared diced shrimp to the mix. Here in France we get our shrimp with shells and heads in. I pealed them and boiled them and used the water to boil the pasta.  What a wonderful meal. Never made anything like it before. While prepping my wife came into the kitchen and looked at the 20 cloves of garlic. She was skeptical but by the end there wasn’t a morsel of garlic left in her plate.

Pasta cooks very well in less water. Use less salt because the pasta absorbs most of the water. I agree, don't skimp on the garlic. Crush it roughly in a mortar with pestle. I added some cooked chicken as a protein, so used chicken broth as well as half and half, and 1/2 cup Parmesan. Delicious!

I chopped up about 10 medium-sized shrimp, lightly searing them.  Here in France, we get our shrimp with shells and heads on. I boiled the shells and heads and strained them.  I used that water along with enough tap water to cover the pasta. The pasta used took quite a while to get to just not quite al dente so I had to add boiling water to get to that stage.  It was a wonderful meal. The copious amounts of garlic did not overwhelm and the cheese while not tasted, added to the texture nicely.
Very good dish.  I would make more sauce next time as has been suggested.  I added shrimp and roasted broccoli at the end.  I used whole grain spaghetti.  The recipe is a keeper.
This was delicious.  We followed the recipe but used bucatini with broccoli on the side. Wonderful!

So delicious with a pound of shrimp.  Add one minute after the garlic.  Add a couple extra minutes cook time after you add the sauces to firm them up (mine were medium size so not very large).  Recommend extra sauces (little extra garlic if you need it) to coat the extra food better.  Definitely splurge on the green onions.
Sauces aren't ample enough; next time I'll double it. would also double the parmesan cheese dose. otherwise- a great side!

SO delicious and very easy! I was concerned that the fish sauce might make it too "fishy" tasting, but not at all. Highly recommend, but watch the video to get the technique right. This dish is all about technique.

Initially, we
Ben I read the recipe calling for all that garlic I panicked
I made it with all the garlic
Wow , it was great
There are still no vampires around the county

Made this with about 8 cloves of garlic grated on a microplane. The garlic flavor was very present but not overpowering in the final dish. I wasn’t brave enough to try it with 20 cloves. Added a little more soy sauce after tasting. Didn’t have scallions, chiffonaded some basil and mint. Delish.

The garlic was a bit of a pain to prep (I used a garlic press), but otherwise, this was a quick and easy meal that everyone liked. It probably goes without saying, but if you don't like garlic, you won't like this dish.
Dang this was good

Definitely save pasta
Water and no need to drip
The water
Off
The pasta too thoroughly
I've used Worcester sauce in place of the recommendations and it's quite lively.
Added some firm tofu and spinach to a smaller bag of pasta and helped the nutritional value.
PERFECT. 8-53 at our table and all loved it.  Shhh! Just don’t tell them it has fish sauce!

For many years I’ve used Canterbury’s Crack and Peel
garlic Popper, available on Amazon. Simple mushroom- shaped wooden tool that sits comfortably in your hand and easily peels and crushes garlic. Indestructible. Made in VT. Great gift for cooks.
Don't forget to salt the pasta water!

This was divine. I don’t consider myself a garlic lover and my relationship with it is sometimes troublesome (I do believe that there is such a thing as “too much garlic” and it has to do with the context). But this recipe… wow. I love it when recipes contribute to some brain flexibility. Made it as is and will repeat many times for sure. Don’t skip on the scallions, they work really well here.
I added some crab meat.. so good! Also… I used a fraction of the garlic this recipe called for and it was very very garlicky. I can’t imagine using 20 cloves.
Was very excited about this dish.   It just didn’t work for my family.  Garlic overpowered and made the dish bitter.  Followed directions to the letter.  Threw out the entire dish.

I love to cook and this recipe is fabulous!  Next time, I will add shrimp.  
I also doubled the cheese and the three sauce ingredients.  Enjoy!

The garlic cloves I had were exceptionally large, so I only used 10. Which I felt was just enough. This recipe is so persnickety with the method of cooking this, but not the amount of garlic. It would be more helpful if, instead of 20 garlic cloves, which is an open ended amount, it was measured by cups or grams or ounces. 
Also, boiling the pasta in a shallow pan does make it overcooked and creamy, but it’s also quite dangerous. When you’re swirling it around so that it doesn’t clump together, some of the water does come out of the pan and burn your foot, which is what happened to me, so I wish that issue was addressed.
I enjoyed the results of this recipe, but it is flawed.
To save time, process a bag of peeled garlic until desired texture.   Spread into a thin layer in a ziplock bag. Freeze. Break off chunks as needed.

Delicious. I have made this a few times, and I have recently not using a little less than the full 20 cloves of garlic and it still tastes great .  Whole house smells like garlic for about a full 24 hours after cooking but it eventually goes away :)
instructions are awkward.  the second time around this dish was easy.  Very rich, so portions can be small.  a very cost effective dish.

I cooked this recipe from Kenji’s book “The Wok.” I didn’t have fish sauce or oyster sauce on hand, and I was planning to serve this with tinned smoked trout on top, so I used soy sauce, Worcestershire sauce, and the oil from my trout to approximate the flavors. I also added some mushroom umami powder while cooking the garlic to add some more earthy flavor.

I topped it with cilantro. I’ve never tried the original, but from 2,900 miles away from SF in Philly, this is definitely entering the rotation for an easy, delicious weeknight meal!

If you're a slow, clumsy cook, budget plenty of time to prep the garlic, but this is a real 15-minute recipe once you have that ready to go. Followed the instructions exactly, even getting out the molcajete for the second time since we registered for it on a whim in 2013. Lazily put the cloves in there whole, but would've saved time if I'd chopped them up even a little bit. The noodles turned out delicious on the first attempt—unusual for me. Will make often, without adjustments.

So I made this about 10 months ago with tamari because I didn't have shoyu or light soy sauce on hand. This time I had light soy sauce on hand and decided to substitute Maggi for the fish sauce, which I love BTW. I used 1 teaspoon of Maggi Aromé because I wasn't familiar with Maggi. It was sooo good. I think I'll go for two next time. 

If cooking for one, consider making the sauce separately and using leftover sauce for other dishes.

Have been looking forward to making this for some time....  made it last night (followed recipe exactly) and found the results to be decidedly mediocre.  The garlic flavor was there, no surprise, but little else was going on.  Even added a little salt, which I didn't think would be necessary considering the parmesan and the Asian sauces.  I did use whole wheat spaghetti.... don't think that would negatively affect the outcome?
Yes it would. The noodle should be a good vehicle for the sauce, and whole wheat spaghetti is too firm and non-absorbent. Try egg noodles instead. This is not intended to be a healthy dish :)

This recipe is sorely lacking based upon the original restaurant recipe and is "blanded down", which is disappointing coming from someone as talented as J. Kenji Alt. This dish requires fish sauce and a little soy for the proper flavor. Cooking it with spaghetti isn't normal, either. A wheat noodle (a pancit or chow mein noodle) better absorbs all the umami flavors and is perfect for the grated parmesan.
@Renee Chantler the recipe contains fish sauce and soy
Use fresh egg noodles from an Asian market.
Easy weeknight dish.  Yummy.

I had made the recipe before but this time I watched the video and noticed that he made a comment about adding a salty element at the end (fish eggs) but I went with the pancetta I had in my fridge instead.  I finely chopped it up and cooked it in another pan and added it at the end when I tossed it with the parmesan.  In hindsight, I probably could have cooked it with  the butter?  I still added the scallion. Oh my goodness!  It was akin to a jacked-up version of a carbonara!  SO GOOD!!!!
I regularly use a mortar and pestle to prepare garlic/ginger for one reason: It's the easiest way to do it (clean-up takes like 5 seconds).
It needs another flavor dimension. Next time I’ll add red pepper flakes. It does have great garlic presence.

This was so very good. I made it closely according to the recipe and it turned out awesome. It seemed like a lot of garlic but I cooked the garlic "low and slow" so that it wouldn't burn or brown and it didn't taste garlicky at all. I make cacio e pepe a lot and, like that, preparation and patience make all the difference. I set everything out and then took my time. Thanks, Chef!
Obsessed with your video and made the noodles tonight. Amazing! So good. My wife keeps saying, “That was so good.” Thanks for the big amazing flavor.
Quite nice. However I did add sautéed shiitake mushrooms to the beginning of this recipe which added a meatiness that also deepened the umami experience of the dish.

I've made this several times now, with different types of noodles - from Japanese udon to angel hair - terrific dish every time. Kenji's video - as always - is helpful and entertaining. The recipe is easy-to-follow and comes together quickly. This is just the thing for those days when you can't decide what to cook, don't really want to cook, or are cooking for one. Thanks, Kenji.

I tried this last night. The difficulty for me was judging the amount of water to use to cook the spaghetti. I used too little and lost about one ounce to the bottom of the skillet. An amount of water in the recipe would be helpful. It was still a very mild pasta. I’ll be adding additional sauce tonight.

I did add shrimp to the sautéing garlic. Tonight I’m trying some lobster tail to see if reheated it’s a little better.

Massive winner with my Family! Added canned 
 chopped clams to the mix!
If you switch from butter to ev olive oil, and from fish sauce to anchovies, you could almost call this Sicilian. Ah but then, there's the oyster sauce and soy sauce--so call it fusion cuisine!

FWIW, I did a bunch of online research and the Taiwanese consensus is that TOASTED sesame oil is used for cooking this dish, and “rice wine” refers to Shaoxing. Thai basil is key. Agree with others that NYT recipe should specify toasted sesame oil if that’s the intended ingredient.

I love this with so many meals.  One of my favourite dishes of all time was the Garlic noodles and Dungeness Crab at the restaurant Crustean; it was said that the An family actually kept a separate kitchen to keep the recipe a secret.  When I make it I add a small amount of sugar, Maggi sauce, and some broth and we use Yellow noodles when we can find them.  so many dishes go well, or better, with this.
Thoughts on using tinned fish with this?  Mackerel, sardines, chopped clams?
I added about a pound of peeled shrimp to the pan after the garlic had some color. This dish was like an ultra-umami shrimp scampi!

I replace 1/4 of noodles with stringy zucchini.

Editor’s note: This comment has been anonymized in accordance with applicable law(s).
I cooked bacon with the garlic, I highly recommend. Add the garlic when the bacon is halfway done. I accidentally burnt the bacon and garlic.
I doubted this modest recipe, but shouldn’t have. It was excellent and earned a spot in the repeat line up.

This was a hit! I reduced the amount of pasta but kept everything else the same. I used my mini food processor to prepare the garlic. Served with jumbo shrimp sauteed in butter. Pretty quick and easy (though rather a lot of cleanup).

This sounded like the kind of easy pasta dish I love, and I happened to have all the ingredients on hand.  I cut it in half just for myself, and used the same proportions.  It just didn't knock my socks off.  I think it was the oyster sauce that didn't go with the other ingredients for some reason.  But the butter/garlic/cheese mixture was nicely creamy, so maybe I'll just stick with that.  And a shower of parsley.  I just won't call it San Francisco-Style Vietnamese American Garlic Spaghetti.
I personally think this can benefit from acidity; I add chili oil but open to any suggestions!

In another comment the suggestion was that the original sauce from the resaurant probably used a little tangerine juice. So maybe a little sweet citrus, or sweetened citrus? 
Or maybe some rice cooking wine would work?
For years, I have  been making a similar Marion Grasby recipe for lunch. Perfect umami comfort food. Will give this a try.

I really wanted to love this recipe. I didn’t. It was labor intensive for such a mediocre result. I followed the recipe exactly, used good quality parmesan, and fresh garlic (the only kind I use). It was surprisingly boring- one note, and I actually had to ADD salt (I used a few more dashes of soy sauce).

This is the Thanh Long recipe. One of my favorite restaurants of all time from when I lived in SF in the mid 90s - it is still open out on Judah St. in the Sunset and still excellent! These noodles combine with their also famous Dungeness crab. Is there any reason for cooking the spaghetti in a saucepan?
@SteveR more starch in the pasta water and quicker boil. They use this method in some recipes on Serious Eats

This is the bomb! The ultimate comfort food. Made as written, and the video helped. Amazing flavors and so simple. For two, reduced the pasta to 250 gms, garlic by half (10 cloves) and the full for the sauces. So yummy.
Another stellar review from my guests. They are Vietnamese and polished 2x the recipe off. Happy smiles all around.

so gas! Paired it with miso butter brocoli recepe (also a cheeky 20 min baddie) and two oceans chandonarry where the indian ocean meets the pacific and the grape growing season is delayed! big up- leo ♡(ﾐ ᵕ̣̣̣̣̣̣ ﻌ ᵕ̣̣̣̣̣̣ ﾐ)ﾉ

Made this multiple times.  Sometimes parmesan sometimes romano, great either way. I feel it needs more sauce so I double the amount of sauce/butter and double the cheese.  Grate the cheese with fine setting & add after taking off the heat to avoid clumping.  Sauteing shelled shrimp in the butter then adding the garlic is a nice addition.  Have used prepared garlic, not as good , less flavorful, garlic press almost as easy.

I find that adding different techniques for the garlic adds layers of complexity to a somewhat purist dish such as this: garlic press; sloppily chopped garlic; oven roasted garlic, all tossed together.. I add the roasted smashed towards the end. And don't forget the freshly ground Tellicherry pepper.

Extremely delicious and easy. I halved the recipe and used a garlic press. Would be a great side dish for chicken, too. I bet it would also be great served with a fried egg on top, like many Asian dishes do. I had to add quite a bit of pasta water to get the creamy emulsified coating to manifest.

I would have thought the sauce was more like a Mexican Mojo de Ajo with a bit of fish sauce, but see where the oyster sauce would work. I would still be tempted to add a few bruised coins of ginger and top with chopped green onions, or chopped basil and mint. I guess I just need to go back to my hometown and spend the $100 and get the noodles and crab in person rather than try to make it at home.

I had an extraordinary meal at Thanh Long's Sunset location about 15 years ago. There's no way an East Coast home cook can hope to duplicate those crabs, but I'll tackle Kenji's recipe tonight in hopes of reliving part of that memorable meal.

Does anyone have any thoughts about adapting a recipe like this to shirataki noodles (i.e. noodles made from konjac root)? I've used them for years, but have never figured out how to cook them so they soften to become more pasta-like.

The cooked shirataki noodles I purchase in pouches have a toothiness similar to well-cooked spaghetti. Rinsed and gently heated, they absorb flavors well.  I'd think these would be a great alternative to wheat pasta.
Supposedly if you boil them for 3 min they'll take on a more "al dente" texture.
Can (should?) this be cooked without a wok?
I'm going to try it in a 12" cast iron skillet - I've had excellent results using it for other wok recipes.

This looks really good, but my partner is deathly allergic (as in, he almost died once) to shellfish. Any non-shellfish substitutes for the oyster sauce and fish sauce? (As I understand it, fish sauce can contain shellfish.) Thanks.
yes, look for vegan sauces. They would have no shellfish at all
Most chefs suggest using Lee Kum Kee Vegetarian Stir-Fry Sauce as a substitute for oyster sauce.
These definitely need some sort of protein component; mentaiko or crab would be excellent. Very tasty, but very plain on their own.
I like the idea of thinly sliced, dry-fried mushrooms.
All this talk of crab alongside this recipe makes me wonder if adding crabmeat to the dish would be a good addition.  I think so.
Anyone out there know a good sub for oyster sauce ? I have a shellfish allergy and it makes me queasy to use it - thankfully don’t need Epi pen- yet
Most chefs suggest using Lee Kum Kee Vegetarian Stir-Fry Sauce as a substitute for oyster sauce.
Learned from Starry Kitchen in LA to add a bit of chicken bouillon and brown butter for extra umami. (They’ve posted their version on Reddit.)

Two new cooking tools in my kitchen:
1.) I use a meat tenderizer that is round heavy with a flat head.  With the skin on the garlic, light pounding of the garlic results in releasing the garlic oils.  
2.) finally found a pasta cooker for the microwave oven.  The cooker is rectangular in shape and fits easily on the turntable dish.  Pasta long and short fits in the cooker, and it cooks pasta in 12 minutes.

Made these last night, delicious!  Added some chili oil but otherwise kept it ) 
"pure":-)   I'll experiment with adding some additional ingredients too.
The sauce is a lot more flavorful with 1 tbs. brown sugar. Love the suggestion to smash garlic with a meat mallet. Brilliant
Delicious. Sprinkle with pepper flakes when serving. Get the pasta perfectly al dente for delicious texture.

I may be a bit dense here but why cook the pasta in so little water?   Isn’t it easier just to put it into a big pot of boiling water and then transfer it to the pot with the sauce and add the reserved cooking water as needed?  It seems unnecessary - what am I missing?
"Joseph Joseph Garlic Rocker Crusher Mincer Press" on Amazon. My new favorite garlic tool.
How many level tablespoons of minced garlic should I substitute for 20 cloves?
I would in hindsight add more of the fish, soy, and oyster sauce.  The I would have added some red pepper flakes to give the dish a bit of a kick.  Oh, also, cut back on the butter.
This was delicious.  Very easy and full of flavors.

I did not have oyster sauce but used 2 teaspoons of sesame oil in its place. I also added a dash of kosher salt, cilantro and chili crisp. I served it with parm, cilantro and chili crisp on top. It was so delicious! As an addition, I stir fried red peppers, orange peppers, poblano peppers, asparagus cilantro in sesame oil, chili crisp, A dash of so￼and a few tablespoons of the garlic sauce and some left over pork from the Malaysian tacos from a delicious pop up called Sekali in the Boston area.

After seeing a food vlogger post from her restaurant how much he loved her garlic noodles, I tried this recipe. It has the exact perfect amount of funk from the fish sauce. This is truly a delicious plate of noods!
This was very tasty! I used fresh ramen noodles and added roasted shiitakes and chopped radish.
Made as prescribed, but with 12 oz of GF Italian spaghetti and vegetarian versions of oyster and fish sauce. Amazing.

I was tempted to go with chopped garlic from a jar but heeded the advice of others and it was worth the effort.  Smashed the garlic with the side of a chefs knife between wax paper. Followed the directions mostly to a "T" - didn't have fish sauce so I subbed in Worcestershire (hey it's got anchovies) and the end result was delicious.  Came together quickly - this one is going into the rotation.

Saute leeks, mushrooms, and sun-dried tomatoes along with garlic. Use extra butter for additional ingredients. Reduce garlic a bit. Reduce oyster sauce (too sweet).  Toss in large tub of spinach just before serving.

I only had whole wheat spaghetti, which is not a good substitute. But this was delicious enough to take another swing. I think I may double the sauce or halve the spaghetti. And, as suggested, I'll toss the cheese before serving.

Great dish. Pretty much followed the recipe, but bumped the fish/soy/oyster measures a tad more as I was using a 17.6 oz/500g package of imported pasta (would bump then just a tiny bit more next time, too). Added Argentine red shrimp, frozen peas and actually had some tobiko in the freezer from a previous sushi session, so I guess I went full-dress on this one, but will definitely keep the basic prep in the rotation as well.

I made this dish the other day, with the addition of some asparagus and wood ear mushrooms. I only had one bulb of garlic, so that's what I used (diced), along with dried udon noodles. It tasted great and will add it to my regular rotation.
I made this with shrimp sautéed in the butter and garlic. It is so good. I definitely make this anytime I need a little quick comforting bowl.

This was very very good (eating as I take a minute to write this). But how did people end up with any leftover pasta water? I only boiled mine for 7 minutes tops, and a few strands of spaghetti are sticking to the bottom of the skillet.
I didn't have oyster sauce or fish sauce so I didn't include them. Good anyway. Strong garlic flavor.

I'm not familiar with the original dish but we found this just okay and pretty salty. I added salmon roe at the end so perhaps that was the culprit. For my taste it needed some kind of citrus/herb or other contrast too. Thumbs up for quick and easy though.
Loved it, but salty. Added some lime juice.

The flavor here was great and I enjoyed the cultural fusion and the provenance. I love NYT Cooking but I’ve had mixed results w/the recipes; this one rocked and I’ll be using it back East to share my SFBA with friends there.

I made this last night for a group and it was a hit; I divided the sauce after the garlic step to accommodate our vegetarian, and subbed in sesame oil for the oyster and fish sauces in a separate pan. To avoid all the chopping I used stir-in garlic paste (1 tsp = one clove). For protein I quickly sautéed some Argentinian Red Shrimp; our veg pressed and sautéed tofu, since there seems to be a tempeh shortage right now. Served oven-roasted broccoli with more garlic and ginger.
Delicious--full of umami.  Never thought of the synergy of Asian and Italian flavors mixed.

so good! Been on a homemade pasta kick so used fresh pasta. Also added a teaspoon of dark mushroom soy sauce (was a little sort on oyster sauce). Best addition was some dried black trumpets foraged from a couple summers ago!

Not enough flavor. I added vinegar, chili basil paste, and lime juice. Also, my wife cant eat cheese so I subbed in tahini to thicken things. You can’t mess up garlic noodles and there are lots of ways to riff.

A bit underwhelmed by the garlic (perhaps because I minced instead of crushed), and my 9-year old son didn’t love it. He added marinara. I added chopped Calabrian peppers, and we were both far happier. Maybe it was the (old) wok?
A flawless recipe! My only concern is cooking cheese in my wok, after which I had to scrub it harder than I'm used to/comfortable with. @Kenji any advice?
This looks wonderful and I can't wait to try it.  Can anyone recommend a dairy-free alternative for Parmesan or Pecorino Romano?
Amazon, Whole Foods, etc. sell dairy-free grated "cheese." But really, this recipe doesn't need any.

Miso

I've used white or red in pesto

This is an abs fab, easy, cheap meal.
I recommend cooking the pasta to al dente; it was still undercooked and hard as directed. I added cocktail shrimp at the end to make it a really yummy meatless meal
Do you think I could make the garlic sauce ahead of time and jar it, then cook up small batches of pasta as needed?
Yeah sure. I bet you could make like ice cubes of the sauce and then melt them into the pasta as needed. Kinda how those frozen pasta bags from the grocery store do.

"WARNING: The USDA advises against storing minced garlic in oil in the fridge or at room temperature, because there is risk of producing botulism toxins. If you want to mince garlic ahead and store it, freezing is the safest way to go." https://www.theyummylife.com/Mince_Freeze_Garlic

Has anyone doing the low carb thing tried Shirataki noodles with this recipe? I have just discovered these (made from the konjac root). I think they're pretty amazing but am just wondering how they hold up in a dish like this. Obviously would adjust the method from using dry noodles.
20??? 20 cloves of garlic?

I used 21.  It was just one clove too many.

20 is the bomb

Question: ‘20 medium garlic cloves’ mean? I grow my own garlic and each head is the size of my fist and contains just 5 cloves (sometimes 6) - so 4 whole heads is A LOT of garlic! Close to two cups! Not that you can ever have too much garlic. But still - does anyone have a suggestion as to what quantity works for this recipe?
You should be good with two heads of your garlic, I believe.

Cut recipe in half for just one, but only about a qtr pound of noodles. Finished w a squeeze of tangelo because there was one on the counter, My new favorite comfort food and ready to eat in minutes. A winner.
I only wish I’d read the note about doubling the sauce. Oh well, practice practice practice…
I haven't made this recipe yet, though it sounds like a great dish to add the mullet bottarga (cured mullet roe) which I have waiting in my fridge.

Other than using minced garlic from a jar, which I still smashed via a mortar and pestle, I followed this recipe exactly, including cooking the noodles in a frying pan, which I'd never done before.  It is stupidly easy (it really did only take 15 minutes) and it's delicious.
Made this as written!  So good!  Increased the Asian ingredients just a tad and sprinkled scallions served with Indian chicken meatballs on the side. Yum!

I halved the recipe and used 2, 12" skillets (1 nonstick for the "finishing" pan). I also used nonstick pan safe tongs to do the finishing/tossing of the pasta. I added a couple of TBS of the pasta water during the finish. Simple, flavorful, and quick to make. I probably would use a wok or dutch oven if making the full recipe.

I have a garlic twister ($10 on Amazon) works great for making paste. However, my noodle sauce came out dark brown, not at all like the photo. If I do again I might saute the garlic in olive oil and then add butter. I have black (squid ink) noodles I want to try this with also, as the overall taste is very nice.

Never would I or any Italian that I know (and I am 85 so know a good number) would ever 'cook' Parmesan or Pecorino.  As for mincing garlic it's a snap to use the knife smash and mince process but recently I have become enamored of my rasp, or grater - it has a fine mesh and does the job as well as a mortor and pestal, my first choice for the amount of cloves this recipe calls for. Advised by another commenter, I will look for Maggi online  and avoid the very saltiness of fish sauce.

Having found last seasons garlic, I used this green garlic for this dish and it's wonderful. Milder and sweeter than full grown cloves the dish is still redolent but not at all hot like some older garlic can be. Just elegant- I adjusted the amounts of soy sauce and oyster sauce to match the delicate flavors of this garlic. Really lovely. Farmer's markers at this time of year have much green garlic (I'm in the wine country in Northern California) , if you're interested in trying it.
A key ingredient missing here is Maggie. It’s almost more important than the soy sauce :)
I have been obsessed with these noodles for years. I have always heard that the secret ingredient is Maggi Sauce.  I’m surprised it’s not one of the ingredients.
Cut the recipe in half. Not very flavourful. If I try again I’ll double the sauce, add red pepper flakes and skip the parm. It would be tasty w sautéed prawns.

My kids loved this.  I adapted it with a little less garlic and no oyster sauce because I did not have any in the house.  Was still great.  Mixed in some steamed broccoli florets near the end so the broccoli became coated in the sauce.  Served with miso glazed salmon.

wooowee,this was very garlic-y but good.I had crab &shrimp stock I had made previously in the freezer so I used that instead of water to pan cook the pasta.I used 6 cups for the one pound of pasta but could have gone with less since I had to take out the pasta and reduce the liquid.I topped the pasta with Vietnamese caramel prawns.The butter in the sauce had great flavor but next time I will try olive oil or half olive oil--I found the sauce too rich and the texture not ideal texture cooled down
I was skeptical about the amount of pasta water used but this recipe made me a convert! I cut up the garlic then used a mortar and pestle to fully mash it. This dish was a winner winner!

Made this tonight following recipe exactly; wouldn't change anything, though a splash of tangerine would be interesting as others mentioned adding. Now my favorite pasta recipe. So easy and what a great comforting dish worthy to serve to company.
This is just fantastic. Will become one of our regular dishes! We did a half recipe for two and sort of wish we had more left over...

Next time, I'll add peas, broccoli or brussel sprouts... used rice noodles this time - good sauce flavor. Family said could go with a little less garlic next time... they aren't fish lovers and were leery of the fish sauce but ended up liking it!

Just made this & added cooked prawns. Mixed results; flavors good but... Used Trader Joe's GF spaghetti, b/c husband has celiac. Pasta water was extremely starchy & thick; should have thinned it more. Probably won't use this method again w/GF pasta. Reduced amt of (GF) tamari, which is much saltier than soy sauce. Thanks for excellent suggestions to use meat tenderizer on garlic. Def couldn't tell it had THAT much garlic. Might try recipe again.
Followed the recipe pretty much to the letter (a tad more cheese). I was particularly surprised cooking the pasta this way, but it worked! Complete comfort food.

Used whole-wheat spaghetti because that’s what we had. It really stood up well to the intensity of the garlic and I think I’ll use it again. Followed the recipe as directed and it was sooooo good. Next time, I’ll try reducing butter and maybe eliminating cheese to see if it can be slightly more healthful without giving up too much flavor. Thank you for this wonderful dish.
Fantastic recipe, such complex flavors. I added some frozen green peas at the end.
LOVED IT!  Made it as printed!  I will make again and again.

I sautéed some fresh shiitake mushrooms and added them in at the end  along with the green onions; otherwise followed the recipe exactly. The blend of flavors was delicious, and more subtle than I expected, given the quantity of garlic. Best eaten immediately, but with only two of us, we had leftovers which heated up nicely in a skillet with a little extra water. I will definitely be making this again!
Simple and delicious. I added a crumble of good canned Italian tuna packed in olive oil. Highly recommend.
Delicious! You can cut the recipe in half and use Udon noodles
Smashing garlic with the flat side of a big knife blade (push blade down with the heel of your hand) is a quick way to loosen the skin before peeling.  Two birds with one stone.

Adding some blistered cherry tomatoes to finish (grilled, roasted, maybe broiled) goes a long way to add some brightness to the dish. For reference, had this recipe in mind for the grilling technique, just skip making the yogurt sauce (https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1014987-grilled-cherry-tomatoes-with-curry-and-golden-raisins).
The second time we made this we added shrimp just as we were finishing sautéing the garlic. It complemented the flavors of this dish quite nicely.  Thanks for another delicious recipe, Kenji

I made a half recipe, following very closely, but, save for exact pasta weight, I eyeballed the rest. The both of us were enthusiastic about the results, S deciding that we should eat this every week even though she long ago lost her sense of smell. Umami reigns, and I appreciated the good fish sauce I used.

We had a cheap bottle (under $10!) from TJ's, Roustabout Meritage from the Paso Robles region that seemed to fit this dish very well. Simple good food.
Suggest reducing the amount of fish sauce and using low-sodium soy sauce. Was too salty.
Thoughts on substitutes for the oyster and fish sauce to make this vegetarian?
In Kenji's YouTube video of this recipe he mentions Maggi seasoning as an alternate source of umami. Might be worth a try!
Be sure to add scallions
I used a micro-plane grater for the garlic.  Amazing results. This is a fascinating dish - simple, cross cultured, delicious.

I made it for myself tonight for the first time and loved it. Convenient to scale down for one because the ingredients are easily divisible by  four. Went with shrimp tonight; next time - bottarga! Should be great.
Delish!  Added 1 lb of shrimp marinated in olive oil, lemon zest, salt, and pepper to the garlic mixture then topped with the cooked pasta.  Shrimp perfectly cooked.  Will make again and again.

Loved the flavor but I had the same issue with the cheese that i have when I make cacio e pepe - cheese gets globby and doesn't ever turn into the creamy goodness.  Any tips? Next time I will grate cheese on top at the end..
If you grate it with a microplane it's so fine that it'll melt right into your sauce. That's worked for me, anyway.
You can buy garlic paste at Indian/Pakistani food stores.

I do not recommend using the jarred stuff, it has some preservative that leaves a funny taste and the processing really takes the edge off the garlic. However, you can cheaply buy already peeled fresh garlic cloves in bulk at many Asian markets. There is also a frozen product from Israeli "Dorot Gardens" that is the equivalent of 20 minced garlic cloves (or teaspoons), individually frozen. It is widely available and even at Trader Joe's.

Made this as directed.  I really loved it... so easy. The results wayyyy exceeded the effort.  On my second making, I added some sautéed chicken to make a bigger meal out of it.  I could also see adding red pepper, or broccolini, or, pretty much anything.

If you look at the contents of MAGGI it contains GLUTAMIC ACID. MSG is derived from this.AKA  Monosodium glutamate. I avoid ANY product that includes it. I can't be the only one who ,in the days when MSG was added to 
 all Chinese dishes in restaurants, developed an MSG Headache. Wouldn't wish this on anyone. Many Chinese restaurants add NON MSG to menus. Stick to Fish Sauce.

Then you had better skip this recipe, because parmesan is full of MSG. Really though, please give this article a read: https://fivethirtyeight.com/features/how-msg-got-a-bad-rap-flawed-science-and-xenophobia/ Happy cooking!

Jess, I've heard that, but I've also had bad reactions to high levels of artificial MSG in food. My guess is that it's very easy to add more of the powder than typically occurs naturally in food. My FIL falls into this group- "Some people may experience adverse effects from MSG due to a condition called MSG symptom complex (MSC) (1Trusted Source).Symptoms include weakness, flushing, dizziness, headache, muscle tightness, difficulty breathing, and even the loss of consciousness." I've seen it.
Leary of adding Italian Parmesan cheese to Asian flavors. Does it work? Anyone tried this recipe?

First of all, kudos to J. Kenji López-Alt et al for the recipe.  A truly bizarre panoply of ingredients, so acknowledge that, and then get out the wok.  It's a magical dish.  Followed as written, but I did, as others suggested, add Maggi.  Oh, and I also own one of those rocking garlic crushers.  They, uh, rock.  

Cook this!

This was delightful!! Amazing flavor. I saw some comments about reducing the amount of pasta and modified how much we used. Ended up with 10 oz, whatever was left of an already opened pack, and DANG those noodles tasted like I had been catapulted head first into a big, beautiful garlic patch! Truly, I’m a garlic fanatic, but felt that 14-16oz is the right amount of pasta here, especially served alongside some chicken thighs or other well-spiced protein. Thanks for this keeper Kenji!
Preparing the garlic this way makes it taste as if it came ff a store bought jar of prepared garlic. It has that cloying bitter sweet taste.
Wow, this is delicious. Perfect hang over food. I made this with ramen noodles and it was amazing. Didn't have oyster sauce so I did an extra tsp of soy and fish.

Doubled the garlic and was great.  Used ghee instead of butter-always good.  Next time I’ll double the cheese and the soy, oyster, and fish sauces.  Saw a comment recommending Maggi, a staple in much German cooking, a great addition to any braise.  Will try with this.
I used a vegetarian sub for the oyster sauce (coconut amino "hoisin") but I did count out my garlic... it's very good.

This is a great dish, and it's now in regular rotation.  Sometimes we add sautéed chicken, but the noodles do stand alone.  For me, they taste different than the ingredients might imply (if that makes any sense) but I could eat the whole batch if I'm not careful.

I've really been looking forward to making this. Used 100% buckwheat soba and tamari due to wheat allergy but made to the letter aside from that. I'm pretty disappointed. I love garlic and this is SUPER garlic-y, but the whole picture is very rich and intense and I was done after 2 bites. It wasn't, surprisingly, very salty with unsalted noodles. Added some Thai basil and a splash of rice vinegar after my first bites and that helped a lot, also tried lime juice, that was not as good. Meh.
We added cherry tomatoes, basil, and meatballs. Nice additions

Sauce did not come together
Didn’t sit well with Sam’s stomach
Crab and shrimp tossed in make great additions. May try lobster next time

I would normally say there is no such thing as too much garlic, but in this case it was just too much for me and the family. 
We gave it a good try but it was inedible. This is the only NYT cooking recipe that didn’t work out for me.
add the pasta water, more cheese, and either press garlic or get a mortar and pestle. could also use some salf

Good but not amazing. Would love to try the version at San Francisco’s Thanh Long restaurant because then maybe I could perfect this. My guess is that you need to use fresh noodles which I did not have time to make the day I tried it. Overall I would decrease the garlic a bit. Perhaps my cloves were just too big. I wish they would give estimates in weights because knowing what a medium garlic clove looks like is hard. But you could easily weigh 4oz of crushed garlic cloves for example on a scale

Replaced noodles with miracle noodle angel hair pasta and added shrimp for protein and dang if it didn't turn out to be the best low-carb meal I've ever made. Agree with previous posts, about pre-minced jar garlic , you can taste the difference for sure but it's a good enough trade off for being able to make it quickly without ruining it, at least for me
Great when made it as written, and will try the roe next time.

Followed suggestions halved garlic and doubled sauce and added extra cheese was really good and kids loved it but still pretty garlicky I am a greengrocer and new season and old season garlic has very different intensities. Will reduce garlic by another two cloves next time.
Use Maggi

Made this 2 days ago: 
first of all, it refrigerates and reheats nicely. 
I ended up doubling the sauce volumes (fish, soy, ouster) to get enough flavor onto my water-butter-cheese to create that creamy slick noodle covering that made everyone at dinner so happy!
How do I make this less salty? I salted the water- oops.

Just use regular water from the sink. The starched pasta water is great, but you can still achieve the same emulsification without it. Much better to lose out on the starch than to add salty water to the already salty sauce. In his video, Kenji takes a moment to call attention to the fact that he isn't salting his pasta water for that reason.
Not my favorite Kenji recipe for sure; used rotelli noodles but still, didn't hit home at all for me.
Quick question— step 3 says “add the cheese to the wok”. First mention of a wok. Is this meant to be “add the cheese to the skillet” that has the reserved pasta water?
The wok is mentioned in step 1

This is SO good and so flexible for other veggies and comes together quickly. What more could ask for? I put some Korean chili paste in for heat and it was delicious. 

Also, sautéed some spinach and put the noodles on a bed of that and delish!
Add miso paste for a yummy twist

I just made these noodles. Really good! The only thing I’d change is the amount of butter. The noodles were a little greasy when I thought a drier noodle would be better. I forgot to buy scallions so I added a handful of chopped cilantro that I had. Yum!

I live in San Francisco and have been a huge fan of garlic noodles are my a few local restaurants. These are just as good! (Little less butter adjustment)

If the noodles look too greasy, that means the sauce didn't fully emulsify yet or it broke. The solution is to add more pasta water. I halved this recipe when I made it and I still ended up using over a cup of pasta water at the end (added gradually).
Sometimes I'll put in a tablespoon of hippy dust at the end, or a few finely chopped anchovy fillets at the beginning.
hippy dust is "nooch", or nutritional yeast

I had a few lobster tails and wanted pasta.  I don't know how I came upon this in my search, but boy, I am glad I did.  I made as according to directions and it turned out absolutely perfect!!!  I cut the lobster tails in half and sauteed them in a bit of garlic, olive oil, and butter, then served them in their shell on top of the pasta.  Perfection.  This will be a staple in my house forever!!!!  Don't  adulterate this recipe!!!! It is perfect!
We served this with left over chicken adobo.. hoo boy!     Easy and delicious!
mmm garlic.. big fan.. add a squeeze of lemon to your bowl.. bc lemon is always a great idea.
Mince garlic until it’s almost a paste (at least for some of it).
I made this dish and followed it to a t— added tobiko and some simple stir fried pink shrimp and it was amazing.
We had to omit the oyster sauce because of an allergy, and without it, this is just garlic-y spaghetti. It's delicious though!
Excellent- so simple, but so much umami flavor. Will make again.

Having all the sauces on hand, this was such a good pantry dish. I added some pre-cooked crab meat at the end which was perfect. 

Also, for those afraid of peeling that much garlic, Kenji used pre-peeled garlic in his YouTube video making the dish, and he noted while it's not quite as good as unpeeled, for this dish the trade off is worth it.

I found this to be a very odd recipe in the end. It was bland which I found surprising given the amount of garlic. But the recipe did not do justice to garlic really.  It lacked punch. The amount of scallions and parm could have helped if there were more of each. Whats the point to a small had full in a pound of pasta. The same goes for the parm. I also found it silly to cook pasta in a frying pan when a regular pot would do. I would say for me, this recipe was an annoying FAIL.
Not worth the effort; even when the sauce comes to the right texture, it still tastes like a lame riff on alfredo, but extra garlicy.

Dressing pasta in oyster sauce always impresses friends here in Rome for originality, as does the simplicity. But Italians abhor garlic; 20 cloves in a dish for four people would send my guests running for the elevator, with a stop in the loo on the way. I make this with one clove, and add guanciale (a Viet version of carbonara). In season I add black truffles.

So, you made oyster sauce noodles and not garlic noodles. 
You really should give it a try as written, or cut garlic in half, or try slicing the garlic to mellow the flavor, just to get an idea of what this fusion dish is all about, and you might actually like it...
I had high hopes for this recipe, since I've enjoyed garlic noodles in San Francisco many times. In the end, it was just meh. I mostly regretted having wasted all that garlic on this.

I’m impressed at how easy it was to make. 
The taste was awesome. I made it as written but I used 12 oz of shrimp. I also used vegan butter as an additional test.

Woah! Garlic bomb!  15 minutes cooking time? Hah. Try peeling and mincing 20 cloves in 15 minutes.

Otherwise another genius recipie from Kenji Lopez Alt.
Delicious, though the garlic flavour can be overwhelming
I love this recipe so much because it’s SO quick, easy, and tasty. The sauce is good on anything but my favorite is just mixed in with some ramen noodles and fresh herbs.
Easy tasty. Lots of garlic.
I used shrimp and some of the suggestions from the comments like tangerine juice. Delicious.

The first time I made this, I wasn’t impressed. I followed the recipe exactly as written. Then I watched a video of Lòpez-Alt making it and he used half the amount of pasta listed here. So I made it again doubling the sauce ingredients and used a big handful of scallions. The result this time was so good, we couldn’t stop eating it. I added shrimp and the two of us ate almost the entire pound of pasta.

By accident, the small pieces of olive-oil-roasted-broccoli that were also on my plate got mixed in to the noodles. Delicious! Works really well with the creaminess and balances the garlic, which I always use more of anyway.

I was hesitant to try this recipe because, sadly, I have never tried anything like it.  But it was truly delish.  I went heavy on the oyster, soy and fish sauce, and topped with shrimp.  The garlic is so important here - mine was not great, with big green germs and slightly bitter flavor.  Can't wait to try this with sticky new farmer's market garlic.  Seek out the best oyster sauce you can find, too.  I served it with chili crisp.  My husband went crazy.
I found the flavors a little unbalanced. Added a splash of rice vinegar and it was perfect.
Shh, don't tell: I used high quality organic granulated garlic. It was probably sacrilegious, but it was so delicious and so quick to put together that I'd do it again!
So, I had salted my pasta before reading the comments. BUT, I think, because I used reduced sodium soy sauce and unsalted butter, it was the perfect amount of salt.
Quality of ingredients is critical for best taste.
I found it much easier to use a garlic press, especially for this amount of garlic.
I've added this to my rotation ... with shrimp and spinach, it's a 2 pan meal.  Yum!

Vegetarians and vegans do not use any fish or oyster sauce in their. It should be made without those ingredients. The main reason I avoid lot of far east restaurants is that they always add them as it is part of their secret sauce possibly.

also...for vegetarians (and vegans) you also need to eliminate the Parmesan Cheese since it contains animal rennet (you may be able to fine some without it at Trader Joes). If you google animal rennet you may never enjoy cheese again.
I like using fresh Shanghai noodles, thick and chewy
This was absolutely perfect. Did not make one change. Minced the garlic, didn’t need any extra water. I want to eat the whole pan!
Quick. Easy. Would put some l shallots or something for extra texture next time.
I get fried shallots and fried garlic at my local Cambodian store, also fresh shitakis add so much.
Good n tasty

Awesome as is!  Haven’t found need to increase sauces if done as is.   Added shrimp tonight as suggested in description- right before adding garlic to butter and perfect.  Yum! But with the shrimp addition I multiplied sauces, butter and garlic by 1.5 to compensate for the add in- which worked well but think I could have simply doubled everything as well and that may have been even better.  Happy place noodle dish.  Yum.
Super easy and very delicious!
Pretty good. Will make again. Will prob increase the sauce amount next time. Instead of spaghetti I used a pound of twin marquis Hong Kong style pan fried noodles, which are always great
Super flexible recipe that lends itself to to whatever you have in your pantry

I made it as is. I cooked the garlic gently so as not to burn it. I like fish sauce and would probably add a little more next time. Seems weird to add cheese, but it worked. I chose Pecorino Romano for it’s melty quality as Parmesan tends to clump.
I use an empty beer bottle to smash garlic
I'm hoping there is a substitute for fish sauce & oyster sauce as we have a severe anaphylaxis to fish/shellfish. Can anyone comment/suggest?
Instead of the roe David use a bit of anchovy whole or paste. Pretty good!PMI
I use more garlic because I like that flavor.

I’ve been swooning over these noodles for decades, every time I’m in SF I HAVE to go to Thanh Long out in The Avenues for their chili garlic crab and these heavenly noodles.
I can’t wait to make this as soon as the big Dungeness crabs are in season!
I’m going to make this as a side dish.  Any suggestions what to serve with it?  Something Asian?
I used thinly slice Egglife wraps in place of the spaghetti and it’s a flavorful protein meal.
I meant oyster sauce.

I am a hardcore lover of umami flavors. I was skeptical about this dish, but it was delicious, with a couple suggestions.

1) Those who doubled the sauce per serving are right. And although I had my doubts about the cheese, without it, it's not the same. I used grated parmesan Reggiano.

2) I made my own noodles using my Marcato pasta maker for spaghetti. 300 grams bread flour, 1/4 teasp. salt, 150ml water, kneaded for 15 mins. Cooked for 1 min. 

I plan on adding bok choy, mushrooms, shrimp
Better than takeout and almost as easy! Added a pound of shrimp with the garlic, as suggested, and needed to double the sauce as a result. Topped with sesame seeds--a nice touch!

I was expecting a much more flavorful dish. I followed the recipe exactly as written. This came out meh. The flavors were quite muted. I did like the way the pasta cooked and I will try this method in other pasta dishes.
Made because of the hype and will be making again often. Easy and delicious. I added chopped bok choi (wilted) and fried tofu cubes, which I cooked before everything else and then set aside.
We just discovered Thanh Long this summer. Terrific place. Yes, the garlic noodles are a must; as is the roasted crab. A perfect pairing.

Thanks for suggesting vegan (or vegetarian) oyster sauce. I’m allergic to fish and seafood so can no longer eat many of my favorite Asian foods. Does Maggi have any fish or seafood in it? (Noticed someone said they use it instead of fish sauce.)
You can access Maggi nutritional information at: https://www.goodnes.com/maggi/products/asian-seasoning-sauce-800ml/
The mixture of butter and Parmesan with fish sauce tastes pretty much like you’d think it would. Works better with chili oil on top, but a hard pass on ever making this again.

Amazing.
- Be very careful not to brown the garlic at all, it adds a strong bitter note even slightly browned.
- Replaced one tsp fish sauce with maggi, created a great balanced flavor. Could probably use 50%-100% of all of the oyster/soy/fish/maggi sauces.
- I grilled four small chicken breasts dipped in flour separately, and chopped them up and mixed them into the pasta at the end. Combined well, and the flour coating helped the sauce stick to the chicken.

Made as is and is easy to cook and delicious. Elementary school kids asked for 3rds. That’s a tell tale sign. When the noodles sat for a bit and got gummy and stuck, I just added more pasta water and warmed it up in a skillet. The pasta loosened up again.
Note to self: garlic should weigh 50--70 grams

Delicious.  I used double the sauces, but otherwise made as written. Loved boiling the noodles in a frying pan. It was all delicious, we scarfed it down.  You could easily “dress up” the recipe by adding chicken, tofu, shrimp, peas, or anything else you wanted.

Everyone loved this quick one!  I briefly sautéed chopped shrimp in the butter first and then removed them (added again at the end) to sauté the garlic.  I also added some chopped broccoli to the pasta as it was nearly done cooking.  The dish is great on it's own or with the addition of broccoli and shrimp.  Great weeknight meal idea!

Very delicious! The only modifications I make were adding about an extra tsp each of soy sauce, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and parmesan after everything was mixed with the pasta. I think it's a little too much pasta for the sauce ingredients, so next time I'd double the sauces and cheese (but keep the same amount of garlic).

Hmm I tried this recipe last night. Smashed and minced the garlic. For 20 cloves of garlic we found it strangely lacking in flavor. Not sure why people are crazy for this recipe. My 7 year old asked why did I make just plain noodles. Am I missing something? Palate not refined enough??

The sauce is delicious and subtle 
next time I’d use 3/4 of a pack of pasta to go with this volume of sauce

It’s ok. I think just making some pasta  aglio e olio with a dash of soy or oyster would be really good.  This was soooooo salty (I used unsalted butter and didn’t salt my pasta.  Also, I can’t imagine using 20 cloves of garlic?!?!?!

One way to control sauté: life the pan off the heat entirely. It's easy no need to watch the flame, just watch the sauce/pasta/butter. Watch and lift off heat! Works every time.

When adding the grated Parm or Pecorino cheese, I sprinkle a portion of the amount to be added on the pasta and, using tongs, grab the naked pasta at the edges and fold it over the cheese.  This allows the cheese to melt and emulsify with the liquid and coat the pasta.  Done incrementally, the cheese is less likely to clump on the (cooler) implement, be it spoon or tongs. Adding a bit more pasta water early on helps, too.

I freeze whole heads of garlic. Once frozen, pull them from the freezer, defrost for c. 20mins--not much longer!--and then smash the heads with your fist until most of the cloves disentangle. Then somewhat more gently smash the individual cloves--the skin should peel off easily. Really, trust me on this! Then pop the naked cloves in a ziplok bag and back into the freezer. They'll be as good as fresh in your recipes.

I just discovered a new trick for removing garlic husks (though I'm sure others already know about it). Take a large plastic lid, such as from a big yogurt container, and place it top side down over the cloves and crush. This removes the husks, and you can further smash the garlic as you please. Another trick—add a pinch of Maldon or Kosher salt to help mince the garlic.

I think even a peanut or almond butter, or sesame paste or sunflour butter would work too...it would be a little different flavor but still a garlic noodle...
This was great! So easy, quick and unique!
Capers! We don’t use fish or oyster sauce and use a mushroom sauce that works well. Didn’t really notice much difference in the cooking of the pasta. Good recipe. I’ll make again.
Added garlic shrimp and edamame.yum
Just wondering, I love this recipe not what size are the garlic cloves?  Russians are very large, should I use fewer?
Great and Easy to Make
What if someone is allergic to oysters? Is there a substitute for the oyster sauce? Another that might work?

Some substitution ideas: - mushroom sauce or mushroom broth (Lee Kum Kee and other brands make a mushroom version of oyster sauce for vegetarians) - a bit of dark soy sauce + increase fish sauce - hoisin sauce I'd also direct you to the miso recipe that's similar to this! https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1020979-five-ingredient-creamy-miso-pasta -
Use vegan oyster and fish sauce, available from Whole Foods, Trader Joe's, and online variously.
Add sardines
As I'm not a big fish fan, I'd throw in some bacon bits.

Maybe make again, but thoroughly poach the garlic and invest in very good garlic. Add more cheese, maybe some lemon zest. The skillet idea is dumb. Don’t do that. Definitely 1-2 cups of pasta water required.
This is a great quick meal! lots of room to experiment as well. It has become my, oh shoot Im cooking tonight meal, and everyone is happy.
Yumm!! Craving these right now!!

The first time I made it, I followed the recipe to a T.  I found the end result was bland.  The second go, I added a big pinch of pepper flakes while tossing the pasta.  Next time, I will add some fresh ground pepper as well.
I love adding seared shrimp in buttery red pepper flake goodness and a couple pinches of rpf into the sauce to bloom before adding the noodles

This is a delicious recipe and so easy to make. Have smashed and chopped the garlic and smashed is definitely best. 

Tried the original in San Francisco and this is definitely much better and more umami. The crab however is still a perfect addition to both.
Excellent. I used what I had : Udon noodles, cotija cheese (can’t get good parm here), and chopped broccolini for the green stuff at the end.
One of my fav Kenji recipe

I like to pan-fry the cooked pasta in some hot oil (with a heavy iron skillet pressing down on the noodles as they fry).  I also add peas.  And sometimes I also add Asian fried-chile oil to the sauce.

I substituted the fish sauce and clam sauce for white wine and the juice of a jar of stuffed green olives with garlic, 
Kept the soy sauce keeping it vegetarian.
It was delicious, divine, and delightful!
Thanks!
Please do yourself a favor and make this with five-spice chicken on top. The perfect combination and one of my favorite meals from the Perilla restaurant in SF.
I purchased oyster sauce and fish sauce a while ago and they were both getting old so I looked for a recipe using both and this fit the bill. I recommend it. I am a sauce man.
Very delicious.  I’ve yet to try it without peel-on shrimp like in the notes, but this might be my favorite recipe on here so far.  Soooo good, easy peasy to fix.
This was tasty. I followed the instructions closely and I think you need more water for the pasta as I had none leftover when the pasta was transferred to the sauce pan.
Delicious! Followed the recipe as it was written.

If you’ve lived in San Francisco for a while and visited the restaurants that serve these noodles, you’ll likely have learned the local lore about just how hard the family who runs this restaurant has worked to keep this recipe a secret - according to some, all the way back to Vietnam. I find it in very, very poor taste that Lopez-Alt went out of his way to sleuth this. I’ve stopped using any of his recipes as a result.

So for anyone who has never visited San Francisco, let alone lived there, they should not be permitted to experience this recipe?  Get over yourself and widen your perspective. Kenji has made enormous contributions to cookery, especially for the home chef. I'm grateful to him for this recipe.

If you read through the story that this recipe is featured in (link is just above"Preparation") you will see that this is Kenji's interpretation of Ms. An's recipe, not one stolen from her.  I would think any good chef would be able to eat a dish and create something similar. I am not a good chef, so need Kenji to do the (re)creation.In the story,  he honors the original and doesn't claim this recipe as his own - even though what follows is his own.

@ben
Apologizes Ben, but that’s just weird.

Definitely double the sauce next time. Try hoisin instead of oyster for a different flare as suggested. Try Maggi instead of soy sauce. Needs more pasta water and cheese than I had planned. Try the cooking noodles as per the recipe. Try adding spice?

If 4.5 ★s were an option, that's where I'd go. This was delicious! But it required much adjustment. For starters, we're 45 mins from Gilroy, "the garlic capital of the world," and our local garlic is HUGE. I cut back to 15 cloves, but they were probably equivalent to 30 grocery store cloves. Similarly, whose "small handful" is the measure for scallions? Volume measurement would have been helpful. Conversely, ¼c cheese was hardly enough. I used at least ½c of both Romano & Parmesan.
I made this recipe as-is and enjoyed it, then the next day tossed the leftovers with lemongrass chicken, cilantro and lime juice. It was great!
Loved this recipe, very surprising flavor combination. Added lump crab meat which was extra decadent and extremely delicious.
Delicious!! Cooked as written and substituted Maagi (yellow cap) for light soy sauce. No scallions 'cause I was feeling lazy.

Dare I say it, but I found the 20 cloves of garlic to be too much and overpowering. Maybe suggest a weight or volume of garlic as some cloves are larger than others. That being said it was a good dish that I paired with a top sirloin steak.
I found this to be surprisingly one note and frankly, boring. I was disappointed with the result.
Good- just not that interesting. I did like all the garlic, though!
Pair it with miso butter broccoli to up ther intrest factor :)

I've learned never to try one of these recipes without consulting Most Helpful comments, despite the huge quantity at times (185! and some not at all helpful), because I've learned a lot from them. CURIOUS: nobody has mentioned that in recipe's last step, it says "increase heat to high" but heat (I've re-read this many times) was never lowered. THOUGHT: Instead of the flowery verbose intro, the recipe could give a bit more guidance.

The first step ends with removing it from the heat, which was a medium heat.
In the video he stresses to add the cheese off the heat and stir it thru the noodles, then put it on the high heat and stir to emulsify, adding pasta water as needed to make it creamy. 
(In the video he added probably close to half a cup of water?)
Has anyone tried just using a garlic press? I have a mortar & pestle, but they’re heavy and a pain to wash.
Decided to keep going with the turbo theme dinner by poaching asparagus and a salmon fillet in the reserved pasta water. Dumped it all in a bowl with some fresh garden chives and it was SO good!
note about garlic... if -as is often the case in April in Wisconsin- your garlic is showing signs of age and hotness- chop it and steep it in hot water for a minute or 2 to take the bite away

To round this out as a meal, I cooked two branzino fillets in the garlic and butter before adding the oyster/soy/fish sauce mixture, then added a few handfuls of spinach to the sauce. I also chopped a bunch of asparagus and blanched it with the pasta for the last 2:30 the noodles were cooking.  Reserved a cup of pasta water, drained, and dumped everything into the Dutch oven to combine to avoid mess. I added an extra teaspoon each or the savory sauces since I was adding so much ingredient volume. Delicious! Will definitely make again.

Delicious.  But, ....  You have to really be a fan of garlic to enjoy this.  I am such a fan, so, for me  it was terrific.  My wife even liked it once she learned that just like steak sauce doesn't taste like steak, oyster sauce and fish sauce don't taste like oysters and fish.  Geez!
Topped it with roasted chickpeas for extra protein and it was really good. Cheese clumped up in the pan, so next time I'll add it slowly.
This is infinitely better when made with fresh Chinese egg noodles or lo mein noodles.  Also… use Maggi instead of soy sauce.  And add msg.

A few years ago when I had four roommates, this was the first thing we cooked in a series of dinner parties. We quintupled the recipe, for 100 total cloves of garlic. Every time a guest walked in the door, we'd shout "start peeling garlic!" It was a big hit.
enjoyed the recepie.   learnt some thing new about garlic.  nicely explained

title: Buldak Pizza (Korean Fire Chicken Pizza) Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

comments.commentBody: I didn’t have time to marinate chicken, so I cooked it, chopped it up and tossed in a lightened up version of the marinade before putting on pizza. This was so delicious and I can’t wait to make again! Red onions worked well too!

Cheese Buldak is one of my favorite dishes, so I'll definitely try this. But... I'll start with 1/2 teaspoon of black pepper in the marinade (same as in Maangchi's Cheese Buldak recipe here on NYT Cooking), not 1 tablespoon. Gochujaru and gochujang  aren't what make buldak hot -- the pepper does. I'd cook the chicken as per the recipe, taste, then add more pepper if it seemed necessary.

I am preternaturally excited by this dish & recipe—guess I am expecting a spicy a fall!  
1) love that you can really making 2 dishes in one—so a bit of extra chicken, and you’ve got a chicken dish—
2) it’s great that it can marinate 1-3 days,  so both a fun project & a make ahead decadent meal for a busy day. 
3)however, the clear & flexible shortcuts (I may try w/my own go-to quick pizza dough upon 2nd attempt) outlined here also make it inviting! 
Yes to red onions! —& maybe chili crisp!?
If you dust your pizza peel with stoneground cornmeal, the pizza will slide easily from the peal to the stone without tearing from the weight of the ingredients. Works every time!
7 minutes to cook a chicken thigh? Is it supposed to be a bit raw inside?

I made this recipe as directed, and found it quite spicy (as someone who enjoys/can tolerate spicy food). 

The second time around, I only used 1 teaspoon of black pepper, honey instead of corn syrup, and an entire 15 oz can of crushed tomatoes. Delicious!

This was delicious. I was a little concerned it'd be too spicy, but it turned out perfectly with 3 tbsp gochugaru. I did use more chicken than instructed and think that was the right move, but I wish I'd pre-cooked it a little longer. With a pizza stone at 550, I did the chicken for 8 min and then the pie for about 11, but next time I'm going to try 10 for the chicken and hopefully cut the pie time down to 8 or 9. Definitely a labor of love, but really solid recipe and so fun to put together.

Fantastic pizza! I did buy really good pizza dough (New Haven area resident), but followed the marinade and sauce recipe to the letter. Just one other change: using chicken tenderloin and letting it cook on top of the pie is pretty full proof. The pizza really takes 10-15 minutes to cook on a stone in a 500 degree oven.
You are a braver cook than I if you're putting raw chicken on top of a pizza.
So I will just add that I layer this differently: sauce, cheese, garlic, scallion, peppers, with diced chicken on top. The pizza takes 12-15 min to fully cook on a stone in my 500 degree oven

This recipe is awesome, and the pizza dough recipe is great. 

I didn’t have a pizza stone or peel… so I put 2 small baking sheets in the oven to heat, pressed the dough into rectangles, and arranged the pizza on the hot sheets. I probably had to cook them for more like 12-15 min at 515 degrees F

Also the spice is not playing around… if I make again, I’ll try to take it down a notch. But such good flavors!!

I only marinated my chicken about 2 hours, but it was still wonderful. I also used my favorite pizza dough which I had in the freezer, but otherwise made as directed. 

It was a fantastic pizza! Definitely going into the party recipe binder. I also used leftover sauce on rice cakes the next day. Delicious.

So incredibly good. The texture of the crust can really hold up to the toppings. Used a baking steel and ended up with such a beautiful underbelly, with just the right amount of char. My oven goes up to 550 F and my pizza was done at 9 minutes stretched out to 14.5 inches. Also, I used a thermometer on the chicken thighs and they were done at 9 minutes, to those who are doubting the quick oven time. Used a recipe online for pickled serranos. The sauce alone is worth trying. So good.

Pizza was pretty disappointing. It was unbalanced, and sauce was too salty. I don't think this is the recipe's fault, though, just that the concept itself is impossible to pull off. Buldak itself is a pretty heavy dish, and generally relies on a reliable supply of banchan (side dishes) to balance the greasiness and heaviness of the dish itself and to cleanse your palette. I think putting something greasy and heavy like this on pizza dough is only going to make it more heavy and more unbalanced.

I only marinated for an hour or so. Also I used Mark Bittman’s basic pizza dough recipe, which doesn’t need to sit over night (I think it’s not quite as good as recipes that take longer but it works for my weekly pizza routine). So this doesn’t have to be a multi-day affair if you want it tonight and still have an a few hours to let the dough rise! Otherwise I followed the recipe and it was delicious. Don’t skimp on the pepperoncini.

I made this with sweet earth mindful chick’n and it was great! I followed the instructions exactly except I only had active dry yeast. I read that you could substitute them 1:1 as long as you use very warm/hot water in the dough. I was worried because it didn’t rise much but it ended up working fine. Don’t forget to add the garlic slices because they’re a real treat. It ends up being pretty intensely flavorful. I drizzled a little kewpie mayo on top and I think that helped balance it out nicely.
Am a huge fan of fusion, but this didn't do it for me.  The buldak marinade was fantastic though.

This was SO good. I don't have a pizza stone so I used my normal pizza dough recipe in a cast iron, but I followed the sauce and chicken recipe exactly. After two pizzas, I still have sauce left over and I think I'll be tossing some yakisoba noodles in it
Confused by how the sauce will reduce to 1 and 1/4 cup when this is what I have in the start?
