You've added 1/2 cup stock and 1/2 cup tomatoes to the remaining  marinade in the pan before you start reducing, right?

We enjoyed this quite a lot.  I like saucy pizza, so I made extra sauce.  Glad I did.  The recipe makes a bare minimum amount of sauce as written.  

I partially cooked the chicken in the sauce prior to putting it on the pizza, because I had doubts about if the bake time was long enough, and I didn’t want salmonella.  It didn’t end up overcooked, so I think this was a good call.

Next time, I might do a little less chicken, but more pepperoncini and maybe some red onion?

I made the dough in the morning and left it to rise in an oiled bowl until mid afternoon then punched it down and folded it under as indicated (without dividing), added a little more oil to the bowl and let it rise again for about three hours.  When time to bake punched it down and cut it in half and used my fingers and palms to stretch it out on a slightly cornmealed cookie sheet.  Never used flour in these steps.  It was a fabulous crust and I will make it again.

Delicious but had to improvise a bit. Used 1 tsp red pepper flakes instead of  gochugaru; followed all steps for the chicken including marinating overnight. Used store bought dough on a 1/2 baking sheet since I don't have a pizza stone. Convection baked 500, preheat 30-40 minutes. Usually for pizza I pre bake the dough for 6-8 minutes before any toppings to crisp the bottom. I didn't do that this time but will do so next time since the center of the pizza crust was under done. This is a keeper!
This was good.  Recipe makes two!  I added corn kernels & kewpie on one, for the win.  Tasty tasty.
This was so good! It’s fun to put together and so delicious. I used store-bought dough to save a little time- followed the recipe pretty closely. Perfectly spiced and cheesy. Yum!
Excellent recipe, followed it to the letter but differed in how I cooked the pizza (ooni) and it was amazing!
I don't get it are we supposed to broil this?

Re: the raw chicken comment - it cooks seven minutes, but then cooks an additional eight-ish on the pizza. 

Minor point - I don't think the chicken stock added a lot to the sauce and created a pretty wet sauce, even when it was reduced down.
I wish someone could tell me how to find mild gochujang. In my market, all the products are labeled in Korean.

We used a different pizza dough recipe we like and I used a combination of arbol chiles and piquin chiles ground into flakes as a substitute for gochugaru flakes. Boy oh boy, was this pizza delicious. Time to make it again!
Made with Abbots chopped faux chicken and still turned out great! Also a drizzle of honey right when you pull the pizza out is so good!

This is absolutely delicious and well worth the prep. I shaved off some time by using pizza dough from a local bakery. Subbed honey for the rice/corn syrup. Used a little less gochugaru and black pepper than called for. We loved every detail!

One comment said the flavours were not balanced and too salty. I agree when it comes to the marinade, which I’ve adjusted each of the three times I’ve made it. I now add 2 tablespoons water to the marinade to thin it slightly and used agave syrup in place of corn or rice syrup. It worked much better this time.  

I make the pizza dough in a Kitchen Aid stand mixer and it’s so easy.
not the best, not the worst

stats.Total Time: 25 minutes, plus time for grill to heat

comments.commentBody:
Another way to "grill" (or at least brown/char) the corn is to coat the shucked ears very lightly with oil and turn them several times in a hot cast-iron skillet. Cool and scrape as directed. That's the way I do it; in a large enough skillet, I can easily do both ears (or more than two) at once.
And please, do not discard the cobs! Save them to make a slightly smoky corn stock.
I think you don't even need the oil, since the skillet is already lightly coated in oil from normal cast iron maintenance.

If it were me, I'd add some finely chopped fresh summer tomato! The combination of the grilled summer corn and summer tomatoes adds complexity.

And, please, always use serrano not jalapeño!

Agreed that mortar and pestle is key for extracting flavor (same applies to pesto). Only difference that I do with guac is to add the lime juice with the onions to sort of pickle them and mellow their flavor a bit. Not sure if there is a downside to that. Adding roasted corn and cotija is fantastic.
I think I'll be very lazy and use TJ's delicious frozen roasted corn.
Thanks for the suggestion. I was wondering how I would make this once corn season ends!

If you have left over guacamole, cover with tortilla chips pressed slightly into the surface and put a lid or wrap on the container.  When you go to reuse it, you'll have to throw those chips away - they get soggy - but it keeps the air off the guac so it doesn't turn brown.
Don't throw away the corn cobs! Toss in the freezer, and use for vegetable stock and soups.  You won't believe how much flavor they pack!

Guacamole is similar to gazpacho in that both go back many, many centuries and come in many forms, colors and mixtures.

Rather than debate whose version of guacamole is most "authentic," open your hearts and minds to trying something a little--or maybe even a lot--different.

As for choosing avocados, we buy Haas avocados only when they are hard as a rock, leave them in a bowl on the table for a day or two and eat them when they first show signs of slight give or softness.

re: keeping guac green - scrape leftover guac onto plastic wrap and then roll it up like you would a compound butter (to make a log, but a short one to minimize surface area) and twist the ends like a taffy wrapper, eliminating as much air as possible. the only way to keep it green is to keep air away from it. pits will not do anything. citrus does not do anything either. the amount of citrus needed to keep it from browning would make it inedible. (1/2)
highly recommend steaming corn first and then high temp char. you will retain more flavor and remove part of the starchiness.

Made this for Saturday night appetizers and it was a delight. I used my Japanese suribachi for the first time to make guacamole and I will always use it in the future. The result was much better than just mixing everything together and it seems to work as well as a molcojete. The grilled corn really sweetens the dish and I used Parmesan reggiano, which makes everything better. I also used Fresno chilies, my favorites, perfect balance of heat and sweetness

This sounds amazing and perfect for end-of summer.  I make something similar but as more of a salad, with the avocados chopped vs mashed, and sometimes I'll throw in some diced cucumber for additional crunch.

This dip was a huge hit at a summer party.  I charred the corn in an iron skillet as suggested and it looked beautiful.   I added some halved cherry tomatoes since we have a bumper crop and substituted fresh parley and oregano from the garden since I had forgotten to buy cilantro.
I will definitely be making this again and will remember the cilantro.

What's all the concern about keeping the leftovers green? Who has leftovers? This was worth all the work and thank you for the tips for those of us who have neither yards to grill in nor kitchen space to store a molcajete.

I love guacamole, and have a favorite recipe, involving charred tomatillos. I also love corn, and cheese, so when I saw this, my mouth watered. What I learned is that, while I also love pasta and raspberries, they don't go well together. I made this exactly as laid out, going so far as making a second trip to the market to get fresh corn, when the avocados didn't ripen soon enough. Overall, this is just a weird combination of ingredients. It's not terrible; it's just not good.

I made this for guests 8 months ago. Talking with them today they brought up the “best guacamole ever!” We all agreed.

True, tastes differ. But it’s always tempting to figure out what went wrong. The general key is to taste, adjust and taste again.

This recipe calls, in step 2, for a pesto to be made, suggesting 2-4 minutes to do it. That’s not enough time if using granite. Don’t cut corners: the onion, chile, cilantro and salt need to be nearly emulsified. And the Chile must be hot.

To make it last longer, and avoid the browning of avocado issue, we divide the onion-cilantro paste into two and mix one large/jumbo avocado with half the paste on night one and the second on night two

Toasted corn,garlic clove smashed, some crushed cumin & coriander seeds in the cast iron skillet as Suzanne did. (Do the corn first, as the seeds burn easily!) "Mike" suggested chopped tomato. That went in, too, after everything cooled down.
Perfect!
A good sprinkle of Fruit Fresh over the top will retard browning.  It is available in grocery stores that sell canning supplies.

Omg.  So so delicious.  The only changes we made were to grill the corn over charcoal heat al estilo Mexicano (meaning don't overthink the grilling - soak, partially shuck, remove the silks, re-cover with its own husk, and grill over medium heat for 10 or 15 minutes) and used roasted garlic instead of onion, as raw onion tends to upset the stomach.

I’ve found that if I squeeze a lime (or an orange) over the top of the leftover guac, like a citrus buffer between the avocado and the air, before sealing the bowl with plastic wrap, it will stay green overnight.

If you have a potato masher, use that to mash the avocado in a regular mixing bowl if you don't want to buy a mortar and pestle.  I've found it to do the same job with the smashing, but without having to buy an extra tool...

You can keep the guacamole from oxidizing by going heavy on the lime juice and if you line the surface with plastic wrap, avoiding air bubbles, you can refrigerate without worrying about it turning brown.

An equally tasty and quicker (and lower-fat) way to "grill" the corn is to give each ear 4-5 minutes in a microwave after first dampening the husk (if attached) under the tap, or wrapping it (if not) in dampened and squeezed kitchen paper. Then scrape off the kernels and throw them in a hot griddle pan (with no oil) and toss them about for around five minutes until lightly charred.

I agree! Microwaving the corn ears while still in the husks is so easy. 3 to 4 minutes, depending on the size. Then cut off about 1/2 inch off the bottom and squeeze the corn ear out while grasping the silken end with a kitchen towel. Pops right out with nary a thread of silk on it.

I really want to try this but I hate cilantro, tastes like soap to me and all 5 of my brothers. Worst gene we got, lol. Anyway, I have never seen a guacamole recipe that calls for it, so I'm going to make it without, use Hatch green Chile's, like the majority of my fellow New Mexicans, and hope for the best. I  usually don't put tomatoes in my guacamole but will this time as it will be nice with the corn. And yes, I know that I'm changing the recipe  but adding cilantro will ruin it for us.
If I can not find Cotija cheese, what is a good substitute?  I am near Toronto.
Feta cheese
Feta. Cotija is a dry salty crumbly cheese
greek feta, less salty
So my question is about scant and its definition. A scant 1 cup would be ⅞ cup, not ¼. This is very imprecise. Anyone else think it is inaccurate?
Recipe says scant 1 cup or 4 OUNCES. Probably 4 oz. is the weight (close) equivalent of 1 cup chopped onions by volume.
Mike, why do you prefer serrano to jalapeño? Is that all the time or just in a guacamole scenario? I am intrigued :)
If you don't have a grill, you can roast the corn under the broiler in your oven. Also, a bundt pan works nicely to hold the corn while cutting and holds the kernels.
Meh.  I did not find this to be better than my standard guacamole.  And a lot more work.
What’s a good substitute for cotija?

Delicious. One note about some of the notes. Adding tomato to guacamole is a risk because it can make it watery. I'd only add if I was eating it right away and not saving any for leftovers. Refrigeration would only make the wateriness worse.
I added some toasted pepitas since I didn't have any cojita; while not an exact substitute, it does make up for some of the flavor.
Used a few substitutions because we didn't have all the ingredients. Canned organic corn grilled in teaspoon of olive oil and Costco goat cheese (always in the frig instead of cojita. Delicious!

For the last 50+ years, people from San Antonio TX have made guacamole by mashing ripe avocado with Pace's Picante Sauce (Mild, Medium, or whatever) to taste. Squirt a bit of lemon in it if you like ... or not ... 

Fast and fabulous.
Delicious but will add garlic next time.

Nope. Avo, a pinch of salt, lime juice and...should you be feeling adventurous...maybe fresh garlic...is all that's required. Anything else adds too much moisture...and preciousness. And, please, just use a fork to squish it all into the serving bowl. Your guests will remain impressed and happy.
I use a salad bowl and a meat cleaver (or chopper) instead of mortar and pestle or Cuisinart. I like the slightly uneven texture produced.
Hi Kenji - sounds awesome.  The article mentioned serving with Tajin but it's not in the recipe.  Wanted you to know.
Anyone “roasting” corn over a gas burner with any success? I roast thinner eggplants and peppers that way…
yes, works fine
Save the avocado seeds and put them in the guacamole. This will delay the browning.
From AvoSeedo: "Putting golf balls in your guacamole would be just as effective as putting pits in there to keep it from turning brown" because it's keeping oxygen from the guac.
We have frozen avocado in our freezer. Has anyone have any thoughts on using frozen avocado in this recipe? Thank you.
I added some roasted poblano - delish!

Cut in half about a dozen cherry tomatoes and gently folded them in at the end.   Added 1/2 a diced red bell pepper.  Added a little tajin seasoning.  Spritzed with a little olive oil at the end to loosen it up a bit.
Halved the recipe, had to use lemon instead of lime. Can’t do dairy so used Traders Joe’s alternative parmesan shreds sprinkled on top. Delicious and so happy to use my granite mortar and pestle.
I've never heard of a coal grill! Not sure I would want to eat anything cooked over coal.
"Coal" is another way of saying "charcoal"
Maybe short for charcoal?
And there are chimneys you can buy to ignite the coals without using lighter fluid.
Wonder if you could sub frozen corn that was seared/charred in a skillet.
I personally prefer a chunky guacamole, so I'm looking forward to adding the grilled corn for another layer of texture.

This is a fantastic recipe!!

I've made it for company twice and everyone love it.

OK, I made one change (which is not needed, but sometimes a bit over-the-top is good). I added some diced crispy bacon slices that were leftover. 

The guests loved it, one in particular. She said, "This is the mortar of the gods!"

but if you ever find yourself using only using half an avocado, use the half without the pit - keep the pit in the other half and it will protect the otherwise hard to cover hole from air and you can press saran wrap over the rest. (2/2)
Good recipe, make sure to Salt n pepper.  Add Garlic as well. Maybe a teaspoon of Chile powder on corn mixture to add heat..
Use traditional tools! No metal!

Added some tajin and some chipotle powder and 1 clove garlic. The garlic rounded the flavors out a bit and the tajin sharpened them up. The chipotle added some heat since the jalapeno I had was a bit anemic, and complemented the smoky flavor of the corn
I've found pressing plastic wrap against the leftover guacamole extended the life of the guac without darkening. I could be wrong, but I think it's oxygen that causes the darkening.
This has been my go-to method for many years.  It works!
I added a clove of grated garlic & half a juicy diced tomato. Necessary!!
irresistible
chopped garlic is a must in my guac recipe.  I'll look forward to checking out the difference without.

This pairing of grilled corn and cotija cheese with guacamole is fantastic. Instead of the mortar and pestle/molcajete (we have neither) we just used our standard guac recipe and it was still brilliant. Made it last evening with some grilled vegetable tacos for a fun meal and passed around tortilla chips to scoop up the enhanced guacamole.
This has too much cilantro for my taste. Next time I’m gonna cut the cilantro in half.
Using the mortar and pestle left me with severe discomfort on my hand from the compounds released from the jalapeños. 4 hours later my hand is still on fire.
Why serrano and not jalapeno?
Serranos are hotter. They tend to be more reliably hot, whereas jalapeños are frequently lacking in heat.

Wonderful recipe. Members of my family have problems with pepper and onion so I need to tame them a bit. I grated the onion and inner portion of the Serrano pepper. Threw away the skin. Pickled in the lime juice. Used mortar for the cilantro. Smashed the avocado and added the pickled onion Serrano slurry to taste along with the cilantro. Then cheese and corn as written. Everyone ate seconds and the avocado did not turn ugly at all. We ate it for several days.

I don’t have a large mortar and pestle so I used the alternate method and it was a game changer. So much more flavorful than just chopping the onions and cilantro. I’ll be adding a molcajete to my kitchen for sure.

I liked the addition of the grilled corn for a change of pace but wasn’t that crazy about the cotija. I’ll omit that next time.
Added some cumin, not quac w/o it.  Corn is great add!
This is a winner, but, it was 100 degrees today and I was not going outside to grill. I shucked two ears of beautiful yellow corn and charred them directly on my gas cooktop. Delicous!
If I keep a pit to the avocado and place it in the guacamole it keeps it from turning brown.

We loved it, the fresh corn adds a nice dimension. Had leftover tomatillo salsa that I made, the combination is wonderful. The cotija was pretty salty, so taste before you add the salt. Will make again when corn season rolls around.

For the corn I was able to roast it over the stove burner, with a strong exhaust fan—like you would for peppers. Also could not find Cotija but Feta was recommended as a sub so I used that and it was great.
Don't pre-cook the corn!!! The grilling is enough to draw out the sugars and keep it crunchy in the dip. Toss in whatever diced veg you love or some black means. Leftovers??? LOL!!

I'm allergic to limes so always use fresh lemon juice in my guac.  It adds a slightly different zip, I suppose but it's delicious.  Also, I find that a finely chopped up tomato is perfect for a great guacamole.  I'm wondering if I could sub the corn for the tomato.  Hmmmm.  I may try this - or maybe both at once!

If you want to prevent your finished guacamole turning brown, press it firmly into a clear glass storage dish, making sure to get all the air bubbles out, then cover with plastic wrap, pressing the plastic wrap onto the guacamole, not letting any air bubbles remain under the wrap. Put a lid on the whole dish and pop into the fridge.
It's hatch chili time out west. I roasted one and put it in the guacamole together with one cob of grilled corn. Used lemon instead of lime. All good.
This was good, but it needed a little spicing up. I seeded the serrano pepper. I think the next time I make it (and I will) I'll leave the seeds in.

Delicious! And substantial. I used half a bag of the frozen roasted corn from Trader Joe’s because corn is out of season right now and it turned out so well. I also added a few dashes of green Cholula hot sauce, a bit of salt, and anotber squeeze of lime at the end to season, all of which amped up the flavor. Will make again!

Absolutely delicious! I introduced garlic into the mortar-and-pestle blend and added diced tomatoes alongside my cast-iron grilled corn. Impeccably done. Three serrano peppers to this quantity gives a rather satisfying level of heat that gets tamped down nicely by the rest of the guac.
Leave the lime out. It helps prevent the avocado from oxidizing if you don't plan to serve it right way, but it masks the flavor of the avacado.
Forgive me if someone already mentioned this: grill the avocados. Simply halve them and grill them for about 5 minutes on each side. This opens up a whole new guacamole flavor layer. ✌🏼

My ex’s grandfather made these at his diner in Oklahoma so they were always known in the family as grandpa burgers. No cheese, onions diced instead of sliced, served with yellow mustard. Very good. Frying the onions separately is definitely NOT the same thing and not better. But it was a very bitter divorce so I might need therapy if I made these.

Wowowow!! What a delicious burger! We followed the recipe exactly and took the suggestion to put a cast iron on the grill to prevent onion-house.  I made a couple with the Impossible meat too and my vegetarian husband was a big fan as well!  Was worth prepping everything in advance before bringing it out to the grill since everything was quick.  Highly highly recommend this recipe.  Bravo Kenji!

one of the best items i have purchased is a portable induction cooktop. when i want to make fried chicken, which just makes a spectacular mess, i pull out this piece of magic, set it up outside on a table and get to work. same would go for these, which i am going to make this weekend. outside.
Trader Joe's brioche buns are my go-to for hamburgers. Soft and sweet but much sturdier than other brioche buns I've tried.

Does anyone remember the old "White Castle" chain in NYC. The burgers in this article are very similar to the 25 cent burger that you purchased at White Castle by the sackful. And, when the NY Daily News ran a coupon, it was "Katie bar the door". 

The missing element in any good "cheap" burger is the amount of old grease on the grill and in the walls of the establishment. This gives the finishing touch to the unforgettable flavor. Alas, Mr.Lopez-Alt. you can never get this at home.

@Tucsonbabe
There use to be a White House burger at college park, university of Maryland.  Always open late and the motto was buy them by the bag.  Just a tiny place but well attended. I thought of that as I read the recipe.  Brings me back…
@dorothy. Also—Little Tavern in Baltimore—Same concept.  Daddy would take me on his days off.  Krystals Burgers in the South are fairly close.
I dislike the sweetness of Kings Hawaiian rolls - does anyone have a go-to that isn’t a sugarbomb?
Agreed. Kings Hawaiian makes a less-sweet roll: Kings Hawaiian Savory
Martin's Potato Buns for sliders or regular size.
Martin's Potato Rolls seem to be popular among burger cognoscenti, as are Pepperidge Farm Soft White Hamburger Buns. Both have slightly less sugar per bun than the Kings Hawaiian rolls.

Interestingly, the article mentions Telway Hamburgers for an inspirational take on the preparation.  The original owner of Telway's was an Oklahoma veteran! I'm a OU Sooner grad, now residing in Michigan. I've been to both onion burger counters and I can attest - delicious! My tip: use a cast-iron skillet, heat it slowly until it's fire hot. Have everyone and everything ready, at the table and be ready to sit down when you put the meat on. Prep takes a while, but the cooking goes quickly!

These are very tasty--I will definitely make again. My family used to like huge, fat, juicy burgers, but we're not the carnivores we once were. I love onions, but raw onions are too harsh, these cooked onions are mellow. I followed the recipe with no condiments and the only thing I would recommend is a little additional salt and pepper at the end. Thank you!
I was craving a nasty little burger on my hike, so picked up ingredients for these on the drive home. Excellent! To aid mallard reaction I usually quack ten times over each patty before flipping.
I found it very challenging to keep the onions on the burgers when I flipped them.
Love to see this. My parents used to take us on a really inconvenient route home from OU football games to pick up onion burgers in El Reno. So good, and totally worth the extra time in the car!

Wow! 5 stars! Kenji is a true chef, and impressive food scientist. I love his book “The Food Lab.”

These hamburgers are like White Castle on steroids!  Will cook again soon.  So good!
I’m a vegetarian. I made this recipe using 1 package of Impossible ground. Was perfect!
My mom, who lived through the depression as a child, made her hamburgers with onions, bread crumbs and an egg added into the raw meat that she hand turned. They were alway juicy and delicious.

This turned out great. We added some minced garlic to the hamburger meat while forming into a ball. We also cooked mushrooms on the blackstone at the same time and added those and Swiss cheese to the burger. Delicious.

John Steinbeck’s still-amazing and still-all-too-relevant Dustbowl exodus novel The Grapes of Wrath features a stop at a highway diner where the short-order cook is making these almost exactly as written. I wonder if Steinbeck was evoking the El Reno joint or if the practice was more widespread, even back in the Thirties?
Use Martin's potato rolls instead of the sweet Hawaiian rolls.

These were stupid delicious! We used Beyond Meat instead of ground beef, added salt, lots of pepper, red chili flakes, and a clove of smashed garlic to the “meat” mixture. Everything else done as is. Under no circumstances should you fry the onions separately! It would absolutely ruin the magic of this recipe. If you think you put on too many onions, you didn’t so add another handful! The onions are charred, steamed, caramelized, sweet, and bitter all at the same time. Will make again and again!

We’ve made these as directed and they are amazing. I saw in the comments that someone made them with ground turkey. We like to keep our red meat consumption down but turkey burgers always felt flat and needed lots of condiments. Tried making them with this method and it is the only way to go with turkey. I used ground thigh and a little oil in the frying pan. Also went with dijonaise, as it is still turkey. It won’t replace the original recipe but it gives us another version, just more to love.
Made per instructions with Impossibleburger. Delicious!
Honestly smashing the meat only eliminates deicious juices.  You're way better off frying the onions them separately and then adding to the meat mix and then frying the burgers.  You're welcome.
Seems to me this misses the whole point of the exercise; to allow the two ingredients to interact.
The folks in El Reno have been using the method described in the recipe for 80 years. I bet they know best. You're welcome!
you'll miss out on the triple effect of onions cooked three ways (caramelized/seared/steamed). The smashing allows for more charring and the huge quantity of onions adds moisture to the burger
I would use Martin's Potato Rolls instead of the overly sweet King rolls. It's a savory meal.
Kenji has a YouTube show. This OK onion burger has an episode. Well worth watching.

The “hay stack” of onions bring welcome moisture, even if using higher fat ground beef. I used mandolin-ed shallots instead of white onions, and plainly dressed the bun with grainy mustard and arugula mix for dinner tonight (July, 2022). Don’t be afraid to really let the onions caramelize and crisp up (just an extra minute or two). Great burger method.
If the burgers are anywhere near as glorious as the lovingly explained instructions, they will be to die for.  I'm in!

I lived in Oklahoma for the first 65 years of my life and have eaten at both burger joints in El Reno. I now live in Colorado (the place I've been trying to get to all my life) and tonight I tried this burger recipe and cooked the burgers as directed in a large fry pan on my induction cooktop. The burgers were the BEST burgers I've ever made and were at least a close second to the burgers in El Reno if not equal. I think we'll have burgers again tomorrow night... Thanks for sharing this recipe!

I fiercely dislike everything Kings Hawaiian and very much like Martin's Potato products. King's is WAY too sweet for anybody other than sugar addicts. On good days I simply bake my own, but not in this weather.

I'm not a big red meat eater, once a week tops, but this was exactly what I've been wanting. I was scared at first,  no oil in the pan? Thinly sliced onions as the only condiment? All that grease frying and making the kitchen hotter than it should be? Don't be scared, just do it, exactly as written. Don't mess with perfection, and yes, I used the Hawaiian buns, sweetness be damned. And if the New York food cart workers can take the heat, well, so can I. Should have added more onions!
What a pleasure it is to read the notes! Everyone is always adding some useful tip. Let me add the Martins Potato rolls are great.
I do this with beyond beef or impossible burgers.
I made these all last summer.  That's all I have to say.  Were they good?  Must have been.

He presses down a hissing hamburger with his spatula. He lays the split buns on the plate to toast and heat. He gathers up stray onions from the plate and heaps them on the meat and presses them in with the spatula. He puts half the bun on top of the meat, paints the other half with melted butter, with thin pickle relish. Holding the bun on the meat, he slips the spatula under the thin pad of meat, flips it over, lays the buttered half on top, and drops the hamburger on a small plate.

This Okie is thrilled to find this recipe! I’ve tried & tried to recreate a good ol’ greasy-spoon onion burger to no avail. Success!! Used a regular hamburger bun since no OK diner would dream of using a Hawaiian bun.
I made these today. My mom said she was speechless. Very happy with these 😊

I have alpha-gal and can't eat beef. I substitute ground turkey a lot for hamburger, but turkey burgers tend to be dry and blah. Somehow, I knew this recipe would be just right, and it was. Delicious with turkey, and if I grind my own turkey thighs, probably even better. The burgers were juicy, probably because of the onion. I had to persuade my griller husband (strongly resistant to following instructions) to follow the method as described, and he was glad he did. Thanks for this one!

White Castle is 100 years old. Their promo video said they rehydrate their onions before using ...cheaper and more concentrated flavor. Kudos to Kenji for pitching the greatness of the onion-ground meat combo. Whether at El Reno, in a 100 other joints, at White Castle, or in kababs, meatballs and similar grilled foods all over the world...is a classic combo.... https://youtu.be/aVkOzTOTLGo
A piece of foil gently pressed at the rim/edge of the skillet is a safer substitute for the dishtowel. When ready to clean pan, scrunch the same foil piece for scrubbing.
this is the cheeseburger ralph fiennes serves at the end of the menu! just a well made cheeseburger.

I’ve found it also helps to get as much water out of the onions first, then they stay put in the meat, and don’t add liquid to the meat. I dice them and then wrap them in cheesecloth and squeeze them out , then let them continue to drain while wrapped in the cloth by setting aside in a strainer over a bowl, maybe a couple hours.
I don’t think less onion water inhibits searing. You need a really hot iron skillet. Put onion side down first then they get super crunchy and brown right away.
These were delicious but the steamed buns were a little soggy so second time I grilled the buns so they were crisp! Otherwise fantastic recipe
I agree. I don"t steam and it till comes out Fantastic. Making tonight:))

The pan needs to be insanely hot for this to work as intended. The char and sear of all ingredients is the goal; I let my skillet heat on the gas grill for 20+ minutes on high with the cover closed before launching the patties. The cook is fast, so stay sharp. An excellent prep!
Try Euro brioche buns, available at WF.
My Detroit-born husband has been making these White Castle style since we married 40 years ago. If the patty isn't cooked on a bed of thinly sliced onion, it's not a true hamburger by his reckoning.

These directions sound so complicated!  To, simplify - you make 4 patties from 1 lb. of beef, pile a bunch of thinly sliced onion on top of each, cook them with the onions on top while smashing, then flip patty and onions over and finish cooking.  Right?

Everything should be as simple as it can be, but not simpler. 

When to salt, how to scrape to get all the Maillard reaction into the final product, bathing the buns in onion steam--these are details important to the successful execution of the recipe. Don't over-simplify.
A bun that is not a sugar-bomb? I suggest you bake your own at home. My favorite store brand is Oroweat Potato Buns. You can't go wrong with them.
Great recipe. Family loved it. Next time I think I might do the onions White Castle size. I will also use a wider steel grill instead of cast iron, so I have more space and heat. Thanks!
Check out the linked article for helpful pictures

White Manna (https://www.whitemanna.com/) in Hackensack, NJ, has been making these for decades. Sitting at the small counter and watching the cook handle a flattop filled with these gems - nothing written down, never missing a beat or miscalculating an order - is as much fun as the eating. I haven’t lived in NJ for years, so I’m looking forward to recreating the experience at home. Thanks, NYT.
Very tasty!  I used red onion for my little haystacks and followed directions to the T.  I did have to put grainy mustard and sliced cornichon's on the bottom bun.  Thumbs up all around.

Many of us now have infrared thermometers, for example, as a result of the Covid pandemic. 
What is the IR temperature when a drop of water is sizzling about the pan in Step 3 ? Maybe 350 F or ?  I'll give it a try and let you know.
Tried this recipe as is and absolutely loved it.
Did these on my pizza steel on the grill. Gonna be hard to ever make burgers another way again.  Just do them described in the recipe. Wow they were good.

This recipe delivered big time -- huge flavors and a startlingly bad mess in my cast iron skillet.  So many burnt on onions.  My wife felt a little over-onioned and asked me to cut back on hers next time.  Pro tip - I used Wonder buns!  Perfect softness and absorbed the onion steam like a sponge.
This is the only way I’ll be cooking burgers for the rest of my life. Might recommend two slices of cheese instead of one for extra gluttony. Also don’t shy away from the salt.

Made this for dinner, and it was a hit! Really, what's not to like? It did splatter grease everywhere, so lots of clean-up. The house has a heavy smell of fried onions. And I managed to set off the smoke alarm. But these burgers were delicious, so if I can figure out how to avoid the grease splatter and setting off the smoke alarm, I'm willing to live with the heavy onion smell for these.
Use a splatter guard - basically a round screen that sits on top of the pan.  Amazon carries it in several sizes.
I made these inside and they were indeed delicious. However my house smelled like a diner for 24 hours. Will cool this outside next time. So juicy. Def use 70/30 ground beef.

Nothing better than making these on a Blackstone griddle!  Used a little less beef in each patty (3oz) and they were amazing...nice crust on the patties, onions cooked perfectly, cheese melted and oozing....wouldn't change a thing.  Don't be afraid to really pile the onions on the balls of raw meat.
Had these tonight. Delicious. The onion didn't get all that crispy, but the burgers were delicious anyway. A keeper.
Try mixing dry onion soup mix into your burger meat along with a tablespoon of worshtershire  sauce. About 1 envelope per 2 lbs. of  meat. Onion bliss!

I have not tried this version yet, but they remind me of my Mom's "special hamburgers"...small patties cooked with onion and garlic, nestled on ordinary buns and basted with butter. She would then wrap each in foil, and bake in the oven. The buns turned our crispy on the outside and almost custard like on the inside. We ate them with ketchup, pickles, and oven fries. Not something we ate very often, that's why my memories are so special!

Fantini Bakery French rolls. Not real big, squarish. But, not sweet at all, not fluffy. Sturdy texture, neutral flavor. I buy in supermarket. Maybe a New England thing. I detest soft, sweet, mushy rolls. Just NOY right.
Crazy good. Naysayers re: American cheese, please just try it once before you dis it. It lends a creamy mouth feel that is unparalleled.

Burgers are plenty good. Used 73% lean meat, sliced yellow onion, thick slice of Boars Head white American cheese (which is really cheese, according to the label), & Pepperidge Farm Soft White hamburger bun. 

The steps in the recipe seem overly complex. I didn't use sheet pan or parchment. I made 4 oz patties, 3 inches across. Piled sliced onions & cooked in cast iron, medium high heat, flipped & steamed cheese & buns under cover. Reminds me of patty melts. White Castles aren't flipped.

An entirely different take on the burgers I grew up with and have made/eaten my entire life. Followed the recipe and used Martin's potato rolls garnished with dill pickle slices and yellow mustard.
Steaming the buns is key.

My daughter loves “flat top” burgers so gave this a tumble. However, only used 1/4 onion per burger which was a mistake since the onions stole the show. Definitely use full amount of onion! Also highly suggest putting griddle on grill outside since the onion does permeate. Also used a 1:1 beef pork mix (it’s what I had) and made 1/4 lb burgers. Worked great as fat content in meat is important. Will be adding to my burger menu in future.
Supah, I like Dave's Killer Buns...larger than rolls (that's a plus), but honest bread with nutritional value. We love these burgers, recipe just as written.
Boomer Sooner!  These are yummy, and I will make again. Unfortunately, I did not have the originals while living in OK.

Absolutely delicious!  My adult daughter isn't a fan of homemade burgers but liked this one so much she requested it again for the second time this week!  The only thing I did differently is I  used a mix of 3/4 chuck and 1/4 sirloin and I seasoned the burgers with Monterey Steak Seasoning only on one side instead of just salt and pepper on both.  Thanks for a great recipe!
This is also what they do at White Mana in Hackensack, NJ

Cooked in cast iron last night - very meaty flavor - very good overall. The single element which bothered me about these burgers - that makes me want to remedy it for future enjoyment - is that this burger needs more than pickles for acid to balance against the very flabby, juicy taste here. I attempted to better the balance w/yellow mustard & ketchup, but not right. - Next time, maybe a more acidic pickle - or a more tannic Bordeaux!

Made these exactly as written.  Perfection - no condiments needed.  My husband and I agreed best burger we’ve made and near the top of those we’ve eaten. Found it easier to smash the onions into the burgers with a potato masher.

You know, many of us have IR temperature sensors. It would be helpful to cite a specific temperature for the cast iron pan or griddle.  Same for  the outdoor grill using charcoal, propane, pellets or other.

Growing up, Johnnie's Grill in El Reno, OK was the go-to burger joint for fried onion burgers. Always fascinating to watch being cooked. Always, 3 to eat & 2 to go (one for each jacket pocket) for later.

Been making onion burgers lately & they are our new favorite right up there with Kay Chun's Korean Cheeseburgers. The recipe we use has you thinly slice the onions in advance, salt them (about 1 teaspoon sea salt) & let them sit in a strainer over a bowl for about 15 minutes.  Just before cooking, squeeze them with your hands to get as much liquid out as possible.  Makes the onions more pliable & less watery when cooking. Last time I mixed chili crisp with mayonnaise to spread on the buns.
If you don’t eat meat, try roasting some portobello mushroom caps in a mixture of butter, soy sauce and a drizzle of maple syrup, and use those in place of the beef.

I did not like these.  They came out too thin, and as others have said, give your house an extreme case of the onions.  The onion flavor manifests as one-dimensional and crude, overpowering everything else.

I have been smashing my Onion burgers with my tortilla press. I got some pre-cut parchment paper that's the right size for the press.

For Onion burgers the grease is the condiment.

Ronnie Spector made a version of this called Mother Daughter burgers, after her mom. You very finely mince the onions and add to the burgers along with salt and pepper. I like to use a flat plastic 6-inch round lid from one of my containers to smash the burgers very well on a waxed paper covered plate prior to cooking, Then, refrigerate the patties for 30 minutes or so before cooking, if time allows. If not proceed to the grill. Use any bun you like. Simply delicious.

These were soooo good! Made my own potato rolls (easier than sourcing them in Melbourne, tbh) and had them with pickles and mustard. Excellent. Kenji’s recipes just work for me, and I love that he gives us the “why”, not just the “how”.
1 minute is plenty. Cover with cheese on patties after turning. Cut heat after one minute. Keep covered for 5 minutes.
I've made onion burgers before but Kenji's video made all the difference. His tips for doing this on a gas grill were spot-on - these turned out great. I'll be making these this way from now on.
Recipe is easy to follow but the burgers tasted better cold the next day than they did fresh. Maybe it was the pickles I used (sandwich) but it just lacked...something.
Make sure your towel is washed in unscented laundry detergent.
How do you avoid having some burnt and bitter onions or is that part of it? Recently did smash burgers with onions and they burnt.
see my suggestion above

George Motz (please read the article associated with the recipe above) has a YouTube video describing the method of making the OK Onion Burger. At 11:32 the technique for tucking the onions under the patty is described: https://youtu.be/t0AH7h9zZ_4?list=PLAzrgbu8gEMIkFTtpY-Vn0Kjj_LQ0pE0M&t=692 Also K.A-L. describes the science of how the onion infuses the patty with, 3-Mercapto-2-methylpentan-1-ol, or as I tell my friends, magic.
Thank you Jon.Turned out great.
Martin's Potato Rolls might work.
I like Arnolds Sesame Seeded hamburger buns. They use healthy Soybean oil and I also like the  seeds.
Anybody try ground turkey thigh?
Non slider White Castle burgers.  (That is not an insult)
I haven’t eaten beef in years but this method sounds like it might work with ground turkey. Has anyone tried making onion turkey burgers?

It should!  The recipe calls for a high fat content in the ground meat ~20-30%. You may want to amend your recipe accordingly with some additional fat so the onions do not quickly burn up and the onion flavors can coat and infuse the burger.  (see/read the associated article about how the OK onion burger works.)  

Good Luck!
Can you grate the onions
The article goes into detail about how onion cells release more of their savory, umami (meat-like) compounds when sliced into rings parallel to the onion's equator.

Using a cast iron pan (or plancha) is essential to getting the brilliant interplay of crispy-crunchy exterior and moist, beefy voluptuousness!! Served with some chilled Bojo, these Okie Burgers in Paris are taming the sting of the current heat wave!
Anyone have thoughts on whether the cast iron is necessary if using the grill? What happens if you don’t use it? Do the onions on top fall through/burn?

Like a lot of Kenji's recipes, this one worked just perfectly. I used my smaller dutch oven pan to place over two burgers on my griddle pan (one like Kenji has) and that worked well - you need a high dome pan for that. These tasted really good! Be sure to use LOTS of onions, and also soft roll bun.

The onion burger was very good, but a bit too smoky for regular preparation in my kitchen.  The stove's exhaust fan was on high before cooking started, but we still had to open windows to prevent the smoke alarms from buzzing.  

The "char" was definitely a nice part of the flavor profile, but next time I'll turn down the heat a bit.  We "steamed" the bun by microwaving a frozen hamburger bun, and that was fine for the first time.

A bit complicated for a burger. I prefer to caramelize the onions. Cook one side of the burger 2 to 3 minutes flip and  top with onions add cheese cover and cook 2 minutes. Serve on toasted brioche bun top with dill pickle and tomato. Add mayo mustard or ketchup on the lower bun. Use grass fed beef. Simple and delicious.

I don’t make burgers often, but reading this recipe made me go for it. They were tasty but I was confused about the cooking time. First ones were overcooked, the next batch nearly raw in the center. The recipe says 1-2 minutes and then 1-2 minutes again “til done” but that’s not counting all the fiddling and smashing (I figured). 
How do you know when done? Can someone please detail the timing for me? Thanks!
This is how they make them in White Manna in New Jersey!
Tried this last night, and the taste was terrific!  Used Swiss cheese and brioche buns (lightly toasted on the cut sides; not steamed as called for in the recipe).
Really good.    Takes 4 min have e everything ready, including a soft bun
I followed recipe, with cast iron pan smoking hot on charcoal grill, but the onions and meat threw off too much liquid to achieve smash burger crispness. Suggestions?
Drain your meat once it's done, wipe the excess fat from the pan, then smash

I combined this recipe with Kay Chan’s Mushroom Hamburger (to which I add an egg to help it bind) and the result was awesome. The biggest challenge was flipping the burger with the onions on top. 

Otherwise, we’ll be making this again and again!

This is really good, but:  something gave us the impression that the griddle had to be BLAZING HOT through the whole process, which led to an epic amount of smoke, emergency fans set in rarely-opened windows, questions like, “why are our fire alarms quiet?” and a burned cheese halo around angry overcooked pattys.  Either make this outside on the grill or temper the heat just a little after pre-heating the pan.

I did this on the grill using a flat griddle. Because this happens fast I found the need to tweak based on the equipment in order to get a really crisp oniony Edge on the burgers. I think the cook wants to try this again and again to get it right because it's worth it. I used a better quality cheddar cheese to substitute for the Kraft American Cheese and the results were lauded by the guests on my deck. Still I want to try again and get the burgers a little more crispy.
You, Sir, are a barbarian (not really).  How could you substitute cheese for American Processed Cheese Food Product?!

My only problem with most cheddar is that it doesn't melt - it merely separates.  American cheese doesn't do that nor does Gruyere.  The only way I've found to keep the cheddar from separating is to shred it in advance and pile it on so it's more likely to melt.

Put more onions than you think you need, they brown nicely and blend right in with the burger. I used spicy habanero cheese, added garlic, and a GF bun . Condiments of ketchup, Mayo, pickles. Fabulous. To make it on a grill in this triple digit heat wave, I just used my trusty cast iron pan. July 2022
The best burgers I've ever had!

I cooked these longer on each side (4 minutes) because DH will only eat a well-done burger, but they were still delicious. He said they were the best homemade burgers he's ever had. Will slice the onions differently next time so I can have the "haystack" that's described - my rings stayed intact instead. But no matter, these were worth the clean up and I hope to make them again.

Buns are crucial. I went with Kings Hawaiian; Martin’s would have done well. But don’t mess with the buns. For those who prefer medium well, the onion keeps the meat moist and crumbly. I think a 12” pan is too small for four patties. I continued to “smash” a little while it was on the pan. I put the pan on the grill, and closed the grill for a little while on both sides, which created a welcome steaming effect. This is how we will cook burgers from now on. You get your protein, vegetable, starch

This recipe is GREAT! Quick way to make a burger-- I do have a high powered exhaust fan. Be sure to leave it long enough on the second side. Even made one without the onions for my pickier kid. Will make again!
We've made it outdoors with our Lodge cast iron griddle on the charcoal grill and indoors on the stove top. A mandoline is essential. Always perfect!
Wouldn't step 6 insure that the burger is overcooked?

made these inside & who in the world would not want the residual lovely aroma of fried onions lingering as a nosey reminder of that magic...yes.... & my In n Out cheeseburger masterpiece get both whole grilled onions & raw for a  fabulous interplay of sweet & savory
These are easy and excellent with few ingredients!  My family loves them!

Given all the contention about Hawaiian rolls vs Martins Potato rolls, I've opted for Thomas' Sandwich Size English Muffins which have exactly the right ration of bread to meat, don't get soggy from the meat juices, are wide enough to accommodate a wider burger, and get a much nicer toast on them.  They work as well for these smash burgers as well as my 6 oz burgers - I found that 8oz burgers are more than folks seem to want to get their mouth around.

I adore alliums, especially shallots, but an onion burger sounds just great. Looked at the ingredient list & was dismayed, truly disheartened to see “American cheese.” 
I’ll have to do some major tweaking here. “American cheese” is a grotesque, plasticky, phosphorescent nonfood item that does not deserve mention in an august paper such as The New York Times.
@supah I used potato buns and will again in the future. Soft like the Kings option minus the sweetness.
Martin’s rolls are excellent but if you lean left you will not buy them.
Cooking time needs to be more precise. Medium and Well-done times... Otherwise perfect.
I made two, just for myself. Took the second one for lunch the next day--wonderful! My wife's homemade buns, using the cast-iron on the grill. MMMM

I followed the recipe, and these were a real crowd pleaser.

The key was to use a well seasoned, hot griddle, which produced a wonderful, crispy fond.

These were amazing!  I made them exactly as the recipe on the grill with the cast iron skillet.  They came out perfectly medium-well inside and charred on the outside.  I used brioche buns from our local bakery and they were perfect. 5 stars all around!
Followed the recipe and they came out great. Used a mandoline for extra thin onions. Even the kids who don’t like onions and cheese gave the burgers a thumbs up! My only regret is I didn’t make more.
Made it on the grill just as written. Very easy, very fast, and very delicious! It's given me another recipe jewel in my burger recipe treasure chest!

To those American cheese naysayers, you all do know that you can get proper American cheese in most delis. Boarshead makes a white and a yellow American cheese.  I’m sure there are others.  No, American cheese isn’t the pinnacle of cheeses but it’s a great melting cheese that adds creaminess to a dish. You don’t need to resort to Kraft singles.
Made these last night with Impossible fake meat and they were great. Even the carnivores loved them.

All the goodness of a smash burger but without the problem of the meat sticking to grill after the flip. We used 75/25 ground beef from Costco, brioche buns, sweet onions, and sliced up some Velveeta cubes since I forgot to get American cheese. Cooked them on a cast iron griddle on the gas grill. Easy, fast, and delicious!

I had my doubts about this recipe but I followed it closely.  The results were incredible. This is now my go-to burger recipe.  I did not have a domed lid so I used a round cake pan for steaming. I also used pre-formed burgers piling on the onions and then flipping the burger on top of the onions cooking 2 minutes per side. Steaming the roll makes a huge difference in the quality of the final product. Next time I will add the pickles and maybe a touch of mayo.

Tasty, but next time I want an onion burger I will just do the onion/bun steaming thing separately because it just turned into a mess when I cooked it as written. I used Impossible burgers and cooked inside on a big Staub braiser with glass lid. To anyone else thinking of cooking with plant-based, I'd highly suggest you ignore the "no oil" directive and add some when you cook the patties, mine burnt and stuck to the pan.
These are delicious! Will definitely make outdoors next time
Super easy and delicious, I used a non-stick electric skillet on hi and was able to crisp the edges and bottoms of the burgers as well as brown the onion when they were flipped over,'

I’ve made a lot of new friends this summer making these on my Blackstone Griddle! So simple but so good! My go to now! Many people have said it’s the best burger they’ve ever had. Anyone giving this less than 5 stars did something wrong.

WOW, these are great! We used big fresh onions from our garden and served them on toasted slices of a sturdy country miche bread.  The taste was similar to a a patty melt. Yum, yum. Can't wait to make these again!

There doesn't appear to be any reason for the parchment and baking sheet in Step 1.  Otherwise, this was an easy and delicious upgrade to burger night.  It seemed to work fine with bison meat and some red onion instead of all yellow/white.

I’ve been making Kenji’s Diner Burgers from his Food Lab cookbook for about two years now but couldn’t get a great result because I was trying to do my own adaptations for the grill (house gets too stinky and smoky if done inside). I saw this recipe and his grill adaptions and tried it out. They were fantastic. My family absolutely raved about them. Even the picky tween who doesn’t always like onions liked this. This will be my go to burger recipe.

Delicious recipe. Very easy (hardest part is slicing all the onions). And I agree that they don’t need condiments; they’re so flavorful and decadent with the caramelized onions. I wouldn’t change a thing!

I always make burgers in my cast iron skillet but never thought of doing the onions this way. The burger, the onions, the cheese ,the buns-WOW! I will never or almost never make a burger any other way!

I started making buns from scratch - which elevated the simple burger into the stratosphere. I use King Arthur Flour’s Beautiful Buns or Hamburger Potato Buns recipes, which can be started at 2 and done by dinner with very little hands on time. If I have little longer time and want more pins he, the subtle but magical Hawaiian bun with real pineapple juice is the bomb.
So good!  Thought it would be extra time, but not all. Delicious burgers that the whole family loved.
Being an Okie who regularly goes to El Reno for an onion burger I have to say your recipe looks spot on.  You must have gone to El Reno to watch Marty make them at Sid's.  Good Job!

This recipe is creates a burger much like the one you can get at White Castle. When I was growing up in Indianapolis in the 60s WC was the go to place for a cheap but delicious oniony burger. 
12 cents at the time!
Nice work here!

I learned to cook these at J&J's Diner in Port Huron, Michigan 40 years ago.  (I was a carhop as well as a cook - tells you that it was the real deal!)  

It's a very simple recipe but you do need to follow the instructions exactly, which is why they're so detailed.  But it goes quickly and after the first time you try it, you could do it in your sleep.

Just made these.  Wow!  

Tucsonbabe's (and my) NYC/LI White Castle memory. The steamed bun, flat patty square, oozy cheese, and those marvelous onions....  Only 4 must-haveingredients including fatty 20-30% ground beef and American cheese slices. 

But it's less about the 4 ingredients than it is about the process! So do *exactly* what the recipe tells you to do.

Seriously the best burger I have ever made! It’s a hit every time we make it,  we make homemade Big Mac sauce which is a crowd pleaser.  Also Kobe beef puts it over the top.  Key is slicing the onions thin and we use a large sweet onion.
What is your recipe for the Mac sauce?
I got regular burgers on top of a separate pile of charred onions. No magic.
Delicious, straightforward, quick as advertised. Way more approval than a burger usually gets.

I made this with Impossible Burger "meat" and enjoyed it on the best of buns, Dave's Killer Bread 21 Whole Grains, and a side of roasted potato wedges and ketchup. Absolutely delicious, guilt-free comfort food.
Killer burgers!  Left off the cheese,  we aren't cheese eaters.   I fried peppers and onions just right. What a party our mouths and tummies had!

I’m not too old but in the early 60’s Steak & Shake made what today is a SmashBurger … really crispy edges, but they didn’t go so far as the OK folks with the Onions which would be a big hit with me! I have no idea when they started making their burgers, but it’s rare to see one of them open anymore.

At this time of year, virtually everything I make gets garden tomatoes added on, and this burger was no exception. This was the jiuciest and most delicious burger I've ever made. Will use a sturdier, toasted bun next time as this was too soft & mushy with the added tomato slice.
Excellent. Did with veg burger.

I made these in a small slider size for my book club's discussion of The Grapes of Wrath, which has a lengthy description of Al, a diner cook, making these burgers. (Quoted below by Vin C Sayville NY). Excellent.
Did them on the BBQ grill with a stone and they picked up that great smokey flavor - it is now our go to for our outdoor burgers.
The best. Dream about these.

Wow..these are delicious!! Honestly know that I have had burgers with onion placed into the burger there is no going back. It makes the burgers so flavorful and moist. I definitely recommend taking the extra step of putting them on the grill as the char-grill flavor adds so much flavor. Just be careful when you flip on the onion side as there was flare-up. Really really good. Highly recommend.
Todd-O-Phonic Todd turned me onto to these, pow pow pow!
This recipe has become a go to! We add a homemade “Big Mac Sauce” to the bun and serve with pickles and a tomato. It’s so good and so simple!

Unbelievably delicious! Followed the recipe exactly, cooked in cast iron on the grill outside.
Added mustard and pickles.  Made twice: once with a potato bun and american cheese from the deli counter; second time with a brioche bun and regular packaged american cheese.  Second iteration was better.

I thought I followed the recipe but ended up with raw meat in the center and barely soft onions. I really don't understand how 4 oz of beef and onions can get cooked in 3-4 minutes. To carmeluze onions properly, it takes at least 25 minutes at low heat, per Julia Child.
@Suzyque Either your pan isnt hot enough or you didnt smash the burgers enough. 3-4 minutes is more than enough for me for everything to cook.
What part of Oklahoma are these from? I am a 3rd generation Oklahoman (northeastern) and have only heard of this in recent years.

I absolutely love these burgers.  Using a spiralizer or mandolin on the onions is key, I just can't prepare them as well with a knife.  There is a fine line between packing the meat enough and too much, making the burger too tough (error on less compacting).

If you taking nothing else away from this, having an "onion towel" to steam the buns is a wonderful and universal trick.
Really happy with how it turned out, but next time I'll season the meat thoroughly (not just sprinkle on sides) and keep the bun/cheese phase going for at least 3 minutes. Love all the onions!
I'm from OK, and I approve this message. Delicious recipe.
This was an awesome burger - watch the video and follow along - had for dinner 3 nights ago and had to have another last night - will probably have another before the week is up!!

Never had a better burger. I watched the video several times, I ended up cutting the onions myself as thin as I could since I have grown to love my fingers and lack the nerve to use another tool - I used gruyere since I had some and there's a snowstorm going on outside. The cosmic oneness was achieved and Jake and Andrew loved this - No onions were wasted - I am the proud owner of an onion towel now.
Perfect. Followed recipe exactly. Video was helpful. Super easy will definitely make again.

So good! I made them on a Weber griddle (just like a blackstone) over med-high heat. Made 5 patties per pound at 3.2 oz each and used kenji’s ratio of beef to onion at 1.6 oz onion… which looked like a lot but really cooks down to the perfect amount. I was a little concerned with not toasting the buns, but I arranged as in the video using a dome and that achieved the desired effect of softening and infusing a bit of that onion flavor… and thankfully didn’t experience any condensation that he warned about. I used King Arthur Flour’s milk bread rolls recipe using 100 g dough balls, baking separated on a sheet pan with parchment, and topping with egg wash and sesame seeds. 

I love it when NYT recipes have a video! Thank you! Makes it easier to see what to look for.
This has become my go-to burger.  Delicious and easy to make large batches for parties.

Made this at DL for Mark F. and me.  Used the Weber; worked okay but I didn't get enough burnt edges due to having the patties over a cooler part of the fire.  Result: Delicious!  Probably cut fewer onions because I lost a fair number between the grates and in transfer from plate to grill and back.

There are a lot of good ways to do hamburgers. The smash burger is one of the best. This is one of the best smash burgers.

No fuss or gimmicks, just a solid burger.

Would easily do this again.
Cook outside, but delicious!
Love the video! Can't wait to try this. Was thinking about doing these as sliders but don't think it will work. Maybe? Thoughts?
Made exactly as directed. Excellent!
made this tonight, very tasty. No significant changes other than using Beyond Meat and greasing the pan a little with olive oil. would make again

This was delicious for my partner and myself.  Watching the video, I used a Chinese style cooking basket steamer top instead of a towel to cover the pan allowing steam to escape.  It was difficult for me to turn the burger with onions intact.  I was inspired to buy a better tool to turn. The burger was juicy, very full of flavor and the onions were wonderful. At every bite we had something good to say about our dinner.
If you have an opportunity to go to El Reno, OK, and have a legit onion burger, you must do so. They are so good. After 30 years moving from OK, I have great memories of them.

stats.Total Time: 20 minutes, plus time for heating grill

comments.commentBody: It took considerably longer for the water to evaporate than the recipe quoted.  I turned the heat all the way up and it was done in a jif.  I used Korean pepper flakes and a few cloves of garlic.  My wife and I loved the flavors and the corn stayed hot all through dinner.  I always follow new recipes the first time around and take artistic license when I make it again.
don't know why this recipe needs a "disposable aluminum tray...."  just use one you can WASH and keep out of the landfill.
Delicious. I poured the leftover glaze into a small bowl, and it took all of my willpower to keep from slurping it.
Using Harissa in the honey-butter is my (delicious) method of choice. I usually just use the powdered variety rather than making my own and then adding it.

Did not have a disposable pan so made the  honey butter glaze in a cast iron pan on the cooktop and put the corn in that pan after cooking on the gas grill. Worked great and then used the leftover glaze as a sauce on grilled boneless pork chops.
I didn't have access to a grill but proceeded to use this method with boiled sweet corn, and it was delicious. My husband ate three ears with enthusiasm!

I’m a huge Lopez-Alt fan, but for me, this was a miss.  Without a brushing of oil, the corn took a long time to brown and got a bit dry.  And the glaze took FOREVER to thicken.
Instead, do this:
- Brush corn with the lightest possible coating of vegetable oil and grill over high heat until nicely browned. 
- Meanwhile, mix a couple tablespoons of softened butter with an equal amount of honey and season with salt and pepper.  Brush onto corn during the last couple minutes of grilling.
Tried it as written. Great responses!  Looking forward to trying some variations.
Delicious and easy. I did it on my gas stove.
Don’t know what I did wrong here, but my corn kernels lost all their water and the result was a chewy, albeit buttery and sweet, cob. Disappointing.

Made it last night. Two observations, one is that grilling corn without any protection dries out the kernels, and second leaving the corn in the sauce for a long time drives them out even more. I would do this again but next time leaves the corn in the husks and not leave the grilled corn for too long sitting in the sauce.

As with others, I found that it took longer than stated for the liquid to reduce and the corn was over cooked at the end.  In future I might start to reduce the liquid early (and on high heat) so that by the time I put the corn in, it is almost done.  But it was absolutely delicious and the method straightforward.

What a winner of a recipe! Very easy but the taste is amazing! Used Aleppo pepper as the only other thing I had on hand was red pepper flakes. Didn't really see the need to wait for what others have said is a lot longer than a "few minutes" for the water to reduce, so put the pan on the grill (gas) as it was heating up. Roasted corn per directions and then into the butter mixture while grilling meat which added up to 12 minutes. Wife and I both said it is our new favorite corn method. Thanks!
It's corn!!! A big lump with knobs! It's got the juice... it's got the juice... can't imagine a more beautiful thing!!
made the gochujang version of this that's in the description. wonderful!!!! a squeeze of lime helped deepen the flavor.

I wasn't grilling other stuff, so I cooked the corn on the grill early and did the butter/honey glaze in a pan on the stove.  Let it sit there on low heat, adding water occasionally, as I cooked chicken in the oven.  Super easy, low pressure, cooking. Was hot, ready, and delicious when the chicken came out. A big hit with our guests.

It's fun to see a common street food of Mexico make its way to the vaunted pages of the Times, but why suggest obscure chili flakes from Korea, when there are so many great options also from Mexico (yes anchos, but many many more) ... where virtually all chilis came from originally anyway?  I'm all for appreciating cuisines from everywhere, but I don't think it's ok to ignore where delicious traditions hail from ... after all, that's half the fun!

This was amazing, i changed it up a little, by adding a little more butter, and replacing the gochugaru with laoganma chili crisps. I thought the suggestion of using a disposable pan was ridiculous so I used a baking tray with a tall lip and it worked perfectly!
Big hit with my family— used  1 1/2 tsp gochugaru flakes. A pleasant heat, not too spicy. Liked the all-grill cooking method.
I made this following the recipe exactly.  It was just meh - I think it would have been better to just make honey butter to slather on after grilling the corn.
I rub my corn with a bbq compounded butter then wrap each ear in foil.  Next time I’ll brush honey, butter and rub on there.
Turns out I don't like the flavor or  smell of warm butter, honey and chilles. Ripe socks.

In response to "Turns out I don't like the flavor or smell of warm butter, honey and chilles. Ripe socks."

I wish my socks tasted like butter and honey.

Reply
Is this helpful?
This was delicious.  I had farm stand corn that I ended up not being able to use for 3 days and I feared it would be starchy and tasteless.  This method worked!
Awww, man! Don't stop at gnawing off the kernels. There's a lot of that great flavor left on the cob. Kenji strikes again!
Old grilled learned new trick - thanks…easy to adapt preferred flavors and excellent process!
@ Jane, a disposable pan was one option. The other option described in the recipe instructions is a "a similarly sized stovetop-safe vessel that you don’t mind getting a bit dirty."

The sugar in sweet corn turns quickly to tasteless starch once picked.  

So we only get local sweet corn and use it immediately..  In the Northeast that means not until late July.  Anyone buying corn in the Northeast for Memorial Day doesn't know what SWEET corn is.
I thought the butter mellowed the spice too much.  It just tasted like buttered corn.

Answering George on sweet corn:
You are right about the corn that was available when I was young, in the 1950s.and 60s. Since then, new varieties have been developed that lack the  gene that changes sugar to starch. The new varieties, I think they are called “super sweet,” stay sweet for DAYS, even without refrigeration. Worth looking around to find some.

Fried Corn nibbles.....Take Frozen Corn out  from a packet thaw at room temperature dry it  with Paper Towel thoroughly...sauté with crushed garlic, Coarse Black Pepper and freshly squeezed lemon juice. Absolutely delicious and so simple to make.
Fine, but the sauce doesn’t add anything over grilled corn with butter

Made the gochujang version in a cast iron. Following advice from other commenters, I lightly oiled the corn and added 1/2 the water (but wound up adding an additional 1/2 cup later on). I thought this was delightful and a great way to make grilled corn a bit more interesting. Served with Rick Martínez’s Gochujang burgers and it was a hit!

title: Watermelon Salad With Fried Shallots and Fish Sauce Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

comments.commentBody: The dressing is a classic Vietnamese nuoc cham and should be adjusted while making since different limes have different levels of sour/acid and chilis vary in heat.  The measurements given are estimates based on what will generally taste right. Taste once combined and if too sour, add water and a little palm sugar, if too sweet, add a little lime, if too salty, add water and balance with lime or sugar.  Also, use a higher quality fish sauce, such as red boat (available on Amazon).

While I love all of the ingredients separately, the dressing wasn't well balanced and was too salty. Next time I would use less fish sauce (2T) and more lime juice (4 T). I would also conserve any watermelon juice and add it to the dressing.  I used 3 Thai chilies, which was more than enough heat. Much more than that and it would be inedible for most people. Also, skip the mortar and pestle and just put everything into the blender. It won’t make a difference and one less thing to wash.

Ellen, most experts agree that grinding in a mortar and pestle does in fact 'make a difference', but that is down to taste.  The idea that using a blender instead of a mortar leaves you with one less thing to wash is just bizarre, however.  I mean, you do have to wash out your blender after you use it, right?  I also think you'll find that washing out a mortar is much quicker and easier than washing out a blender.
@Daniel Step #1 presents using a blender vs. mortar and pestle.  If you only use the blender, there is indeed less to wash.   Not too bizarre.
For anyone planning to buy the fried shallots at an Asian store, the product is often labeled as "fried onion" or "fried red onion" rather than as shallots.

Excellent salad. Refreshing, spicy, multi-textured, deeply satisfying. I used 2 thai chiles and that was more than enough for my taste so if you don't do real spicy go easy. Brown sugar instead of palm and fried onions from a can.

This is absolutely amazing. I had a third helping. We cut back on the fish sauce (by half, substituting a little water) because we were using Red Boat rather than a lighter one, and it was amazing. Great quoc cham, great salad, and the herbs and fried shallots take it over the top.

I may be mis reading the recipe - but I think theses steps are inverted: If using homemade shallots, skip to Step 3. If using store-bought shallots, toast them dry... I think the recipe meant to say if using store bought skip to step 3, if using home made toast them dry....
Is there a preferred brand of store bought fried shallots?
Binned the cilantro (a personal thing), and added a little white vinegar to the dressing (a la Vietnam nuoc cham I think as someone already mentioned ). Very happy.
This dish is glorious!! Incredible Asian flavors wrapped up in the most perfectly summery dish 😍
Made this for friends last night and of course didn’t have any shallots fresh or fried so I just added extra peanuts and luckily no one knew how much better it could be and it was delicious.
When I was in Thailand the cooking teacher recommended mushroom sauce as a vegetarian substitute for fish sauce. What do you think?
Always on-board for Southeast Asian flavors so this was a hit for us. Added a bit more lime juice to the dressing. Was a tad sweet but next time I’ll search out palm sugar.

This salad is outrageously delicious and absolutely worth the trouble of multiple steps and all the garnishes. I used a habanero instead of Thai bird chiles, and I only used one. That was plenty hot enough and I love hot. I do agree you need a fruitier chile. Serrano or jalapeno wouldn't have matched the salad as well. I found the fish sauce to be extremely fishy, so I added some mirin and that helped. Next time I would use half as much fish sauce and the other half ponzu or just soy sauce.

Very tasty but the dressing wasn't as strong as other commenters were suggesting. I ended up using all the dressing and then added extra lime juice on top. I used 2 chilis and could have possibly gone up to 3.

The salad I made would have easily served 8, and I absolutely followed the recipe. Someone said it doesn't overnight wall, but I just had some left over for lunch and while the peanuts and shallots are not longer crisp, it still tastes fantastic. And there are still about 4 hearty servings left! BTW, I made my own fried shallots and it wasn't onerous. The worst part was trying to slice the little nubby shallot ends on the mandolin. And now I have a jar of lovely shallot oil!
This was a huge hit at last year's 4th of July cookout. Favorite dish for quite a few ppl, will make again!  As always, taste as you go and make adjustments for salty/sweet/sour.

Only used 2 Thai chiles and maple syrup instead of palm sugar and it came out great. I cut the watermelon into thin triangles, added tomatoes, halved grapes and nectarines and added a bit of basil on top. Used half the dressing on the salad as the recipe called for and although it tasted strong before adding it to the watermelon, it was almost delicate once it was on it and infused the fruit with a hint of flavors in the dressing. Everyone loved it. Very elegant salad.

So delicious! If this appeals to you, you’ll probably like it. 

Pok Pok in Portland (RIP) used to do a similar salad with more fruits in it, so I added halved cherry tomatoes. I could also see throwing in apple chunks and grapes like they used to do, but that ups the effort. I might skip the peanuts last time, as I don’t love them. 

I made the dressing in the mini chopper, which worked great. I sometimes find fish sauce too fishy, so I tried the dressing as the recipe is, and also diluted with a little water. I liked it both ways. 

Due to allergies, I might make this for a family member using vinegar in place of the fish sauce, or maybe by adding some water and salt. 

ONE Thai chili was pretty spicy for me. I can’t imagine what twelve would do — I wouldn’t be able to eat it. 

Yummy!
Turned out great - I added only 1 chili and used a food processor to make the sauce.  I also threw in some cutie mandarins that were on their last legs.
Please, can you suggest a vegan alternative to the Fish Sause?

You can actually buy vegan fish sauce and it’s awesome! I’m allergic to seafood (to the point where even just a tsp of fish sauce in a dish with lots of other ingredients causes an allergic reaction for me), but I love the flavor that fish sauce brings to any dish, so I hunted down an alternative. I purchased the 24Vegan brand on Amazon (which has tripled in price since I bought it last year so may be cheaper elsewhere), and it brings the glorious umami/perfect funk just like regular fish sauce!

I was dubious about making this. It didn't sound good. My bf insisted and, boy, was I surprised! It was absolutely delicious! Unique, flavorful and refreshing! I followed Ellen's advice and reduced the fish sauce and increased the lime juice and it was perfect! I used 4 chiles without the seeds and it was plenty hot! (In the first batch, I used 10 chiles with the seeds! Inedible.) I also made it in a food processor because I don't have a mortar and pestle or a blender. Totally fine.

This was a big hit as a side dish to Memorial Day burgers and hot dogs! Used two Persian cucumbers, 4 or 5 Thai bird's-eye chiles (which seemed to be just enough), and a little more than half of the recommended amount of fish sauce.

Since I had a very tasty Green-Papaya-Salad in a restaurant nearby I was desperately seeking a recipe for the salad sauce. By luck it turned out to be exactly the one from the recipe above. 
I instantly tried to make it and there it was: same heaven like a few days ago!
Some side note: Limes are very easy to get nowadays. Make sure to choose the right ones, preferably soft and ripe, as they are sweeter and juicier. It can help to add like a 1/4 ts more lime juice to the sauce.

This was amazing! Made dish as specified and thought everything was perfectly balanced. I used 6 chilis and it was hot! Would dial it down for those who like less heat. We did half with pineapple and half with watermelon and both were delicious.

Love this so much that I will make the dressing and keep at the office to dip my watermelon in at lunch.  I could have added another chili or two as more heat would have been welcome.  The entire recipe was a hit with all my guests...they were glad they were guinea pigs.

Made this with homemade fried shallots which are much better tasting than the store bought version we found here at H Mart in Seattle. The salad doesn’t overnight well as both the watermelon and the cucumber let down alot of moisture - causing the crispy shallots to ‘limp out’ as far as their texture is concerned.
Added tomatoes. And feta.  Seated the cucumber and salted it for an hour.
We found the dressing way too salty. Doubled the lime juice and added 2 oz shallot oil....NICE.

Wow! I wasn’t expecting to love this but it’s quite delicious! Everything works together so well. If you were on the fence I would definitely give it a go. While I’m sure it was not as delicious we used generic fried onions from the grocery store, as well as brown sugar and a habanero for the dressing.

This is a wonderful, refreshing salad perfect for a summer night's meal. I made it tonight and added around 20 poached shrimp to it. It was a major hit.
