I used the mortar and pestle to make the sauce, which was pretty difficult and messy and even after 30 minutes of grinding didn't fully incorporate the skins of the chilies. I think next time I'd just use a blender or food processor, but overall the combination of textures and flavors and the mix of spicy, sweet and salty is perfection.
Completely addictive!!

I'm now obsessed with this salad. The dressing is very similar to what you would have in Thailand as a Papaya Salad. With any recipe, taste and adjust as you go. I added a bit of maple syrup to sweeten the sauce as I found it a bit sour- But some may like that.

Excellent,,,
Used light brown sugar , 1/2 of a habanero pepper. 
Cut back on fish sauce by half
Toasted canned French fried onions as directed
Made dressing with immersion blender in 2 cup Pyrex
Saved cut up fruit separately from dressing to have leftover 
Delicious. 
Would be great for a potluck
This was SO GOOD!

I used half the amount of watermelon each of the three times I’ve made this, and all of (store bought) shallots on top. Two or three large chiles, not jalapeños but not sure what kind. It was a random bag. Everyone loves this!

My local Vietnamese place makes a similar salad with mango instead of watermelon, but otherwise the same nuoc cham sauce, fried shallots, and peanuts. They bulk it up to a full entree with daikon radish, carrots, and fried tofu.

It’s hard to find red chiles where I live so I replace them for a squirt of sriracha when making nuoc cham and it does the trick.
Making this tonight, can’t wait to taste it
Reporting back, this was so delicious I ate the whole thing on my own…

Made this exactly as written except for substituting a Fresno chile and coconut sugar (what I could get at the world's greatest grocery store - Zupan's in PDX). The flavors are phenomenal!! I loved this so much! Bought a mortar and pestle to be able to make this and it was well worth it. Highly recommend - 15/10!!

Very good summer salad. If you don’t have a giant mortar/pestle, use the food processor or else you have to make the dressing in batches in the m/p. I substituted piloncillo for palm sugar.  I found this in the baking aisle with Mexican spices.

Update: since this salad is my latest obsession, I tried doing it last night as salad dressing for a regular salad. It didn’t really work. 

I ended up adding watermelon to the salad because there wasn’t enough liquid in the dressing as written to dress everything in the salad. I think I’m going to try it again, but I’m going to add some oil to the dressing ingredients in order to make it a dressing for a vegetable salad that might work better.

The first time, I made it per recipe. Thereafter I have made it minus the shallots and cucumber, I like the salad better they way. Watermelon, dressing, herbs, peanuts -> perfection. If you are a som tam addict like me, this will hit the same pleasure point.

Brought to our office picnic (I know…pushing it) and even the non-adventurous liked it. Used brown sugar and a mortar pestle.
2nd time round found the palm sugar (old, rock hard) which I zapped to soften slightly then blended everything in the VITA mix. Much faster and just as good!
I grew up in SE Asia and grinding chilies in the mortar  pestle (for sambals) was an almost daily thing. I don’t miss that chore one bit.

This is endlessly flexible. It is one of my favorite summer dishes! I make it off-season long.

Sadly, I forgot to add the cucumbers because I was so hungry while I was making it. It was still pretty good, but it was missing one of the texture elements.

I add cherry tomatoes to this every so often, which makes it more savory, but it’s good that way, too.

I finely chop up the hot peppers and mix the dressing together in a glass jar. I can’t deal with the grinding and mortar and pestle action. I also sort of just estimate everything because I’m too lazy to measure it for this dressing. 

I can’t use more than one Thai chili for this amount of dressing. Otherwise it’s much too spicy for me. My cousin and I and I have also used Serrano or jalapeño pepper when we can’t find the Thai peppers.

My favorite way to do fried shallots is to use America's Test Kitchen's cold oil method. You put the shallots and oil in a skillet. Turn on the heat. Once it starts sizzling you turn it down to medium. Let it cook until they're crispy. Simple and it works.

Microwave for 30 seconds can = burnt, bitter bits. Also skip the plastic wrap (unnecessary). Fill one cup glass measure (convenient handle, right depth/surface ratio) to 1/2 full with minced shallots plus neutral oil to cover (I like avocado oil). Microwave in 10 second bursts. The shallots will bubble as they release H2O. Go 1-2 bursts more till golden (not brown), they will continue cooking from internal heat. Drain on paper towels, blot as needed, salt lightly while still hot.

I adore these and put them on everything. The most important part of the recipe is to take them out several shades before the desired darkness. They take a while to start browning, but once they do, they will be done within minutes. I also quickly press out oil while they're draining, toss them so they separate again, then blot on paper towels in a bowl, then salt. They are amazing! I do large batches to make about a quart at a time because deep frying always does seem like a fair bit of work.

For a speedy microwave version of crispy shallots, put thinly sliced shallots and olive oil in a small glass bowl, cover with plastic wrap, poke a few holes in the plastic. Microwave for 30 seconds, then add additional time, depending on your microwave until they are lightly brown, crispy and fragrant.
I used half the amount of oil called for and they came out perfect.

I use fried shallots in my chili crisp recipes, and have made them a lot.  As the recipe notes, once they look done in the oil it is too late, they will end up burned.  For me, trial and error worked best as to figuring out then the color was just right.  Now I nail it every time.
idk why I trusted the time on the recipe, mine clearly hadnt finished but they had taken some color so I just went with it.  Now staring at a big pile of shallot mush.  Guess Ill refry it?  Idk lol
These were great but took about twice as long to cook as the recipe said, maybe even more. Nevertheless, they were delicious.
So delicious on top of a cold plate of yellowtail nigiri or seared albacore. One of my very favorite sushi condiments.

This worked perfectly and was super easy. I didn't bother with a mandoline, just sliced them as thinly and evenly as I could. You don't need to "stir constantly", just give it a stir every minute or so.
it took me abt 30 mins!

made as directed.  Took a bit longer than 10 minutes, but it was clear when it started to brown and went very fast after that.  I'm guessing that when the water finally boils off, things accelerate.   I'll be sprinkling these on top of another outstanding NYTimes recipe, Twice-Cooked Pork Tenderlon!
no need to home cook unless you live in a small city in southern New Mexico where Asian groceries are few & far between.
OK- now I can say that I've done this. They were tasty. But the results were not worth the effort. Next time, I buy them pre-made.

I can't get this to work for me! Have tried the recipe and 2 reader's notes but I get either skinny burned shallots (in a pan on stove) or uncooked shallots (microwave technique). I'll try again. Any other hints appreciated.

This is the *bomb*.  I did go over to SeriousEats to compare, but this recipe hews closely to that one.  Used 1 lb of my garden shallots to 2C veggie (canola) oil & watched carefully when they turned a light golden color.  Immediately took them off the burner, sieved them & put them on 2 x paper towels.  This method worked for me & while I'm not a fan of using this much oil, the fried shallots are exceptionally tasty! And a good use of my many, many garden shallots.  Gotta' plant less next year
Forgot to add - slicing them on the 2mm slicer in my Cuisinart took 10 seconds - much easier than a mandoline if you have a Cuisinart.

To which kind of Cuisinart appliance are you referring?

Thanks for the tip!

Excellent recipe.  Great to have on hand.
Kenji, or reader, I ‘m looking for a great recipe for garlic chips.  
Suggestions? Thanks,
Made half the recipe.  Why did shallots turn out soggy, not crispy?  I used a mandoline as specified. After 10 minutes the oil was foamy and rising up the sides of pot.

I don’t know if my shallots had more moisture content in them or what (maybe my heat wasn’t high enough?) but it took me way longer than the recipe said to get to the right color and then they were never crispy, they turned into more like caramelized onions. Would try again with a much bigger pot as the bubbles from the moisture of the shallots don’t attempt to boil over.

@More time needed (and bigger pot) I’ve had the same challenge you described! After prolonged frying, they were brown, but not crispy yet. After draining them on the paper towel, we ended up putting them in a moderate oven on a sheet of parchment paper and watched them closely until they crisped up (maybe 5-10min?). I too would like to know why this happened? Did you ever try your experiment with a larger pot?

If you're still getting soggy shallots like I just did, just put them back in the pot with the oil and keep frying. I get tripped up on Kenji's "pale golden brown" description, which is accurate and what does happen once you've tried for the amount of time he calls for. But keep going until you can actually feel and see the shallots crisping as you stir. They should start to change texture before you take them out, which takes place about 1-3 minutes longer. Then after draining, they will keep crisping.

title: Spaghetti Aglio e Olio e Fried Shallot Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

comments.commentBody: I've been creating carmelized shallots in the microwave.  Sliced and in a pyrex dish with oil, I start at 5 minutes, and add 30 more seconds as needed.  Like all frying, they will darken as they cool, so pull them lighter than you think they should be.

Following up on this, the microwave method is also on Kenji's original Serious Eats recipe as an alternative. There's also a separate write-up by Sasha on SE that link to this method and a connection to America's Test Kitchen / Cooks Illustrated. 
tl;dr - microwave method also works, tested by legit people

The canned fried shallots are a staple in our house. Such an easy way to add a umami punch to oatmeal, soups, sandwiches. Will try this soon on spaghetti. Look for a bright red coffee-can type container in your Asian supermarket. I’ve never seen any brand other than “Bull Head Shallot Sauce.”

Shallot sauce is not the same as crispy fried shallots, which are dry and sold in cellophane bags. The oil-based sauce in a can is a spicy condiment. This recipe is using crispy fried shallots instead of the traditional Italian bread crumbs (cucina povera).
In spaghetti with garlic and oil, cheese, pecorino or other is not used, toasted breadcrumbs are used!
Growing up we always used a mixture of toasted breadcrumbs and toasted chopped walnuts rather than cheese or shallots. Today I would add pecorino Romano to taste too!’
Consider adding anchovies for a bit of punch

I've been making this for 30 years and it's my official comfort food.  Easy and quick. Sometimes I add scallops or shrimp to dress it up. I tend to use whole wheat angel hair pasta for the nutritional value and I've grown to prefer the flavor.
I made this and it's really tasty!--BUT I didn't follow the directions in Step 1 (did anyone else?): how can you put enough water in a "skillet" to cook a pound of pasta?!!
@Ellen Doré Watson, I used a saucepan (12” in diameter) and filled it with about three inches of water. Serendipitously, that was exactly the right amount and worked like a charm!
But can anyone recommend a great brand of fried shallots, especially southeast Asian ones?
It never ceases to amaze me how the simplest things can be the best. I remember the first time I ever had this dish in Italy and thinking, 'How have I lived my life without food like this?'
I don't know if they're great, since I haven't tried others, but Trader Joe's has them now and I like them a lot.

This is very good and its worth the effort to make your own fried shallots. I don't own a microwave so I did it Kenji style in a wok. Turned out perfect and was easy to do. The rest is a cinch, didn't have chives so skipped that, a perfect lunch!

This was fantastic - we used store bought fried onions and heated them on a pan beforehand. We added vegan Sweet Earth Mindful chick’n strips for extra protein. So much more flavorful than those bland garlic and oil pasta recipes we’ve encountered before.
Excellent recipe, very tasty, very well received.  But for my taste when I make again I'll cut the oil in half -- it'll still qualify as spaghetti aglio e olio but just not as much of the latter.
I made it but cheated a little, using French-fried onions left over from Thanksgiving instead of the fried shallots. And you know what? It was great. Easy, tasty, vegan.
I followed the chef's advice and got packaged fried shallots from the store. They were disgusting. Threw them out. Made my own. Better but no big deal. Recipe meh.
Upon reading this article, I went to an Asian grocery store and the closest thing I found was fried red onions.  Might these be an acceptable alternative?
Great recipe. My only change: double the shallots. 1/4 cup is way too little. Use the original amount (about 1/8 cup) in the garlic oil, and the rest for topping.
We always add anchovies for a bit of umami.
I’m confused by step 1.  Aren’t store bought shallots already toasted?
Grated Pecorino cheese is a must
can you make carmelized onions in the microwave too?  Like what's used for french onion soup?

Very easy and good the next day. I doubled the shallots and used a whole bulb of garlic. Otherwise, I think it would have been bland. I also added some parmesan that I happened to have. I think you could do 1/4 tsp. of the red pepper flakes. I think you can drop a tsp or two of oil---I accidently used less.
So easy and quick if using store bought shallots. I like to use chickpea spaghetti for a little protein.  A perfect weekday dinner!
I always make my own fried shallots. But I tried to find them in several Asian markets.  All brands I was able 5o find had preservatives.  Any suggestions for where to find pure shallots
With the homemade shallots, this is my family's favorite pasta.
Slicing the shallots made me cry but the end result was worth it.
I LOVE garlic- but for me this was WAY too garlicky. Would suggest halving the garlic. Otherwise, easy peasy and as I have several vegan/veggie familay members- a hit.
I LOVE GARLIC but wow- couldn't speak to anyone I know for a month and the taste was overwhelming. Maybe halve the garlic. Otherwise a hit with our vegan family members.
Trader Joe’s now sells crispy shallots. Work perfect and can use straight out of the bag.
If I want to use store-bought mayo, where do I start? Is it the mayo in place of the egg and oil?
Yes it is. But if you go this way, use a really excellent commercial mayo like that from The Ohai Cook. I use it all the time for Samin Nosrat's caesar salad dressing recipe.
Hi, Kate! Thank you for your question. We've updated the recipe tip to reflect the substitution.

I made this with the French's fried onions since I was too lazy to fry shallots and no way could I go to the nearest store and buy good ones.  In spite of that, this is really delicious and will definitely make again.
If we're using store bought mayo, where do we start?  What do we omit?

This recipe is great and so easy. The fried shallots (or equivalent - I used French's, too) really made all the difference - a much tastier crunch than croutons. Once we finished the recipe as written, my crew found the Dijon overpowering, though. We whisked in ~two heaping tablespoons of store bought mayo and that made all the difference.  We received lots of compliments on the salad, though of course we credited NYT!

Store bought fried onions/shallots end up in most of my salads anyway, so I tried this one... This is my new go-to Caesar! I've already made it twice, because we ran out of dressing and it made me sad.

It's a fantastic dressing, SO easy, uses only one egg, and ingredients that I have around anyway. (The first time I made it, I only had sub-par bottled lime juice, so subbed for the lemon and it was STILL great.)

My only question: How long can the leftovers sit in the fridge until I should toss?

Insanely good! My husband is the chef in the family, but I always make the salads -  and this one is a keeper! My only note is to follow directions precisely then add more water and lemon to taste. 1/4 cup water was added to make a nice consistency. I swear you will make this often!
@Kate Yes. Use around 1/2C Mayo and whisk in the lemon juice, Worcestershire, mustard, garlic, and fish sauce. Add EVOO to taste and consistency.
April 2023: good! Halved recipe, used store-bought mayo and vegan fish sauce, then need to increase the other things because at first it just tasted like mayo. But everyone liked the final product
https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/12459-mayonnaise

My aioli broke and I had to repair it by slowly whisking it into a teaspoon of cold water. Next time I would make it in the food processor, the way I usually make mayonnaise or aioli. Other than that, the recipe was a success.

Two great tips for making Caesar salad I learned from Chef Kimball Jones in his book "Sharing the Vineyard Table:" 1) Always taste the dressing on a piece of the lettuce you are going to use in the salad, and 2) toss the romaine with your hands ensuring each leaf is evenly coated.
What do we do with the garlic cloves before adding to the dressing? Grate? Mince? I can’t imagine they should be added whole.

I'm confused about the shallots being added into the aioli.  Do you add the fried shallots into there?  Or, do you use regular chopped shallots, make the aioli, then add the fried shallots on top?  I've just made the shallots, but I'm hesitant to add them as part of the aioli--that seems weird to me! But I'm no expert. I just hate to "waste" them in the aioli as they're some trouble to make. I'd rather use them on top (which the recipe also directs) where you can see and taste them.
Hi M&M.  Use the fried shallots in both the salad and the dressing.

Started with half cup of mayo as my base instead of the raw egg procedure.  Added everything in the blender, but used reduced quantities of mustard and fish sauce and zero neutral oil- just olive oil.  Mustard was the too dominant taste and not to my liking; I used Grey Poupon course ground, my favorite for salad dressing.  I really liked the store bought fried shallots in the dressing and topper.  Even tho I will never make this again, it has inspired me to use these shallots in more recipes.

I made this in a blender, but it's very thick and the blender had trouble, so next time, I'll either use my processor or a stick blender. I added more lemon juice at the end. It's very good on everything - fabulous on steamed broccoli or brussels sprouts, baked potatoes, a chicken sandwich, your finger...
Went straight off the directions and it turned out amazing!
MAKE THIS. Even your picky salad-avoiders will like it. No one will think less of you if you sub mayo for the egg and oil. Crispy fried shallots from the Asian market make it extra easy.

I really wanted to like this dressing. I always have fried shallots on hand and they are wonderful as an added crunch to salads, particularly when entertaining people avoiding gluten. This just seemed like a waste of a good egg and expensive oils for a so so dressing full of soggy crumbs.
I just fried a sliced sweet onion in butter until crispy and it worked really well in place of the fried shallots.  This recipe was delicious.

I had to use anchovy paste because I didn't have fish sauce, but otherwise followed the recipe. The fried shallots in the linked recipe are amazing, both in the salad and all by themselves. Don't buy them, make them!!

No notes. Unreal dressing. I replaced half the oil in the dressing with the oil I used to fry the shallots. I was confused about adding the actual fried shallots in the dressing, but it totally works. I would only add them using an immersion blender though. I don’t think a regular blender could get them as fine as they need to be.

I used half the amount of neutral oil in the aioli recipe.
Also added 1 tsp of anchovie paste to the aioli dressing mixture.

You don't know how much this recipe made me smile. Will be making it this weekend. Spent several years of childhood incarcerated in a convent boarding school in north of England. Our secret treat was Walls ice cream bars layered with potato "crisps" and sandwiched between buttered white sliced bread! We were told, "only bad girls' would eat something like that. Hence, "Bad Girl Sandwiches" , fondly remembered to this day 65 years later.
You should try chocolate or vanilla bean ice cream between oatmeal cinnamon cookies.

@LC
Chocolate or plain condensed milk drizzled over white (wonder or Japanese milk bread) or good vanilla ice cream scoop in an Asian bread bun (Japanese milk).  Yum 😋

My cat gets the squeezed out water, (but will not eat tuna???) I get a perfect sandwich with Ruffles, and chopped green olives instead of relish, celery, and onion if I am feeling 'fancy' but always, always, always the olives.  If I am out (unthinkable!) of olives I don't make a tuna sandwich. As in period. 
By the way, I use ordinary, stuffed with pimento, olives in jars from the isle, not fancy olives from an 'olive bar'.
@Alice Casella Suggest trying tuna tinned tuna in oil for your cat.  Love my cats…..

I have full faith in Kenji for any recipe (try his Detroit pizza recipe!) but I must add my own tips here. The crunch begins with celery, diced small and added with some diced onion. But complement all the salty/savory elements with the sweet snap of diced apple. The apple transforms good tuna salad into whooooaaa good.

@Pizza Quixote
Love!

The obvious problem with this recipe is that first one has to buy a bag of potato chips. When I do that, they don't last more than 24 hours (size of the bag doesn't seem to matter), so there's never any around to make this sandwich unless you make a trip to the store just for lunch.

@Just Here for News (and Arts, and Food)

That is why you buy a lunchbox sized snack one at checkout along with the one you “plan to use.”  Then hide it our of sight upon returning from store. Not foolproof, bc the fool hid it.  But could work.

My only comment is if using tuna in oil, resist the urge to dispose of the oil down the sink drain as called for in Step 1. Dump it into another container and discard, which is what you should be doing with any other fats, oils, or greases you don't want.
Mix liquid oils with coffee grounds makes it a snap to compost.
@DPH great advice. Not pouring grease, fat & oil down the sink thing all cooks and kitchen help should know. So unless you’d like plumber’s bills use an old-fashioned grease can or container.
Substitute chickpeas for the tuna for a vegan treat.
If you grate the egg on the small holes of a box grater it disperses through the tuna very evenly and is very fluffy too
My favorite sandwich! But on good rye bread, no relish, lots of black pepper, and has to be Ruffles.
I always add at least 1 tsp of horseradish. The mayo smooths out the bite but leaves the flavor.
If you have cats, don't get rid of the tuna juice!  Give it to the cats.  They love it.  lol. And I always use some dijon mustard in my tuna salad.
My cats adore tuna juice. But don’t try and give them tuna; they won’t eat it!

More celery, Kewpie (instead of olive oil) and chopped preserved lemon (amd maybe pickled red onions) in the tuna mixture. No need to spread mayo on bread - use romaine or Boston lettuce leaves on both sides of the filling to keep toasted multigrain bread from sogging up.

Sixty years ago my dad introduced us to piling Kitty Clover potato chips on the tuna salad then smushing down the top slice of bread (Wonder, of course). He said it was more efficient and saved time. Also, our tuna salad was always made with mustard (yellow, because we didn't know there was any other kind) and slathered atop a slice or two of good old pasteurized processed American cheese food. Still the only way I'll eat tuna salad.

Here is my recommendation. Relieve yourself of worrying that you have chips but no bread in the house. Use the chip as a vessel to move the tuna salad to your mouth. This works best in front of the tv.
Tuna salad is also great on Saltines. But we're talking about sandwiches here.

Tuna salad is best on an English muffin with a slice of cheddar or American cheese toasted under the broiler. But I do like it with potato chips as well…but not in a sandwich…I scoop the tuna salad into them like a dip. Chicken salad also good this way.
It HAS to be the cape cod salt and vinegar crisps in my tuna sandwich. In my house, we call them “the cods”, said in a Massachusetts accent. Absolutely mouthwatering.
Yessss... they're sturdy and have great flavor, don't get lost in the mix.

In addition to sweet pickle relish, sugared jalapeños or even just the hot sugar juice liquid is an excellent addition. A good sprinkling of curry powder is delicious too, with apple and celery. To bind it all together a spoonful of Hellman’s Mayo and a bit of rice wine vinegar.

@Jill Turner
Wickles jarred pickle chips are sweet spicy a bit crispy and delicious in this tuna salad.

@Jill Turner
Ooooh! Dash of sesame oil and Sri racha and you’ve got tuna sushi sandwiches - sprinkle with furikake to finish!!!
Consider: sliced peperocinis.  They add a nice kick and the vinegar sharpens the tuna flavor.

these are all great comments but would like to say: add a little dried/chopped fresh dill along with the lemon juice. also-love the other’s comment about the diced apple. i put diced apple in my potato salad and it takes it up a notch, too

I met Brian Noyes of Red Truck Bakery in Georgetown a few years back, he told this story: " Here, a teenage Noyes returns pictures (used for a story at the California paper where he worked as an art director) to John Wayne’s house. The star invites him in for a tuna sandwich packed with potato chips. Noyes’ extrapolation: "There are no rules."
It was so nice to learn about adding chips to a sandwich.  Duh. Where have I been?  I do, and will continue to add cashews to tuna salad and red peppers. YUM!

@Julie Aldige

Hey, they’re really good crumbled over vanilla ice cream!

Really?  You E-coasters never learned to swap out that slimy lettuce your mom put on your sandwich with chips from a snack sack?  We used Fritos or BBQ chips on tuna, bologna, or PB&J starting in elementary school.  Course, at 74, I’m here to tell you, in middle California, we weren’t into much that was gourmet in those days.  I also found that NOTHING made the liverwurst she insisted on sending every so often “for variety” any better.  Perfect waste of good chips.

What we ate in the good old days before air conditioning: If it was hot Mother chilled the can of tuna and the plates in the refrigerator until it was time to make dinner. We would eat our tuna-potato chip sandwiches on the stairs leading to the basement.
Thanks for sharing this memory, Connie—it takes me straight back to summers at my Grandma and Grandpa Goodwin’s house, chilled plates, tuna, and all.

Au contraire.  There should be NO large chunks of tuna in any tuna salad.  It will make it too dry and gross.  Also, don't try to get too fancy with classic tuna salad - mayo, pickle relish, minced celery and lemon juice.  Maybe a crumbled hard boiled egg, some scallions and tiny bit of herbs..... That's it!

You listed 8 ingredients to avoid "fancy." The recipe calls for about 9. That's a fine line separating fancy from your non-fancy version.

And - au contraire - I like large chunks of tuna in my sandwich.

Finely chopped red onion or scallion yes, but instead of relish, chop up some capers - a nice salty whack that tuna salad needs IMO. I think I saw the idea to put chips on top of a tuna salad sandwich about 50 years ago - on Captain Kangaroo if I remember - but maybe not. "Chipwich"

White albacore, Green pepper, celery, mayo, hard boiled eggs, seasonings and lemon juice.   (Or vinegar in a pinch- still recall my mom explaining what vinegar and lemon did in recipes  to an 8 year old me).  Outside of mom’s chicken soup, my fave comfort food.   Mom probably just used salt and pepper to season, I swear by Penzy’s Fox Point Blend, and often add Dijon mustard to taste.  Potato chips are a new one for me and I can’t wait to try!
Trader’s joes crispy jalapeños are so good in tuna sandwiches if you like some heat. They’re the jalapeño version of French’s fried onions.
sounds horrid, but we had tuna casserole with cream of something soup, peas and crushed potato chips baked to goopy perfection!

Us too! Maybe cream of chicken or cream of mushroom soup? I think it must have been a low-cost meal for my mother to prepare for our family of seven. Two cans of tuna, one can of soup, a few chips, leftover peas: she probably got it on the table for about $1, maybe $1.25 in the 1950s and 60s.
Must be salt-n-vinegar chips. With a V8 on the side. My favorite lunch. Even better if made at the corner deli.
@MHurwitz that sounds perfect. Like a luncheon paragon of delicious rehydration/electrolyte restoration.

This is something I started doing years ago at diners, where a tuna sandwich on toast was often served with side of potato chips. 

The thin, flavorless, mushy diner style tuna salad was transformed by opening the sandwich and reconstructing it with the oily, salty food-service potato chips. 

It may be 30 or 40 years since I've had that, and now I have a craving.

Kalamata olives are always in.  Another is a spoon full of Dill pickle juice (Claussens) and a small amount of minced dill pickle.  I always use Olive oil mayo, plenty of fresh ground black pepper, maybe a dab of Old Bay.  The chips add another layer entirely.
A dab of Dijon mustard gives a nice tang to my tuna salad, which is similar to this recipe but omits the egg.

If your tuna is packed in olive oil, keep some of it to mix in.  And don’t drain the can into your sink, to avoid clogging your plumbing with grease.  We save  excess oil and bacon grease in a closed container destined for the trash.
The best tuna melt I ever had included garlic in the tuna salad.  I always grate a small amount into the mix now as a back note.
Feeling pretty accomplished since I have been doing “The Crunch” since grade school. Cafeteria food in the 50’s needed innovation.  Necessity is the mother of…

I was today years old when I learned about putting potato chips in a sandwich. This recipe is next-level - the add-ins are delicious foils for the tuna. I like the other comments about adding olives and apple. Will make again and again. Every Thursday, it's my turn to make sandwiches for a handful of unhoused folks in my community. Even though I usually stick to the tried and true, I'm thinking they too might love the potato chip/hard-boiled egg/fresh herb factor.

Tuna salad always ALWAYS needs to be made with good chopped kosher style pickles and mayo.  Absolutely amazing - but now I’m just
dreaming about the addition of crunchy chips - OH MY!

I have been dreaming of this all week since I first found the recipe.  It did not disappoint! It brought back the school cafeteria sammiches [sic] we had as kids with the updated flavors of green onions.  Spectacular!

Nothing goes better with tuna sandwiches then potato chips (LAYS) or Fritos, yummy!!!  Not sure why I never put them directly on the sandwich.  Love it!  I of course will continue to eat a few more on the side :)
I would like to suggest French's potato sticks for the requisite crunch (plus more on the side, of course !!!)  Harder to find than used to be but worth the trouble !!!
Don't forget to save the tuna water for your cat(s)!

As a young teen, I moved from Ohio to the Philadelphia area.  There I learned that school cafeteria tuna hoagies (submarine sandwiches) were never consumed without first adding a layer of potato chips.  The habit remains to this day.
Funyuns on my dill pickled tuna salad sandwich. Takes me back...
Followed pretty closely except I didn’t have a HB egg. Not a game changer for me. I’ll still eat my chips alongside with a crunchy dill.

I have been reminiscing about this sandwich. I grew up eating my tuna sandwich with potato chips - mayo and some diced celery in the tuna - but never knew until now that it was a "real thing" that anyone else ate. Thanks so much for the memories!

Sometimes I add chopped scallions and chopped cherry tomatoes.  Sometimes I add chopped nicoise olives and capers.  Sometimes I add kidney beans.  Sometimes I add it all.  However I mix it up, it’s always delicious.
One word: Doritos.

I used Safe Catch Elite tuna to satisfy this pregnancy craving. Made the recipe exactly to spec, with herbs straight from the garden. It was perfect. I love recipes that are simple foods but take careful and loving prep. The fine chopping, measuring, and gentle combining of so many pieces that work so well together made this the culinary pride of my week. A joy to make and a delight to eat. 

And what a positive comments section. Seems this sandwich makes everyone happy.

The amount of flexibility this recipe offered with optional ingredients felt more choose-your-own-adventure more than recipe. The result of that adventure was the single best tuna sandwich i've ever had in my life.
Only never pour oil of any kind down any home drain.

While I appreciate the addition of chips to a tuna sandwich, I must admit that I am slightly confused (confounded?) by the instruction to squeeze the hard-boiled egg through my fingers. I can’t imagine a scenario in which this would preferable to chopping the egg. I feel like I would be finding yolk in between my fingers for weeks.
Yes, i found it peculiar as well.  (im not squishing a hard boiled egg with my bare hands) Call me conventional but I went with a fork and a mashing motion.

Be happy that tuna know how to swim!
My preference is for a light, crisp potato chip like Lays in this recipe. Kettle Chips are too thick and not salty enough.

I get a can of Italian tuna fish with olive oil at Costco or ShopRite... right now I can't remember the name!! It has a wonderful flavor, and I use most of the oil in my mixture. Although I live in NJ, I grew up in York County, PA where potato chips reign! Martin's, Utz,Hanover, etc. I will try this sandwich combo!
No way. If you can get fresh celery in season, keep the chips on the side.

Adding some finely chopped tart green apple (and I love cilantro added too) is lovely.  For me, a good squeeze of lemon (makes up for less mayo, which I prefer) and a dose of black pepper from the peppermill at the end is a must.
I once used pickled, chopped yellow pepprocini (banana?) peppers when I was out of relish, and really liked it - they also add to the crunch.
No egg!!!  Yes to celery and sweet gherkin pickles. Not too much mayo.  Fresh lemon juice (although I grew up on the bottled stuff).

Just made for lunch. Used most of ingredients minus salt with sambal oelek p/o relish and corn chips and less mayo. It was really tasty and next time will add chopped apple, toasted walnuts & curry powder to round it out.
Another “crunch” element in tuna salad  is chopped nuts. Pecans are my favorite.

I don't ever chime in with my alterations to NYT recipes but with this one I feel like I have to. The absolutely only way to go with this is Wavy Lays. The thicker, wider ridges and the resulting mouth feel can not be dismissed!
Since 7th grade it’s been barbecue potato chips.
This is a bit off topic but another great pairing is peanut butter and mayonaise.  Topping it with some iceburg lettuce is a nice variation.
Add good sliced dill pickles to the mayo and peanut butter and you my favorite combo.

Black bread with peanut butter, cream cheese, slices of banana, a couple squirts of honey and some shredded lettuce.

Back to tuna: add chopped chives, chopped black olives, scallion slices, half mayo/half sour cream, chopped dill, plus lime flavored tostidos. I also like to add chopped hard-boiled egg, but that mean planning ahead.
I’ve added Ketchup chips to my tuna sandwiches since grade 8– cafeteria fare. Love it to this day!!

Mama always added chopped apple, sweet pickle relish & pecans or walnuts. Those are required and I sometimes add dried cranberries or halfed grapes. We kinda like sweet! I make chicken salad the same way.
My husband will be thrilled about the potato chips! He's been adding them to burgers and any other sandwiches forever!
I serve my tuna salad on tostadas, and add finely chopped tomatoes on top!  Plenty of crunch and no bread.

Hello ?   Use Old Bay seasoned chips.  Several companies make them.  Seafood <> Old Bay.  
‘Nuf said.
More than enough.
Was really glad the recipe said to peel the egg

I guess you don't read comments in the Cooking section often. Absent the instruction to peel the egg, a half dozen people would be asking whether to peel, and a few would wonder what to do with the shell after peeling. Or will tofu "work" instead of the egg.

On the other hand, what kind of people need a recipe for a tuna sandwich made with potato chips? (Hint: The kind of people that don’t cook, and therefore don’t know to peel an egg before adding to a tuna sandwich.)
This is the exact recipe I grew up with, handed down by my mother, now of blessed memory. Thanks for sharing!
Capers, I have to have capers in the mix. I've never tried apple but will do soon.

Potato chips - total game changer.  I have been eating tuna sandwiches for 50 years, and never thought it could be improved from the mayo/sweet gherkin/albacore mix on toasted whole what bread, but I was wrong.  Kenji, thank you!  I never post comments, but this one deserved a shout out.
It was always potato chips on a bologna sandwich for me as a kid. Always. Now I never eat bologna. Tuna sounds fabulous!!

I didn't add celery or onions, because I didn't read the recipe first, just took the basic idea from the article. Instead of potato chips, I crumbled up TJ's Crispy Jalapeño Strips and used them liberally. Also, just mayo, no olive oil.  Served open faced on sourdough bread.  So good!!!
Potato chips on a tuna sandwich? Sounds great. I have been putting potato chips on peanut butter and jelly sandwiches for years.
I have been putting chips on my sandwiches since I was a kid -- Ruffles or Lays only please! I use chopped dill pickles in my tuna salad instead of relish.

Like others said: Adding potato chips to tuna salad! Where has this been all my life?!?!?

We like to add a little finely diced or grated carrot to our tuna salad. And we usually add mustard, whatever kind we have on hand.
So amazing! Really great with an arugula/kale mix, open face, with red pepper chili flakes!

Instead of chips, I use french fried onions & dill slices on the bread rather 
than relish mixed in. And celery never darkens the door here, not a fan.

This was outstanding as is! I’ve made it 4x and prefer the shallot to other onions. I’ve made it with full fat Greek yogurt in a pinch and loved that too.
Add chopped celery, a finey chopped or grated tart apple and a spoon of hot mango chutney instead of pickle relish.
My tuna salad add-ins: Dijon mustard and pickle relish, after the mayo. Fabulous sharp/sweet/tangy mix.  Will consider the suggestion of capers.

Really good. The tuna is less “fishy” in this recipe. I used Dave’s multigrain bread and an assortment of herbs. Subbed pickle juice for lemon juice, used Terra chips. This would be good with canned chicken as well.

A great sandwich -- but I had no potato chips.  I do have crunchy fried onions that I buy in a package at my South Asian grocery store.  Sam put me on to these:  Thank You, Sam!  
Having no potato chips, but needing the crunch, I used a handful of crisp fried onions.
Tuna and potato chip sandwiches are a classic New England summer beach treat! Highly recommend Ruffles brand!

I have very fond memories of my long departed grandmother making tuna salad with finely, finely minced white onion, soaking in cold water to tame the hash taste. Thank you chef for prompting that long forgotten memory.
No. Tuna celery carrots apple lots of dill and just enough mayo to bind
I detest mayo; mashed avocado is a good substitute.
I feel vindicated that Ruffles on a tuna sandwich IS recognized at legit!

Yes…a delight from my childhood!  We didn’t “fancy it up” with celery and onion, however—just Miracle Whip and pickle relish and occasionally a hard boiled egg.

Unclear what you would be saving the “good” tuna for. We are fortunate in Italy to have the finest canned tuna in the world, called “ventresca” meaning from the belly. It’s all I use.

Followed some suggestions and added apple and green olives, and gently grated the egg while it was slightly warm so it was fluffy. Used well-toasted brioche, Bibb lettuce, and topped with some spicy microshoots bc I had them. This sandwich took a while to make and it was beyond worth it. It wasn’t too creamy, it wasn’t too tuna-y, it was just perfect. 

Made and ate a second helping in one sitting. Thank you, thank you.

Never waste good flavors! That yummy "tuna juice", whether oil or water, is enjoyed by both my cats and dogs too! 

Likewise bacon fat (I add bacon for crunch sometimes in tuna sammies), chicken fat (schmaltz), etc. can be saved vs down the sink to add excellent flavor to potatoes (think home fries), meat, veggies, whatever you want to fry/ saute/ cook. 

This is what recycled little jars are useful for. Help your budget, the planet and your taste buds by using/upcycling whatever you can.
Delicious!
Am I the only one that sips the tuna water instead of pouring it down the drain?

I always have a tuna salad but this recipe sounds good!

This was really good! I added cubed red bell pepper, which was a nice bonus. Didn't have any olives, but those definitely belong here as well.

I've never wanted to comment on a recipe more than this one! Growing up in Pittsburgh, we all know the chips go ON the sandwich, not beside it. Teaching my NY grandsons the joys of chips on sandwiches.

Great recipe! Thank you. I used only 1 tablespoon of olive oil. Also, used nonfat Greek yogurt instead of the mayo. Plus added a little extra yogurt since I decreased the oil. Could probably omit oil entirely. Delicious!

This sandwich is next level. Used tarragon and tasted wonderfully new and fresh!

Back in the late 1950-early 1960s on my short suburban street in southern New England, adults (aka parents) would have summer Friday night lobster dinner parties. The bread was what we called cheap white sliced, but the crushed potato chips made it special. Thank you, Kenji, for bringing back fond memories.

This sandwich was so good. I omitted the egg because my significant other doesn’t like egg in tuna salad. Also, I had dill in the refrigerator so I chopped that up and added.

Definitely putting this on repeat.
My go-to beach sandwich!

This was the best tuna sandwich I’ve ever had, and I’ve tried countless recipes and tried many tuna sandwiches over forty years. So happy with this recipe, from henceforth known as the Perfect Tuna Sandwich.

Use some hummus
 instead of the olive oil.

Step 1- wash tuna can. Otherwise it’s a perfect recipe.

Ruffles or pringles for me. I love Kettle chips but they can be too crunchy.

I do like the idea of incorporating chips into this, but sometimes a recipe can be fall into the "restaurant" category. I've been making great tuna sandwiches with finely chopped celery, scallions and capers. Mayonnaise, some salt and pepper. Romaine lettuce and/or a good thinly sliced tomato. Toast. Good quality ingredients. Makes a great sandwich.
Olive oil AND mayo AND more mayo spread on the bread is a lot of fat. Eliminate the olive oil and use whole fat yogurt instead of mayo for a great taste and healthier option.

Contrary to my expectations, I liked the crunch of the chips in the sandwich. If I don't have potato chips in the house, I'm not making a sandwich.  ALL sandwiches are better with a layer or two of plain potato chips.

peanuts are very good in tuna sandwiches

Then EVOO, mayo & lemon juice. BUT instead of bread, top Akmak crackers with it! Yowzer!

My favorites are roasted pineapple, pickled ginger and water chestnuts with the indispensable Best Foods mayo, Dijon and sometimes if I'm in the mood a squirt of Balsamic. I like it best on slices of toasted Acme Sourdough bread.

Pour olive oil down the sink at your peril. When it’s cooled it coagulates.  Fats like this are notorious for clogging drains (and sewer systems).  Pour the oil into a plastic sandwich bag that has a paper towel inside.  Press out air, seal, discard.

Let me amend my earlier note: After the tuna and mayo (or Miracle Whip), add Dijon mustard, pickle relish, black pepper and garlic powder to taste. Adds a mixture of sharp/briny/savory to the tuna salad. Can also add finely chopped onion, red onion or shallot, if you like. Definitely toast the bread.

I love turning tuna salad into a tuna melt with cheddar or Swiss— just takes a minute or two in the broiler or 30-40 seconds in the microwave.
If using potato chips -- the point of this recipe -- you will not want toasted bread. The contrast between fresh bread and crisp chips is what makes this special.

Recently discovered a really yummy new way to do tuna...
Add all the veggies is you want... But mix a spoonful of good Dijon mustard, a little bit of Dukes mayo ( I am partial to Dukes.. much more savory and no sugary taste), and a spoonful  ( or more) of Chili/onion/garlic crunch  ( I use Trader Joes but use your favorite).  Salt and pepper if you need it.   Takes it to a new level.. .then move on with adding your chips or whatever...  I didn't love Tuna salad before this...

This is a fantastic yet simple recipe! Made it basically as written (improv comes after scales) except I didn’t have whole leaf lettuce so used mix from bag (don’t judge). Sourdough was what I had on - and it made for a nice handful of sandwich. Slid some Durkees on the bread before adding tuna mixture. Definitely in the summer rotation!

I'll have to try the olives!  Today I made it as suggested, and the potato chips really perked everything up.  I used to love tuna salad sandwiches, but then I got bored with them; this will change that.
I followed this recipe and served on toasted fresh sourdough rye bread from the bakery. Delicious! We had it for dinner with a salad.
Toasted English muffin, tuna salad, one slice of canned pineapple, one slice of cheese. Put under broiler to melt cheese. Yum.

Keep it simple- Mayo, sweet relish, Foch yellow mustard. Whenever we go camping our go to is Hawaii rolls with melted American. Add spicy bread and butter pickles, finish with salt and vinegar kettle chips.  Pass em out and keep ‘em coming.

Very good but needs a little something more. I think some of the reader suggestions of jalapenos or hot sauce would do the trick. Also horseradish which I love. However I hate pickles and relish and cucumbers. Substitute capers for the relish.

Lately I’ve been using finely diced kohlrabi instead of celery in my tuna salad and it’s delish.  Adds more variety to the veggies my kid consumes.

One bag is needed for sandwiches and the energy to make them, and one bag to go with lunch.  Maybe small dill pickles on the side.  Perfection.
Have always had mayo and ruffled potato chips on all of my sandwiches.  Thanks for making it legit!
Besides the already mentioned Miracle Whip, what else can substitute for the mayonnaise? Thanks.
Someone mentioned mashed avocado. You could also try using plain yogurt/labneh.
Sour cream.

Instead of mayonnaise, consider mirin, rice wine and white miso.

Fish water! That's what the cats call the juice squeezed from the tuna can. They love it. For my tuna salad, capers are a must. Also, I keep a little jar of malt vinegar in my fridge with a sprig of tarragon in it. Great splashed into the salad—just a little bit.
Tuna and potato chips has long been a favorite of mine. In fact, chips with pb&j, on hamburgers too is divine. Sweet, salty, crunchy…..

My wife buys wasabi peanuts from Nuts.com, whacks them into large fragments of shell and nut with the handle of a carving knife, and uses the debris on top of her tuna salad sandwich.  A breath-taking variation, and a great outlet for her 2023 angst!
I always add chips and capers!
I always add a few drops of Tabasco sauce to my Tuna Salad.  You can also make a very tasty tuna casserole with vinegar and salt potato chips instead of noodles.
I like Old Dutch sour cream and onion chips on my sandwich.
Add diced apple
I was putting potato chips on my tuna sandwiches 50 years ago. Nice to see the NYT catching up.

My mom would cut up sweet gherkins into teeny bits and it was magical. She served with Wise potato chips. Sometimes she would skip the bread all together and put the tuna right on the plate and we’d scoop it up with our chips. Years later at a work dinner at Elements in Princeton one of the passed appetizers was a blue fish salad on hand made potato chip and I had that moment at the end of Ratatouille with the food critic…Magical all over again. Thank you for this trip down memory lane!
When I was a kid, we put celery, relish, and seasoned salt in tuna. Nothing else. And we served it on challah, with mayonnaise – and potato chips.

Per author's end of recipe Tip: "This recipe calls for extra-virgin olive oil, but for a more classic flavor, omit the olive oil and use up to 4 tablespoons of mayonnaise instead." Yessiree, that's how we've made this twice. Timely for this recipe to pop up again as "of the day" with Mother's Day straight ahead because my mom never ate potato chips except with a tuna sandwich. I miss her. Her fav tuna to use here was an OR line caught. I recommend it https://www.oregonschoice.com/shop
My boyfriend and I have been talking about making tuna salad melts (yes melts, we're a little crazy) for DAYS. This is definitely my sign to make it for dinner tonight!
Cheap white bread with sour cream & onion chips. Left out the celery. Yum.

All these years and I’ve never had chips in tuna salad. I read the comments and added some chopped olives, otherwise followed the recipe as written. So, so good. I thought there would be some left for today’s lunch. No such luck. Next time,if we don’t have olives, I’ll add capers.
I like toasted sunflower seeds in my tuna salad.
"More than seems reasonable" - Translation: till they're falling all over the plate :) I have some arugula sprouts that will be a perfect replacement for parsley.
Use Tonno in olive oil- Skip the other binders. Ad vinegar and other ingredients to taste. Leave out the bread and eat it with a fork (or large serving spoon over the kitchen sink).
Thank you for the ad for unadorned Tonno. I read this recipe because I want to make a tuna salad sandwich.
Love the "chipwich" that I was introduced to as a kid.  I use just mayo, no olive oil.  Lacking from this recipe, but in my tuna salad is capers, just roughly chopped - little flavor/salt bombs.

So glad to find this recipe! I used Italian Mix for herbs instead of fresh, cheap soft hamburger buns (warmed) and followed the suggestion of more mayo and no olive oil.  Nothing “artisanal” about my ingredients, (which I think is the point), and the result was a crazy delicious and satisfying sandwich. (DO chop/mince the ingredients carefully and make sure they are combined well).

My husband is not a mayo fan, so I reduced the mayo to 1 tablespoon, olive oil to 1 tablespoon, juice of a small lemon, and used arugula microgreens and ramp leaves vs parsley, and ramp bulbs vs. onion. Served it on my homemade no-knead rye bread - delicious! Would add more pickles or some pickle juice next time, and I used corn chips I had on hand, but will try it with dill or salt & vinegar chips next time. 
Note: nutritional value is for  6 servings vs recipe being for 2 (if you're counting!)

Private secret to great Tuna salad,  shhh… mix tuna with miracle whip, dried onions sweet relish and pinch of salt. Chill x 1 hr. and slather tuna salad on side of bread, cut in half, layer lathered side with Kettle chips, complete sandwich and enjoy.

When I was in high school my BFF and I would bring tuna salad sandwiches to school and then would buy the sour cream and onion potato chips from the canteen to “smash” on top of the tuna.  It was delightful then chased down with an orange soda and I can’t wait to try this “grown up” version!
Did the flavor remind anyone else of fried fish with tarter sauce?

We would put potato chips in our tuna fish sandwiches in junior high school.  So this is a throwback for me.
I loved this! I ended up swapping celery with shredded carrots, and used dry parsley and added paprika. Loved it!

Great recipe, would be fine to try all the variations in the Comments. Used two eggs and went easy on the mayo. "5 minutes" is a typo, unless you have 6 additional people helping you ... otherwise, more like 35 minutes!
I love this so much - thanks for the walk down memory lane!  Infinite variation possibilities!

I enjoy tuna salad in several variations, but find them all to benefit from adding a little something flavorful and spicy, such as: sriracha, gochujang, adobo, tabasco, salsa macha, cholula, chili crisp, or other hot sauce

Very delicious mixture -- and I will never slice or chop a hardboiled egg for egg salad again. Thank you J. Kenji López-Alt!  Squishing through one's fingers makes perfectly sized, irregular chunks of the whites and schmushes the yolks into tiny bits.  Brilliant!

If you are shying away from this sandwich because of the bread (or even if you're not), try tuna salad—the ingredients minced or shredded pretty finely; a chunky texture here would make it more difficult and/or messier to eat—as a dip for sturdy potato chips. So good. I prefer a wavy chip, as a thin chip, like Lay's, aren't sufficiently substantial to handle the tuna salad, at least not without compromising its structural integrity. I suppose kettle chips would work too. Enjoy!

Please no red onion! Overwhelms flavour of most things. Scallions add nice balance. Never added egg, but will try. Cape cod chips are nice and crisp, much more tasty and less "fake" than ruffles. If adding olive oil, why not use the olive oil from the tuna instead?

Red onion is the classic addition to tuna salad. Soak for 10 minutes in cold water to get rid of the harsh taste if you prefer. I prefer tuna packed in water. Tuna packed in good olive oil is expensive; tuna packed in regular oil is questionable oil. Use an olive oil that you like.... you're in control.
Pro tip: Use Duke's Mayo
I've been putting potato chips in my sandwiches for years, they're great in turkey and roast beef too.  Salt and vinegar are always my first choice.
Chips are too salty for me, extra celery for crunch, no relish or pickle, lettuce on both sides so bread doesn't get doggy. If no lettuce around, thinly sliced cabbage works well and adds morecrunch.

Beware, while the flavor is good you can get the same effect with a bite of an awesome tuna sandwich, then popping in a potato chip, and this way you won't immediately think you did a poor job peeling the boiled eggs. I say this from experience.
This totally doesn't need mayonnaise.

My Mom used to do this over 20 years ago.  We all used to laugh at her because she put potato chips on her tuna, her cottage cheese, and more.  And she never gained an ounce from it. Thanks for the memory jog.

This is a family fav. My mom used to make these for our dinners at the beach. The salt air plus tuna and chips is a favorite childhood memory. We used Laura Scudders chips (a So Cal favorite). Thanks for the memory. I’mgonna go make a sandwich now.

I strongly recommend AGAINST draining the oil from the can of tuna into your sink... unless you enjoy spending time with, and money on, your plumber.  Grease and oils will congeal, harden, and build up in pipes, eventually leading to a blockage.  Squeezing the water into the sink is fine, but you are better off squeezing the oil into a disposable container and putting it in the garbage.  Take it from one who learned the hard way.

This cracks me up. My high school served sandwiches with potato chips all week long and on Friday it was a tuna sub. They kept the chip bags that had the broken bits in the bottom and used those to garnish the subs. It was divine. I cannot eat a tuna salad sandwich without them.
The tuna salad itself is good, though quite standard.  Putting chips inside the finished product is an affectation.  Plus, they quickly loose their crunch from the liquid ingredients.
What does this mean:  “grab the boiled egg and squeeze it through your fingers into the bowl with the tuna”?

I love this recipe with one major change: I eat the chips separately. My concern was that if you didn’t eat the tuna fish all at once (and I didn’t), the chips would get soggy. The results were perfect and I will do it again!
Loved this!  The extra chip crunch was terrific.  Also enjoyed all the add ins.  I enjoyed other commenters' kitty comments.  Next time they get to.lick the can.
This was filthy good using two leftover soy-marinated, five spice deviled eggs in place of the boiled egg and olive oil. No regrets.

Delicious sandwich! Made a couple of small changes the second time I made it - used Kewpie Mayo for extra richness, skipped the EVOO, and added a little Dijon mustard for additional depth. Skipped the hard boiled egg, as it gets lost and I feel the egg white adds an odd texture. Wrapped it in a large spinach and herb tortilla wrap with spring mix salad greens. A wonderful lunch on a Friday while working from home. :)
I make this and add cumin and chili powder to taste. I also use fresh spinach instead of lettuce. It's delicious.
made a simpler version with just mayo, salt/pepper, egg, pickle and cucumber. yummy and quick!
Add in a teaspoon or two of mustard. Gives it a nice flavor boost!

Absolutely delicious!  We put a grip of pickled japaleno's and lightly toasted the bread for extra crunch.  We also stuck with mayo only, and went a little lighter to make it slightly "healthier", and added fresh tarragon, parsley, and chives.  SO YUM.  Next time will add a little more lemon juice for additional brightness and some fresh dill too. Def. enough for 2 robust sandwiches.  A great easy lunch!

Step 3 starts, “when ready for lunch….” How far ahead can steps 1 and 2 reasonably be done? I craved something like this in the hospital after having my son, and I want to prepare it ahead of labor this time around.
Washing your hands before grabbing the egg doesn't give you the benefit of some nice oily tuna juice in the crumble.  Every little bit counts (smile)...
Perfect tv dinner!
Both pimento stuffed olives & capers make the tuna salad more interesting.
If you like capers, try subbing capers for dill pickle. I promise you won’t be disappointed!

My Brother was making these sandwich’s back in the early 60’s.  Tuna Sandwiches were a staple in our house.  I am going to send him the recipe..
O yes, another thing -Tuna Dip.  Tuna with all the water left in and some Miracle Whip to make it “dipable”.

I made some smoked tilapia because I had the smoker going and I had some tilapia. The fish wasn't  stand alone good so I tossed it in this recipe as the tuna and went with shallot and tarragon and dill pickle. It was so good I've never tried it any other way!  So good!
Really, this is an original recipe.  You have to be kidding.

Pack the tuna inside peak sweetness hollowed-out summer tomatoes with a tiny dot of balsamic atop the stuffed hole. Chow down while eating a bag of chips.  

Will definitely try this —esp with egg/horseradish/pickle/celery.   But, having just downed 3 peak summer tomato tuna bombs for lunch before seeing this recipe, I wanted to pitch this highly scientific variation!
I made a keto version substituting sour cream onion for mayo and Quest chips for regular chips and served on Nature's own keto buns. Only 6 net carbs.

Not all canned tuna should be drained. A lot of the more “premium” brands specify that you should mix the packing liquid back into the tuna. If you’re using regular bumblebee or starkist, then for sure drain.
How is it possible to prepare all these ingredients in 5 minutes? It seems unreasonable.
Best tuna sandwich ever!

Tuna salad has always been a 5-minute quick meal for me so I don’t pass out. This sounded so delicious, I had to go in. And now I will never make tuna sandwiches any other way! I will skip the crumbled chips in the salad going forward. I feel guilty enough with those on the sandwich, plus those on my plate ;), and they offer plenty is salt and crunch. I also used shallots and vary the herbs depending on what I have on hand. And skip the celery bc I rarely have it, but I find the relish fills in with depth and crunch adequately.

title: Caldo de Costilla (Colombian Beef Rib Soup) Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

comments.commentBody: Simple for sure, but still a special-occasion meal if you factor in the cost of short ribs or oxtail these days. Where I am, they run at least $12 a pound, so that is some mighty expensive soup.
@Equilibrist in Colombia short ribs are one of the cheapest cuts of meat. Caldo is definitely not a food for special occasions in Colombia :)

I made this using neck bones (all I could find at a decent price).  I would advise adding the cumin seeds when adding the scallions and cilantro, since they're so light they floated up with the scum and it was impossible to avoid scooping them out.  There was a sort of thinness to the broth, I can definitely see it as a base that other things should be added to

Another note:  After straining the soup it should be chilled so the fat can be removed.  I followed the instructions and chilled it after cooking the potatoes and adding back in the beef, and it's impossible to skim the fat off with so many solids in there.  Chill, then skim and cook potatoes

Born in Mexico, we grew up with this special soup. We always seared the meat for extra beefy flavor.  Now the short ribs are pricey, but you can use chuck roast, cut into chunks and sear.  We added chunks of carrots, onions, potatoes quarters, tomatoes. Wedged cabbage slices topped the soup before it's last simmer.  All seasonings mentioned, and served with a side of salsa or pico.  For a more filling meal, add a spoon or two of Mexican rice. 
Serve with freshly made warm corn tortillas.

This recipe proves food can be delicious without complicated flavors or techniques. I used beef shank rounds and neck bones, which are far more economical that short ribs. I recommend a generous three-finger pinch of chopped cilantro and green onion for each bowl.

Simple but delish recipe! I made it with short ribs and the broth was amazing. Added less than 4 quarts and loved the idea of saving extra broth (for Pho). As a dinner on a rainy, cold evening was perfect. Puppy got couple of big bones and we were all happy. :)

I followed this recipe exactly as written. I was skeptical to not brown the meat first no salt till the end and no black pepper, ever. But it was just delicious!!  Try not to change a single thing. I used beef ribs.
Add cumin seeds with cilantro and scallions
Check! Cumin seeds initially float, and get removed with skimming.
I’ve been sick with  this week with Covid, when I saw this recipe I sent it to my sister and asked her to make it for me . It was so delicious and brought back memories from my country Colombia

I was recently in Cúcuta, Colombia. I had this soup(caldo), and also with chicken, it was absolutely delicious! I grew up eating this type of food, but had forgotten how delicious it was! And it sure did make my stomach feel better after a long flight and many activities the day before! My stomach was in knots, my father was dying. And my nerves were crazy. The soup was very soothing! Muy delicioso! Gracias

Bring the water to a boil with only the meat, then skim until you get bored. Then add the halved onion, green onion, cilantro, and cumin seeds. If you follow the recipe as written, it will be impossible to skim effectively. Many traditional recipes add carrots, but that cruciferous dimension can be gotten by adding a small bunch of parsley with the cilantro. I don't really see the point of waiting to add salt until the end. I added it with the aromatics. Very tasty!

I lived Bogota (expat) and loved it. I subbed parsley for cilantro. The soup was flavorful and lovely, and I will make it again. I think Colombians can make this soup to cure hangovers, because many Colombians have live-in help--this is a time consuming recipe, and no way I could throw a batch  together in the midst of a hangover. I read the recipe several times, yet was a bit confused by the first application of herbs and scallions. I shredded the beef and added to soup without bones. Sabroso!

@DreaAn yes exactly in many places in South America. This is served the day after a big party and it does help with curing hangovers. In fact, it is often called Laventa Muerto, the “raising of the dead” soup because again it helps you recover from a bad hangover and get you back on your feet to rejoin the living.

I grew up in Colombia and this is a wonderful pick-me up soup! So simple and satisfying. My mother suggested me to only add the cilantro bunch during the cook, but at the end only top with green onions (the broth already has a lot of cilantro flavor already). I also used boneless short ribs and the result is much better (absent the price difference). The cumin seeds should be added after you scoop out the scum.

I make this recipe about once every month. I absolutely only use short ribs which run about $10 a pound for me. I use about 4 to 6 ribs but then I also use a pound or two of short rib meat without the bone cause I like it very meaty. I also cook with just the stems of the cilantro tied with a string and reserve the tops of the cilantro for serving. I also smashed the garlic and cut the onions and leave the skin on because everything gets strained anyway, and who knows, maybe garlic skin and onion skin are good for us? After about three hours of very low cooking all the meat gets pulled out and put on a plate and put in the refrigerator and the broth gets strained and also put in the refrigerator. When everything‘s chilled, I cut the meat off the bones skim off whatever I want off the broth and rebuild a soup with potatoes that I’ve cooked separately. Instead of salt, I use a tablespoon or two of beef flavored better than bullion. This helps round up the flavor and give it even a meteor flavor - lol meatier.

This was a delicious dish. Adding the cumin before skimming the scum meant losing most of it since it rises to the top.
I would have added the salt in the beginning since the meat really needed it.
I think that beef shins, a less expensive cut, could be substituted for short ribs.
One of my favorite meals when I lived in Colombia! The broth is simple and healing.
For those who are saying that the beef makes this an expensive dish, try buying it at Aldi. Aldi has beef ribs at a very nice price. I plan to make this brothy soup!
I do wish you could give the weight, or chopped volume, of the cilantro. Bunches come in very different sizes in different places. Thank you!
As others have mentioned, neck bones make for a delicious and economical alternative considering the prices for short ribs and oxtail right now.
You could also use Osso buco instead of ribs.
Definitely a thin soup with a delicate flavor.  I added roughly cut carrots, and a tablespoon of Better than Bouillon to add sweetness and savor.  Delicious.

5 stars with a few tweaks: -I used oxtail that was on sale which was great but very fatty so would recommend trimming some fat before simmering so broth doesn’t get too oily (had to scoop a lot of oil out); -salt & pepper the meat well before simmering OR add bouillon cube to the water while everything simmers so meat absorbs some sodium (meat will be bland otherwise). -Took closer to 4-5 hours to get the meat totally tender. Great recipe overall!!

Question- I've made variations of this broth/soup over the years but don't short ribs leave quite a bit of fat to clear out? I liked one suggestion of using   cut up chuck roast, neck bones and general soup bones. Any suggestions on easy methods of getting a clear beef broth as in the photo?
Thanks, love the recipes and suggestions

Delicious-both light and rich at the same time. I did use short ribs, and they were amazing. Took the advice of reviewer ciaobella and boosted the flavor of the broth by adding a bit of cabbage, carrot and tomato to the liquid when cooking the meat.  After removing the meat at 2.5 hours, I continued cooking the broth, lid off, with with the veg and bones (many had fallen off) for about 30 minutes to concentrate the flavors. Strained as directed, but did not remove any fat - I like the fat :)

Hearty soup that reminds me of haejangguk or Korean Hangover Soup without the spicy gochujang. I couldn’t find the oxtail or short ribs so I tried it with beef rump roast which still came out very tender. I also added half a jalapeño in the beginning and the subtle heat from that blended nicely with the fresh cilantro. Also, the number one ingredient is salt. Without a healthy amount of salt, it’ll be pretty bland. Add enough though and all the flavors just pop. Will definitely make this again.
I was so disappointed in the flavor I went back to the recipe to see what I left out. Turns out I didn't forget anything. I will not be making this again.
Put garlic onion, and scallion in the blender and add

title: French Onion-Braised Lamb Shanks With Barley and Greens Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

comments.commentBody: Buy your meat at the butcher and ask to “crack” the shanks. The butcher will slice through the bone, but leave the ligament attached. The result is the marrow now cooks into the stew and develops the rich flavors unique to lamb shanks.

The lamb shanks I get all give off a great deal of fat as they cook. So I would skim off fat from the top of the liquid before serving, or better yet, remove the lamb from the finished dish, chill the sauce/barley/greens separately from the lamb, and remove excess fat once the sauce is cold. Then recombine the two parts the next day to reheat.

Suzanne F: Great point. 
Interestingly, the reaction to lamb fat is culture-dependent. Those who eat lamb rarely if at all find its odor repulsive, but folks in lamb-eating cultures find it appetizing, especially when combined with spices/herbs, and save it for cooking (like schmaltz/lard in European cuisines). An Iranian friend told me that kebab vendors there grill the tails of fat-tailed sheep, and use powerful fans to blast the aroma toward the street to attract customers.
@Suzanne F good call, although I will wait until I reheat to add the barley and greens.

Very nice recipe. Interestingly, India's Parsis prepare lamb/mutton similarly, browning loads of onion at the start, then browning the meat. But they typically follow up with pressure cooking - an Instant Pot will shorten Step 4 to 40 minutes.
Not sure about the need for dry red wine. Dry (brut) is a fancy term for "not sweet": cheap wines often use sugar to mask immaturity/impurities. This recipe, however, benefits from mild sweetness, so even a Trader Joe's "3-buck Chuck" would work.
It's "Three" Buck Chuck now?  I remember when it was a third cheaper.

@Jed - Prices were raised a while ago. I guy I know, who drinks this exclusively, was furious when it happened, but he had little choice but to stick with it. (Aldi's has some $2.95 wines as well - the Moscato and Zinfandels, which are intrinsically sweet, are surprisingly decent.)

Prakash Nadkarni - Sugar is added to cheap wine not so much to mask impurities but to make it taste as if its natural sugar content were higher. This process is called 'chaptalization', after one Monsieur Chaptal, and in France it is legal.  However, in Italy, Spain and Portugal chaptalization is forbidden by law; these countries are much sunnier than many parts of France, and the natural sugar content in wines produced there is therefore higher.  No added sugar therefore has to be added.
"...cook until the wine is reduced by at least half, a minute or two." How do you turn two cups of wine into one cup in a minute or two?

Recipes are constantly doing things like this. Compensating for unrealistic times is quite annoying. I would also say that 45 minutes didn't get terribly far with caramelizing the onions, but I decided they were OK like that. Put the dish in the fridge for a couple of days after the first stint in the oven, to reheat, add barley and spinach and finish later.

I grew up in the Middle East where the only red meat we could regularly get was lamb.  Yes, it can sometimes have a strong odor and flavor that is off-putting for some people, but that is often because what is sold here as "lamb" is actually often older and therefore more akin to mutton, which can be very "gamey" in odor and taste.  The meat from a true lamb is delicious and delicate, and worth trying to find from a reliable butcher or farmer.

Conversely, if you enjoy more robust flavors and are fortunate enough to be able to purchase hogget or mutton shanks, either works beautifully in this sort of recipe (with a bit more cooking time, esp. as they're usually larger), with more flavor and often less fat.
Soaking for 10-15 min in wine will "freshen up" meat and kill off gamey smells. Ditto using Vodka or Sake to brighten up Fish you should have cooked last night.

Certain family members object to wrestling with large pieces of bone-in meat, so I typically remove all the meat from the shanks in stews & return it to the pot before serving. In my family we call this “company style.”  I’ll do that here as this recipe definitely looks good enough for company.

I rarely follow recipes, use mostly for inspiration.  Limited free time so went for it.  Used farm (New England) raised lamb shanks, homemade no salt chicken stock.  Delicious outcome.  Many textures (meat, barley spinach), many tastes (herby meat [extra fresh rosemary], sweet from tomato paste & lamb, earthy from barley, plum notes from the wine.  Will make again and maybe use short ribs.  Maybe use curry, jasmine rice and slight sweetness from a chutney.  Definitely a winner as is, thank you.
Can this dish be made with farro?
@Pups224. Oh yes, that would be perfect
@Pups224 I love farro with braised dishes.

Quite nice, really hit the spot on a cold winter evening. I used 3” lamb neck chops rather than shanks, as that’s what the butcher had, and added a dab of anchovy paste along with the tomato paste. Red burgundy wine worked well. 

Had to be away from home for a few hours while this was cooking, so reduced temp to 250 and cooked an additional hour, no problem at all.  Served with mashed potatoes rather than barley. A great take on a classic dish, thank you Kenji!

Made this for Christmas eve dinner and it was a huge hit. I used store bought broth with added gelatin. I salted the lamb shanks about 1 hr before cooking and browned them in oil, letting them get really dark, then I drained the oil/ fat from the pan and continued with the recipe. I did not add the carrots until the tomato paste step because I was worried it would interfere with carmelizing the onions. I used a cheap ($3) cab for the red wine and it still came out delicious. I thickened the broth at the end with some corn starch to make a gravy to go over mashed potatoes. Next time I would make the caramelized leek/ onions in the slow cooker the day before so I dont have to stand at the stove for an hour. Overall this was a high quality meal without being too fussy.
I've cooked this recipe twice. I find that falling-off-the-bone tender is achieved at 325°, rather than 275°, per the original. With that, it's a great dish, suitable for company.
A lot of work for an "Okay" result.

Most of the time this is in the oven and it only has a slightly longer prep time compared to other braises with these steps because of the caramelized onions. Flavors take a little bit of time and work to build. If some basic cooking work isn't for you (the steps here to caramelize/deglaze/reduce are the same for braises/stews around the world from bolognese to curry to mole), there's a whole industry of people who will do it for a price.

Pressure cooking through step 4 can reduce the time but changes the result significantly. Per Kenji's other work, the oven step with a cracked lid does a few things:
-Reduces the broth
-Adds flavor through caramelization of top layer and anything sticking out of broth
-Maintains lower liquid temp so the meat is more tender (it is possible to overcook and get dry stringy meat with wet cooking). Time and temp affects texture a lot. 
Its doable other ways, will prob still be good but not the same

@Richard - RE: Instant Pot vs oven
* Using the rule of thumb of 1/3 non-pressure cooking time avoids stringy meat. This works even for drying-prone meats (chicken breast, pork loin) where marination+added fat also helps. 
* Diligently browning meat & veggies in Steps 1-2 pushes caramelization/Maillard reactions as far as they'll go. 
* Pressure cooking always needs less liquid because the steam has nowhere to go. You can cook off excess liquid later, but you don't need an oven for that

Just made this. Didn't add barley and accompanied it with mashed potatoes and broiled asparagus. Absolutely spectacular.

Onions took about :45 to 1:00 to carmelize.

I'd never used gelatin added to chicken broth and it was wonderful.

Still have a lot left (three eating it), will make polenta tomorrow to serve with it.

We're I to make it again, I'd put in more carrots.

The lamb shanks I got were pretty big (2 weighed 3lb) and they took much longer to cook than the recipe suggested - they were falling off the bone after about 4.5 hours in the oven. But the end result was delicious - rich and tasty

Absolutely delicious! A new favorite. Used rich homemade stock. Carmelized onions about 35 minutes. Made this over a few days: cooked through addition of barley on Day 1, leaving barley a bit underdone (had to leave the house). Refrigerated a few days, then reheated, cooked an hour at 275, then added spinach for just a few minutes. Adding the greens for the last few minutes is the only change I’d suggest - oh, and the dish was all the better for flavors marrying a few days.
Use american raised lamb. the New Zealand lamb can be quite strong and the meat to bone ratio is much smaller. The american lamb cost more but in my opinion well worth it
Is there a gluten free substitute for the barley?
How about brown rice?
Prakash, I think a more accurate description of a good wine for this recipe would be a dry (but not spare) fruity wine, at least that would be my choice.
While I love lamb, my wife finds it distasteful. Could pork or another meat be substituted?
This recipe would probably work with pork shoulder cuts, beef chuck, or beef shanks. Good luck! :-)
We are an alcohol-free home. What do you suggest replacing the 2 cups of wine with?
