Can an instapot be used?

I expected this recipe to be more like "French Onion Soup with Lamb" than it actually was.  This recipe reminded me a lot of Ina Garten's Beef Barley soup with oxtails.  The next time that I make this, I won't use any red wine or tomato paste and may instead try white wine in order to get more of a French Onion soup flavor.
Is the heat oven to 275 degrees F or C? Turning a 275F oven down to medium seems difficult.
275 C = 527 F, so safe bet it's a slow oven at 275 F
I echo the farro substitution question.
Pups 224, I have the same question about using farro.
Ours isn’t falling off the bone after recipe’s coking time. Did anyone find it needed more time?
yep same here, seems like it's going to need quite a bit longer
@irene this is very dependent on the size of the shanks. Larger shanks may need an hour or more extra.

My onion and leeks have been going for about 30 minutes and are not even close to being lightly caramelized. Could it be the leeks were too wet?  I cleaned them and tried to shake off the water.  But they were still wet.  What can I do?
This was so delicious! Couldn't find lamb shanks so used short ribs.  It took an hour to caramelize the onion mixture but so worth it. Next time I will add more greens.

Great result, a special dish.  Start to finish was near five hours.  Next time, I'd like to try the 2 1/2 hour braise in the pressure cooker.  If someone has done so with success, please post the timing used.
Cooking this now but doing it on the stove top with short ribs (could not find lamb). Smells great.

Really delicious, hearty dish. For the two of us, I used two 1lb shanks, cutting off as much fat as I could before searing, about 1 1/2 lb onions,  3/4 cup barley, and about 2 cups of  turkey stock leftover from Thanksgiving. Made a day in advance. So tender, lamb basically melted. Very adjustable recipe and just so good.

As good as this is, it becomes even more amazing the next day after it has cooled and a bit of the fat scraped off. I could not find lamb shanks so used shoulder chops. Also added some orange juice for brightness and to cut the fat a bit. A keeper!
Not anywhere near long enough to make the meat fall off the bones. I must have done something wrong.
@sablonnieres Another commenter suggested 325 oven instead of 275. I think that would make a big difference.

Delicious, and comes out well cut in half as dinner for two. Actually saved the leftover sauce/onion mixture and served with pasta.

Also used store bought bone broth rather than stock to amp up the body of the dish.

I finished making and eating short ribs made via this method.  I used store-bought chicken stock and added some gelatin to it.  The recipe worked as directed.  I give the prep four stars.  The gravy was excellent.  There is a short rib recipe by Alison Roman that I prefer, ‘Tangy Braised Short Ribs.’  PLEASE NOTE, the rate at which the onions carmelize is a function of the diameter of the pot.  A larger diameter leads to quicker carmelization.

I've never made lamb shanks from scratch.  I luckily started with this recipe which was absolutely deliciousI got my lamb shanks from the butcher but did not know that I should ask to have them cracked.  So they weren't.  But that didn't stop the recipe from being terrific!  I'd do it again soon!

Amazing recipe, I used ossobucco  cut shanks and they probably could have braised another half hour to be fork tender. As is, they were easily tender and cut with a butter knife. Flavor was great but maybe add a tiny amount of vinegar when finishing to cut some richness. I recommend skimming some fat before adding the grains.

We just picked up our yearly lamb from a wonderful, small herd farmer. Shanks are one of my favorite cuts as there are so many ways to prepare them. And she gave us 4 extra shanks as some others didn’t want the shanks! Made a small batch version and it was very yummy. Next time I would add a germolata at the end to brighten the dish.
Excellent!i did not use the barley as mashed potatoes are a perfect pairing. I did add a tablespoon or so of vinegar at the end to sharpen the flavor.
Didn’t use the barley. Cut the recipe in 1/2 and had 8+ servings. Very delicious!  Leftovers especially!  Mashed potatoes the perfect accompaniment.
How much is “one bunch” of spinach?

This was delicious. I cooked onions for 1 hour, not browned but very soft. Cooked lamb for 4ish hours, then took off the bone. Kept warm and reduced the sauce on the stove as didn't put gelatin in the stock. Served chunks of lamb with sauce spooned on top with polenta. Came out great.

I have made this twice, and it is delicious. It does take time, so I save it for vacation time. The first time, there was a lot of fat, but I scooped it off, and it was fine. This time, I put the browned shanks on a baking sheet in the oven, at 275, while the onions caramelized (45 minutes), and a lot of the fat stayed behind on the baking sheet.
Flour in leeks and onion
I found this recipe to result in a stew where everything tasted the same and overall the flavor was too heavy. I prefer to be able to taste the subtleties of each major ingredient.

2 things…

1) 3 pounds of onions in an 8qt Dutch oven is not going to caramelize in 40 mins. Split the onions and do them in 2 batches.

2) If you’re using store bought stock definitely do the gelatin trick and reduce significantly prior to adding the shanks back in and going to the oven. (Home made stock is definitely best.) Also consider BEEF STOCK instead!

This was easy to prepare and so delicious. We love a good braise and this was a nice alternative to our usual go to’s such as short ribs and coq au vin. We ate heartily with enough leftover to freeze for another day. Loved it!

I thought this recipe was delicious as is, but there was a layer of flavor in the sauce that I felt was missing and that is some kind of pork, be it bacon, pancetta, etc... I would cook it down in the beginning, remove it from the pan, and brown the shanks in the fat.  Then proceed as written adding the bacon back in right before you put it in the oven.

Outstanding recipe! We cute the entire recipe in half for 2 people and still had left overs. We made this with shortribs because the butcher was out of lamb shank and did take the extra time to caramelize the onions. Served over ultra creamy mashed potatoes because why not? The gelatin tip in the notes worked like a charm for our store bought stock and I will keep that tip for future recipes. The sauce was SO luxurious! Truly a 10 out of 10 recipe and would be perfect for a dinner party.

Followed recipe except as noted below. Exceptionally delicious! There were a ton of leeks, but they reduced quite a bit.

We like lamb a lot and this was a big hit.

Because it's quite rich we served with lemon wedges which added a bright note and balanced some of the fatty mouth feel.

Subbed turnip for carrots and polenta for barley. Will definitely make again. Would easily double to freeze some.
Really need someone to convert this to instapot speeds
I wasn’t a big fan of this. Caramelized onions   wine = too much sweetness for me

This was overall great. Only change I made was to use half chicken stock and half beef stock (there was only one container of chicken stock at my grocery store from the more “gelatinous” brand). Start to finish it took about 6 hours. I was moving slow, but the onions took about 30-40 minutes to get to the lighter cooked stage and the barley took over an hour of cooking. The lamb took about 3 hours of cooking to get tender, but my shanks were huge.

Only change I would make is either use less rosemary or use thyme/marjoram instead. I used about 3.5 sprigs of oldish rosemary I had in my fridge, but it still overwhelmed the final product a little bit.
Made this exactly per the recipe and found it to be quite tough despite braising over 3 hours.

Tried this technique, and the result was as advertised — beautiful scrambled eggs. And it’s not complicated or fussy. 

I find myself wishing this website could screen out comments from people who didn’t actually try the recipes.
@malahat I wish this website had a mechanism to print comments. They spark my imagination beyond the posted recipe. NYT, here’s an opportunity for you to engage in impactful growth. Take it.
@Elle Jebelle. Simply cut and comments you find useful and paste them into private notes. You can edit them down from there.

Kenji does it again. These eggs are incredible. I cooked them without telling my wife and tried to play it off like it was just a good batch. They are so good, she caught on. If you don’t want to use this much butter, use less butter.

Like so many NYT Food recipes where I have all the ingredients on hand, I had to try it this morning.  I believe in respecting the recipe and making it as described before I make adjustments.  I made it as described but halved it for just me.  I used a well seasoned cast iron pan.  The eggs were fluffy, creamy and delicious, just like I imagine royalty get from their 5-Star chefs.  I'll play with it now to reduce the amount of butter to protect my arteries like a Weight Watchers chef.

OK, I tried the recipe. I did use a little less butter, but otherwise faithfully followed. I, too, have been making eggs for multiple decades, and in my experience, this technique produces scrambled eggs that are at least a couple of steps above the norm, with less time/effort than the very-low-heat method. For those of you who are set in your egg technique, please move along -- nothing to see here (and no need to comment). But for those open to improvement, I say give this a try.

Making creamy scrambled eggs requires nothing more than a good whisk and... eggs. Zero butter. Even in the pan. If you whisk till foamy, pour into pan and continually move around with a rubber spatula under medium-low heat, things will turn out beautifully.
@rtrach yes but even better w a splash of water! Main thing is keep it moving imho.

Tried this for lunch. Man is there a lot of butter for the amount of eggs it makes.

Pros:VERY DECADENT, great taste.

Cons: a little too rich for my taste.

Verdict: very tasty but probably won't make again unless I'm trying to gain weight for a hypothetical wrestling tournament due to how rich they are.

Good eggs! I doubled the recipe. Corn starch added no flavor. Served with English muffins to wife and kids. Plates cleaned! I think a lot of reviewers are missing the point; it's a technique to get fluffy eggs, quickly.  Like many of the reviewers, my usual eggs use only salt and butter, and turn out nicely, but I need low heat and constant stirring. At larger quantities that can be 15 minutes. This recipe gives me more grace, with quicker cook time.
Just made these and they are *stupid* good 10/10 beautiful

These were the exact right fluffiness.  I think the corn starch and the perfectly hot pan technique is a great idea. If you wanted to use a little less butter, you probably could be ok, but it's really good with the butter.  I made two eggs-- cut the recipe in half and it worked. (I also added a few chopped onions at the end if anyone is interested)

For some reason this recipe is getting a lot of negative attention about being "too much work" but honestly it took me less than 5 minutes and they were the best scrambled eggs I've ever had. I finished them in the time it took for my bagel to toast!
I add a big splash of sparkling water to my eggs. The carbonation fluffs the eggs nicely.
I made this exactly as written. I love you, Kenji. But these eggs where inedible. Soft and creamy but also slimy. They tasted like eggs cooked in corn starch with too much butter.
@Andy 100 percent. They were gross and had a ‘processed’ mouth feel.

This technique is INSANELY GOOD! Ignore the know-it-alls who pan it without even trying it, follow this recipe exactly and you will get the most delicious scrambled eggs ever. I used 8 eggs plus 4 yolks, a full stick of butter and a tbsp. of corn starch to 3 tbsp. of whole milk. Compared to the French/Ramsey method which takes 15-20 minutes, I think these actually taste better. Thank you Kenji!
@Jay I always add extra yolks. Fabulous eggs.
@Jay insanely good—by adding extra yolks and milk. So—a different recipe.

I dip the handle end of a teaspoon into the cornstarch and add that pinch to the eggs while scrambling. It takes all of 1 second, and makes for much creamier, smoother scrambled eggs. There's really no reason to skip this.

This recipe is unnecessarily complicated.  I have been making scrambled eggs for 60 years; it was the first thing I learned how to cook by myself when I was 7 years old.  Scramble the eggs (now I use chopsticks - they mix the whites and yolks more thoroughly); heat butter in a pan; add eggs and move them gently around the pan with a spatula as they cook.  Turn off the heat before the eggs are completely set and slide them onto a plate.  They are fluffy and creamy and yummy.

I use more-or-less the same technique - but I mix the eggs with a little milk in a jug before adding them to the melted butter in the pan. Cooking the eggs takes less than 5 mins - the trick, as you write, is to remove them from the heat before they are completely set, as they continue cooking in their own heat. Overcooking them (a matter of seconds!) is what kills the creaminess...
I always microwave the empty plate I’ll be eating off of for about 45 seconds just before cooking the eggs to ensure they don’t cool too quickly while I eat.
@Bill how does a dry ceramic plate heat in a microwave?  Or are you using some plate with a Microwave a susceptor secreted inside?

I had a dog named Scott who wore a blue ribbon on her tail. That is what you sound like when you have not tried the recipe and insist on giving you thoughts we do not care. Made this recipe as written the first time and it was delicious. The second time I use bacon fat in place of the butter for greasing the pan and it was even better. Made some for my wife and she said it was the best scramble eggs ever and passed on the recipe. Thank Kenji keep writing recipes we who try them love them.
Using refrigerated (hard) butter and a coarse cheese grater works wonders vs using cubes.
Impressed! I had to try it immediately. They are far more delightfully textured than my way of doing eggs.
and far more caloric, and far less likely to be a regular item in your egg portfolio.
I just turned 71 & have been making scrambled eggs since my dad taught me when I was 5 years old. This recipe is like eating soft pillows of wonderful ness!

Okay, I was just as skeptical as everyone, but tried this recipe today using 2 eggs. I skipped the cold butter in the eggs, made my slurry with whole milk and used less than 1 TB butter in the pan.

A revelation! Delicious, creamy, soft, tasty eggs - I am a convert. The volume seemed larger too. So much easier than my old method of cooking a long time over low heat and periodically moving pan off heat.

Try it haters!  You just may be surprised!

The primary benefit of the cubed cold butter is that it significantly increases the "holdability" of the eggs. So if you're going to wolf them down straight away, you probably don't need it as much. But if you're cooking for a crowd and would like to hold the eggs warm for 20 minutes, the butter makes all the difference. And as others have noted, this "higher heat" method does produce more volume than the low-and-slow method, no question.

I asked the grill cook at the Summit Diner in Summit, NJ how he gets his eggs perfect- every single time. 
He said use room temperature eggs.
And lots of bacon grease on the flat-top griddle. 
I've been all over the world and had scrambled eggs in many places.
But none so delicious- albeit un-healthy as the Summit Diner.
Ok, this tangle of goodness only get into my belly once or twice every six months- that's ok, right?

@Warren Bobrow, 6x Author, Cannabis Alchemist. The Summit Diner is my favorite diner.  I love to take visitors there for the best breakfast in NJ.  Thanks for divulging their method for perfect scrambled eggs.  

P.S.  J Kenji rocks!

I wonder if all the people that unabashedly tout their own 'superior' techniques for a dish do actually cook the recipe that they are so quick to criticize and then compare the end result to their own methods? Like many others I also had a technique for what I considered to be wonderfully creamy scrambled eggs, but I cooked this recipe anyway and it really was wonderful - and quite a bit better than my own.
The extra egg whites can be used in meringue and whiskey sours.
@Kevinto complete a perfect breakfast!

I ran across this recipe a few days ago and made the eggs this morning.  To all the naysayers, I disagree.  The butter and slurry were prepped in the time it took the pan to heat up, so no extra time at all.  The eggs were easy, and far fluffier and creamier then my usual efforts, even those made with a bit of milk.
Super delicious and definitely will be the method going forward.

I don't disagree that it's possible to make amazing eggs without potato starch and butter and all that — but seriously, try this before you say that your eggs are perfect the way they are. You just might change your mind. And although it sounds like a hassle, it literally took me 90 seconds longer to prep than just making eggs the "usual" way. And to make it easier, it's not really necessary to cube all the butter — only half of it. The rest goes into the pan to foam, so  cubing not needed.

Made these for breakfast this morning. Whole thing took about 5 minutes (10 if you include me nervously double checking the recipe and trying to do quick maths-- I only made 2 eggs). They are PERFECT. These eggs are so silky and creamy and delicious. I did not know that scrambled eggs could be so luxurious. If you either don't have the time or the ability for lots of whisking (I myself have an old ice cream scooping injury) this technique is for you.

Wow, I was skeptical because I thought I made good scrambled eggs already, but these are the best scrambled eggs I've ever had. They taste velvety and rounded like the chickens had a good life with lots of extracurriculars. I felt I got more yield from 2 measly medium-size eggs. Used half the potato starch and water for 2 eggs and less than a tablespoon of butter.
I used tapioca and halved the recipe with equally great results.

Some of the early commenters may have missed the preface or are assuming everyone is an amazing home cook. A (forgiving) scrambled egg recipe without the fuss indeed. https://www.nytimes.com/2021/02/19/dining/perfect-scrambled-eggs.html

What is the difference between an amazing home cook and a regular one? Its a matter of following directions and after an attempt or two you're set. You're never going to do a recipe perfectly first attempt.

Looking up recipes online for simple dishes like eggs often leaves you with an awful breakfast. Its like looking up how to make peanut butter and jelly.

Look up a more basic recipe on YT by someone like Gordon Ramsay - fantastic dishes with little work and no skill required!

Made this exactly as the recipe called for and they are fantastic!  The naysayers on here didn’t actually try the recipe. It’s worth the few extra little tips and tricks to get these results. Kenji never disappoints!

Like some of the other commenters here, I can make perfectly good scrambled eggs without corn starch or half a stick of butter. These eggs are better than perfectly good. In the words of someone I served them to, “These eggs are insane.”

If you are even a slightly curious person, make them before commenting.

I just made this recipe by Kenji and it's fabulous, just like all of his recipes.  I know I can trust any recipe by Kenji and his cookbook is incredible.  As a former Home Economics major (yes that degree existed once upon a time) I can only say that if I'd had Kenji's book at the time I would have enjoyed my foods/nutrition classes so much more and understood why I needed so much chemistry for that degree.

With this level of butter you definitely need some toast or salad with it.  I made this to eat straight up and found it pretty greasy.  Cornstarch is a great trick and I'll probably use that going forward when I do larger batches of scrambled eggs.

Off the charts--the scrambled eggs of my dreams, silky, tender, delicious. And I used only half the butter. We ate them with warm homemade challah.
These are in no way an "everyday" dish for us but I will return to them for a special breakfast or supper as an easy comfort dish. Pure golden buttery egg love.

So...just made these. I increased recipe ratios for six eggs. Otherwise, I followed the recipe and instructions to the letter. 

Honestly, the best scrambled eggs of my life. My GF agrees. Couldn’t be happier with the texture, fluffiness, and creaminess. 

Next time, if I make 6 again, I will reduce the butter from 6 tbs, to 5. There was enough buttery glisten left on the serving plates that obviously didn’t didn’t absorb, that I think I can get way with that.

Made these with olive oil instead of butter. Imparts a wonderful taste. Rather than the water try white wine in the pan. Then drink a few glasses of the wine. Forget the eggs. Use a chocolate chip cookie. Then drink the rest of the wine. Heaven.

I followed the recipe using cornstarch.  I won't make it again.  Fluffy, yes, but the texture was unpleasant;  slimy doesn't quite describe the feel. 

But try it anyway.  You probably have the ingredients, and you might like the results.

Tried, but never again.  Of course, they're tasty - two tablespoons of butter per serving would make anything taste good.  That's over 200 calories just for the butter and 23 grams of fat, close to half the daily recommendation from Cleveland Clinic.   

Maybe I'll wait until my 90th birthday for the next serving!

Made this once as written - too much butter! So rich! Made it again with half the butter, but I used half & half instead of water for the slurry (as suggested in the recipe tips). Perfect! And for the folks from Hawaii, who love their scrambled eggs over rice, I added soy sauce to the mixture before cooking. Even more perfect!! This is a keeper, as is this chef's method for the perfect boiled eggs.

He says that "For creamier eggs, you can replace the water with milk or half-and-half."  But it's only a tablespoon and a half of water in the recipe!  What kind of super-concentrated milk is being used :) !
Really wish people would try a recipe before commenting. Great recipe for “loose” scrambled eggs. The butter makes it very rich. A nice change from my normal scrambled eggs.

Made these for breakfast this morning. Would probably cut down on the butter because it was a little too much for me, but the recipe was foolproof and got me fluffy, delicious eggs quicker than cooking on a low flame like the typical scrambled eggs recipe. Again, as other commenters said, don't comment on the recipe saying your method is better/less complicated if you didn't even try this. It's awesome!
That's a lot of butter for 4 little eggs.
Use Jumbo.  Problem solved.

My mother made the silkiest, butter-infused scrambled eggs for 11 children on an almost daily basis before we went to school. She did this effortlessly. With no slurries just a double boiler. For some reason all of this mayhem about scrambled eggs reminded me of her standing in high heels dropping egg after egg into the double boiler along with a large knob of butter and some room temperature cream. They remain the finest ones that I have ever eaten. Thanks for the memory, but not the recipe!
@Elena, the Alice B.  Tolklas recipe for scrambled eggs had about half a pound of butter. Maybe your mom used her cookbook.

Way too much butter here. And I am pro-butter!  A Tbsp per egg is too much of a good thing.

Just mix the eggs as usual, heat your pan as usual, add a little butter (1 tsp.) and a dash of olive oil. Once the pan is ready add MOST of the beaten eggs reserving a bit. When the eggs are nearly done add the remaining egg liquid, give a quick stir and serve.

Followed the recipe to the T (not really that hard anyway) though used cornstarch. Came out absolutely perfect and my girlfriend who was very skeptical until the first bite was totally impressed!

The many steps might look laborious but it really comes together in about 5 minutes. Definitely worth it!
Yes, you can cook scrambled eggs in many ways, often easier than this recipe. But! They will not be these eggs, which are seriously the best I've ever cooked. Wonderful recipe and technique.
I don't like eggs and these eggs were great. I raise chickens, so have a surplus of eggs and I am always trying to find good ways to make them. I will definitely make this recipe again.

An earlier NYT recipe for "perfect" scrambled eggs called for adding a splash of half-and-half to the eggs and cooking the blend over very low heat, pushing and folding, for a few MINUTES.  
  I add a large pinch of shredded cheddar. Diners will not taste the cheddar, only the added flavor dimension.
  With zero fanfare or introductions, I served it to my wife and waited to see how or even if she would respond to this novel recipe.  She looked up and said, "These are the best eggs I ever had".

Lovely recipe. I was never a big fan of quick egg items until I came across this. On my second attempt, I decided to spice it up a bit to satisfy my Bengali tongue. I just added a bit of black pepper powder and paprika in the mix, along with some mirepoix mix veggies. And it just got better. Toasted the breads on the skillet after the egg was done, so that's a good use of the buttery grease. With some blue cheese on the bread, turned out to be a satisfying meal on a weekday morning. :)

We made this recipe last Sunday for "Brunch".  It was AMAZING!!!
Moist and fluffy eggs without being runny or undercooked!  Love all the butter!

Prior to learning this clever technique of protecting the egg protein (from high heat) with a starch slurry, I did not really like scrambled eggs at all. And yes, I had made them the ways all the people discussing their supposed simpler alternative methods made them. What this ingenious recipe will make is something far more delicious and delicate that what you get with those other methods. It's not too much work- it's actually quite simple. Just give it a shot.

I agree with others, don’t comment if you haven’t made it.  I appreciate the tip on ensuring the Pan is hot.  I made half the recipe and unfortunately, due to allergies, I needed to use earth balance instead of butter.  These are the best scrambled eggs I’ve made in 60 years. As a Girl Scout, I also made scrambled eggs at 7 yo, lol.

I'm a fan of Kenji, I've been to chef school and cooked professionally (briefly), I respect the work of chefs, and I agree that if you haven't actually cooked this recipe just as he instructed you have no business criticizing it.  I just made this as instructed down to using potato starch and high-quality eggs from my own free-ranging Maran hens, and my opinion is that I would not make them again.  Just too heavy w butter for my taste, but they may be better on unbuttered toast or grits.

Made the recipe exactly as written. Like many people, we are connoisseurs of perfect scrambled eggs so I couldn’t resist trying it. The amount of butter is too rich for me on a daily basis but these are definitely special weekend scrambled eggs! The texture and butter almost give the effect of the eggs being mixed with cheese. Wow!
Simple enough and rewarding for the effort. Different texture and feel, but fluffy and soft
It worked perfectly, and is perfectly simple. We don't like underdone eggs. These are just right and still soft. Per my 90 year old mom, who is dealing with dementia: "Would eat here again." :-)

75 years old and been cooking since I was 10 with scrambled eggs being the first thing I cooked. You know that expression, can't teach an old dog new tricks. Well, I'm here to tell you that is hogwash, at least for this recipe. I've been very dissatisfied with my scrambled eggs for quite a while. After trying this, I'm now excited about scrambled eggs again. Phenomenally good eggs. Decadent, absolutely. Restaurant quality (good restaurant) eggs that makes you go, WOW!
I’m 8 and I made the extra creamy eggs. They tasted like cream, which tasted very great, and were just creamy enough.

This butter trick may be why cheesy scrambled eggs turn out so nicely (cheese added mid way, or early-ish). The same trick with the melting fat keeping parts cool. 

On this basis, pre-grated cheese, which is bound often with potato starch, should work even better.

Made a double batch in seasoned cast iron skillet using tapioca starch slurried with milk. I used half the amount of butter, and they were great! What a delightful recipe, producing wonderfully tender eggs in a very short time. My young son, who despises quick-cooked scrambled eggs because of the texture, pronounced these "very good". What a cool technique - thanks for sharing, Kenji!

Worked great! For the scale-up nerds out there:

- 1/2 t cornstarch/egg dissolved in water at ratio of 3.75:1
- 1 T butter cubes/egg (w/about 1/2 T reserved for coating the pan before cooking) 

Made an omelet w/pre-sautéed ingredients (ham cubes, spinach, mushrooms, green onions, mini bell peppers), some finely shredded cheddar cheese … added cheese just as eggs started to set, then folded in other goodies just as eggs looked nearly done). #Winner

Like most other home cooks, I’ve scrambled a gazillion eggs in my life and there is nothing “wrong” with my regular approach. But I tired this recipe, as written, and these eggs are wildly good. 

If the recipe doesn’t appeal to you, don’t try it and dob’t comment. But if you love discovering new ways to make and elevate old classics, give this a try; you won’t be disappointed!
What kind of sorcery is this? Best scrambled eggs ever. I never thought I needed a recipe for that but gave this a shot, and glad I did!

I made this for lunch today.  Almost foolproof very fluffy eggs very quickly.  We liked it, but, I still consider very small curd and super moist, my ideal scrambled eggs.  And that takes time.  This reminded me of Japanese tenshin eggs because of the texture and addition of potato starch.  It is always made with potato starch called katakuriko for fluffiness and rise in making round omlette very much like this texture.
Has anyone tried Arrowroot instead of Corn Starch. I use it for thickening my recipes.

Made this today. It took about five minutes total, from slurry to sliding them out of the pan. It’s really very simple. I did cut the butter in half, thinking two tablespoons for two people was adequate. Verdict...absolutely delicious. I’m a convert. As soon as I drop these five confinement pounds, I’m going full out for the butter.

I just made this recipe and am eating the eggs now as I type this. Wow. I had believed that my scrambled egg game had no room for improvement, and I was wrong. I followed the recipe but cut down for 2 eggs, and only used 1 tbsp of cold butter cut into 1/4" cubes per recipe. These eggs were the softest, fluffy and buttery, perfection. The addition of the corn starch was profound, I am converted to this technique.

Made the first time with cornstarch - the second time with tapioca flour and both were a revelation.  The pockets of butter were delights.

I did end up cutting down the butter in subsequent makings and it was still outstanding.

Definitely recommend doing the water method for the skillet - it evenly heats up the pan so the eggs cook evenly.

This has become my go-to scrambled egg recipe! It comes together super fast and is so good! The taste is rich and buttery and the texture is like sunny yellow velvet! 

If the extra step of cornstarch and chopped butter is too much trouble first thing I the morning, just prep the butter and make the slurry the night before. But honestly? It took less than 2 minutes for those extra steps and it is soooooo worth it! Thanks Kenji!

I thought these eggs were spectacularly silky/creamy when cooked as written.  Most restaurants that I have been to do not make them this good.  And, the procedure was only two very small steps more than I would otherwise do.  This one's a keeper!

I thought I made pretty good scrambled eggs although I never got them as golden and fluffy as this technique does. These were ah-may-ZING! My only tweak was to add an extra egg. Served over bread, toasted with gruyere cheese and prosciutto, alongside a steamy latte. My teenage son LOVED them. Happy New Year!

Been making it this way for years except without adding any of the butter to the eggs or water to the pan.  Can't eat eggs any other way now!  I do let my butter brown a bit before adding the eggs--SO good, but kinda ugly.  I crack my eggs into a ball jar, then add salt and the cornstarch slurry, lid it and shake it up til frothy.  I pour it from the jar into the hot brown butter and go from there.

Fantastic recipe for super creamy eggs! I usually go the low & slow route a la Samin Nosrat’s method and those always turns out with a lovely custard-esque texture & taste great but I tried this recipe today and the eggs were Delicious so this might be my new go to. For reference, it was just me so I just used 2 eggs, 1 tbs skim milk, 1 tbs cornstarch, salt, 1 1/2 pats of butter (1 for mixture & 1/2 for pan with the water).

I am a lousy cook.  But these eggs were a hit in my house.  My husband, who usually does not complain but never compliments, said "What did you do to these eggs?  They are delicious."

I am a convert.  These are our eggs from now on...

I've been doing this for years, and yes, it does create delightfully creamy eggs. I found I can get the same effect after watching the episode of "The Bear" where Sydney makes Natalie an omelet. Whisking the eggs through a strainer leaves a residue of what I can only call "goo" in the strainer. That seems to be what makes eggs tough. A final whisk in the bowl beneath and you get delightfully smooth scrambled eggs.
Low and slow with a splash of heavy cream work perfectly every time. Why all the awful additives and tons of butter?

@llebon I dislike eggs scrambled slowly over low heat. I find them dense and sticky and wet as opposed to silky and delightful. This method produces something better, to my palate and many others’. You do you, but don’t tell the rest of us we’re wrong.
I’ve done this with just hot water. Whisk eggs briskly, then right before cooking them, add just a splash of warm/hot water. They cook up fluffier. It’s an old diner trick.
I tried it. I liked it. Fluffy but not gooey.

I have been making delicious scrambled eggs for years and I agree with most of the advice in this recipe except one thing. No milk, cream, crème fraîche or even butter added to the eggs. I whisk in full fat sour cream. This makes for a deliciously fluffy scrambled egg with no chance of being watery or broken. Also, if you like a little cheese in your eggs, the sour cream binds to the cheese and the result is the flavor of the cheese without it separating from the egg.

Lovely! I had a nonstick pan at hand, so cut way back the amount for the pan (didn't skimp on what went into the eggs, however!). Stirred in 2 chopped scallions, reserving some for sprinkling on afterwards. Was skeptical of the small amount of salt, but it was just right—any more would have masked the subtle richness of the beautiful pasture-raised eggs that I used. I don't know why anybody would kvetch—prep time really was nothing, and the process of cooking is half the enjoyment of eating!
My family felt there was too much butter -
I made this as written and next time I will cut the butter in half. I found it a little heavy and far too greasy… delicious but greasy.
Same, maggie.  I had to use paper towels to dab off the grease like I do with the bacon which is a bit counterintuitive.  lol.  2 tbsps is pefect imo (or 1 tbsp for a personal serving).

I've made these a few times, stuck to the recipe exactly as written.... These eggs are amazing! I added a thin layer of cheese right before they were done cooking, then wrapped in a soft tortilla, added hot sauce. Amazing breakfast!

To paraphrase long ago radio food personality Arthur Schwartz about deep frying:  even Styrofoam cooked in enough butter would taste good.  It amazes me that cooks try to get as much fat into a recipe instead of trying to minimize fat and not sacrifice the dish. I made this with less than half the fat.  It came out very light and fluffy and still buttery.  The irony is that I like my scrambled eggs denser, like there is actually something of substance in my mouth,
Instead of butter, I use cream cheese with chives to create super creamy scrambled eggs.
Corn starch?  No. Nutritionally useless (in fact, kind of bad for you) and it adds a sliminess I noticed.  Low heat, lots of butter (no, butter is not bad for you) and salt is the way to go.

So easy. So good. I enjoyed these in part because they remind me of my mother’s scrambled eggs, which I have never been able to replicate. She died before I was old enough to learn any kitchen skills, so I am especially grateful! 
Regarding another comment wishing that NYT would stop allowing comments from folks who haven’t actually made the recipe, I mostly agree, but there’s often so much to laugh about, I think I’d miss the comedy!
forget the corn starch just use cream cheese and they are creamy and delicious. you came wn use flavored cream cheese such as scallion.

This seems a tad pointless to me. You can get good creamy scrambled eggs by reserving half the butter you use (and four tablespoons seems far too much to me) , removing the eggs from the heat while they are still creamy and stirring in the reserved butter, which prevents them from cooking further.

Overly complicated. There's no need to whisk it beforehand like for an omelette. The way I've made scrambled eggs all my life is very simple and comes out much better. Just put butter (I use the I Can't Believe It's Not Butter because less unhealthy). Let it melt slightly to grease for a second then add eggs. Boom. Just stir/scramble it up as it's cooking. Done quickly. Sometimes, if you're feeling fancy, you can add fried onions and turmeric. It's called Akuri, which has a nice flavor.
Has anyone tired this with olive oil instead of butter, and if so how much and when did you incorporate it?
I recently tried adding some creme fraiche to the eggs just as they were setting, decadent. I'm looking forward to trying this recipe
Using milk instead of water also makes the eggs creamier!

True, but at the expense of taste. I find that the addition of milk further dilutes the taste of already bland eggs and also makes them look pale instead of golden. I used to add milk, but now substitute grated Pecorino Romano, the way my Italian-American mother did.
Add a tablespoon or two of ricotta when scrambling eggs. Same result, definitely easier.

Amazing, decadent and easy to execute. For the sake of my arterial health I will try to limit to once a month, which will be quite the challenge.

As someone else has already noted, I finally understand why restaurant and hotel scrambled eggs are so addictive.

Just tried this recipe for the first time. The flavor is outstanding. Best scrambled eggs I’ve had, however the amount of butter left a greasy/filmy feeling in my mouth. I may try halfing the butter next time.
I made these exactly as directed and they were PERFECT!!!! Best scrambled eggs I’ve had in my life.

Been making scrambled eggs this way for years — perfect, foolproof scrambled eggs on the workweek breakfast table in about 2 minutes total. I do not add butter to the slurry— I use a tablespoon or less for the pan — but the more butter the creamier and more luscious the end product.  It’s chemistry folks.  The starch slows down the development of albumen, which is what Gordon Ramsay accomplishes by technique.  Don’t be tempted to increase the starch - a little does the trick.
my problem with this recipe is that i dont want to eat 2T. of butter in my 2 eggs. the trade off of extra fat for creamier eggs is not one i'm willing to make more than once (to say i tried it).
Maybe this is nice for supermarket eggs (I did make this), but not for local free range eggs, if you can get them. All of the butter and starch just blurs the taste of truly high quality eggs.
4 tablespoons of butter for 4 eggs!  Pass the Lipitor.
Try replacing the starch with ricotta cheese.  Mighty creamy, tried it this morning.

At the risk of being one of "those" people - questioning the need for the slurry.

I read somewhere that butter is what actually does the job; cold butter melts as the eggs cook, blocking proteins from coagulating too much. I put thin film of oil in a pan, add the beaten egg, and then add a bunch of little butter shavings I whittle directly from a paring knife into the pan. Restaurant quality effect achieved, and no extra dishes.

May have to do an experiment to see if the slurry matters.
This recipe is better than "restaurant quality." The reason is the starch. It binds the water, providing moisture without wetness, and interferes with protein coagulation. The results are amazing.

Follow this recipe to the letter and it will be brilliant. Do us all a favor and don't post unless you've actually made it, following the very simple, clear instructions. If the amount of butter bothers you, you could always experiment with cutting back, but then it's not this recipe. It's your recipe. Thanks, Kenji!
Made twice nice. Great, fluffy eggs. It's OK to use less butter. The police will not arrest you. I miss the full-egg flavor when nothing is added to the mix. Maybe it needs green onions?

I like what I consider essence of egg scrambled egg "British?" style.  Lots of butter,  well beaten eggs (use more than usual  -- as they condense.  Slowly stirred as if a lumpy Hollandaise -- the smallest of curds.  There are many many ways to make scrambled eggs!! (I did not care for the addition of cornstarch -- and yes they were smooth  -- but I rather think I tasted the starch!)

Not possible! I make the best scrambled eggs - learned from Cook's. Add a small splash of water to 6 eggs and whip for 30 seconds. Then heat the pan to medium-low with a pat of butter. When the butter starts foaming a bit, whip the eggs another 15 seconds, then add to the pan. When the edges of the eggs start whitening and sticking to the pan, use a flat wooden spoon/spatula to move them around the pan - not stir; not poke at. I push and lift, push and lift until they are done. Et voila.
Adding this much butter to just about anything will make it delicious and creamy. Four tablespoons of butter for four eggs! Wow!

Ever since the final scene in Stanley Tucci's  "The Big Night", I feel like there is only one way to really give scrambled eggs the justice they deserve.  I loved the simplicity of this simple meal — a heated pan, olive oil, three eggs beaten, a pinch of salt, lightly fried (with a little light mixing), turned once and then turned out onto a plate with a (hand torn) chunk of fresh, crusty bread.  Heaven!
Also, starch absolutely excels at scrambled egg recipes that involve watery vegetables like tomatoes.   It soaks the juice right up, when it would normally trickle out of the eggs.

That's an insane ratio of butter to eggs, and the starch isn't necessary. For creamy scrambled eggs all you need is patience. Low heat--the lower the better--a little bit of butter, keep the eggs moving all the time, take them off the heat when they're no longer runny. That's all you need for spreadably creamy and absolutely delicious scrambled eggs. I salt my eggs and like to mix in a bit of yogurt, but that's entirely optional.

I almost never watch the video if they have one.  But it feels like I've met Mr. López-Alt (so fun!) AND it would eliminate repetitive questions and comments on the thread.
I understand some of the folks complaining that others don't use the recipe exactly as it's written, it must be frustrating.  However, as someone who grew up with Hungry Man TV dinners and Little Smokies sausages, I never learned how to substitute ingredient  At 62, I came here to learn. Thanks to ya'll for the info!

I made these this morning with the exact same ingredients and it didn’t turn out well. 
As an accomplished eggchef, and fan of most NY times recipes, I did some introspection afterwards and figured out why - I didn’t turn up the temperature.
Very important to use high heat. I, and many readers, I suspect, usually cook eggs at low-medium heat, and I didn’t break the habit today. The starch slurry needs much higher heat. Can’t wait to try these tomorrow morning and I will not gloss over the instructions that clearly say “medium-high heat”!
A dollop of whipped cream cheese stirred in with eggs is another way to get fluffy,  savory scrambled eggs.
*Is* it the potato starch… or is it the butter? I’ll go have another sip of Vitameatavegamin and give it a good ponder while the “nutrients” kick in.
@Buck380    Vitameatavegemin!!! Brought me back to my childhood. Thank you. We could use more Lucy in our world right now.

Phenomenal! My usual scrambled eggs are not in this universe. I halved the butter
and they taste so creamy. Complements a bitter green salad very well.

This recipe looked like a real find, unfortunately it did not deliver for us. The amount of butter was a serious overkill, it made the eggs way too fatty. I’d rather increase the fat by adding some cheese. It’s less fat, and it adds more flavor and softness. Adding the starch - I used corn starch - gave the eggs - excusez le mot - an almost slimy undertone, which my wife and I both did not appreciate.

I did appreciate the hack of dropping a tablespoon of water in your pan in order to know when the temperature is right. Every recipe you make leaves you with more experience, whether you like the end result or not. So, thank you for this recipe.

I am a great fan of scrambled eggs.  Usually, I add nothing at all, just 100% fresh, lightly beaten  eggs, no oil or butter. I  just tried this recipe and was disappointed. The cornstarch took the freshness and natural creaminess out of the eggs and did not produce the nice curls I usually get.  The texture was very slightly floury in spite of the butter, which added nothing.  Perhaps I am spoiled living in France where you can get very fresh excellent eggs which need no improvement.
Warm the plate so it doesn’t chill your eggs!
A few drops of lemon added to the mix just before cooking insures fluffiness.Instead of butter added I use half and half. Works fine

I agree with Andrea and rtrach; eggs, a little( about 2-3 oz) milk or cream, a wisk and whip it good. Butter if the spirit moves ya.
Sauted peppers,onions mushies if you have time.
Can one get away with using a cast-iron or carbon-steel skillet here? Really trying to steer clear of nonstick.

Rubber spatula, hot pan, (test pan hotness with water till it evaporates) butter in a pan, salt and a little butter, tad of potato starch, whisk, move eggs lovenly with spatula + amazing difference, deserves wine!.

The secret to creamy scrambled eggs is cooking in olive oil (not butter). Heat olive oil in pan while you beat eggs in bowl. Add salt if desired. When pan is hot add the eggs and stir with spatula until desired consistency is achieved. Remove from heat immediately and enjoy. Quick, easy and delicious every time.

This is great but I learned a simple way that works for me, add the butter half way through the cooking. Just as it melts the eggs are done.  Whole milk or half & half but not too much or they will be runny.  Simple perfection.
Made these as written and they were fabulous!! Not sure I can handle this much butter most mornings though, unfortunately.
Try Jacque pepin’s method, keep moving it on and off the heat and mix a bit off reserved egg  &cream at the end and cook off the heat .
Salmon Nosrat suggests a little squirt of fresh lemon in the eggs as you scramble them in a very similar recipe. Really good; maybe worth a try with these.
My mom used to make scrambled eggs with cubes of cream cheese mixed in.  Always came out very creamy.

Reading comments prior to testing is quite enjoyable and often beneficial. But in order to respect the cook's instructions and rationale I rarely alter the recipe on its test run. (I often attach helpful and promising notes for possible use later, depending on the original outcome.) So I will use the butter and the starch and report back later! Thank you all for your thoughtful contributions. My personal cooking adventures are enlivened and enriched by your spirited participation in this forum.
Ugh. I can taste the chalky cornstarch in every bite. Very unpleasant.

If you're finding the recipe overly complicated, I recommend reading the linked article which explains WHY they wanted a recipe with these properties. (Mainly, as Kevin said, because it's fast and less prone to sudden overcooking.)

It turned out perfectly. It was easy.

I know many are whining and touting their simpler recipes. Folks, try it. And if you don’t want to, that’s fine too. Just stop complaining about others who do enjoy trying differently.

I think the world of Kenji. Followed recipe to a T. Liked what the potato starch did to the texture. And if you truly don’t have more than 2 minutes to spare, this method is fine. But - as a scrambled egg aficionado- I think heat itself affects the taste of an egg. IMHO, the low and slow method renders a creamier, even better tasting result.
Made this as written with exception of all the butter. Used only 1 tbs in egg mixture and 1 in pan. Excellent results. Would make great eggs for egg sandwich. Will definitely make again.
Just made these with much less butter and they were amazing - people need to stop hating! I can’t wait to make these for my partner and pretend like I’ve perfected scrambled eggs.
What about using arrowroot instead of cornstarch?
I'm 65, been cooking all my life and didn't realize that I didn't know how to really make eggs until I made this recipe. Perfection!

Made as written: luscious. My sauté pan is pretty curvy, 10" is measured top edge to edge. Cubes of butter finally melted just as eggs were done. Served on toasted unbuttered rye bread was a good contrast to the rich, slightly unctuous eggs. Maybe a touch less butter in the pan next time.
I made this with two eggs and half the butter.  It was good and very quick.  As a personal preference,  I would cut butter down even more.  I love butter, but this was too much for me.

I've made these twice, once for me and once for my boyfriend. We're on the opposite end of the scrambled egg spectrum - I like mine barely cooked, and he likes his almost browned. He thought these eggs were fantastic, since this recipe produces eggs that can be both very cooked and still tender. I was less enthusiastic, I think because I cooked mine so little that the starch didn't have time to fully cook and gelatinize, leaving them a little powdery, even using tapioca instead of cornstarch.
I followed the recipe to the letter, and it calls for way too much butter. Four tbsp is way too much for four eggs. They end up swimming in butter, and the taste of it is too strong when cooked.

I eat two soft scrambled eggs for breakfast each morning and was excited for a new technique. Unfortunately I think this recipe has too much butter, making them far too rich. I thought they were almost inedible.
Yes, there are many ways to make pleasing scrambled eggs, but these are unusual: shapely and pillowy. I'd call them butterbomb eggs, and have them as a treat.
A chef once taught me the key is not moving the eggs around in the pan too much. Slow, low and gentle.
Nice technique and will do again but too much butter. Left an oil slick on the plate.

Even better not to whisk the eggs beforehand. Just drop them whole in the hot skillet and let the whites set until they lose their translucence. You may help the process by spreading out the whites (NOT the yolks) around the skillet with a spatula. When the whites are set, break open the yolks and quickly scramble them with the whites, remove, and serve. This keeps the yolks from cooking too fast and keeps the eggs remarkably moist while avoiding any sliminess due to undercooked whites. Trust me

"perfect weekday eggs without the fuss?"  Cutting a half stick of butter into 1/4" cubes and whisking corn starch into water until no lumps remain is "fuss." Thoroughly enjoyable, but admittedly not quite perfect, soft-scrambled eggs can be had every day with less fuss than this.

I get very good results whisking the eggs thoroughly with a dollop of half and half, cooked in enough butter to generously coat the pan, 1-2 tbsp. Melt butter, reduce heat to medium before adding eggs. Toss about a minute, remove from heat. Use prewarmed plates if possible.

Definitely not too much work. Definitely too much butter. Used 3Tbl for 4 eggs and it felt greasy. I felt greasy eating them. Cooking a little water in the pan as a test for the proper 212F temp was helpful- and they cooked beautifully- maybe just cut down on butter!
You lost me at corn starch. IMO the secret to good scrabbled eggs is low heat and patience, but I also like a little half and half or light cream in the mix.
Too much butter for every day use by healthy people. Dangerous suggestions.

Every commenter saying they've cooked perfectly "creamy" eggs since they were toddlers, by throwing eggs in a pan and pushing them around with a spatula, have never eaten truly creamy scrambled eggs in their lives.

Unlike some of the readers, I did not feel compelled to attempt to improve upon the recipe before trying it.  I followed it exactly, other than using my regular skillet. The eggs were delicious and not really an excessive amount of work required. It delighted me to use my late grandmother's gravy base shaker for making the starch and water slurry. I will definitely use this recipe again.

We followed the recipe perfectly.  All I tasted was butter.  Not worth the time, fuss, or the calories/cholesterol for me.   I like my scrambled eggs with additions of leftover veggies or fresh spinach with feta or another cheese, so guess I'll stick with my own tastes.

I need to start by saying: I don't like scrambled eggs. I've never liked scrambled eggs. For my entire life, the texture of scrambled eggs have made me nauseous. I'm just not a huge egg fan in general. So it means something when I say this is the best egg recipe I have ever tried. I LOVE this. These scrambled eggs are so good, I could have them for breakfast every day.  I followed the recipe exactly. Would not change a thing. It's perfect. I look forward to having this for breakfast regularly.

At 80 years old, I've made thousands of scrambled eggs in my lifetime, and these were the best yet. The brand of eggs and the color of the shells makes no difference, but the technique makes all the difference in the world. Thanks for this life-changing revelation.
My husband is the Sunday breakfast chef, eggs being his menu, and he tried the water in the pan and butter method this time.  Excellent and did not take any extra time.  Really.
Interestingly, IHOP may have been ahead of the game.  They put pancake batter (i.e, starch) into their eggs which gives them a similar look and texture as these eggs.

Way, way too much butter. This half a stick of butter for four eggs! I made the recipe. The eggs taste good, but I could only eat a few bites. My 16-year-old son, who normally inhales eggs, could not eat all his portion. It was simply too rich.
I don't have the patience to soft scramble eggs. I followed the recipe (using corn starch) and these are by far the best fast scrambled eggs I've ever made

Genius!  I love butter but I did slim it down by half and still these eggs are so creamy and delicate and delicious!  But do yourself a favor. have the bread toasted and the fruit sliced and everything ready to go because these babies come together quick.  Perfect weekday or weekend breakfast.

When I read the recipe the butter cubing and cornstarch slurry sounded like too many steps to be worth it. But in practice it’s really fast. I’ve made the recipe a couple times with 1/4 the butter and it’s still been pretty good.

Made as directed, half batch, 2 eggs, 1TSP cornstarch and 2TSP water. Salt and pepper in the pan after pouring in the egg mixture. Excellent outcome. I have an infrared thermometer (less than $30) and put my eggs in the pan at 225F over low heat. Steady gentle stirring with a rubber spatula took 2 minutes to reach a proper set. Much of the flavor is due to the butter but that's okay with me.
I make good eggs, this gives a little more leeway, and is far faster than my old method. I will use it for larger batches.
God bless butter, god bless these eggs and, most importantly, god bless J. Kenji López-Alt
Just followed this recipe as written - what a decadent treat!  As recommended, it is important to take the pan off the heat when the eggs are almost set.
I thought adding the water to the pan while it was heating up before adding the eggs did help keep the eggs moist and the pan not too hot. So I liked that new trick!

I found though soft, the starch made the more underdone egg a bit glue-y or gelatinous, which had an unpleasant feel on the tongue. But I enjoyed how the curds gathered in the pan. I might choose much less starch and butter.
OMG.  My whole life [4,000 years of it] I have tried every recipe for THE best scrambled eggs.  My search has --FINALLY-- ended.  Thanks Mandy Lee and Kenji.

Made this with potato starch and room temp eggs from out backyard chickens. By far the best scrambled eggs I've ever had. Of course now I'm going to try to reduce the butter but I'm guessing that this recipe as published will remain the gold standard.

Wow!  I have been making mediocre scrambled eggs for decades and never had them so light and fluffy and deliciously creamy as I just made with this recipe and technique.  Thank you.  They came out like scrambled eggs at the Ritz Carton Sunday Brunch or the Peninsula Hotel.  This quality has eluded me for years.  So simple and so good. This was a gift.
The texture of the eggs was lovely, but much too rich for me—I’m going to try cutting the amount of butter in half next time

I like marbled flecks of white throughout the eggs, so I stir them lightly with a fork just until the yolks and whites are swirled together a bit. They will get further worked by the spatula while they're in the pan. 

Not to take away from the critical role of the butter here, but once in a while scrambling eggs in good olive oil is a refreshing change of flavor and texture.

These are the creamiest eggs I have ever eaten! I've done butter (or evoo) whisked in before, and it's great. I used tapioca flour - curious to do some side-by-sides to really see the impact of that step.
I did not have potato or tapioca starch, so used cornstarch.  1/2 tsp per egg seemed too much using cornstarch as the eggs were gummy, not silky - next time I will try 1/4 tsp per egg.
Would arrowroot work as a thickener?
A tablespoon of butter per egg takes a high quality protein and transforms it into a fatty sludge.  I don't need to make this recipe to know that - the ingredient list speaks for itself.
Great recipe! A go-to breakfast for my wife and me is a ton of vegetables scrambled in eggs. The cornstarch slurry works WONDERFULLY well in that concoction. Thanks for the tip.

We found these to taste more like rich, stodgy polenta than eggs. Yes, the texture was creamy and they would probably be great for large batches to prevent rubberiness in a serving dish, but we wanted scrambled eggs right from the pan and these did not taste like eggs. A disappointment for us.
Excellent results!

I’ve tried a number of *perfect* scrambled egg recipes, including one copying a famous chef and following his video.  I had come to the conclusion that I just didn’t like scrambled eggs.  Out of curiosity I tried the NYT recipe shown here.  It’s very easy, less fuss than many others; it worked perfectly and tastes amazing.

Way too much butter.  I could barely taste the eggs over the richness of the butter.  The good texture of the eggs is offset by the greasy feeling in my mouth afterwards. Not to mention this has 17 g of saturated fat, more than Ben & Jerry's!

I have a sunny side egg for breakfast every morning so I tried this instead today. Made it with just one egg and about 1/2 tablespoon of butter. Texture and flavor were absolutely perfect. A sprinkle of chives on top helped balance out the richness.
Very good and creamy, but a little too rich for my taste. Like, I could barely finish it. I wonder if it's possible to cut down on the amount of butter used.

"replace the water with milk or half-and-half…"

or sour cream.

I used potato starch which is my best friend for extra crunchy fried chicken.  Followed the recipe to a T.   The eggs were delicious.  Best I have ever made or had.  I did add some chives and served the eggs with homemade sourdough discard english muffins.  Definitely a breakfast fit for a queen.  Lot's of butter yes, so just eat these once in awhile as a treat.
BTW! I tried it using potato starch instead of corn starch. Works better.
Easy and very creamy. Water trick is great!

The addition of starch of any kind is repulsive to any lover of a sublimely silky result..& I protest : constant stirring is NOT the way to go ! 
 Gently whisk the eggs a little, allowing just a partial setting of the egg mixture in only a MODERATELY hot pan to develop, one need only 'disturb' the eggs by FOLDING a little & patiently waiting for the mix to more fully set just a little more.
Turn off the heat ...waiting to achieve the overall 'firmness' preferred, turn out onto a warmed (!) plate
Fantastic recipe!
Amazing eggs! Who would have thought to incorporate starch and bits of butter?  I do add a tablespoon of grated Pecorino Romano--enough to add zing but not enough to taste obviously cheesy.
Tried this and its delicious!

I made this exactly as written and it was INCREDIBLE!  It made me happy to eat it with a little side of browned sausages.  I used 1 tsp tapioca starch and 1tsp corn starch just because I wanted to marry them.  It worked beautifully.  You could easily use less butter if you want, and it would still be delicious, but with the full 4 TB, the butter flavor just comes alive.  I've always though good scrambled eggs should taste like popcorn, but with a different texture.  This is it!

well I tried this recipe and gave it one star - aweful!  Far too much butter.  I'll stick to my own - eggs, a little water/egg a little salt and <1 minute in non stick pan.   It's very rare that I don't like a NYT recipe.

Large eggs are 70 calories and a tablespoon of butter is 100. I hesitated making this as it more than doubles the calories of 4 eggs, but gave it a shot anyway. While the eggs remained fluffy, they were dripping with fat from the butter, and made for pretty slimy eating. Wouldn’t make this way again and wouldn’t suggest anyone ever add this much butter to eggs.

The best - from The Nero Wolfe Cookbook, 1934.
6 lg eggs
1 cup light cream
½ teaspoon salt
2 grinds black pepper
2 tbsp butter
4 tbsp clarified butter
several drops tarragon wine vinegar
Bring water in double boiler to simmer. Mix eggs, cream, salt, and pepper, whisk vigorously. Melt butter in double boiler, add egg mixture. Cover for 15 minutes.
Cook clarified butter in a pan on low heat until brown, add vinegar. After 15 minutes, begin stirring until eggs are as desired. Spoon butter over eggs

I tried the recipe to the letter.  The texture was good, but I found the butter overwhelming.  I tested the following modification several times:  use a tablespoon of sour cream per egg, whisk only until blended, then cook as described using 1/2 tablespoon butter per egg.  Wonderful texture and more palatable to my taste.
This works very nicely. About five minutes, start to stop.
I made a slurry with potato starch and milk, cut back on the butter mixed in with the eggs and for cooking. Best scrambled eggs ever — delightfully creamy but not over the top rich.

Reading this recipe takes much longer than making it. Easy enough to memorize for future use, I’m going to make it several times before I make alterations, or even form a judgment. Scrambled eggs are the “little black dress” of any family menu, so I consider it worth investing time to learn to make them delicious every time. I admit that I used truffle salt instead of kosher, and not a big enough pinch because I needed more at the end. For the first time out of the gate, results were promising.
This recipe is incredible, although I did not measure the butter nor cubed it. Instead, I grated the hard butter into the egg mixture and dropped few thin slices into the pan.

If you haven’t made this recipe, you cannot knock it. 
It’s outstanding, leaving the eggs fluffy and creamy.

Mandy Lee (Lady and Pups) recommends potato starch, not cornstarch — she refined her recipe for 15-second scrambled eggs. Yes, literally 15 seconds! And she serves them with a mushroom cream. It’s amazing.
Potato starch is 8 grams of carbs per tablespoon. A total of 16 for this recipe. Bear that in mind. No doubt it works well for the recipe.
I add a table spoon of  heavy whipping cream in to the mix. Yum it makes it fluffy and rich
I must say -- I make very creamy scrambled eggs but these were really delicious! Worth the little bit of extra fuss!!

I had arrowroot powder in the pantry, and that seemed to work just fine. The texture was excellent - light and fluffy.

I wonder if the cubed butter/extra starch combo creates a kind of "puff pastry effect" where the reinforced egg layers trap air given off by the melting butter cubes.

I will definitely user this technique again!

Understanding chemistry somewhat, I do not doubt this would be a great recipe. Something important, corn starch adds carbohydrates into the mix. If one  uses 2 tblsp cornstarch. it adds 14 grams of carbs, which put this off the Keto, diabetic menu. It is important to note this, especially as eggs are the universal go to for those of us who must restrict consumption of carbohydrates. I currently use a Gordon Ramsay recipe for eggs which requires a disgusting amount of butter and is 0 carbs.
The recipe calls for 2 tsp corn starch, not Tbs.  Your point is still valid, just not the number of grams, which would be much lower.

These are the best scrambled eggs I've ever made -- I shared a bite of mine with my husband, and ended up giving him half of them because he loved them too.  (I agree it's annoying when people say, "but I did this instead," so I will only say I like pepper in my scrambled eggs, and added a few grinds to the egg mix, and it all still turned out fabulously!)
These were so velvety. Love them.

This should be called scrambled butter. There is way more butter than egg in this recipe. On top of that, this is way more time and  effort than simply scrambling eggs low and slow. And finally, it tastes disgusting. -5 stars.
Has anyone tried using arrowroot as a corn starch substitute (allergy reasons)?
I'm sure it would work just fine!  Also, I used potato starch, which is as cheap as dirt out by me (a whole 1lb tin is 99c in the Kosher food aisle).  Would that work for you?
I didn't add cornstarch, just the cubes of butter, with a bit of water on the pan to get right temp---Fantastic!! This is easy and delicious.
Just did 1/2 the recipe, as it was written, for a quick supper. Great!

Rarely, if not never, do I comment.  However, as a former professional cook,
I used the technique- a little bit of slurry, a small amount of cubed butter added to the eggs, and without all of the butter and a small amount of olive oil, this recipe sings.
No more hard eggs.  Soft. Soft. Soft. Even when it cools.
This at first felt a bit fussy, but the article made a good case to try it out. What a hit it was! Excellent and worth the effort.
These eggs are totally good but there's a half stick of butter used for every 4 eggs. Of course they're good--I'm just not convinced it's because of the slurry or the cooking technique.

Also have been making scrambled eggs my whole life and typically use the low and slow method for extra creaminess, but this takes time and isn’t foolproof.  This method takes little time and results in the best scrambled eggs my wife and I ever had, each time we’ve made it. Especially when making breakfast for many people, this will be our go to recipe for now on.

I wish people would quit condemning butter and fat.  It's so old school (and sadly, still in much of the conventional "nutrition info") that fat=bad and can't be farther from the truth.  It's the type of fat that makes the difference.  Grass fed and/or organic butter is some of the best fat you can feed your body, as well as good quality olive and coconut oils.  Now, if you're concerned about caloric intake, that is another story.  Still eat the butter, just not as much of it.

Like many people who are commenting, I didn’t think I needed this recipe because I felt like my scrambled eggs were always quite fluffy and delicious; but this morning I thought “why not” and now am not sure how I ever truly enjoyed my scrambled eggs before. These are exceptional.

Amazingly fast and delicious. Made with 2 eggs, and used cornstarch. In a hurry -- skipped the cubed butter -- just melted a plop of ghee into a small cast-iron skillet. But I did wait for the water to evaporate first. That is a neat trick. Fabulous eggs in about 90 seconds.

I actually prefer this w/o the butter cubes, which makes the dish healthier anyway.  I also much prefer firmer scrambled eggs cooked on med/med-high heat, as Kenji suggests, as opposed to the French style, cooled on low heat.  The starch slurry is genius to keeping the egg tender, even when cooked at higher heat.  Love it.  Thank you Kenji!

I made this recipe exactly as stated and it was excellent! You just need to have everything prepped and ready to go as it goes fast! Definitely worth the wee little bit of extra time it takes to have it prepped.
I made the eggs today. Was not too time consuming.Don’t understand what complaints were about. Best scrambled eggs I have ever made.

Meh. I am not fat phobic but think that's a lot of butter. Yes the eggs were soft and fluffy but I get similar results just adding a splash of cream adn avoiding slurries and butter bits and water in the pan and...
.
Delicious, and the right amount of butter in my 35th week of pregnancy.

I consider myself an aficionado of scrambled eggs.  I love them! I made a double batch this morning for the family and they were magnificent. Soft creamy eggs... mmm.. very easy to make, the only extra step is adding in the cornstarch slurry which takes 30 seconds and boom, off to the races.  Haters gonna hate. But this is now in the one recipe to rule them all.
these were fantastic! however, it is A LOT of butter! so just an occasional treat for us. i might try making it with less butter and see how that goes.

I made this three times this week. Very easy to make. I had to adjust the amount of eggs and it was still a hit. I enjoyed it so much. Even an hour later and cold it was still good. This will definitely be my weekend fave moving forward.
I thought they were amazing especially with eggs fresh from my chickens.

made the recipe as stated (halved for one person) and it was delicious, but a little too rich and greasy for me. i made it again today without adding the cold cubed butter to the whisked eggs but following every other step and they were amazing! very soft and creamy without needing to take a nap afterwards. i will forever be adding a cornstarch slurry to my scrambled eggs now!
I followed this recipe exactly as written. I appreciated the fluffliness of the eggs but I couldn't get over how greasy they were (and I love butter). Probably won't make again.
Great Recipe. l stirred in some silken soft tofu - almost the same texture as the eggs - and for color and flavor a nice ripe tomato and some frozen peas.

Found myself on my own for breakfast and decided to try these eggs. So just for one person I divided the recipe by 4 and it still worked out great. Very simple; used a small skillet that never gets used otherwise. I have an electric stove which can be hard to figure out the temperature, so the water technique was helpful.

Made these last night exactly as the recipe called for and was truly astounded at how good they were. I served them on charred tortillas with some chipotle sauce and ate the whole thing myself. I'm not the biggest egg fan and normally cover them in cheese, but these did not need any to be incredible. I'm already craving them again.
Made this as written and it did indeed yield perfect eggs. Maybe a little decadent for my usual tastes but kudos where due.
Tasty, but very buttery. I will try with half the butter and I suspect it will still come out well.
The corn starch works well, but this is far too much butter for my taste. I cut it down to one tablespoon total.
With this amount of butter you should not have a problem getting soft eggs without any other ingredients! Silly recipe!

The addition of some starch was effective; I used arrowroot.  Subbing whole milk for the water helps me to cut the butter to 1 tablespoon, which allows the taste of farm fresh eggs to predominate, and avoids heart attack.
I made these the other night for dinner as was very disappointed. Followed the recipe exactly, no improvisations at all. Nothing special and butter pooling on the plates.
Delish! Used two eggs, 1/4 tsp corn starch, 1 Tbsp butter and a splash of milk. Grated the butter, used chop sticks to mix everything up all at once ( I didn't mix the corn starch and milk first).

Soft consistency. Taste of the eggs is lost by the large amount of butter, and I think the starch also gets in the way of the eggs. Overall was tasteless. But the consistency is nice if you like this sort of soft curd.
Try making scrambled eggs in a double boiler.  It's very easy to control the degree of creaminess in this no-fail method.
Followed directions and this was way too buttery for us.  It tasted like a butter bomb.  I may try again and half the butter.
They're definitely good.  You can eat them and say to yourself, "Next time I'll use less butter.". But only if you feel like it.
Would arrowroot work?

For those of you who have informed us of the one and only proper way to cook scrambled eggs, perhaps you can ask the food section to feature your recipe. Until then - I have chickens and eat a lot of eggs. I’m old and have been cooking eggs for a really long time. This is the best scrambled egg recipe I have ever tried. And it’s so easy!

Made this recipe at home. Incredible! Honestly, probably the best way I've seen when it comes to making scrambled eggs. Normally I'd use cream to up the sumptuous texture, but cutting butter into cubes worked great (and I rarely keep cream on hand). You could also try shredding the butter as well, it worked pretty well when I gave it a try, but you have to shred in slow strokes to avoid sticking. 

Also, if you're watching your calories, cut the butter used in half. Still tastes phenomenal.

This takes a bit of prep that is a annoying (cutting the butter into little squares is a pain), but it’s easy to cook and it makes some of the most delicious eggs I’ve ever had.  I don’t even like scrambled eggs and I loved these.  I grated a little parm on top!

Well, these are exactly what it says on the tin: creamy, buttery scrambled eggs, fast. I’m super sensitive to egg texture so I usually make eggs on low heat, but this makes them attainable on a workday morning. 

The only thing I would change is whisking the eggs before adding the butter, it’s hard to get them mix them with the big butter chunks.

I've always wondered why hotel scrambled eggs are so much better than my own, and now I know.  This is a terrific recipe.  I made it following the directions exactly, except for halving the recipe and using an 8 inch skillet.  Tender and super delicious!  Recently I've  been making eggs the low and super-slow way, where it takes 10-15 minutes to make a creamy scrambled egg. This is much faster and much better. My new go-to scrambled eggs.

So good! The butter adds more flavor than heavy cream or milk I would usually add to my scrambled eggs. I always thought my scrambles were above average, but this is definitely better. More flavor in the same quick time. I will make them this way from now on. I may reduce the butter by 1 Tbsp. (in the egg) because they were a tad too greasy for me.
Truly. These are the holy grail of creamy scrambled eggs, and so fast/easy. After a few times plain just to revel in the creaminess, I started adding my usual favorite—hunks of chèvre. OMG.
Best scrambled eggs EVER.

Great technique! Produces delectably creamy eggs. Like other readers, I found the full amount of butter tasty but way too rich. I cut the butter in half the second time with no diminution in flavor. The use of potato starch is a game changer. Since they’ve just come up in my garden I also added a smidge of finely minced chives for a delicate allium hit and spring color. Wow!
This is an easy, quick recipe for excellent scrambled eggs.  Can't believe it took me this long to discover corn starch.  Thanks very much for posting, Kenji.
Followed recipe exactly and it was easy, quick and came out perfectly. Added some shaved black truffles at the end to gild the lily.
Yup...fluffiest eggs I've ever scrambled! Great tip to put water in the pan to evaporate to check for temp before adding butter and then egg mixture. I'm sold!

Adding to the chorus: These are the best scrambled eggs I have ever made. I always thought of “restaurant eggs” and “home eggs” as different dishes… but no more!  I used about half the butter and the dish was still excellent.
I’ll never make scrambled eggs any other way, absolutely delicious.
Fabulous!
I tried this recipe for making scambled eggs.  Now this is the only recipe I use for them.  The eggs come out perfect every time.  Soft, moist and perfect.
Made it exactly as is. I had no idea scrabbled eggs could taste this good.

Delicious--and decadent!

Next time I'll try reducing the butter by half to 2 tablespoons total.  Also add ¼ teaspoon salt to the egg mixture.

Great technique for making sure the pan is the right temperature.
Excellent eggs. Don't let the pan get too hot at the start.
yes it's good, but it is also 1 tablespoon of butter per egg so I propose not such an accomplishment at not a great idea to add to a regular diet.
Does it have to be a nonstick pan?  (Because I will go buy one just to try this!)

Because of the high protein content in eggs, they tend to stick to a regular pan.  With a nonstick pan, the eggs remain loose and released from the pan.  Even Julia Child kept a nonstick pan around just for eggs.  I've had good luck with Oxo nonstick.
extraordinary. worth the extra steps.
We try to avoid so much butter, so we just whip a tablespoon (oor so) of plain yogurt into our eggs and cook with some olive oil. The eggs are not only fluffy but they taste buttery, as well!

I have never had better scrambled eggs in my life!  And I'm 83 years old and have had a LOT of scrambled eggs.  My problem in making them is that I had potato flour in the house but not potato starch.
