I tried it using the flour, but the slurry was much too thick so I just kept thinning it with more water--until it looked more like a cornstarch slurry.  Can any of your professionals out there tell me how I can adapt this recipe to use potato flour instead?

Honest to God the best scrambled eggs I've ever made. And I'm an egg freak...spent a year perfecting my poaching technique. This was stupid good first time. Followed recipe to a T, even though it seems an obscene amount of butter...it's a keeper.
I cut this in half for a solo breakfast and didn’t have a smaller pan so made them in a sauce pan. They were great even though I overcooked them a bit. They cook super fast as the recipe indicates!
I've found that these hold up longer than usual if you're making brunch for a crowd.

I made these. They have an odd taste - more like egg flavored butter with an aftertaste. If you want the clean fresh taste of eggs I would not recommend these. I love butter, but I found this recipe to be kind of silly. There are better ways to enjoy eggs and better ways to enjoy butter.

These were very good but teetering on the verge of too velvety for me. I folded in some frizzled shiitake mushrooms and they were incredibly rich. They won't be my new go-to method but perhaps every now and again for something extra decadent.

These scrambled eggs really are the best.

That said, I make them with half the cornstarch and they seem just as good to me. Somewhat less butter is somewhat less fabulous, but for daily use, a bit less rich seems fine to me.

These are pretty much the creamiest eggs you could make. I used cornstarch and had a great result. The amount of butter was a little too rich for my taste, so next time I’ll halve or third the butter.

I'm really looking forward to trying this recipe to see how it stacks up against what I often recommend to friends for creamy scrambled eggs: whip up a few tablespoons of plain yogurt with the eggs maybe even a little milk and cook them as usual (i.e. not too long).

Tried this technique. It worked fine, but still—. We keep our own hens and I found that the additions of starch and a lot of butter blurred the flavor of farm fresh eggs. A good fix for store eggs, I guess.

This is an excellent recipe I used this morning using corn starch.
The result was fantastic.  I highly recommend it without making any changes to the listed recipe.

These eggs are fabulous--light & buttery rich. You have to pull them off *right before* they are fully cooked or they will be overdone with a more rubbery texture. Try this recipe before you review, it really will change your breakfast/brunch game. A little kosher salt & freshly cracked pepper & this is as divine as scrambled eggs can get. Thank you so much for this recipe I didn't know I needed. I can never go back to whatever atrocity I made before.
I made this as written the first time and it was good, but I found it too buttery. Halved the butter the second time and preferred it that way.

Oh my, it's just scrambled eggs!!!  But no, these are anything but that...this recipe is easy, delicious and memorable.  Don't let anyone talk you out of trying this.  And, yes, butter makes everything better!  Thank you Kenji.

I am known in my family as the expert at scrambled eggs. But these eggs are creamy and rich. I felt uncomfortable putting so much butter in these; it’s not necessary to have great eggs. But it did make a taste difference. I consider these weekend eggs; a special treat rather than a regular method. You can achieve great eggs just by not overcooking them and drying them out. But these were indeed great. Adding water to the pan created a steaming effect that was interesting and helpful I’m sure.

I made these today with high expectations based on the reviews, but with some trepidation about the amount of fat and calories.  The end result?  They handle well in the pan and don’t stick.  They look nice. But in terms of taste, I would definitely not make them again.  They were greasy and and tasted a bit like eating a stick of butter with some eggs mixed in.  My son had the leftovers (without me telling him it was a new recipe or what I thought) and also said they were greasy.
I have only one thing to add. Fresh Chives. No perfect scrambled eggs without them
By far the best scrambled eggs I’ve ever had. I swapped truffle butter for regular to make it extra ridiculous.

This is great!  Works wonderfully and super creamy and tasty!  Thanks for the tip.  Have you tried whisking your eggs in a copper bowl though?  You can leave out the carb slurry and get the same result.  It's a real thing.  Look it up.  Try it!
My nephew taught me his method to creamy scrambled eggs. Cold eggs, cold butter, cold pan.  It works!
I make lovely scrambled eggs, but this improved them without much extra work.  I’ll probably adopt this even when cooking for 1.  Thank you!
General curious question: why is water not in the list of ingredients? Especially if one can substitute it with milk.  Mies en place but still have to search the recipe for water amounts.
Fantastic. I never want to eat scrambled eggs any other way.

This is the first recipe I have tried since I subscribed fr NYT cooking, last week. I tweaked it a bit and used garlic salt, cut butter amount to half. Just didn't have the heart to use 4Tbsp of butter for a breakfast item. Awesome results.
Phenomenally delicious; best scrambled eggs I've ever eaten. However, the copious amount of butter makes them a little too rich. Next time I'll try them with half the butter.
Made this recipe exactly as stated. I am today old when I learned how to make the best scrambled eggs I’ve ever tasted.
Best eggs I’ve ever made. I used three eggs, a little over a teaspoon of cornstarch, and two tablespoons of butter and still came out incredible.
This is a fabulous recipe. It came together very quickly, the whole family raved, and I will make this again.
Wow these were luscious. Really not much more work than a "typical" scramble but this will be my go to for sure.
Very luxurious eggs. For a treat. Super easy.

Made these as written…and then again with minor adjustments to suit my taste. Reduced cornstarch to 1 tsp….I found with 2 tsp I was able to “taste” the cornstarch and was almost too creamy/starchy textured. Also cut butter in half. I love butter, but 2 tbsp in one serving was a little to buttery for me. Lastly, I used a bit of milk with the cornstarch instead of water. Very good method if you like soft, creamy scrambled eggs. If you like ‘em dry, with ketchup…no. :-)

Made as suggested by other readers: using milk instead of water. Used two teaspoons cornstarch and three TBS milk. Four eggs. Some fresh shallot. Next time, I will halve the butter. The curd on these is definitely restaurant-quality.

ohhhh no. I love nyt recipes for the most part but adding starch to eggs is a hack. it takes a ton of whisking by hand but a creamy scrambled egg can be achieved, as proven by my sister--a CIA grad and CEC. Work it!
With her in-depth understanding of food, your CIA grad CEC sister would probably be open-minded and amazed at the results this recipe achieves. No other egg technique can do this.
Best eggs of my life.

Definitely a step up from my usual scrambled eggs. Make sure however that your pan really is just above boiling - I followed the instructions but my pan was obviously still a touch too hot and the eggs a bit too firm.

Overall though a nice, simple improvement (the starch is a super easy addition) on standard scrambled eggs.

Watching the associated YouTube video (Google it) is also useful by the way and adds some background and insights.

I didn't know eggs could be so good until I tried Kenji's recipe.  I have to have my eggs well-done, so before Kenji they were always dry and rather rubbery - now using this method my eggs are well-done but still creamy and delicious.  Also, his method for hard-boiled eggs is great.

This is a fantastic scrambled eggs hack! I prefer mine cooked but not dried out (i.e. not at all wet) and a little cornstarch (even <1/4 t. in cold water and salt for 2 eggs) has been a miracle. Custardy but cooked, a combination I had never actually achieved with any consistency before. Thank you!!!
Why not just add 8 tablespoons of butter to 3 eggs ?
This is so good ignore the haters. It reminds me of summer camp and hotel continental breakfast.

an egg is 80 calories of near-perfect protein.  1 extra tablespoon of butter adds an absolutely unnecessary 100 calories.  I'm not calorie-phobic, but ... for an occasional treat, ok.  Not a daily scrambled egg recipe.

Been messing with various scrambles lately, and this one is a keeper. I cut some fresh dill and chives from my herb garden, chopped two spring onions, which I quickly sautéed, and threw them in the egg mixture. My wife said, “I can’t believe I ate it all”. I keep fresh Ghee on the back of the stove. Next time I am going to substitute half the butter with the Ghee.
I love the fact that eggs have no carbs, so though these are probably mind-blowing eggs, I'll pass on the starch. Diabetes will do that to you.

I'm T2D (on Ozempic) and this recipe doesn't tick my BG up at all.  It's a nominal amount of carbs and the results are luxuriously good.  We all react differently but it might be worth seeing how it impacts your BG.  I've used half the cornstarch used in the recipe with good results.
Made this for family today - got raves all around.  Absolutely the best scrambled eggs I've ever made.  I used Kerrygold butter to assure extra creaminess.

This is my go to for scrambled eggs since the first time I tried the recipe  It takes something so ordinary and so easily makes it go to 11.  Fluffy, creamy and delicious.  It's got me wondering what it might do for Quiche.  We will find out later today!
Fantastic idea!

Just made this for the first time, to the letter, not expecting too much based on the comments. 

My god, these are good scrambled eggs. Not even close to the many ways I’ve tried previously. All told, it DID take only five minutes and was simple as can be in one bowl. (I even had to use spreadable butter for lack of a stick and it was sublime.)
2 medium size eggs, 1/2 teasp. corn starch with about a tablespoon of water to dissolve it in, a drop of olive oil.  It totally worked!

I have made two versions of this: One exactly as written, and another using half the amount of potato starch. I prefer the latter. With the first, I wouldn't call the eggs "creamy." The mouth feel and taste is not creamy; it's, well, a tad starchy while being smooth. Not sure smooth is the right word. When I think of creamy eggs I think of the custard like texture of slow cooked french scrambled eggs.

I was very skeptical that this recipe would be significantly different from traditions scrambled eggs, because when it comes to eggs, I have always considered myself to be an “oeufen eggspert”. Boy was I wrong. Best scrambled eggs ever. Life changing event!
Fabulous eggs. My son wanted the recipe author to know that he thinks they’re the best scrambled eggs he’s ever eaten. He’s 12. I’ll teach him how to make them like this next week.
I just finished cooking this, and MAN OH MAN. It’s a lot of butter, but these eggs are fantastic. I wont make them this way all the time, but they’re definitely the best scrambled eggs I’ve ever had.

I agree with the other reviewers who wrote that they could get the same results without the extra butter. These were tasty but I like my eggs without the extra butter just as much, and they are healthier.

Sure you can put a serving plate near the stovetop, but two "better" suggestions. 1. If your oven is on already, heat the plate in the oven so the eggs stay warm -- be careful not to overcook. 2. Pour boiling water into a casserole. When eggs are ready, empty the water -- save for your plants -- and dry the casserole quickly. Your eggs will stay warm for a long time, which is important if you are serving eggs to a crowd.
4 tablespoons of butter for 4 eggs?! I thought it was too much, but I followed the recipe anyway.  I was right.

Best eggs I’ve ever had. Fluffiest, bar none. I’m talking lofty, buttery, golden egg pillows. To the naysaying “cooks” claiming they get the same results without the cornstarch or ample fat or remotely following this recipe whatsoever, no you don’t, and hush. No one wants to eat your pre-salted, and therefore watery, broken down eggs.
This recipe is perfect exactly the way it is. Simple and easy with fabulous results. I make these scrambled eggs all the time.
I wonder if Arrowroot would make a healthier substitution as the starch?
We made this for breakfast today, exactly as written. This recipe delivered!

My initial thought was that this recipe was needlessly complicated, but it really came together quickly and quite nicely. Dare I say it tasted a bit institutional? 

The texture was perfect for eggs to be stuffed in burritos, but for stand alone eggs, I will continue with my haphazard way of splashing milk on beaten eggs and mixing in shredded cheese.

Why so much hate for Kenji’s recipes? I wish people would try it first before insisting the way they’ve always done something is better. Have an open mind. These eggs are amazing. The cornstarch gives you a much wider cooking window before the eggs dry out. For those who think this takes too long… yeesh… it’s like two extra minutes of work and then you can cook the eggs at pretty high heat. Try it!
This recipe is my go-to for scrambled eggs.  They turn out flawless, fluffy, light, and delicious every time.
So simple and delicious! I halved the butter and it was still amazing. Thank you!

When I followed this recipe to the letter the scrambled eggs that resulted were a dream. My children were also flabbergasted at how good the eggs were. I short-cut this recipe now with less butter and less care about temperature of the pan, and I still get great eggs, especially when I add a drop of Kirin to the eggs. But the results you get from the 100% version of this recipe still produced the best eggs I ever ate, or served.

Just made this and it was spot on.  Who knew scrambled eggs could be so creamy?  Given the amount of butter, I may not be able to have these on the daily due to my high cholesterol, but these are perfection!
Pro - the eggs were indeed creamier and fluffier than usual. Con - it’s too rich for me with all that butter. I may try again with less butter.
Followed recipe exactly and got some beautiful, creamy, moist, buttery eggs! WOW.
Made this exactly as directed, and it was excellent! Thank you!!

Made this as written. I thought there was too much butter. After I put it in the bowl when it was cooked, there was little puddles of butter. I'd probably half the amount of butter next time. It was nice and fluffy though!

This was a revelation.  Potato starch in eggs?  Really?  But it works.  I am not a fan of scrambled eggs, but I was tired of cooking two types of eggs on Sundays, so I gave this recipe a try.  The eggs were cooked to my desired oneness (well done), but still soft and creamy.  Try it!

I started to make scrambled eggs my usual way over super low heat with eggs that had been whisked with some milk, but then I remembered this recipe. The eggs and milk had already been mixed but I added the potato starch slurry and butter chunks, then cooked as instructed on medium (the highest heat my ceramic nonstick pans can handle). Oh. My. God. I will never make eggs any other way again! So fluffy and tender! Family raved also.
A stunningly easy, always reliable way for me to make a large volume of truly delicious scrambled eggs all at once. Thank you so much for this recipe - it alone was worth the subscription price! ;)

My eggs are always creamy and fluffy because I stir them slowly the way my Belgian mother did, and either add heavy cream or grated mozzarella.  I don't need my eggs to be quick, but I do need them not to come out hard like scrambled eggs always seem to be on TV or in movies.
These are great. Only modification I made was reducing by 1T butter. Will absolutely make again!

It is almost like eating a stick of butter. If you frequently eat buttery foods or want to splurge on a buttery food, this is likely totally okay. But it was just a bit too much butter. I do appreciate the starch and feel like it would still be delicious with a little less butter. Man, I’ve typed butter about 1000 times
These are fantastic.  And they hold well if you are waiting for something else to finish cooking.

Wow …. I followed the recipe exactly, just doubled so that it would be enough for the whole family. It was swimming in butter and had a super weird off-putting taste, I assume from the cornstarch? We each ate a bite and then the rest was tossed.

I admit it, I haven’t tried it yet but I will. I will also say this though. I hated breakfast eggs as a child. I really still do. However my grandmother made scrambled eggs differently than the solid blocks my Dad presented. Yes she added butter which was delicious. But she also added milk. I see from one of the recommended articles that cream was included in another recipe. She whisked both the raw eggs and she constantly moved the eggs in the pan with her fork. To me they were heaven.

I use Julia Childs's recipe:  smear soft butter all over the bottom and sides of a COLD skillet.  Mix up eggs -- NO additions.  Add eggs to pan, and THEN put pan over LOW heat, and cook, stirring lots.  Makes delicious soft scrambled eggs.  Higher heat and eggs makes eggs rubbery.

lol. This recipe was good, but my habit is whisk thoroughly, lower the heat, relax, cook them low and slow, stirring often but gently. Pull them from heat quickly while they’re still moist. Around my house we call my scrambled eggs “clouds”. A little technique goes a long way.

These eggs were amazing and extremely buttery!  Like other reviewers, though, I will make them again, but will decrease the amount of butter until I find the right proportion for me.  When you make these the first time, follow the instructions as written before making any adjustments.  Also, as a courtesy to others, until you actually make the recipe, save your comments until you do.

I call these Marry Me Scrambled Eggs I thought them so delicious, both taste and especially
 texture.  Used 1/3 less butter and still amazing!!
Use about 1/2 as much butter as the recipe calls for. But otherwise they are great!

Julia Child's time tested and simple omelet recipe for has worked well for me for scrambled eggs, too. Drop a pat of butter in a regular pan (not nonstick) and turn up the heat, break in the egg and stir it fast around the pan with a fork until it just comes together. For the omelet just move eggs to the edge of the pan to give it some shape, and flip onto a plate. Always creamy, no prep or ingredients needed. A grind or two of salt and voila!
Can confirm. These are amazing. I use a lot less butter and some milk, but the potato starch is my forever trick now. The kids love it.
Very buttery which overshadows egg flavor. I will try starch without butter.

So good, but, as frequently noted, way, way, way toooooooo much butter (she said, having subbed ½&½ for 1 tbls of water in the slurry). Having made this recipe to raves a couple times before, this time, I set the eggs atop two ramekins ½ filled w/ leftover scalloped potatoes (warmed for 2 mins in microwave) & baked them at 325º for 15 minutes. The eggs rose up majestically as fluffy, scrumptious crowns on the golden potato base. Chef's kiss.
Easy and fast and excellent, first try.
Scaled back to two eggs and it was still so easy. These are PERFECT eggs for breakfast burritos!

Followed step-by-step X3 for a Mother's Day Brunch celebration - WOW! Rich & creamy overload - in the best way possible! Not too sure I'll ever look back to what I once assumed were just plain 'ole, scrambled eggs.
Dudes a genius. Idk how else to say it. Delicious.

Stirring the cooking eggs - especially constantly - is a no-no.  Stirring breaks up the egg proteins, so you'll end up with grainy eggs.  I always use just butter and full cream, no milk or water, and I fold gently until done. I'll have to try adding a pinch of potato starch to see if it really improves the texture.
How about using a whisk, much better than chopsticks
I appreciate the thoughts on corn starch.  But I cannot understand the need for “kosher” salt.  Seems pretentious to me.  Also why unsalted butter when salt is added later.
Another (easier) way to get creamy scrambled eggs: Add a single wedge of Laughing Cow cheese.
Just a note as an aside - Ive started to use a butter/olive oil combination from a tub sold in the dairy department and it’s great.  Even put it on toast plain.

Deliciously decadent.  Not entirely sure the cornstarch slurry is necessary - the butter alone seems like it'd be enough to be responsible for these eggs' rich creaminess.  But whatever, it makes me feel like a scientist to add these "secret hack" ingredients J's so fond of, so I'll play along!  Sure didn't hurt, I'll tell you that.  PS: Echoing others here who've noted that you could cut down on the amount of butter and still have a plenty buttery result!

The last sentence of the opening paragraph doesn't make sense. How does evaporating off half and half while preheating the pan make the eggs creamier?  Alternatively, if adding milk to the uncooked scramble, long LONG teaching is that it will make the eggs tougher, not creamier.
I tried this recipe as written the first time.  It made some lovely scrambled eggs that were a bit too rich for me.  Tried it a second time with half the butter and fell in love.
Love these eggs. I use 1 tsp of cornstarch and 2 tablespoons of butter. 4 is a lot.

I would recommend putting 1 maybe 2 tablespoons of butter in this recipe. 

I put three and it was way too buttery.

Made exactly as written except reduced slightly for 3 eggs. Very easy and results in perfectly creamy eggs as advertised. Next time I plan to add some garlic powder to the eggs before cooking - bet that will really make it sing. I can also imagine it made with some gochujaru or smoked paprika or za'atar or...
I made these with half the recommended butter amount. Fantastic! I’m curious about the purpose of the water in the pan before adding butter.

Bar-none the best scrambled eggs I have ever made. Followed his instructions to the letter, adjusting down for 1 w/ 2 eggs and only a minute of cooking time. Perfection. 👌🏻 Will absolutely make again.

A friend of mine adds a spoonful of whipped cream cheese to her raw eggs for scrambling, along with a splash of milk or water. It’s an easy addition that results in a bit extra creaminess to make the eggs fluffy and special.

I reached for the cornstarch, but realized that I didn’t have any. I had just opened a can of cannellini beans, so I added about a tablespoonful of the liquid to the eggs instead. The results matched the description of the texture in the recipe. I surmise this workaround will work with other beans including chickpeas.
For only four eggs, four tablespoons of butter is overkill -- and by that, I mean it could eventually kill you.  Replace half that with a spot of cream or half and half instead.
I can't condone 1 T of butter per egg. That's just too much to be healthy.
Not for me. Did not enjoy the weird texture from the corn starch. I’ll stick with a splash of cream.
Or you could just low and slow in butter and salt and add a little creme as you turn the heat off. Call me old fashioned!
Great scrambled eggs in a hurry.
I made this with plant-based butter in a stainless steel (not non-stick) pan. It was easy, quick, and delicious -- and I don't usually like scrambled eggs.
These are incredible!! The best eggs I have ever had.

Sometimes the most basic recipes are the most amazing recipes.  i have been making scrambled eggs for the majority of my life.  I have followed the slow and low heat method for creamy eggs, but this recipe is a revelation.  I mostly followed the recipe to make a quick lunch.  I used olive oil instead of butter because that's what I had on hand.  The eggs were exactly as promised:  fluffy, creamy and done in about a minute.  I think this is my new scrambled egg secret weapon.
What does the water in the pan do? I'm not questioning it, I just don't understand its purpose.
It shows you when the temperature is just right to add the butter and eggs to the pan.
Too much tuna! er, butter I mean. I used 3 instead of 4. I feel like I just took a month off my life. They did look pretty.

Funny to see the Kenji Armed Guards out in full force, the koolaid has been drunk.  

I made the recipe as is and it was fine, and because of A1c made it again with substituting the corn starch with heavy cream and just as fluffy but improved taste.
I made these, and they were not a hit.  They weren't terrible, but everyone in my family preferred regular scrambled eggs, which are both less hassle and better for you.
We didn't like this recipe.  The eggs looked great and were creamy, but they tasted gluey.  When I want creamy eggs I'll use the cold pan method.

These are quite good, I especially like a good scrambled egg, seasoned, in a small warm flour tortilla, various toppings, or on a tostada over a smear of refried beans. The eggs stay more tender even when serving more than one or two people.

I made several mistakes when making these the first time: I added all the butter cubes to the corn starch fluid, but I then rescued about 1/3 of them - and promptly forgot to add those to the pan until the eggs were almost fully scrambled.  I also left the scrambled eggs in the hot pan while I buttered my toast.  In spite of all that, these were PERFECT!  Soft, moist, creamy. This has become the way I'll always make scrambled eggs.  I just love recipes that turn out perfectly in spite of myself.

I like Gordon Ramsay’s technique of cracking eggs directly into a cold, unheated pan with some butter or olive oil — no whisking beforehand. Then continuously whisk, and every 30 seconds pull it off the heat for about 20 seconds, continuing to whisk, until done.
The video was great, thank you

Yes, delicious and silky. However, anything with this much butter is gonna slay, and I was already a scrambled egg fan cooking with about 1/8 the butter and a few more seasonings. Tasty experiment but not changing my ways.

I don’t usually have something to say but I feel compelled with this one. I have made scrambled eggs a thousand times, I think I’m pretty good at it! If you like scrambled eggs and this recipe is intriguing to you I suggest you try it, just as it says. I would also recommend you do it on a Sunday, maybe when you slept in because you went out last night, throwing health and conscience to the wind because you already are in reboot mode. You will not only enjoy these eggs immensely, but you will get a good laugh out of it, it’s hilarious how rich these are! Then going forward you can just know that adding the starch to your normal scrambled egg wheelhouse really is nice and makes them better, maybe I’ll even add the tiniest little scrap of butter to my favorite breakfast staple in the future if I’m feeling dangerous. These are the egg version of triple chocolate mouse cake with heavy cream and an opioid garnish. I even put cheese on mine, probably took a year off of my life, ha ha. Enjoy!
Will this work with vegan butter?

Eggs at room temperate. Check. 
Your favorite cheese grated into egg mixture then beat and cook. Check

A few years ago I saw a TV chef prepare scrambled eggs and it was life changing. The simple trick(s) she employed was to beat room temperature eggs with a small splash of milk or half-n-half and then pass the mixture through a wire sieve. The result is smooth, fluffy scrambled eggs every time.
Picture perfect & delicious

Try this for the easiest (and IMHO best) scrambled eggs. Whisk 4 large eggs (or whatever, just adjust to suit) with a tablespoon or two of water and a pinch of salt in a Pyrex jug. Microwave on high 40 seconds, whisk, microwave 30 seconds, whisk then microwave 10 seconds at a time until you get your desired consistency, noting that the eggs will continue to cook a little after the microwaving has ended - that’s the nature of microwave cooking. Butter some toast (or not), serve. No fuss or fat.

Cold tablespoon of butter in a cold pan, heated at high until the butter is foaming brings it to the right temp. Whisk eggs and a teaspoon of heavy cream and a big pinch of salt. Vigorously shake pan with one hand and swirl eggs with a utensil with the other. Whole process about 3 minutes. Perfect every time.

I have a quick way to cook scrambled eggs in the microwave (and I am not a fan of microwave cooking). Beat an egg or two, sea salt and a tablespoon of mayo in a microwave-safe dish. Cook covered for one minute, then let sit a minute. Uncover and stir, or just place the formed mixture on toast to make an egg sandwich. Sometimes I add a dash of hot sauce before cooking.
What if you want to incorporate spinach and goat cheese? How/when do you do it?

Curious, since I may be making these for one, with two eggs rather than four. Should I cut the starch in half? I've tried a lot of the methods in the comments and they're fine but I am excited to give this a go.
A question, would it work to substitute  goats cheese for some of the butter?

Honestly, cutting up the butter and adding corn starch, etc. will take longer than just mixing eggs and pouring into a cool pan and stirring with mindfulness will (not much as it tempers, then checking in once curds start to form, and then maintaining heat). You can easily perform several other tasks when you have your order of operations in place. It is simply technique that anyone can do with practice.

Oooh, yummy!  Way more fat than I like, but yummy!

Another way to get delicious, creamy scrambled eggs without all this fuss and butter - cook them in a double boiler.

This is a vintage technique, but works every time.  If desired, use a thin slick fat to keep eggs from sticking, but it is not absolutely necessary.

Double boilers are a much underutilized kitchen equipment staple.

Too complicated. My method, discovered in the chaos of raising toddlers, is simpler and delivers results:

For each egg, start with a tablespoon of butter melted in the pan.  Crack eggs straight into the pan. Gently swirl with the corner of the spatula to mix for a minute or 2. Don't overcook.

Tasty flavor bombs of buttery yolk with an interesting texture.  

Made this once while rushing everyone out the door and will never go back.
You can make creamy scrambled eggs with just some olive oil and a whisk. I top mine with cracked pepper and salt or Old Bay seasoning.
Just tried it. Yummy! Easy!

1 T. of butter per egg?  Yikes.  For 4-6 eggs, I take a glass bowl and whisk the eggs, which makes them pretty foamy, preheat a non-stick pan with maybe 2 T. butter, cook on any heat I feel like although usually medium to medium high, use a large cooking spoon moving the eggs gently, (but I'll happily try a spatula), and get them out of there before they're overcooked.  They come out plenty moist and fluffy.  It took me years to figure this out and have never looked back.
We did Not like the result. The eggs did not so much taste creamy as weirdly gluey due to the tapioca starch we used. I think we will go back to our old methods.

The recipe is easy to misread...people may think it's 1T butter per egg (4T mixed into 4 eggs).  It's not:  it's 2T butter chopped up and scrambled into 4 eggs; the other 2T for the hot pan.  Still too much butter overall to my taste.

Pretty good both taste- and creaminess-wise. Trouble is, as we moved recently we didn't have starch in the pantry this morning, and 1:1 xanthan gum replacement didn't blend in with the rest. I mean I ate it fine, but wife did not, citing the rubbery texture of gum islands. The quest for a keto version is still on.
Absolutely delicious!!

Made it this morning, I added pepper and thyme. 
The best scrambled eggs I've had.
As an "amateur chef" I appreciate tips such as using evaporated water to gauge the temperature on the pan and using cornstarch and butter in the egg mixture.
Next time, I'll add some hot sauce to the egg mixture.
Kenji does it again! These are amazing. It didn’t take any longer than my usual, but so much creamier! Get his book, The Food Lab. There is an entire chapter on eggs.

Excellent!  I always add Melissa Clark’s herb mixture of fresh parsley, chives and thyme to eggs whether scrambled or omelet.  I only had potato flakes for this recipe but it turned out fine mixed with cold water
Does the ratio of ingredients in this recipe stay the same if you double or triple the number of eggs?
Delicious! Tried it this morning - genius move for the potato starch. I usually do one egg + egg whites, so will play around with the ratio and report back!
Yummm!

My scrambled eggs are always so disappointing I had to try these, and they are wonderful. I agree that 1 T of butter for one egg is just not a sustainable way to enjoy scrambled eggs, but I’m glad I tried it once. I’ll incorporate the tapioca starch and far less butter next time and see what happens.

If you have made French scrambled eggs, you know the difference between soft, silky, custardy eggs and American scrambled eggs as readers have written in comments.

French scrambled eggs are very delicious.  However this recipe produces a similar scramble in a much smaller fraction of time.
I find that if I break the eggs directly into the pan and then scramble them with the spatula, the taste is so much better than if I mix them in a bowl and pour the already beaten eggs into the pan.
Simple and delicious. Top with fresh ground pepper for a great start to your weekend
I used potato starch and half recipe. This was easy and fast with outstanding results. Thanks, Kenji.
Truly delicious.  Decadent yes, even with half of the butter, but much fluffier for me.

To those crowing about stirring:  Yes, constant stirring on low heat can produce superior results.  The author acknowledges this in the article.  This recipe isn't meant to compete with that method, but instead provide an alternative that is less demanding on time and attention while still producing good results.

My mother always made scrambled eggs by adding milk to the pan while cooking the eggs and mixing them together. I have used this technique and it gives fluffy scrambled eggs that I like. No need to measure the milk added. Just add some and then add more to get the consistency of fluffy scrambled eggs that you want.

I had to use arrowroot and it imparted a slight flavor that I didn't care for but the eggs were very creamy. I will go back to my own technique involving a fairly hot buttered pan, turning the heat down when the eggs go in, and slightly undercooking. I use half and half as well.

I was actually surprised at how good these are given the somewhat unorthodox methods. I will say there was a bit too much butter, next time I would only put 1 tbsp in the pan. Also, to all the people arguing over how to make scrambled eggs, please find something better to do with your time. Just make your food how you like to eat it.
We made these per the instructions and were met with delightful eggs, at turns creamy and fluffy. An easy technique and one we will continue to use.
We liked the texture but could taste the potato starch.  It was an interesting flavor but not what we were looking for.
I just happened to have tapioca starch, aka tapioca flour, could not taste it at all.

I usually add milk prior to bearing my eggs. Approximately one tablespoon for two eggs. When I want really soft creamy eggs, and I have it on hand, I substitute whipping cream for the milk. Have not tried the potato or corn starch but will give it a try.
Too much butter! Worth playing around with smaller amounts. Starch works well. How about cheese instead of the butter?

Thanks. This is helpful for people trying to crack the Starbucks egg bites recipe. They use rice starch. Now I have an idea of how much to try. I started using cottage cheese to moisten scrambles and omelettes, per the SBUX ingredients list, a generous teaspoon for three eggs, and haven't made a tough dish since.

Instead of the butter chunks, I gently whisk in a tablespoon (for 3 eggs) of Greek yogurt (not low fat), leaving some clumps. Same creaminess with a subtle tang. I melt a pat of butter to coat the pan before adding the eggs. I have not tried the starch addition as these are already quite creamy.

Followed the recipe and the outcome was delicious, non-rubbery eggs. However, it’s too high in butter and too unhealthy. I’ll stick with a gently fried egg prepared with a thin coating of olive oil in the pan.

I made these this morning and they were delicious!! Taste exactly like great scrambled eggs you would get at a restaurant. Did cut back on the butter by about a tablespoon, as that’s all we had on hand. Would recommend!

I cook scrambed eggs in a small non stick saucepan.  First, grate parmesan cheese l so that the pile looks like a bit more than half a cup. Break 4 eggs into a bowel, add salt and beat with a fork. Melt a little butter in the pan over a medium heat and add the eggs.  Add the grated cheese and stir to lift the cooking egg from the bottom of the pan.  The cheese disappears and in five minutes its done. Creamy scrambled eggs every time.  You hardly notice the taste of the cheese. So add more... !

Very delicious eggs. We had them for a late breakfast. Yes, the recipe seemed a little fussy, but now that I know how good it is and how the eggs stayed soft and creamy to the last bite, I will make them again. It will be easier each time and the process is so simple that actually looking at the recipe won't be necessary after a few repetitions.

Made this AM, using half the butter, on cast iron pan. 
I typically do the slow, "Gordon Ramsey on YouTube" style. 
This was indeed faster, almost too fast, for making coffee at the same time. 
Very creamy/tasty. Not more effort but requires retraining my brain from a known (if slower) route. Wife described it as "very buttery", can't imagine the full butter route.
Made this today,  so creamy and delicious, and my son asked for seconds so I made another pan.  But the second time,  I substituted half the butter in the first step with velveeta cubes.   Sublime.

The no-fail "trick" I learned years ago: Keep 'em moving. Don't stop for a billionth of a second. Shake the pan about an inch or two above the flame once or twice, stir with whatever...but keep them moving. They should take just seconds to reach a creamy consistency. Don't salt until afterward. Also, I whip the eggs with a fork for about a minute or more to get plenty of air into them.   Delicious with salmon roe, by the way.
OMG, following this recipe I finally made good scrambled eggs!

I made these eggs exactly as written except I used 6 eggs instead of 4 while keeping the other components the same. These eggs were delicious and cooked perfectly, but they didn’t taste much different than when I mix the eggs with 1-2T milk (no butter). The trick of putting 1T of water in the pan and then 2T butter was excellent. I will use that method from now on when making scrambled eggs!

Anyone hating on this, try a side-by-side comparison with your alternative and THEN report back. These came out great and fast. Also very forgiving of mistakes. I forgot to add butter to the pan and had to let the dog out in the middle of one batch and it still came out just as good as the more careful batches. Weekday win.

The trick to get the pan to the right temperature with the tablespoon of water is awesome but wow these were waaaaay too buttery and rich for me.

Adding the butter to the egg mixture doesn’t make sense to me. Better to keep it to one tablespoon that’s added to the pan, I think.
My wife likes here eggs well cooked. So adding a bit of grated cheddar pleases her and everyone else partaking.

Was okay, but I agree with other posters here. You can make scrambled eggs just as creamy, if you use a little butter, heat slowly, continuously stir and take the eggs off the heat just as they are starting to cook well. IMO, this is a very unhealthy recipe, far too much butter (saturated fats and cholesterol). Makes me feel guilty eating this much butter combined with the eggs, so reduces my enjoyment of the taste.
Exactly. Low and slow is the way to go. I don't know why these professional cooks/chefs have to always complicate things.

I am one of those who also makes great scrambled eggs. A go to comfort food in my family for .... well, forever. But, these are by far the best I've ever ever made. Agreed, a little less butter is fine, and I was worried I would taste the arrowroot (I don't use corn or potato starch) but not even a bit. There was a lightness here that I've never managed, and yes, now I have to double my walk today, but it's worth it. Besides in Arizona right now it's 65 degrees!
Made with 4 eggs, but only 2 tablespoons of butter, and they were sublime. Gone in less time than they took to make.

Do you all add salt before or after cooking?  
I’ve read recipes that say never add salt before start cooking, but I like it well blended in, so I usually add it in part way to doneness...  
Can’t wait to try!

Made these exactly as written. I agree with some folks this is still pretty fussy when pitched as a weekday morning recipe. But the whole thing came together in less than 10 mins. These are very rich, calling for half a stick of butter, so I probably won't be making these again any time soon. However, they are incredibly delicious. I seasoned them after cooking with a little more salt, some black pepper, a homemade hot sauce, and some homemade sourdough.
I actually made them, and they are soooo good. Made with two eggs, potato starch. WOW

Made these this morning and they’re delicious. I’m a big fan of wok scrambled egg and tomato, and potato or corn starch is a transformative ingredient there, so I was down to try these. It took about 5 minutes to put together and cook. The water trick for the pan helped me finally nail the right temp, I cut back the butter in the eggs by half (1.5 T) and would probably dial back to 1 T next time (it’s plenty for us with the add’l butter in the pan). Topped with a little salt & fresh pepper, yum!

yup — absolutely the best scrambled eggs i’ve ever had.  made with 2 eggs in a 8-inch cast iron skillet; used arrowroot. served on top of a piece of whole wheat toast (unbuttered:). yes, a lot of butter — next time i may try a bit less — maybe...
I loved them. So fast and so fluffy.

So I have to give Gordon Ramsey some credit here. Too many people over cook their eggs, salt too soon, and never take the eggs of the heat. Follow his video and add a dollup of Creme Fraiche and you’ll get perfect eggs everytime.

I add a tsp.-tbs+/- of water to my eggs while beating with a fork. The steam from the water as the eggs cook makes them nice and moist. Will try adding some butter to the eggs. This is all way to complicated.
If you add one or two tablespoons water to the eggs before whisking them they come out very fluffy.
I made them. As per directions with 2 eggs and they were phenomenal!
I made this per instructions but with half the butter: 1tbsp cubed and mixed in, 1tbsp in the pan. I’ve never made scrambled eggs as good as this. Delicious.
Try adding sour creme or creme cheese.
Very good—you can definitely taste the difference. Fluffy yet silky. Dunno about weekday but it’s worth trying if you love eggs.
Excellent procedure. The eggs cook more evenly and the butter flavor is retained, even without using the full amount.

Wonderful recipe!  And, don’t be afraid to play with it to make it perfect for you.
Two eggs work well for a single serving, for me.  I love the added butter and could possibly handle even more!  My butter was salted, so I added just a small pinch of kosher salt to the mixed eggs.  To keep it ‘low-carb’, I didn’t add starch.
I cooked slow on med-low in a buttered, cast iron, pan and the result was a perfect breakfast.
Thank you!
Cornstarch provided fluffiness. Added some provolone cheese. Great scramble eggs.
Eggs cooked with potato starch this way have a superior texture..I have been doing this for a couple of years now on weekends or when I have guests and everyone loves them. Try it! Thanks Kenji
Made with potato starch. I thought the butter whisked with the eggs made it a little greasy. Will try next time with a Tbs of heavy cream instead.
I made these as directed with potato starch. Delicious but a little greasy. I’ll use less butter next time or sub 1 Tbs heavy cream.
Not so sure about these.... I followed exactly and used cornstarch, but I think the starch gave them a slimy texture. Plus I don’t think the method really saved any time over my normal method

I prefer my scrambled eggs on the drier side, and my partner prefers wet, runny scrambled eggs. This recipe is a great compromise for us, especially if I plate his first and let mine cook for another 30 secs. Great if you’re trying to time multiple hot brunch items. I used tapioca starch (which I keep around for Pão de Queijo) and there wasn’t any noticeable taste or texture weirdness. I agree that the butter is excessive. I used 3 tablespoons for 6 eggs, and even that was a bit decadent.

I gave this a try this morning and really liked the result. And I'm happy to see the use of butter rather than cream for richness. I have some lactose issues that aren't triggered by butter. I learned to cook with cream and it's such an easy way to provide that pump of fat so it's nice not to find a way around what for me was a crutch.
Thank you! I'll prepare these this evening, scoop into hot toasted taco shells and top with avocado and shakes of sauce.

Correction:

Thank you! I'll prepare these this evening, scoop into hot toasted taco shells and top with avocado and shakes of hot sauce.
Creamy, restaurant style. Reduced butter to 1 T in the pan.
Would arrowroot work as the starch?

For scrambling eggs or omelets, I add 1/2 or. More TBSN Greek yogurt and hand whip for a minute before cooking in some small amount of butter. Experiment, add herbs, tomatoes if you wish. The yogurt results in fluffier eggs.
Just made this - that’s a lot of butter! But the dish was delicious, 3 eggs and 3 T of butter, and I’m completely full. Will try with less butter next time, though, to be only a little decadent.

I think I make pretty good eggs. But this recipe delivered the goods! Bravo! Having stated that, next time I'll experiment with half the butter (maybe less in the eggs and less in the pan) because it sure has a lot calories. But then, Julia Child always said butter was the secret to good cooking !
I will try the starch and butter approach. My trick is to my get the eggs 80% cooked, then mix in a little whipping cream to finish. For snap use ranch dressing instead.

A long ago boyfriend taught me how to make the best scrambled eggs.  Just add a few small clumps of cream cheese to salted whisked eggs (fork whisked a minute or less) and cook low to medium with the push spatula gently method and remember to preheat the pan.  Works great and no one tastes the cream cheese just the creaminess from the melted cheese which blends in as it cooks.  Cheddar crumbles work too. The boyfriend is long gone but the scrambled eggs remain!

Made exactly as the recipe directs.  Best scrambled eggs I ever had.  Previously, I added a tablespoon of coconut milk to 4 eggs and used the low heat method - very good but not as good as this recipe.

Potato starch, a Passover staple, can be purchased all year at Japanese markets, a pleasant surprise, & way better graphics. (In Berkeley, at Tokyo Fish Market.)  As for butter, one French chef recommends withholding it from the whisked blend, but placing a pat on top of the hot eggs as they exit the pan, as some restaurants do with steak. Nonetheless, one egg already has approx. 70% max daily cholesterol, so proceed at your own risk. We use Canola oil for hot-pan cooking: no burnt-butter smell.
A bit too much butter for me--hard to taste the eggs for the butter.
A simple splash of milk into your eggs before scrambling, then cooking in butter, always moving the eggs around will make them creamier than this recipe.

"For creamier eggs, you can replace the water with milk or half-and-half." is misleading as to whether to replace the water in the skillet or the water in the starch mixture. Put this aside in the ingredients or steps.
Not sure if you are serious but the water is the pan is just as an indicator of the pan's heat as it all evaporates. The replacement is for the eggs mixture.

I often add about 1 tbsp sour cream per 2-3 eggs, mix moderately, salt, then cook. It's interesting to learn the function that this added fat has on creamier eggs! I like a runnier sour cream for this, like Nancy's probiotic sour cream.

The best scrambled eggs I've ever made. If you have the time as the recipe suggests let your whisked eggs sit with some kosher salt. My eggs turned a brighter orange color and that color stayed in the cooking. It made the eggs more flavorful as well. Next time I'll probably add a little less butter because these were rich, but delightfully so. Will be using this method many more times
Great technique. Eggs were creamy, fluffy and velvety. I will likely reduce the amount of butter when I make it again, it was too rich for my taste.

A lesson on how to complicate cooking so you don’t bother! Just add cream to your beaten eggs and do a start/stop cook in the microwave till it’s  a wet creamy consistency, will carry on cooking on your warmed plate😋
Adding the Tapioca starch worked wonders for my scrambled eggs. I added 2 tblspns of butter instead of 4 and they turned out super creamy
I’ve been making scrambled eggs for more than 50 years, since I was 18 months old. I am qualified to say that all one needs for perfect scrambled eggs is  this recipe. Bravo!
I agree with Andrea - that’s what I do.

I halved the butter and it was delicious. Might be too rich otherwise. Also, whipped the eggs and slurry first, then mixed in butter, and there was no sticking butter to pull apart. This recipe took barely any extra time than the usual scramble (maybe less - it's a fast cook) and results were better.

Way too complicated. My secret ingredient is club soda and eggs at room temperature. Take eggs out of frig the night before.For two people: 5 room temp eggs scrambled with a splash of club soda; 2 Tbsp unsalted butter, melted in cast-iron skillet; as soon as butter is melted add the eggs and start stirring with silicone spatulas; as soon as they set, take odd heat and keep stirring to desired doneness. The club soda makes them light and fluffy.
Everyone keeps offering their take on fluffy eggs yet no one has commented that this recipe is titled "Extra Creamy Scrambled Eggs".  Creamy isn't synonymous with Fluffy.
Creamy and delicious!  (Do you think the tablespoon of butter/egg has anything to do with it? :) )

I always add a tablespoon or so of liquid (milk/water) to scrambled eggs. Putting corn starch and butter in this way is not a lot of trouble. I like the use of water to determine the temperature of the pan. Reminds me of my mother using a wooden kitchen match in a pot of grease to determine the right temp for deep frying. I made using two eggs and about one tablespoon of butter, divided. The eggs cooked quickly and were some of the best I've eaten. I will use cornstarch routinely now.
It was a good recipe, but not worth it overall. I can make creamy eggs, although not as cream, with less butter.

My secret to creamy eggs is not to use a frying pan, but a smaller deeper pan or pot so the eggs retain moisture. Eggs & salt combined. Put butter in the pan. Add eggs before butter is fully melted. You can cook over moderate heat stirring constantly. For two eggs, my smallest pot.

Good recipe

Works well with Egg Beaters, too.

Thanks
Meh. Creamy bordering on greasy. Even this butter lover feels the need to take a shower.  A solid plate of eggs but nothing to swoon over.

I agree with Elgin Martin's comment/question. There is no water IN the recipe to replace with half and half or cream other than the water used to heat the pan. Is this a correct statement in the recipe and, if so, to what liquid is he referring?
The starch slurry has the water or milk.

The eggs were silky and decadent: a textural triumph. But even as a self-professed butter enthusiast, the preparation was a bit too rich to eat *just* as scrambled eggs (there were pools of butter oozing out of my tortillas that I ate the eggs in). If I were to make these again I would definitely eat them on some plain steamed rice or some nice porridge that can be further flavored by the extra buttery goodness.

For me, my tongue is not the final arbiter of what's 'best' and the addition of four (count 'em - FOUR) tablespoons of butter alone is enough to put me off on this recipe.  The yolks also carry a lot of cholesterol.  A more healthy approach is whisking together three whites with one yolk, a pinch of salt, and a little 2% milk and cooking 'low and slow' in a pan coated with olive oil, not butter.

Made these this morning. After reading some of the comments here I opted to use just 1 tbs of butter for the cooking, as I was also using a non-stick pan and I didn't want the eggs to be greasy. They were perfect! Creamy, soft and fluffy and cooked in a flash. No joke, make sure your plate is next to the stove when you pour in the egg mixture as the eggs will be done in about 2 minutes.

To each his own. I put some butter in a cold pan and turn the heat on high, watch the butter melt and just as it stops sizzling (don't let it burn) I pour in the beaten eggs. Push them from the edge to the center as they cook until done like you like 'em and voila! This happens quickly and are always tender, fluffy and creamy.

Since I was a child I didn’t appreciate dry scrambled eggs and I created this recipe that added about 3 spoons of milk to a pan with 2 spoons of vegetable oil and a pinch of salt. When mix was boiling I added 2 scrambled eggs. Nowadays I use extra virgin olive oil, milk and salt in the same proportion but I crack the eggs open & scramble them in the pan while mixing with the mixture in low heat. When a thin layer of eggs is still undone, I turn heat off and add  (pepper/chives/diced tomates)
These are so good. Game changer. Sure maybe not everyday eggs but perfect for weekends when you’re feeling more decadent.
Very sad to report, I threw them out. Followed recipe exactly, they looked fine, but taste/texture!?  Will return to my usual. (Although I liked technique for ensuring proper pan heat,)
I'm always in search of the perfect scrambled egg.  These are easy and delicious!
I loved the texture, but we tasted the cornstarch. I wonder if kudzu would work? Anyone tried that?

When I read 15 minutes to stir eggs to make scrabbles I thought - who takes 15 minutes cooking scrambled eggs?!  I'm sure adding the starch would fluff them up but the altered taste not sure I'd like. Heating a pan, water will tell you it's at 212°F or wait 20 seconds add the butter it's melted? You're good to go. Chop up a green onion fine add that in the same time it take to so the starch slury and you'ce added a whole level of flavour. Nice recipe but the whole weekday time saving premise? No
So, for those who have an IR thermometer, what would the ideal pan temp be?

Yes you get creamy and moist eggs. But in my mind this method is for people that don’t like eggs, don’t like their texture and specially don’t like their taste. Here all I tasted was the butter. I’ll keep making mine the way I always made them since childhood: a little bit of olive oil, adding the eggs unbeaten to the pan, and folding the egg whites first, and only folding the yolks at the very end. Always tasty, moist and eggy. Salt and pepper tableside.
Tasty but unbelievably greasy and slimy and I even used half the butter. I definitely don’t recommend using more than 2 tablespoons...

My Mom made incredible scrambled cheese and eggs.  She just whisked eggs and cheese together and then cooked it over a double boiler.  It never failed and we never failed to love them! They were so tender and delicious!
Made half recipe, as written.  Best scrambled eggs ever!  Yes it’s a lot of butter but perfect as a Sunday morning treat.

Made these this morning. They came out like three-star hotel eggs—fluffy, delicious and shiny with all that butter. Just a pinch of salt was plenty, and much less than I usually use. But as tasty as these eggs are, all that butter doesn’t sit well.
Cuttys in Brookline was mentioned. Their Super Clucking Sunday chicken sandwiches are amazing! There is always a line several blocks long. I hope this will return after the pandemic.

Wow, way too much butter. That was by far the dominant flavor. Weekday eggs? Eat these five times a week and you’ll be courting a heart attack for sure. First time I can recall Kenji letting me down so thoroughly.

Thermoworks makes a flat probe instant read thermometer to measure pan surface temperatures.  Given the use of their needle probe thermometer, I don't understand why it's not used more widely.  It's great for knowing when cooking utensils are at high temperatures just shy of oil smoke points (e.g. woks, searing).  It would also be great for knowing when your skillet was just shy of 212 F.  I no longer look for beads of water to skitter across woks or hold my hand over heating skillets.
Made them this morning. Fiance loved them. I was able to split the difference between the "looser and softer" scrambled eggs that she likes and the "more well done" eggs that I like. Will make again.

I eat butter all day like it was a snack but this is too much butter. Good otherwise! Might be good at half. Usually four eggs in a blender with a splash of water and a dash of heavy cream cooked in half butter and half oil is the same level of fluffy and fast but without the dripping butter effect.

I tried it and there definitely was an improvement over my eggs of yesterday. However, I wonder if it is just because of the heart attack dose of butter being added that these are so much better? Hold on, my arteries are seizing up... :-)
Just made this recipe (used arrowroot/tapioca flour). Absolutely perfect and delicious!
I have always found that adding a small amount of water to my eggs, about two tablespoons to 2 eggs, as I whisk them adds to fluffiness without extra fat.

I used 2 eggs for a single serving. The preparatory steps involving the starch and the butter cubes would presumably go faster with more experience, but I didn't enjoy the fussiness they entailed--such as dealing with butter cubes adhering to each other when whisked. The eggs cooked in less than 15 seconds, and they were unctuous indeed, but the strong butter taste wasn't to my liking. However, I will definitely adopt the water method for heating the pan going forward.

Adjusted recipe for 3 eggs, followed instructions, and cooking time, and they were wonderful!  It’s best to have all ingredients ready before beginning, as when I mixed the cream(instead of water ) to the cornstarch, within seconds it became frothy and thick.
I had fun making this with my 12-year-old son. The texture truly is extraordinary; he described it as “cotton candy” eggs.
I followed the exact recipe. The amount of butter is sickening. All I can taste is butter and they have a greasy texture. No fluffier or creamier than slow cooked with a little milk.
These were okay but, too much work. Especially, for a work day. I made them on a Sunday. Agree that there is more butter than I would use if I made again. But, I won't.
Milk. Add Milk to the eggs.....cook in butter. DONE and fluffy!
This is hands down the best scrambled egg recipe I have ever encountered. It’s a few more steps but so worth it! Truly creamy.

And if you don't use nonstick cookware? I use stainless, so how much oil/butter should I put in the pan before adding the eggs? And since it's not nonstick, should the temperature be lower to prevent scorching and sticking?

I followed the technique after watching his video.  So I used 2 T cut up butter and 1T in the pan.  We liked the result.  I like my eggs super soft so next time I would use an 8" skillet instead of 10" and lower my heat.  The chopped chives were delicious on the eggs.

Tried this last night for dinner except I added colder melted butter to the eggs and just whipped them up with a little cornstarch. Saved a little time. 5 eggs was just enough for the three of us. It was so good I would have added a 6th egg :)

This was outstanding. I’ll never understand the compulsive need to come to a cooking site, not make the recipes and leave your own recipe. How about you start your own cooking site or better yet, try the recipe and build on it. Share that.

Did this a little differently to avoid stuck butter bits - tossed butter with starch (arrowroot works well) THEN added water, slurried, and then eggs. Worked great. Threw in mixed herbs, yummy. Not a huge difference from my usual scrambled eggs but cool science in the article!
Fluffy yes but greasy tasting. I would decrease the amount of butter in the pan by half.
I tried this and think the amount of butter is obscene. It does taste good but really, there's going to be a butter shortage if people start cooking every egg with a tablespoon of butter.
After a lifetime of scrambling eggs, I thought I knew it all. But, wow, who would've ever thought corn starch. These are devine.
i must have done something wrong, probably overcooked, because it was non-stop butter. left an oil-slick on the plate and in my belly. will try again with less better and less time on the heat.
I use a non-stick pan with bamboo chopsticks
Somebody has way too much time on their hands.  Surely an artifact of the pandemic.  When I want my scrambled eggs I don't want to go to cooking school first.
Followed the recipe as written, but halved as it was just for me. Worth the extra steps: creamy, soft, and very tasty. Comfort food for a chilly morning.

Incredibly good recipe for getting the texture of scrambled eggs that take much less time than low and slow.  Would definitely use half the butter next time for my personal taste.  I do like letting my curds set a bit harder, but the addition of the corn starch has them retain so much moisture and were really amazing

As simple as scrambled eggs can be, this really is a great new perspective on how to prepare them!

What a fascinating approach! Some lazy weekend morning I’d love to try it. However, my dietary restrictions don’t allow for cornstarch. Do any of you who have tried this have thoughts about a way around that? Thanks.
I used arrowroot. No issue with 1:1 substitution.
This is divine. Not a lot of work and the creaminess is extraordinary. Kenji is my egg guru.
I wonder if this recipe is meant to be 4 teaspoons? I just made it and the butter pools all over your plate.

Using an immersion blender to scramble the eggs is easy and very effective.

Sprinkling a bit of shredded cheese adds fat, saltiness and taster even umami (depending on cheese).

Personally, I need a bit of contrast such as chives, scallions, red pepper flakes ... added at the very end so it stays separate.
For everyone who is so concerned about butter, did you ever think of trying a plant based butter, like Country Crock?  I use it on eggs all the time and the taste is great!
Way too much butter.  Not decadent, just dominating butter flavor.
This made me realize while eggs with a splash of milk are so much fluffier.

does this technique help with omelette making as well? I've been having trouble getting the pan just the right temperature so that the eggs cook slowly enough to permit the addition of cheese, etc without getting overcooked.

After making this scrambled eggs recipe, it becomes harder still to want breakfast anywhere but home. This is simple and requires little modification to the process. I prefer eggs poached or fried in generous amounts of hot olive oil, but these are delicious.

Maybe it's just me, or maybe it's because I'm British, but I always add a small splash of milk plus a half teaspoon of mustard to the melted butter in the pan. As soon as the milk is nearly evaporated I add the eggs and gently stir. I don't over-manipulate the egg mixture beforehand or after adding to the pan. I aim to mix the eggs without thrashing them to death. Tip onto the toast just before they seem perfect as they'll continue cooking of course. Works for me anyway!
I can confirm these are indeed extra creamy, extra buttery, soft scrambled eggs. so good.
Low and slow eggs are delish! These quicker creamy eggs are also very delicious! There's more than one way to make scrambled eggs!!! Thank you Kenji for showing us a new technique.

I would have to agree with Andrea. I add a little milk to the eggs to make them softer, a pinch of salt, and voila. We made this recipe the other evening, and while the eggs were good, I'm not sure they were any better or faster than what we normally make, and frankly 4 Tbsp of butter for four eggs is a little horrifying.

Way too much butter.  I used half of what's called for and it overwhelmed the taste.

I like to whip egg whites first then add yolks, makes a very frothy and light mixture.

I substituted arrowroot 1:1 for the starch with no problems. I was skeptical about incorporating the butter in the starch slurry but it broke down just fine once I added the eggs and salt. I also used a cast iron skillet which called for preheating on low to bring the whole surface to an even temp before kicking the heat up to medium high and adding the water. Don't over-stir if you like fluffy eggs. This cooks fast so don't wander away for a coffee refill!
Just made this- I halved the butter and it was still delicious. Much smoother and fluffier than my usual scrambled eggs! Delicious.
Followed exactly for 1/2 recipe (2 eggs) KJL being the master. But holy cow that’s a lot of butter. Will experiment with less next time. Starch addition is a keeper.

The MOST IMPORTANT thing for great scrambled eggs is --- good eggs. This means local, farm raised, fresh eggs where the chickens have been able to wander around and eat grass and bugs and worms and whatever in addition to their regular feed. This improves the flavor a lot over supermarket-bought factory eggs. Then it's a simple matter of beating the eggs with some cream and a pinch of salt and cooking with some butter in a non-stick skillet over medium low heat.
Life changing eggs. Just try not to think about how much butter you're using...
Just made this today and had scrambled eggs so good, so creamy and soft, I felt like I was sitting at a restaurant, not my home. I’ll be using this technique moving forward!
Just made this and it’s fantastic, but incorporating butter cubes with egg mixture was kind of a pain.

Made this tonight for breakfast for dinner and the texture was great but I didn’t think the taste was there compared to Kenji’s soft scrambled in The Food Lab. Yes, that one takes 15 mins but in terms of flavor it is worth the 15 min rest time.
I will try this, BUT, adding a TINY amount of cream of tarter also creates the creamy scrambled egg as you use the same cooking technique and low heat.

My eggs are always creamy.  I always use a small pot, a dab of butter to line the bottom of the pot, whisking the eggs with half and half or creme fraiche, placing the mixture in the pot and stirring occasionally on very low heat while I cook bacon or sausage. Everything done at the same time.  Never fails!  Yum yum and yum!  No muss no fuss!

I've added a bit of water to my eggs and I always use butter in the pan.  I might try the slurry though just to see how they turn out.  For me, taste is the most important thing - more so than presentation - so I'm anxious to see how these taste as well.  And thanks for the recipe.  There is always something new to learn!
I add a T of sour cream & a t of milk to 2 eggs well whipped & cook on med heat & add butter then fold in eggs from side of pan & take off heat to finish.
The most delicious!  a good cook is always open to try new ways and ideas. Maybe my way is the best, but how will I know if I don't try other ways.. Truly these are better than mine.

My creamy scrambled egg method is to separate yolks from whites. Break yolks with fork and set aside.  Add a few bits of butter and bit of salt to the egg whites and whisk until frothy. Add whisked whites to a warmed pan and push around with a small spatula (I like wood) until just short of plating and then add in the yolks, incorporating into the whites just until warm.  Serve immediately.  Creamy, yolky and satisfying.
How and when could I add cheese?

Great to learn a new cooking technique and I appreciate that. Thanks.  I just would not cook this recipe because of the amount of butter used. We have to balance food with health. If each egg came packed with a tablespoon of butter, I would probably not be eating many.

I've reminded my son how to make  great creamy scrambled egg this last week.  With careful gentle cooking they can be very successful in the microwave which is how we like them.  A step on from my mother years ago who always made them in a double saucepan but equally as good.  He knows now how long to leave them between bursts, how vigorously to whisk them and when to add the butter.  No slurries here!

I like to heat my cast iron skillet while cooking the eggs.  Then turn down the heat on the eggs and cover them with the hot skillet for the last 30 seconds.  Heating from the top and bottom makes the eggs very fluffy without turning the bottom of the eggs brown.
As I learned in Paris, four tablespoons of butter and a double-boiler create the softest and most delectable curds. No cornstarch needed.

Of interest, ihop puts flour in their scrambled eggs.

I tried potato starch last time (1/2 qty) and was pleased with the results.
Like others, I did not use the whole 4 Tbsp of butter. I added 2 Tbsp and it was delicious. I used a microplane grater to put a little aged cheddar on top and some snipped chives.
I discovered this recipe several years ago and, up until then, I thought my scrambled eggs were good. But these eggs are unbelievably good!  No comparison. Enjoy your eggs.

I made these as directed and found them unpalatable.  Too much butter, way too much cornstarch.  I will go back to making them the way my grandmother did - crack eggs into a bowl, add s&p and a large pinch of flour - whisk/stir until frothy.  Melt a small amount of butter in a pan. Add eggs, cook/stir on med-low until almost finished, turn off heat & continue stirring/folding until desired level of consistency.  It's an extra 2 minutes max.  Cooking scrambled eggs on too high heat ruins them.

I add a little sour cream to my eggs before whisking gently with a genrous turn or two or three of black pepper and about a tablespoon of water. Cook them in a pan with butter or olive oil.  Using a wooden spatula, pull the eggs in from the sides and stir gently.  Perfect every time.

I couldn't help but try this immediately. Adjusted to 3 eggs, and a total of 2 T. of butter, with 1 teaspoon going into the pan directly. I tasted when they were creamy and medium, which is my preferred doneness. Very good. Then I continued cooking until more set, which is how my husband likes his eggs. His comment was they were the best eggs I've ever made...Even on the well done side, they retained a nicer texture than I can usually achieve for someone who doesn't like any liquidness in eggs.

Making breakfast for one over here, so I halved the recipe.  2 tablespoons of butter seemed like a lot so I did 1 instead. This is the best recipe I've tried for scrambled eggs. I usually just melt some butter in a pan and add the scramble and have been happy for years, but the tapioca starch does do wonders.

What is the point of evaporating a tablespoon of water in the pan before adding the butter (not being snarky, just haven't seen that before)?
I think it is a method to show that the pan is the perfect temperature.
Just prepared these eggs for my breakfast.  To describe—lovely, creamy and delicious.  I halved the recipe for one serving.  Thanks

I have always added some heavy cream to the eggs, and not so much butter.  My mom got this strong cream from the top of our delivered milk---in the olden days!!  80 years later this is still foolproof for making super creamy eggs.    Yum.
Just perfect eggs!

I love this idea!
 I actually whisk a couple eggs with a pinch of sea salt. I heat the pan on medium/low, add two teaspoons of butter. As soon as the butter melts, add the eggs and stir with a baking spatula for 30-40 seconds and I then add a tablespoon SOUR CREAM. Continue lightly stirring 30-40 more seconds and serve.
This was fantastic! I made up a single serving for lunch with sauteed greens and toasted rye. Delicious and it was ready in minutes!
Wicked easy. Wicked good. Some prosciutto, sliced avocado and English Muffin on the side = dinner.

Why not just cook them gently in a lot butter, in a small saucepan, 
constantly stirring? Stop while still quite moist.They continue cooking when you take them out of pan.

1/2 stick butter with 4 eggs!!!!

A lot less butter and wisk eggs with some water or milk to make fluffy.  :-)
This may be a dumb question. When increasing or decreasing recipe, does one change amount  of the starch and butter?
Out of this world!  Best 4 minute recipe I've ever made, and now my only way to make scrambled eggs

I always add a few splashes of milk to the beaten eggs. I melt a tablespoon of butter in an 8” skillet before adding the eggs. I do add into the beaten eggs some dehydrated onion flakes, parsley, salt & pepper.

I don’t understand all the obsession with the amount of butter.  I have a much simpler and healthier option, just heat the pan up with the water as suggested by the chef and then grab a full stick of butter and coat the pan until the entire surface is coated.   No need to put several tablespoons in with your eggs as long as the surface is coated the eggs will cook amazing and not stick to the pan.
Yes, but that's a different recipe.  This is decadent, for sure, but try it once - it's absolute heaven.  Not to be made all the time.

I agree these eggs had too much butter for my taste so I adjusted it. The real helpful tips were salting ahead of time and letting them sit for a few minutes and the evaporating of the water technique so the pan really is at the perfect hot temperature. Thank you Kenji for the great tips!
excelent
Article is wonderful: https://www.nytimes.com/2021/02/19/dining/perfect-scrambled-eggs.html
Did a one egg version. Turned out smooth and tasty. Next egg will get half the butter. Thanks for the salt tip.

this is a great recipe for egg.  I have no patient to cook my eggs soft scramble during the weekday morning.  I tried it this morning without any butter (which I forgot), and it came out so quick and delicious. it's going to be my morning go to now.
Beat the eggs with chopsticks! Better for the ears.
Cooked it last night.  Delicious.  But, I think I'll try a bit less butter in the mix and in the pan when I make this again.

Made a couple of eggs using this recipe this morning and absolutely LOVED them. I served mine with a dash of lemon pepper and a bit of Parmesan & Romano cheese. Heavenly! This one is a keeper for sure.

This was delightful. Sure, it's got a lot of butter. But life is short, right? So make these eggs, which literally took me 5 minutes (and only because I'm slow at cooking and re-read the recipe 10 times). Put them on top of some avocado toast. And have a lovely breakfast.

I've made this recipe twice and it is beyond delicious! These eggs remind me so much of the super creamy french-style scrambled eggs, but instead of stirring for 20 min, I can whip this up in under ten. The first time I made this I didn't have cornstarch and put a bit of half and half in instead. It was still great, but don't skip the cornstarch. I also halved the butter the second time around but I would suggest putting a little more than 2 tbsp. in. Fantastic recipe.
they indeed were creamy and nicely fluffy, but too rich for my taste. i'd cut the butter in a quarter and try that instead.
Well dang it. These eggs are simply delicious following the recipe. Now I’ll experiment to see how much butter I can cut out while maintaining the deliciousness!
My egg-hating husband loved these! Thank you for saving me from carb heavy breakfasts for eternity. I used 3 tbsp of butter and still felt it was very rich.

Creamy and delicious with the little chunks of butter, but I am not a fan of cornstarch slurry - it is more like ooze than cream. In lieu of butter, sometimes we like to add grate cheese, a little at a time throughout the cooking process, saving a bit for the end. About a 1/2 c for 5 large eggs.
I've found that using olive oil instead of butter gives me the creamiest scrambled eggs ever. The water in butter evaporates when cooking where as olive oil has no water. Try this, you'll like it !

This was easy to make if you are a beginner.  They were perfect....next time I will add some cheese or shallots- this is a good base recipe not only for breakfast but for lunch or dinner.  Good for kids!!!

Great alternative to low and slow. As long as you know where your starch, butter and eggs are, this recipe is insanely quick. Far less volume loss than when making low and slow scrambled eggs. I used half the butter my second time making them. Might even do less next time.

Key points: 
Butter for oil, temperature control, and flavor
Low and slow helped by cold butter cubes 
Starchy slurry
Maybe use milk or half and half
Excellent eggs!  Try the full recipe before adjusting!

The recipe was great! It was super easy and quick. My family loved it and thought it was better than regular scrambled eggs. My one comment would be only add one tablespoon of butter to the pan. Or alternatively, half butter and half olive oil, so it isn't too rich. People should try it before criticizing it.
The system to heat the pan is great. I never thought I'd say something was too buttery but me the eggs were.  Consistency was lovely

Made it this morning using cream. Excellent! Best scrambled eggs I've ever made. I've tried Gordon Ramsey's method, the double-boiler method, and standard "add a bit of water and push it around a hot pan with butter." This method is superior. It cooks fast. There are nice, soft curds. The flavor is eggy and buttery. But, the eggs aren't greasy at all. I didn't think it was too rich.

I am not a very good cooker of eggs lol, I can make just about anything else but.  However, I just made these, the pan is still hot, and my family thinks these are the best scrambled eggs they’ve ever had.
Thank you Kenji, for a great recipe.
Waaaaay too much butter. I would try again either halving or even quartering the butter. This is just a greasy mess.
Just follow the recipe. Most wonderful scramble eggs ever!

I eat a LOT of scrambled eggs! In higher altitudes like mine in Colorado you really don't want to beat your eggs very much
 I forget why but was told that straight away because my friend knew I loved them. You might want to check it out if you're in higher altitudes. In other words this recipe isn't for us.
Spot on! It's worth the fuss. A few more batches and it will be normal. This would be worthy of a five star restaurant breakfast standard.

I made this, exactly to the recipe.
