I liked it, but it is definitely a sometimes food. I like to eat eggs often for breakfast, but this recipe contains too much butter to eat regularly. This recipe is to normal scrambled eggs as cake is to bread. Frankly, I'll stick to my own recipe because I feel heavier eating them.
Do NOT use 4 T. Way too greasy. 1 T will suffice.

Added a few more eggs and kept all else the same... tasted amazing and plenty for five people to share (with some sourdough toast, of course!).  I also added a teaspoon of Tumeric, which is a must no matter what kind of eggs I’m making.  Trust me, it is amazing!
Nothing exceptional. And way too much butter!
Careful with the salt!
Big Kenji fan. These eggs are delicious, you don’t need butter for your toast. Make the recipe and enjoy the experience .

Mom always said “Butter makes it better.”  But this is a bit over the top. Amazing taste and texture but soooo rich. This is like a slice of cheesecake for breakfast. Save it for special occasions, like trying to get your BMI over 30 so you can get a Covid vaccination shot with 1B.

I made these as written using potato starch. I thought they were fantastic, light fluffy and delicious. My husband, on the other hand, found them gluey. I’m going to try cornstarch next and see if he changes his mind about the recipe.

Just made them and yes they are delicious!  Five stars.

But that's a LOT of  butter.  Next time I might try cold grated cheese in the mixture instead of butter (while still using melted butter in the pan.)
This elevated scrambled eggs to another level. Such a simple tweak yielded great results.

Made this with tapioca starch and 1 T of butter in 4 eggs, 1/2 a T in the pan (only because someone in our household doesn’t love butter) and these were great - fantastic texture and flavor. Easy enough to make this my go to!

I watched Kenji's video about this last night and made some eggs this morning. In the video, Kenji explains that the butter is not essential to the starch slurry and he makes it clear that you could go with much less butter. I found my egg-substitute scramble to be more tender than without the slurry. I eschewed butter and used oil for the pan. Chris Kimball says egg proteins unwind during cooking (opposite to Kenji) and uses high heat (barely smoking) and oil to get fluffy creamy scrambles.

Eggs have always been one those those "first loves" for me because standardly... thats the easiest thing to make when one starts learning how to cook. I made these scrambled eggs this morning and when I took my first bite all I could hear is hallelujah. I am never going to put plain eggs on a frying pan again!
PS: I did not use 4 tablespoons of butter. More like two.

These are delicious! In true NYT cooking fashion I did modify using 1/2 the butter in the eggs and in the pan for 2 Tb total. I don’t shy away from butter but too much butter in eggs don’t sit well with me. Conclusion- amazing. Genius technique. A little fussy but worth it. Fluffy, just like a perfect brunch spot. Thanks Kenji!
Our favorite local diner makes amazing scrambled eggs and I always wanted to know what they did differently than I did as home.  This recipe is it.  Restaurant/resort style eggs.
This is so much Extra. Want fluffier eggs? Add a little bit of milk as you whip them.
I am the worst at cooking eggs... But this was easy and delicious. Few ingredients, beginner skill, can't screw this up...

Well, WOW.  I think I found my new late night snack fix.  I just made this with 2 duck eggs and scaled back other ingredients accordingly.  Used potato starch.  Also used my trusty 8" cast iron pan.  Came out light, fluffy, smooth as silk, and as pillowy as a a cloud.  Will do this again.  Duck eggs of course have a bit more yolk so that's extra smoothness but I am sure it's dandy with good old chx eggs also.

I've been making the original Lady and Pups version for a few years and can never go back to scrambled eggs without a milk + cornstarch slurry.  If you enjoy a soft and creamy scramble, this technique is amazing.  I halve the butter (about .5 tablespoon per egg) and it still comes out great - I melt it in the pan beforehand rather than whisking it into the mixture.
Waaaaay too greasy. They were good but it felt like I was just eating melted butter
The best eggs I've ever eaten. Thank you.
These eggs came out beautifully even though I didn’t use quite as much butter, perfect for those days when you don’t feel like or have enough time to cook your eggs low and slow!

I made these according to the recipe, although I was a bit wary of the amount of butter. The eggs are indeed very creamy, but I think the butter is a little over-the-top. I will try again with half the butter and see how it goes. I should say that we're not afraid to eat rich foods in moderation in my household.

Folowed the directions to a T but did sub in half and half for the water. Cooked 1 1/2 minutes. My husband said they were the best homemade eggs he’s ever had. He said, “This is a compliment...they remind me of diner eggs”. I agree. So good.
I made this recipe this morning and it absolutely lived up to the hype. Thank you.

Wow. I just made these and no doubt these are the best scrambled eggs I have made and tasted. I followed the recipe exactly and the only slight problem I had was that some of the eggs browned slightly as I was not folding them quickly enough. I made 3 eggs and used 3 tablespoons of butter for the egg mixture but kept the butter for the pan at 2 tablespoons. All and all it turned out perfect and this goes on our Sunday breakfast list of items to make and when we have time.
perfect!  Works with 2 eggs as well.

Amazingly simple with magnificent results!  My slight alteration for future version will be to cook the mixture in olive oil, leaving the butter in the eggs.  Four tablespoons is a lot of butter, and I don’t want quite the overwhelming fat load so early in the day.
These are the best eggs I’ve ever had, made no changes to the recipe. I’ll be making scrambled eggs like this from now on!

This was fantastic. I used to use the Gordon Ramsay method (butter+eggs, lower heat, move the pan from heat to no heat every 15 seconds or so, stirring constantly, add salt/pepper last) but this is a definite contender. I might like it even better.

Contrary to some reviews, this recipe is not difficult nor fussy. But it’s a LOT of butter - and I love butter. I reduced the amount by half, and the eggs turned out great. Then again, I’ve never had complaints before...

I enjoy your recipes, also I often substitute ingredients with what I have at hand. I especially appreciate the comments of your members/cooks.
What I do miss are nutritional values. Could you include them please. Many of us have health conditions and have to watch cholesterol and carbs and don't have the time to research them. Thanks and keep inspiring us to cook well and with imagination.
Well, I did it after saying this is ridiculously fussy for eggs.  Results? DECADENT.  AMAZING TEXTURE.  PERFECT.  I don’t eat scrambled eggs that often, but when I do, this will be my go to.

I've made scrambled eggs for decades but these eggs are next level.  They are phenomenally creamy and flavorful. I did add some thinly sliced green onion to the melting butter (before adding the eggs to the frying pan) and it added some more flavor. Yum.

I found these eggs had way too much butter.  I tasted butter all morning. When I halved the amount of butter both in the pan and in the eggs, they were much more flavorful. The eggs were delicious and I'll use this method again.

These eggs were delicious but not worth even the extra minute of work- and definitely not worth all the extra fat and calories from the amount of butter. Try it out for something new then go back to your old ways.
Thanks for all the tips. I've consistently made creamy eggs for the last 3 days using your techniques.
Ok, this is fantastic. The eggs cook crazy fast (I do 2 eggs and it's done in ~20 seconds!) and are delicious.

Wonderful!  Although I really think these would work with less butter. Like maybe half - but I think it’s important to have some in the eggs and some in the pan because you get almost a browned butter effect with the pan as hot as it is, and then a gentle melt of the butter chunks in the eggs as they cook, adding a second layer of flavor. This is not a heart healthy recipe at all, but will be a treat once in a while.

Ok, yes, it seems fussy, BUT, if you actually follow this recipe, it's not hard and it's quick. Bottom line, it works! Fabulous! We love scrambled eggs with instant ramen and petite peas and sriracha. these scrambled eggs were a wonderful addition seasoned with trader Joe's everyday seasoning

Good recipe for people that don't know how to cook eggs properly I suppose. There's nothing wrong with it but this much butter isn't needed it coats the pallet and lips as I ate the eggs, and the starch addition (tried each starch) doesn't make a difference in texture compared to eggs Ive made with just eggs properly emulsified and some oil in the pan. Fully emulsify the eggs control your heat and movement and you'll end up with fluffy eggs every time. cooking pro for 15 yrs, cooking eggs for 25

I made is recipe with Tapioca starch. Definitely decadent and really interesting texture. Although I prefer a regular scrambled egg, I’m curious if this one travels better, say for an egg sandwich. Will make again with less butter.
Best scrambled eggs I've made in a while. So much quicker than low and slow but produces the same creamy texture!
Superb! No changes needed, perfect and delicious as is!
Just add some cream cheese when you cook your scrambled eggs...
These are wonderful! I reluctantly cut the butter in half because I’m counting calories at the moment, and even so these eggs turned out creamy and fluffy and delicious.
Loved this recipe! Feels weird not adding milk to the eggs, but the texture was fantastic.

I followed the recipe exactly. Once again, despite the naysayers, the author of the recipe is spot on. I love the fact that he also tells the NY Times readers how to scale the recipe. As a long-time follower of J. Kenji Lopez-Alt, I know what he writes is well tested.  My request to the NYTs: increase his contributions to the paper.

Just made these eggs, exactly per the recipe. . . except that I substituted LOL-Spreadable Butter for the butter.  The eager recipient found the eggs delectable!  This recipe is my new go-to, for sure.

Two comments re: these current NYT comments.  First, many folks mind that this recipe would require them to change their lifelong cooking habit.  Second, as long as posters 'fess up to  not following the recipe exactly, I really enjoy reading how they modified the printed recipe!

Ok.  So I tried it.  Sounded like too much work.  But as someone else said....once you get used to it it's routine.....and if it works, maybe you should give it a shot. And guess what... It's way beyond the best scrambled eggs I've ever made or ever had.  Even my wife who is a scrambled egg picky snob said these were the best she has ever had! And now that I have gone through the routine..including boiling some water as the pan heats....it wasn't so bad.  I used tapioca starch.  Excellent!
Easy, quick, and gives a great result with two eggs and one yolk.
This is delicious, but the four tablespoons of butter for four eggs is wicked rich. I made these eggs for breakfast, served with half an English muffin, and couldn’t eat anything else until evening.

Made this a couple of times, following the recipe exactly. Unanimous verdict from the household ... cornstarch adds an unpleasant slimy texture. I'm a massive Kenji fan - there's a much-thumbed copy of The Food Lab right next to my stove - but this one's a fail.

These eggs are delicious and worth the effort, but I disagree that they take 5 minutes. I consider my knife skills average for an experienced home cook, and chopping half a stick of butter into 1/4" cubes, then separating them, is pretty fiddly and takes me close to 5 minutes all on its own.

Great approach for fluffy, creamy soft scrambled eggs. I include less butter than called for when beating the eggs with the slurry, so it’s not as overwhelming, but finally have a recipe that approaches those amazing eggs at Bette’s Oceanview Diner in Berkeley - mmmm.
Too much butter. Going to try less.
I made these today and they are delicious! Very decadent. For next time I will use less butter (for my waistline) but this is a treat yourself day!
Definitely tasty, but way too much butter. Next time, I'll cut the recommended  4 tablespoons in half (one for the eggs, one for the pan).

I made similar eggs with milk (and slightly different formula the other day) but I'm excited to try these as is (sans milk), even though it's a ton of butter! YUM.

(I did: 3 eggs, 1/2 tsp of cornstarch, 1/8 tsp additional cornstarch per egg, 1 tbsp of milk per egg - all mixed - then I cooked them in 1 "pat" of butter (about 1tbsp) with the same method of letting them set and then swirling them with a chopstick. They were lovely.)
Wow. Amazing. Used Corn starch.
Delicious. Most tender eggs I’ve ever scrambled.
This is a game changer in my search for the perfect eggs. But cold butter in tablespoons! seriously‽

I tried this and felt the taste was bland the next time I added dried parsley and black pepper to the egg slurry before I added it to the pan 30 seconds before the eggs were done I added a tablespoon of grated parmesan and Romano cheese. Delicious!

These eggs are amazing! I used 3 eggs, a smaller amount of slurry and one tbsp butter diced into egg and one tbsp in pan because they were just for me but otherwise followed instructions exactly. I mean, wow, never had better eggs and the extra slurry step took no time at all and was so worth it! Follow the recipe.... you won’t regret it.
Tasty, smooth. I'm at a loss to understand the boiled water in Step 2. What's its purpose and it is not clear regarding how much should remain before swirling in the butter.
This was AMAZING! So delicious and easy. My new go-to recipe for scrambled eggs.

Made these eggs this morning. Eggs were creamy & a bit firm. Not all butter incorporated into eggs so they were a bit greasy. tasted fine but the family consensus is not as good as usual. We will be going back eggs beaten with heavy cream, butter in the pan on med low and slow cooked. Just as creamy and more tender. and less bother.
Excellent. A little more work but not that much. Remember to add Pepper which is not in the recipe.

I made this just as the recipe says and found that for my tastes the ratio of butter to eggs is too high.  I also felt I couldn't taste the eggs as well (and no I don't have COVID!).  I would try again with half the amount of butter and see if I like it better but so far I prefer other scrambled egg recipes with less butter compared to this one.

These are the best eggs I’ve ever made of any kind. I used tapioca starch, one extra egg, and finished with a generous crack of pepper and some finely grated white cheddar. Sublime. My breakfast-making confidence has soared.
Maybe I’m missing something but these tasted starchy and overly fatty. One of my least favorite egg recipes.
this note will seem redundant to those who praised this recipe, but i can’t resist adding my paean. Following the recipe to the letter, the eggs are mahvelous, darling, simply mahvelous!

Magnificent! Best scrambled eggs I’ve had outside of Paris. Easy and quick enough for every day but I’ll probably save them for special occasions because of the butter. Truly worth the calories-just not every day.
Use water not milk and cook slowly.
Not sure why the need for water that evaporates.
Believe that was an attempt to control the pan temperature and get it to the desired starting point.
I love all the great reviews that diverge from the original. LOL
Wow, this is a step up to the egg game. These tasted amazing!! I guess if you throw a half stick of butter in anything it's gonna help!
Mine came out oily or greasy.

Pretty good, wanted to love them.  Too corn-starchy and buttery, but pillowy & moist!  Pretty easy, and scrambled AREN'T easy to make right.  By comparison, love that “test-kitchen” recipe using ~8 eggs + 4 yokes, but longer to make, and hard to scale.  Followed this recipe for 2 eggs, but in a 10” cast iron skillet (closest pan we have), and cooking was ~ 30 seconds (so the pan temp seemed right).  At very least, will likely adapt the cubed butter going forward.

The recipe indicates that you COULD do this with less butter.  But, even butter-loving Julia Child would say you SHOULD do this with less butter.  Other than that, the texture and technique worked out well.  We'll try combining this starch technique with Gordon Ramsey's creme fraiche technique at some point and see what happens.

I found these almost unbearably rich, even after cutting back the butter (I cubed, but didn’t incorporate, the whole amount). I would try the slurry idea again but leave the butter out of it, just cooking the eggs in butter.
I totally agree with Michael!  Cooking for one, reduce recipe, keep ratio.  Perfect.  Thank you.

Excellent technique! In particular, if you're someone who likes your scrambled eggs fully cooked, with big curds, but want a creamier texture, look no further.

I strongly suspect that the benefit of the starch will still persist even with less butter; will be trying that next, as this is too much for a weekday.

Share

I finally made these delicious scrambled eggs for brunch today and ate all four creamy, buttery eggs!  This fine recipe proved to me, once again, that you can always learn something new.  At 76 years of age, I have just enjoyed the best eggs of my life and they were no trouble at all!

To add two tbsp of butter (over 200 calories composed largely of saturated fat) to a portion of two eggs (maybe 160 calories for two large eggs) seems a bit of...overkill (pun intended).   ...and don't forget, for creamier eggs you can replace the water with half-and-half!

Regular scrambled eggs are fine with me.

Also, the instructions say to push and fold the eggs "until they are slightly less cooked than you'd like them."  Could there be an issue here with salmonella and other pathogens?
Fun, easy recipe and definitely worth trying out. My wife and I are regular scrambled egg eaters, and this one passed the taste test. Easy to make and super fluffy. Will make again!
This is a great technique but the amount of butter feels like cheating? I served it on unbuttered toast.

I liked the technique. Unfortunately the fat to eggs and starch ratios are off for me. The eggs came out fluffy with the cornstarch slurry, but super oily and starchy. It also just tasted like sponges of oozing butter, without the flavor complexity of cream (because there was no milk or cream used in this recipe). Half a stick of butter for 4 eggs was too much. I felt sick afterwards, and had to use oolong tea to wash it down. I'll try the technique again with my own ratios.

I made this with 2 eggs (half recipe), and cut the butter slightly to 1 1/2 T, and 1 t corn starch. Used the water trick to have the pan at correct heat. The eggs cooked super fast and they were great. 

This is much faster than my standard old method. But my toast needs to be finishing when these hit the pan. Everything else ready. My new scrambles.

Simplified: Too much effort to cut butter in little pieces, plus it's too much butter. The cornstarch mixture is a good idea, then just cook the scrambled eggs as usual (waiting for a T of butter in the pan--for two eggs--to start bubbling). I cooked it both ways, and simplified was just as good
Tried these this morning, meh and a tad grainy. We'll go back to the usual.

I made these today. I halved the butter and they came out very rich and delicious. They were still VERY rich and I don’t think they’d be good with the full amount of butter. I’m happy for the starch trick!

I do 4 eggs each time, whip 'em good first, add a little milk, medium to med high heat, 1 tablespoon of butter, iron skillet, keep the eggs constantly moving once in to create the curd you want--last 20 seconds, pan off the heat--they come out fluffy and great.  It's about working quickly and efficiently. There's a lot of 'much ado' with scrambled eggs--you gotta keep it simple..

These are indeed the lightest, fluffiest and creamiest scrambled eggs I've ever had! I reduced the amount of butter because these eggs were sharing a plate with a cinnamon roll, and I didn't want to have a heart attacked before lunch. They were still wonderful. Also, the nutritional yeast was sitting on top of the cornstarch in the cabinet. I went ahead and added a teaspoon of that as well! Delicious!

I doubled this recipe and found it simply too rich - too much butter. I only had cornstarch on hand - so perhaps one of the other thickeners would work better? Followed recipe to a T and the eggs were indeed creamy - perhaps I will try with half the butter though I do typically get pretty nice creamy eggs by cooking them gently.
Looking forward to trying this recipe/technique with farm fresh eggs!

Well they’re certainly creamy 
But I had to check,  and re check, four tablespoons of butter!
I feel a little Ill now
I cooked this receipe and loved it.  Did it with cornstarch, followed the receipe as is with 4 eggs.  It is VERY easy to do and the eggs came out fluffy, creamy and moist.
Amazing recipy! I cooked it with tapioca starch and add oregano to serve. Delicious.
The. Best. Scrambled. Eggs. Ever.
I made this with half the butter and it was fantastic. The eggs were fluffy, moist, and flavorful. I will definitely use this technique again.
Terrific recipe! A revelation. Thank you for teaching me how to make creamy but firm eggs every time.
What is the sound of one egg beating?
So, so delicious. Creamier than the slow scrambled eggs that we often make at home. I cut the butter in half just to watch calories a bit, and it was still super creamy, buttery, delicious.
SO. GOOD. Will never make eggs any other way.

I've made this several times now - it's foolproof and really quick and easy. Honestly, it's worked great just kind of eyeballing the cubed butter/cornstarch amounts too which makes it even easier. It also even works fine with slightly less cubed butter. This is the definite way to go for perfect scrambled eggs.

I'd been making the Thomas Keller version, and the Jacques Pepin previous to that but this is quick and easy and makes a nice treat mid-week when you need something to get you through.

YUM
The 4 tablespoons of butter add 400 calories, but I am sure it is delicious.
A lot of work without much difference.
Definitely the best homemade scrambled eggs.

Cooked this as written and cooked this the old way, butter without the starch, whipped together in the pan a la Pepin.  Also tried it as written with a little water. Its the fat (the butter) that does the job.  The addition of starch didn't change things much, in either attempt.  Maybe throw in some turmeric instead.  That actually changes the taste some.

This was an interesting recipe and was nice and fluffy.  The flavor of the eggs was great, but there was so much butter in this recipe.  I thought it was somewhat excessive (even though I love butter).

I'm going to try this with a little cream and less butter to see how it turns out.
This was excellent! Period.
Lovely consistency but too rich for me. Just tasted like butter! The Mark Bittman low and slow method is the one for me. Glad I tried it though!

I am a big fan; this is the tops.

I use half as much butter and still find it very creamy.
It was a little more work - but the creamiest eggs I have ever eaten.  Can't wait to do again.

I tried this recipe last night and it resulted in the first plate of actual creamy scrambled eggs to ever come out of my kitchen, despite the many attempts I have made with low heat (that's just me! I am pretty hopeless). 

Like others, I did feel that the butter was a lot. Has anyone tried reducing and does it still come out okay? Also, am I the only one that almost never has room temperature butter? What happens when you use butter that is fridge temp?
Just let the butter sit out for a while or overnight. It'll be perfect.
These do not come out the same as “low and slow” eggs. Made these eggs this morning and my daughter thought I’d added cheese to them. So good.
This was too much butter for my tastes. It had a very oily consistency. I'll cut the butter back by a third or half next time. Otherwise, came out looking like the picture.

When I made with cornstarch it added a taste to the eggs.  Is the cornstarch meant to make more creamy?  I cut back on butter but otherwise follow the recipe.  Quick and great taste.  I agree that if you haven’t tried the recipe keep your comments to yourself.
Turned out great!
03/20/2021 Gotta say this was no improvement over my usual scrambled eggs, and the main noticeable effect of the cornstarch was to create a kind of unpleasant skin. Won’t do again.
wonderful eggs.

This will now be my go-to!  But the key is the corstarch, not butter. I've made this twice: 1) with the amount of butter in the recipe, and 2) with only 1/4 tbsp butter per two eggs. There wasn't much difference, honestly. If you're using a non-stick pan, the butter is adding flavor, not slickness, so a little goes a long way.

Overall, these are silky, smooth, dense, as opposed to fluffy and curd-ful. Can't wait to use this method to make an omelet!

This is such a cool technique that doesn’t feel like it should work but totally does. Fast, easy, perfectly fluffy eggs (and if you have a texture-obsessed toddler that means a lot). Thanks Kenji for your crazy attention detail. It is appreciated!
Amazing fluffy texture and so easy to make, but I felt sick after eating these from too much butter. Next time I’ll try with much less butter.

I tried this exactly as written. I also tried the diner trick of whipping the eggs well and then right before cooking them, adding just a splash of hot water.

Both were fluffy, this recipe yielded creamier eggs, though, which we love.
Used a blender  instead of the whisk . Recipe is great
Tasted very buttery but was creamy
Very little faff for the creamiest scrambled eggs ever. All of you moaning about the effort of chopping a bit of butter up and making a cornflour slurry don't know what you're missing!
The first time I try a new recipe I follow it to a “T” and this one was no different.  Lovely new take on scrambled eggs, but WAY too much butter for me.   I am dizzy from the fat overload.
Definitely halve the butter - the full amount leaves butter pooling on the eggs.
This recipe is so easy and good that my husband requests it on special mornings, and sometimes just weekday mornings because he likes it so much!
Don’t knock it till you try it! These eggs are surprisingly easy to make and worth the extra 2 mins of effort. My family of 4 all agrees.
I love the recipe.  The first batch I had to toss, way too much butter for me.  Now I use about 1/8 the butter listed.  For 3 eggs I use 1/2 tablespoon butter.  Thanks for the recipe!
My husband, who is an excellent cook, made these. Honestly, this recipe is gross. The texture is very strange. Not creamy. Almost slimy? Just make eggs, don’t add a slurry to them.

These eggs are creamy and brilliant. I made them exactly following the recipe (plus adding a little bit of fresh cracked pepper) the first time but there was way too much butter. Now I use just over half the recommended amount - and use milk instead of water for the slurry mix - and it’s absolute perfection to my palate.
I've made this with just the slurry and without the cubes of butter for an easier breakfast and it still comes out 10x better than eggs alone.
Just made this for 7 for brunch and I used exact multiples of ingredients. Cooked in two batches in one large, nonstick skillet. Everyone raved.

These are the best and most decadent scrambled eggs I’ve ever had.  This is now my go-to recipe.  And they’re very easy to make. 

I recommend making the recipe as is, then adjusting the butter to taste in the future.

My favorite variation of this is adding a Thai chili pepper (thinly sliced) to the  egg mixture.  Then when it’s finished, sprinkling cut chives on top.

Pretty good recipe, but I would use less butter. While it tasted good, I don't think it would be a good idea to eat it regularly for breakfast (health-wise). However the author of the recipe has great techniques - using corn starch & the hot pan idea were great. Will try this technique again but with less butter.

Frankly, I cannot understand how anyone could possibly like this recipe. The resulting eggs taste distinctly of cornstarch, not eggs. We found them completely disgusting and inedible--threw them out and started over. (Yes, I made sure that I was using the amount of cornstarch called for by the recipe, and the cornstarch was not bad or stale.)

Simply fabulous!
After 40 years of trying and failing to make good scrambled eggs, I am finally the Egg Queen!!
Thank you!1

I think folks complaining that this recipe is “too much work” have not actually tried it.  The whole process from start to finish is quicker than cooking eggs on low heat.

The eggs turned out wonderfully. I have always mixed my eggs with a bit of milk, salt, and pepper and cooked on low heat, but they turned out runny a quarter of the time.  This recipe made by far the best scrambled eggs I’ve ever had.

This method makes the absolute best, fool-proof scrambled eggs. And I hate scrambled eggs— except that, now, with this recipe, I love scrambled eggs. P.S.—I left out the salt, and added two slices of (yes) Kraft American cheese. Great cheesy eggs…

Kraft American cheese gets a bad rap.  The fact is that it's the best melting cheese around and works best on burgers as well.  I admit, however, that I don't know what's in it or whether it's actually cheese or better-living-through-chemicals.
I scaled down on the butter (I used 2 tablespoons) and the egg still turn out nice and creamy. Great recipe for a nice quick breakfast. Thank you for this recipe!

This was genius. I make big batches of Greek scrambled eggs (another excellent NYTCooking recipe) scaled to 25-30 eggs for weekend brunches and had resigned myself to accept standing at the stove for an eternity of constant low heat curd pushing. This was by far the fastest batch and the BEST texture I have ever achieved. Thank you Kenji and NYTCooking!
Insane amount of butter. I couldn't finish it.

These were the creamiest eggs I’ve ever had and I made them exactly per recipe…until I decided to beat in full fat yogurt 1 generous tablespoon per 2 eggs. Same slow-ish heat and buttered pan but none of the other corn starch, water or butter in batter fuss. Works well with added cheese—I’m partial to chèvre or cheddar. Even creamier than original, and easier.
This recipe doesn't call for slowish heat or "batter". It calls for med-high. Your yogurt recipe is an entirely different recipe and technique.

I have tried this recipe several times and used my recipe as well.  Kenji's recipe delivers every time.  Creamy and soft plus it gives you extra time until the eggs are done in case you are topping warm toast with the scrambled eggs.  Awesome recipe!

3 eggs beaten with about a tablespoon of cream and about a tablespoon of sour cream, cooked in hot melted butter, stirring and sort of flipping. Takes about 1-2 minutes I think although I haven't timed it.

Best. Eggs. Ever. Not difficult at all, and anyone saying that it’s too much faff has clearly not bothered to even test the recipe. Easy and quick and the most beautiful buttery eggs ever. A real treat.
Add butter when you make your scrambled eggs and they will be creamier!  Thank you.
Excellent! A bit fussy but good result.
Delicious!! But a little too butter-forward for my taste. I’ll make again but cut back on the butter and add another pinch of salt.
My mother taught me to put milk or cream in eggs decades ago! This recipe should work very well as I have done eggs this way my whole life!!
This is a great technique that renders scrambled eggs soooo much better than normal.  The only issue is remembering it.
Terrific recipe. Yeah, it took 2 minutes longer than how I usually scramble eggs. But I made a huge lifestyle adjustment and just committed to it. Worth it.
I would kill to watch a video of JKL-A and Jaques Pepin cooking scrambled eggs side-by-side. Nothing but love on either side. I'm definitely going to try this soon.

While this is a "cute" approach to creamy eggs there is a much better way to make them. Try the French approach to making scrambled eggs - it doesn't require anything added to the eggs, like a starch. You cook the eggs low and slow, stirring them. I first had them at a bed-and-breakfast in Ghent, Belgium. I loved them and looked into how it was done. Check out this from ATK: https://www.americastestkitchen.com/recipes/10329-creamy-french-style-scrambled-eggs
The whole point of this recipe is that it's fast enough to make on a weekday morning. That America's Test Kitchen recipe calls for 14 minutes of constant stirring.
Wonderfully fluffy yes however nearly 800 calories in this whole dish!  400 cal. in the butter. ~360 cal for eggs and 20 cal. for cornstarch.  Very delicious.
These eggs were the bomb.com!  Small amount of work and really upped my scrambled egg game.
It tastes excellent, my new favorite scrambled eggs recipe!
Excellent. I think it's the butter...

This recipe is a winner.  It is now the only way I cook scrambled eggs, a breakfast favorite.  It results in fast, creamy, incredibly delicious eggs.  I have always thought of scrambled eggs as fairly innocuous.  However, this recipe elevates scrambled eggs to another level entirely, and I have delighted in making them like this since I discovered the recipe.  I wouldn't make them any other way.  I am grateful!

I reduced the butter to half, and they still tasted very good, and I also halved the tapioca starch as I didn't want them too soft, and I wanted to balance the reduction of butter. I couldn't bring myself to put that much butter in my scrambled eggs. They were perfect, in my opinion :)
And now this is the only way I want my eggs cooked. Thanks Chef Kenji!

Taste in scrambled eggs tends to be pretty specific. That said, I followed directions, found them simple enough, but the texture was not to my liking. Perhaps I took them out too soon, fearful of overcooking, and found them matching another commentators phrase :“slimy”.   I will try one more time and edit here. But go back to the recipe from the Sconset Cafe cookbook. It uses milk ,far less butter,and softened cream cheese chunks to stir in until set.  Can add parsley and  dill to the Cheese.
Just made them, they are excellent, my wife said they were her "best ever". Easy to make but just different enough to put on a good show.
The starch can go directly into the eggs without dissolving in water (eggs are mostly water). Whisk. Grate in 2 tablespoons cold butter. Whisk. Proceed.
Holy Moly these are delicious. Followed the recipe exactly. Very buttery. Looking forward to trying them with slightly less butter.

Wow! These are awesome. The eggs are rich and delicious. Made exactly as recipe states (used corn starch as I couldn't find the potato starch) though I halved it for only 2 eggs. This would not be for an everyday breakfast for us as it is way too rich but wonderful to try and have in my repertoire.
Made it as written using cream instead of water and added shredded sharp cheddar to the bowl while whisking. Fantastic.

I followed the exact recipe, portions and all, save for cutting the butter content in half. Turned out marvelous. I also sprinkled some Parmesan / cracked pepper on top after they were done, because that’s my business. We will always be adding starch to our scrambled eggs from now on! Thank you, Kenji!
Perfect exactly as written!
With Half the Butter? Still verrry rich and definitely wonderful. I’ve long done Quick Scrambled Eggs with a starchy slurry but the double use of butter is brilliant. Thanks, Kenji!
Delicious and worth the effort. Perhaps not a part of the regular rotation given the amount of butter - but a delight nonetheless.
This was incredibly good. The creamiest, most buttery eggs I’ve ever had! Made this on Christmas morning and everyone gobbled it up.  Don’t listen to reviewers that haven’t made it!!!
Great recipe. Perhaps tone down a bit the butter amount ( or measure it better than I did..), but truly a great upgrade to my standard scrambled eggs.
I normally only go for sunny side up fried eggs and have skipped past scrambling recipes.  No longer!!! This was wonderful, recommended by my mother and an absolute keeper.
Made as described, LOVED them. I might try with half the butter next time though.
I was sceptical about adding corn starch but as I love scrambled eggs, I thought I’d give it a try. Wow, it really does work, the eggs were so soft and creamy! This will be my new way of doing eggs!
Great lazy morning egg recipe.  Decadent with Kerrygold butter. The curd is pretty special...creamy, fluffy and slightest bit of tooth.  You could maybe cut butter in half, but why?

I tried this recipe, hoping the result would be more like restaurant scrambled eggs. I rarely ever cook eggs for myself at home. I do, however, make eggs regularly for the plongeur. I followed the recipe precisely, using potato starch. Compared to my standard method, three beaten eggs cooked at low heat in about a teaspoon of salted butter, he didn't like this recipe at all. Since he doesn't cook, he'll never know how much butter he just ate, and I'll never tell. Won't be cooking it again.

How are we whisking cold, cubed butter in with the starch and water, then eggs? The butter will remain in cubes… no? Just wanting to make sure I don’t have to break it down further to ensure better disbursement in wet eggs. Thanks!

Yes, but if you cut them into smaller 1/4" cubes they do break up a bit and blend OK with the rest of the ingredients (but are still in chunks).  Once the pan is ready and you drop in the mixture, the cubes will melt as the eggs cook.  Basically, it's not intuitive but seems to work out in the end.  Hope that helps!
Delicious in the moment but so rich that it lead to stomach aches in my household.
An excellent recipe, worth the minimal effort it takes to enjoy fantastic scrambled eggs

I made this exactly per recipe. They were delicious, but a bit too rich for me. Next time I’ll cut down on the butter. Maybe start with half and see how that is. 
But the corn starch gives such a silky texture. Very nice. And faster than the low and slow I usually do. Also, I usually add fresh grated Parmigiano Reggiano, and while I’ll probably still will, I’ll make it a couple more times without it so I can adjust the butter to my taste. Good technique.
Everything’s better with a half stick of butter.  This is a very tasty and not-so-smart recipe.
Recipe is truly fantastic. Makes a huge difference.

Fabulous recipe. I can see why it ranked so highly for the inevitable year-end ranking. I, too, consider myself a scrambled egg perfectionist but this old dog isn't too proud to learn some new tricks. One very important distinction that I wanted to raise, however - there is far too much butter in this recipe. We are making eggs here, not cakes. I cut this ingredient in half - basically one tbsp in the mixture and one in the pan. It's still just as creamy but won't clog your arteries as fast. :-)

Still find the best “creamy” eggs recipe is the method with water and very slow cooking. No fat at all. Takes a bit more time but is fabulous. They truly have a creamy texture (salt and chopped parsley add flavor).
Can't wait to try this. One tip I like is to use chopsticks to mix and scramble the eggs.
Why is no one discussing the salt? I guess it's not the main point here, the starch is... but adding salt before cooking eggs hardens them, no? Either way the starch is a great little hack
Best eggs ever! I often reduce the butter a bit and it is still wonderful.
Try adding a dollop of mayo....
I make mine with a splash of cream and plenty of butter and slowly scramble on a low heat. But I will try this method.
I'm sure this is delicious. It has a ton of butter in it- a quarter cup for 4 eggs!

Love these ideas! 

I've found that if I heat the plate on the top of my toaster oven while my bread is toasting, then when I put the eggs on the plate, they stay warm and delicious longer.
Just way too much butter. Half the butter, half the corn starch and water mixture.

These eggs were delicious, but I don’t understand how the cold butter is supposed to dissolve when I’m whipping up the eggs. My clumps of butter remained clumpy. It was fine, but I don’t get the recipe exactly on this point.
Worked very well in a cast iron, just make sure to get it evenly warm. I found it doesn’t need to be hot, the way I would for a fried egg.

Very yummy, but some aspects of the recipe (cutting the butter into 1/4 inch cubes, the water in the pan, the instruction about where to put your plate while you're cooking) seem needlessly fussy. Could this recipe not be simplified to: "make scrambled eggs the way you usually do, but add a little cornstarch mixed with water to them before you cook them, and double the butter."

Meh, liked ‘em but didn’t love them. If I make them again, I’d probably reduce the butter and the starch by 1/3 (or increase the eggs to 6). There’s a kinda weird, filmy texture to these. Also, since I already make reasonably soft scrambled eggs, so stopping 1-2 minutes early may also be part of the problem for me — would probably stop in the future at the time my eggs are done instead.

These are absolutely delicious, and I'm very glad I made them, but IMO not worth the amount of butter they call for. At the very least, I wouldn't make these more than once a month, for fear I'd drop dead of a heart attack half way through the meal.

I followed the recipe exactly using a non-stick pan, and fresh farm eggs from my CSA.  The texture was wonderful, the eggs were not greasy and despite all the butter it didn’t present as overly rich. It’s a great technique and I’m sure it  could be modified to use less butter or even duck fat, which I’ll try next time. Omitting or limiting the corn starch I think would be a mistake.

This recipe is fantastic. My eggs are both creamy and fully, and they cook fast. I used 2 tbsp butter and it was perfect for me. I'm sure it's delicious with more, but I wanted it to be a little less rich. It turned out beautifully.

Tried this for breakfast this morning with the recipe as is. Definitely more for someone who can tolerate the richness of the butter. I find that I can make eggs with similar fluffiness without the abominable amount of butter this recipe calls for.
Two teaspoons cornstarch. It's a pudding.

Superb. This is what you'd expect from an Alice B. Tolkas or Fannie Farmer recipe: Legendary. The eggs quick much more quickly without toughening (I'd previously used a low heat with a long cook). And the texture is indeed "pillowy." The hardest thing is cubing the butter; and how hard is that?
This works! And it only took 15 mins from mix to plate.
Either I used abnormally small eggs or there was WAY too much butter in this recipe.
This was a great, quick tip. Lovely creamy scrambled eggs in less time. Perfect.

Yes, I made it. Twice, actually because I assumed that I messed something up the first time because these were the most normal scrambled eggs ever, except they took more fat and more work than my usual ones. They were good, but not worth taking the simplest meal and adding steps, and honestly not better than what you probably already make. Sorry to be a naysayer, but I don’t get the hype at all.
Wow!  Much faster than low heat methods.  Easy and fantastic results. I did cut down on the butter quite a bit and it seemed to do no harm.
Really fantastic.  Followed recipe exactly.  I thought the amount of butter was too much - eggs were greasy and releasing oil on the plate, so I will back off a bit next time.

While extra creamy, I thought the addition of the large amount of butter and cornstarch diluted the "egginess" for much higher saturated fat cost. Will try it again, but so far eggs w a bit of milk in a pan w less butter tastes better to me.
The recipe works even with less butter. I used 2 tablespoons of butter in total and the eggs were fantastic, much better than my previous attempts.  This will be my new standard for scrambled eggs.

Made this today.
Very easy. Some reviews claim that it's to much work. It's not.
Is it a better? No. If you're experienced, perfect eggs of any kind are easy, but it does take practice.
Not a fan of this though.
They taste different, with a loss of egg flavor. They are creamy and fluffy, but...
They have the same "off" flavor that is  simular to the technique of adding baking soda to onions when carmelizing them. Always willing to try something new if it elevates or is easier
This does neither.

I love these egg! Very easy to make and creamy. I used cornstarch because that’s what I had and I cut the recipe in half since it was just for me. Only change I’ll make is using slightly less butter. In all fairness though I just eyeballed the 2 tablespoons I used so it could’ve been a measuring error.
Too much butter. UGH. I love butter, to an unhealthy degree. This was still too much. Bleh. I’ll keep experimenting to get the proportions right for me.
Definitely a special Sunday morning or brunch item because they are very rich and creamy. Eggcellant!

Having read the comments before trying this, I cut the butter to two tbsp (one in the eggs, one in the pan). Also grated the butter rather than cubing it. We used three eggs rather than four. And it was terrific and not at all greasy.

Wow, I have been scrambling what I thought were perfectly presented eggs for decades and it took some thinking just to get me to follow the recipe. The scramble was as visually appealing as it was fluffy and delicious. Thank you.
The best, never tasted any better anywhere else. And now we know how to make it in our own kitchen every morning.
Great recipe!!! I made the recipe with a less butter and added Penzeys Justice seasoning and it tasted great. I always wondered how to make creamy eggs and now I know their secret!

This was delicious (& one of my sons likes well done scrambled eggs, the other super runny!) agreed would cut the butter by 1/3-1/2 next time (but my husband said it worked out because if you put these on a bagel you don’t need to butter the bagel :). )
Easy, soft, flavorful. Perfect scrambled eggs! Thanks for the recipe.
I go to Gracie Mews and ask for soft scrambled...perfect everytime.

This recipe is phenomenal!  I've cooked it many times and the eggs come out creamy and delicious every time!  You can't go wrong as long as you follow the recipe.  My only "critique" is I dialed back the butter just a small bit, but that's me.  No matter, it's still wonderful.  Kenji also has a video on his YouTube channel for this as well (it was for this exact NYT Cooking recipe).  Great work Kenji!
This was easy to do, and the results were delicious and quick.  There was a lot of butter, and I might use less butter the next time.
Don't err on the side of more butter. I had slightly over 4 tablespoons and the eggs were almost too buttery.
This is delicious!
What does the water do for the pan?
The absolute best!

I made them, and it was good. I grew up in Hawaii and like some Asian flavors to eggs, so when they were done I drizzled on some sesame oil, adds a nice touch of nuttiness - overall, really good eggs and they do stay moist - and it was not much work at all - as good or better than any scrambled eggs I've had eating out.

How does watching water evaporate help? All water will eventually evaporate, unless you’re living in a rainforest, regardless of the temperature of the pan. If you don’t say how quickly it’s supposed to evaporate, this is totally useless.
My uncle made the best scrambled eggs, not too wet, not to dry. His secret was scramble the eggs well with light cream, salt, and pepper. Cook them in butter. Don't over stir.
What is the purpose of the water in the pan, and then letting it evaporate off?

This recipe is absolutely delicious. For better consistency, I registered 280 F on the center of my Scanpan when applying the butter to the pan. You don't really have to wait for water to evaporate as your test.

Tried this in the am for Sunday breakfast with husband.  Followed it exactly. Seemed the real purpose of the exercise was to properly heat the non stick pan. Good eggs but heart attack on a plate. Next time I will try starch slurry with a T of half and half and spray the Pan with ghee. Perhaps a Pat of butter in the eggs.

Finally!!
A good use for the pound of potato starch I bought to make homemade ramen (which required all of a teaspoon, and I’ve made only once).  
Thx, Kenji!! 🙌🏼

such a complication of something simple
forget the starch
butter or cream is ok
no substitution for doing it slowly on low heat though
if you want fat eggs you will not get the unctuous scrambled kind lopez is striving for.
this is not a substitute… poor research jil’s

We loved these!  But I admit that I was nervous to use so much butter...I used 1 1/2 tb. for the eggs, and 1 1/2 tb. for the pan.  This recipe is totally a keeper, and I do think I will try one more slightly less butter scramble...a total of 2 tb. versus the original 4 tb.
Yes yum.
I downright crave these eggs now. I've experimented with a little bit less butter and 3 tablespoons is plenty, 2 might lessen the tastiness. Delicious!
I really wanted to love these but I couldn’t get over the aftertaste - there was a sense of chalkiness from cornstarch. The texture was otherwise great. Definitely pillowy.

These are definitely the best scrambled eggs I’ve had.  Rich and creamy and only marginally more work than other methods.  The amount of butter in them definitely makes them something to make only occasionally, but what an occasional treat they are!  Be sure to serve them with some slice tomatoes, fruit salad orange juice to help temper the richness!
So good!
Mouthfeel was too slimy for me.  And I didn't like the starchy taste.
ABSOLUTELY AGREE.  Glad I'm in good company.  I thought so, too.
Would melting the butter first then adding it to the starch mixture make sense and or effect the end result?
Add an extra egg yolk (no white) no matter how you make your eggs.  Richer and better color.
Cut back on some of the butter due to health concerns.

Cut back on the (potato, corn, etc)starch due to health concerns!
Why add unnecessary carbs to a perfect (and delicious) protein?!?
Sounds divine. Drug companies that sell meds for high cholesterol are see their profits soar, if you eat this recipe often!!
Used half the potato starch and water for 2 eggs and less than a tablespoon of butter.
Very light and fluffy w large curds. I did follow the recipe exactly which I do every time I try a new recipe. Very tasty. Might try less butter next time but very good as is. Simple to make.
Look I LOVE butter. But WAY too much in this recipe. Next time I will reduce by 1/2 and see how it goes
I made this today.  I followed the recipe exactly.  I like butter, but there's much too much butter in this recipe, 1 TBS per egg.  Overwhelms the egg flavor and textures.
Very tasty eggs. I think next time I might only add 1 T of butter to the eggs to lower the fat content. Thanks Kenji

Abacadabra!!!!     

This method? Two eggs and soft curds, fast? Sure. Fool proof and no diligence necessary. Just use a corn starch/butter chunk slurry, adjust the proportions and pan size (add more slurry to keep it loose if a larger pan). Be sure to have the toast and bacon or sausage done already, because the eggs will take no time.
This was super creamy but just too buttery.  I’d try a bit less butter next time.

I have tried this type recipe but now  I use my Breville/Polyscience Control Freak induction device which maintains the non-stick pan at a constant 235 degrees F and premixed egg mixture pus egg white in a carton from Walmart or Aldi adding the potato starch slurry adding to the the pan with a little duck fat instead of butter.  It is quick, tasty  and saves the trouble of keeping fresh eggs and I think healthier as there is less yoke.

It's Sunday morning so I decided to try it! It is very good and very creamy! Perhaps a bit too much butter for me. Next time (and there will be a next time) I will reduce the amount of butter by half!
Made as described and they were great however I had to take a shower afterwards!

Thanks to Ina Garten, I make the most delicious scrambled eggs simply by adding a little heavy cream, salt and pepper to the beaten eggs and cooking them gently in melted butter. Everyone says their the best eggs they've ever had, and want to know my secret. Heavy cream. I can't imagine adding starch to eggs. Try them this way, and thank Ina.
If you can't imagine it, then try it.
I agree with the comments about it being very rich... To my taste, the amount of butter really overwhelms the recipe.
I agree about this calling for too much butter. The serving bowl was a butter slick. Meh.
Used one TBS of mayonnaise instead of butter not quite the same taste but very good. I use a battery powered milk frother to mix the egg mixture and added salt and pepper after plating.
Yummy!  Truly the best, but all this work will make it a rare dish in our house. Serve with sourdough toast and Palisade peach jam for an extra special breakfast.

Recently, I saw a YouTube of a Chinese guy making this method called Hong Kong eggs.  Using oil, not butter, and adding evaporated milk.  Of interest, find a different video called Chinese eggs which take a bit more work.
I do not recommend using this recipe. Four tablespoons of butter with only 4 eggs is absolutely over the top. The result is a sickeningly rich, greasy mess. I love butter, but this is too much.
Just as good but easier to add a tablespoon or two of cream, then cook and stir/fold on low heat as directed. I scramble in the pan to save a dirty bowl cause I’m lazy, obviously.
I love butter more than anything but this is too much. Use 3 tablespoons and throw some grated sharp cheddar on top. Otherwise they were great.
I find the egg tastes a little 'dusty' for adding the slurry. Had anyone else found that?
This is amazing. It was a bit too rich for me (but not for my partner); I couldn't even finish my 2 eggs! I'm going to try it again with 6T of butter, rather than 8. Perhaps she won't mind...

This is my most favorite way to make scrambled eggs now, and I thought I was pretty good at scrambled eggs before. I always use cornstarch and they turn out great. I like them best on a Saturday morning with toast and fruit.

I get light, fluffy scrambled eggs by using a Tupperware shaker, add eggs, add milk @ 1/8 cup per egg. Shake shake shake... Fry in butter. Scramble as soon as the eggs start to form solids.salt and pepper while cooking.
Excellent recipe! These were honestly the best scrambled eggs I’ve ever made. Give it a try.
There is no need to add starch to eggs at all. Do it French style. Low heat. Stir.
I just tried this—I am not a good cook, but I can follow directions. These eggs are fantastic, and I’m glad there are recipes like these for people like me

I am a huge Kenji fan and consider him as gospel when it comes to cooking. In this case however I followed the recipe to a tee and am not convinced. The curds, while fairly tender, seemed tight and on the verge of releasing liquid. And as others have noted, they were a bit greasy. I am looking for  the perfect scrambled egg formula, and this was not it.
The best scrambled eggs I've ever made. Thank you.

The starch really does work.  I have used the low temperature cook method to get a similar result.  I like this better, same result much quicker.  After using the original recipe, which was delicious but kind of drowned in butter, I made it with a healthy (not that kind of healthy) dollop of heavy cream in the egg mixture instead of the butter.  I used the same butter in the pan.  Still very soft and creamy just not swimming in melted butter.

Like many other commenters, I’ve never “needed” to change from my low-and-slow method to produce great scrambled eggs. My first reaction was to dismiss this recipe as completely unnecessary. However I tried it this morning and the results were phenomenal - ultra creamy and decadent. Maybe it was the 4 tablespoons of butter, but I will use this technique again!
Way too much butter in my opinion. It’s good if you use half the butter.

Eggs are my nemesis. I love them, but man I always mess them up. These were the best eggs I ever made.  Fluffy, bright, flavourful, not runny. I ate ‘em with some fresh cracked pepper and a great sense of self-satisfaction. Ignore the smug naysayers round here.
Love these eggs! My boyfriend and I made them as part of a nice Sunday morning treat

Another recipe that leaves one scratching one’s head by deliberately omitting details, sloppy editing ,or simply arrogant presumption. 

“Make sure your skillet is at just the right temperature by heating a tablespoon of water in the skillet and waiting for it to evaporate. For creamier eggs, you can replace the water with milk or half-and-half.”

Imagine the scummy, flaky or burnt mess remaining after evaporating over a medium hot stove! And what is “just the right temperature, anyway? Ugh.
Nice and creamy eggs!  I a little oregano and parsley because it’s how I like my eggs, but it didn’t hurt the texture at all.

I used to dislike scrambled eggs because they are always either too runny or too overcooked. This recipe turned me into a fan. I reduce the amount of butter though. I recently made some compound butter from leftover Thanksgiving herbs & I highly recommend using it as a substitute to regular butter!
Fantastic recipe. Husband loved it!!!
I make this recipe all the time.  We have them for breakfast & dinner.  They are so creamy and soft.  They remind me of the eggs we had in France.  I've passed this recipe on to several friends.

Wonderful recipe for a weekday morning-- it's much faster than waiting a long time, and it works with just a few eggs in a small skillet with cornstarch. It makes a big difference in getting creamy egg results fast without actually adding cream or milk.
Subbed limoncello for water. Topped with caviar and dill. Perfection.

Took the advice of commenters and used about 2.5 tblsps of butter; the tablespoon of water took longer to evaporate than I expected.  Cooked extraordinarily fast.  Glad to have a plate at the ready since it was done in two minutes.  Sprinkled with minced parsley but forgot to add salt until I was about to bring it to the table.  Served it for a dinner with guests and elicited kudos from everyone.  Definitely a keeper.
Use an immersion blender to scramble the eggs.....terrific results!
Love the easy recipe AND extended discussion of cooking chemistry! I subscribe to Cook’s Illustrated, appreciating subtle nuances that temperature & ingredients impact quality. More Science!
Pass this one over for the new hot egg dish: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1021931-extra-creamy-scrambled-eggs . Corn starch always gave this dish a weird taste...
I've had success with this method but using cream of tartar in place of the starch. It gives it a unique, tangy kind of flavor and my kids love it.

This intrigued me so I thought I’d give it a go. I cut the recipe in half since it was just for me. These came out fabulous. Soft, fluffy and creamy. Definitely a bit more effort than your quick scramble but one worth doing if time allows. Thanks.
I am eating these eggs right now, so creamy. I like scallions in my eggs so I chopped one up & added it to the melting butter til sift & then added egg mixture. Really Good!
I have been using this recipe for several months now and love it. The best scrambled eggs I have eaten.

I make my scrambled eggs in a covered glass jug (Anchor) with a splash of whole milk and or cream, normal salt (Kosher salt is only useful in preserving meat for confit) and pepper, with a handful of grated tasty natural cheese. Cook on High Power in microwave for 30 seconds, stir, cook for a further 30 seconds, stir, and a further 30 seconds.
Followed the recipe and used corn starch. I went with a leisurely stirring and had pillowy, soft eggs that might be the best I have ever had.

I tried this recipe with and without the chopped up butter mixed into the eggs before cooking.  Honestly, it didn’t make much difference, requires dirtying a knife and cutting board, and adds more time for little benefit. The cornstarch is what really makes these significantly better than standard scrambled eggs.
I tried this recipe twice and it just is too greasy. Next time I’ll try it with less butter.
The best eggs I have ever made.
I made this recipe and it took a couple seconds longer than usual and I loved the result. So tasty.

Made these exactly as written and my partner called them “perfect”. These were fabulous, fluffy, buttery, and buoyant. I watched Kenji’s accompanying video on his YouTube channel, and it was the perfect pairing. I will save this and make many times over!
Wow. Best eggs I’ve ever made and I love eggs. I followed the recipe exactly! Delicious.
Fantastic. I followed the recipe. No substitutions. Best eggs I’ve ever made and I eat a lot of eggs. Thank you! This is my scramble recipe going forward. My only concern is how did I miss this?

I just made this for 6 people. EXCELLENT! It got rave reviews. Definitely the best scrambled eggs I’ve ever eaten. I didn’t need as much butter for the pan, but I followed the recipe to the letter otherwise. 

As others have pointed out, one big advantage to this over the low and slow method is that it is much quicker (even accounting for cubing the butter) and so is a good method when you’re in a hurry or trying to prepare several other dishes for brunch.
What a delish treat on a Keto morning!!
Adjusted the recipe for only two eggs and WOW YUM. Had to add a tiny bit of extra water for the cornstarch slurry but other than that, no changes needed. :)
Best eggs I have ever made or tasted. Did not modify recipe. Amazing.

This method works to make great scrambled eggs--I have made it this way, but I'll stick to my easier method which is to beat 4 eggs with about a tablespoon each of both sour cream and heavy cream until frothy, and cook in a 10in non-stick pan after melting about a tablespoon of butter in it.
This is, in my opinion, the best way to cook scrambled eggs. I’ve gotten numerous compliments on them made this way. Follow the recipe. You’ll love them.

This is an excellent way to get creamy eggs.To the melted butter add a small amount of ricotta or cream. Keep the heat low and be patient and alert, gently scrambling, Take it off the burner just before it looks all dry..
Delicious but why add starch and copious butter when slow scramble on low eat equally delicious

Made this for Easter brunch yesterday.  I tripled the recipe and used a full dozen eggs.  As I was cooking the dozen eggs, I kept thinking to myself that "this is a lot of butter" and thought perhaps I might get some complaints about the amount of butter.  Anyway, the dish turned out great: the curds were very light and fluffy and did not taste of too much butter.  I loved them as did my oldest daughter.

Delicious and decadent. Yes, a little too buttery, so this time I served over dry toast. I made 2nd batch with half the butter. So so so good. Someone on here commented "Slimy"??? No way. The curd is light as popcorn and smooth as silk.
Cut the butter by half. Very good, but artery clogging. would be just as good with less.
Can I use instant tapioca "bubbles" instead of the "tapioca starch" called for?
For me this was too much butter (maybe because I used a ceramic pan?), and was actually greasy in the pan, but the texture of the eggs was fabulous. Will try again with a little less butter.
So, where is the mistake in ingredients (butter)...the list or the instructions?  Ingredients call for 4 tablespoons, whereas the instructions include 5 tablespoons.
Step 1 calls for half the butter cubes (i.e., 2T), and Step 2 calls for the remaining 2T.

I tried to follow as written, but struggled with the timing, just very different from my usual.   I ended up with creamy eggs, but not amazing, and I felt I could detect the texture of the starch.  It was also too much butter for me.  My usual technique is to cook loosely beaten eggs with salt in a bit of butter over a low heat, stirring sporadically,  and to add milk as it sets to temper the final stage of cooking.  The result is very creamy, with a lovely taste and it's less fussy.
Why the starch?  Butter, eggs, salt, pepper and cook slowly

I use Sally Baking Addiction’s recommendation to let all muffins cook at high heat for first five minutes to get a puffy top - I did this for this recipe at 425 and then lowered to 375. The muffins needed around 15 minutes in total to cook this way (5 at 425 and 10 at 375). I also only added 1/3 cup of brown sugar because my yogurt had some in it already. It was great!

I tried this with tapioca starch using a little less than a teaspoonful with two eggs.  Yes, they were wonderfully creamy and fluffy, but there was a definite taste from the tapioca that I did not enjoy.
This was spectacular! The best scrambled eggs yet.

This is a mind-blowing, 100% consistent recipe with stunning results.

When running low on cornstarch I discovered I could use 1/4 tsp for 2 eggs and still achieve eggy heaven-on-earth.

Many thanks, JKLA!!
Oh, and I used EVOO not butter. SOOOOOOOOOO delicious.
I think the texture is fantastic, but a bit too buttery for me. I’ll try halving the butter next time and see how it turns out.

For anything egg I follow Julia Childs. A little butter melted over medium low heat, add lightly beaten eggs, stir every few seconds until firm but not cooked, turn off heat and add a little cream or milk and let the cream or milk cool the eggs in the pan while you stir. Only season after cooked with salt/pepper to taste. So simple, and velvety soft scrambled eggs.
My new method for scrambled eggs! I made it according to the recipe except that I used olive oil in the egg mixture, butter in the pan.

I made this today after reading positive reviews from people who had tried the recipe. I followed the recipe faithfully, but was disappointed with the result. My eggs just seemed really greasy from all the butter. They tasted okay but not better than my normal scrambled eggs, which are lighter, fluffier, and healthier for me. It was an interesting way to cook eggs and I may try it again sometime with less butter.

My dining window opens in an hour. I love eggs almost any style, but especially soft scrambled. Seems like just the right amount if butter. As suggested by Kenji I’ll replace the water with (whole) milk. Can’t wait!
why does the salt have to be kosher? salt is salt!

Kosher salt does not have iodine, like most ordinary table salts. There is really a taste difference between the two. (Personal note: I grew up thinking I didn't really like salt only to discover that what I don't like is the chemical taste that iodine imparts to it.) That said, I think you could use sea salt to good effect here.

Wow, I couldn't believe the difference in my scrambled eggs - light and creamy. I always wondered what the secret was. Love the texture.  I used tapioca starch and it worked wonders.  Great keeper recipe !
Decent with some fresh bread and smoked salmon and a grapefruit to finish.

Made exactly as written above for 2 eggs, in an 8" Calphalon skillet. I used corn starch and will be curious to try with potato starch in the future as I know that is the preferred starch for this technique. Very soft and decadent result. Maybe a little too much? I think you could cut down on the butter and still have a pretty good effect. I also think it would make a pretty decent omelette if you went a slightly different way in the cooking method.

This is terrific. You get the slow-cooked taste and texture but are done in a fraction of the time. Took 3.5 minutes from start to finish. Follow the method to a t, and you will not be disappointed. Wish my diet could accommodate the 2TBPS butter per serving daily, but worth the indulgence 1-2 times a week.

Try the Gordon Ramsay method….whisk 3 eggs with a little butter over a medium heat for 30 seconds with a spatula, remove from heat for 30 seconds while still whisking, place back on the burner while whisking for 30 seconds. Keep repeating this process until you get creamy scrambled eggs. If you like them crispier, keep repeating. It only takes a few minutes and no need for tapioca, corn starch or heavy cream. Works like a charm every time

1-2 minutes? I do make scrambled on a HOT 1935 Wagner Ware skillet. Drop the eggs in, pre-salted and peppered, into sizzling canola or 1st processed olive from a friend's farm in Greece. |
BOOM! 
20 seconds max! Ovenmit on the hand, plate ready. Akin to hunting. 
Never let in linger. 
Always fluffy. fully cooked. No carbonizing. CLEAN and savory.
No butter nonsense. Hens and Cows? Yuck! 
Always perfect.
Ask my 8-year-old. THEY are the toughest egg critics in the world.
I doubled it and used a bunch of egg yolks I had left over from meringue plus a couple of whole eggs  - it was fantastic!

As good and quick as all the 5 star reviews. I added come finely chopped scallions that I seared before pouring the eggs in and a dollop of goat cheese on the side once on my plate.

Only problem, wanted to make it with 4 eggs per person it is so good.

Wow, these eggs are fantastic! So soft and moist. I had to use what I had on hand, substituting potato flour for potato starch and salted butter for unsalted (I left out the salt, but added some to taste in the finished dish.) I also did it with one egg which took a lot of calculating!  I’m always experimenting with ways of trying to make one scrambled egg moist with large curds. This did the trick & is the best of all methods I’ve tried. Thank you, Kenji!

I pride myself on being a good cook but by never consulting a recipe I have struggled to make consistently good scrambled eggs. This is the way. Used potato starch. Gentle, lazy pushes and folds with the spatula generated the most sublime texture. Thinly-sliced American cheese laid over top after plating melted perfectly as I dug in. Heed the tip on cooking time for fewer eggs - trust your instinct, not the clock!

I made this recipe as written except I tripled it and wasn't able to use a bigger non-stick pan as suggested (because I didn't have one).  It still turned out great and was super easy.  My only two comments: it needed more salt than suggested (in my opinion) and was a bit too buttery. I think that if I make it in such a large batch again, I will cut back the total butter by a couple of tablespoons.
Made this recipe as written and the eggs came out perfectly done! Truly the best scrambled eggs I’ve ever made for the family.

Another way to make really creamy scrambled eggs is to add sliced American cheese, half to one full slice per egg. Cheese goes in first, then eggs. Keep things moving to avoid scorching the cheese and to mix meeting cheese with the eggs as they firm up.
Following instructions to a tee led to perfect eggs for two. Great on an egg sandwich.

Quickly fry 12 shrimp then add them to the bowl of scrambled eggs.
150g or 12 large Shrimps
¼ tsp Salt
¼ tsp White pepper
¼ tsp Sugar
½ tsp Sesame oil
½ tsp Cooking wine

Cornstarch isn’t necessary. Neither is tapioca starch. No milk either. Makes the eggs watery. Just whisk those eggs with salt and pepper and pour in pan with melted butter and then follow recipe. Done and done.
Bit involved for eggs but man are the results worth it. Genuinely creamy and eggy.
These are the best scrambled eggs ever.  Thanks Kenji
Fabulous! I followed the recipe exactly. Thank you chef!

Saw his video last night. Made the eggs just now and…WOW. Scrambled eggs are my favorite thing in the world. But I suck at making them the way I like: creamy and fluffy with big curds. Who knew the secret was a corn starch slurry! Love it!

Delicious. If you want a recipe for low cal/low fat scrambled eggs, whisk a few and cook them in a drop of olive oil. If you want soft, creamy eggs, make these. This was fast and couldn’t be easier. No need to look at the recipe if making again and easily scales down for one or up for many. I won’t eat them every day, but I’ll eat them again!
These are by far the greasiest eggs I've ever had. Cut the amount of butter in half.

I used cornstarch and it does make a difference: the eggs are more moist and tender than if you didn’t use cornstarch. 

I replaced water with half-and-half, and reduced the amount to 2 tbsp of butter (for 4 eggs) instead of the full 4 tbsp and it was still a little too buttery for my tastes. I think you can get away with less… 

I should have left the eggs cook for a few seconds longer because they were still a little too moist for my liking. Bad judgment call on my end.

I tried this recipe, following instructions to the letter. Yum. Best scrambled eggs I've ever made. Yes, it's a lot of butter, but I don't make scrambled eggs for myself very often, so I'll take the extra fat and calories that make this recipe a winner.
Worked JUST as written and I snazzed it up a bit with a sprinkling of finely sliced spring onions and a handful of grated Gruyère added at the same time as the eggs. Delicious! Thanks Kenji!👍🏼

Delicious and extraordinarily rich and creamy. Great recipe for entertaining, and to serve with additional breakfast sides (fresh fruit to cut the richness) Honestly, the creamiest scrambled eggs I’ve ever tried. However, I don’t think I’ll make again for myself.

For myself I whisk 2 eggs, salt and pepper and a little 1/2 and 1/2 , in  a hot pan  I melt a tab of butter add the eggs , once they have started to firm up I remove the pan from the heat mix the eggs with a fork and always end up with perfectly cooked creamy never dry scrambled eggs . Adding salt does not make them tough or grey.
This was really good. I wonder if there is a way to do this with egg whites.

For those chemistry-curious obsessive this sister article will be of interest… Salt before. Rest for 15mins. Read the whole article to get the deets! Thanks Kenji! https://www.nytimes.com/2024/04/24/dining/best-way-to-salt-scrambled-eggs.html?smid=nytcore-ios-share&referringSource=articleShare&sgrp=c-cb

I don't buy it. The creaminess is nice, though no different from several simpler techniques I could mention. And the cornstarch - combined with simply too much butter - dulls the flavor of the eggs. A great technique if you are looking for that special "made yesterday and reheated" flavor.
Wow this recipe is a 10!  It’s easy and crazy delish.
Best. Scrambled. Eggs. Ever.
I'm good with eggs. Loosely followed this recipe to see if it could improve my already pretty tender scramble. It absolutely delivered. I'll stick to this technique from now on. Do it!
These eggs are incredible. So creamy and melt in your mouth. Made exactly as written and it was perfect

In my house we adore scrambled eggs and this recipe is the only way we do it now. The addition of potato starch is genius—the eggs become unbelievably creamy—and these make up part of our regular breakfast-for-dinner meal with crusty bread and a salad. I’ve found that grating the butter produces better results than cubing it—that’s the only change I’ve made to the basic recipe. Such an indulgence!
Did this and it was amazing. Best scrambled eggs ever.
Used Korean sweet potato starch and reduced the butter a little. Beat eggs I ever made!
Cannot go back to low and slow when this is so quick and yields wonderful results. I did half the butter as personal preference.

I've only recently come to understand that I truly love scrambled eggs. I've always avoided them because they're usually terribly bland and dry. I learned to cook them correctly, and now I eat them all the time. This recipe should be the gold standard. I'll never cook them another way again. I cut back on some of the butter and could reduce it a bit further, but these are out of this world. What a decadent, yet fast and simple breakfast. Thanks!

I must have done something wrong. I followed the recipe to a T (except cut in half) but my eggs have a weird, hard texture and very little flavor. Will have to try again, maybe with the full four eggs?
It was amazing!  Cut back slightly in butter and added fresh mushrooms and spinach to the meal. Awesome!!!
Yumalicious! I wasn't sure about the bits of butter left in the egg slurry, but this made unbelievably soft curds. I drizzled with chili crisp.

This resulted in perfect scrambled eggs. 

I used potato starch.

We keep cutting down the butter...
The recipe as written is delicious, but unbelievably greasy. Like, 'the eggs slide off your fork because they're sitting on a layer of butter' greasy.

Started off at half portion... No loss in quality, less greasy, but still a lot.

Somewhere around 1.5 Tbsp of butter (not 4!) is the sweet spot between delicious, fluffy, and not dripping with grease. Maybe it's just me, but I want eggs that don't shine when the light hits them!
I made half for myself with great results. Easy, elegant, and satisfying. Thank you Kenji!!
The best eggs I have ever cooked! Have shared it with many friends who also agreed.
I rarely leave reviews, but make an exception here.  This is such a great recipe as it delivers scrambled eggs exactly how I want them to be.  Thank you, Kenji.

Nothing spectacular about this method. Like hundreds of others, I’ve been making eggs for decades that always come out fluffy. Just following simple French cooking techniques. Simple. Cornstarch wasn’t a game changer IMHO.

If you’re skeptical this recipe can produce soft, creamy eggs in a fraction of the time compared to other methods, read on…

For years I’ve made this style of scrambled eggs, the way they’re served in France, using traditional recipes. The only downside to any traditional method is how long they take. So, we only have them on occasional weekends.

I tried this method today on a school-day morning when I don’t have a luxurious amount of time to make breakfast. The test was serving it to my daughter who can, miraculously, tell if French-style scrambled eggs are over/undercooked by just a hair. Ha! The result: she loved them just as much as the long-cook method! Thank you J. Kenji López-Alt!

I have 2 scrambled eggs every morning. I tried this recipe today, using the water and cornstarch.  I didn’t mix any butter into the eggs but I did use a bit more butter in my pan. I didn’t realize until I tried this recipe that my eggs were kind of tough!  These eggs are perfect!

Julia Child’s recipe tweaked  ..beat 2 or more eggs 80 times with small or large whisk , I add a big splash of heavy cream while beating   & a bit of grated Parmesan cheese. Scramble in as many pats of unsalted butter you’d like cold pan on lowest heat and yes it takes forever but in the end you’ll have the tenderest most delicious scrambled eggs ever. Add black pepper .
