I've used this method twice, and it really is the very best. However, the second time, I did it exactly as written, except that I cut the butter in half, and subbed olive oil in the pan for the last step. Both ways are delicious, but that much butter seemed excessive.
Good texture but way too much butter, will use half or less next time

I did not expect the recipe to be this good.

Add a little bit of garlic and herb Boursin cheese and you will not be disappointed
These were good, not great. Made it exactly as written. Just way too much butter. If I make them again, I will half the butter. My search for the perfect scrambled eggs continues.

Another easy and delish way is  turn the pan on medium-low and add a generous pat of butter. Then add a dash of water to your eggs before whipping them. Add the eggs and when it starts to set along the edge of the pan, begin pushing them around with a flat wooden spatula or similar, until they get to desired consistency. Flat spatula is key, imo. These eggs are incredible, I promise. Takes 4 minutes.
These are now the only way my 10 year old daughter wants her eggs. Creamy and lush with a lovely texture and so easy to make.

I skip the butter and salt. A generous amount of Skim milk whisked into the eggs with just a bit of cooking olive oil placed in a heavy duty nonstick, Denmark made Scanpan produces phenomenal scrambled eggs.
Eggs, a little cream and a spot of mayonnaise is all you need. Quick easy and delicious.
Too much butter
These are amazing! 3 tablespoons butter is sufficient for us but you do you.
I’m totally hip to great scrambled eggs, along with what one needs to do to produce them.  But 4 TABLESPOONS of butter for 4 eggs?  I love butter as much as anyone, but that’s just stupid.

I do something similar, but with less butter. I put 1 teaspoon of potato starch into 1 tablespoon milk or water or cream into a non-stick skillet and mix till no lumps remain, crack 4 eggs into skillet, break yolks and swirl 5 seconds. Turn heat onto medium-high (or just medium if cooking with gas or induction). Add 1 - 2 tablespoons of firm butter, cut into half inch cubes and salt and pepper.  Stir and scrape with a silicone spatula. When butter is melted and incorporated, the eggs are done.
Why do you put water in the skillet first?
Did you watch the video? He explains why.
@hahadavis “Make sure your skillet is at just the right temperature by heating a tablespoon of water in it and waiting for it to evaporate.”
For creamier, eggier scrambled eggs sub a bit of water of milk or cream.  Whip well and do not over heat or over cook.
4 eggs, about a tablespoon or so each of sour cream and heavy cream, beat until fluffy, scramble in a pan with melted butter. Much easier than this.
brings additional flavour and consistency that I personally don‘t like at all…I‘d rather go for the, was it kenji‘s recipe, with adding cold cream close to finish line to stop further coagulation.
For extra creamy scrambled eggs, try stirring in 1 tablespoon of mayo for every two eggs right at the end, just before serving. It adds a silky texture without altering the flavor.

Close to my own recipe. 
You don't need to whisk potato starch into water, do it directly into already whisked eggs, whisk again to incorporate.
Make sure you butter is of really high quality (probably European style).
Also this type of egg does well with the on/off burner style.
You need to get the eggs to stop cooking once they are done or they will dry out. Add creme fraise and incorporate. And don't add salt until the end.

So, tried it once and wan't impressed. Tossed it, and tried again. Used to work in restaurant kitchens just after college, Cappy's on Broadway in SA, Tx, and Pipe Creek Cafe, Tx. I might be a little prejudice because I like my eggs and my guests at Flowering Fields B&B in Va did too. I'm pretty much a purist & keep it simple, low heat.
My take away is this isn't bad. I would eat it if someone served it to me. Only new thing I do now is salt before whisking eggs + a dash of olive oil w/butter.

I have been making scrambled eggs for decades in a treasured glass double boiler.  Just eggs,
a splash of water and sometimes some grated parmesan cheese.  They have always received raves for the creamiest eggs ever.

Followed directions precisely. Did anyone else have the problem I did — the butter pieces all ended up inside the whisk and I had to pick them out and switch to a different implement. I thought the finished product was much too buttery and the cornstarch slurry added a taste that was unnatural to my idea of scrambled eggs. Had to quickly cook up some sliced mushrooms to put on top to give the eggs a different flavor. Will go back to my short order cook method of scrambling eggs.
Would anyone use this technique with omelettes?
Great technique. My adaptation: I used a medium hand grater to shred the cold butter, similar to some pie crust recipes.
They were definitely fluffier than most eggs -  mine ended up a bit more like an omelet than scrambled which was fine!  Needed a bit more salt and pepper, but be the judge

Oh what a beautiful way to start my Sunday morning! Loved that I made the day extra special by trying this very easy and delicious recipe. A side of wheat toast with homemade strawberry jam completed my self-care. Thank you for motivating me to take time to indulge myself!

There’s just too much butter - it coated the eggs in grease, turning this from a light, fluffy egg dish to a heavy one. Kinda gross. Could probably do less than half the butter  and have a fluffy, delicious result
buttery goodness, even I can make these! Delicious, thank you.
I followed exactly the instructions and it came out great.

I like to beat eggs in a bowl with a fork until the yolks are mixed with the whites. Heat skillet at moderate temperature with a tablespoon of butter. Pour the eggs into the skillet. As the eggs set, tip the skillet and use a spatula to keep the setting eggs apart from the liquid eggs. Repeat until all the eggs are lightly set. Do not scramble the eggs; rather, fold the eggs into the middle of the skillet. Use the spatula to lightly chop the folded eggs. Result is a hearty, moist texture.
Delicious, perfect scrambled eggs. Not too complicated. Quick and easy
These are delicious!!  I did find it to be a little too much butter, even for what Kenji describes in the video as “weekend eggs.”  But overall, great.
Great hack; makes a difference. What is really difficult, though, is getting farm fresh eggs that taste--well, like eggs.

Cooked these this morning with a couple of changes:  i added some chopped cherry tomatoes, drained, and since I didn't have butter, some grated Fontina.   Wow!  Best scrambled eggs ever-smooth and silky. Will definitely be making these again.
Made as written, except used only 1 tablespoon butter total, about 3/4 in the egg mixture and 1/4 in the pan, and added fresh finely ground black pepper and smoked paprika. Fantastic!
@ORMojo "Made as written," except that you reduced one ingredient by 75%.
@Tom g I think they were talking about the process
Rather than butter, I prefer using an oil (such as sunflower or grapeseed) that tolerates heat.  Butter burns easily and turns the eggs brown.

I tried this recipe perhaps 3-4 years ago on a whim and it's the only way I make scrambled eggs now.  As mentioned by others, it's not a difficult recipe at all and the butter definitely adds to the overall end result.  I will sometimes add tomatoes, feta, or a bit of bacon or sausage, but in it's purest state, these are some really great scrambled eggs.

I made this quickly one time & eggs turned out yummy, even though I failed to notice kosher salt, which would have improved them, I’m sure. Because I was in a rush, I turned the heat on too early and then had to take the iron skillet off the burner and cool it with tap water. So not ideal recipe-following but the eggs were delicious and fluffy. I cut the recipe in half for all ingredients and used 2% milk not water. Next time I’ll use whole milk, a bit more butter, kosher salt, & heat pan right.
Excellent, simple and super great.  I added white pepper and nutmeg as I usually do when cooking eggs.
Made these ,to the letter. They were nice but not the best I have ever had. The few times I have had better ,I'm still trying to puzzle out what was done

I had all the ingredients so I had to make this immediately. My 4 year old and I gobbled them up.  Took 5 minutes - the only thing I did differently was add some cheese at the very end. They were perfect!
For the best, silkiest eggs, I would do what they did on "The Bear." Before cooking, put the beaten eggs through a sieve.  Then go on with your recipe. The difference is incredible!
These eggs cooked very quickly. My husband and son loved them. Excellent with some hearty dry toast since there is enough butter in the eggs. Delicious!
If you have a conduction stove like I do, I know exactly what temperature the butter melts the way the water drops tell you.  It's #4 with my steel cookware. This seems like an easy upgrade.
Made scrambled eggs this way with corn starch. Revolutionary! Soft and creamy just how I like my scrambled eggs,
I eat eggs almost every day. Made these this morning. Nice! Probably will use less better next time.

I myself do not like them completely homogenous. Salt pepper beaten with a fork tip they combine with variations present. Cook over medium heat high or high moving constantly. Remove from pan while still shiny. Moist tender creamy with texture. Why work harder to make processed food. Some grated cheese on top is nice. Most important is finding the freshest eggs with the brightest yolks that are bulging up not spread out.
Very good eggs, but they quickly get kinda weird in texture, like 5 minutes of cooling and they get kinda weird. Like powdery like the starch. Tasted great though
So good!!!!   I even decreased the amount of butter.

Healthier version? Use a non-toxic, non-stick pan with just enough healthy fat to coat (such as organic coconut oil, it adds creaminess & doesn't leave a prominent coconut flavor).  Add a hint of salt to help break down the eggs before scrambling with a whisk - for as long as your arm can bear.  Follow step 3. Turn off the burner about 2 mins. prior to finishing the eggs but keep the pan on the hot element continuing to gently fold the eggs until creamy. Top with mashed avocado or leftover guac.
Pretty good, but in my opinion, it's not worth the extra trouble.
Amazingly fluffy and easy to make.  What a great recipe.
Fantastic eggs, added some chives like Kenji did, made them very flavorful.  I served them with some smoked salmon.  Delicious combination.  This will be my go to in future!
I tried but still prefer potato vs corn starch

This is so me having a quick meal in the evening when things are crazy busy. I have found that adding a few dabs of cream cheese makes great scrambled eggs and allow you to cut way back on the butter. Give it a try!

AMAZING!! I cut the recipe in half beside the water (on accident) and they still turned out fluffy, delicious, and perfect! Super easy and will make again! 
Also used cast iron and they didn’t stick!

These were good. We’re accustomed to wonderful scrambled eggs, thanks to James Beard. This recipe doesn’t improve on those, and we could taste the corn starch a bit in Kenji’s iteration. James uses the same amount of butter, I tablespoon per egg. All the butter goes directly into the pan. Adding water, 1 tablespoon per egg, to the eggs before whisking results in the same wonderful creaminess. A little less fussy, but this was a very easy recipe, so “fussy” probably is the wrong word.
This recipe got my egg-hating 4 year old to ask for a second and third helping of eggs. Kenji always knows best.
Prepared as per recipe. Simple, easy-to-do, and more delicious than I could have imagined.

May be a good recipe, haven't tried it but don't think u can beat using whipping/heavy cream as the liquid and plenty of butter when cooking, for large amounts I always use a saucepan/sautepan not a cast iron frying pan.
Does it matter if the eggs are straight from the fridge or should I allow them to become room temperature?
Wow! Literally the best scrambled eggs I've ever eaten. So rich and decadent-tasting. I used corn starch and water, but will try with cream or half/half next. Highly recommended!
I use arrowroot powder and nut milk for a similar effect.  Super creamy and delish. And my butter is always Kerrygold.

You can substitute some half-and-half or cream to get the same results without the hassle of dealing with butter.  Add a pinch of grated or shredded cheese to the batter for a nice flavor boost, and send for an egg beater if you plan to make this again.  You many never go back to the whisk.
Very fluffy indeed, but I'd go half on the butter!

I made this recipe this morning and they were by far the best eggs I have ever had! My husband loved them too! Definitely your weekend eggs, as he says, because of the large amount of butter but I’m going to make them again with just a smidge of butter for everyday cooking!
Prepared half recipe exactly as written. Did not notice or taste a difference from my usual low and slow scramble.

These are the eggs you see in photos that make your mouth water, but you never quite managed before. Just as Roy Choi elevated humble packaged ramen to noodle perfection, Kenji López-Alt has given us weekend morning heaven. Maybe I’ll cut back the butter most
Too rich for us. Though, I only added 1/3 of butter, my 91 yo mom asked me not to make it for her again. Lol. Thank you though.

This recipe raises the ancient philosophical question: “Can you ever have too much butter?”

The answer, I have now learned, is Yes.  It is a good recipe.  Next time I will cut the amount of butter in half.
Made these with a healthy degree of skepticism that they could be that much different to regular scrambled eggs. They were a little fiddly to make compared to regular ones but well worth the effort.

These were very good (not too much butter at all!)... But honestly I get just as good a result without the starch and with butter and a little water or milk by just cooking them on low heat, handling them just right, and plating them when they're still very wet. Timing with eggs is critical, and Americans typically way overcook scrambled eggs and omelettes.
I get it… and I’m no “healthy cooking person” but it’s more like having eggs with your butter! Maybe I’ll try duck fat next time:)!
Delicious! I added snipped chives, making an already wonderful recipe even better
Just came back from France and was searching for the way they made these delicious creamy scrambled eggs. This is it!

Fresh mozzarella (the only kind we have in Italy) works fine and is far superior to processed supermarket “mozzarella.” I’ve been grilling pizza with it for decades. Just cut the cheese balls in half and blot them on paper towels for a bit. Moisture problem solved. Italians also put other cheeses on pizza including Gorgonzola and Fontina.
No you certainly have normal mozarella in italy in addition to the water packed stuff. I've seen it with my own eyes.

For those who want to try using fresh mozzarella, my Other Half figured out a spiffy way of removing a lot of the watery whey from it.  He'll put a thick slice of it on a big plate in the microwave and melt it (full power) though not fully cook it.  Lots of the whey comes out this way.  We drink the yummy juice and then carefully put the hot cheese on our pizza or such to finish cooking.  Note, we've only done this technique with things in the toaster oven.  Never tried on a grill.

Make your own dough - and try to use "00" pizza flour. It doesn't spring back after rolling it out like dough made with all purpose flour used in most store-bought pizza doughs. Double 00 pizza flour is becoming more and more available at local grocers.
Whole foods in the DC area has it.
Avert your eyes, purists, but I roll out my crusts and freeze them. Then I pop them right from the freezer onto the grill. It's so much easier that way, and makes weeknight pizzas a snap.
Roberta's pizza dough - as in the recipe the Times has printed - is easy and so good. I would never buy ready-made dough.
Another Kenji homerun. Brilliant ideas.

Cook the crusts on one side before the party begins, letting them cool on racks. Then when ready, lay them cooked side down on kitchen counter and everyone tops their pizzas.  We finish them off on the grill and hold them briefly in heated oven until all are cooked. Pizza party is everyone’s favorite!
To transfer the raw dough from counter to grill, I roll it onto a rolling pin and unroll it onto the grill.
I dont get the skin part in step 2

Can also use a flour sifter  (I  use the one with a crank handle like my mama used)
Try running the all purpose twice, wont be the same as 00 but close.

All the turning the dough isn’t really necessary. Throw it on the grill,  once it releases form the grate flip it and add your ingredients while it’s still on the grill, close cover grill about 5 minutes until cheese melts and sauce is hot.
Making pizza crust is easy, reliable and without the junk that's in store dough.

A very good recipe for the grill. I use my sauce instead of the tomatoes. Crispy a little Smokey crust. Starts with good dough. Dough from Al Cohen’s in Buffalo via Lexington Coop is an excellent start

It was raining so I used the grill piece on my gas range for the Roberta’s dough. After both sides were done I put on the toppings and finished the pizzas on a pizza stone at 550 in the oven. Delicious!  And now we can have grilled pizza thru the winter. Thanks Kenji!
Also, brush olive oil infused with fresh herbs (Basil, Oregano and maybe a pinch of thyme), garlic, crushed red pepper, salt and pepper before topping.

I have used a fish BBQ grill double grate to get the char hack going then add  flame roasted toppings and wait for cheese to melt by covering the entire fish grill with a dome from a wok.  Not advisable for round pizza, more the oval shaped.

I've made grilled pizza like this recipe many times and though it takes some practice to avoid overcharring and underdone dough, mostly it has worked out OK. Not amazing. I've since graduated to an Ooni pizza oven which produces Neapolitan pies that are light years ahead of anything made on a grill with temps reaching 950 F. Using a 24 Biga style dough at 65-70 percent hydration yields a pizza that can only be described as glorious and on par or better than any Neapolitan pie I've ever tasted.
Would you be kind enough to share your dough recipe?

Skip factory made frozen pizza dough and buy it fresh from your local pizza shop. Making your own is fun but if you're entertaining you've got enough to do besides making dough.

Also a pizza stone will work...let the stone preheat to a high temp then put the dough on.
Agree with most comments that a homemade dough is better, but I do use Trader Joe's pizza dough very frequently and am happy with results.
milady has recently cooked her best ever pizza - I'm guessing her secret is making the dough the day before, then resting it (letting it prove or something) in the fridge overnight.

Yeah, this is about right. I’ve been doing this for years and it’s the best pizza, truly. Consider making sauce though. A bit of garlic, onion and oregano in addition to torn basil makes a difference. Up here in Boston I can buy already ground chorizo and I recommend it. Also don’t worry about cheese placement; if you close the grill it functions as an oven, which also heats from the bottom. Above all, experiment with toppings, you almost can’t mess this up and you can serve it to company.

I have been grilling pizza for years without this added step of grilling it without toppings first, flipping it twice, etc. It has always come out with a nice char and super crisp. As long as the pizza is thin I find this method unnecessary.

I make grilled pizza on the awesome Weber Q all the time in just one step. Once you flip the dough the first time, spread a thin layer of toppings and close the lid. After 90secs-2min the cheese will be melted and everything else will be hot and ready to eat. High-quality toppings here are key: fresh mozza, a good pepper spread instead of tomato (TJ has a great one), grilled eggplant, tapenade. I am very hungry right now.

Forget the flipping and deferred topping placement. Use homemade high hydration pizza dough in an oiled rectangular pan placed on a pizza stone (preheated for at least 1 hour) on a gas fired grill. I also place another oven stone on the upper rack over the pizza to simulate a real pizza oven. The result is a decent Roman Pizza “fatta in casa”.

It's always interesting to read someone else's pizza method, though I am rarely persuaded to change my own. I have gradually abandoned mozzarella in favor of (gasp) brie, and use other heretical cheeses such as Gouda and Skellig. It seems a shame to buy dough when it is so easily made with the dough cycle of a bread machine or with a dough hook in a stand mixer. After obtaining some of the storied 00 flour from Italy, I think (so far) I prefer the bread flour I have heretofore been using.

“Working with one piece of dough at a time, shape each into a ball by stretching it and pinching it into a point at the bottom, forming a sort of “skin” that wraps the whole ball.”

What does this mean?
Can one do this with a pizza stone?
A pizza stone would defeat the purpose of cooking over direct heat. And I suspect the stone might retain that grilling "flavor" for some time, which may or may not be favorable for you.  Cheers!
Easy and simple, yet mighty tasty.  Was a hit with the family…will be making again and again!
No link to a NON store-bought pizza dough recipe?
I've been using this one since it came out https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1017931-pizza-dough refrigerate over-night, at least, or for a couple of days.

I have been making grilled pizzas for many years now and this article has several tips that will provide a short-cut to great results versus the trial and error you might experience without it. The points about hard cheeses as an initial layer on the dough is a good one, but I have also had good luck topping with softer cheeses, grilling per the directions here and then sliding the grilled pie under a low broiler for a couple of minutes to thoroughly melt the cheese on top.
Summers in Texas mean the oven is off. We put a Pizza stone in our gas grill and wait until it hits 500F to bake them.  Baking time is about the same as the oven, but peek in at 2 minutes to go.
Game changer! Excellent strategy for cooking pizza on the grill.

This was great fun. Very worried at first about applying the dough directly to the grill grate. But not a problem it turns out. We bought ready made dough at ShopRite, which was surprisingly good, and divided the ball into three, as the recipe directed. Flowed directions. The pizza themselves were small (individual sized) since we divided one store-bought dough into three. Cooked very rapidly - so you do need to watch carefully so as not to char. Yum. Yum will do it again.

I made this exactly as written, except I made Kenji’s NY style pizza dough from Serious Eats a few days before grilling instead of using store bought. I really enjoyed this technique and as someone without a pizza oven or baking stone/steel, this was the best pizza crust I’ve been able to make at home. I made one traditional cheese pizza, one with pepperoni, pickled jalapeño, and hot honey, and another with prosciutto, arugula, and shaved parm. I think this would be fun for parties!

I grill pizza at home all the time, and the tip about grating the pepperoni was a game changer. Made my best ever grilled pizza this weekend. 
My store's housemade fresh mozz is fortunately pretty low-moisture, so was happily able to grate that too. Amazing results.

This is the best pizza dough cooking technique ever! Made this for my birthday dinner and we were literally stunned at how good the bbq crust was. We cheated and purchased two 22-oz bags of dough from Whole Foods, prepped all the veggies in a pan, and used both fresh mozzarella and fontina, layering inversely as suggested, ending with sauce. It’s labor intensive ( we bbq’ed 8 pizzas) but soooooooo worth it!

Galbani makes a "low-moisture" mozzarella, but I haven't been able to find it recently. I hope they still make it. Never use pre-grated mozz; it has a cellulose powder to prevent clumping. If you don't have a grating attachment for your stand mixer, buy one. They're worth every cent. Partially freeze the cheese before grating to make your life easier. Don't over-heat the grill; you'll burn the dough. Rather than a pizza stone, I prefer a steel (they weigh a ton but hold the heat beautifully.)

This was great! For a quick weeknight pizza this beats using an oven and a sheet pan hands down. Personally, if I want good pizza, I’ll buy it out. I don’t have a pizza stone or a peel or 00 flour on hand. So, that said— this was delicious! The crust was crispy and light and not doughy and soggy like my usually homemade pizzas with stir bought dough. Would recommend!
I did it!  It was easy and good!  I found it easier and more comfortable than baking pizza in the oven.  Now I need to pay more attention to finding the best crust recipe.

You had me going until "freeze the pepperoni and grate it, then mix with cheese" in the attached article.
As a teen, I decided chocolate chip cookies would be better if the chips were chopped in a blender to spread the chocolatey flavor more thoroughly. Those cookies died of loneliness in the cookie jar. 
This affects me the same. Grated pepperoni doesn't allow for the many distinct tastes and flavor bites of pizza. Yes:grilled pizza; no:grated pepperoni. Why not mix it all into the dough? NO.
It's not remotely the same. Chocolate melts, pepperoni doesn't. We chop our pepperoni into tiny cubes and they get insanely crispy and delicious nestled down amongst the cheese,
Pizza dough recipe in the nytimes cooking section is easy and better than store bought.

Ok, help me out.  I usually roll out the pizza dough (Roberta's dough recipe from the Times, baked in an Ooni oven), as I haven't figured out how to get it thin enough otherwise.  That's recommended in this recipe, but others say that rolling out the dough is a bad idea because it eliminates all of the air pockets from the crust.  Anyone?

I rolled it out thin & found if the grill is hot, no problem getting char and puffy air pockets (3rd crust puffed up like a balloon!). Used store bought dough that I'd frozen. Not ideal but still came out great - can only imagine it'd be even better w/ Roberta's dough!
Wonderful recipe. Kenji knocks it out of the park again.

Cooked on small electric grill. Next time I'd undercook dough on 2nd side to allow for extra cooking time once toppings are added. (Electric grills don't retain heat well & don't allow for dual heat zones. Keep lid closed during all cooking.) Used burrata & homemade ricotta (let ricotta drain extra to prevent too much moisture.) Topped w/ prosciutto just before pulling off grill - let prosciutto warm up a minute w/ lid closed. Finished w/ arugula. Tasty & fun!

I have not made this yet, but adding extra coals in step 7 is not practical for most conventional charcoal grills. This would require removing the grill cooking surface to add the additional charcoal briquettes.
Came out okay but a lot of work

I made ours on a gas grill, and wow. The flatbread is really crispy and tastes like a wood fired crust. If you are organized, it really isn’t much of a production, and once you try it, you’ll be obsessed w all the things you could do. I used one store bought Wegman’s pizza dough, which made 3 pizzas. Sky’s the limit here, people. Try it! Great summer fun.

The best pizza we ever eat is our own grilled pizza. I use Bobby Flay's pizza dough recipe. Grill the first side, do a 90 degree flip for some cool grill marks, then flip it over for the other side. We like a "supreme" style pizza, so I have all the toppings prepped and ready to go: caramelized onions, Italian-seasoned ground beef, black olives, sun-dried tomatoes, bell peppers, cheese, red pepper flakes. Decorate your own pizza and back on the grill until cheese melts. Delicious!

I made my own dough, and the cooking method worked just fine. Lots of bubbles and charring, beautiful pies. But honestly, this pizza wasn't as good as pizza I make in the oven, and this was a lot more logistical work. Pass.

title: Thin but Juicy Chargrilled Burgers Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

comments.commentBody: Ketchup on a burger or (gasp) a hot dog is delicious. And I’ll have a glass of white wine with it because that’s how I roll.

Massaging ground meat (which additionally disrupts muscle fibers and releases intracellular protein) is widely used in Middle Eastern/Turkish/South Asian cooking, often combined with light salting. For kebabs, if you DON'T do so, the meat is likely to fall off the skewers during flame-roasting. Sometimes, chickpea flour, egg, or breadcrumbs are added as both binders and meat extenders.

Putting the veggies on the bottom only causes the juices to flow out and into your hands or onto your plate. Put mayo (or another similar condiment) on the bottom bun instead. This slows the progression of juices into the bottom bun long enough to eat the burger without having them drip all over you. Also, a griddle is for frying, not for grilling. If you want to fry your burgers that is fine, just don't confuse it with grilling, which is what this recipe calls for.

@Michael B - as I "fondly" recall my 4 month career slinging burgers at Wendy's in college 30+ years ago ... White, Red, Green; White, Red, Green (mayo, ketchup, pickle, onion, tomato, lettuce).  I still use that sequence today!

This is almost exactly how my parents grilled hamburgers in the sixties.  My mother used wax paper and a wooden hinged hamburger press to flatten the burgers, then my father threw them on a hot charcoal grill, and flipped them once.  I can still taste them. Simple. Perfect.

Here in Texas, it's not unusual to dice up some smoked brisket and add it into the ground beef, which adds some smoky deliciousness to the finished product. Of course, do not add ketchup as the picture shows topside. That is, quite simply...verboten.  :-P
RE: forbidding ketchup, or anything else that is simply a matter of taste,  as Ringo Starr said:  "I don't subscribe to your religion."

@jet I guess you didn't notice the emoticon, which generally signifies a joking (or in this case, semi-joking) comment. If you like ketchup on your beef, or your prime rib well done or even Coca-Cola in your cognac, have at it.  :-)
Ketchup on a burger != well done prime rib

I have always found that if you lightly form your thin patties but then put them in the freezer for an hour before you put them on the grill, they hold together on the grill grate and sty juicy in the thin center.

I thought the point of the article (and smashed burger concept) was to use a griddle on the grill, not to place the patties directly onto the grate.

There's a must-read New Yorker article from years back, easy to find, on how Pat LaFrieda conquered the gourmet chuck-sirloin-brisket burger market (your butcher can do it for you), which was followed by a NYT Food Section piece on how to prepare the burgers at home using rye bread, caramelized onions and pimento cheese.  Best burger in my book.

No, that is totally not the point of the article. The point of the article is how to get a thin burger grilled directly on the grill, as opposed to smashed and cooked on a griddle or a cast iron pan. Kenji was clearly after a thin burger that was kissed by smoke unlike smash burgers which are not.

I made these last night, and they were a surprise hit -- 10/10 would make again; in fact I may have to update my default technique. 

Didn't have parchment, so I used a pair of silpat sheets; peeling off the patties was a bit of a pain, so in the future I'll probably use silpat for one side and parchment on the other.

Second try, I used a tortilla press and parchment to flatten the burgers to 7 or 8 inches, and paired the patties not with a potato bun but with toasted multi-grain bread. These were fantastic - and in their simplicity, reminiscent of how my grandfather, a Navy cook in WWII, made them at home: each bite delivered every ingredient (e.g. good bread, local tomato, mayo, raw onion) in balance with the others, rather than being overwhelmed by a beef patty so thick that the 'toppings' escape notice.

When using Impossible or Beyond, thin burgers tend to cook too fast and dry out before you get a good char even on a hot grill.  To solve this stick formed patties in the freezer for at least a couple of hours (we keep a few patties in the freezer at all times) and drop them directly onto a hot grill still frozen.  This lets you get a good char and still have some pink left.  Impossible/Beyond are shipped to stores frozen so refreezing causes no change to taste/texture of your burger.

I had been under the impression that thawing then refreezing has an adverse effect on any food. Do you know why this is not the case with Impossible/Beyond? I am not a vegetarian, but my teen daughter has recently become one, and I'd like to make sure I'm preparing this unfamiliar-to-me food as best I can.

Made as written and they were a hit. My wife and son enjoyed a smaller burger and I made mine a double stack. I always struggle with getting grilled burgers just right and this technique made things very easy. Will definitely be a repeat and perhaps my new default grilled burger technique!

@Tammi - Freezing or refreezing food is about taste/texture not saftey. A frozen peach is pretty much only good for smoothies and pie after one freezing. A bag of Cheerios will taste like the original (cardboard) after 10 times frozen/defrosted. Impossible and Beyond are good for at least two cycles with little discernable change. Same with cooked. We freeze half a Cajun meatloaf with Impossible and it heats quite well. I have not tried a third time. Good luck with your daughter
Made a few and then froze them. Came out great!
Good to see you putting the vegetable toppings on the BOTTOM bun, as the Lord intended. If using cheese, this also allows the melted cheese to meld with the top bun, for the perfect cheeseburger.
fantastic burger did it on the Big Green Egg at about 400F will try 600F next time.
Perfect burgers! Ours cooked on gas grill for 2 minutes first side and flipped for 1 minute with cheese toppers. Will press burgers and cook them this way from now on…great flavor, still juicy.
Veggies on bottom makes sense to me because I always eat burgers upside down so the juices hit the "top", usually thicker bun.
It sounds fabulous wand will try this weekend.  Maybe ketchup, maybe fry sauce. maybe rooster sauce it's all good. Grill on.
if you’re using charcoal, don’t forget to oil the grill after it gets hot, otherwise you run the risk of the burger patties sticking

I've been grilling burgers for decades and will never grill again, except like this. Gas grill--burgers got good char but were still medium rare in the center, flavorful and the brie took them over the top. Not too fussy...perfection!
It would be helpful if the recipe simply specified a target thickness for the formed, uncooked patty instead of going through the whole golf ball rigamarole.

I was confused. I thought you cooked these burgers in a cast iron pan or griddle, not directly on the grill. I used the pan, cooked the burgers with onions and mushrooms cooking between the burgers. Great with sliced garden tomatoes, mayo and home grown lettuce.

But if you're cooking veggies simultaneously (delicious of course!) won't the steam they release slow down the cooking? The goal for this recipe, at least, is the hottest surface you can have, and that would mean cooking the meat all by itself.

This has made me rethink my approach to burger condiments.  Maybe you could toast the cheese onto the bottom bun, then pile on the tomato, onion, lettuce (or whatever else) and anchor it all with the patty and the top bun?  I hate burger slippage.
We don’t have a griddle so I used my cast iron skillet on the grill and these turned out great! I liked knowing after less than 2 min they were perfectly cooked through but not dry!
You don't need either a griddle or a cast iron pan as this recipe is specifically designed to cook the burgers directly on the grill.

We liked this a lot. It was easy and delicious. I did it on my gas grill at its hottest and they came out great. I was a tad confused by the placement of veggies and condiments so simply did as we’ve always done and put them on the top. We survived. Will now include this along with a few other burger recipes at the top of the list.

Lid closed

Took a little longer to cook. Went by look rather than time.

For a delicious sauce, try your favourite BBQ sauce and mayo - nothing else.
Also make sure it is a good potato bun like Martin's Potato Rolls.
Good Montreal Steak Spice doesn't hurt either.
Only in our times will this be called a thin patty and a thin burger.  Looks very thick to me.  And I wonder how some of the advertised taller burgers are eaten?  Knife and fork?  Who knows?

Rather than butter, spread mayonnaise on the buns and toast. Less wet than butter and less likely to result in smoking or a "burnt butter" taste on a hot grill. Another approach is to use ghee or clarify your own butter.
The burgers were delicious and moist. I used Swiss cheese and put them on brioche buns with ketchup, iceberg lettuce, red onion, and heirloom tomato. From now on I will always use this method.

What is the benefit of kneeding your ground beef? Serves no purpose. The less you bother the beef the less myoglobin it will lose. Just shape the patty with as little aggression as possible, season it, grill it.
Kenji spends three paragraphs in his article explaining why you knead these and not other burgers.  Long story short, if you don't they will fall apart immediately due to how thin they are.
Season the burger with a little thyme.
Does this work on a very hot Viking griddle?
Why don't you try it out on your Viking griddle and let the rest of us know how it turns out?
I mill my own chuck. Kneading not necessary.

Only because I am a Kenjii fan did I decide to try this burger and I am now an even stronger believer in him!  Everything worked as he said it would and I ended up with a delicious, uicy, pink, medium rare burger that I would have never believed possible from a squished beef mix.

Our burgers invented by my better half are seasoned with Lipton soup beefy onion mix, Ketchup, parsley, garlic, onion, salt and pepper. We have not tried to pound them as flat as he suggests. I wonder if the ketchup is enough of a binder the keep them together. Or we would  need to add eggs...etc.

I have taken to cooking on a silicone mat. If tends to slow the process down and minimize flareups. I suspect that would not work well with this technique.
No Lipton soup. Never ever.
This sounds like meatloaf.

These turned out great. Except, my “golf ball” sized balls of meat didnt make burgers 1/2-1/4 in wider than my buns (standard brioche- not huge). I guess i underestimated how big a golf ball is! I’d go for at least an 2-2.5inches in diameter for each ball.
This is the burger grill recipe I’ve been searching for. Super fast, thin burgers that hold their shape, a good crust, not a hockey puck.

These were fantastic!! I’m not much of a meat cooker, nor am I very good on the grill, but the method worked great! Thank you! I used Penzeys Chicago steak seasoning and a tortilla press to flatten the patties between wax paper. I will definitely be making these again!
Cast iron pan smoking hot no oil or butter.  Nix the outdoor grill for 100 good reasons starting with cleanliness

And no pepper -  it burns, But a clean grill isn't a problem, and if you use actual charcoal - not briquettes, which are implied here -  it's fast and smokey. Charcoal just calms down and gets a rosy glow.
These were perfect! THE perfect grilled burger. And so very simple. I didn't bother with pressing another pan on top, I just pressed them out with my hands until they were very thin.
I do not have access to a grill. How can I cook a burger inside so that it tastes that it was cooked outside on a grill?
Try cooking the burger patties in a cast iron skillet or grill pan that you have preheated over a high flame for at least five minutes.

I  suggest that instead of preheating a cast iron pan on high flame (especially if you are using an electric stove), preheat on low heat (1-2 on knob) for 5-10 minutes, and then bring it up to just under or at the medium point for your stove's heat knob (4-5 for mine), and then let heat another several minutes, maybe as long as 4 or 5. Cast iron is such a good heat sink that you should get an excellent sear at that heat level, but with less smoking, burning, and sticking.

I've made these twice and plan to make them again this week. These are the best cheeseburgers I have ever eaten. Juicy, a bit of crunch and lettuce, tomato, red onion! for me ketchup! As a kid I hated thick burgers, didn't eat burgers for years and years (etc) but I found this recipe last year and can't recommend it enough.

Wow Kenji, where have you fled?  Been reading and following you for many years and have learned much. Recognize most of what you do is about technique not "recipe", but this oversimplification and unoriginal offering  sadly equates to one star. :((

When rounding the patties, are they really supposed to be golf ball sized? That seems quite small considering it says the meat should overhang on the bun when served. Maybe I'm not pressing hard enough? Or maybe my burger buns are too big? Is this just me or did anyone else have this issue?
For a 4 oz patty it's really more 'lacrosse-ball-sized,' I think.
What is the purpose of placing the hot coals on only one side of the charcoal grill. I f you only cook the burgers on the side with the hot coals why do you have a side without any coals?

I mix chopped onions and mushrooms into the ground beef and I use a patty press to make the patties.  Instead the usual Heinz ketchup, I use the Primal Kitchen brand.  Sliced avocado makes a great addition.

title: Grilled Oysters With Harissa-Parmesan Butter Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

comments.commentBody: Parmigiano on oysters would be a tough sell here in Italy where cheese and seafood are never mixed. For my next terrace barbecue I’ll up the North African vibe by substituting my homemade preserved lemon for the cheese. And as an expat New Englander I’m pretty sure I could shuck all those oysters raw before the coals are even hot; no need to fuss with pre-heating them on the grill first.
Which is why I enjoy Kenji’s recipes. He’s not afraid to try something new that might turn out to be delicious.

I fully endorse breaking culinary rules, as my IG followers know. But to the Italian palate, mixing cheese and seafood can be literally stomach-churning. Not saying it doesn't taste great to other palates...
Maybe about the Parmigiano on the oysters, however, I like what tastes good so I used Pecorino instead. Wow! so good!

Made as written - terrific and very popular!

Make extra harissa butter - it is wonderful brushed on corn (grilled next to the oysters).
Very good recipe, I only used 3 tablespoons of harissa paste and it was plenty flavorful (and spicy). This harissa butter is also great on grilled chicken thighs.
We didn't have a food processor so adapted the sauce a bit: melted the butter, mixed with the harissa and Parmesan, skipped the garlic.  It still turned out wonderful!
8 min the first round and 4 min the last round… needed extra time since I used large oysters and the coals may not have been hot enough.  Delicious - definitely will make again
Excellent! It took the full four minutes for all of the oysters to open. The crumpled foil is a nice technique to prevent spilling the juices.
I love the idea of crinkling foil to hold the oysters. Everyone probably has foil; not so much rock salt.
How long would the harissa-Parmensan butter keep in the refrigerator?

Wife yells at me when I put a slice of cheese on my tuna sandwich; “you can’t put cheese on fish”. I tell her there are no rules in eating. (lobster Mac and cheese, any one?).
The a small dollop of Harissa-Parmesan on each oyster was a perfect complement on my grilled PEI Belle Du Jour oysters adding a nice tang and finish to the brininess. Very tasty.
Thoughts on how this would work with mussels?
Grilled for 8 min the first round and 4 min the second round…
I left them raw and set the harissa, parm and garlic aside for someone who doesn't appreciate oysters.

In Maine we harvest fist-sized oysters at low tide that are too big to eat raw; it would be like putting an entire hamburger in your mouth. Those are ideal for grilling. We save the small ones for raw. There are no compromises, only using all of the harvest in the best way.

as an Australian I first discovered harissa during my gap year in Europe 1982-83 - I bought and opened a mysterious small can of bright orange stuff I'd never seen before 

tasted it - and it blew my mind !
I sprinkled some breadcrumbs on top to keep all the juices in the oyster
These were amazing. Huge hit with with everyone. Made per recipe, but next time I might add a sprinkling of parm at the end and use my searzall to add some char. Whole Foods had the Mina harissa.
Could this oven-baked or broiled? No charcoal grills in our big city apartment
Delicious. Reminded me of gochujang.
Can these be adapted for oven roasting; not everyone has access to an outdoor grill.

title: Grilled Oysters With Lemony Garlic-Herb Butter Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

comments.commentBody: I use a cast iron oyster pan for grilling which prevents the oysters from falling over. The pan  can also be put in the freezer to serve raw oysters on the half shell. I got mine at William Sonoma, not at all expensive but I am sure you could find it elsewhere.

If you do not have a grill, use the oven broiler (5min.). Shucking oysters is not that hard, there are plenty of videos to learn from and it will become another kitchen skill. I use a stainless steel wire rack designed to keep the oyster upright placed on a sided baking pan to catch the overflows which will happen. Guests have most liked my grilled oysters prepared with butter, a tad of minced garlic and a coating of Parmesan cheese. I have tried all sorts of other mixes but simple seems better.
For grilling I use massive fist-sized specimens we harvest ourselves along the Maine coast, and they are brutally hard to shuck, so pre-heating to open is a timesaver.

I had doubts when my oysters were on the grill--how was I supposed to shuck hot oysters?--but they cooled off fast and the shucking was fine, though I was clumsy and lost a lot of juice. With the sauce, they were divine. For the post-shucking grilling, I left them in the pan with foil (pan on grill) & put the grill lid down - they need longer than 1 minute that way but less messy and it worked great. Also very pleased with my decision to serve them with grilled flatbread to mop up all the juice.

What if you just remove all the cooked oysters to a bowl. Let the liquor escape into the bowl and reserve. Remove the top shells and spoon an appropriate amount of the lost juices from owl into the oyster cups. Add the butter and done.

This was tremendous!!  The oysters popped open easily on the grill and taking off the top shell with an oyster knife was a breeze. The lemon herb-butter was delicious and when put back into the oyster cups for another minute to bubble the overall result was devine!  We served this with crusty bread and a side salad for a light late summer meal but over pasta would also be delicious. Anything to soak up the rest of the luscious sauce. Definitely suggest a dozen per person as serving size!

If you do not have a grill, use the oven broiler (5min.). Shucking oysters is not that hard, there are plenty of videos to learn from and it will become another kitchen skill. I use a stainless steel wire rack designed to keep the oyster upright placed on a sided baking pan to catch the overflows which will happen. Guests have most liked my grilled oysters prepared with butter, a tad of minced garlic and a coating of Parmesan cheese.
I purchased oyster pan that has scalloped oyster size holes and is cast iron for grilling. Purchased at Amazon.
Saved for the lemony garlic herb butter

I shuck my oysters first and remove any impurities, sometimes even rinsing the oyster if I see mud. Purists will say that rinsing removes all the oyster juices, but the oyster will release more juices after it’s rinsed, and the purity of the taste makes up for any lost juices. I then put my garlic/butter/olive oil/salt/pepper/herbs sauce on the oysters and arrange them directly over the coals, allowing one oyster to support another so they remain relatively level. Remove to a plate when cooked.
There should not be impurities or mud inside oysters, they are not like clams. Rinsing an oyster is a felony in Maine where I lived for 20 years.
Oyster pans are probably overrated. Does one really need a special pan? Better to develop confidence around a fire with a long pair of tongs.

Sadly this was a total fail for us. Followed the directions to the letter but only one actually popped open. The butter was delicious and we used it on filets later in the evening. Which oysters did you all use?  We used “happy roasters” from Whole Foods- have never gotten a bad oyster from WF before. So bummed.
Very good! We shucked the oysters first & added the butter sauce. Cooked on a gas grill for 4 min.
Can this be used when grilling fish like swordfish or tilapia?
A version of this recipe has a long history in New Orleans (“charbroiled oysters” most famously of Drago’s)
Simple, easy, and delicious. We make this recipe just about every time we go to the Oregon coast. If there's no grill where we're staying, we've found that the oven works just fine.

title: Grilled Oysters With Buttery Soy-Sake Glaze Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

comments.commentBody: When opening the oysters hold them over a bowl to save the brine.  Mix the brine into the sauce, and use this to glaze the oysters with.
Skip the aluminum foil; place the flat, shucked tops of the oysters upside down on the pan, then position the oysters between them. The tops will hold the oysters flat and upright.

In spite of the 114F heat, I decided to grill oysters.  I made the recipe exactly as written, and it turned out incredible! I will definitely make again!  
I read the comment about sake substitutions, and I would recommend the use of good quality sake, which imparts a unique flavor that marries well with the shoyu and ginger.   Although I have not tried it, I suspect the vermouth or sherry would not have the same subtle taste.
Sake substitutions…? If any?
Dry vermouth or dry sherry can both sub for sake.
Nice sauce

The recipe list calls for 4 Tbls of unsalted butter. 
Where does this belong in the process?

@missed it - whisk into the sauce as you remove from heat (after removing the ginger and garlic). The butter rounds out the sauce, thinking it a bit and making it less sharp. Folks with a lactose issue could probably leave it out, but without it’s a bit thin.
Great recipe.  We added a bit of white miso paste to the glaze.  Grilled the oysters over the fire pit and used the glaze on skewered grilled scallops, as well.
Any suggestions on how this could be put together without a grill?
I would also like to do this in the oven maybe? Under the broiler?

@JA from Amsterdam - worked great in a toaster oven style air fryer (instant pot brand) at 400. Just be careful with the timing compared to the size of your oysters. Or if you are ok at shucking, remove the flat tops of the oysters, drizzle the sauce and roast. I did it that way, and added a bit of panko on top of each one for a bit of crunch. Made the sauce as per recipe. Delicious!
could you try this same approach with hard shell clams?
I really wanna see video of this!
Love this recipe!! Stumbled upon the best oysters I’ve ever had and tried this recipe. Oysters were from Little Peconic Bay off of Long Island. From the Shellworks farm.
Love this recipe!! Stumbled upon the best oysters I’ve ever had and tried this recipe. Oysters were from Little Peconic Bay off of Long Island. From the Shellworks farm.
Great recipe, would recommend cutting the sugar in half and adding some spice like fresh pepper or Asian hot sauce.

This was fantastic. We halved the recipe and served as an app before grilling the rest of dinner. The sake-soy glaze was so good that my kid asked us to save it for him to put on other things. We didn’t use the butter (oversight) but didn’t miss it, I think.  May have to make again to see! I agree with Joe to open the oysters over a bowl to save the brine.
Use half the sugar and less soy

stats.Total Time: 4 hours, plus 13 hours’ to 3 days’ resting

comments.commentBody: I'd like to use my sourdough starter... how much do you think it would change the recipe?
I’d also like to know about this!
Just use less water. So if you use 100g starter, use 98g less water for the recipe. If you don't do this, it might still work, but your dough will be very wet and harder to handle.
Actually, I'm thinking less 50g water, less 50g flour. That sounds about right.

For those asking about whether plastic wrap can be used to cover the dough...I use a shower cap instead - it's never used for any purpose other than covering dough! It works very well and cuts down plastic waste.

I’ve made several sandwich loaves with the original no-knead recipe—tasty but the crumb was a bit coarse. Here I added about a teaspoon of olive oil to the weighed ingredients. The dough was much drier than the gloopy no-knead I was used to and the gluten developed very quickly with the casual stretch and fold method. The next day the loaf formed from the chilled dough took 3 hours to proof. I rubbed butter on the top crust. Slicing into the cooled loaf I discovered I had made—Wonder bread!

Two words: olive oil. There's a reason bakers do NOT put fat into this type of bread, and you just found it (well, one of them) -- you're not going to get the kind of development you want for an "artisanal/country"-type crumb (and I don't mean "crumb-y crumb - I mean that great gelatinous look, good hole structure, etc.) vs. a Wonder Bread "crumby crumb". Your fat tore the entire thing apart.
Instead of inverting a bowl to cover, can plastic wrap be used instead?
I think his point it he's trying to get a good seal while not being wasteful.  Sure, plastic wrap will work.  I reuse clean produce bags to put around/over the bowl.

This has become our household staple loaf. It’s a great WFH recipe, as it needs a few hours of casual tending but the timing is forgiving. I use 300g water, and bake it in a 12” Pullman loaf pan; half the loaf gets frozen, and the other half lasts beautifully in the fridge, without either drying out or growing moldy. It’s especially delicious as toast.

A fine loaf of bread. Not spectacular, but certainly serviceable. Sub'd 100g milk for water in dough per tip. After removing from fridge, divided dough into 4 parts. Rolled each per directions, nested all 4 in loaf pan for merged rise.  Glazed w whole egg + 1.5 tbs H2O; + sprinkling of sesame seeds. Put pan of boiling water on bottom rack. Top came out caramel colored & crusty. IMO it needs an infusion of butter and sugar to give it a bit more life (will experiment with that and report back.)

As a novice baker, I have come to rely on the NYT for many of my bread recipes. This one is simple to follow and yielded wonderful results! Plan to use it often...it is THAT good. Look forward to substituting rye flour to make a nice rye loaf.

This turned out better than expected. I will definitely make it again. You have to use a scale and fresh yeast, and pay attention to the water temp. Mine rose tremendously in fairly cool weather. Rested in the fridge for 24 hours, used the egg wash and sprinkled with sea salt. Needed to bake for about 50 min at 40. Amazing.

Double recipe 

800g flour
16g salt
200g milk
320 water
1.25 tsp yeast
1/4 tsp vinegar 
Egg for top

A tasty sandwich loaf, slightly soft, good flavor.
In lieu of 400g, I used 300g KA bread flour and 100g white wholemeal. I upped the water to 300g , I added 2 tblspns of bakers dry milk, a 1/4 cup yellow corn meal and 1/4 cup flax seeds. After a night in the refrigerator, 2nd rise took longer than recipe, about 4 hours in a 72 degs  kitchen. The result was beautiful though. This recipe is a keeper.
The sandwich loaf comes out very well, but I will reduce the salt by about half

Made this twice, and produced curiously flat loaves. The first time, I refrigerated the dough for 3 days. Even after 4 hours for second rise, the dough never crested the pan. And Singapore is hot. The second time, the dough was in the fridge 15 hours. Second rise of 4 hours, didn’t crest the pan either. There was a little more oven spring but not much more. And yes, I’ve used the same yeast to make other breads with no problem. I could continue tinkering with this, but, seriously, why?

The same thing happened to me the first time I tried this. I changed a few things the second time - I made sure my water was 90 degrees, made sure I had a tighter seal with the inverted bowl, and used fresh yeast instead of what had been sitting in my pantry. The second loaf turned out better! Hope that particular tinkering helps a bit.

I've made the whole-wheat version twice, each time with a 3-day rise. First time, made according to recipe; dough was very stiff, barely rose in the refrigerator, and needed 5-hour second rise to reach top edge of pan, resulting in a squat, dense loaf.  Second time: reduced the flour mixture by 60 g., leaving all else the same: dough matched the recipe description and rose nicely in refrigerator and pan (8 1/2 x 4"). Bread has a nice chewy texture, good flavor. Will make again, as adjusted.
I have tried a variety of no/low knead breads and they usually fail to rise enough in the last rise before baking.  I always use fresh yeast.  Any suggestions?

I've made this loaf several times now because of its simplicity and minimal effort, and I really like the outcome. I used a glass pie dish as cover for the metal bowl. I also stuck to the exact gram measurements but varied the types of flour and liquid. Every time I was rewarded with a flavorful loaf that has the perfect size for a two-person household.
Can I simply use the original no knead recipe and method, and put it in a loaf pan, to make a sandwich loaf?
Why is there less water in this recipe than the non-sandwich version? My dough is not 'slack' at all and is barely rising. :(
Thank you for being brand-specific about the kosher salt measurements.

the whole wheat variation worked well for me with buckwheat flour. added a bit of honey and nigella seeds to the “jelly roll” step, and some honey to the egg wash. it’s really nice with herring or seaweed flakes or honey+butter. not the most spectacular looking loaf but moist and tender.

Just an added note to say that I baked this today after a night in the fridge. After forming the loaf I put it in the oven to rise and forgot about it for 6 hours. But the bread came out perfectly, perhaps a bit more aerated than after only 2-3 hours, but not at all overproofed or collapsed. It certainly is a forgiving recipe!

I've made this three times already. I need to let it rise for 4 or more hours. Only once has it crested the loaf pan and then it started collapsing before I got it in the oven. Right now I'm 4 hours in and it is an inch from the top of the loaf pan. I did use 150g of rye flour, 295g water, and 2.25 grams of Bob's Red Mill dry yeast. (I have the awesome Kitchen Aid scale which can read to 0.01g!) What else can I try next time?

I increased all of the ingredients by 10%, then by 20% to get a loaf that rises to sandwich-loaf height. Forming the loaf in the pan, and letting it rise for an hour before refrigerating, seems to work better than refrigerating right away.

I’ve made this a few times now. Mine never crests above the pan even after 4  hours of rise out of fridge but it still rises sufficiently in the oven, whether I’ve used sourdough (fed or under) as part of it or not. I’ve used 50-60 grams of freshly ground whole wheat and it still works. Very flexible and adaptable.

I was looking for a good white bread for summer tomato sandwiches, and nothing from the grocery store or bakeries worked.  This recipe is perfect if you just want a good basic white bread!  I followed the recipe exactly and sprinkled a little coarse salt on top.  I let it rest in the fridge two days, and it did take a while to rise—more like three hours.  I’ll definitely keep on making it.  It’s good!

Tasted fantastic!! Added a pinch of sugar to my 90°F water for my active dry yeast. Took ~3 hrs to rise after 24 hrs in the fridge. Needed to foil top of bread after 15 min of baking at 400°F, lowered heat to 300°F for an additional ~30 min bake.

I've been baking for 50 years, blah, blah, blah. The lemon juice/vinegar is to make you hard tap water less so. If you happen to have low pH water you don't need that; but never had that luxury. If you use bottled water you may still need the acid etc, etc. Hard water is a problem for these high hydration loaves; IMHO. Sourdough, again IMHO, doesn't have enough acid to solve the problem so I use 1/8 teaspoon of white vinegar and get much, much better loaves.

I’ve made this recipe numerous times, have yet to match the 30-45 sec initial mix time but never a problem. A generous cup of grated cheese in the dry ingredients makes this into an excellent cheddar cheese loaf for those that care to try it

Experimented with adding whole flax seeds into the bread (not just on the crust). I think it turned out great! Add a little more water to the dough during step 1 (maybe 10 more mL?), then during step 3 sprinkle seeds around the dough after you’ve done 2 rounds of folds but before you begin your 3rd round of folds. Keep adding seeds per round of folding until you think there’s an even enough distribution. Continue with the rest of the recipe! Yummy.

This was easy enough for a semi-novice bread baker - appreciate the detailed instructions. However, the loaf itself is meh. I did the whole wheat variation, and it’s very bland and lackluster. Maybe it needed longer fermenting in the fridge (I did about 24 hours), but the taste seems like it’s missing some sweetnesss.

Made the Low-Knead round loaf very successfully. Trying this sandwich loaf which looked good out of the fridge but definitely isn't rising in 2 hours or even 3. I'm wondering why with the low-knead round you take it from the fridge and bring to room temperature, then proof for 2 hours, but the sandwich loaf goes straight from fridge to proofing (or not as the case may be. Can't see where I went wrong.
This turned out great! For a simple recipe, it makes a very favorable bread. Will definitely make again!

Disappointing. I followed the recipe to the letter and the end result was a spongy, uninteresting loaf. The original recipe, which omitted lemon juice/vinegar and called for room temperature proofing for 18 or so hours, produced a much airier, chewer loaf that had a delicious crust. My bread has a sad crust. I toast it and it works but not worth all the time and effort.

This produced a beautiful loaf with a great crust and crumb with minimal effort.  However, I found the flavor to be a bit lackluster (used King Arthur bread flour, whole milk, and just an overnight rest).  It reminded me a bit of the cheap loaves of french/italian bread you can find in the grocery store bakery section.  Will try adding whole wheat or rye flour and a longer rest to see if the flavor improves.

Does anyone have a good suggestion for a vegan substitute for the egg wash? I've tried olive oil in the past and it doesn't give the same shiny brown crust as an egg. 

Thanks!
I use non-dairy milk (2 tbsp) and maple syrup (1 tbsp) combo

I have made a no knead sandwich loaf based off of a food52 recipe 2-3 x a week since covid began.  I augment that recipe with my leftover oatmeal, quinoa, etc to great success. This low knead recipe is too much effort for my daily bread.

I’m making Kenji’s recipe right now, but I just looked up that food52 recipe you referenced. It really does look incredibly easy! Thanks for your note, I’ll be trying that one next time. This NY Times recipe will work for me today because I have the time at home, but it’s great to have a couple of options.

I have not been successful with bread, but this came out well. I used a scale and followed the directions, except I did the initial mixing with a metal spoon. I hate putting my hands into wet ingredients, and there’s no reason one must use one’s hand. I made this because I make a bread stuffing/dressing for Thanksgiving, and it has become impossible to find unsliced bread in the supermarket.

Really impressed by how easy this recipe was and how tasty the bread turned out! I made two slight changes (greased the pan and brushed the top of the loaf with butter, and baked it for about 50 minutes), but otherwise followed the recipe to the letter. I've been intimidated by trying homemade bread for years, but I guess I shouldn't have been!

Update: I've also made this loaf with buttermilk. I replaced 60 ml of water with buttermilk and omitted the vinegar/lemon juice, but otherwise followed the recipe exactly. It turned out beautifully: nice light texture and a slightly richer flavor.
Perfect loaf! Followed directly with weight measurements. The dough was less sloppy than described but it all turned out great in the end!
Really nice loaf for sandwiches. Have made twice, the first time using 100g spelt, the second time using 100g kamut (as the whole wheat option). The timing is very adaptable.
299 g water little bread. 1 tea malt.2 TB potatoes
THIS IS GREAT

Replace 25 grams flour and 25 grams water with 50 grams sourdough discard. The dough wasn’t as shaggy or loose as I thought it should be based on recipe description but still turned out fine. Suffices for when my kids are tired of sourdough.

I love that this is a one loaf recipe. I've made it so many times that I can now improvise, but it's great as written.  It's so good toasted, which is what I was looking for.  I was inspired by a local pizza place that makes all kinds of sandwiches on house made Italian bread. Once this is toasted it is perfect for this.
Great bread. Followed notes that said rising would take around 3 hours (not 2 hours as stated in the recipe). Used water/milk wash for the top as well instead of egg wash.

I did the rye substitution. It was in the refrigerator for 36 hours. It has large bubbles in the dough when I took it out, so I know the yeast was working. It sat outside for 4 hours and it still had not risen to crown the pan. It did not rise during the baking, either.
Can anyone explain to me why it took 4  hours for the loaf to rise to the top of the pan? Is my yeast bad? Was it not warm enough in my house?
I usually warm up my oven at very low temp and turn it off. I then place my dough inside the oven and it helps with the rising in no time. I hope it will work for you too

First time trying this recipe and I followed the advice of another cook to sub in some buttermilk (I had to use it before it expired). It was in my fridge for about 30 hours and has sat on my countertop in my not-warm kitchen for 5 hours and it has barely risen. I’m not sure if I should go buy a loaf of bread for the deli slices I already purchased or if this will actually rise and then produce sandwich bread. Not sure where I went wrong but it’s not gone well for me.
Is 90 degrees Fahrenheit ? Would be nice to specify since some of us use Celsius

Way way WAY too complicated and too many "steps".  No need to use cloth towels.  I follow Lahey method very loosely and could write the directions in about 5 short sentences.  Last proof on big parchment sheet.  When ready, plop it with the risen dough into hot Le Creuset (I have handled 1000s of hot pans in my life and it is not that scary!).  Yes, the risen dough is flatish.  But it always turns into a perfectly round loaf for me. Original recipe is not fussy.   No need to make it so complex.

I do the last 2 hr proof in an 8 inch round cake pan lined with parchment.  I have a second 8 inch round heating up inside my Dutch oven.  To bake I lift the bread dough out of the proofing pan by the parchment and settle it into the hot pan in the Dutch oven.  This gives me a lovely round loaf.

I've done the original recipe a number of times. The only thing that really bothered me was handling the screaming hot Dutch oven. My sister passed on a tip that I have used to great success. Do whatever you need to do to get the dough to the baking stage. Then put the dough into a cold Dutch oven, lid on, and put that into a cold oven and set the temperature at 425. Once the temperature reaches 425 begin timing. 30 minutes lid on, 20-30 lid off. I really can't tell the difference.

@JimJ I preheat my ungreased, dry ceramic crockpot liner in the oven for 20 minutes. I pour the risen loaf out of a bowl into the hot crock without touching it, only pulling out the oven rack it is on far enough to both support the crock and easily receive the dough. Then I make a few scissors snips on top of the loaf, put the crock pot lid on (as long as it has an ovenproof knob) and bake as directed. I never handle the flaming hot crock until cooking is done and then with oven mitts after pulling the oven rack out to a safe distance.

The knob on the crockpot lid is an aftermarket find of all metal. Before I got it, I tried covering the old knob with aluminum foil. The knob discolored, but did not melt. To be safe, I replaced it. Old crock pots with ceramic liners are often found at secondhand stores for very little “dough”.

Several years ago I did side by side tests of preheated enamel bakers vs inverted $5 stainless steel bowls with identical results.   This eliminates the risk of burns associated with the heavy enamel,pots.  ( side by side tests of preheated stainless steel bowls vs room temperature bowls yeiled almost identical results as well.)
I'll use Lahey's classic recipe with the bit of lemon juice added next time.

I am fairly intrepid when it comes to bread-baking and did it for a living years ago. I am university educated with a baccalaureate of science and this is the most unclear, complicated way of baking bread I have ever seen. Also, I find that there is as little consistency between cooking scales as there are between volume measures and that, while weighing ingredients is imperative for large scale commercial baking, for home cooks, really not so much. Consistency is more important.

have been baking no-knead and other loaves for years now. love the addition of acid.
also, since I live in a small apartment and store lots of different types of flour, I find it convenient to add wheat gluten to King Arthur AP flour to achieve bread flour. their organic flour its 11.7% protein vs 12.7 in bread flour, thus in a 400g recipe I use 396g of flour and 4 g of gluten (about 2 tsp). works great!

"Form one hand into a stiff claw." This has to be one of the better recipe instructions to come out of the NYT. I am practicing, now, at my desk.

As always, brilliant work from Kenji. In my experience, it is possibly more rewarding to try different tricks and improvements, as he did, on the way to something special, than it is to repeat the successful recipe more than a few times. Bread is fun and rewarding and that really comes through in this article.

@Kevin D.
My hand is already a stiff claw.
@Kevin D.i have no idea how to turn my hand into a stiff claw. Please explain. I’ve never heard of this phrase.

I always add a touch of vinegar to bread baking liquids because our water has a pH that is above 7, ie our water is alkaline. If yours is, too, you will likely get better results if you add 1/4 tsp rather than 1/8th. Or use a pinch of fruit fresh with the dry ingredients.

Mr. Lopez-Alt's Cook's Illustrated version of the Lahey/Bittman recipe for No-Knead Bread had a great idea for using a parchment sheet to support the proofed loaf into a screaming hot Dutch oven. I use a 16" x 24" sheet of brown paper that I order on Amazon. I spray  the paper with a circle of cooking spray and let it proof in a 10" non-stick skillet, which helps the dough to keep its shape during the proofing. I lower the bread into the Dutch oven when ready to bake and never burn my hands.

I have always used a Lodge Dutch over preheated to 450 degrees and use large silicon oven gloves to handle the pot.
I let the white, wheat, kamut and rye flour stand about 24 hours at room temperature before baking.
I use 2 tsp of  proofed active yeast. 
I used to spread by hand into a rectangle on a floured cloth before baking but now just carefully scrape the dough into the hot pot.
I estimate when I have added enough water.
More water means bigger holes.
The bread is very tasty.

We have been making variations of this every 2-3 days for a long time.  You can skip the entire "flour a kitchen towel and proof the dough on towel-lined bowl" step, which is a giant pain.  Do the second knead right in the bowl.  Cover with plastic and let it do the second rise in the same bowl.  No towel. When ready to bake, just gently dump the dough out onto the baking sheet or pre-heated dutch oven (the older method).  I've never understood what the floured towel is supposed to do.
This says nothing about slashing the top of the bread before baking, is this not necessary with this recipe?
I greatly appreciate the way this recipe distinguishes Diamond crystal kosher salt from Morton kosher salt! Thanks

Instead of just mixing with your hand "in the form of a stiff claw", squeeze the mixture in your hand to incorporate the water and flour (so the motion is to repeatedly "make a fist.") Do this for a minute before proofing. This hydrates the flour much better and improves the structure of the final loaf. Doing 3 stretches of the dough over the course of the first 3 hours of proofing also improves the structure--either a one-handed lift-and-fold or a two-handed lift-from-the-middle.

A dutch oven technique:  mix the dough with a dough hook, remove hook, allow dough to rise in bowl sealed with sprayed wrap over night. Spatula dough onto floured surface, form rough ball, cover with same wrap, let rest while washing bowl clean.  Fold and form dough ball after a few minutes resting, spray clean bowl well, ease ball into bowl, ball-bottom side up, proof until doubled, remove wrap, spray 'pour' side again, place bowl lip over edge of dutch oven, pour gently. Cover and bake. Works!

I made Jim Lahey’s recipe once, but burned my hand on the Dutch oven and never tried it again. I tried this today and it may become my new go to bread recipe. 

I have a great baking stone in my oven, so I just inverted my banneton onto a parchment covered peel and slid the dough and parchment onto the baking stone and covered them with the metal bowl instead of fooling around with a baking sheet. I highly recommend doing so if you can.
The author should clarify whether the water temperature of 90 degrees is Fahrenheit or Celsius, especially in the context of a recipe giving metric amounts.
With respect, you need to apply common sense.  You always want warmish water, never hot. 90 degrees Celsius is 194 degrees Fahrenheit!

It's clear that the recipe uses Fahrenheit for all other temperatures given — I doubt anyone's idea of room temperature is 60-70º C, and I hope no one is baking bread in a 500º C oven. As Maeve stated, a bit of common sense goes a long way. As for the metric measurements, this is becoming standard for baking even in the U.S., as weight is more precise than volume.
Walmart sells a 5 pack of what is called “ flour sack towels” for an extremely low price. They would be perfect for this. I have also started using them in place of cheesecloth. They last forever.
@Ellen Oliver also IKEA

In the early pandemic, I used the original no knead bread recipe and produced a tasty but flattened loaf.
Then I made sourdough starter. Similar no knead technique with better result. Mix, knead and proof (preferably overnight) in the same high sided heavy plastic mixing bowl. No flour needed for stretching or moving dough and no floured towels. (How to clean them?)
Second proof in dutch oven then placed very hot oven.  
Rivals $7 farmer's market sourdough.
Hi Meg, any chance you'd be willing to share your measurements for your sourdough adjustments? We'd be grateful for your help!

Wonderful bread, but unnecessarily complicated recipe.  I made it the way I do all my breads, skipping the complex steps and it came out delicious.  The way it's written will put off most newbie bakers, which is a shame since the outcome is good minus all the fuss.

I notice in this version of no-knead bread that there is no beer for flavour complexity as in the earlier version. I am wondering why? I’ve been happily making a caraway rye, whole wheat and white version of it for ages now and am loathe to change but could always be convinced ...
Can you share the recipe (or origin of) for the beer caraway rye/wheat/white version? Sounds exactly like what I've been looking for!

This does seem extremely involved, and I found myself "Amazon-ing" for a high-sided metal mixing bowl until I realized, ah ha, that is exactly what is provided with a Kitchen Aid mixer. I may try it just to see, as I have always been disappointed with my flat no-knead loaves. I use parchment paper to get the dough into the baking vessel, either a Romertopf or an Emile Henry bread baker.
The Kitchen Aid mixer bowl is not wide enough for the loaf size...

I am confused by the measurements in this recipe.  In the tip, it is suggested to use King Arthur Bread Flour.  This flour has a weight of 120 grams/cup.  Yet the recipes calls for 400 grams, which is 3-1/3 cups of flour, not the 2-2/3 cups in the recipe.  Is there a mistake, should one use 400 grams of flour or assuming the 2-2/3 cups is correct, use 320 grams?  It is a big difference.  It seems odd to stress accuracy in measurements, but offer two different measurements by weight and volume.

This has been a quibble I've had with the NYT Cooking site for years. For unknown reasons, the estimable NYT uses their own "special" flour weights, and ignores King Arthur's weight chart, which is the Gold Standard of Baking specifications. And, despite repeated questions of various Baking recipes, the NYT remains mum on why they do so.
I have never made a bread recipe from this site successfully. I realize I'm in the minority, but maybe this discrepancy is the reason why.

I have been making the Lahey/Bittman recipe for many years with great success. I found this version unnecessarily complicated and the final product was only 2” high as opposed to the lovely high loaf I usually get. I’m going back to the old recipe (but I will add the vinegar.)

Since my dutch oven is a bit large my bread was higher in the middle and flatter around the edges.
