So I put the ring from a spring form baking pan (without the bottom) into my dutch oven and baked the bread dough in this slightly smaller form.  My bread came out looking much better and higher all around.

By minimizing degassing the dough, does the need to score the loaf to open it up go away?  Even with a good lame and imitating techniques shown on YouTube, I'm still not sure I'm doing my loaves any favors by scoring them...

I use Jim Lahey's recipe & method. It's Perfect. Use a Staub Dutch Oven. Never an issue. I do add 1/4 cup powder buttermilk to recipe. Found it enhances flavor. Only other process change was to place the bread in floured Linen banneton & then in the Fridg. This makes scoring easier. As Jim Lahey mentions in his video ... don't worry about what the dough looks like when placed in the Dutch oven. Magic happens in the Dutch Oven. So True. It's a Perfect loaf of Bread. Thank You Jim Lahey!!!

Interesting. I have always added a bit (~ 1 tbsp) of vinegar to pie dough in order to help prevent gluten from forming (or so I thought). Here vinegar is added to aid gluten development. I wonder if that pie dough trick has been wrong this entire time, or if the larger quantity of vinegar causes it to inhibit, rather than aid, gluten development...

The crust is noticeably better when the loaf is baked in a Dutch oven. If using one, you can put the dough in a  Dutch oven that hasn't been pre-heated. Same results as with a pre-heated one without potentially burning yourself.
Also, invert the dough after placing a slightly larger piece of parchment paper over it. I've had instances of loaves completely stuck to the Dutch oven without the use of parchment paper.
If your dough sticks to the Dutch oven you haven’t preheated it enough. I learned that the hard way.
When forming a stiff claw, should one also snarl?

In place of water, I often use whey (left over from making paneer). This has a bit of lemon juice in it from the curdling process. This works very well and also increases the protein content of the bread.

Agree that it is way, way, way too complicated.  Check out YouTube's Artisan Bread with Steve -- no weighing, no touching the dough -- KISS -- Keepin' it Simple, Steve.  I will try the addition of lemon or vinegar in my next loaf.
The addition of acid is arguably useful, but otherwise this "update" serves only to complicate a simple, perfect recipe. Moreover, the original recipe produces a better-looking loaf.

Overly complicated!  Bread sticks to the towel in the last proofing. Using a baking sheet lined with a copper mat, the bottom crust was stiff as a board. There has to be an easier method to make this.
I've found a well-floured couche does the trick. The towel never works for me.

I live in Mumbai and use whatever ingredients are avail. w/ good results. This only takes 5 min of active involvement. Mix 4c whole wheat and 1c AP flour with 2tsp salt and 1/2tsp yeast in a large bowl. Add 3c water. Mix w/ wooden spoon until smooth. Cover with plate and let rise about 4-5 hrs (hot climate). Oil a deep cake pan and dust with semolina. Deflate dough and transfer to prepared pan. Let rise. Transfer in pan to preheated cast iron pot w/ lid. Bake 25 min covered   20 min to brown.
This seems more complicated to me! I will stick with the original as it produces consistently good bread every time. An Emile Henry Bread & Potato pot helps.

I used a bag of all purpose flour from the $1 store and made a fantastic loaf while vacationing with Jim Lahey’s original recipe.  
Keep it simple and make good bread available to all.

I do a double loaf raison bread variation in an oval Dutch oven. Start by putting an ounce of Calvados in a bowl with about a cup of raisons, mix and microwave on high 30 seconds. Mix again. Let them sit will you blend the dry ingredients. 

Double all ingredients.

Sub out 1/4 of the white flour for whole wheat. Mix well the dry

Drain residual Calvados. Sprinkle some granular brown sugar, a half teaspoon of cinimon, and a teaspoon of cocao to lightly coat the raisins. Add to dry. Finish as per

The original Lahey recipe excels in its simplicity, but you can simplify it even further.  No need to proof the dough on a towel:  Just leave it covered on the work surface, and use a dough knife or kitchen scrape to lift it off the surface and into the form.  I bake it in two cast-iron Pullman loaf forms -- scale the recipe to 5+ cups flour, and divide the dough in two before proofing. (Add 9-10 ice cubes in the oven when baking uncovered to add moisture at the start.)
Not worth it at all. The original is a delight, this yields a flatter, denser loaf.

I've never understood why a towel is recommended for no-knead bread. It just makes more mess; much easier to get dough out of an unlined bowl or off parchment. Also, in a bread class at a bakery, we learned to use water, not flour, on hands and surfaces to prevent sticking. Works great! less mess and less extra flour added to dough.

On Serious Eats, Kenji has the ratio: 100g flour to 70g water to 1.5g salt to 1g yeast. Stick with that: this recipe does, with more salt (2%). Do a series of bowl folds at 25, 50, & 75 min. Refrigerate for 2-3 days for nice cold-ferment flavor. Shape it on parchment. Allow it to proof 3 hours, maybe 4 if your house is chilly, maybe 2 in summertime. Preheat your vessel in the oven for an hour. Drop the parchment in the preheated vessel, cover, bake halfway. remove lid, finish to 209˚ internally.

I've been baking no-knead bread with a Dutch oven all year, so I was intrigued by this recipe. I will say that the measurements and the addition of vinegar were great! The dough really rose nicely both times. But the technique of using a cookie and bowl was a DISASTER! The bottom burned badly and stuck to the cookie sheet. Maybe 500 is too hot (I usually do my Dutch oven at 475, then turn down to 425). But turning the dough onto a cookie sheet with no buffer like cornmeal was a mistake.

I have been making 5 loaves a week using the Lahey/Bittman method for months, and have been anxiously awaiting this recipe. I think it’s an improvement, primarily because the dough is not as wet. It’s much easier to shape and transfer the loaf. I also don’t not miss dropping the loaf into the hot, heavy Dutch oven. I will not be going back. 

PS I usually replace 60g of flour with whole wheat.  Still trying to get rid of the giant bag I bought at the beginning of the pandemonium.

I too found all of the steps very complicated. After reading it about three times and still confused, gave up and used my Dutch oven with excellent results. I crisped the loaf on a pizza stone for the last 12 minutes to give it a crunchy crust. Thank you Dutch. First Spinoza and then the Dutch oven. You are truly an amazing people.

I've been making the original Lahey bread for years.  This new recipe seems very complicated.  I'd love to see the instructions in video format, which would make it much easier to follow - large bowls, medium bowls, baking sheets, etc....???  And Step 4 in a video would help a lot, too.
He ended up doing a video of this that's up on YouTube if you're still interested.
Sorry, but this is far to complicated a procedure. The whole point of Mark Bittman's recipe is to make bread baking easy and not be time consuming. I'll stick with the old recipe, thanks.
Agreed. Jim Lahey's recipe is so easy. Mix. Ferment. Shape (only change: prove on parment in a bowl for easy transport to hot dutch oven, bake). So simple. Great results.
Is there a reason to pre-heat the oven for 30 minutes?  I understand with the dutch oven approach that you want it very hot - just not sure what the extra time would do in this setting.

When the oven temperature dial states it has reached your desired baking temperature (such as: 450 F or 500 F), the Dutch Oven is not that hot yet. The additional time is to allow the Dutch Oven itself to reach your desired temperature.

I have been making the original recipe for over a decade.  I decided to try this update.  It is not as good, imho.  I will use the acid again, good idea.  But I will go back to mixing the way I always did.  And I will try the cooking technique once more, but I bet I go back to my Dutch oven. This makes a smaller loaf than I have been using, so I will need to adjust the acid somehow for 4 cups of flour.

Like so many sourdough recipes this is a little too precious, and tends to put off beginners. I'm a handsful kind of baker. I don't weigh grams. I use measuring cups. The lemon is a great addition. I let the dough rise in a plastic King Arthur bucket with lid. Folding the four "corners" carefully is important to preserve air pockets. I use a flour-dusted proofing basket and a steel mixing bowl for the cover. It is so much less work than sourdough by the "book" and it tastes and chews great!

I see absolutely no point in this "updated" version of the original No Knead Bread Recipe.  I have made bread using the original recipe over 100 times now--with great results.  One tip:  you can cover the rising bread with a large stainless steel bowl for its two hour rise (the bread is on a floured or corn meal clean cloth).  It does rise higher that way.  But, it is so simple.  No hand as a stiff claw needed.  Seriously.  Add the vinegar if you wish--that part is simple.

Step one: reference a tried and true easy recipe
Step two: complicate it unnecessarily 
Step three: go back to original recipe

The improvement, if there is any, of this version over the original, is limited to the addition of a small amount of acid to improve structure.  Otherwise this is just adding steps and technical / precision “enhancements”.

Stick with the original Bittman adaptation.  Easy, low effort and excellent results.

If I want to make whole wheat bread, does it mean I still have to use 300 grams of bread flour and 100 grams of whole wheat flour? In that case how is it a whole wheat bread if only 25% whole wheat flour?
This turned out great. My only challenge was from a bit of sticking to the towel, so next time I'll add more flour, or follow Kenji's suggestion and use rice flour.

I found this recipe to be extremely easy to follow. The end result was a really nice, crunchy, chewy loaf - popping and crackling out of the oven. The crumb was beautiful - I liked the density and chew. I will definitely make it again and experiment with different flours and seed additions. The structure of the recipe is really tight. Excellent work!

I used to bake bread only on special occasions because on the time and effort. Then no-knead came along and the simplicity and time savings combined to get me to making bread 1-2x week to great success. 
This ‘updated’ recipe seems to bring back the complexity and time consumption. I’ll try, but color me skeptical.

I have been baking bread using Jim Lahey's recipe for years thought it was worth trying this updated version.  I followed the note replacing 1/4 of the King Arthur bread flour with KA whole wheat, folded the dough every 20 minutes, let it rest in the refrigerator for two days, and  baked it in a  cast iron Dutch oven.  This more labor intensive version produced a noticeably inferior result. It is nice looking for the structure is less satisfactory, too spongy. Not worth the effort / not better.

Excited to make this recipe. Relatively new to breaking bread (only 4th or 5th time) however the instructions seem overly confusing for what is a relatively simple process from Steps 3 to 5. Needed two pairs of eyes to determine what is happening. I'd consider simplifying the language.
We were particularly confused with reference to the multiple bowls. Would consider adding that into the "ingredients" or add a "tools" section.
I use an oval Roemertopf/clay pot soaked in water for 30 min. and lined with parchment paper. Place in cold oven. 30 min. with lid on, 20-30 min. With lid off.

Super heating any EMPTY enameled Dutch oven is asking for the surface to pop and pit and will void, for example, your Le Creuset warranty and ruin your pan.  Better to use the stainless bowls or a non-enameled pot or do as one writer suggested placing the dough in an unheated Dutch and into a cold oven and raising the temperature slowly.  Crinkle your parchment paper well before placing the dough on it will allow you to just drop paper and dough into your preferred pot - hot or cold.
I assumed this would be a shortcut recipe. It is not. Way too complicated for a no knead recipe. I’ll pass, thank you, and knead.

Looks like a minor update to Lahey's recipe. Kenji added precise measurements, which are useful for repeatable results. An acid ingredient for better gluten development. A little bit of folding for better crumb. Then the whole rising in a bowl with a towel and inverting onto a baking sheet. Not sure the last addition is necessary compared to the dutch oven trick, if you've been doing it that way for years. Going to give it a try this weekend!
Place the dough on a piece of parchment paper for second rise. Simply lift the edges of the paper when it’s time to put it into preheated Dutch oven. Couldn’t be easier.
This recipe is really hard to understand. I’m a novice-level baker and all I wanted was a simple bread recipe. I’ll be looking for a simpler one from now on.

Using the dishcloth is messy, at least for me. It's much easier and cleaner to use parchment paper (turn out the dough onto a big square of parchment paper, then hold it by the corners like a sling and lower into the hot Dutch oven or whatever baking container you use) Also, I don't use my hand to stir b/c the dough IS very sticky -- I use a big spatula. I've baked the Lahey loaf at least 50 times over the past couple of years, and used the big spatula and parchment paper works really well. :-)

This recipe is terrific for a semi-high altitude baking environment — 4800 ft above sea level. Beautiful rise. We have rarely enjoyed such a great crust. I did not have a metal bowl that I thought could survive 450 degrees so I inverted a Dutch oven over the loaf for final baking. Awkward to remove it. Needed four hands. But, bravo!

I get using hands to form the loaf to proof it.  But no need (no pun) to use hands mixing the flour and water - a flat silicone spatula is perfectly nonstick.  Also, when stretching the dough, do not tear it!  Doing so ruins the autolyse process that allows the proteins to maintain the loaf’s shape.  Stretch gently only a few times.

I prefer the cold oven method...for the final proof of the dough, put the shaped dough into a parchment lined (dusted with flour) Dutch oven, cover and place into a cold oven. Turn oven on to  425F and set timer to 30 mins. After 30 mins, remove cover and continue to bake for another 20-30 mins. Internal temperature of done bread should be approx 210F

Too fussy. The point is simplicity. I bake perfect bread with modified original recipe: dough should be firmer and hold shape! Adjust flour to be firm
1.5 c water. 1 tsp yeast (not "rapid rise"). 1.5 tsp salt. Lemon juice if you like. Mix. Add 3 c. flour and mix well. shape dough. rise in bowl until well "risen". (it flattens on top). put in fridge if you want.

bake: oven to 450 with dutch oven. shape 1/2 dough. score. sprinkle on coarse salt. rise 20'. put in dutch oven with lid 25'. no lid 15

I've been making all types of breads for nearly two decades, but I was intrigued by the apparently easy lightweight baking technique for boules using a sheet pan and a bowl. I followed the instructions exactly, but it didn't go well; the bottom burned and the top stuck to the bowl, which was quite slippery and awkward to handle and remove. A dutch oven preheated before adding dough either on parchment or a little semolina/corn meal yields a beatifully shaped, crusty loaf every time.

For my purposes, this recipe lacks the most attractive feature of the original recipe - simplicity. Just figuring out the flipping around, right kind of bowl that can go in the oven, where the towel is supposed to go - my brain does not form a picture of written instructions.

I just pulled this out of the oven and am waiting for it to cook enough to slice.  Made according to the instructions and my initial reaction is that it is a very small loaf for all that trouble; I’ll probably go back to Lahey’s original recipe and incorporating the suggested vinegar.  Many thanks to the commenter who suggested using an 8” round cake pan inserted into the Dutch oven to promote a rounder loaf.
I followed the ingredients list from this recipe.  Used  the instructions from the Bittman recipe.  Turned out nice.  Wish I could post photos.

I make this same recipe for rolls— 4 oblong (sometimes olive loaves) or 8 round rolls which I slash by cutting a cross with scissors like a Roman Rossetti roll. Adjust baking time. Shape and rise rolls on parchment on a cookie sheet, slide it onto a preheated baking stone or metal baking sheet. Easy and good!
I seem to remember that many older recipes for French Bread use a bit of vinegar in the dough.
I think that was to mimic the taste of sourdough.

Quoted from the article:
Mr. Migoya suggested that a small amount of acid could improve the formation of gluten bonds; in side-by-side tests, a drop or two of vinegar or lemon juice made an appreciable difference in dough strength.

French bakers did not have to mimic the taste of sourdough. The use of starters was commonplace. As stated in the related article, these recipes and the "original" no-knead recipe are based on methods that have been long in use.
The accompanying article says the vinegar or lemon juice is added to help strengthen the gluten bonds.
Which yeast should be used at higher altitudes?

I've had great success with the no-knead recipe until I decided to invest in a beautiful covered baker in a loaf shape.  Now I can't get any "oven spring" in my bread and it is dense and somewhat stodgy.  Why would the shape of my pan change the result?  Any suggestions?

I have made this twice now, once on a baking sheet and once on a cast iron pan, and both times it stuck fiercely and I ended up losing half the loaf to the pan, even after letting it steam/sit/cool etc... help!

You might try lightly oiling the baking surface or go with the parchment paper trick. I've done both, and both worked. Also, make sure the pan/Dutch oven is good and hot. And don't let it cool in the pan you baked it in. Remove and cool on a rack.

Use a parchment sling - it works well and allows you to gently lower the dough and then later remove the baked loaf from the very hot Dutch oven. The parchment will get brown but I find I can use it many times before it becomes too dry/brittle.
What would be the perfect stainless steel bowls for this technique to replace the Dutch oven? It says a diameter of 11-12 inches but what about depth?
I used the ones from Ikea. They were perfect.
I'm making a lot of cheese these days and I've taken to saving my whey for bread. I'm wondering has anyone tried that with this recipe?
Use a dutch oven, as with home sourdough baking.

Produced a perfect loaf first try — this is a great recipe !! I’m not a competent bread maker.  Kenji’s recipe delicious. My loaf could have been slightly taller but I had some trouble separating dough from the towel. More flour next time, and there will certainly be a next time !
sometimes, if you let it rise too long your bread will fall in the oven. Remember it rises a LOT in the oven.
I am a regular baker of sourdough no-knead bread. Is there a sourdough version of this recipe?

Made it with half whole wheat flour, half white, and it was fine. Will keep upping the whole wheat until it stops working. (Didn’t bother with any of the new-fangled techniques except the vinegar, happy with the result).
I would have thought that the smooth side would be the top of the loaf, but if I'm reading this correctly it is the bottom. Is that right? Also, why no scoring?
I followed this recipe exactly as written and it came out picture-perfect with a taste that exceeded what I expected. Thank you for sharing! It's a keeper!

Just beware "lower-middle rack".  Ovens vary, but you might want to go with middle-middle to avoid a burnt bottom.  Otherwise yay, very straightforward to make!  Sooooo much less work than all the pandemic sourdough of last summer.

Intrigued by the updated recipe I made the bread over the last two days.  I’ve already ruined a coated dutch oven pan making bread and didn’t want to ruin another stainless steel pan so just used the the Dutch oven cold to start.  A nice crisp and chewy crust.   Great flavor with nice holes edge to edge.  Gonna double the recipe next time.....

Lehman's updated recipe which has to be deciphered from YouTube is easier and quicker.
3 cups of flour (variation 2 cups white flour + cup whole wheat)
1 ½ teaspoons salt
¼ teaspoon active yeast
1 ½ cups hot water
¼ teaspoon red wine vinegar
4 hour rest sealed in bowl with plastic wrap, oven 450 degrees, half hour w/ dutch oven inside. let dough sit on parchment paper withwrap on top for that half hour. Bake with lid on half hour, lid off 8 minutes, check color, Remove let cool on rack half hour
Can you provide the link?

twice i've tried and twice i've gotten Ugly Bread. the measuring system seems so scattershot. some ingredients in grams, some in cups and tb and t spoons. different measurement for 3 kinds of salt. stop this madness. i'll stick with professor calvel's pain rustique at which i excel.
I tried it. Better results than with my dutch oven. Perfect crust on the first try and not having to handle a hot oven was a plus.

At last, after years of trying I was able to produce a bakery-standard loaf with a gas oven.  Crispy, chewy crust and soft interior with satisfyingly large holes.  Great recipe.  And really not difficult once you figure out the steps.  Thanks, NYT!
I use a covered oval clay pot/Roemertopf for bread baking. Soak pot first for 30 min. and place in cold oven.

I have a 6 year-old starter that I feed regularly (and share with brave friends).   I have a successful recipe that I make weekly, but I might try it in this recipe since the proofing time is long. Any ideas about how much to use?

What do you Bakers think of the idea of adding the salt AFTER adding the water, or simply dissolving the salt in the water? The concept being that while the salt is essential for the fermentation/rise, you don't want it in direct contact with the yeast as it may retard the yeast growth.  Also, no Baker's Lame slash on dough?

That's the reason some bakers autolyze. Add all the liquid & the yeast to the flour, mix it up & leave it for at least 30 minutes, or up to 2 hours. Then add the rest of the flour along with the salt & proceed as directed according in your recipe. Autolyzing helps the flour hydrate better and gives bread a better rise & improves flavor. Shirley Corriher's book Cookwise suggests it and I've been doing it for years with excellent results.

I followed the recipe exactly, except I baked the bread in a preheated Dutch oven at 450 instead of the inverted bowl method. The bread came out beautiful and perfect crumb. I’ve made Dutch oven bread many times. I think the most useful part of this recipe was proofing the dough in the mixing bowl. It gave me much higher loft than I normally get. 

My only complaint is that 400 grams of flour made for a pretty small loaf. All of that waiting left me hungry.

I don't deny that this makes a nice loaf of bread, but it seems a lot of fuss and bother to avoid kneading. Kneading, especially for a small loaf like this, isn't some feat of super-human strength. For simplicity, 12 ounces water, 20 ounces flour, 2 tsp (at least) salt, 2 tsp yeast. Mix to combine, then knead, give it 2 rises if you have time, just one of you're pressed, then bake it at 425 for 30 minutes...Voila!
Meg mentioned adding sourdough starter to the mix. Anyone know how much to add?

I am making this now & it is finishing baking in the oven. 2 suggestions: use rice flour on your dish towel to line the proof container—nothing sticks to rice flour! Use a sheet of parchment between the dough in the proof container and the baking sheet. I have nesting stainless bowls which worked very well in this no-knead recipe.

Amazon sells boxes of 30 Reynolds pre-cut sheets of parchment, 10.7X13.6 in. that are the ideal size for slings. They're easy to work with because they stay flat (unlike rolls or parchment). I can get a second loaf from each sheet if I'm careful. I've found this to be the least expensive way to go. My oven accommodates two Dutch ovens, so I always make doubles to save energy.

I followed step 1 exactly as written and my dough is extremely dry to the point where it's almost crumbly. This is not at all like Lahey's recipe, at least for me. I'm using King Arthur flour, 400g as stated in the recipe. A couple of comments mention a weight discrepancy between King Arthur conversion charts and the instructions in this (and other NYT recipes). Should I have used 320g?

I find this recipe to be muddled and complex. I’ve been making no-knead breads following Jeff Hertzberg and Zoe Francis’s Artisan Bread in Five Minutes a Day for quite a while and making loaves that look better than the one pictured with phenomenal taste and texture, and a lot less bother. I may add some acidity now.

I made this today after having made the original for the past year with no fuss, and will never follow this updated procedure again. The dough has always been sticky and wet, which isn't an issue usually. Now? It spread out on the baking sheet and stuck to the inside of the large metal bowl, to the point that the bread baked right onto the bowl. Impossible to remove the bowl without ruining the bread. Absolutely unnecessary. Will stick to the Dutch oven in the future.
I have also made the original recipe for years with great results.  These new instructions are almost comical---not going to try it.

I couldn't keep track of which bowls were which or whether my hands were supposed to be facing up or down. The ingredient proportions may be good, but I don't know if these preparation instructions are worth it.

Perfect is the enemy of good!

I definitely agree that the towel and bowel instructions are not written well. I just lightly spray the original bowl after I fold the dough, and throw the bread in again and cover, for the second rise. It's amazing enough this way that I don't know if I'd be able to stand it if using towels and extra bowls made the bread even better.
How can I add fresh Rosemary and maybe some garlic to this wonderful bread?

I've found it easy not to burn myself - I'm not sure what other people are doing?

I put the dutch oven and its lid next to it in the oven, 2nd to lowest rack. 

When it's time to back I put on an oven mitt and pull the tray out far enough to plop the dough into the cast iron with my other hand. 

Then I use the mitt hand to put the lid on and push the tray in. I never even touch the pot. I don't get how people are burning themselfves.

The first time I made this it was dense and stodgy, although I followed the instructions to the letter. 
I just tried again with much better results. I used whey leftover from making quark for the liquid and used about 300g. I skipped the bowl-on-bowl thing and instead made and rested the dough in a 2qt Cambro container with airtight lid and used the proof setting in my oven for the resting and proofing stage. I baked it in a preheated clay bread baker. Success!

I'm always amused by the idea that "room temperature" would be steady over 12-18 hours. What a rich-world concept. "Room temperature" here is distinctly seasonal and fluctuates when the sun goes down.

Perhaps I'm mistaken, but wouldn't bread have been proofed at inconsistent room temperatures for the entire time humans have been baking bread? If conditions are that much of a concern you might be able to find a recipe from a local baker that's adapted to your local conditions though.

It would be easier to knead the dough. I thought the objective of the original recipe was to make it easy for non-bread bakers like me to turn out a no-fuss, no-muss tasty loaf of bread. This ain’t it.

There is no need (no-knead!) to do dish towel stuff. My variation for the second rise: oil a bowl & sprinkle/dust bottom & sides with cornmeal. To form the dough into ball, wet hands (not flour) gather sides together, lift, turn & plop into the oiled & cornmealed bowl seam-side down. After 2-hour rise, sprinkle top with corn meal before plopping, top down, seam-side up, into hot (450) Le Creuset. Silicone oven mitts help. I use 430 g flour, bread  about 100g whole wheat, 345g water.

Agree with other cooks that is is an unnecessarily fussy version of no knead bread--mixing with hands?  Use the stick end of a wooden spoon--far easier to clean!  Two bowls?  Plastic wrap or better, use a 8c glass batter bowl with a lid so you can see the rise.    And perform the 2d rise on parchment paper (in a casserole/pie plate etc dish to give the loaf some side support)--when ready, just pick up the loaf and gently drop into your heated cast iron pot at baking time.  Nothing scary here.

I have made scores of loaves of the NY Times No Knead bread from the original recipe with excellent results every single time.  I don't see a need for this update and its complicated instructions.  Seek out the original recipe.  It is easy and it works beautifully.

Just use a bowl with a lid for the mixing & rising and bake in a Dutch oven. The secret to success is not over handling the dough and allowing at least 15-16 hrs for the first rise; ignore all the confusing instructions.   Gently fold the dough as instructed after the first rise, place fold-side down on a floured surface (try a silicon mat), and cover with the bowl until it’s time to bake. I don’t bother with parchment. Just use your hands to pick up the dough & place fold-side up in the pot.
I recommend using oven mitts when taking the bowl off, not just towels. you need your whole hand protected from the steam trapped under the bowl!

Of course! I've never been burned baking bread in a dutch oven. Ever. I think people make up problems so they can solve them and sell their new recipe.

I've also used a baking stone with a big metal mixing bowl inverted over the bread. Works great.

I don't like the baking sheet idea: the bottom burns.

I place the bread in an unheated enamel oven, in a complete cold oven, which reduces the risk of burns to zero.  I just oil the dutch oven, put the bread in, then put the dutch oven into a cold over, and then turn the temp way up to 450 or higher. 
I always get a very nice oven spring, and the bread comes out perfect.  In fact, with a sourdough starter instead of yeast, I get a better loaf.

I’ve been happily churning out no-knead bread using the King Arthur recipe on their site. And it only involves 3 steps (mix flour, water, yeast and salt; proof at room temp for 2 hours, rise in fridge for 2 hours to a whopping 7 days). I basically get sandwich bread with a good crust (bake in oven with water). Best part is I can take out dough portions & bake throughout the week for fresh bread. For those of you who have tried this recipe and its myriad steps, is the taste worth the effort?

Learned from the old recipe. Have made this bread (without acid) every week since 2017. It's truly effortless and people think you're a master baker. The "effort" is timing it: mixing and waiting 15-18 hours, and making sure you'll be around to turn on the oven to bake it. Yeah, this recipe is so much more complex. Just watch his original video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=13Ah9ES2yTU (don't use towels - parchment for 2nd rise, covered with a cloth napkin). Why complicate it? A chef thing?

I agree with you. I will stick to KAB’s recipe. I did not have success with this recipe and found it too way too long and was not worth the effort. Also, if you have issues with a KAB recipe, the people at KAB will respond and offer advice. I bake a lot, followed the instructions and even weighed the ingredients but consider this a fail.

My first loaf is in the oven and I'm already thinking about future variations! I'm interested in adding mix-ins like olives, sundried tomatoes, cheese, etc. Any insight into when is the best time to add bulky mix-ins so that they don't impact the gluten formation/deflate the loaf?

After mixing I cover with towel; let set 5-6 hours. Line a smaller bowl (smaller than Dutch oven I bake bread in) with parchment paper and let dough rise about 40 minutes. Heat oven 450 degrees and have Dutch oven heating up  then transfer, carefully, the dough on the 
parchment into the hot Dutch oven. Bake covered 30 minutes uncover bake 15 to 20 minutes remove and cool. I also spritz water into hot oven a couple times for browning.
Can I use 1 cup of ground flaxseed and 1 cup of whole wheat in addition to the white flour. How would I adjust the water measurement?
Probably.  You’d be adding 2/3 as much dry ingredient again so add that much water.  And why not experiment- it’s just one loaf of bread!

I followed all the technique and weight measurements, down to the claw-shaped hand, with superb results. Only mistake I made was to take someone’s advice and bake dough on parchment, which stuck to the bread terribly and had to be cut free. Baked per Bittman in cast iron Dutch oven.  Fantastic bread full of crusty flavor.
Some suggestions re parchment - pull it out when you remove the pot lid. That usually works though it does sometimes get stuck.

I've now made this many times and feel confident to offer the following summary : 
1. The wetter the mix the bigger the holes.  So add a bit if your flour is absorbent and/or you're using a mix
2. Don't leave out the acid by mistake :-) 
3. The tea towel thing is to be avoided.  Use baking parchment with lots of flour (it feeds the mix) 
4. The 2 x cake pans idea is a really good one- thanks Cath 
5. Use a dutch oven.  It has handles.
Amazing! Great crust, excellent crumb! Will be my go-to bread from here on out!

The Venn diagram of people complaining that Kenji's method of using bowls instead of a Dutch oven is too complicated and the people that are going to end up big mad about burning themselves on a scorching hot Dutch oven is a perfect circle.
Cooked it. Shared it. Family loved it.
This all seems terribly complicated. Why bother?  Why not just make kneaded bread?
A bit too cottage core for me.
Unnecessarily overly complicated. Speedy No-Knead Bread recipe is a much better option.

WHY IS THIS BREAD SO GOOD?! I don’t even feel like I know how to make bread and my family said this was the best bread they have ever had. It is out of this world. Better than any bakery on the planet. I followed the recipe but baked it in a parchment satchel in a covered Dutch oven with a little water added to the pot. If you can, you should watch Kenji make it on YouTube. I felt less confused after watching how he did it.
If ever a recipe needed a video….this one is it!

Oh my lord - I read through this recipe three times and still cannot make sense of the dance with flour, bowls , towels.  I have just made the dough according to recipe  - the stiff claw hand was easy! Really chilly morning here in Kerry , my hands freezing.  I’m going to continue with my usual method of a second rise in a wicker banneton and plop the dough into heated Dutch oven. Yes, you have to be careful handling the hot pot but come on, the method as described in recipe would have me heading for a Guinness before 11 AM. Stay tuned.

I use the original no knead method. Final rise is on parchment fitted into the (washed)original mixing bowl. I sprinkle the top with Everything Bagel Mix supplemented with more garlic salt. When rise is done then lift the parch into the hot Le Crueset and that into the oven.  45 mins. later, life is so so good.
Why must we keep messing with Jim Lehey's original recipe? It's easy and it's perfect. No tweaks necessary. I just don't get it.
For the final no-knead proof, I let the dough rise in a greased bowl. Then I quickly shape before dropping it into the Dutch oven. Works great.

I've been experimenting with Andreas Viestad's no-knead rye bread recipe.  He uses carbonated water and beer to increase the rise and impart a malty flavor.  I haven't burned myself (yet) transferring the dough to a 500 degree Dutch oven but I can see how my handling the dough would tend to deflate it so I'll attempt the towel manuever described here.   It would help to know what size Dutch oven to use to achieve the best results.  Is an oval or round pot best?  Does it matter?
I think I'll try baking it in a Pullman loaf pan to see if I can get some good sandwich bread out of it.

The large bowl for the final step bumped up against my loaf and stuck. 

Next time I might preheat the bowl with the oven, a la the original recipe. That way a bump will burn off right away rather than stick.
In the parentheses of step 6, shouldn't it be the bowl that is inverted? That's how I'm reading it anyway.

The Lahey recipe allowed for the addition of small amounts of wheat germ or flax seeds, worked in before the second rise, making the bread more nutritious. I miss that with these instructions, though the texture is better.
The timing is such that it needs to be made at night to rise overnight. Otherwise you end up baking in the middle of the night. If you the first step too long it never recovers.

One detail I would like clarified. At the end of step 4, “until top surface is relatively smooth and taut” is a little vague. Are we talking 4 or 5 tucks, 10 or 20 tucks, or what? A ballpark figure would be helpful? After letting it rise for 2 hrs, the surface still springs back, but I worry that might be because I made it too taut

I made the Mark Bittman published recipe for this the other day. Having left it overnight, it had risen and then fallen some before I got up the next morning. I decided to bake at about the 14 hour mark in case it degraded completely and it baked just fine, with decent ovenspring. Anyone have a comment about the dangers of the dough falling before baking?  I’m mindfully applying my sourdough techniques to this process, and that tells me I should catch it before it starts to fall. It was great.

Bread was better than original no-knead.  Crust good, more malleable and chewy. 
However bottom burned and hard.  Is there a way to prevent that?

Mine burned the first time I made it. A couple of things that come to mind are: oven temps vary, and rack level. This time I lowered the temp a bit (like, a few degrees) and moved the oven rack up to the middle.  It still got a bit burnt but less than last time.  Next time I plan to tweak one or both again.  Hope that helps!
I use an insulated cookie sheet pan on the bottom to prevent burning.
Why so little yeast?
The long ferment time makes complete use of a very small amount of yeast.

I have made the original recipe many times with a dutch oven. I never had a problem handling it, but using the bowl method is a welcome alternative for some bakers. I did the final rise of the dough on a sheet of parchment paper, then transfer the dough, with the parchment, to the hot dutch oven. The parchment gets a bit crispy but never burns.
The towel is just a huge sticky mess! I will never do that again. I prefer the original method, with maybe a few personal tweaks
Thank you for the tip on using metal mixer bowl as cover in oven.  All my other metal bowls have some rubber at the base.

This worked very well for me because my dutch oven has a knob that can't be exposed to such high heat, so I have to remove it and stuff the hole with foil to try to seal the pot. Big hassle. This was a perfect solution. J. is a wizard.
The YouTube video is a help.
I made this with the slight modification of using a Dutch oven for the final bake. Amazing. Best bread I’ve made. The texture is perfect. Soft inside and an amazing crust.

Clarification, Please:  In step 5, it says "Carefully lift the dough, place it smooth-side up into the towel-lined bowl..." Smooth-side up: what does that mean? Isn't it actually smooth side down, against the towel, so that  when you invert it, it is now "smooth side up"?  Please let me know.

Thanks
I flipped the whole thing over, per the instructions, so the smooth side was on the bottom of the baking dish. This meant that the top ended up with beautiful curves and ridges.

A big improvement over the original No-Knead Bread recipe. I baked mine in a glass pie dish with a metal bowl on top, and it's crusty and perfect. Also, TOTALLY STUCK TO THE GLASS DISH. Will bake it on parchment next time.

I baked this and came out with a shockingly (to me) gorgeous and delicious loaf of bread.  I'm blown away by how it looks and all its flavor -- I wish I could post a photo. And while I am proud of myself, all I did was follow this recipe, so I am blown away by both of us :-)

Everyone has different ideas, but I can only say that this is THEE BEST French style bread I have ever made, and I have made many breads. You have captured not only the essence of the bread, but you've made it incredibly easy to make as long as you have a pizza peel and steel, which I have as I make homemade pizza dough every week. Thank you for such a great recipe!!!

I made a second alteration In this recipe the second time I made it. (See my earlier note for the first one.) I pushed the whole wheat flour to 200 grams instead of the recommended 100 grams, and pushed the water to 300 grams. It was great. It rose well and the flavor and texture were very good.

Put tons of flour on towel to prevent it sticking.
Fan in oven, 450, go longer than you think to try to get a crispy exterior……

You might want to check you oven temperature.  It took 10 minutes after I took the pan on top off for a nice crisp crust.  Of course I preheated my oven for an hour as most oven say there at temperature but it is only air, so I have tracked with my thermocouple and it takes about an hour to stabilize at 500 degrees.

No knead to have a hot pan in the oven to make this!  I followed the recipe verbatim and it came out great.  I have a residential commercial range and I let it heat up for an hour getting to 500 degrees. I also have a stone in my oven that provides radiant heat.

I’ve made this 3 times. Everything works well except when I invert the proofed dough onto the parchment-lined baking sheet the dough spreads out a lot and cracks a lot. It spreads so much I can hardly put the bigger bowl on to cover. Today when that happened I gave the dough a few folds to tighten it up before covering it with the larger wet bowl. Any ideas on a remedy for this spreading? Maybe 1/4 tsp vinegar instead of 1/8? More folding in step #4? Has anyone else had this problem?

I make oval, tall breads (for sandwiches!) by doing a fair amount of cloaking, more than Kenji does, to make the top surface smooth and taut. (Google How to shape wet dough ArtisanBreadinFive), going for an oval shape. I slash with my bread knife. Never fails.

Also, I started putting the parchment and dough in my covered fish poacher. No need to preheat, and if I'm making 2 loaves, they both fit.

This is a very tasty bread but be warned:  the dough sticks to anything and everything it touches.  When doing the second rise, DO NOT use a towel to line the bowl, even if you coat it with flour.  It sticks.  I used parchment paper and it even stuck to that a little bit.  When covering with a metal pan to bake it, use a REALLY BIG ONE.  If the pan touches the dough, it will stick.  I would recommend going back to the more traditional dutch oven method.
I've used beer instead of water for deeper flavor.  I also like to use dried onion flakes to make it onion bread.  I usually use 2/3 bread flour and 1/3 rye or whole wheat flour for nuttier flavor
Has anyone trying adding Rosemary? If yes, please share how and results. Thanks!
I add fresh rosemary leaves and sea salt on the dough before putting it in the oven.  Some of them fall off but enough stick.
Rather than an inverted bowl, I use a stainless steel food cover (cloche) that has a handle on the top. Much easier.
No need to pre-heat baking vessel. I use Lodge Combo Cooker. I load cooker cold, reduce oven temp to 450°, bake 20 mins, remove lid, bake additional 20 minutes. Perfect.
How would this recipe be for pizza?

Makes the best "grandma" pizza ever! I use the original recipe that makes 3 loaves. Keep the dough in the fridge and pull off a piece to make pizza. Let it rest about an hour at room temp so I can stretch it into a 15x10 rimmed sheet pan. Prep the pan with some olive oil, stretch dough into place.  I also brush the top of the dough with olive oil. Add sauce and toppings and cook at 450 for about 15-20 min. Rarely order out pizza it is so good.
Look up Jim Lahey's pizza dough recipe -- it's similar to this but there are differences in technique.
I've used the original for years for both regular and sourdough.. always with my Dutch oven.  Turns out amazing. Wish I could post a photo.. beautiful!
Summer in NC means my kitchen is about 78 degrees. Should I change the room temp rise time? Thanks.

You may be able to get away with a shorter time, but there is no one exact amount of time required for the resting. I've done anywhere between 12 and 18 hours (at 70 degrees or so). So you could go with the short end of that on your first try.

I've made this bread since shortly after it was introduced. Every loaf is gorgeous. The only change I've made to Lahey's recipe is that you can actually -- according to your dough -- move to the second phase after only three hours. I and my family adore this bread.
If I use a Dutch oven, what size (in quarts) do I need?
A 5-6 quart, although if you have a larger one that should be fine as well.

would love to try this with sourdough starter instead of yeast! If anyone has tried it, share your measurements please :) I've tried Bittman/Lahey recipe and enjoy it, been curious to try it with bread flour (which is why I am checking this recipe out). Might just try the old recipe with the starter and do bread flour and see what happens!

I've always made no-knead with sourdough starter. Use the basic no-knead sourdough recipe on Breadtopia. Something like a quarter-cup of starter to 3 cups of flour. (I'm sure they list grams now but I always went with their original cup measurements)

This seems like a "whole Megillah" and a lot of time, just to avoid some kneading.  Question: is the effort worth it---i.e., is this superior to other kinds of bread?

I guess I'm lazy and impatient, but I make a pretty nice homemade wholewheat low-sodium bread by having my bread machine do the kneading and then letting the dough rise on a cookie sheet covered in parchment paper.  Usually I do just one rise, and it comes out fine.  Sometime short, sweet, and simple does the trick.
We live in Tucson, AZ so it seems that I need to add more water than such recipes usually call for because of our dry air and thus drier flour.
Has anyone tried this with gluten free flour?

Towel? Baking sheet? Stiff Claw? Nope, not gonna go there. Keep your hands out of the dough and mix until flour is incorporated using a wooden spoon. Do your second rise on a parchment sling in a shallow bowl and lift it to place in the hot dutch oven when it's time to bake. These instructions are unnecessarily complicated, require tools that you don't need, and don't yield a better loaf for all of your trouble. My take-away from this article: add a few drops of vinegar and carry on as usual.
I have a baking stone in my oven. Could I invert the bowl over the preheated baking stone and cover that with the moistened bowl?

I’ve always weighed my flour and use 120 grams of bread flour for a cup. This recipe uses 150 grams. I’ll stick with my calculation. Otherwise I agree this may be overly complex for what May be a scant difference on the final results.
Ken, raisins.
What about using a starter. What would the correct amount to use.
Jim Lahey’s original recipe is perfect, I’ve been using it for years with great results.. No need to complicate it.  But as others have said I’ll try the vinegar next time.

I preheat my pot for 45-60 minutes. To avoid trying to put the dough into a very hot pot without burning my hands, put the dough on a piece of parchment paper that's slightly larger in diameter than the pot itself.  I place this in a flat-bottom mixing bowl that's approximately the size of my pot and refrigerate overnight. Once the oven & baking pot are hot, I remove the dough from the refrigerator, slash the bread and pick up the dough by the edges of the paper, slowly placing it in the pot.
Smelled great, but completely glued to the baking sheet and couldn't transfer (I should have used parchment on the sheet, perhaps)
It is okay to put a metal bowl in a hot oven?  I have never done that.

This bread turned out beautifully with very little effort but the floured towel did not work for me at all. I had a terrible time getting it off and was very nervous watching my nicely proofed dough deflate as I tried to gently pick off the sticky dough. Next time I will use parchment paper as other people suggested or just a well oiled bowl. Otherwise, this was really easy and made a nice, crusty loaf that we served with the NYT slow cooker chicken thighs and 20 garlic cloves. Yum!

The descriptions are a bit complicated, but I made this recipe today and was pleased with the results

Read closely, measure to the gram, temp the water, proof the yeast. The one change I made was more water. It helps to find examples of what shaggy dough looks like, then go off of that, than to blindly trust a bread recipe on how much water to use

This recipe uses common tools and ingredients to emulate more advanced techniques. It could be clearer, but it's solid as long as you read closely
I used 2 1/2 teaspoons of kosher salt as directed and the loaf was too salty. The original recipe called for 2 teaspoons kosher salt, so I am surprised this was overly salty.
You really have to weigh the salt. The density of salt crystals and how they're packed in packaging is so different among the salts you can use.
I quickly ruined the bottom of my Staub Dutch oven preheating it to bake bread. Does anyone have this problem or solution?
Try the recipe published above. No Dutch oven required.
Y’all’d be better off getting Ken Forkish’s book “Flour, Water, Salt, Yeast” and following his methods.
That's what Lehey originally said! https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=13Ah9ES2yTU Why are they / J. Kenji López-Alt complicating this easy easy recipe? Don't ruin a good thing.

For the second rise, I put the dough on a small silicone baking mat. Slides right off when transferring to a Dutch oven. I have also used a stainless steel pot lined with a silicone mat to bake in. No sticking to the sides of the pot!

I use a stainless steel cloche as the baking cover in the oven. Easier to lift off since it has a handle at the top. And rather inexpensive (for me it was under 10€ on Amazon, but probably lower price at a restaurant supply store).
Is there any way to use sour dough starter instead of or in addition to rapid rise yeast with this recipe?
There are many sour dough recipes for this bread on the internet and in Jim Lehey's book.

I've made my own bread since I found the recipe, 15 years ago. Why specify Morton salt? it contains silicium dioxide that makes possible "when it rains, it pours, an anti-caking agent. Many of us don't want our family to consume microscopic rocks. Why add vinegar or whatever fermented substance that some of us are sensitive to? Also, you don't need accuracy per the tip (within reason). I sometimes use hot water to speed up the process. Water. Not vinegar. Salt. Not Morton's.

Yes, this recipe is a bit fussy, as so many commenters complain, but it produced a delicious and professional-looking loaf. The bowl technique sounds complicated but it's not and the sheet pan worked fine. I did prefer this technique to using a hot Le Creuset.  My only complaint: weighing was a pain that, in the end, produced a too-dry dough that needed more water.
sounds like it would be easier to knead it!

I followed this recipes very closely as I am new to no-knead bread. I had a strange feeling when I inverted the dough directly onto the baking sheet. And there you go, the bread stuck to the sheet and I could not remove it without breaking it in two halves :((. Next time I’ll add flower onto the baking sheet… as my intuition told me to.
So effortless and yet so tasty!!
Very long and complex recipe. Not as delicious as sourdough, but a nice alternative if you’re low on time!

I've only ever made this bread with sourdough starter, the dough is never particularly wet at any stage of the process, even in humid weather, and it's never doubled in size, but it still makes a tasty loaf that I've been baking in a Dutch oven every week for friends and nonprofits every week for many years.

“Cool enough to slice”, sorry!  The bread is good but for some reason did not rise as much as I expected.  It was a smaller loaf than I make with Lahey’s recipe but proportionally less lofty.  I’m going back to the original, not  even using the vinegar as it made no appreciable difference in taste or texture.

I did an Alt/Lahey-Bitman face-off this weekend & also found this loaf to be about 20% smaller (should have weighed them). Wonder why that would be. Seems like the kind of question Kenji would be into addressing...
What would be the proportion if I wanted to use spelt?

Did it as suggested and it made a lovely loaf. OK,I am not sure I did the towel business the way it  said.  And I used my pizza stone and metal cazuela over it (which is the updated version). But some of the best bread I've made (and yes I've done a ton of sourdough).
Great taste and perfect crumb. My oven cooked this too browned though so I’ll reduce cooking time or oven temp.

As others have noted, the original recipe is close to fail safe.  I use a Danish dough whisk, rather than a "claw!"  Next time, I'll try adding a little vinegar, as well as a little whole wheat flour.  Since I use a scale, I'll stop the AP at 390g and add 40g of whole wheat.
Could this recipe be doubled successfully ?
Use Mark Vetri’s bread making technique
I don't see the necessity for this. His original recipe is simple, delicious and comes out perfect every time. Waste of time for the same result.

Last weekend, I did a comparison test w/ the Lahey/Bitman recipe, making 2 batches of each. 1st batch proofed for 24 hrs before backing, the 2nd stayed in the fridge for 3 days. Overall, I preferred the Lahey/Bitman loaves, which had a less dense crumb & a chewier crust, but my spouse preferred the tighter crumb of Kenji loaves. No discernible flavor difference. Kenji loaves were about 20% smaller than L/B loaves. Not sure why. All were pretty delicious. But L/B loaves were a notch more amazing.
Using oven mitts to pull the rack out and then dropping the parchment in with your bare hands was pretty easy. Always handle the Dutch oven with mitts. Not sure why people are burning themselves.
I have made this bread a lot and it is easy and delicious. But why the vinegar? Why fix what is not broke?
Can I leave the bread to rest for 24 hours?
Did not work at all. Bread never rose

I may just not have baker’s genes in my body. I have tried twice and have failed twice. I used the vinegar I had on hand - rice wine vinegar. Could that be the problem?  My attempts reminded me of a book I read years ago about a home gardener who tried to raise tomatoes. By the time he grew one beautiful one, he had spent the equivalent of $64 per tomato. I feel like if I keep trying, I will end up with a very expensive loaf of bread that I could have bought at the farmers market for $6. But I am having fun!

I've been baking the no-knead bread for a long time, and the vinegar makes for a significant improvement in the bread texture. 
I used a dutch oven for a long time, but the metal bowl allows you to swish it with water prior to covering the loaf, which adds just a little steam. I was happy enough with the improved rise that I started using the Brod & Taylor baking shells. I'm short - leaning over an oven door with a hot hunk of cast iron is not my fave, so that's also a plus for me.

I love the journey bread was delicious, my next loaf will be in 2 weeks at my guys bakery , he’s been there 35 years and for some reason I don’t mind paying 12 bucks a loaf and his labor for his incredible family, I won’t tell him we all have  better way,
Wow, what a difference 1/8 of a teaspoon of lemon juice made!  This version is much tenderer and fluffier.   Also, my skills may be improving.

This updated version is way way way too complicated.  The whole point of the original awesome recipe is its easiness and good bread.  This one required a lot of attention and 7 steps with multiple paragraphs to read.  Not worth it.  If I have to pay all that attention there are so many other recipes to choose from.

I made this according to the instructions with brand-new yeast. The one difference was I covered the big bowl with plastic wrap in an airtight seal for the overnight rise instead of using an inverted smaller bowl or baking sheet. Every couple of hours I notice that the plastic wrap has inflated a bit and I "burped" it. The result was a dense and spongy loaf. Could it be that you don't actually want an airtight seal, that the lack of air escape keeps the dough from rising properly?

I have used the original Leahy recipe and gotten decent results. I tried this twice. Both times the dough did not rise much overnight; the result was a small and very dense loaf. Can anyone suggest what I'm doing wrong? Quantities measured precisely with gram scale. Yeast newly bought w/exp date far into future. 1st time covered big bowl w plastic wrap, airtight seal. 2nd time covered w inverted smaller bowl, not airtight. Same result.

This is just way too putzy for someone (like me) who doesn't bake much and isn't adept at handling bread dough. The whole point of the Leahy recipe was to make it possible for people like us to bake really good bread. All the dough handling steps in this recipe just introduce opportunities to deflate and otherwise mess up the dough. If you have to be that good at dough handling to make this successfully then kneading will hold no terrors for you.

I use the ingredients listed here, but still use a Dutch oven. I find this updated version of no knead bread to be even better than the original. My loaves have been picture worthy and I wish I could post one here.

stats.Total Time: 4 hours, plus 13 hours’ to 3 days’ resting

comments.commentBody: I still prefer the original Cooks Illustrated version, using a preheated Dutch oven and parchment paper to move the wet dough. It is the simplest and produces consistent results with the least amount of work.

I've had really good results with a similar process of initial foldings but then transferring the dough to a parchment lined mixing bowl. I put the bowl with dough (in plastic bag) and proof it overnight in my microwave (works great as a proofing box). 12 to 18 hours later, I heat up my Dutch oven to 500 degrees and after 30 minutes, lift up the fully proofed, puffy dough ball with its parchment into it. Bake for 30 minutes at 450 degrees, remove lid, 20 minutes more. No more deflated dough,

I enjoyed this recipe as did everyone that came over for dinner! I would recommend trying it as written as there was a lot of testing that went into it (as any Kenji recipe), take a look at his youtube video as well if you need a visual aide, it is here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6RUDa0FKplk

The folding technique is not a waste of time because it transforms what starts out as a heavy lifeless wet flour ball into an amazing lively puffy happy cloud of bread dough that gets happier with each of these turns.  When that happens you know you are creating the start of great friendship; which climaxes with the eating of a thick slice of it, spread with butter, and accompanied by a crisp white wine.

I've been making the Cook's Illustrated version for years and years and I thought I'd give this a try.  The method is easy as could be, although it's not quite as "slap it together and forget about it until morning" as the Cook's version.  But honestly, the results are so much more like I want them to be.  The loaf was open textured, lively and crisp on the outside.  Perfect chew on the inside.  I think this is a big improvement.

I am (or I should we are, since my whole family benefits) very very grateful to Kenji for taking all the time to amalgamate several techniques to come up with such an easy, straightforward, reproducible and at the same time flexible  system for making truly delicious bread. The flip stage after initial addition of yeast mirrors what I had learned to do with sourdough levain from Tartine (which was wonderful as well), but allows for use of commercial yeast, which fits my schedule. Thank you Kenji

Unbelievable waste of time flipping and flopping.

I suggest this:

1: adjust flour/water combo to make a dough that can stand up on its own. The "shaggy" floppy dough is a pain to handle and it's unnecessary to get the desired "crumb" and "crust" and "hole structure.

2: After mixing everything together in abowl, let it sit, covered (i put a plate on my bowl) for 10 to 20 minutes while you do something else worthwhile.

3: Lift and fold the dough a few times.

4: rest, rise, bake in dutch oven.

If you have suggestions to improve it, give us your actual measurements to improve on this recipe that was painstakingly developed over months and months by a professional chef. Show us the proof, don't just say "unbelievable waste of time, just adjust it"

Followed the recipe and watched the video a reader linked.  It's really not that much extra work and the results are worth it.  Compared to the original no knead bread, I think this recipe tastes better and the finished product is less spongy.  I burned the bottom but that was my fault, I should have moved the rack up higher.

Regardless, it was worth the effort because I learned how differences in hydration changes the texture of the bread.

I've baked this bread for two years, made several adjustments on the original recipe, and incorporated a few from this one: 

1. Mix dough: a few drops of vinegar for strength, plus a glug of olive oil for flavor. 

2. Fold it twice in the first few hours. 

3. Put dough into fridge after 14-16 hours overnight. 

4. Final proof in a benetton/bowl, flip onto a parchment paper, lift to transfer to pot.

5. Bake with dutch oven with a baking sheet on the bottom rack to avoid burning the bottom.
Can I offer a suggestion?  Just mix it in a glass bowl and cover tightly with saran wrap.  This medium bowl - large bowl - inverted bowl nonsense is tedious!

I'll stick with the Bittman method.  Quick, easy, and the bread is always good.
This new method is way too involved for the majority of cooks today.  Life is way too busy to do all that flipping and folding.  That's why the Bittman method was and is so popular.

For everyone struggling with the directions, watch the video. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6RUDa0FKplk If the link isn't allowed, go to youtube and search Kenji low knead bread. He makes the bread step by step and explains everything.

Absolutely worth the effort. Takes some diligent reading to make sure you’re doing it right the first time, and his YouTube video helps, but mine came out fantastic on the first try! I will be using this method again in the future, and it’ll be much easier in future attempts. 

I highly recommend getting a small bag of rice flour for the proofing as I’ve had way too many breads stick to the towel. With rice flour, it very easily just slides off.

If you do the final rise on parchment in a bowl and lift it into the hot Dutch oven, it's much simpler, but don't forget to slash the loaf, right before putting the lid on the Dutch oven. This will allow it to rise higher in the initial stages of baking and develop a more attractive crust. 
It's also nice to scatter Maldon flaked salt over the dough before baking it.

I finally found my go to recipe. My breads have been very dense, which I like but Mrs. turns her nose up at. This had a lovely, crunchy crust, airy interior and "restaurant quality taste." Variations. Added 2 tbsp of ground flax. Folded only 2x before  fridge 24 hours. Folded 3x while chilling. I used a sealed tupperware container. No extra flour to work the dough from shaggy to taut. 10 hrs rest post fridge was genius. That made all the difference in the crumb. Cast iron Dutch oven baked.

Totally Amazing. Great crispy crunch with decadent interior. I followed the recipe closely except for last step, turning dough out onto parchment and baking in dutch oven. Will definitely make again. Very easy with great results.

I ended up making this in my dutch oven -- I didn't have a metal bowl, which I realized too late was necessary -- so instead of inverting it onto the cooking sheet, I inverted it into a parchment-lined dutch oven, splashed some water inside the lid and threw it in the oven for the recommended 25 minutes at 450 + 20 minutes at 450 sans lid. Worked a treat.

I followed the recipe and baked it using the Dutch oven. It was perfect! The dough is more elastic than the no knead and I think the break developed a more structured dough. Thanks for sharing this recipe.

Burned (or over crusty) bottoms are a real issue with this the Dutch oven technique, esp with a gas oven.  I suggest a pizza stone/steel/half sheet on the rack below your preferred setup and moving the loaf directly onto the rack for the last half of the uncovered part of baking.

Made per recipe but with AP flour & 100 grams of rye flour added as per tip. Did the stretch & folds in the bowl. This saves a mess on the kitchen counter. Rested in the fridge overnight. Took out and left at cold room temp all day. Then did 1 more S&F on floured counter and placed on parchment in the bowl to proof 2.5 hrs. Slid parchment & bread into preheated Dutch oven, slashed top. Baked at 450 for 25 with the lid on, 15 off. Great flavor and looks beautiful, custardy crumb, chewy crust.

Want to keep your hands clean in Step1? use Artisan Bread with Steve method. Use the handle of a wooden or plastic spoon to mix the ingredients together. Scrape the bowl and the handle of the spoon once to make sure everything is in the loose ball.
Love Steve!  So glad to find someone who gave him some kudos!
Happy to report that it works very well with sourdough too. I added about 1 cup of sourdough starter and a little whole wheat flour. Excellent results. The inverted bowl can be replaced by a plate.

OMG...this recipe turned out amazing.  I followed the instructions that Kenji provided and we got an absolutely delicious boule.

If you have trouble visualizing the written instructions, go out to You Tube and search for Kenji's Low Knead Bread and his video will come up.  You will be able to see exactly how to do it.  BTW...Kenji is a very funny guy as well.  You will see this if you watch the video.

We will be adding this to the regular cooking rotation...it was that good.

As much as the No-Knead recipe was a “gateway” recipe to baking bread, this recipe improves its taste, texture, and shape.  And the dough is easier to handle.

Kenji’s YouTube video clarifies his steps and their rationale.

Helpful tips from commenters:
1. Use the handle of a wooden spoon for the initial mixing.
2. Rice flour on the towel prevents sticking.
3. A parchment paper sling eases transfer. 
4. Bake in a 5-qt preheated cast iron Dutch oven.*

 *Wear oven mitts and mind your smoke alarm!

Tried both the No-knead and this version this week, and got far more volume, more tender crumb, and much nicer crust with this version. Rather than an overturned sheet pan, which warped and compromised the “steam bowl” seam, I flipped the dough onto a pizza stone (mine was light enough) instead this time. But in both versions the crusts got very chewy and tough shortly after they cooled completely (flavor was still amazing despite the slicing difficulty). Any ideas how to prevent this?
Years of trying recipes to find a bread I like - thank you Kenji - this is it!
Delicious and great-looking bread with effective instructions.  Thanks.

I've been making the original Sullivan Street recipe for years. I tried this version and I'm very impressed! The taste is great with a softer, airier crumb, while the crust isn't so severe. It's a bit more work but, I'm sold on this method.

Done this recipe dozens of times. Makes an excellent loaf and great gateway to sourdough baking. 

No need for instant yeast over conventional. Not to question the food god—I haven’t compared results like I’m sure he has but I’ve been super pleased with the loaves from conventional dried yeast. Just saying don’t let it stop you. (Also, no need to proof yeast you know isn’t dead. I use active dry straight out of the freezer.)

Made a lot of no-knead bread during the pandemic (finally broke down and bought a scale-life changing as far as baking goes), mostly decent, but thought the structure and chew was sometimes disappointing, so I decided to try this. Made a huge taste difference to allow a longer fermentation (in the fridge) and to do the turns. The bread came out crusty with a good internal chew. Recommend using parchment rather than towel though!
did the 2 methods, no and low knead and the latter did produce a muhc better bread, lighter, bigger, worth the extra steps IMO

Recipe writing issue. Step 3 has the stretch and fold instructions. Yet the "repeat" is in Step 4 - step 4 only has the "flip the dough over" step. Therefore the recipe reads as if you are supposed to spend 3 hours turning a hunk of dough over and over again. I assume, however, you are supposed to stretch and fold for 3 hours, right? :-)

I thought this was going to be a slightly more involved version of no-knead so I just started on step one. 

It was a great bread recipe that had nothing to do with no-knead and I finished making it out of spite over the course of three (3) days. Love you kenji.

This was wonderful and easy.  I have been making the no knead versions and was starting to get really disappointed because it was lacking in depth.  This made it wonderful.  I did proof it on parchment and cook it in a Dutch oven which made it really easy.  I will make it again, and again.

I usually use the no-knead recipe and am very happy I switched to this. We followed the recipe closely and it definitely paid off, great crust and texture. I might double the salt next time around, but that’s a personal preference.
I make this bread every week, and everyone loves it!  Fantastic recipe.  If I could give it 10 stars, I would.

Full disclosure. I made this in my bread machine. Yeah, I know, sacrilege. I put all the ingredients in yesterday and ran it on the "Dough" cycle, put it in the refrigerator overnight, took it out this morning, let it come to room temperature, and then ran the "Bake Only" cycle. What I got was delicious. Crisp crust, chewy inside. Reminded me of a cut English muffin. And, yes, I will do it again.

I make some kind of bread most weekends.  Is this one better than other 'no/low knead' boules?  I don't know.  They're all good.  
I chose it for Thanksgiving because of the forgiving "it can be in the fridge for three days."  
One thing to consider is the proofing time.  Because my house is at an energy saving 66 degrees in November, it took a lot longer for the final proof.  At 3 hours the poke/spring thing still wasn't there yet. I cooked it anyway and it was good enough.

With all the flipping, inversions, and even mention of a “stiff claw”, this sounds like some weird gymnastics routine.  It’s a lot less complicated and my suggestions:
1. use a flexible silicone spatula (sturdy enough not to break) to mix and fold the dough; hands stay clean and you can scrape the bowl.
2. use a plastic bag to cover the bowl while rising (as a child of 1st generation asian immigrants, we always save all plastic bags.  They’re perfect for this and then we can throw it out.)

Kenji, did you try shaping the dough and then doing the refrigerated proof (as one would with sourdough)? That would allow you to go directly from the fridge to the oven which would greatly improve the oven spring.

Steps 3 and 4 may be written a bit differently than intended.  I assume that when step 4 says "Lift the dough and flip it over… Repeat this every 20 to 40…", what's actually being repeated is folding (step 3) and flipping (step 4).     Do others agree this was the likely intent? (I mean, simply flipping the dough over every 20 minutes would be pointless, right?)
It makes me tired just reading the directions for this "low-knead" bread. Thankfully the stores where I live have plenty of amazing bread.

This made delicious bread, and I like the results much better than the old No Knead recipe.  I also really appreciate not needing to handle a heavy, burning hot dutch oven.  It made an impressive-looking loaf.  Thank you!
This seems waaaay too complicated & time-consuming to me!  But I’ll add the tip of periodically stretching the dough during its slow refrigerator rise to the old recipe.
How can I convert this recipe into a sourdough version?
Turned out beautifully but did bake it in a preheated Dutch oven.

After a year of experimenting with all kinds of breads this goes to the top of my list of successes.  The only change I made is to rest the dough before baking in a linen lined basket that I’ve  even using for sourdough.  Excellent crust and great flavor. Perfect with just a little salted butter.

I make sourdough. So I use the measurements from the NYT Tartine recipe and follow this technique. I use an overturned, preheated Dutch oven on top because I don’t have oven-safe stainless steel bowls. Those folds are a little more effort, but result in a MUCH better loaf. I appreciate Lopez-Alt’s clear descriptions. The Tartine recipe is unintelligible.
One more add for this excellent recipe. I have better results baking on a sheet than in the enamel pot.

I loved this bread!  I made it to spec and followed recipe instructions for adding another type of flour, in this case rye.  I was a little skeptical of the folding but it’s really simple. You do need to be home to do it.  Look at the video- all is revealed!  Better than the no knead which I like very much.
Proof in a Dutch oven.  Since you're not getting the Dutch oven up to 500*, it's easier to proof it there then worry about transferring the prooved dough over to it as the baking vessel.
Not much extra work? I don’t think so!

This recipe is a winner! The steps look long (on account of J. Kenji detailing the thinking behind the steps), but the actual process is very easy, after doing it the first time. As others have noted, it's worth watching the YouTube video.

12-18h resting in the fridge gave the best texture. 

Dutch oven vs overturned sheet pan: the end results are similar, but you're less likely to burn yourself with the sheet pan way. I put parchment paper on the sheet pan and it's easy to lift the loaf off.

I made this bread and it came out beautifully.  I was so disappointed when it had no taste at all.  No yeasty-ness after two days in the frig.  I'm sure I put the salt in, and my rapid rise yeast was in date.  But my bread flour was about a year old. Could that be the problem?  Note:  I used info from Cooks Illustrated on cutting parchment paper so it looks like a 4-bladed ceiling fan and you put the dough on that for rising and moving it back and forth.  Works great.
Great base recipe, but I went with the old parchment - Dutch oven technique rather than the 800 bowls. Beautiful crackly crust, open crumb, delicious flavor.

The critics just read the imposing walls of text and concluded it must be complicated. Go watch the video: the whole flipping and flopping technique is really intuitive; it's also much faster, easier, and involves less cleanup than a Dutch oven.

I have made no knead bread for years.  I never understood this nonsense of putting the dough in a pre heated cast iron casserole or a metal bowl or whatever.  IT IS NOT NECESSARY !  Just bake the dough uncovered on a cookie sheet and spray the hot oven with water a few times in the first couple of minutes of baking.   See Chef John's No Knead Ciabatta recipe for the basic approach.  Dead simple and works every time.
This is an easy no fail recipe. My go to bread recipe for the week. It’s an easy thing to do when home for a couple of hours and take very little tending to.

Fun to try a revised version of this wonderful recipe.  Worked great. A bit better crumb than the original or ATK version, imho. Folding multiple times seemed to help with the structure.  Used my daughter’s banneton proofing basket and gave a nice set of rings around the side of the loaf.  Next time will try a slice across the top and a little dusting of flour just before putting it in the oven for an even cooler look.

I’ve done it both ways now. Several times using the Dutch oven method and this, my first time, using the bowl over the sheet pan method. Right now I can say that I prefer the second method better. For one, it’s easier and safer. The new method produces an airier loaf with more holes that is less dense. I also like the flavor of the new method better. I’m going to try it a few more times and then I’ll know for sure. Both are very easy to make.

This is my yeast bread recipe of choice—it makes a loaf that has the perfect balance of crust, chewy crumb, tangy flavor and not too ginormous. 
It is incredibly forgiving—I have left it mid-fold stage on the counter for hours by accident, forgone the refrigeration, and doubled the yeast and still produced a delicious loaf that is beautiful too. 
I live in a humid climate and have found that you have to dial back the water 1/4-1/2 cup to compensate for saturated flour.

This recipe is delicious, and I think the extra “low-knead” steps makes an appreciable difference from the original no-knead recipe. But after baking two horribly misshapen loaves, I’d caution those trying this recipe to be mindful of the size of your bowl. If you’re worried it’s too small — it probably is. I don’t seem to have the goldilocks bowl that will make this work, I’ll probably repeat the recipe but just go back to cooking in a preheated Dutch oven.
If you have a big domed wok lid, you can spritz it with water on the inside and plunk it over your dough on the baking sheet. Works if your big bowls are not suited to a hot oven.
Can active, fed Sourdough starter be subbed for active dry yeast starter?

@Alice I was wondering the same thing. I guess you’d have to figure out the weight/strength substitution of starter to powdered yeast. And perhaps eliminate the acid? I make a similar dough using starter- also no knead - from the Clever Carrot website.

I made this (using a dutch oven, but otherwise exactly to spec, 24hr cold ferment) and it was the tastiest loaf I've ever made. On the blond side by the time it reached 209-210 degrees internally, and the ear was a little flat, but the flavor and chewiness were incredible.

This "overly fussy" method uses only two bowls in the entire process, and they have low enough thermal mass that you can touch them quite soon after they come out of the oven (compared to that LeCreuset pot that's still burning hot 15 minutes later!).
