Low-mess and low-trash (no paper or plastic to throw away) methods are a nice feature. (The floured-towel is unfortunate, but if you do this a lot, you can just keep it in a ziploc between bakes.)
Instead of fussing with two bowls, why not get a gallon-size container with a lid at the grocery store? I mix everything in the container and snap the cover on. Easy peasy.

I managed to mess up the timing on the folding and proof but it still came out phenomenal with nice crunchy crust, uniform structure inside, and springy inside.  Delicious.  

I followed all the measurements exactly but accidentally fell asleep early and left the dough (covered) on the counter overnight after a few folds, instead of refrigerating.  I gave it another couple rounds of folds when I woke up in the morning, then baked in a Dutch oven at 450 for almost an hour, checking every ten minutes after the first half hour.  My six year old did some of the folding, so between that and accidentally leaving it out, I’d call this recipe pretty forgiving!
Must you use bread flour? Or can you use whole wheat flour?
You need bread flour, it has a higher percentage of gluten. You may be able to mix the flours with some adjustments, but that would require trial and error.
How big is a "medium" or a "large" bowl? For all of the pains-taking fussiness of this López-Alt recipe you would expect a more precise description of the equipment.

I wondered about this, too, and found the answer in his “Updated No-Knead Bread” recipe from 5/4/21:  “You’ll need one metal heatproof bowl (no rubberized bottoms) around 11 to 12 inches in diameter, a second bowl of any material around 8 to 9 inches in diameter, …”

Hope this helps!
With all the inversions and hand techniques, some pictures would be useful.
The recipe author has a Youtube channel (search his name) where he demonstrates the recipe!

First off, I'm a bread baker.  But even after following the (aneurysm-inducing) instructions to the letter, this turned out flat as a pancake.  The original Bittman No-Knead recipe is still the best.  This was unnecessarily complicated for a disappointing result.
Mea culpa...I take back my previous post.  When I finally cut into it the bread was fantastic (albeit "flattish").  Agree with the other poster here, I'm going to try the Dutch oven next time.
Solid overnight loaf. Would welcome suggestions on how to prevent crust from going from crusty to chewy as the loaf cools - I place on a rack but that doesn't seem to have been sufficient here.

Ortolana, I have heard that you can keep it crusty by letting the loaf cool in the oven after you turn it off with the door ajar. Just put the loaf directly on the oven rack. I did this for my first try at this recipe and it stayed nice and crusty once cooled. The crust did soften quite a bit the next day though.
I don't understand why the smooth side of the dough would end up on the bottom, am I misunderstanding the directions? Also, what about slashing the dough? (Maybe these are related...?)
I had the same thoughts, but the seam on the top seemed (haha) to create the allowance for the oven rise.  I might try to slash it this time.  If you did, I'm curious.
Is there any way to make this bread with gluten-free flour?

Wonderful! I found Artisan Bread in 5 mins a Day, which speaks to my chaotic life. That allowed me to bake more bread, esp. since I wanted to share with neighbors during COVID.

This trick of covering the bread with a bowl is the magic the recipe needed! Ice cubes in the oven was good, but this is amazing - when I remove the bowl, there is still steam underneath! I may have to re-slash, but the crust is amazing. 

Rustic is ok, but I use a 7" cake pan for 1 lb. of dough and it makes a nice boule

Yes, I jumped on the pandemic breadmaking bandwagon. Yes, I made many many versions of no-knead bread. Yes, I created triumphphant, disasterous, and mediocre loaves. This recipe produced by far the very best crust, hole structure and flavor. My final challenge: creating a loaf that is round and high, as my loaves tend to spread, even when proofing in a boule. Other than placing the dough in a loaf pan (heaven forbid) I  search for a way to achieve the tall round artisalan loaf. Any suggestions?
A rather tight ball of dough before it goes into the oven, the finger spring test, and a very hot oven at the front end could help

I add a small handful of dried rosemary before the first folds. And I sprinkle course sea salt after I score the top as I'm about to bake in the Dutch oven. I am looking for the best suggestions how to cool the loaf, maintain the freshness, and not lose the crustiness even if it means leaving it overnight on a bread rack
We love in a dry environment but don't want the top to get soggy from the coarse salt.

I have made this three times in the last week. I’ve made the original Jim Lahey recipe at least a dozen times and way prefer this! 
The first two times, I ripped the crust removing the bowl- this partially deflated the loaf. The third time, I increased the initial bake to 30 mins vs 25- bowl came right off! 

This recipe reminds me of the King Arthur cheesy pan pizza, a fave at our house.

Excited to try the loaf variation.

I tried this version and found it annoying compared to the original one. I just put it on parchment and plop it in the hot pan. But I found it interesting that, unless my math is wrong, a cup of flour in the original recipe weighed 129 grams and in the updated one it weighs 150 grams.

I can't figure out the "tall-sided bowl" to invert. I have several mixing bowls but they all fit in each other so different diameters and when inverting none form a "tight seal." I have a couple of dutch ovens, but again, none that that are the same diameter as the mixing bowls so that fit perfectly without one going in the other. Is there a particular brand where one inverted would fit exactly on top of another bowl?

I don't cook/bake, so I have to follow recipes exactly.

Thanks,

Hi there! It's easier when you watch the video. Here is a link to it: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6RUDa0FKplk I watched it a couple of times and when I made the bread the second time it came out like his. I hope this is helpful. His channel has a lot of very good videos and he demonstrates the recipes step by step.

I believe that as mixed, my dough was not as sloppy as was expected. Let it ferment in the fridge for a couple of days and came back to a dough with a lot of gas, which was encouraging. Baked on a metal pizza pan with a large metal mixing bowl inverted. Got one of the best crusts ever but loaf was weirdly dimpled in the center.
1 g instant yeast = 3 g frest yeast

I guess my stainless steel bowl wasn't big enough-- the bread stuck to one half when I tried to remove the bowl for the final browning in the oven, and I had to use a knife to cut it free, hampered of course, by my oven mitts. 

The unstuck half does look very nice, but in future, I'm sticking with the Dutch oven approach.
This recipe worked perfectly for me as a novice. I was amazed by the results. Now I’ll try it with sourdough. Any tips?

I made this this weekend.  Forgot the acid in the water.  Otherwise made as directed.  I floured the thin cotton towel REALLY well.  The dough still stuck to it.  I think I'll go back to proofing just in the bowl next time, but the loaf still came out great. Not sure if an improvement over the dutch oven, but no worse.  The folding just seemed like folding that other recipes suggest.  No time waste or big deal and did give a good rise.

I've made both this and the no-knead recipe and I found that the extra water in this recipe made working the dough a lot more difficult, my first time I just ended up taking the dough out and kneading it normally, adding enough flour to make it workable, and in successive times I've just used the proportions in the no knead recipe. It came out fine that way but I'm not super picky about the end results so I get if people are invested in the recipe as written.

the size of the necessary bowls should be included here. some readers may not know that they need to revert back to the no-knead recipe for the bowl sizes. this recipe, as written, only works with an 11-12 inch metal bowl which, i venture to say, not everyone has...

Step nine says to "splash some water into the inside of the larger metal bowl."  Metal bowl?  Who said anything about a metal bowl?  Then entire recipe up until now just says to use a "large bowl" and a "medium bowl".

This bread comes out amazing every time and is very forgiving. I'm new to bread making (re: pandemic hobbies...), but have found it to be just enough effort to get the joy out of baking while also being flexible. This recipe has won the adoration of all who eat it, and got my sis-in-law making her own bread too! I like the way the low-knead version comes out a little more than the no-knead. I use a bread pot (basically a small dutch oven) rather than the bowl idea.

Big Kenji fan here, had to try this. Watched the video first as other readers recommended-worth doing. Great project for a gloomy weekend. Followed directions. To avoid buying new gear, used a big Pyrex bowl and plastic wrap for steps 1-4; placed dough smooth side up on parchment paper in covered bowl for steps 6-7 to proof; then lifted into preheated dutch oven for steps 8-9. Did not flip upside down, instead slashed the top. A perfect well-risen loaf--crisp crust, delicious. 2 thumbs up!
Not as much flavor as the original
Loved this bread.  Love the Bittman version, too.  Both work.  Similar results.  And sometimes playing with your bread is part of the enjoyment.

I can’t compare it to the original recipe as a baker because I have never made the original recipe.  I have tasted it though, and this seems comparable.  My family liked it very much, and I found it fairly simple to make.  I don’t understand the strange comment about flipping and flopping; shaping the loaf is basically the same way I shape any boule.  I recommend this recipe to anyone, seasoned baker or novice.

Made just as recipe/video recommended.  No deviations. I am not a great baker, but I follow instructions well.  Hits all the senses--perfectly lovely, browned crust; cracklin' as it cools, warmly aromatic, and so sweet with butter and jam.  It doesn't get much better.
Very good. Not sure about all the palaver with cutting boards and upside-down bowls. Cling film is the way to go!
I believe it's to demonstrate that you don't need a dutch oven to bake the bread in. Not everyone can afford/wants to invest in a big, heavy pot

Ignore all the guff. Mix 5 mins with dough hook. Stretch in bowl as described. Leave 3 days in fridge in a bowl with cling film. Turn out into pot and shape. Prove 3 hours. Bake 250C until browned to your liking, about 50 mins in cold pot. Job done. Increase amounts by 1.56 to het a proper sized 800g loaf.

In a high humidity climate I find that I always need to use a little more flour (3c) and less water (1c). And in the tropics we can get away with same day baking, no overnight rise, with a little more yeast (1 tsp).

I made the Rye version as written except that I used about 20 grams of whey in place of some of the water.  I make yogurt and have a lot of whey and find it gives a nice tang to bread recipes. 
I make a lot of no knead breads and this one is not terrible, because it's bread, but it is probably the worst.  It's very flat with a dense chewy crumb.   
I need to experiment more.  I think it needs a lot more liquid and maybe the yeast has to be activated in the liquid before adding to the flour.

500g flour(250g bread flour)
10g kosher salt
heaping 1/2t instant yeast
~375-400g warm water

500g flour(250g bread flour)
10g kosher salt
generous 1/2t instant yeast
375-400g warm water
Is there a reason why saran wrap is not recommended for covering the bowl? Moisture, breathing considerations?
@Jackie waste.  Plastic lasts hundreds of years. You only use it once. The bowl can do its job for many lifetimes

Iffy recipe. Sometimes it's great but others there are lots of problems. 
The dough loves to stick to my towel no matter how floured it is, which knocks out any of the small lift I've ever gotten out of the final rise. 
My yeast is still very alive and active and I have the standard blue and white tea towels.
Are you weighing your ingredients? Subbing AP for bread flour? You dough might be over-hydrated as a result.
@TeaShirt This is 2 years 2 late but worth a shot. Try using rice flour on the towel (if you’re not already). It doesn’t get absorbed by the dough like wheat flour does
A lot of work. Probably worth it.
The best thing to cover bowls of rising bread is a transparent plastic disposable shower cap with elastic, sold in big packs for use when dyeing hair. Can be washed and re-used a few times.

I have been trying to sort out baking hype from reality.
Seems like we're letting the flour "fermentolyze," building gluten strength, doing a long ferment and a longer proof than I've seen elsewhere.
I appreciate the attempt to make something work without fancy equipment.
But:  
No need to score the top before baking?  If there's enough moisture in the baking vessel the oven spring is OK?
I mean, I know this is a Saint Kenji recipe and should work fine.....
The flavor and crust on this method was outstanding! I highly recommend it. Worth the time.

Step 4 says “repeat THIS  every 20-40 minutes”. Repeat WHAT????  “This” cannot mean “this step” as that would make no sense.  Does the author mean, “Repeat step 3 and the first part of this step 4 (folding, flipping and covering the dough) every 20-40 minutes . . . . ” ???
I have made this recipe many times (white and whole wheat) and it works like a charm. Only change is to use a Dutch oven (preheated).
Would temps and times be the same if I use my ceramic cloche?
I think this version produces a much tastier version than the straight no-knead, with satisfying "big" holes and a chewy crust.  Also, the bowl and sheet pan are 100% easier to work with.
@Katherine this was exactly what I was curious about!

This is so easy and good--I've been making it a couple times a week. But why the extremely complex instructions? I mix the ingredients in a bowl that fits the fridge, use my index finger to mix water into flour, and clean the finger with a knife. The bowl goes right into the refrigerator, covered with a pot lid, till it's time to take out and proof. Then I plop the dough into a banaton, let rise, dump into a presoaked clay baker, and bake. One bowl to wash, and terrific bread every time.
I use the opaque plastic liners used by cereal manufacturers to pound the cutlet.  It holds up better than parchment or plastic wrap.

My grandpa worked for a food company, used to bring home damaged rolls of cereal/cracker box waxed paper liner material. Grandma swore by them, great for pounding meat & for rolling out pie crusts too. Thicker & sturdier than regular waxed paper. Grandma taught me to collect those waxed paper liners out of cereal boxes, one of many useful tips she passed on to me. Great tip, thanks for bringing it to folks' attention. I've noticed though that lately they've been making them thinner... :(
Be sure to squeeze the lemon wedges on to the schnitzel just before eating.  Schnitzel without lemons is, objectively, not good.

Schnitzel with lemon & a sprinkle of salt is the best.  Growing up with a German/Austrian mother, pork schnitzel was a regular meal in our house.  It is also delicious cold, with lemon, for a picnic or light snack.  Do not leave out the lemon!

Agree with John G. - don't ever use processed lard found in large American supermarkets. I've found the best place to get excellent lard is any good Mexican grocer - one's that make their own carnitas - they sell their own rendered lard in tubs for a few bucks.

I have got to admit: Vienna Schnitzel is the REAL schnitzel. I prepare it as often as I have time and with guests whom I know enjoy it. But I'd not previously heard of the Vodka step. It works! Outstanding!!! There is a good option for the 'clinging' blanket and a fuller meal for those hungry and in need of lotsa carbs etc. I do them with chicken breasts accompanied with a substantially saucy potato gratin [somewhat overly creamy] occasionally infused with small pieces of broccoli. WOW!

Sir, I commend you. I agree, Vienna Schnitzel is the (only) REAL Schnitzel ;-)

And not having previously heard of the Vodka step myself, I shall try it soon.

@Robert E. Gahan 
Sounds amazing. You’ve inspired me to try this. Thank you!

This was a lot of sturm und drang.  2 cups of flour to dredge 4 cutlets?  5 rimmed baking sheets?  After the dredging, egging, and breadcrumbing my fingers were like mittens. Each step had to be inspected but there are no remedies for when the cutlet doesn't pass inspection.

Also, I don't store large quantities of used fat until it smells fishy.

I don't get the overwrought recipes that J. Kenji Lopez-Alt comes up with.  Good food is simple and accessible.
Then don't make Kenji's recipes. The rest of us will make his recipes... and we'll think about you and what you're missing.
Kenji must have an enormous kitchen. Set 4 rimmed baking sheets in a row next to your stove? Who has counters this size???

@AM  these are 1/4 size baking sheets measuring 9x13.  Very useful and inexpensive.  Buy 4 or more at a restaurant supply store (Volrath/wear-ever 5220).  You will use them much more than the larger baking sheets.  A half sheet of parchment paper fits perfectly as do the stainless steel wire racks that you wisely bought at the same time.  One of the most versatile pans in the arsenal.  Great as plates for a cookout, holding pastas like ravioli to dry before cooking (criss-crossing the pans lets you stack them many high in a small area), 3-part dredge stations, holding mise-en-place, bringing food to/from the grill, endless uses…  The only small drawback is that they are not dishwasher safe as they are aluminum but cleanup is easy.

First of all real schnitzel is Veal not Pork! If you put them in the frying fat and keep pouring the hot fat over it with a spoon you'll get just the puffy crust of a Viennese Schnitzel as served by Figlmüller at Wollzeile. Serve with potato salad and lamb's lettuce. If you cook in oil ad at least some butter to the mix for taste and discard after max 8 Schnitzel. Don't wait for "too dark or fishy" as described above. A German Riesling or an Austrian Heurigen makes it a complete experience.

"Real" schnitzel can be any meat. This isn't a recipe for wiener schnitzel, which is always veal, but a general recipe for schnitzel done in the Viennese style, which is more commonly done with pork than veal even in Austria. You'll find Viennese pork schnitzel on every menu that serves schnitzel in Austria.

Compliments from a Viennese! This is the real thing, down to serving it with cranberry sauce, a lemon wedge (not just for decoration), some Viennese potato salad on the side and NOTHING else! I'm impressed.
As another Viennese, I agree wholeheartedly! I am impressed as well.

Surely you jest!  Store bought bread crumbs?  I regularly buy bread from an Italian bakery and when near the end of the loaf, save it and let it dry out.  Save the ends in a zip lock until enough is there to run in the food processor.  These crumbs are kept in a glass jar and never spoil.  This of course is because this type of bread has nothing to spoil, only flour water yeast and salt.  And about this quart of fat?   1/2" oil in a 12" stainless pan, cook until the edges start to brown, flip.

Another tip for a "puffy" wienerschnitzel is to add a tablespoon or two of cream to the egg mixture...in the past I have found this to give me a good soufflieren effect that reminds me of what I used to get when I lived in Vienna.
After years of having heavily breaded fingertips, I have found that using kitchen tongs is a lot less messy.
@Don This! As I read the instructions I kept wondering “Do you have no tongs?!”.

I pound the meat with my wooden rolling pin. My "authentic" (so I thought anyway) Viennese recipe calls for a bit of nutmeg in the egg & parmesan cheese too. Really wouldn't dispense with the nutmeg. 
I fry in olive oil + butter--not nearly so much & only rarely do the layers separate--but the flavor is very good & there's much less waste. I'd caution everyone against many minutes "warming" in a 225º oven.

If you're a baker and appreciate your equipment, I would caution against using a rolling pin to pound everything in your kitchen that needs flattening or crushing.  Wooden mallets and metal mallets were made for this purpose and are available everywhere.  Save the rolling pin for pastry and cutout cookies.
I also prefer pork tenderloin as a substitute for veal.
This worked spectacularly, but I had the best results at a cooler oil temp, around 350.  The puff was dramatic and fun.   Hope you have a kitchen cleanup crew, because this makes a serious mess.

I once worked as a flavor chemist for an international food conglomerate. I studied the oxidation of fats and oils and the by-products that formed.  Saving frying oil and reusing it is folly and bad for your health.  You can make fried foods with a bound breading in a skillet with a little oil and butter.  Discard any grease left at the end.
Read the recipe first, before buying ingredients. About halfway through it throws about seven kitchen gadgets at you that you might not have known to have ready, or might not even own.
Store-bought lard? The awful processed stuff that should be avoided? I use freshly rendered lard from butcher or farmers market. But 2 pounds of this precious ingredient would be a shame to use.

I love the vodka idea. I had never heard that before. My Slovenian ancestors always blended some sour cream into the beaten egg for breading. That helps with the puffy crust as well. We also "fry'" the schnitzel in about a 1/2-1 inch of oil in a large baking pan in the oven at 400 degrees. This avoids oil splatter around the stovetop and allows for more constant temperature.

Will have to try the vodka and pounding the meat thinner. My family loves chicken schnitzel. I make mine Danish style after a restaurant I frequented in Winnipeg, Manitoba. I highly recommend their potato salad: 

4 cup cold boiled potatoes, finely diced

1 large Kosher-style dill pickle, finely diced

4 Tbsp mayonnaise

½ cup green onions, finely sliced

½ tsp salt

½ tsp pepper

Add some fresh parsley or dill too!

Schnitzel is any meat cutlet NOT veal cutlet.   Wiener Schnitzel translates as "Viennese cutlet" and yes traditionally made from veal.  The Vienna Restaurant Figlmüller at Wollzeile, since 1905 and considered 'Home of the Schnitzel'  house special is  Schnitzel vom Schwein (Pig).  They don't even have veal on their menu as per the menu on the website.  Pork, veal....it's alllll goooood!
Tofu?
Does anyone also find this recipe hilariously prescriptive?! I feel like it should tell me how I should feel during every step.
1-2 eggs is plenty for 4 chops!!!!

I prepared my meat ahead of time and placed them on a parchment lined baking sheet and then cooked them all at once. I had fantastic results and the process was not as messy or stressful as the method instructions.
Hungarians like (love) their schnitzel with mashed potatoes and cucumber salad, and of course a squeeze of lemon!

Living in Vienna, I was most impressed with this recipe because, in the accompanying article, Kenji hit many high spots ... among them the video of the Schnitzel preparation at Meissl & Schadn.

Having researched this myself, I knew every part of this recipe, except for the idea to add Vodka (or even water) for more consistent puffing ... and having learned this additional point is really great. I will try it soon!

I used panko breadcrumbs but pounded them until very fine in a plastic zip bag which worked well. Also used Gin instead of vodka and corn oil instead of lard. I achieved a good amount of puff on each piece even though my flour dusting was a bit heavy handed in places. This recipe was the closest I’ve ever got to authentic schnitzel eaten in Austria.
Could I use chicken, instead?

This recipe is legit. I’m honestly amazed at how good it came out. Crust was perfect and puffy, and meat was flavorful and moist. Add some nutmeg and paprika to the flour. Don’t worry about being so fussy with it.

Make sure to blend the blend the breadcrumbs in a food processor until they are super fine and run them through a sieve after to catch any that didn’t get fine. The breadcrumbs in the US are much coarser than the Semmelbrösel used in Austria. Doing this step allows the bread to bind together better and puff more when cooking.
My Danish mother-in-law would place an anchovy on top before serving, of course with a lemon wedge.

The schnitzel was quite tasty, I chose to use canola oil, am now in the process of filtering the cooled oil as suggested, using a coffee filter and funnel into a glass container. One should note that  this is an painfully slow process, - like over an hour- though the captured oil is indeed quite clear. Very glad I am doing this in the sink - as it would have made a mess of my kitchen counters.

Was anyone able to reuse the oil? The breadcrumbs sunk to the bottom, and I was unable to skim them out. After frying 6 cutlets, cooled, and filtered, the oil was spent. Way too dark to reuse. So not even a second fry - let alone "several dozens [of] fries."

Wonderful and 'authentic' Austrian Schnitzel! Finally a coating that looks and tastes just like the fantastic restaurant made Schnitzels.
thank you!
P.S. give us the sides recipes as well!

under the advice of butchers at bunzel’s meat market, milwaukee, wi, i now ask my butchers to shave the pork loin in the meat slicer. this saves me from having to pound it, and if the pork is high quality, renders a tender schnitzel while not destroying its texture and taste. don’t waste time and energy pulverizing an already tender cut !
Years ago Kenji published instructions for how to clean cooking oil with gelatin over at  Serious Eats. Works like a charm and saves alot of money.
Best schnitzel I’ve had.  Made exactly as written. The vodka wash worked wonderfully.

This is utterly ridiculous.  What you are cooking is simply breaded pork chops.  I have made breaded chicken cutlets and breaded pork chops many times over the course of my life.  Never have I gone thru such elaborate steps.

Susan, I agree 100%!  My husband is from Munich and we lived there for many years - I've made a lot of schnitzels....veal, pork and chicken....and, like you, have never gone through such involved and time-consuming steps as outlined in this recipe!
@Marcia DittmaierThe only fun and new thing I like is the vodka. Should have told us to put it in the freezer so our shot would be cold.
I have made schnitzel both with center-cut boneless loin chops and with pork tenderloin; I prefer the tenderloin. Is there a reason why the chef prefers the loin chops?

Don’t forget the spaetzle! Marian Burros has the easiest I’ve seen on NYT. Just mix flour, water, eggs and salt/pepper and drop spoonfuls in boiling salted water, then drain/drench in butter. This is how my German mom made them. Milk/water only for thinning batter as needed, and IMO, no need to sautée or fry after as per some of the the fancier recipes here.

Hello! Austrian here. I must say I’ve never heard of spätzle as a side to schnitzel. We mainly have them with more sauce heavy dishes or simply with cheese and fried onions. Over here classic sides for Viennese schnitzel would be potato salad (vinegar, oil, beef broth, onions, no mayo), buttered potatoes with parsley or fries. Maybe Germans do it differently?

@Carrot 🥕 
As the child of Swiss-German parents, we've always had schnitzel served with homemade spatzli, as well as a vinagrette laced green salad on the side (and lemon wedges to spritz). I'm drooling now just thinking about it 😋
For a lighter version, or if pork isn’t your thing, you can use turkey breast instead. It’s delicious & always offered as an alternative in southern Austrian restaurants.

I used clarified butter, which of course turned quite brown after the second frying.  I think next time I'll try lard from the butcher.  I made this as the recipe instructed and got only minimal puffing - I was hoping for more of that effect.  But it was still absolutely delicious!

This is a very good recipe, we have made it several times. The other night we did not have any vodka. So instead we used bourbon. I cannot say with 100% certainty that is what gave it an even nicer flavor but it was as nice, perhaps better.

I always season the meat before I bread. I use garlic salt and a little poultry seasoning. This way the seasoning stays in the meat rather than falling off into the pan if the breading separates. Also, the seasoning is more concentrated...cooking without garlic...sad.

To serve, we use applesauce on top of the schnitzels, kluerse and rot Kohl.
Lingonberry jam is a must, squeezed lemon is a sin.
Here’s a tip if you have a taco press - line with a zip lock bag or parchment and use the press to flatten your little cutlets. Easier and faster than pounding or pressing with a skillet.
Incredible! So simple and sort of magical in how much the coating puffs up. Kenji, you’re our hero!

 Would this work using whey protein isolate in place of the flour and making bread crumbs out of homemade bread made from vital wheat protein, oat fiber, and golden flaxseed meal?

This worked very well! 1.5x the recipe (everything except the lard). On my first/test schnitzel, I let it rest for a minute and then tried it and there seemed to be a bit of a pasty thing going on between the breading and the meat so I cooked the rest slightly longer and then put them in the oven and by the time I served them, they were perfect. Wow.  Heat up to 375+ and two minutes in total. Will have to try that.  Somewhere along the line and a French chefs ideas,  I tried to keep the heat at 325 and cook until "browned." When you use pork these are called an Iowa Pork Sandwich.
I use a tender using mallet so they are easy to eat.
Dip in f!our, egg and then crushed saltine crackers.
Just fry in vegetable oil.
When my son went on a European trip with friends over Christmas break and he ordered schnitzel in Germany. He was surprised when it came he said My Mother makes these!

Loved the recipe but 30 minutes start to finish is really hard to stomach for anyone who doesn’t work in the industry. Definitely much more a 60 minute and making the bread crumbs was 30ish minutes on its own in addition.
I always pound my meat with wax parchment!

@luxy 
Really? Prescriptive?!? TBH I was thinking about how very nice it was for those who've not made schnitzel before to have full instructions, and tips (vodka! who knew?) for those of us who have. Relax.  No need to be so judgemental... Thx Kenji as always 😘

Following the tip, I did use plain crumbs because making my own seemed like too much trouble. And I used half neutral oil and half tallow because it’s really expensive right now. Otherwise, I followed exactly. It was amazing. I was worried it would be greasy but it was not…at all! Do not skip the lemon and jam. That takes it from nice to DANG. I’ve committed to try more global foods by making a classic dish from wherever that weekend’s Formula One race is being held. This with the warm potato salad (erdapfelsalat) represented the Austrian Grand Prix well!

Everyone in my house of 4 conservative diners gave this a thumbs up. The recipe author is a master of technique - he reminds me of Jacques Pepin. Careful attention to technique will reward you; not exact adherence to the recipe. Use what you have. I had leftover tinga chicken and the old jar of sauerkraut. Thank you Mr. Lopez-Alt for expanding our family's world food vocabulary!

I've just made a version of these for lunch and they were SUPERB!  No Kimchee or Sauerkraut in my fridge, so I marinated raw hipsi cabbage and chopped spring onions in a  mix of garlic/chili sauce, gochaguang, and rice wine vinegar for an hour. I made the batter with GF Flour as that has rice flour in it. One of our supermarkets here sells "Stir Fry Oil" which is flavoured with ginger and sesame and that is what I used to cook. I can see myself making all kinds of variations on these. Thank you!
I've been making a lot of these lately too -- I use sourdough discard in the batter, for even more help cleaning out the fridge....

How did you know I had kraut and two scallions left in my fridge this morning? Delicious, made it as written but with pickled jalapeños. Can’t wait to try variations—this is right up my improv style in the kitchen and my salted-pickled taste preference.

I was perusing the NYT in my PJ's when I read this recipe.  I realized I had some left-over kim-chi in the fridge that had lost it's appeal as a stand-alone.  I chopped up the kim-chi, added a few sliced fresh jalapenos and used 3/4 c flour (all purpose), 1/4 c corn starch, 3/4 c club soda, fried in a non-stick pan coated with veg oil and VIOLA!  Delicious!  This looked and tasted so authentic and complex, yet took all of 2 minutes to prepare.  This will be in the regular brunch rotation!

Great recipe! Much better than Korean pancake mix from the store. Having made this twice now, I'd cut the amount of add-ins. It gets stodgy and gummy with too many add-ins. Thin and crispy is the way to go. Sauerkraut and ham was a nice combo.

Made these with what I had on hand: replaced the sauerkraut with 1/2 spicy fermented garlic kohlrabi and 1/2 cabbage kimchi, and used chickpea flour instead of AP. The kohlrabi was an impulse buy a while ago but I couldn't figure out what to do with it. I know now! These were absolutely delicious.
Why is the sugar needed in the batter?
It also helps to make them crispy.

A video or something would be nice. This was a disaster for me. My “pancakes” were way to thick...too much chunky to batter ratio, maybe? Anyway I couldn’t spread them thin enough even though the batter part was very liquid-y, and they ended up gummy and undercooked tasty, and basically a messy heap. The flavor was good but how do I make these into the thin pretty pancake I see above? A major fail. Next time I’ll find a you tube recipe for this and try that.
Mine were also a disaster. "Messy heap" describes them well.
Had big time issues with sticking.  Anyone else?  I liked the flavor, though. Will try again.

I really liked the idea of this but wasn't sold on the results. I think I might have made them too thick. The texture was just unpleasent to me. The dough was very soft and chewy and the sauerkraut and peppers and red onion didn't go along with that.

What an interesting, delicious way to use up sauerkraut.  My batter was slightly thick so I had a tough time getting my cakes thin and crispy enough but they were delicious!  I should have thinned it with a bit more water.  Next time...

Great use for leftover sauerkraut. Skipped the red onion (but used the scallions); added a small carrot, slivered; finely diced jalapeño (instead of the cherry peppers); about a tablespoon fresh ginger. Mixed the batter in a large measuring cup, and used half for each 10-in pancake. Had to cook each side longer than recipe says to get them done and brown enough. Can think of many variations, depending on what’s on hand.
I had to add a lot more liquid to get the batter to be thin enough to flow around the bowl when tilting. Did anyone else experience this?  Also gummy - I think I over worked it. Thanks

Delicious. Followed the recipe exactly except added more water to get the right consistency. I liked using peanut oil for frying. (If you’re shopping for non stick, I can highly recommend ScanPan. A bit expensive but they last for years. Always great results.)
This was easy and delicious as promised, but didn't make enough for our hungry 4. I'll double it next time. Added julienned carrots.
I need to work on technique as it turned out a little gummy, making one giant pancake probably didn’t help as it was too thick. It was still delicious though.

Made with zucchini and carrot option.  Delicious and made a nice and unexpected side to our black-eyed peas.  I grated the zucchini and carrot and let this combo drain for an hour or so.  Not much juice until I squeezed it.
Could I somehow use sourdough discard to make this?

So good!! Two hungry adults polished ours off. We used beet kraut, white onion and pepperoncini. The dipping sauce— yum! My only tip is yes, make sure you spread out using the back of your spoon or ladle.

Really enjoyed the zucchini and carrot version and the recipe was a great use of veggie leftovers. I made smaller pancakes and served with kimchi fried rice, and it was a super easy mid week dinner. Also really appreciated that the batter was thin. I added extra scallions because I enjoy the flavor. Loved the sauce and will be reusing for dumplings!
Awesome, I doubled the recipe and it created a hearty dinner, left the sugar out of the sauce. Will make again

Hmm... Didn't work for me. Very crispy edges, but otherwise strange texture and not flavourful. I did the shitake, cabbage, kimchi version. Prefer my usual okonomiyaki and dipping sauce for this type of preparation/vibe.
These were easy and tasty. I never knew the trick about adding cornstarch to flour to make things crispy. The amount of liquid was not enough so I added water.

Put these in the air fryer on 400 for a few minutes after pan frying. It dried them out and crisped them up perfectly. 

I also served with sour cream and it was reminiscent of a latke. Would definitely recommend!

Easy to make and delicious. I've made them 4-5 times and had better results when I've either increased the amount of vegetables or slightly reduced the amount of flour. Otherwise, the pancakes tend to be thicker and heavier than I prefer. Next time I'll make more than 2 pancakes and spread them thinner, which should also help too.

This recipe is both excellent and pretty darned easy. It's my first experience making this or, frankly, any Korean food, and it turned out beautifully. I put into the batter leftovers of kraut and some steamed broccoli, added the onions & scallion as well as some minced garlic. Fantastic! Will make again, over and over - and who knows what will go into the next batch? Thanks!
I skipped the onion and pickled peppers and used homemade sauerkraut (unspiced or flavored, just cabbage and salt) pickle juice and fresh fennel leaves. Turned out great!

Thought this was great! I wonder if some people who said the texture was too thick missed the 3/4 c of water? My second pancake was much better than the first, I figured out a little better how to flip it and I think my pan was better seasoned. Didn't have any pickled peppers so left them out. I did kraut and mushrooms. Yum!
This is phenomenal, especially if you make the Kimchi version -- with or without the mushrooms and cabbage.  Use what you have!  I've used kimchi, carrots and lots of scallions.  Zucchini also works.
Made the recipe as written other than adding finely chopped giardinara instead of pickled peppers (as I didn't have any).  I loved the flavor but the texture was gummy.  Any ideas?  Did I overmix?
Is there a substitute for sauerkraut itself?  Can I use a can of  bean sprouts instead?

Look at the variations! This is really a template for a pancake that can be varied in infinite ways, and it's ideal for using up odd bits of leftover veg. As Kenji exemplifies in the variations, just be sure that whatever vegetable you use is more or less as cooked as you want it to be before you add it to the batter, since it won't have much time to cook afterwards.
Could I use Gluten Free all purpose flour for this?
When I make the dipping sauce I use Korean chili flakes.
I’ve been told that in Korea they’re called, “Psssst, psssst, psssst,” because that’s how they sound in the pan.

I just whipped these up for a quick lunch (for two adults), and they were superb. Substituted sauerkraut for Wild Brine Kimchi, and some thinly sliced jalapeño for the pepperoncini, and still had a delicious result. Definitely going to be making these again, maybe with further ingredient experimentation!
Such a great recipe! Excited to try other variations. I used some beet-kraut, white kimchi, and my own kimchi with a little ginger (I’ve been on a fermenting binge lately). So good!

01/13/21 - made this with sourdough starter discard, TJ’s sauerkraut, a bit of baking powder, and red onion. Measured nothing. First one was too think and soft (like batter) in the center. I made the second one thinner and it was crispy and delicious. Will try again actually using the right measurements!

I've tried the other Pajeon recipe on the NYT website (link below) and I've had on and off success making it gluten free. Has anyone else played around with GF flours? Have you decided on one? I'm terrible about writing down what I've tried and what has worked. https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1020550-vegetable-pajeon-korean-scallion-pancakes-with-vegetables
I tried to make this using gluten-free flour. Didn't really work. Maybe I need to use another kind of flour like almond or coconut? Any advice?

I had good results using Cup4Cup and Bob's Red Mill Potato Starch in the zucchini/carrot version.  (Batter was a little thick, but next time I'll just add more water or carrot/zucchini water.  I don't think it was due to the flour.)

I've made these twice with various ingredients and they were delicious. They were crisp around the edges but soft in the middle, so you couldn't pick up a piece to eat it. Is this the way they're supposed to be? Or did I not cook them long enough?

How do you put "two 10-inch pancakes or three 8-inch pancakes" into "an 8- to 10-inch nonstick skillet"?
Or what did I misread?
I think they mean 1 at a time and whichever size pan you have ;)
How I read if:  If you use a 10" skillet you should have enough batter for 2 pancakes.  If you use an 8" skillet, then 3 pancakes.  See step 5.

THIS WAS AMAZING!!!! Hubby said he felt like we just had dinner at a restaurant. Even my super picky son had and enjoyed a few bites.
WILL MAKE AGAIN.
Just yum!!
Loved it.  One of my best friends is Korean and she made this for me years ago and knows every time I am at her house it is our first appetizer.

Also for an easy add in use pre-packaged coleslaw (the ones with carrots and cabbage). Make sure it's the coleslaw that is grated really thin.  Some brands have thicker pieces.  U can grate just about anything and add it to batter.
Very yummy, and a reminder of the utility of a non-stick pan (must go buy one today).
I’ve made these over and over and over again. Simple, cheap and delicious.
Has anyone tried this gluten free? With a rice flour or something else?

Was excited to try this last night. Thought I followed recipe instructions faithfully-esp. in how much to stir the batter--but ended up with a browned, gooey product instead of the crispy pancake I was looking for. Not sure I'll be trying again anytime soon, but am still interested in what went wrong. Any thoughts?

Try the potato starch, it's a distinctive texture when cooked.  There should be very little batter in the Western sense, the main ingredients in jeon are just barely covered, not swimming in batter.  Start with half the cold water until you mix everything with the dry ingredients first, only add more water if you need it.  Kimchi and sauerkraut should have enough juice already.   This makes for a proper nicely chewy and crispy jeon.   And try adding more oil around the edges after flipping.

OK. My very first comment after many years of subscribing to NYT cooking.

Sorry to disagree with most, but this was AWFUL. I followed the recipe, substituting pickled green beans for pickled peppers. 

My advice, leave sauerkraut alone, vegitarians! My mouth hurt after eating these. Not what I'm after. Sauerkraut needs to mellow for hours with some kind of pork (bacon, kielbasa, other sausage).  Don't try to make it a veggie dish. It is meant to compliment a meat dish. Happy to debate this.
disagree
@nope Username checks out.
Delectable! Sauerkraut and veggies that were in the fridge. Love that this has no eggs! And that it tasted yummy
Though they were very tasty, as so many people said, they were also terribly greasy.  What did I do wrong?
I made this gluten free by using Bob’s 1 to 1 gf flour mix. I thought it was tasty and crisped nicely. However I’ve never eaten it before so I can’t compare to the gluten filled original.

I wish this had worked for me, but it was kind of a disaster - albeit a tasty one. The large pancakes fell apart (and stuck to the pan) and when I tried to make smaller, thicker ones, they were gummy. I hadn’t overworked the flour, though I maybe did add too many veggies (1 head bokchoy) and I was using kimchi rather than sauerkraut.
Way too much oil! I guess it depends on the pan you are using but cheez!

I overworked the batter and, yes, the end result was dense and gummy, but the flavor was good. So, next time maybe I will quickly mix the liquids and flours together and then gently fold in the veggies? Seemed hard to not overwork the batter and get the veggies incorporated properly otherwise.
I used savoy cabbage and found that after it was salted and rinsed it was so reduced that it seemed more like a condiment than a salad. Not sure what I did wrong.
Korean / Czech household here.  The pancakes were great, because we omitted the unnecessary sugar and starch. We used pickled red onion with jalapeño. Delicious.
Not bad but I think I will stick to bratwurst and sauerkraut in the future

Our sauerkraut was not particularly “juicy” so I compensated with extra water, and it still came out on the thicker side. I wonder if there is a difference in water absorption between starches? We used cornstarch. The resulting pancakes were also more crispy/browned than the image, but what’s not to like about that. I also added a generous amount of gochugaru for personal taste but otherwise made it to the word of the recipe. I lined a plate with a napkin to absorb some excess oils for those concerned about greasiness.
Gochujang is not a sub for doubanjiang. Gochujang is more pasty and much milder and sweeter, doubanjiang is salty and maybe more akin to doenjang (fermented soybean paste) with spicy chilies.

I used oxtail and skipped the gelatin since oxtail is fairly gelatinous already. I cooked it in the instant pot - sauté function for searing meat & veg & aromatics, add liquid & set 45min at high pressure and 20minute natural release. 

I also clean (aka blanch/boil) the bone-in meat and skim off the scum, then rinse in cold water & pat dry before searing. It’s a chinese method for making broth based soups & cleans the bones of impurities for cleaner flavor & a clear broth.
any idea how long cooking this might take in an instant pot?

The common conversion for regular to pressure/instant pot cooking time is usually to reduce by about two thirds. In this case that works out to around 40-50 minutes converted from the 2-2.5 hours mentioned in step 7. Amy+Jacky at pressurecookerecipes use 42 minutes plus 30 minutes natural release for their recipe. I haven't tried their beef noodle recipe yet but have had success with other recipes from their site. Woks of Life had 100 minutes for their's, which I did try but it was way too long.

I've done this several times only in an IP (8qt DUO) utilizing Amy + Jacky's & Woks of Life recipes. I've used combination of beef shank, oxtail, short rib, and chuck roast in the the IP. High Pressure 35-45 minutes with 30 min Natural Release is all you need for tender meat when submerged in the braising liquid. So if you don't want to think about it, just do HP 40 minutes + 30 minutes Natural Release. It is going to be delicious regardless of meat type from the Doubanjiang + spices!
Chinese cooks add 1 tablespoon of cooking oil to the pot of boiling water, in order to preserve the bright green color of Chinese broccoli, baby book choi, etc.

Not authentic in both spices and cooking technique.  Tomatoes are fine in a beef soup but don't belong with the noodles. Bay leaves appear in some regional Chinese cuisines but not Taiwanese.  Five spices are used for braised beef served thinly sliced by themselves, but unusual for the noodle soup. Vinegar is a resounding no.  Meat is not seared but parboiled and rinsed to get rid of the lumpy scummy protein.  One can call this Chinese with an asterisk, but not Taiwanese.

Beef should be blanched and rinsed, but not seared, in Chinese/Taiwanese cooking. Doubanjiang, on the other hand, needs to be cooked briefly over high heat with ginger and scallion before any liquid is added to deepen the flavor. Star anise pods and optionally Sichuan peppercorns are all the spices this soup needs. Five-spice blend is for an entirely different braised beef dish.  Adding it to a broth with doubanjiang simply muddles the flavor.

I know this is a super weird idea but could I make this vegetarian with oyster mushrooms, shiitake mushrooms, and agar-agar? Serve with noodles and tofu? It's a different soup entirely but I love the idea of a vegetarian version and wonder what others think my choices would be.

This recipe is a very meat forward recipe. when I learned how to make Taiwanese beef noodle soup, I was amazed to learn that it did not use beef stock but bean paste and aromatics for the main flavor. I think this would be great w mushrooms (maybe try rehydrating dried mushrooms in a soy sauce, vinegar, sugar mix to really add umami meatiness), (fried) tofu, bok choy and tomato. Using agar has not caught on to me yet but I understand how it could add texture to the soup. maybe i will try too

I made this the day before the recipe was published (a slightly different recipe but not that different) and put in fresh shiitake, which were delicious, as well as bok choy and baby sweet corn. You'd need to make a very sturdy broth, though, as this is the kind you can basically stand a spoon in.
We cooked the soup in the oven covered in a Dutch oven at 275 for 4  hours as we escaped to a wine bar. All of the meat fell off the bone before serving! Loved this.
I found the soup a little too sweet so I would cut back on the brown sugar and taste until it was the sweetness level you want. This was also much better the third day.

This recipe, quite a project, sounds marvelous.

Before we begin, however, the pictured ribs are not English-cut (also called cross-cut and flanken). They are back ribs. Please clarify as they are not necessarily interchangeable.

It's all in the eye of the beholder, of course, but I think these look exactly like garden variety meaty Short Ribs and nothing like back ribs, which, if typical grocery store BRs have a paltry amount of meat and are mostly bone. Further, the Flanken Ribs I usually come across are also like Short Ribs, but have a thinner cross-section and less meat.

Finally, the price of Beef Back Ribs are chump change compared to Short Ribs . . .usually half the price
Pretty sure those are English-cut short ribs pictured, as the recipe calls for-- that, is cut parallel to the bone.

Made this so we could have a meal in one bowl that would warm us as we ate - daughter and her wife came and sat on the porch while we opened door and sat inside. This was delicious - used fresh fettuccine noodles and cooked lots of bok choi - and our local sauerkraut on top- broth was delicious. Everyone enjoyed it and with a good Beaujolais it was a perfect dinner. Thank you!!

Check out Alison Roman's short ribs, which I made last night. Almost identical when all is said and done, but without the doubanjiang and multiple asian aromatics or the additional liquid that turns it into a soup. For anyone who may be tiring of the doubanjiang/sechuan peppercorn approach to everything (which I heartily endorse), you might want to try Alison's recipe, then add 4-6 cups of water or broth and you have a "plain-old" beef noodle soup that is still an umami bomb.

"Almost identical ... but without the doubanjiang and multiple asian aromatics or the additional liquid that turns it into a soup." 

So...not identical at all, yeah?
... what are you talking about.

Big head slap. I made this tonight and was looking at the comments and came across this one. Said to myself, right, almost identical...NOT. Then saw that I WAS THE COMMENTER. Couldn't stop laughing. Forgive me, AJ (really, I meant to focus on the "if you've been eating too many Sichuan/Doubanjiang recipes lately" give Alison Roman's short rib recipe a shot").
I made this with venison shanks (we live in Montana) and substituted riesling for the Shaoxing and it was one of the most delicious soup bases I have ever made!
Can you substitute oxtail for the short ribs?

I will try this soup again next time but put all the spices in a bouquet. While you can strain the broth, you may lose some of the delicious beef strands which have fallen off due to the longer cook time.
I use miso paste + chili garlic sauce or miso + spicy chilis as a sub for tobanjan. I don't use it enough to buy it, frankly, and the sub tastes good to me!

This is very similar in taste to my Taiwanese mom’s beef noodle soup except I never bothered to ask her how to make it so I’m so glad Kenji jotted it all down for me.  I subbed the 3rd tablespoon of dark soy with regular soy as I’ve over salted a few things in my day.  I also tend to salt everything as I go so all that dark soy scared me. I added some carrot chunks at the end just like my mom does. I would definitely make this again...comfort food from my childhood.
Locating gelatin in New York during a pandemic is proving impossible.
Loved this but watch the ribs—mine took much less time to be ready. The overnight rest was important!

OMG!   Made this for the Polar vortex chill.  So complex and the broth sticks to the noodles!  I used bone broth which made the broth thick and delicious.   Worth the wait to simmer for hours.  A definite favourite - Thanks Kenji!

Chantal, that makes a good vegetarian soup but improves with “darkening” ingredients like steeping water from dried mushrooms. I’m just vegie leaning so I used beef broth to get around the high level of animal fat.
I lived in Taiwan for acoustic of years. I never heard or ate beef noodle soup with doubangjiang. Good grief!

This not a quick meal - it is a wonderful weekend project well worth the effort.  Use all the proper spices and ingredients they are widely available.  The resulting broth is gold!  Leftover broth used in a few other dishes during the week.   Fantastic!

This was so so so good. I had made the Taiwanese beef soup from other recipe, and this was superior. Kanji’s recipes must be followed exactly, as he is a very technical cook. But when you do, it is a magic. I did everything according to the recipe except that I used kimchi as a topping and I thought it was brilliant. I suppose you would have the similar satisfaction with your kimchi topping, which can be quickly rinsed for the recommended effect for the fermented vegetable toppings.
Great idea about kimchi. Much easier to get than the preserved Chinese mustard greens).
Beef shank like Osso bucco are often cheap and available where I live  so that’s what I had used. Didn’t need gelatin as the marrow and all the fat provided enough.
We lived in Taiwan for 2 years and the Beef Noodle Soup is so good.  It was served with a side of greens, chilies, and oil.  Short ribs are very expensive though, any substitutes?

I find beef neck is a super substitute and far less expensive - choose meaty ones, they take a bit longer to cook but the gnarly collagen in them will enrich any broth - rinse in warm water to rid them of bone chips from the butcher's saw and pat dry. Just pick the meat from the bones when you serve it up.

Used cubed chuck roast (2.85lbs) but otherwise followed recipe as written. Chuck needed less water than short-ribs to cover the meat, so only added about 0.5 qts water before braising. Removed meat, strained solids. Divided beef among three 1-qt deli containers, each held about 10oz of cooked beef. Poured stained broth over meat to allow to cool over night but the combo mixture only came to about two-thirds of a quart volume. I topped each container off with a little tap water.

Sometimes I wonder which food trends will seem silly in 20 years. Sheet pan dinners? No way, they’re great! Szechuan’s moment, with ma la and fermented bean paste everything? I hope not! But, “you could find veal bones, beef tendons, etc., ...or just add a packet of gelatin to some stock and it’s the same!” could stop. And maybe the noble short rib was underutilized in western cuisine slow braises.... but when can we go back to acknowledging that it’s also super fatty and persnickety?

Interesting and attractive recipe. As a person living in East Asia, where beef is at a premium and our locale boasts a variety of pork that is nonpareil, I want to try this using pork ribs chopped short with a cleaver, Asian style, rather than beef. All else as written pending modification on the fly for the different meat or lessons learned in the doing.
I just did 3.5# short ribs in instant pot for 50 min and natural pressure release. Came out lovely and tender
Could Gochujang be considered as a substitute for Doubanjiang ?

Unfortunately, I don't think so. Gochujang is spicy and honestly, kind of sweet to my taste buds. It's basically a sweetened, thickened pepper paste.

Doubanjiang can be spicy but necessarily so, and adds a fermented, salty funk more than spice, and definitely not sweetness. It gets its funk from fermented black beans, of which gochujang has none.

Personally I wouldn't substitute either for the other, but if you absolutely had to, I'd pare down the sugar if you do use gochujang.
Could kimchi be used instead of the pickled cabbage or sauerkraut?

It's just a topping, so I you do you, but this dish is traditionally not spiced with hot chilies, so it will change the flavor profile. I think it is worth at least getting saurkraut for the acidic but non-spicy zing to cut through the fattiness of the broth.

I made this the other night using my pressure cooker--not an Instant Pot but equivalent--I used short ribs and well-marbled chuck roast. I used Knorr chicken broth powder with tomato instead of regular chicken broth. I cooked it for 1 hour on high pressure and the chuck was falling apart but the shortribs weren't as gelatinous as I'd have wanted. Next time I will cook longer with only the ribs. The soup was delicious even though I forgot the sugar. I will definitely keep this recipe for winter!
You can also use tauchu or Thai fermented soy beans for the stock.
So, are there other ways to achieve the gelatinous gravy described?
yes, you can use beef tendon when making the soup.

This recipe is amazing, sweet, savory, multiple flavor profiles, meat came off the bone when it came out of the Dutch Oven! Highly recommend this dish. Lots of prep work but it's worth it in the end result. The aromatics made the entire house have a wonderful bouquet.
I was lacking gelatin, fennel seeds and time to refrigerate for a day. Still great!
Insanely delicious!

Made this as written, and my guests were crazy about it and lingered over the broth all evening instead of looking for dessert. I used short ribs from our Asian grocery store that were cut in long slabs with small bones in each piece, and that made for a beautiful aesthetic. Will make this again, next time for one of our most discerning chef friends. It's that good.
I used the instant pot for 40 minutes with the meat setting - next time, I'd probably do 35 because 40 was too long. I'd keep at least half of the fat - taking away too much took away the flavor

This was incredible. And very easy. Obviously not a fast recipe. But most of the time is completely unattended. You don’t even need to peel the ginger or garlic! I’ve made it twice (full spice mix both times); once exactly as directed, and once a vegan version with fried tofu, veggie stock and some potato starch for thickening and MSG for unctuousness. Both were quite good, though the beef version was the winner, surprising no one, not even the vegans who only got to smell it.

I used two mods:

1) I added 3 or 4 whole cloves since that's how I am used to making a very similar Chinese braising liquid and also cloves pair very well with beef  -- I was actually surprised the recipe didn't call for them.

2) I didn't have gelatine so substituted chicken bone broth which has gelatine for the plain chicken broth and it worked quite well.

The broth is divine!  I've made this twice.   Don't omit any spices!!! They all blend to make the flavor incredible.  Best on day 2 or day 3. I added sautéed shiitake on day two.  Best combo of veggies...bok choy, Chinese broccoli and a little carrot for color! I rarely eat beef (only once or twice a year), so I was thinking that I would made it vegetarian...But, this dish needs to be made with beef!!  Brought back childhood memories.

I have been doing the Woks of Life version with minor tweaks and it is amazing; I told them that the 100 minutes was a mistake but they seemed to disagree -- needs to be 45 minutes max in the IP. The broth is fantastic and my kids think it is the best thing ever. Reminds me of Taiwan, among the very best food memories. I add star anise and bay leaves and also use the spice packet: Lu bao.

Once familiar with the ingredients, not that hard to make. But the road there is a challenging one. Hard to procure ingredients with difficult names. My closest market (I now know) is a Korean/Japanese market - not a Chinese market. Amazon helped, but nonassociated sellers send some stuff quickly while other things are still out there (somewhere) a month later. So Chinatown it was - and that is a daunting trip. Next time however this will be easy and there will be a next time.

Made several times. Wonderful. All ingredients are available in my markets and I have made the recipe as given. My Asian market also has beef shank and I like it better than short ribs for this with no other changes. As others have said, this is a project the first time and I have since worked out some efficiencies like a spice basket/strainer. Never a thought about not using all the spices because the broth is great.
One of my favorite recipes ever. This is what I make when I'm looking to impress guests with something they may not have had before.
This could be one of the best things I've ever tasted!  The only substitute I made was using Black Bean Garlic Sauce instead of the doubanjiang  and pickled ginger instead of the sauerkraut.

This was amazing... followed recipe to a t... made fresh egg noodles ( with a duck egg)
savory, satisfying, subtle spice that can be increased at serving... used cabbage and kale and topped with peanuts for crunch...  thanks kenji!

35 minutes at high pressure in the Instapot worked for me. I used 3 lbs of back ribs. The real key to the recipe is letting the broth sit over night in the fridge so you can remove the fat. I also agree with the  note about using doubanjiang instead of Gochujang.

I made this following the recipe precisely and am well pleased. Used a three year-aged doubanjiang from Pixian County in Sichuan Province. The aroma was very pronounced and intoxicating while cooking but not detectable when eating. I think the recipe makes soup for eight people rather than four, unless they are sumo wrestlers in training.

Great recipe; for me I needed to simmer the short rib closer to 3.5-4h to get the desired level of tenderness. I've also added tripe (needs much less time, maybe 45 mins) and stewing beef (can be cook for the same amount of time as short rib) for extra protein and variety. Will try to add tendon next time.
So delicious. This soup is in the regular rotation now. Yes, it is a project, but your house smells amazing as it cooks!
Too many finicky ingredients.
This tastes nothing like beef noodle soup. Hard skip on my end.
do you have a more authentic recipe Terrsa? I'd like to try it@Teresa

title: Hot and Numbing Stir-Fried New Potatoes Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

comments.commentBody: As someone living in HK, this is delightful! Just improvised a smashed potato version. First boil new potatoes in salted water with baking soda. Post smashing and letting them dry out, coat them in hot oil flavored w/ minced garlic, star anise, green & red sichuan peppercorn, dried chillies, saving the fried aromatics for later. Post crisping the smashed potatoes in hot oven, combine with fried aromatics, cumin powder, more salt and fresh spring onion. Use some of Kenji's roast potato hacks!
Curious what living in Hell's Kitchen has to do with the recipe being delightful -- I suspect it's equally delightful on upper East side, or even Cucamonga for that matter...
I had to read twice to get by "post smashing" LOL.
Jeff, I am pretty sure HK refers to Hong Kong, not Hell's Kitchen

The spice mixture is addictive. It reminded us of chili crisp. The instructions to separate the spices into two bowls seem overly complicated. After reading them more than once and trying to follow what to mix with what, we decided to just throw everything on the potatoes. They came out excellent!
Hate for the first comment to be so deviant but i made it for lunch without the potatoes (sorry), and can confirm the spice mix goes well on a stir-fry of leftover veg + lamb.
I bet it would be great with cauliflower, too.
I thought the same thing, I did use potatoes and thought the spice blend was worth redoing for really anything I feel like!

As an oceanographer, I don't regularly taste seawater, but I do measure its salinity. A very salty ocean, such as the Red Sea, contains about 40 grams of salt per kilogram of seawater. If you weigh the salt it doesn't matter whether you use kosher salt or table salt (or any other flake size). One possible conversion to volume: use 3 Tbsp. of Morton kosher salt per 1 liter of water.
@SJW - this is the best comment I’ve read in NYT cooking so far: thank you for your excellence, exactitude and helpfulness
If you need a few laughs, goggle "salted water for boiling" and click on the Epicurious link.
Thank you, Philip. I needed the laugh.

@Philip turns out, this recipe is incomplete. I just learned that, after many decades of boiling water successfully,  I should have been waiting till the water boils, before adding the salt. Adding it to cold water apparently runs the risk of pitting my stainless cookware. Someone at Epicurious needs to update the recipe.

OK, for everyone who's confused by "water as salty as seawater": it means very salty, saltier than you think it should be. It's not a literal measurement, but more of an encouragement: go ahead and make the water even saltier. This is a useful shorthand, because people will use very different amounts of water (though I understand why some cooks would prefer exact amounts). Salty water enhances flavor, and doesn't make the final product too salty!

The potatoes are good with just salt and pepper, but some minced fresh rosemary and chopped garlic puts them at home on a traditional Thanksgiving table.

It was in the article...
@Todd not sure those flavors exactly scream Hong Kong night market street food…

YUM! We aren't really into frying things and I don't own a wok or anything similar to a wok; so I changed up the recipe. I diced up the potatoes; tossed them in olive oil; then sprinkled "quite a lot" of the spice mixture #1 on them; and then roasted the dish at 425 for 45 minutes. & finally cranked it up to 450 to crisp them for 10 minutes. Then I sprinkled the spice #2 on everything. It tasted great & I'll definitely make it again. Also want to try the spice mix on steak!

For people complaining that they don't know how the salty the sea is: I think you're taking it too literally. Just salt the water fairly strong, so it tastes more or less like what you imagine salty seawater would/could taste like. A few palmfuls of salt, give or take, would be fine. It's hard to mess this one up unless you drastically over- or undersalt the water.
I make a version that is flavorful but not hot.  Salt, pepper, and then a very generous shower of seeds: caraway and fennel are my favorites.

Two pounds of potatoes would have been too much for the poor wok, my wife and I used half that much.
I followed the directions for the spice blend, and I ended up with a small bowl full of craziness.
I used just a three finger pinch for the potatoes and that was enough for me, and I love ma la food. It was too much for my wife, and she spent the night coughing and sneezing.
Don't get me wrong, I enjoyed it. It is a good recipe. But my goodness, I've never made a spice blend like that...
Sounds great, but it seems a bit precious to grind the spices in two batches...

That is just if you have one of those spice grinder things with the whirling blades. If you have a mortar and pestle, just toast everything except for one teaspoon of cumin seeds, then dump it all into the mortar, but pick out the arbols and put them back into the wok, and toast them some more. Make sure you get all sides. Dump them back into the mortar and give everything a good bash. Grind it all up. Toast that last teaspoon of cumin seeds, then pour them into the mortar, and crack them...

it's for texture/pops of flavor. it's really not a big deal to finely grind some and coarsely grind the rest, especially as you aren't switching equipment or cleaning in between. if you're using a spice grinder it's barely a few extra seconds. i'm sure it wouldn't ruin the dish if you couldn't spare it though!

Crazy me, I saw the recipe and looked over it briefly.  I didn't look closely at the "form" of the spices such as the star anise.  So, I adapted the recipe to use what I did have on hand.  I used anise seed, out of cumin seeds, fennel, 6 small Thai peppers (I like it hot), and black peppercorns.  I thought it needed just a bit more flavor, so I added about a rounded 1/2 tsp. of turmeric to my mortar.  Like I said, had to use what was on hand.  It was excellent.

Amazingly, the Epicurious link is still active in Sept 2023.

Seriously, for not just a few but many, and clever, laughs, goggle “salted water for boiling" and click on the Epicurious link.

A few of the bestest ones:
Use tears to enhance the flavor
Is there a vegan version?
Do I add the salt to the water, or vice versa?
Make a double batch and freeze any leftovers
@Shari Mauthner - after you seconding the Epicurious link, it did not disappoint. This is the best part of the internet for those that love cooking.
I think “See Tips” refers to the Ingredient Substitutions Guide. Scroll to the end to find Herbs and then Spices.

I simplified. I toasted all the seeds together. I used black pepper corns. I left out the chicken bouillon. I cooked the potatoes in the oil as directed, then added all the seeds etc together, stirred and tossed and then couldn’t stop eating it. New favorite. Yum!!!!!

Love this dish. I do it a little differently in that I boil, smash, and roast the potatoes, then stir fry them. I also make a large batch of the spice mix and keep it in the freezer. Everyone I serve it to loves this dish.
Where are the Tips?
I can't find the tips. Help, please.
I'm guessing this could be made with regular potatoes cubed to about 3/4 inch.
That would sure save some serious money.  Small potatoes are sure expensive.  I would go with red ones, they hold together and don't get starchy.
Do you peel the potatoes?
Nope just clean them. I did not bother scrubbing them.

What the heck does "very salty seawater" taste like? Ok, duh more salty than regular seawater. Thanks. I actually can't remember what seawater tastes like, other than "salty". I know this is a cool trendy way to describe how to salt your cooking water... probably very helpful when Noni's were teaching the tots at their apron hems to cook... but, along with "a slow oven" and other charming vagaries, it's fun but not helpful. Would it kill ya to say "about 3T per qt of water"?

It varies a lot based on what type of salt you use and the exact quantity of water. Also, the point is that it doesn't actually matter that much. Pour however much water is needed to cover the potatoes, dump a few palmfuls of salt into the water, taste it, and adjust as needed (which will be probably be unnecessary after you've done this once or twice). Quick and effective, and no need to measure everything.
Just not worth the time and prep. Also, potatoes shouldn’t be “hot and numbing.” There are quicker and easier pathways to better-tasting potato dishes.

Whoa, was not aware of the 'should and shouldn't' list for potatoes.
So relieved we all get to choose our own pathways.

Oh my my these are good!
We were all set to make them when we discovered that the little packet we thought contained Sichuan peppers actually had Bali Long Peppers. Never mind, we used them anyway. Some trouble with the spice mixture sticking to the potatoes - too much oil in the pan? Next time I might put the potatoes, garlic & scallions in a bowl and the sprinkle and toss the splice mixture then. Or use less oil. As it was we scooped the spice onto our plates & dragged the potatoes through.
Thanksgiving version is boring. It had to be said.
I  plan to up garlic.
These are amazing ! Had the incredible experience enjoying them in the Muslim Market street in Xían China . If you make it to Xían the food stall lined streets are a must experience!

RE: the question of salting water - Seawater is 3.5% sodium chloride by weight, or 3.5 gm/100 ml water. I tsp of table salt is 5.7 gm; 1 tsp Diamond Kosher salt is about 3 gm (because it's flaky, it takes up more space).

Wonderful flavor. Use the freshest Sz. Pepper you can find, or the numbing effect will be minimal. Chop scallions finer or they'll fall off. Use less oil to cook the potatoes, and probably less to dampen the spice mix, or they'll be so oily that the spice mix slides off. I'd say "mix all spices but 1t cumin and grind to consistency of sugar; then add last 1t cumin and grind a tiny bit more until the cumin is broken up." The powder-fine stuff seemed hard to get onto the potatoes.
Hasn't any reader or writer or the NYT ever heard of something called a microwave?  Possibly could be used effectively rather than boiling water.

Sea water is about 35 parts per thousand - 35 grams per kilogram. A kilogram of water is a liter. Its salty! While you wouldn't measure your water and salt every time, it would be worth doing it once to get an idea of what you are aiming for.
This is the recipe of the day here in Vermont for…December 6th. Not sure where I’d find new potatoes this time of year but will try to remember this for when early summer comes around.
@Danny V here in Oregon all the standard grocery stores sell baby potatoes year round, I would assume they do there too! The Little Potato Company is a popular brand.
@Danny V I agree, it’s weird to promote out of season foods imo. Or roasting recipes in August lol I’ve seen that one too
Is a pod of star anise the whole star or just one limb of the star?
Hi Genie, it would be the whole star from what I've read.
It's the whole star.
@Genie - the "Pod" typically refers to the whole star. (Star anise is a relatively non-potent spice, so one limb won't do much.)
I don't have a wok; is there anything else I can use to make this dish?
Any deep pan with sloping walls. I use https://www.all-clad.com/d3-stainless-3-ply-bonded-cookware-dutch-oven-5-5-quart.html

I made a smashed potato version last night which was delicious.  I added the potatoes (and smashed them with the bottom of a glass) to a roasting pan halfway through roasting a butterflied chicken.  I drizzled the potatoes with a bit of chili oil, and then put the spice/garlic/scallion mix on everything once it was done.
I used a steel walled skillet that gets MIGHTY hot!

The spice mixture is addictive. It reminded us of chili crisp. The instructions to separate the spices into two bowls seem overly complicated. After reading them more than once and trying to follow what to mix with what, we decided to just throw everything on the potatoes. They came out excellent!

I didn't use all of the oil and regretted it somewhat. While the potatoes weren't dry, as we all know, potatoes love them some fat and they deserve it!   
 The spices come together well, though I did use half the cumin. It made for a really healthy weeknight main with sauteed pea shoots, and I'd make it again.
Is powdered bouillon required or could I use the paste better than bouillon I keep on hand? I assume the ingredient list for both products is the same?
The cube turns into a powder when ground, which is the point; the paste would not.

From all that I've read and tasted, the red Sichuan peppercorns are more floral in taste profile. It's the green Sichuan peppercorns that provide that numbing effect most satisfactorily. Go with green.
They are both quite numbing. However the red does have a floral fragrance and the green's aroma is reminiscent of citrus peel.

When making the Chinese version be sure to have your extractor fan on high prior to adding the spice mix to the cooked garlic.  I did not and was hit by a wall of spice vapors.   Some of the ground spice must vaporize..

While these were really good, this falls into the category of stif-fries that I will NEVER make again indoors. We used half of the cooking oil, but the house still reeks of peanut oil, after running a full-size fan exhausting out the kitchen door and opening the front door to draw the air through for an hour. It's not worth it! Is it just because we have one of those microwave fans over the stovetop? I don't know, but no more oily stir fries for me :-(

> Is it just because we have one of those microwave fans over the stovetop?

Probably.

Microwave fans start at about 150CFM.  A real "approved for Asian households" hood vent goes from 500 CFM straight to heaven.
These were a huge hit at Thanksgiving, the spice profile was a perfect contrast to the heavier dishes.

40 minutes and 15 ingredients doesn’t exactly qualify as a simplified method for potatoes, as written in the Times article. Sounds more like a promotion for the wok book. Think I’ll stick with mashed.

Wouldn't change a thing. So good I eat it cold straight out of the fridge. Had spice mix leftover (just added to the potatoes to taste).  Made with cut pieces of regular potatoes (to maybe ping-pong sized wedges) and worked just fine. Just be careful to watch the potatoes or you'll have Sichuan mashed potatoes!
Can someone explain what "new" potatoes are? Are they labeled thusly in the grocery store?
To obtain the true sea-saltness boil the potatoes with samphire.

Made these for a party in SF yesterday.  They were a huge hit, including among some picky eaters.  Used 2 pounds of potatoes from "The Little Potato company".  I didn't have a wok - used a deep iron skillet and it still worked great.  I don't think I've ever gotten as many complements on something I took to a party.  Will make more of the spices and try it with other foods like cabbage, cauliflower, shrimp, maybe even popcorn!

@Finny i’m replying to my own post… I’ve made this two more times since that party I posted about and have been getting raves from everyone. I think this is going to be my new favorite thing to take anywhere and make any time.!  This recipe makes my New York Times subscription worth it
