If you don't want to use chicken bouillon (my wife is vegetarian) use some Chinese mushroom seasoning powder and maybe some MSG.

I grew up in a Cuban household and ate delicious ropa vieja on a regular basis. My mom never added olives and raisins to ropa vieja, that was reserved for picadillo. I've seen this before in different recipes and I don't know if they are an American melding of the two dishes of if my mother's recipe was just more straightforward.

Hi, Maria: As the daughter of a Cuban mother, I spent time in my childhood in Havana with my Cuban grandparents. This is an American take on "Ropa Vieja". The classic recipe does not have olives and raisins, as you say.

In our Cuban household we enjoyed two meals from the one cut of meat.  My mom would boil the beef first so that it could be shredded. The stock would later be used for soup.  For Ropa Vieja she would sauté onions, peppers, couple of mashed garlic cloves and then add a can of tomato sauce.  Salt, bay leaf, dry white wine or vinegar would then be added. The shredded beef would be added and simmered for about 20 minutes.  Never raisens, capers, olives, or chicken stock. She garnished with pimentos.
This is how I was taught!

I and four others who I served it to were underwhelmed by this dish. It was a bit sweet and lacking much complexity for a dish of this nature. Frankly, we would have been better off just marinating the flank steaks or covering the, with a dry rub, grilling them and then serving it with the peppers and onions, black beans and maybe a pico de gallo.

Again, not bad, but not the best use of these ingredients and time.

@victor p. Or you could find a ropa vieja or pulled beef recipe that you liked. Carne asada isn’t really an alternative to ropa vieja, they’re very different and come from different cultures altogether. And actually, traditionally, the beef is supposed to be poached first and then shredded, like pulled pork, not braised.
@Sai this is the second comment where boiling or braising was brought up for the meat and then shredding it. Clearly this is a technique I haven’t learned. Thank you for the suggestion.

Being a Cuban of a certain age I have been eating Ropa Vieja all my life. Like other Cuban commenters I must point out that this Cuban standard does not include olives, raisins or capers. Those ingredients are part of Picadillo, another Cuban standard recipe. BTW plenty of minced garlic should be included with the sautéed onions and peppers. I have seen this mistake before. Just offering my comment for clarification.

I’m a Cuban born and I’ve never heard of adding raisins and olives to ropa vieja. These were reserved for the Picadillo . Also I’ve never heard of using the flank raw for this dish. Usually the flank steak is boiled with an onion and after it’s cooked and cooled it is shredded. Starting a Sofrito with onions, peppers and garlic then add the shredded beef until it is well mixed with the onions, etc. add the tomato sauce, wine, salt, pepper, thyme, oregano and bay leaves and cook for 1/2 hour.

I was disappointed by this; it was too sweet for my liking as a beef dish (it borders on "dessert" sweetness).  Also, considering how long it simmers, cheaper cuts of beef should be used.  If I made it again I would cut out the raisins entirely, and go with whatever cut of beef was on sale at the market that day.

I nearly didn’t follow the link to the Braised Peppers & Onions RECIPE thinking it would only be peppers and onions! Thankfully I did not skip over it because this is where all the seasonings are for Ropa Vieja! This makes the difference between bland and yummy!

This is also a great way to cook a turkey leg.

Made a few alterations - now it's one of our favorite comfort foods. Used a 2.5 to 3 pound bone-in pork shoulder (easy find at Whole Foods, about $12). Toasted the cumin, then cooked the onion/pepper ingredients in the same dutch oven the meat was browned in. Returned the pork to the dutch oven with all other ingredients, covered and simmered for 3 hours, turning the meat once. Pork falls off the bone it's so tender. Served with both braised greens and rice. Not traditional but it's delicious!

So a lot of varying opinions on this recipe. Personally found it to be great. Fixed a double batch of this for Christmas to go meals for the family (Covid style meal). It cooked for about 5 hours in a cast iron dutch oven in the oven at 250 degrees. Used flank steak and was incredibly tender and very flavorful. Used the braised peppers and onions recipe, a must. Also refrigerated it for 4 days. Dead on about improving taste and texture as mentioned in description. No raisins. Thanks for this.

Just cooked and devoured.  This dish is amazing. My wife’s favorite special at our neighborhood taco shop. Nuvotaco is awesome, but she admitted this recipe is better!!  

Thank you Kenji!  You continue to inspire me and make me look good!

This was a delightful twist on traditional ropa vieja.  I was short on time so I put everything in the Instant Pot for 30 minutes (high pressure) after searing the beef and preparing the peppers.  It was delicious!  Highly recommend.

In all the years I've eaten ropa vieja, I've NEVER seen anyone make it with raisins!!! I swear, it's like once mainstream outlets get a hold of something and want to "make it palatable for the masses" you guys think raisins is the secret ingredient to everything. People, ropa vieja does not have raisins in it, at least when the people within the cultures that created that recipe make it.  If you want to cook raisins with your savory meat, whatever, but don't pass it off as ropa vieja.
My husband is Cuban olives and raisins do not belong in ropa vieja let alone capers.  The recipe seems to have forgotten cumin, paprika and Oregano.

We have a lot of Cuban restaurants in Tampa, and this recipe looked more like the picadillo than the ropa vieja we usually see here. That said, it was a stellar recipe.  I did not find it overly sweet or bland at all, but rather wonderfully complex, rich and completely delicious. Like most dishes with complex sauces, it was even better the next day. I used sirloin flap steak, which worked great, but will definitely try it with ground beef next time.

I'm Cuban born and will add to the chorus. Raisins, capers and olives are found in picadillo not ropa vieja. Vitally missing- a generous amount (at least 1/2 cup ) of a good dry or amontillado sherry.

This dish was wonderful.  Although it took a long time to prepare, it was well worth the effort.  For the two of us, it will easily provide three hearty meals.  We had it tucked in flour tortillas with a bit of cheese this evening and a side of beans.  Yum.  Leftovers will be over rice and then later in tortillas, again.  I made exactly as written (including the recipe for Braised Peppers and Onions).

This recipe is not the one most Cuban families are use to cooking.  The meat is boiled first with onions and a little salt, so you end up with very soft meat and a delicious meat stock.  My mother would take the stock and make “Sopa de Platanos”, Plantain soup.  The meat would be left to cool down so it could be shredded and some lemon added. The meat then would be cook with green peppers, onions, garlic, olive oil and olives.  The meat was served with white rice and black bean and/or tostones.

Sorry to say, this is not the original recipe for Ropa Vieja, it's close, however some of the ingredients on here don't belong in this recipe. Olives, capers, raisins, chicken stock, and crushed tomatoes. 

My mother being Cuban, made this tropical dish with white rice, sweet plantains and avocado slices, of course we loved it every time. I will share with you my mom's recipe and take a picture of it. You will always make it and enjoy it.  Be well!
Did you post your mom's recipe?   I'm cuban and would love to be able to make it.  Just learning how to cook at 55 years old, but just thinking of this makes my mouth water.  lol.  ty.
Leaving out the raisins was a good idea
EASY RECIPE!  While it does take 3 hours to make, very little of this is working time.  Appearance notwithstanding, this delicious dinner takes rather little effort.
I am also from a cuban household and I agree- no raisins or olives in this dish- only in picadillo. It would make it sweet and that is not the taste of ropa vieja.
Ropa vieja does not take olives, capers and raisins. You are confusing ropa vieja with picadillo.

While not traditional, I thought the golden raisins added something interesting when combined with a tbl or two of Sherry Vinegar (did not go full picadillo by also using the olives and capers). Also used smashed garlic cloves in the onions/peppers base to prevent risk of burning while softening everything else. Turned a supermarket "London Broil" into something much more.
In light of the comment about picadillo, would this work with ground beef?  The flank steak of yesteryear, which was a bargain cut, has become luxury food.

I'm just an American who happens to love making 'ropa'.   The recipe I've used, from a Cuban friend, doesn't include raisins or olives, but always flank steak. It's a wonderful dish for a crowd, and can be served with rice + beans, or tortillas and fajita-type fixings, and can easily be spiced up with hot peppers as desired. It freezes beautifully too.

This will be the kind of comment other people will make fun of- and that’s ok! I used the peppers and onions to deglaze the pan I had browned the meat in- I figured they would braise enough along with the stew. Realized I was out of cumin! Added taco seasoning instead, then canned chopped tomatoes with peppers, red wine, bay leaves and 2 spoons of apple cider vinegar, since I wanted it tangy. Came out delish!

I can’t speak to the authenticity of this dish, but thought it was one of the best new recipes I’ve made in a long time. It had the right balance of sweet and sour. I chose not to shred to meat and ate it like a beef stew. To add a bit more texture, I topped it with toasted panko. Everyone loved it and I’ll be adding it to my regular rotation for hearty winter stews.
Can you braise the peppers and onions  in Step 2?

In Venezuela, it's "Carne Mechada" (I think desmechada is the Colombian spelling.) I've never seen it with olives and raisins in Venezuela or Colombia, but this could certainly vary by region or household.
I agree with Maria O. I learned how to make ropa vieja from my Spanish mother-in-law who grew up in Cuba...no raisins and capers. She used brisket and it’s boiled to make soup first.
Why no sofrito?

I am Puerto Rican, so I will not claim to be a ropa vieja expert, but we make it back home too. We never serve it with greens. You serve it with rice, black beans and fried ripe plantains. I also wouldn't cook the mojo (onions, peppers, garlic mixture) separetely. I brown the meat which we would seaosn with adobo (not the Phillipines Adobo, it is a dry rub made with salt, pepper, garlic powder, cumin, a little but of tumeric, etc). This recipe seems a little bland.
The Cubans I know make ropa vieja using previously cooked pot-roast. ¿Verdad?

This is a small variant of the traditional Spanish interplay between salty and sweet that results from mixing manzanilla olives, salt, vinegar, and raisins. For those who claim that it is too sweet, just reduce the amount of raisins half. I used to follow the traditional approach of boiling the beef and then shredding it by hand, before sauteeing it. However, this old method hardens the meat, while braising it, like it is shown in this recipe, results in a more tender beef and better flavor.

Out of this world delicious.  I made this as written except doubled the recipe to make for two families.  Both families loved it, adults and kids!  Next time I'll cook even more so we have leftovers.  I was home so I just cooked it on low all day long, my house smelled amazing and the meat was incredibly tender.

Not to 100% traditional, but good enough for a weeknight meal. Omit the raisins and capers (no Cuban would include these in ropa vieja!). I added some olives as a substitute for additional salt and to add some color and braised 2.5#'s of flank on simmer for 3 hours (could have gone an additional 30mins for more tender meat).
My family loved it and adding some red wine adds a nice complexity to the flavors.  You could use any beef that can be shredded easily

This is picadillo with flank steak, if you were to make this with ground beef and start with a onion, garlic, green herb base then add the meat, olives, capers, raisins etc you would have a delicious picadillo.

After reading the comments of some others, I have to say that I think authenticity is optional here....because it is so very good! 

That being said, a light hand with the olives would not go amiss.
A little bit on sweet side- but I didn’t add the olives. Next time want to add a variety of spices- cayenne etc. and I did chuck roast which I would recommend as well.

My husband had smoked  a pork butt, and since it is just the two us, I'm always looking for recipes where I can use the pork. I found this one. I prepared the braised peppers and onions and then removed them from the pan and set them aside. I added some oil to the pan and took the previously shredded pork and added it to the pan to crisp it up and get it hot. I continued with the recipe and served it with the suggested rice and black beans. We will be making this recipe again adding some heat.

Didn't have flank steak so used stew meat, took much longer (of course!), eventually put it in the crock pot to finish. Felt dish was a bit sweet, added white vinegar to try and cut the sweet taste a bit. Could have perhaps used more olives, brine from the olives to cut the sweetness.

I was a bit disappointed with this dish. First, the two TABLESPOONS called for in the Braised Onions and Peppers is a bit much. The raisins and olives make no sense (they doin picadillo). Finally, in the end the meat was a bit tough.

I will say, I have had ropa with olives and capers before but traditionally that's not included in this dish. Raisins never goes in this dish, ever. I think they're trying to blend an eastern hemisphere palate with a western hemisphere palate/culture.

Using what was at hand, I had currants and since my husband doesn't care for sweet foods at all, cut that in half. He devoured it! I didn't use bell peppers bc I had a bunch of different ones from the CSA & used them. Pasillas were great! I splashed a bit extra olive brine in to aid in reduction & the last half hour it really got silky & lush. I had a mix of flap steak & carne asada style thin-sliced chuck. I followed the rest closely. DELISH. All in one pot - peppers & onions first, then meat.

Raisins and capers? that is not how we made it at home.  I marinate overnight and cook the flank steak with onions and garlic. Then I thread by hand and cook slowly with peppers, onions and hot sauce.  I use D'Elidas  Salsa from Panama.

Used tri-tip since it was on special. Cut it against the grain into 1 1/2" thick slices. It shredded beautifully. Also left out the raisins and added 1/4 cup white wine and a splash of red wine vinegar as others have noted. Cooked the onions and peppers in 6 quart Dutch oven, removed veggies and browned the meat. One pot for everything. Flavors melded together beautifully. Everyone loved it!

This was my favorite dish when I lived in Miami. Bring back so many memories. I don't remember the raisings tho.
If you are in Miami Beach, the restaurant in the corner of 7th and Collins has THE ABSOLUTE best Ropa Vieja I've ever had! Worth the visit, cheap and delicious. Ask for a table on the sidewalk area. <3
Agreed! Puerto Saga is a must when in South Beach!!
*Sagua*

I misunderstood the flank steak and used leg (beef) instead, including bone   marrow. Which ended up very well.

To add a bit deeper flavours to the beans I fried them in / with bacon fat.

Overall delicious.

Really good. Skipped the raisins because we’d been eating a bunch. Made veggies with 1T cumin then added more to sauce.
A keeper
As a Venezuelan, this recipe seems slightly bizarre. Raisins? Seriously? Also, it's carne mechada, not desmechada.

Made the pepper mixture on day 1. Made the flank steak on day 2. Let the stew sit overnight. On day 3, we had the best stew. Modifications that I made: omit the raisins, but added a tablespoon of white vinegar. The capers gave the stew some interest.

Phenomenal recipe!  I never would have thought to use flank steak but it was the perfect cut to shred. Served this with the suggested black beans and rice and sautéed greens with bacon to a dinner party. Everyone was blown away. The olives and raisins added a good bit of flavor to the dish. Mojitos helped get everyone in a Cuban state of mind!
I modified a lot to make it savory instead of sweet. No raisins or capers. Can of diced tomatoes instead of tomato sauce. Green olives added. This was a good use of crappy Park Ave brisket.

Made as written - it was great! For all those who say this recipe isn't 'authentic,' you gotta remember...ropa vieja originated in Spain and traveled across the Americas. There are versions with raisins, garbanzos, carrots, and potatoes. All of them are 'authentic' to a city, a region, or even a family.  The point of this recipe was to learn how to use the 'building blocks' of taste described in the article.
What would be a good vegetarian protein for this?
Linda McCartney's pulled pork (frozen)
How about tofu that has been frozen and then thawed to give it a more meaty texture?  Otherwise, I'd just add the black beans to the other ingredients without the meat.
The meat alternatives that you find on the shelf in Whole Foods or other similar stores.
Has anyone subbed mushrooms and/or tofu for vegie take on this recipe?
Can this be done in an Instapot?
There is no explanation as to how to braise the vegetables. Any recommendations?
If you click on ‘braised peppers and onions’ in the ingredient list, it brings you to a link with instructions
How would one cook this in an Instantpot? Anybody know?
Carne Deshebrada is an authentic Mexican filling for tacos, tamales, enchiladas, burritos etc. and is much more flavorful.

Just made this with changes (I know, annoying). All same but used 2lbs chicken in slow cooker, cut broth to 1/2c. High for 3.5hrs. Ladeled out most of liquid, shredded chicken, added liquid back to desired level of sauciness, served over rice. Hot sauce on the side. DELICIOUS. Everyone devoured, including kids. A keeper!
I cooked this in a slow cooker after searing the meat in a skillet. I used the same ingredients except I omitted the stock. Happy with the result.
It is fantastic, very tasty. I made it with white rice and black beans. Love it!

I appreciate the notes from some who have made this/eaten this in their native Cuba, but they don’t advise on how to make it better/different.  As the recipe stands, it quite delicious.  But if using flank steak, it takes much longer than 2 1/2 hrs to cooks.  I think there sweetness from the raisins is a great flavor.
The addition of raisins, olives and capers is not traditional.  The dish works better without them.

In my family we make a complete meal out of ropa vieja.  I use the flank steak to flavor garbanzo bean soup.  I put together the meat and the dry garbanzos (with seasonings) in the pressure cooker.  After they are done I finish the garbanzo soup and proceed to make the ropa vieja - no capers, no olives and no raisins.
My family loves this meal - sopa de garbanzos, ropa vieja and white rice.  I love it too - it would probably be my choice if I had the chance to choose my last meal.

Are you suppose to dress rice and meat with the braising liquid
or are the ingredients worn out after 2 1/2 hours.

I had a decent amount of braising liquid remaining. I served the meat with the braising liquid over the rice and the liquid was easily absorbed by the rice. If you had little to no braising liquid left, you might try a slower simmer next time and just keep an eye on it. You want to keep the simmer going but not so much that all of the liquid evaporates. I adjust my heat a couple of times.

I thought this was an excellent recipe. Prep work was minimal and the flavor was exceptional. I personally liked the sweet raisins combined with the salty olives and capers. I didn't think the sweetness of the raisins, along with the caramelized peppers and onions, was overwhelming, but if you aren't a fan of sweet with your savory, it might be more than you'd like.

Delicious and easy to make. Minimal prep work is required and the whole dish comes together in a single pot. I served it to my partner this evening with a side of buttery mashed potatoes. She loved it.  Big dollop of potatoes, make a well, and ladle in the ropa vieja. Very comforting. Tomorrow we'll eat the left overs with black beans simmered in coconut milk and some white rice.

Loved this dish.  I used tri tip instead of flank steak.  Very good.  Came out tender (after 3 hours) and shreddable.   The unusual mix of ingredients--yellow raisins, olives, capers, tomatoes and peppers and onions--provoked a lot of comment at our table.  I  have a Cuban friend who says she doesn't add the raisins but that some people do.  I would definitely make this again.
I made this with beef. The recipe does not say to do this, but if using beef I would (and did) use beef stock instead of chicken.
Unpleasant, did not finish. Very little flavor.
Just made this, and it was amazing. Great flavor, easy to make. Will def be making it again. Thank you!

I made this according to the recipe, except I omitted raisins and I only had about a tablespoon of capers. It was delicious. Authentic? Maybe not, but it was juicy, flavorful, and more complex than I expected, given the relative lack of spices. I think getting the steak well browned is the key. I’ll definitely make this again.
This turned out tough, meager, and overly soupy. A good way to ruin flank steak.
This was delicious.... cooked exactly as recipe but minus olives.
Any reason this can't be slow cooked in a crockpot for a few hours after browning the meat?
It needs the reduction that comes from a cracked lid on the stove top. It will be too liquid-y and insufficiently concentrated down into the beautiful brothiness in a crockpot. IMO.

Not sure what I did wrong but it turned out very tough for me. Maybe the cut of flank steak wasn't the best quality? It did creep above a low simmer for a few minutes, almost a rolling boil, so that could have done it. Kind of afraid to try again with such an expensive cut. Otherwise it tasted great! Loved the sweetness, the olives, the plump sultanas, and the peppers.

Peter, the way we Cubans make it is using skirt steak, put it in a Dutch oven for 30 minutes with 3 or 4 cloves of garlic, salt, and water.  Braise until a fork pierces the meat easily, then remove it from the Dutch oven & reserve the 'broth' (drippings from meat and water). Let meat cool and using 2 forks, shred the meat. Reserve in a plate.  There are more steps (add certain spices and dry wine to reserved meat, plus some reserved broth).  Don't have enough room to post the entire recipe

I made this by making the pepper-onion mixture several weeks ahead and freezing it. I left the raisins out because I didn’t have any, but otherwise followed recipe to a T, using flank steak. I wanted to love it but didn’t, mostly because it seemed kind of bland.
The first time I did it I didn't make the braised peppers and onions ahead of time, and the flavor was much better.

Rachael, you also need to use good olive oil and add about 1/4 cup or more of sherry.  It also needs some spices, just read the comments made by Cubans, including mine.  Cubans do not cook with grapeseed oil or Canola, they have no flavor.  People who eat something not authentic (and not identified as such) tend to think that a beloved national dish is not very good, because they assume that is how locals prepare it.

Judging from the number of commenters saying raisins, capers, and/or olives are non-standard I don't know how this will be received, but on the second run I cut the amount of pork in half and added two packages of bean sprouts (about 4 cups raw), and it turned out quite good! My only issue is that the oil from the braised peppers and onions along with the oil from braising the meat makes it a little too oily. Next time I'll grill the meat rather than doing it in the pan.
Victor p was right - it was underwhelming.  My husband and son said they thought it was good, but I think they were just being nice.

I cooked this last night, and based on suggestions, i did not include the raisins or olives. The overall consensus from our family (four of us) was that it  was OK.  This isn't a bad thing...not every meal can be amazing.  We will probably make this again, but not add it to the regular rotation.  It took a while to cook, so it would only be on the weekend where we could make it.

Based on other comments, I did not use raisins or olives and was glad I didn't. Cooked in oven for 2 hours at 300 rather than stove top. I was concerned that 2 tblsp of cumin was excessive and it certainly smelled that way when cooking, but that had mellowed by dinner time.

hi all-- my first time thru this recipe.  Does one
 serve only the meat?  w/o the onions and peppers?

Made this as laid out. I hesitated on adding the raisins but went ahead. The raisins essentially dissolve into the stew which made it very sweet. Next time I’ll reduce the amount or add none at all. I will also cut the olives into finer slices to even out the salty punch throughout. This was very easy and made a nice warm meal for a cool evening.
This was delicious. I left out the raisins bc the combination sounded strange to me but followed rest of recipe exactly. I will make again.
i have studied this over and over and i cant find what temperature the oven is supposed to be. one commenter said 250 but then cooked it for 5 hours. ????
This dish isn't prepared in the oven.  It's made on the stovetop.
This was delicious! I followed recipe with one exception, I used 1/4 cup of chopped prunes instead of raisins. It didn't make it sweet, just added a flavor layer. Will definitely do again.
We made this with Thompson raisins, half veggie broth and half red wine, and Moroccan olives. Excellent.

Made this per recipe & comments...used Instant Pot. I made braised peppers/onions using saute setting. Removed braised veg when finished to brown meat (used beef stew meat b/c smaller chunks). With Inst. Pot off, added braised veg back to meat, added raisins, added some paprika & oregano, & 1/2 cup sherry. Pressure Cooked 50 min High, w/35 min of Keep Warm. Meat was super tender but too much liquid. I wouldn't add raisins or sherry next time, & would reduce broth by 1/2cup. Too vinegary.
I used a pork butt. It was too fatty for me to eat the pork. I do believe it added to the flavor, though. I loved it and had leftovers twice (minus eating the pork).

I have never seen ropa vieja with capers and olives.  For fast cooking I use a electronic pressure cooker and shred the meat. Save the water from the pressure cooker to add some to the cooking of the ropa vieja. In the pressUte cooker I add large pieces of onion and garlic.   In a large pan cook the onion and pepper and add a can of stew tomatoes. Add the meat and a cup of the cooker broth. Add Goya sazón with azafrán and a tea spoon old bay and Italian seasoning olive oil.

Agree. I have made this dish many times and have never used capers, olives and raisins. Garlic, onions, green, red, and yellow peppers, olive oil. I also usually use chicken broth along with tomatoes. My version, just like the taste.

The braised onions and peppers is a must. At the last minute I had to leave the house so I threw everything into the crock-pot (I had already made the braised onions). Omitted the olives and raisins, b/c my family really doesn't like either in their food. I think the taste came out fine w/out them. This was the base for a BYO night, with tortillas, rice, beans, avocado, pico and greek yogurt and was a big hit. I finished the meat with sherry vinegar & will add more heat next time.

The key to this dish is to make it in a pressure cooker.  Any rubbery texture that you may get from a dutch oven or the like is eliminated and the flavors are infused throughout the flank steak.  Shredding the beef afterwords becomes a no-brainer.  And yes, full-blooded Cuban Boy here.
Very nice. Served with black beans, rice and maduros (plantains).
This recipe is outstanding with venison. We’re a family of hunters and we are always looking for a new way to cook venison. And it’s fool proof and freezable.

The end result is too sweet in my opinion. I’d suggest subbing 1/2 a cup of the stock with red wine, and putting in the raisins in the last 15 minutes so that they keep their sweetness concentrated, not disintegrate and become part of the sauce. As an added touch, I’d also fry the raisins in some oil or maybe even reserved beef fat to directly caramelize their sugars. This is what people making pulao or biriyani do to darken the character of the raisins. It makes them meld better with red meat.
Our local Cuban restaurant doesn’t use the olives or raisins, either, but we quite enjoyed the picadillo-ish add-ins.

skipped the raisins and olives (use them for picadillo), but i used a bunch of poblanos, blackened and the grill, then peeled and sliced into strips. The pepper strips and the meat shreds go great together.

In my Cuban partner's family, olives, raisins, and capers aren't traditional, so we omitted them.  
My partner and I are vegetarians, so we made some other nontraditional amendments to this recipe. Instead of meat, we shredded four king oyster mushrooms with a fork and sauteed them in oil with a little salt and pepper until golden. Then, we deglazed the pan with about a quarter cup of homemade mushroom stock, added the tomatoes, along with the braised peppers and onions, and let reduce slighty.

Made this pretty much as printed. I can’t understand where the comments about sweetness come from, definitely wasn’t sweet. The flavours really melded after several days. I found it well seasoned even though there wasn’t much seasoning. The ingredients really came together

If you watch his YouTube video, Kenji makes this completely differently on there - different ingredients, steps and even a different simmering process (puts it in the oven at 300 for 2 hours instead of stovetop simmering). Follow the recipe he does there instead of this written one. I could not bring myself to put raisins in this, maybe somebody could report back on how that worked out for them.

I used this recipe https://www.washingtonpost.com/recipes/instant-pot-ropa-vieja/?utm_campaign=wp_veatvoraciously&utm_medium=email&utm_source=newsletter&carta-url=https%3A%2F%2Fs2.washingtonpost.com%2Fcar-ln-tr%2F45fa80d%2F69304264017d475420a2d9c0%2F598a15879bbc0f6826fb3e3a%2F20%2F42%2F69304264017d475420a2d9c0 last night, and all in, with prep, it took 2 hours. It was delicious, though I thought it could have been a tad sweeter. Next time I’ll try adding some raisins.
This was delicious! I used chicken thighs and no raisins (I don’t like them). A keeper.

I’m Cuban, my mom made great ropa vieja and have had it at many Miami restaurants. The below recipe resulted in one of the best I have ever had. https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=k8PSdgdV0tk Absolutely no capers, olives or raisins, those belong in picadillo, another great Cuban classic. Buen provecho!

Good recipe. I don’t mind the capers, raisins, and olives, but I noticed the absence of garlic and spices such as cumin and oregano. Also, I liked the fact that Kenji cooked the meat in the sauce rather than boiling it first in water. However, once I shred the beef, I cooked it for another hour to impart more flavor. Thank you Kenji, I love your book.

title: Chicken and Cabbage Salad With Miso-Sesame Vinaigrette Recipe
author: J. Kenji López-Alt

comments.commentBody: Start with 1 tablespoon of sesame oil. You may find 2T overpowering. I started with 1T, and I was glad I did. Next time I will add grated ginger to the dressing instead of small pieces in the salad. I love ginger, but prefer it to be less up front in the flavors. I made this one night (using Napa cabbage and romaine), and then used the remaining dressing on a salad with dark greens alongside broiled salmon with a quick marinade of lime, sriracha, and maple syrup.

Excellent! Dressing is fabulous, will be using on lots of things. I mixed a bit of the dressing with the cabbage 45 minutes before serving so it would soften, then incorporated the other elements and remaining dressing right before serving so the other veg and herbs wouldn't get soggy. The ginger is nice; when I make the dressing for other purposes, I may add some ginger to it. For this salad, I soaked the ginger in seasoned rice wine vinegar first to take the bite out of it.
This recipe is outstanding!
Added celery and grated carrots. Yum!

So it’s a Chinese chicken salad vaguely reminiscent of the old ramen coleslaw. Neither of which is a bad thing. The dressing is “finger lickin’ good.” Literally. Couldn’t keep my finger out of it. I used cabbage and carrots which bled a lot of liquid. Strained and was fine. Skipped the cucumbers (because I don’t like them) in favor of radishes. To my taste it needed both tablespoons of sesame oil. Next time I’ll add more sesame seeds (because I like them).  And peanuts would be a nice addition.

I poached the chicken breasts in a court-bouillon with some scrap veggies I had lying around along with some miso. This was a great way to get more use out of that big packet of miso that I never seem to use as often as I want... 

Also soaked the cabbage in a brine for an hour to soften and flavor it before composing the salad. That helps both with flavor and texture.

Use red cabbage! Healthier and a more striking combination. I eat at a Middle Eastern restaurant that serves a side salad - very simple and refreshing - of just shredded red cabbage, lemon juice and salt.
So good. I added some chopped roasted peanuts for a bit more crunch!

I loved this recipe. I didn’t measure the dressing ingredients but rather eyeballed it and adjuster to my taste preference. The components of the salad are great and filling. I had to omit the radish bc I didn’t have any but was still great without. Make this!
Next time put ginger in dressing! Avocado was a nice addition.

I used celery since I was out of radishes and added
half a pack of ichiban noddles and some sriracha in the dressing. Awesome!
This takes 10 minutes if you have a sous chef, 45ish if you don’t. It’s a wonderful summer salad that is good for leftovers.

Great salad, only critique is it's not REALLY 10 minutes (chopping veggies, making dressing, poaching chicken), more like 60 start to finish.  Excellent as written, only added to it: Some crunchy mung bean sprouts, pre-packaged shredded cabbage   shredded carrot; thinly sliced jalapeno for adults. While we adults appreciated the refreshing low-carb salad, for kids we made soba noodles, tossed in a little dressing, to mix in with the salad part (... and admit the noodles are a great addition)!

Really delicious salad however, I would argue the 10 minute time unless you already have mise en place, and the miso vinaigrette prepared in advance as well. Also, one note regarding those who find red onions to be too strong of a taste, try slicing and soaking them in cold water beforehand (for a few hours or overnight). This helps to mellow their flavor.

My favorite summer salad for the past few years. I make a simplified version of this using the dressing as written, plus green cabbage, cilantro, ginger, sometimes shredded carrots, sometimes radish, sometimes avocado, and sometimes dried minced onion, plus plain poached chicken breast. I keep the dressing and protein separate and find that this make a really hardy, hearty salad to keep in the fridge and eat throughout the week. The dressing is def the star, but it’s all great.
This asked for “cooked” cabbage.  Seems like it should be simply shredded, yes?
No. Chicken is cooked, not the cabbage. Look again??
This is super flavorful, quick and easy. Would be amazing with grilled shrimp or salmon. The dressing is what makes this so amazing!
Excellent recipe. Ok to skip shallot and use less oil in dressing. I used both scallions and red onions.
This is similar to Wolfgang Puck's Chinoise Salad, which is the best thing to eat at DEN.

Delicious!  Only change was I used shrimp as the protein.  Sautéed them in some Chinese five spice and Chili oil.  I made the recipe times 4, for neighbors in the area, all going through some difficult times.  Everyone has asked for the recipe!  Per other suggestions, I did cut back on the oil in the dressing, and I put the ginger in the dressing.  I used thinly sliced Napa cabbage as the salad with carrots and radishes.

Absolutely phenomenal salad. I followed the template very loosely two separate times using the miso dressing and fridge dived to make a cohesive dish. I love how easy this recipe is to adapt to whatever you have on hand! Cannot recommend enough, thanks again kenji!

Wow, I *love* this dish!! If you don't anticipate eating it all up at once, I think it tastes even better on day 2 – but the chicken does become mushy that way. Next time, I might leave the chicken set aside, only marinating a portion in the vinaigrette before mixing it into the amount of salad I'll be serving.
Loved this salad. Added a smidge of grated ginger to dressing and added roasted peanuts to salad. So good. Will def salt the cabbage beforehand next time.

Fabulous! Added sliced sugar snap peas and a packet of cooked, drained and cooled high quality ramen noodles (minus the flavor packet) to the salad. With a nice baguette, this was a satisfying supper for a hot summer night. The dressing will be made frequently and live in my refrigerator; perfect sweet/tart/salty balance intense with sesame flavor.
Can make into spring rolls

I made this recipe almost as written. I used carrots instead of radishes and soy curls instead of chicken. To make that sub, I reconstituted 2.5 ounces of dry Butler soy curls in Better than Boullion No-Chicken broth, squeezed out the excess liquid, and browned them in a cast iron skillet filmed with oil. Turns this into a vegan entree salad! Will make it again.

Winner winner chicken dinner!

Absolutely Delicious!!!!  Lovely flavor & depth.

The only alteration I made was using a scant 1/2 tbs sugar, as we normally try not to add any. Oh and didn’t see matchstick ginger til very end and then CBA.

Love this salad! Highly suggest adding the following to level it up. 
-Pack of instant ramen noodles (dry, w/out the seasoning, crunched up)
-Peanuts
-Finish with chili oil & fresh lime wedges 

It saves well in the fridge for leftovers as well, which is nice since most salads don’t. :)

Look at notes.  

Consider a soak of ginger in Rice Wine vinegar if chopped.
The one suggestion for improvement that I have for this extremely excellent salad is to add crispy fried onions/shallots as a garnish. It adds a decadent little bit of crunch.

This worked well for two people for large, dinner sized salads. I used black vinegar instead of balsamic, and rice wine vinegar instead of the white wine vinegar. I also added crushed up instant ramen noodles for nostalgia and crunch. I liked the julienned ginger - I used a mandoline to thinly slice planks before julienning. Avocado or roasted peanuts would both be good additions next time.
I made this exactly as written! Fabulous!

This was delicious and perfect on a hot summer day when you don’t want to turn on the oven or stove. I just shredded some rotisserie chicken and followed the recipe as written. I saw suggestions for adding chopped peanuts or snap pea pods, I might do that next time. A great way to use up some of those veggies in the fridge.
Added toasted sliced almonds! Did a combo of cabbage and Romaine…Love this salad!
Made without garlic or onions in salad or dressing (low FOBMAP diet) and it was still absolutely fantastic! Best meal so far on this diet, so happy! Mixed romaine and green cabbage.

This salad is one of our favorites - probably because of the dressing!  I put the ginger in the dressing.  I use napa cabbage, add carrots and any other stray vegetables to the salad.  I add cooked faro to the salad as a carb; it gives a nice teture.  If I don't have any chicken I serve with tofu and/or chickpeas  - or just as it is.
This was absolutely delicious with green cabbage & cilantro (not a fan of mint).  This is going on my permanent rotation!
Made this tonight; no radishes or cukes in the house, and I substituted onion for shallots in the dressing. Delicious!!
Where is the miso sesame recipe?
There's a hyperlink in the ingredients list to the recipe.
The red "Miso-Sesame Vinaigrette" is a link to the vinaigrette recipe. Run your mouse over the words and click to go to the recipe.
Click on the related article: it is linked there (at the bottom).
Can’t find recipe for miso vinegrette
Try using Toasted Sesame Oil as Your Oil.

So loved this dish even though it was time consuming to make and so worth it. Even loved it more the next day when all the flavors came together and added a bit more dressing. The ginger was a great, I chopped it well, don't leave it out, it added a lot of good flavor.
Kids ate it all—not a shred leftover—and demanded a repeat next weekend. Delicious.

Used rotisserie chicken as a quick and easy way to put this recipe together and it worked nicely.  
And because I had Savoy cabbage on hand, that was used too.  Based on the comments of others I put the ginger (finely minced) in the dressing and cut back on the amount of oil in the dressing.  Satisfying dish and a versatile dressing.  Definitely would make again.
used 1tbsp sesame oil in the vinaigrette, left out the ginger but added fresh korean pear and juice from half a lemon instead. brought some more brightness to the dish. so so so good!
Perfect recipe. I loved it, and most importantly my wife loved it. Added to our rotation.
The perfect comfort food. Quick and easy. I make this when my stomach feels a little sensitive. Can’t go wrong. Always serve on a bed of hot white rice

Love this recipe. I’ve made it at least 10 times in the last couple of months. I end up using most of the dressing. This is is not a 10 minute recipe. Maybe it is. I’m just an amateur, but I can’t it shorter than 20.
I made with romaine hearts and found they were a bit too weak to complement the strength of the other ingredients. Next time will go with cabbage. But this is a fantastic salad.

Added a bit more vinegar to the salad once all mixed together. Added cabbage and more dressing to chicken about half an hour before serving. Would consider a touch more soy sauce to dressing as well as putting a bit of ginger in there in addition to what was in the salad itself.
Excellent!

This was very good. I salted the cabbage in a colander for an hour and then rinsed it. That improves the texture and taste.  Also, because my dressing turned out a little bitter for some reason, I squirted some Henderson's sweet vinagrette on the greens before putting on sesame dressing.  I really liked it, and I think next time I'll put some sliced almonds on top.
I agree that adding roasted peanuts sounds good and would also try finely slices or chopped serrano chiles.

This was delicious! For the dressing, I grated ginger as suggested by another reviewer, and added it before blending rather than adding it to the salad in matchsticks. Other changes: soy sauce for tamari; champagne vinegar; white balsamic; white miso; and only 1 Tbsp of sesame oil. Such a great tasting dressing! I loved the complexity of it. 

For the salad I used half green cabbage, half butter lettuce/radicchio mix, and scallions. Added sugar snap peas.

This was a delicious salad!

This was delicious. To bulk it up a bit, I toasted a couple of packs of broken ramen and a handful of almonds. Then I added them to the salad after they cooled. The recipe made plenty of dressing even with those additions. Next time I think I’ll cut up a jalapeño and throw that in as well.
Delicious. We used a big head of romaine, 3 Persian cucumbers, and shredded carrot and daikon radish with the other ingredients. Also added some peanuts for crunch.
2tbs of seasme oil is too much but other than that this was incredible

Really yummy! I made as written, except turns out my miso paste was covered in a layer of mold, so I left it out. The dressing was still delicious without it. Also, it says to serve right away but I’ve always found cabbage salads hang out well. I’m eating the leftovers right now and it’s still good. Just drain any excess liquid and toss with a little more dressing. Oh and I added a handful of peanuts for crunch. Maybe I didn’t make it as written. Ha!
Made this last night with salmon. Was delicious! I used less sesame oil in the dressing as suggested. Will make again exploring other ingredient variations.

This salad was delicious. The dressing and the fresh herbs made it. Added ginger to the dressing and scallion to the salad. Julienned radish and shredded carrot. Used romaine and roasted salmon seasoned simply. Would be good with any crunchy lettuce/veg/shreddable protein. Would definitely make again.

Made the recipe to the letter and it was awesome. Used the leftover dressing on store-bought "power slaw" mix the next day and it made eating raw veggies delightful. I was skeptical about the miso/sesame flavors combined with balsamic, but I was dead wrong. 

Planning to make another batch to dress salmon tonight.
This was great! It reminds me of Wolfgang Puck’s chinois salad and it keeps well for a few days.

The dressing is really good. The salad if cabbage is used can stay up 2-3 days in the fridge with no to little compromised flavor. Unfortunately, I did not have miso so I substituted with 1 tbs Tamari instead. Next time will make the original.
This is SO good.  I made it with red cabbage, carrots, radishes, jicama, green onions, red onion and cilantro.  Love it
Just great. Will enter regular rotation.
Used red cabbage slices, leftover cooked salmon and daikon instead of radishes.  Any combination would work with this because it is the dressing that makes it really work.
This was great. I used poached chicken breast. That dressing. Wow.

The dressing is a bit sweet so with I doubled the miso and vinegar and it was great.
I made it with both green and Napa cabbage + lightly fried tofu since I didn't have chicken available.  I sliced the cabbage not too thin (1/8-1/4") so it'd stay crunchy.
It's a keeper!
Added slivered snow pea pods, didn’t have romaine so it was all cabbage.  Didn’t have chicken so threw in some cooked shrimp and was fab.

The vinaigrette is so delicious! Added some sliced red peppers, snap peas and slivered carrots, (forgot to add the ginger, oops) all served stop cold somen noodles. My (picky) fam all loved it. This is definitely going in the easy dinner rotation - will be perfect on a hot day, though we ate it on a cold, snowy winter day and loved it!
Outstanding recipe. I used cabbage for the greens.
I did not have any Mizo but it was good with out. I used carrots green cabbage and turkey meat that I sautéed yummy salad, also added peanuts

Using pre-shredded romaine/red cabbage/carrot mix   pre-pulled chicken made this a BREEZE.

Added some toasted cashews for extra crunch — going to just lean in   add canned mandarin oranges next time, too. It’s a nostalgic keeper of a recipe.
I didn’t have all the dressing ingredients so I used Martha Rose Shulman’s Sesame Ginger Vinaigrette instead. (Also on this site)
Omitted sesame because I’m allergic and it was still awesome. I think I’ll add roasted peanuts for lunch tomorrow since it needed some starch and crunch
The dressing is delicious. I would suggest adding julienned carrots for color and crunch. My non-salad-eating partner ate three servings of this!
September 16, 2024. Made this for Lynn and me for dinner! Excellent recipe and the dressing is really easy. Forgot the ginger but will add it to the dressing next time!
brilliant salad. I didn't make any substitutions, but if any particulars were swapped out, it would still be tasty i think. Shared w a friend for a working lunch & they raved!
Add chopped peanuts for the extra crunch you know it needs!

This is the perfect recipe to riff on. I added a layer of thai glass noodles on the bottom of the bowl and a healthy handful of chopped peanuts for the ultimate at-home restaurant quality entree. The dressing was pure gold! No notes.
Great salad. With do crispy tofu or salmon next time. Add peanuts for a crunch!
Add lots of cilantro and mint. Needs more dressing than called for—1/4 cup or so. Perfect with 3 cups cabbage, 3 cups romaine.

This is delicious! Don't skip the mint and cilantro...they add great punch! I made it as listed the first time around, and the second, I skipped the chicken and added mandarin oranges and toasted almonds. Both versions are knock-out! The dressing is the key. I reduced the tamari (miso is already salty) and the sesame oil (only slightly) and made it with an immersion blender. Doubled the batch and happy I did....it's a great dressing with lots of uses!
I used regular green cabbage which is what I had. Napa cabbage would have been better.
I loved this salad. Next time I will poach the chicken ahead of time and marinate it in the dressing for a couple of hours to soften it and get the flavor soaked in. The dressing is fabulous.

Super satisfying, delicious...next time I'll add some avocado...and add tiny bits of ginger..had it with green cabbage and romaine to balance out the texture...happy to have this new dressing in my arsenal.

I’ve been doing this for years but w/o garlic and cumin. I like garlic fresh and don’t want to set my spice tone. I buy peppers at Costco and after a few days, if I haven’t used them, I braise them, either diced or sliced, with onions and freeze them in ice cubes or in those one cup, four section trays. Once frozen I pop them into a Food Saver bag and they sit in my freezer ready to use. I do many prep item like this. Who said cooking for one has to be basic and boring?
I do the same as Martha. Prep, freeze, them I would add my spice profile. Cumin is great for Mexican dishes, garlic more Italian. For me!
Accompanying article says that (freezing) is exactly what they do with it. They then thaw it out and make ropa vieja, baked pasta, etc.
would this freeze well?
Yes. I've found it helpful to package in ziplock bags in several portions. The bags store flat (space saver) and defrost quickly.
The author says that he freezes this in vacuum-sealed bags.  I froze it today in 3-oz. cubes that I intend to put into a zip-lock freezer bag.

The joy of that aroma of peppers cooking. I dry peppers, jalapeños, Thai, Serrano, and ghost out of my garden by the quart with that smell wafting through my house for days. 

I would never adulterate that ambrosia with something as strong as cumin.

I make this frequently on a Sunday and use throughout the week. Freeze what I don't use. The only thing I do differently is start with onions and get them to the almost carmelized  stage then add the remaining ingredients.
2 tbsp ground cumin is a vast amount for just 3 bell peppers and one onion. I found even one tbsp overwhelmed the sweetness of the peppers and onion.
I’m making a big batch of this now and my kitchen smells amazing! Next time, I might take out a bit before adding the cumin, just to keep future options open.

In Italy they call it Peperonata , although some of their recipes call for using tomatoes and peroncinos as well, and yes it freezes beautifully.
Seeing how they use it though in Cuba and Mexico is just a testament to how wonderful this is as a base for almost anything!!

If it's a bit watery after thawing just simmer until it reduces..

Unless you are making Ropa Viejo or one of the other suggested dishes (minus the hot dog), 2 tbsp of cumin seems excessive. I put in 1 tbsp and the texture was powdery, even after 30 mins of braising. Will make again using this technique but without cumin.

Yes. I'm making my second batch now and omitting the cumin. Not because I don't like it, but because it limits the versatility. Perhaps things like cumin or ginger can be added into the mix later, depending on what the next recipe suggests as a complementary flavor?
Made this several times but liked it most when I replaced the cumin by dried powdered ginger.

This works well, I do it without the cumin and garlic, so I can control the flavor profile when I use them. Yes, freezing does work very well. I vacuum seal them and use them later as a quick step for recipe's.
Add tomatoes and you have the base for shakshuka.
2 tbsp ground cumin is a vast amount for just 3 bell peppers and one onion, even one tbsp overwhelmed the sweetness of the peppers and onion.  Skip it or try much less.

I would probably use olive oil, and maybe a bit more than 3 tb, and I would braise for longer, so the effect is a kind of slurpy unctuousness rather than tenderness. And for me no cumin, as the pepper/onion/garlic/olive oil sweetness predominates.
Excellent! And a must is you are fixing the ropa vieja recipe.
Two TABLESPOONS of cumin? I find that a bit much.
For three peppers it is indeed.

Making this now. In order to remove all the moisture, I have been cooking for longer than 30 minutes-it is already one hour. I suspect it is essential to remove the excess moisture for best results? Smells delicious. Will make Ropa Viejo once the peppers and onions are done.

I frequently use a mixture like this, minus cumin, to top a vegetarian  pizza, quick and easy if you use a purchased mini or full sized pizza base.  Just pop it onto a preheated pizza stone and bake for a few minutes with or without cheese and tomato sauce. Since I need a very low fat diet, I saute with water rather than oil or cook in the microwave until just done and it's still quite good. Cooked this way, it requires a quick spray or brush of oil or canola oil on the pizza base first.

This is fabulous! I am coming back to it to make it when I have larger portions of everything on hand. I followed the recipe exactly, except using red onions because I didn't have the yellow ones on hand. I had it on a very low burner, so it took longe than the recipe states. It's a keeper!

I found this a different flavorful way to enjoy peppers and onions. Would not have thought of including the cumin.  Makes it distinctive and so would think about the amount depending on what I'm serving the peppers with.
I'm a vegetarian and decided to try braising potatoes from another recipe on this website and adding braised peppers and onions for the last 5-10 minutes. It was worth the experiment.

I'm always confused w/ how to tell when peppers are done.  I cook for 1 (myself) and always try to cook in big batches; so I doubled recipe in a medium sized enameled cast iron from Ikea.  The veggies fresh cut filled the pot to brim - which I knew would affect cooking time.  I stirred often, and it's still watery.  Onions look great, and tender.  But peppers still have a little crunch, and aren't 'soft'.  Is that 'tender' for peppers?  I know they're not supposed to be mushy, but...
"Tender" means that the peppers are soft and retain a slight soft bite and their structure without falling apart. But they should not crunch any longer.
Two tablespoons of cumin seems excessive

I made a batch to serve with steak. Added a large, sliced portobello mushroom to the mix. I enjoyed the cumin although I probably didn't use a full 2 tsp. Use any herb or spice depending on what you're serving this with, e.g., taggaron might be super with chicken.
Just made this and now eating with some roast chicken thighs. So easy an absolutely delicious, thank you!
only use 1 tea of cumin so you can use this with lots of other things
Yes, cumin doesn't go well with every cuisine, you can always add it later.
Made this last Fall with piles of peppers from the garden. Froze in packets and have been using in bean soups all winter - really elevates the soup! Also used in quesadillas.
Can this be made in bulk and frozen?
Should this pot be covered as in a braise. I found it dried out to quickly without. THX
I thought braises had liquid added? Isn’t this sautéed?
The vegetables are full of water.  If cooked slowly, you probably won't need additional water.  If you do, add only a teaspoon or two at a time.
How about pressure canning?  I have a lot more room on my shelves than I do in my freezer.
This is a delicious appetizer with crusty bread. Use olive oil.
Could this be frozen?
Thank you for this dinner-making idea! Please write more on this topic! I made three meals from this and both of my picky teens ate them.
This is the best thing I have ever made for hotdog relish!  I adore hotdogs and this elevates them to Queen status!  BTW, the cumin was beautiful!  Not too much never too little.
Whoops, Always Too Little!  Cumin that is.  Garlic and cumin married in peppers and onions. Life can be good.
This was better than I expected. I was surprised to leave it cooking without lid for 30 minutes. Need to stir every few minutes to prevent burning on one side.
I assume i remove the dried bay leaves...
